topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a deep neural network, predominantly built upon the **Transformer architecture**, that has been pre-trained on massive corpora of textual data (typically hundreds of gigabytes to terabytes) using self-supervised learning objectives. It learns statistical patterns, syntactic structures, semantic relationships, and world knowledge embedded in language, enabling it to perform a wide spectrum of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks such as text generation, summarization, translation, question-answering, and reasoning without task-specific training.

The defining characteristics that distinguish an LLM from traditional NLP models are:
1. **Scale** — Parameter counts ranging from hundreds of millions to over **1 trillion parameters** (e.g., GPT-4, PaLM-2, Llama 3).
2. **Generality** — A single model serves multiple downstream tasks via **in-context learning** (prompting) or fine-tuning.
3. **Emergent Abilities** — Capabilities (e.g., multi-step reasoning, code synthesis) that appear only beyond certain scale thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PECMT862, an LLM is formally defined as "a foundation model trained on large-scale text data using self-supervised objectives, capable of zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot generalization across diverse linguistic tasks."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Infinite Autocomplete"

Imagine you have read **every textbook in a library, every Wikipedia article, every novel, every code repository, and every legal document** ever written. You have not memorized them word-for-word, but you have internalized the *patterns* — how words typically follow other words, how arguments are constructed, how code flows from imports to output.

Now, if someone hands you the beginning of a sentence, you can predict what comes next with uncanny accuracy. You can also answer questions, write essays, summarize documents, and translate between languages — because all of these tasks reduce, at their core, to **"given some context, generate the most plausible continuation."**

An LLM is precisely this: an **incredibly well-read pattern completer**, scaled to superhuman capacity.

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Token** | Smallest unit of text processed (word-piece) | ~1 token ≈ 4 chars (English) |
| **Vocabulary Size** | Distinct tokens the model recognizes | **32,000 – 256,000** |
| **Parameters** | Trainable weights in the network | **1B – 1.5T** |
| **Context Window** | Max tokens processed in one pass | **2K – 1M tokens** |
| **Training Corpus** | Total text used for pre-training | **~1 – 15 trillion tokens** |

> [!NOTE]
> A common misconception is that LLMs "understand" language. Technically, they perform **next-token probability estimation** — but at scale, this mathematical objective yields behavior indistinguishable from understanding, a phenomenon often called the **"understanding illusion."**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Evolutionary Trajectory of Language Modeling

The journey from early NLP systems to modern LLMs can be mapped across **five distinct paradigms**:

### Paradigm 1 — Rule-Based Systems (1950s – 1990s)
- Hand-crafted linguistic rules, grammars, and lexicons.
- Examples: **ELIZA** (1966), SHRDLU, early machine translation systems.
- Limitation: Brittle, unscalable, no learning from data.

### Paradigm 2 — Statistical Language Models (1990s – 2010s)
- Use **n-gram** probability distributions to predict the next word.
- Core formulation:
  $$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}, \dots, w_{i-n+1})$$
- Limitation: Suffer from the **curse of dimensionality** and data sparsity.

### Paradigm 3 — Neural Language Models (2013 – 2017)
- Replace n-gram tables with **distributed word embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe) and feed-forward / RNN-based predictors.
- Key insight: Words with similar meanings occupy nearby regions in continuous vector space.

### Paradigm 4 — Sequence-to-Sequence & Attention (2014 – 2017)
- **Seq2Seq** encoder-decoder RNNs introduced for translation.
- **Bahdanau Attention (2014)** allowed models to "look back" at all source tokens when generating each output token.

### Paradigm 5 — Transformer-Based LLMs (2017 – Present)
- **Vaswani et al. (2017)** introduced the Transformer, replacing recurrence with **self-attention**.
- Enabled **massive parallelization** during training.
- Led to the modern era: BERT (2018), GPT series (2018 – 2024), T5, LLaMA, Mistral, Claude, Gemini.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of LLM Capability

LLM proficiency emerges from the interaction of three scaling axes:

1. **Data Scale** — More high-quality text → broader knowledge.
2. **Parameter Scale** — More weights → greater representational capacity.
3. **Compute Scale** — More FLOPs during training → better optimization.

The empirical finding that performance improves predictably with scale is captured by **Scaling Laws** (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Next-token probability | $P(x_t \vert x_{<t})$ | Foundational objective of every LLM |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \vert x_{<t})$ | Training signal; minimized via backpropagation |
| Perplexity (PPL) | $\text{PPL} = \exp\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} -\log P(x_t \vert x_{<t})\right)$ | Standard **evaluation metric** for LMs |
| Self-Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core Transformer operation |
| FLOPs per token (forward) | $\approx 2N$ (where $N$ = parameters) | Governs inference cost |
| Context Length | $L$ tokens | Determines max input size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "what is the fundamental training objective of an LLM?", the only mathematically precise answer is **maximum likelihood estimation of the next-token distribution**, expressed via the cross-entropy loss. Vague answers like "the model learns language" lose marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LLMs now underpin critical systems across industries:
- **Software Engineering** — Code completion (Copilot), bug fixing, automated testing.
- **Healthcare** — Clinical note summarization, medical Q&A.
- **Legal** — Contract analysis, precedent retrieval.
- **Education** — Personalized tutoring, automated grading.
- **Customer Service** — Conversational agents, multilingual support.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, when listing applications, always pair the **domain** with the **specific LLM capability** being used. Avoid generic statements like "LLMs are useful in many fields."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 How a Language Model Actually "Reads" and "Writes"

The complete operational pipeline of an LLM involves four sequential stages. We derive each step explicitly.

### Stage 1 — Tokenization

Raw text is converted into a sequence of integer indices using a subword tokenizer (e.g., **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**, WordPiece, or SentencePiece).

**Example Walkthrough:**
- Input string: `"LLMs are powerful"`
- Tokenization result (illustrative BPE): `["LL", "Ms", " are", " powerful"]`
- Mapped to IDs: `[15496, 17307, 527, 3666]`

The vocabulary mapping is a **bijection** $\mathcal{V} : \text{Token} \leftrightarrow \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$.

### Stage 2 — Embedding

Each token ID is converted into a dense vector $e_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, where $d$ is the model's hidden dimension (e.g., $d = 4096$ for a 7B model).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \text{TokenEmbedding}(\text{IDs}) + \text{PositionalEmbedding}(\text{positions}) \\
E &\in \mathbb{R}^{T \times d}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $T$ is the sequence length. Positional embeddings inject order information, since the self-attention mechanism is **permutation-equivariant** by construction.

### Stage 3 — Transformer Forward Pass

The embedded sequence passes through $L$ stacked Transformer blocks. Each block applies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{h}_{\ell}' &= \mathbf{h}_{\ell-1} + \text{MultiHeadAttention}(\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{h}_{\ell-1})) \\
\mathbf{h}_{\ell}   &= \mathbf{h}_{\ell}' + \text{FFN}(\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{h}_{\ell}'))
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\text{FFN}$ is a position-wise feed-forward network, typically:
$$\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$$

The self-attention itself is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V \\
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) &= \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents gradient vanishing in the softmax when $d_k$ is large.

### Stage 4 — Output Projection and Sampling

The final hidden state at position $t$ is projected back to vocabulary size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{logits}_t &= \mathbf{h}_L^{(t)} \cdot W_{\text{out}} \\
P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) &= \text{softmax}(\text{logits}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $W_{\text{out}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$.

**Sampling strategies** to choose the next token:
- **Greedy decoding**: $\hat{x}_t = \arg\max P(\cdot \mid x_{<t})$
- **Temperature sampling**: Divide logits by temperature $T$ before softmax. Lower $T$ → sharper distribution.
- **Top-$k$ / Top-$p$ (nucleus) sampling**: Restrict sampling to the most probable tokens.

### Stage 3.5 — Autoregressive Generation Loop

Text generation proceeds iteratively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1, x_2, \dots, x_t &= \text{prompt} \\
x_{t+1} &\sim P(\cdot \mid x_1, \dots, x_t) \\
x_{t+2} &\sim P(\cdot \mid x_1, \dots, x_{t+1}) \\
&\;\;\vdots
\end{aligned}
$$

This **autoregressive** property is what makes LLM generation sequential and computationally expensive at inference time.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Perplexity Calculation

Suppose a test sentence has $T = 4$ tokens with per-token negative log-likelihoods:
$-\log P(x_1 \mid \emptyset) = 2.1$, $-\log P(x_2 \mid x_1) = 1.4$, $-\log P(x_3 \mid x_{1,2}) = 3.2$, $-\log P(x_4 \mid x_{1,2,3}) = 1.8$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average NLL} &= \frac{2.1 + 1.4 + 3.2 + 1.8}{4} = \frac{8.5}{4} = 2.125 \\
\text{Perplexity} &= e^{2.125} \approx 8.37
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The model is, on average, as "uncertain" as choosing uniformly among $\approx 8$ equally likely tokens at each step. Lower PPL → better language model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evolution of Language Modeling Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rule Based NLP 1950s to 1990s] --> B[Statistical n gram Models 1990s to 2010]
    B --> C[Neural Language Models 2013 Word2Vec LSTM]
    C --> D[Seq2Seq and Attention 2014 to 2017]
    D --> E[Transformer Era 2017 to Present]
    E --> F[BERT 2018 Encoder]
    E --> G[GPT Family 2018 to 2024 Decoder]
    E --> H[T5 and BART 2019 to 2020]
    G --> I[Modern LLMs GPT4 Llama Claude Gemini]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LLM Inference Pass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp1[Raw Text Prompt] --> tok[Tokenizer BPE or SentencePiece]
    tok --> emb[Token and Positional Embedding Layer]
    emb --> tr1[Transformer Block 1 Self Attention plus FFN]
    tr1 --> trN[Transformer Block L Repeated N times]
    trN --> proj[Output Projection to Vocabulary Size]
    proj --> soft[Softmax to obtain Token Probabilities]
    soft --> samp[Sampling Module Greedy or Top p]
    samp --> out1[Generated Token]
    out1 --> loop{Stop Condition Met}
    loop -- No --> emb
    loop -- Yes --> fin[Final Generated Sequence]
```

## 4.3 Three-Pillar Scaling Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[Pillar 1 Data Scale]
        d1[Corpus Size in Tokens]
        d2[Data Quality Filtering]
        d3[Deduplication]
    end
    subgraph P2[Pillar 2 Parameter Scale]
        p1[Embedding Dimension]
        p2[Number of Layers]
        p3[Attention Heads]
    end
    subgraph P3[Pillar 3 Compute Scale]
        c1[GPU TPU Hours]
        c2[FLOPs per Token]
        c3[Parallelism Strategy]
    end
    P1 --> E[Emergent LLM Capability]
    P2 --> E
    P3 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The inference diagram (4.2) explicitly captures the **autoregressive loop** — the feedback arrow from `Sampling Module` back to `Embedding Layer` is the single most important structural feature distinguishing LLM inference from standard feed-forward model inference. Examiners frequently test this in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Large Language Model. List any four distinguishing characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Large Language Model (LLM) is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, pre-trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised learning to predict the next token in a sequence.

Distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Scale** — Hundreds of millions to trillions of parameters.
2. **Generality** — Single model handles multiple NLP tasks via prompting.
3. **Emergent abilities** — Capabilities like reasoning and in-context learning appearing at scale.
4. **Autoregressive (or autoencoding) generation** — Produces coherent text by iterative sampling.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four characteristics — total 3.)*

---

### **Q2. What is perplexity? Why is it the preferred evaluation metric for language models?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Perplexity (PPL) is the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood per token:

$$\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} -\log P(x_t \mid x_{<t})\right)$$

It is preferred because:
1. It is **directly derived from the training objective** (cross-entropy), making it mathematically consistent.
2. It is **interpretable** — PPL = $k$ means the model is as uncertain as choosing among $k$ equally likely options.
3. It is **task-independent** and applicable to any text distribution.
4. Lower PPL reliably correlates with better language modeling quality.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the evolution of language modeling from rule-based systems to Transformer-based LLMs. Discuss the key limitations of each paradigm that motivated the next. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Rule-Based Systems (1950s–1990s)** — Used hand-crafted grammar rules and lexicons (e.g., ELIZA, early MT systems).  
   *Limitation:* Brittle, poor generalization, requires extensive manual effort, cannot handle linguistic ambiguity or unseen constructions.

**2. Statistical n-gram Models (1990s–2010s)** — Estimated $P(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})$ from frequency counts.  
   *Limitation:* Curse of dimensionality — the number of possible n-grams grows exponentially with $n$; sparse counts; no semantic generalization.

**3. Neural Language Models (2013–2017)** — Replaced discrete counts with distributed embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) and RNN/LSTM predictors.  
   *Limitation:* Sequential processing prevents parallelization; long-range dependencies still degrade due to vanishing gradients.

**4. Seq2Seq + Attention (2014–2017)** — Encoder-decoder RNNs with attention for translation.  
   *Limitation:* Still bottlenecked by recurrent sequential computation.

**5. Transformer-based LLMs (2017–Present)** — Vaswani et al.'s "Attention Is All You Need" eliminated recurrence, enabling full parallelization and superior long-context modeling.

*(Award ~1.4 marks per paradigm, with clear limitation justification.)*

**(b)** Describe the operational pipeline of an LLM inference pass, starting from a raw text prompt and ending with the generated output. Use a labelled diagram in your explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The pipeline consists of four sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Tokenization:** The prompt is split into subword tokens via BPE/WordPiece and mapped to integer IDs.

**Stage 2 — Embedding:** Token IDs are mapped to dense vectors of dimension $d$ via the embedding matrix $W_E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times d}$. Positional embeddings are added to inject order information.

**Stage 3 — Transformer Forward Pass:** The sequence passes through $L$ stacked blocks, each performing Multi-Head Self-Attention followed by a position-wise FFN, with residual connections and layer normalization. The attention computes:
$$\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

**Stage 4 — Output Projection & Sampling:** The final hidden state is projected to vocabulary size via $W_{\text{out}}$, softmaxed, and a token is sampled (greedy/top-$k$/top-$p$). The new token is appended, and the loop repeats until an EOS token or max length is reached.

Refer to the **Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.2** for the visual representation.

*(Award marks for: 2 marks tokenization, 2 marks embedding, 2 marks transformer block, 1 mark sampling loop.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define the cross-entropy loss used to train language models. Show mathematically how it connects to perplexity and next-token prediction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** For a token sequence $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T$, the cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\theta) &= -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \\
&= -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \frac{\exp(\text{logit}_{x_t})}{\sum_{v \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(\text{logit}_v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ denotes model parameters. This is the **negative log-likelihood** of the observed sequence under the model.

**Connection to Next-Token Prediction:** Each term $-\log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ penalizes the model inversely proportional to the probability it assigned to the *actually observed* next token. Minimizing $\mathcal{L}$ therefore maximizes the model's likelihood of generating the true text — i.e., it sharpens next-token predictions toward ground truth.

**Connection to Perplexity:** Dividing by $T$ and exponentiating:

$$\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\mathcal{L}(\theta)}{T}\right)$$

Thus perplexity is the **geometric mean** of the inverse probabilities — a normalized, interpretable measure of predictive uncertainty.

*(Award 2 marks definition, 2.5 marks derivation of connection, 2.5 marks perplexity derivation.)*

**(b)** Discuss the three pillars of LLM capability (data, parameters, compute) and explain why scaling all three jointly leads to emergent abilities. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pillar 1 — Data Scale:** Modern LLMs are trained on 1–15 trillion tokens of curated web text, books, code, and dialogue. Larger and cleaner datasets expand world knowledge and reduce hallucination.

**Pillar 2 — Parameter Scale:** Increasing parameters (e.g., 1B → 70B → 1T) increases the model's representational capacity — the number of distinct functions it can express. The Chinchilla scaling law suggests parameters should scale roughly linearly with dataset tokens.

**Pillar 3 — Compute Scale:** Measured in FLOPs, governed by GPU-hours and parallelism strategy. Compute budget determines whether a given (data, parameter) configuration can be fully optimized.

**Emergent Abilities:** Certain capabilities — multi-step arithmetic reasoning, chain-of-thought problem solving, in-context few-shot learning — appear **abruptly and unpredictably** once scale crosses specific thresholds. This non-linear emergence is hypothesized to arise from the interaction of all three pillars enabling the model to form higher-order abstract representations that smaller-scale combinations cannot support.

*(Award ~2 marks per pillar, 1 mark for emergent abilities discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "LLM" with "ChatGPT".** LLMs are the underlying models; ChatGPT is a product built on top of GPT. Examiners deduct marks for such conflation.
> 2. **Writing "LLMs understand language."** Technically, they estimate $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$. Use precise language.
> 3. **Skipping the autoregressive loop** in pipeline diagrams. The feedback from sampling to embedding is mandatory.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** in attention formulas. Examiners test this explicitly.
> 5. **Listing applications without linking to LLM capabilities.** Always pair domain + capability.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **LLM = Transformer + massive data + self-supervised next-token prediction.**
- ✅ **Evolution path:** Rule-based → n-gram → Neural → Seq2Seq → Transformer → LLMs.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Data, Parameters, Compute — scale all three jointly.
- ✅ **Core training objective:** Cross-entropy = negative log-likelihood of observed tokens.
- ✅ **Core evaluation metric:** Perplexity = $\exp(\mathcal{L}/T)$.
- ✅ **Inference pipeline:** Tokenize → Embed → Transformer blocks → Project → Sample → Loop.
- ✅ **Autoregressive generation** distinguishes LLM inference from feed-forward inference.
- ✅ **Self-attention formula:** $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- ✅ **Key distinctions to remember:** Token vs. word, Embedding vs. Hidden state, Greedy vs. Nucleus sampling.
- ✅ **Emergent abilities** appear non-linearly at scale thresholds — a key KTU-favourite topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Natural Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Natural Language (NL)

**Natural Language (NL)** is any language that has developed naturally in humans through use and repetition without conscious planning or premeditation. It is the primary mode of communication among humans, encompassing both **spoken** (phonetic/auditory) and **written** (orthographic/visual) forms.

In the context of **Large Language Models (LLMs)** and **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, Natural Language refers to the human-language text corpora (datasets) that computational models are designed to parse, understand, generate, and manipulate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Natural Language is the *raw input substrate* for all LLM operations. Every LLM (GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Mistral) is fundamentally a probabilistic model over sequences of natural language tokens.

### 1.2 Linguistic Levels of Natural Language (The 6-Tier Pyramid)

NL is not a monolithic entity; it possesses a strict hierarchical structure that LLMs attempt to model. From the smallest unit to the largest context:

| Tier | Level | Definition | LLM Equivalent |
|------|-------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Phonology** | Sound system of a language | Subword token acoustic embeddings |
| 2 | **Morphology** | Structure of words (roots, prefixes, suffixes) | Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) |
| 3 | **Syntax** | Sentence structure & grammar | Transformer self-attention layers |
| 4 | **Semantics** | Literal meaning of words/sentences | Embedding space (vector representations) |
| 5 | **Pragmatics** | Context-dependent intent | Few-shot prompting, RLHF |
| 6 | **Discourse** | Meaning across multiple sentences | Long-context attention windows |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Think of Natural Language as a multi-layered cake. Phonology is the *air bubbles* inside. Morphology is the *flour grains*. Syntax is the *cake's shape*. Semantics is the *flavor*. Pragmatics is the *occasion on which the cake is served* (birthday vs. wedding). Discourse is the *story of the baker who made it*. An LLM has to "taste" all six layers simultaneously to understand any single sentence.

### 1.3 Natural Language Processing (NLP) — The Bridge to LLMs

**Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is the sub-field of Computer Science, Artificial Intelligence, and Linguistics concerned with enabling computers to process, understand, and generate natural language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** NLP is the discipline that develops computational algorithms and statistical models to perform linguistic tasks on human-generated text and speech data. LLMs are the *current state-of-the-art* realization of NLP systems.

The key paradigm shifts in NLP, leading to the LLM era, are:
1. **Rule-based era (1950s–1990s):** Hand-crafted linguistic rules (e.g., ELIZA, SHRDLU).
2. **Statistical era (1990s–2012):** Probabilistic models like HMMs, n-grams, Naive Bayes.
3. **Neural era (2013–2017):** RNNs, LSTMs, Word2Vec embeddings.
4. **Transformer / LLM era (2017–Present):** Self-attention, GPT, BERT, T5, LLaMA families.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Challenge: Why Natural Language is "Hard"

Natural Language exhibits four properties that make it computationally intractable for naive rule-based systems. These are central to LLM design philosophy.

1. **Ambiguity:** A single string can map to multiple meanings.
   - *Lexical:* "Bank" (river bank vs. financial bank).
   - *Syntactic:* "I saw the man with the telescope" (who has the telescope?).
   - *Semantic:* "The chicken is ready to eat" (who is eating?).
   - *Pragmatic:* "Can you pass the salt?" (request, not yes/no question).
2. **Variability:** Infinite ways to express the same meaning (paraphrase).
3. **Context-Dependence:** Meaning shifts based on prior sentences, speaker intent, world knowledge.
4. **Discreteness with Continuous Meaning:** Words are discrete symbols, but meanings form continuous semantic spaces — this is exactly why **embedding** is the cornerstone of LLMs.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ambiguity Axiom (KTU High-Yield):** For any natural language sentence $S$, the number of valid interpretations $I(S) \geq 1$, and for most non-trivial sentences $I(S) \gg 1$. An LLM's job is to compute $P(\text{meaning} \mid S)$ and pick the most probable one.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations: From Language to Vectors

LLMs transform the discrete symbolic world of natural language into continuous vector spaces. The three foundational mappings are:

### Step 1: Tokenization Mapping
A string of characters is split into a sequence of discrete tokens (the model's vocabulary).

### Step 2: Token-to-Index Mapping
Each token is mapped to a unique integer ID from the vocabulary.

### Step 3: Index-to-Embedding Mapping
Each integer ID is mapped to a dense real-valued vector of dimension $d$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Vocabulary Size | $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | $\mathcal{V}$ = token set (unitless) | Determines the softmax output dimension |
| 2 | One-Hot Encoding | $e_i \in \{0,1\}^{V},\ e_{i}[j] = \delta_{ij}$ | $i$ = token index, $V$ = vocab size | Legacy baseline (sparse, $V$-dim) |
| 3 | Token Embedding | $x_i = E \cdot e_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$, $d$ = embedding dim | Core LLM input layer |
| 4 | Context Window | $L_{max} = N_{tokens}$ | $N$ = max tokens (e.g., 8K, 128K) | Limits discourse-level reasoning |
| 5 | Self-Attention Score | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ = key dim | Captures syntactic + semantic relations |
| 6 | Shannon Entropy (NL) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{V} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | bits per token | Measures linguistic information density |
| 7 | Perplexity (PPL) | $\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log p(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | Lower is better | Standard LLM evaluation metric |
| 8 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u\Vert_2 \ \Vert v\Vert_2}$ | $u,v \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Measures semantic closeness |
| 9 | Language Model Objective | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ | $\theta$ = model params | Cross-entropy training loss |
| 10 | BPE Merge Rule | $\text{Merge}(a,b) = \arg\max_{(a,b)} \text{freq}(a,b)$ | $a,b$ = byte pair | Subword tokenization algorithm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** Always remember that $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ denotes L2-norm (Euclidean). For column vectors, write $u \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ explicitly. Never use bare $|$ for absolute value inside formula tables — it breaks the markdown parser.

## 2.4 The Tokenization Pipeline (How NL Enters an LLM)

A natural language string is converted into a sequence of integer IDs through tokenization. The most common algorithm in modern LLMs is **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**:

1. Initialize vocabulary with all individual characters (256 bytes for UTF-8).
2. Iteratively find the **most frequent adjacent pair** of tokens in the corpus.
3. Merge that pair into a single new token; add it to the vocabulary.
4. Repeat for a fixed number of merges (typically 30,000 – 200,000).
5. Final vocabulary contains whole words, subwords, and characters.

**Real-world utility:** BPE enables LLMs to handle:
- **Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) words** by decomposing them into subword units.
- **Morphologically rich languages** (e.g., Malayalam, German, Turkish) efficiently.
- **Multilingual transfer** with a shared byte-level base.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Conditional Probability to the Autoregressive LLM

**Goal:** Derive the fundamental autoregressive objective of every decoder-only LLM (GPT family).

### Starting Point — The Joint Probability of a Sentence

A natural language sentence of $N$ tokens is $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$, where each $x_i \in \mathcal{V}$. Using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$
P(X) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)
$$

$$
P(X) = P(x_1) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2) \cdots P(x_N \mid x_1, \dots, x_{N-1})
$$

$$
P(X) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

where $x_{<i} = (x_1, \dots, x_{i-1})$ denotes the **context** (all prior tokens).

### Step 1 — Convert Product to Sum (Log-Likelihood)

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides (log is monotonic, preserves ordering):

$$
\log P(X) = \log \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid x_{<i}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

### Step 2 — Build the Training Objective

The LLM, parameterized by $\theta$, approximates $P(x_i \mid x_{<i}) \approx P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$. The **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)** training objective is to minimize the **negative log-likelihood** (which equals the **cross-entropy loss**):

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\log P_\theta(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

For a dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of $M$ sentences, the empirical risk becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}\sum_{X \in \mathcal{D}} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert X \vert} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

This is the **exact** training objective used by every autoregressive LLM (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Gemma).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A common 14-mark question is *"Derive the cross-entropy loss for an autoregressive language model."* Memorize the chain-rule step, the log-transform, and the final summation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Embedding Lookup as Matrix Multiplication

**Goal:** Show formally why a token embedding layer is a single matrix multiplication.

### Step 1 — One-Hot Vector Construction

Let $e_{x_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$ be the one-hot vector for token $x_i$:

$$
e_{x_i}[j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j = \text{index}(x_i) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Embedding Matrix Definition

Let the embedding matrix be $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$ with rows $E[j] \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ being the learned vector for the $j$-th vocabulary entry.

### Step 3 — Lookup Operation

The dense embedding $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is obtained as:

$$
x_i = E \cdot e_{x_i}
$$

### Step 4 — Resultant Vector

Since $e_{x_i}$ is 1-hot, this matrix-vector product simply **selects** the corresponding row:

$$
x_i = E[\text{index}(x_i)] \in \mathbb{R}^{d}
$$

Therefore, the input to the LLM's first transformer block is the matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^{\top} \\ x_2^{\top} \\ \vdots \\ x_N^{\top} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping NL Phenomena to LLM Components

| Linguistic Phenomenon | Mathematical Modeling Strategy | LLM Architectural Component | KTU Exam Weightage |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| Lexical Ambiguity | Contextual vector averaging | Self-Attention | High |
| Syntactic Structure | Dependency parsing supervision | Multi-Head Attention | High |
| Long-Range Coreference | Identity tracking across positions | Positional Encodings + Attention | Medium |
| Polysemy | Different vectors per context | Contextual Embeddings (ELMo, BERT) | High |
| Discourse Coherence | Sentence-level pooling | [CLS] token, Long-context models | Medium |
| Sarcasm / Pragmatics | Subtle sentiment shift | Fine-tuning on labeled data | Low–Medium |
| Out-of-Vocabulary | Subword decomposition | BPE / WordPiece / Unigram | Medium |
| Low-Resource Language | Cross-lingual transfer | Multilingual pretraining (mBERT, XLM-R) | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six Linguistic Levels as a Processing Pipeline

This Mermaid block renders the hierarchical processing stages that an LLM effectively performs (even though, internally, all levels are handled jointly by the transformer).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Natural Language Text] --> A1[Phonology: Sound to Phoneme]
    A1 --> A2[Morphology: Tokenization via BPE]
    A2 --> A3[Syntax: Self Attention Builds Parse Tree]
    A3 --> A4[Semantics: Embedding Space Lookup]
    A4 --> A5[Pragmatics: Context Window and Prompt]
    A5 --> A6[Discourse: Long Context Reasoning]
    A6 --> A7[Output: Next Token Probability Distribution]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LLM

This diagram maps the journey of natural language from raw text to a generated token, showing all functional blocks inside a decoder-only transformer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Raw Text Corpus] --> B1[Tokenizer Module]
    B1 --> B2[Token ID Sequence]
    B2 --> B3[Embedding Lookup Table]
    B3 --> B4[Positional Encoding Layer]
    B4 --> B5[Transformer Block Stack]
    B5 --> B6[Layer Normalization]
    B6 --> B7[Language Model Head Softmax]
    B7 --> B8[Probability over Vocabulary]
    B8 --> B9[Token Sampler Top P Top K]
    B9 --> B10[Generated Token ID]
    B10 --> B11[Detokenizer]
    B11 --> B12[Output Natural Language String]
```

## 4.3 Evolution of NLP Paradigms (Block Diagram)

This flowchart captures the four historical eras of Natural Language Processing, terminating at the LLM era that defines this course.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Era 1 Rule Based Systems 1950 to 1990] --> C2[Era 2 Statistical Models 1990 to 2012]
    C2 --> C3[Era 3 Neural Networks 2013 to 2017]
    C3 --> C4[Era 4 Transformer LLMs 2017 to Present]
    C1 -.Handcrafted.-> C2
    C2 -.Probabilistic.-> C3
    C3 -.Distributed.-> C4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (A0, B0, C1) to comply with the Mermaid parser. No reserved keywords (e.g., `end`, `graph`) are used as standalone node IDs. Labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define Natural Language Processing. List any four major challenges in processing natural language.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Linguistics that develops computational models and algorithms to enable machines to understand, interpret, and generate human (natural) language text and speech.

**Four Major Challenges:**

1. **Ambiguity** — Words and sentences have multiple valid interpretations at lexical, syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic levels.
2. **Context Dependency** — Meaning depends on surrounding sentences, speaker intent, and world knowledge.
3. **Variability** — The same meaning can be expressed in infinitely many ways (paraphrase, dialect, register).
4. **Data Sparsity & OOV** — Rare words, low-resource languages, and domain-specific jargon create out-of-vocabulary problems.

*(Any four well-explained challenges earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "tokenization" with reference to Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE).**

**Model Answer:**

**Tokenization** is the process of splitting raw natural language text into a sequence of discrete units called *tokens* (which may be words, subwords, or characters) drawn from a fixed vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$.

**Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) Algorithm:**

1. Initialize vocabulary with all 256 UTF-8 bytes.
2. Count the frequencies of all adjacent token pairs in the corpus.
3. Merge the most frequent pair $(a, b)$ into a new token $ab$.
4. Add $ab$ to the vocabulary.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 for a predefined number of merges (typically 30K–200K).

**Significance:** BPE balances vocabulary size vs. sequence length, allowing LLMs to handle OOV words by decomposing them into known subword units (e.g., "unhappiness" → "un" + "happiness").

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the six levels of linguistic analysis of Natural Language. How do these levels manifest inside a Large Language Model?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the cross-entropy training objective for an autoregressive language model starting from the chain rule of probability. Clearly state the meaning of each term in the final expression.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**The Six Levels of Linguistic Analysis:**

1. **Phonology (1 mark):** Studies the sound system — phonemes, intonation, stress. *LLM manifestation:* Acoustic front-ends (Whisper) or ignored in pure text models.

2. **Morphology (1 mark):** The internal structure of words — roots, prefixes, suffixes, inflections. *LLM manifestation:* Subword tokenization (BPE) explicitly decomposes morphologically complex words.

3. **Syntax (1 mark):** The grammatical arrangement of words in a sentence. *LLM manifestation:* Self-attention layers learn dependency relations and parse-tree-like structures implicitly.

4. **Semantics (1 mark):** The literal meaning of words and sentences. *LLM manifestation:* Dense vector embeddings in a continuous semantic space where $\cos(\theta)$ measures meaning similarity.

5. **Pragmatics (1 mark):** Meaning in context — speaker intent, sarcasm, implicature. *LLM manifestation:* Few-shot prompting, RLHF, and instruction tuning teach pragmatic alignment.

6. **Discourse (1 mark):** Coherence across multiple sentences. *LLM manifestation:* Extended context windows (e.g., 128K tokens) and positional encodings preserve long-range dependencies.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A clean six-tier pyramid from Phonology (bottom) to Discourse (top) with arrows indicating hierarchical dependence.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern LLMs do not explicitly compartmentalize these levels; instead, the transformer architecture learns all of them jointly through self-supervised pretraining on massive text corpora.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Sentence as a Sequence (1 mark):**

Let a natural language sentence be represented as a sequence of $N$ tokens $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$ where each $x_i \in \mathcal{V}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Chain Rule of Probability (2 marks):**

$$
P(X) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

where $x_{<i}$ denotes all tokens before position $i$.

**Step 3 — Parameterize the Model (1 mark):**

Introduce a neural network with parameters $\theta$ (the LLM) that approximates the conditional distribution:

$$
P(x_i \mid x_{<i}) \approx P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Likelihood Objective (2 marks):**

To make the model assign high probability to observed training data, maximize the log-likelihood. Equivalent to minimizing the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy loss):

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\log P_\theta(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

**Step 5 — Empirical Loss over a Corpus (1 mark):**

For a dataset $\mathcal{D}$ containing $M$ sentences:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{emp}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{M} \sum_{X \in \mathcal{D}} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert X \vert} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})
$$

**Term Explanations:** $x_i$ = current token, $x_{<i}$ = context, $P_\theta(\cdot)$ = model's predicted distribution, $\theta$ = trainable parameters, $\mathcal{L}$ = scalar loss to minimize via backpropagation.

---

### Question B-B (Internal Choice Alternative). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the four eras of NLP evolution? Explain each era in 2–3 lines with a representative model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the BPE tokenization algorithm step-by-step with a small worked example on the corpus: `{"low low low low lower lower newest newest newest newest newest newest widest widest widest"}`.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B-B(a) — 7 Marks**

| # | Era | Time | Approach | Representative Model (1 mark each) |
|---|-----|------|----------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | Rule-Based | 1950–1990 | Handcrafted linguistic rules and regex patterns | ELIZA, SHRDLU |
| 2 | Statistical | 1990–2012 | Probabilistic models over n-gram counts | HMM POS tagger, Naive Bayes spam filter |
| 3 | Neural | 2013–2017 | Distributed representations + RNNs/LSTMs | Word2Vec, ELMo, Seq2Seq |
| 4 | Transformer / LLM | 2017–Present | Self-attention, massive pretraining | BERT, GPT-3/4, LLaMA, Mistral |

**Synthesis (3 marks):** The paradigm shift at each era is driven by an exponential increase in (1) data availability, (2) compute (GPU/TPU), and (3) parameter count. Each generation subsumes the previous — LLMs internally use embeddings (neural era) trained with token statistics (statistical era) on rule-validated corpora (rule era).

---

### **Model Solution to Question B-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Initialize with characters (1 mark):**
Vocabulary $\mathcal{V}_0 = \{\text{l, o, w, e, r, n, s, t, i, d, \_}\}$ (11 symbols; `_` represents space).

**Step 2 — Count adjacent pair frequencies (1 mark):**
Top pair: `(l, o)` appears 7 times → most frequent.

**Step 3 — First merge (1 mark):**
Merge `(l, o) → lo`. New $\mathcal{V}_1$ has 12 tokens. Corpus becomes:
`{lo lo lo lo lower lower newest newest newest newest newest newest widest widest widest}`

**Step 4 — Repeat: count pairs again (1 mark):**
Next most frequent pair: `(lo, w)` appears 7 times → merge.

**Step 5 — Second merge (1 mark):**
Merge `(lo, w) → low`. New $\mathcal{V}_2$ has 13 tokens. Corpus: `{low low low low lower lower newest...}`.

**Step 6 — Continue merges (1 mark):**
Subsequent high-frequency merges would be: `(low, e) → lowe`, `(ne, w) → new`, `(new, est) → newest`, `(wid, est) → widest`, etc.

**Step 7 — Final form (1 mark):**
After $K$ merges, the BPE vocabulary contains whole words, common subwords, and individual characters, enabling efficient and OOV-robust encoding of any natural language input.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the log step** in the cross-entropy derivation costs 2 marks. Always show $\log P(X) = \sum \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing entropy $H(X)$ with cross-entropy $H(P,Q)$** is a common error. Autoregressive LLMs minimize *cross-entropy between predicted and true distributions*, not Shannon entropy of data.
> 3. **Missing the `_` (space/end-of-word) marker** in BPE examples. KTU examiners *specifically* check whether students acknowledge that BPE treats word boundaries as part of the token identity.
> 4. **Conflating tokenization with embedding** — tokenization produces integer IDs; embedding is the subsequent dense vector lookup. These are two distinct stages.
> 5. **Writing $|x|$ inside markdown tables** breaks the rendering. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lVert x \rVert$ consistently.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Natural Language (NL)** = any human-developed language (spoken or written), the input/output substrate of LLMs.
- **Six linguistic levels:** Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse. LLMs model all jointly.
- **NLP** is the discipline; **LLMs** are the current state-of-the-art realization of NLP.
- **Four key challenges of NL:** Ambiguity, Variability, Context-dependence, Discrete-symbol-vs-continuous-meaning gap.
- **Tokenization** converts text → integer IDs; **Embedding** converts IDs → dense vectors of dimension $d$.
- **BPE** is the dominant subword tokenization algorithm: iteratively merge most-frequent adjacent pairs.
- **Autoregressive LLM objective** is the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy):
  $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$.
- **Evaluation metric:** Perplexity $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ — lower is better.
- **Semantic similarity** between word vectors uses **cosine similarity**, not Euclidean distance.
- **Four eras of NLP:** Rule-based → Statistical → Neural → Transformer/LLM.
- **Vocabulary size $V$** determines the softmax output dimension; modern LLMs use $V \in [32\text{K}, 200\text{K}]$.
- **Context window $L_{max}$** is the maximum number of tokens the LLM can attend to in a single forward pass.
- **KTU exam mantra:** Always derive the chain-rule objective from first principles; always state units/dimensions of every formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NLP and Language Models Evolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** NLP and Language Models Evolution # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Natural Language Processing (NLP) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > *Natural Language Processing (NLP)* is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computational Linguistics concerned with the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. It involves developing algorithms, models, and systems that can **understand, interpret, generate, and manipulate** human language in a valuable, contextually-aware manner. **Core Sub-Tasks of NLP:** - **NLU (Natural Language Understanding):** Parsing, Semantic Analysis, Sentiment Classification, Named Entity Recognition (NER). - **NLG (Natural Language Generation):** Text Summarization, Machine Translation, Dialogue Generation. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of NLP as teaching a very literal-minded **foreign diplomat** to understand a local language. The diplomat does not inherently "feel" sarcasm, idioms, or context. You must build him a multi-layered translation handbook: 1. A **dictionary** (lexicon / vocabulary), 2. A **grammar rulebook** (syntax / parse trees), 3. A **cultural guide** (semantics / world knowledge), 4. A **conversation etiquette guide** (pragmatics / discourse). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** NLP sits at the intersection of **Linguistics**, **Computer Science**, and **Statistics**. Modern NLP is almost entirely **data-driven** (statistical/neural), having moved away from the hand-crafted rule-based systems of the 1960s-1990s. --- ## 1.2 Language Model (LM) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A *Language Model* is a probabilistic model that assigns a probability distribution over sequences of tokens (words, sub-words, or characters) drawn from a vocabulary $V$. Formally, it models the joint probability $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ of a word sequence, or the conditional probability of the next token given prior context: $P(w_t \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{t-1})$. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a highly-trained **autocomplete engine** on your smartphone. It has read billions of sentences, and when you type "The weather is...", it suggests "sunny", "cold", or "nice" with varying probabilities. A Language Model is the **mathematical brain** behind that autocomplete — it quantifies *how likely* a given sequence of words is in natural human communication. > [!NOTE] > **Why Language Models Matter:** > They are the foundational building block for virtually every modern LLM (BERT, GPT, LLaMA, etc.). Any task requiring *predicting, scoring, or generating* text reduces to a language modeling problem in the modern paradigm. --- ## 1.3 The Big Picture: Why Evolution Was Necessary The evolution from rule-based systems to modern LLMs was driven by three persistent pain points: | Pain Point | Description | |---|---| | **Data Sparsity** | Real language follows a Zipfian distribution — most word combinations are rare or unseen. | | **Long-Range Dependencies** | Meaning often depends on words far apart (e.g., pronoun resolution across paragraphs). | | **Contextual Polysemy** | A single word ("bank") has different meanings in different contexts — static embeddings failed here. | Each generation of models (rule-based → n-gram → neural → transformer → LLM) was engineered specifically to attack one or more of these pain points. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generational Taxonomy of Language Models ### Generation 1: Rule-Based / Symbolic NLP (1950s – 1990s) - **Approach:** Hand-crafted linguistic rules (CFGs, regex, ontologies like WordNet). - **Strengths:** Interpretable, linguistically rigorous. - **Weaknesses:** Brittle, cannot generalize, extremely labor-intensive to scale. - **Examples:** ELIZA (1966), SHRDLU, early Machine Translation (SYSTRAN). ### Generation 2: Statistical / Probabilistic LMs (1990s – 2010s) - **Approach:** Probabilistic models, most notably **N-Gram Models**. - **Key Insight:** Replace deep linguistic understanding with *counting co-occurrences* in large corpora. - **Examples:** HMM-based taggers, IBM Alignment Models, SRILM toolkit. ### Generation 3: Neural / Distributed Representation LMs (2013 – 2017) - **Approach:** Replace discrete counts with **dense vector embeddings** learned via neural networks. - **Milestone Architectures:** **Word2Vec (2013)**, **GloVe (2014)**, **LSTM-LM (Mikolov, 2010)**, **Seq2Seq (2014)**. - **Weakness:** Context-free embeddings — "bank" has the same vector whether it means river-bank or financial-bank. ### Generation 4: Contextual / Transformer LMs (2017 – 2020) - **Approach:** The **Attention Mechanism** + **Transformer Architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017)**. - **Milestone Architectures:** **ELMo (2018)**, **BERT (2018)**, **GPT-2 (2019)**, **T5 (2019)**. ### Generation 5: Large Language Models / Foundation Models (2020 – Present) - **Approach:** **Scale** — same transformer architecture, but trained on hundreds of billions of tokens with hundreds of billions of parameters. - **Milestone Architectures:** **GPT-3 (2020)**, **PaLM (2022)**, **LLaMA (2023)**, **GPT-4 (2023)**, **Claude 3**, **Gemini**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Paradigm Shift — From Task-Specific to General-Purpose:** > Pre-2017: A separate model was trained for each NLP task (NER, sentiment, QA). > Post-2018: One **pre-trained** LLM is fine-tuned (or prompted) to perform dozens of tasks — the **"Pre-train + Fine-tune"** paradigm. Post-2020: **In-Context Learning (ICL)** — the same model performs new tasks from *prompts alone*, with no weight updates. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations form the mathematical backbone of the module. Memorize the variables and the *intuition* behind each. | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Glossary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Chain Rule of Probability** | $P(w_1^n) = \prod_{t=1}^{n} P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1})$ | $w_1^{t-1}$ = words 1 to t−1 | | 2 | **Markov Assumption (Order-n)** | $P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}^{t-1})$ | Approximation: history truncated to last n−1 tokens | | 3 | **Unigram Probability** | $P(w_i) = \dfrac{C(w_i)}{\sum_{j} C(w_j)} = \dfrac{C(w_i)}{N}$ | $C(\cdot)$ = count in corpus; $N$ = total tokens | | 4 | **Bigram Probability (MLE)** | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | Maximum Likelihood Estimate | | 5 | **Perplexity (PPL)** | $PPL(W) = \left( P(w_1 w_2 \dots w_N) \right)^{-1/N} = 2^{H(W)}$ | Lower is better. $H$ = cross-entropy in bits. | | 6 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $H(P, Q) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log_2 Q(x)$ | $P$ = true distribution, $Q$ = model distribution | | 7 | **Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing** | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert}$ | $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size | | 8 | **Self-Attention (Scaled Dot-Product)** | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left( \dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V$ | $d_k$ = key dimension; scaling prevents softmax saturation | | 9 | **Cosine Similarity (Embeddings)** | $\text{sim}(u, v) = \dfrac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert \, \Vert v \Vert}$ | Used to measure semantic closeness in vector space | | 10 | **Shannon Visualization (1948)** | $\dfrac{\log_2 \vert V \vert}{H(W)} \times 100\%$ | "Entropy ratio" — gauge of LM predictability | > [!NOTE] > **Note on notation:** $\vert V \vert$ is written with absolute-value-style vertical bars above, but per protocol we use the single-character notation $\vert V \vert$ only in formula context. In the markdown tables we have used $\vert V \vert$ to avoid breaking table pipes. --- ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Evolution — Engineering Utility | Engineering Need | Evolution Step That Solved It | |---|---| | Hand-crafting rules is impossible at scale | Statistical n-gram models (data-driven) | | N-grams cannot handle unseen n-tuples | Smoothing + neural embeddings (generalization) | | Word meaning depends on context | Contextualized embeddings (ELMo, BERT) | | Sequential RNNs are slow and forgetful | Transformer with self-attention (parallel + long-range) | | One model = one task is inefficient | Foundation models + in-context learning | | Model is too big for downstream deployment | Distillation, LoRA, Quantization, PEFT | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > *Mathematical Treatment of Core Concepts* --- ## 3.1 Derivation: From Joint Probability to the N-Gram Model **Step 1 — The Fundamental LM Equation (Chain Rule of Probability).** For any sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)$, the joint probability factorizes via the chain rule: $$ P(w_1^N) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_N \mid w_1^{N-1}) $$ Written compactly using the product symbol: $$ P(w_1^N) = \prod_{t=1}^{N} P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1}) $$ > **Reasoning:** Any multivariate joint distribution can be decomposed into a product of conditional distributions, but the historical context $w_1^{t-1}$ grows unboundedly with $t$, making direct estimation impossible. **Step 2 — Apply the Order-N Markov Assumption.** Assume the probability of the next word depends only on the previous $n-1$ words. For a *bigram* model ($n=2$): $$ P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-1}) $$ Substituting back: $$ P(w_1^N) \approx \prod_{t=1}^{N} P(w_t \mid w_{t-1}) $$ > **Reasoning:** This is a lossy but tractable approximation. It dramatically reduces the number of parameters to estimate. **Step 3 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for Bigrams.** Count occurrences in a training corpus of size $N$ tokens: $$ P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})} $$ > **Reasoning:** MLE is the parameter value that maximizes the likelihood of observing the training data. It is the *natural* estimator derived from relative frequencies. **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.** Let the corpus be: *"the cat sat on the mat the cat sat"*. - Total bigrams $N-1 = 7$. - $C(\text{the}) = 3$, $C(\text{cat}) = 2$, $C(\text{sat}) = 2$. - $C(\text{the, cat}) = 2$, $C(\text{cat, sat}) = 2$, $C(\text{sat, on}) = 1$, $C(\text{on, the}) = 1$, $C(\text{the, mat}) = 1$, $C(\text{mat, the}) = 1$. Therefore: $$ P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$ $$ P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 $$ $$ P(\text{mat} \mid \text{the}) = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 $$ **Step 5 — Joint Probability of a Test Sentence.** Compute $P(\text{the cat sat on the mat})$: $$ P = P(\text{the}) \cdot P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) \cdot P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) \cdot P(\text{on} \mid \text{sat}) \cdot P(\text{the} \mid \text{on}) \cdot P(\text{mat} \mid \text{the}) $$ Plugging in (with $P(\text{the}) = 3/8 = 0.375$): $$ P = 0.375 \times 0.667 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.333 $$ $$ P \approx 0.0417 $$ > **Reasoning:** Notice how the joint probability quickly vanishes into tiny numbers. This is why we use **log-probabilities** in practice. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Perplexity as a Quality Metric **Step 1 — Define Cross-Entropy.** The average per-word cross-entropy of a model $Q$ on a test set $W$ of length $N$ is: $$ H(W) = -\dfrac{1}{N} \log_2 P(w_1^N) = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1}) $$ **Step 2 — Exponentiate to Get Perplexity.** $$ PPL(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \left( P(w_1^N) \right)^{-1/N} $$ **Step 3 — Intuition.** $PPL$ is the **effective branching factor** — the average number of choices the model is "confused between" at each step. - A $PPL$ of 10 means the model is as confused as choosing uniformly between 10 words. - Lower $PPL$ ⇒ model is more confident and accurate. **Step 4 — Numerical Example.** Suppose $P(\text{sentence}) = 0.0417$ and the sentence has 6 words: $$ PPL = (0.0417)^{-1/6} = 2^{1.1} \approx 2.14 $$ A $PPL$ of 2.14 indicates the model is reasonably confident (closer to 1 = perfect, closer to $|V|$ = random). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Self-Attention as the Modern Backbone **Step 1 — The Computational Problem.** Given an input sequence of $n$ token embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, we need a way for each token to "look at" every other token and weight the importance of each. **Step 2 — Project into Three Spaces.** $$ Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V $$ where $W_Q, W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ and $W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$ are learned projection matrices. **Step 3 — Compute Pairwise Compatibility.** $$ \text{scores} = \dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} $$ The division by $\sqrt{d_k}$ counteracts the growth of dot-product magnitude in high dimensions, which would push the softmax into saturation. **Step 4 — Softmax Normalization → Attention Weights.** $$ A = \text{softmax}\!\left( \dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) $$ **Step 5 — Weighted Sum of Values.** $$ \text{Output} = A \cdot V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v} $$ > **Reasoning:** This is **O(n²)** in sequence length, but unlike RNNs, every computation is fully parallelizable on a GPU. This is the key reason transformers replaced RNNs at scale. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis | Dimension | Rule-Based | N-Gram | Neural (Word2Vec) | Contextual (BERT) | LLM (GPT-4 class) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Representation** | Symbolic rules | Sparse counts | Static dense vector | Contextual vector | Contextual vector | | **Context Window** | N/A | Fixed (n−1) | None (1 word) | Full bidirectional | Full (long) | | **Trainable Params** | 0 (hand-coded) | $\vert V \vert^n$ | $\sim 10^8$ | $\sim 3 \times 10^8$ (BERT-base) | $\sim 10^{11}$ to $10^{12}$ | | **Handles Polysemy** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | **Handles Long Range** | ❌ | ❌ (n-gram limit) | ❌ | ✅ (full ctx) | ✅ (window of 8K–1M tokens) | | **Parallelizable** | N/A | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (transformer) | ✅ | | **Emergent Abilities** | None | None | None | Few | Many (ICL, CoT, reasoning) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Evolution Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Era 1: Rule Based
1950s to 1990s
ELIZA SHRDLU WordNet"] B["Era 2: Statistical N Gram
1990s to 2010
HMM IBM Models SRILM"] C["Era 3: Neural Static
2013 to 2017
Word2Vec GloVe LSTM LM"] D["Era 4: Transformer Era
2017 to 2020
Attention BERT GPT 2 T5"] E["Era 5: Large Language Models
2020 to Present
GPT 3 GPT 4 LLaMA Gemini"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E A -- "Data Sparsity Pain Point" --> B B -- "Polysemy Pain Point" --> C C -- "Long Range Pain Point" --> D D -- "Scale and Emergence" --> E style A fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#EAD1DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Modern LLM ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"] tok1["Token IDs"] emb1["Token Embedding Matrix"] pos1["Positional Encoding"] end subgraph transformerBlock["TRANSFORMER BLOCK x L"] attn1["Multi Head Self Attention
Attention = softmax QK over sqrt dk times V"] norm1["Layer Norm + Residual"] ffn1["Feed Forward Network
FFN x = W2 ReLU W1 x plus b"] norm2["Layer Norm + Residual"] end subgraph outputHead["OUTPUT HEAD"] lmHead["Linear Projection to Vocab Size"] softmax1["Softmax over V"] end tok1 --> emb1 emb1 --> pos1 pos1 --> attn1 attn1 --> norm1 norm1 --> ffn1 ffn1 --> norm2 norm2 --> lmHead lmHead --> softmax1 softmax1 --> output1["Next Token Probability Distribution"] style attn1 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ffn1 fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style softmax1 fill:#EAD1DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Pre-Training vs Fine-Tuning vs In-Context Learning ```mermaid flowchart TD pt["PHASE 1: PRE TRAINING
Massive unlabeled corpus
Objective: Next token prediction
Cost: Millions of dollars"] ft["PHASE 2: FINE TUNING
Small labeled dataset
Objective: Task specific loss
Cost: Hundreds of dollars"] icl["PHASE 3: IN CONTEXT LEARNING
Few examples in prompt
Objective: No weight updates
Cost: Zero training cost"] pt --> ft pt --> icl style pt fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ft fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style icl fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Define a Language Model. Differentiate between a statistical n-gram language model and a neural language model.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *Language Model* is a probabilistic system that assigns a probability to sequences of words/tokens from a vocabulary $V$, typically by modeling $P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1})$. **Differences:** | Aspect | Statistical N-Gram LM | Neural LM | |---|---|---| | **Representation** | Discrete counts of n-tuples | Dense, learned embeddings | | **Generalization** | Poor (zero counts for unseen n-grams) | Excellent (distributed similarity) | | **Parameters** | $\vert V \vert^n$ entries (very sparse) | Fixed weight matrices, dense | | **Context** | Fixed window (n−1) | Can model longer dependencies (RNN/Tx) | | **Smoothing** | Required (Laplace, KN) | Implicit via continuous embeddings | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Briefly explain the concept of "self-attention" in the Transformer architecture. Why was it a paradigm shift over RNNs?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Self-attention* is a mechanism where each token in a sequence computes a weighted sum of *all* other tokens in the same sequence, with weights determined by learned Query, Key, and Value projections: $$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left( \dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V $$ **Why it was a paradigm shift:** 1. **Parallelism:** Unlike RNNs, all positions are processed simultaneously — drastically faster training on GPUs. 2. **Long-range dependencies:** Direct $O(1)$ path between any two positions (vs. $O(n)$ in RNNs). 3. **No vanishing gradient:** No sequential bottleneck; the loss signal flows unimpeded. 4. **Scalability:** Enabled the "scaling laws" that produced GPT-3 and beyond. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks): NLP Foundations & N-Gram Models** > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]** #### **(a) Derive the chain-rule formulation of a language model and explain how the Markov assumption leads to an n-gram model. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Joint Probability of a Sequence (Chain Rule).** For any sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)$, by the chain rule of probability: $$ P(w_1^N) = \prod_{t=1}^{N} P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1}) $$ This is an *exact* identity — no assumptions made. However, the conditional history $w_1^{t-1}$ grows with $t$, making direct estimation from data impossible for $N > $ few tokens. **Step 2 — The Markov Assumption.** We assert that the future is conditionally independent of the distant past, given a recent context of length $n-1$: $$ P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}^{t-1}) $$ For $n=2$ (bigram), this becomes $P(w_t \mid w_{t-1})$; for $n=3$ (trigram), $P(w_t \mid w_{t-2}, w_{t-1})$. **Step 3 — N-Gram Model Substituted In.** $$ P(w_1^N) \approx \prod_{t=1}^{N} P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}^{t-1}) $$ **Step 4 — MLE Estimation.** $$ P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}^{t-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{t-n+1}^{t-1}, w_t)}{C(w_{t-n+1}^{t-1})} $$ **Step 5 — Worked Example.** For the corpus *"I love NLP I love ML"*, the bigram counts and probabilities are: - $C(\text{I}) = 2$, $C(\text{love}) = 2$, $C(\text{NLP}) = 1$, $C(\text{ML}) = 1$ - $C(\text{I, love}) = 2$, $C(\text{love, NLP}) = 1$, $C(\text{love, ML}) = 1$ Therefore: $$ P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = 2/2 = 1.0, \quad P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = 1/2 = 0.5 $$ **Step 6 — Sentence Probability.** $$ P(\text{I love NLP}) = P(\text{I}) \cdot P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) \cdot P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) $$ $$ = (2/6) \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.5 = 0.167 $$ #### **(b) Explain the problem of data sparsity in n-gram models. Derive the Laplace (Add-1) smoothing formula and discuss its limitations. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — The Sparsity Problem.** With a vocabulary $\vert V \vert$ of, say, 50,000 English words: - Unigrams: 50,000 possibilities - Bigrams: $2.5 \times 10^9$ possibilities - Trigrams: $1.25 \times 10^{14}$ possibilities A typical web-scale corpus of $10^9$ tokens cannot even *touch* the trigram space. Any n-gram never seen gets probability **zero**, making the entire joint probability of any test sentence containing it zero — a catastrophic failure. **Step 2 — Laplace Smoothing (Add-1).** Idea: Pretend we saw every possible n-gram at least once. $$ P_{\text{Lap}}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i) + 1}{C(w_{i-1}) + \vert V \vert} $$ **Step 3 — Worked Example.** Continuing from the bigram corpus above ($\vert V \vert = 4$ unique words): - $C(\text{I, love}) = 2$, $C(\text{I}) = 2$: $$ P_{\text{Lap}}(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = \dfrac{2 + 1}{2 + 4} = 3/6 = 0.500 $$ - For unseen pair $C(\text{NLP, ML}) = 0$, $C(\text{NLP}) = 1$: $$ P_{\text{Lap}}(\text{ML} \mid \text{NLP}) = \dfrac{0 + 1}{1 + 4} = 1/5 = 0.200 $$ > **Step 4 — Limitations of Laplace Smoothing.** > 1. **Excessive probability mass shifted to unseen events** — assigns 20% of probability to a context where we have essentially no evidence. > 2. **Smoothing constant should be smaller** — empirically, Add-$\delta$ with $\delta < 1$ (e.g., $\delta = 0.1$ or $0.01$) works better; this is *Lidstone smoothing*. > 3. **Doesn't capture higher-order structure** — fails for trigrams and above. > 4. **Replaced in practice** by **Kneser-Ney smoothing**, which conditions on *how many* different contexts a word appears in, achieving state-of-the-art performance for n-gram LMs. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Evolution to Modern LLMs** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** #### **(a) Trace the evolution of language models from rule-based systems to modern LLMs. Highlight the key architectural innovation at each stage. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Era | Period | Key Architectural Innovation | Limitation Solved | |---|---|---|---| | **1. Rule-Based** | 1950s–1990 | Hand-coded CFGs, regex, ontologies (WordNet) | First attempt at machine-readable grammar | | **2. Statistical** | 1990s–2010 | **N-gram models** + HMMs + MLE estimation | Data-driven; eliminated manual rule writing | | **3. Neural Static** | 2013–2017 | **Word2Vec** (CBOW + Skip-gram), GloVe | Dense embeddings; semantic similarity | | **3.5. Neural Seq** | 2014–2017 | **LSTM/GRU + Seq2Seq** | Captured longer dependencies than n-grams | | **4. Transformer** | 2017–2020 | **Self-Attention + Multi-Head + Positional Encoding** | Full parallelization, full context, no vanishing gradient | | **5. LLMs** | 2020–present | **Scale (params + data + compute)** + Pre-train + Prompt | Emergent in-context learning, few-shot generalization | > **Key Architectural Innovation at Each Stage:** > - **Statistical:** *Probabilistic independence assumption* (Markov). > - **Neural Static:** *Distributed representation* (Hinton, 1986; popularized by Word2Vec). > - **Transformer:** *Self-attention as a replacement for recurrence* (Vaswani et al., 2017). > - **LLM Era:** *Scale and emergent in-context learning* (Brown et al., GPT-3, 2020). #### **(b) Explain the self-attention mechanism with its mathematical formulation. Discuss how Multi-Head Attention enhances the model's representational capacity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Scaled Dot-Product Attention.** Given input embeddings $X$, project into Query $Q$, Key $K$, and Value $V$ matrices via learned linear layers: $$ Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V $$ **Step 2 — Compute Attention Scores.** $$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left( \dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V $$ The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor prevents the dot products from growing too large, which would push softmax gradients toward zero. **Step 3 — Multi-Head Attention (MHA).** Instead of one attention function, run $h$ parallel "heads", each with its own learned $W_Q^{(i)}, W_K^{(i)}, W_V^{(i)}$: $$ \text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \cdot W_O $$ where each head is: $$ \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_Q^{(i)}, K W_K^{(i)}, V W_V^{(i)}) $$ **Step 4 — Why Multi-Head Helps.** - **Different subspaces:** Each head can attend to *different* linguistic relationships simultaneously — e.g., one head captures syntactic dependencies (subject-verb), another captures coreference (pronoun-antecedent), another captures positional proximity. - **Increased capacity** without a proportional increase in compute (since $d_k$ is reduced per head, keeping total cost constant). - **Ensemble effect:** Multiple attention patterns provide a richer, more robust contextual representation. **Step 5 — Real-world Impact.** MHA is the **fundamental building block** of every modern LLM (BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA, etc.). Removing MHA collapses the model to a feed-forward network, which cannot model variable-length context — this is the *single most important* architectural innovation enabling the LLM era. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):** > > 1. **Confusing "Token" with "Word":** Modern LLMs operate on *sub-word tokens* (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece), not raw words. Writing "the model takes words as input" loses marks. > 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ in Attention:** Examiners specifically check whether you remember *why* the scaling factor is present. Just writing $QK^\top$ without the $\sqrt{d_k}$ is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Markov Assumption Mis-statement:** It is *not* "the model forgets old words." It is a *mathematical* conditional-independence assumption. Use the precise phrase. > 4. **Confusing BERT and GPT:** BERT is *bidirectional and encoder-only*; GPT is *autoregressive and decoder-only*. Examiners love this comparison. > 5. **Zero Probability Crisis:** When asked about n-gram limitations, students often mention "sparsity" but forget to compute the *catastrophic consequence*: $P(\text{any sentence with unseen bigram}) = 0$. > 6. **Skipping the Worked Numerical:** In a 7-mark derivation question, an unworked formula with no numerical demonstration typically receives only 3–4 marks. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1:** - ✅ **NLP** = subfield of AI at the intersection of *linguistics + CS + statistics*; divided into NLU and NLG. - ✅ **Language Model** = probabilistic model of token sequences; outputs $P(w_t \mid w_{t-1}, \dots, w_1)$. - ✅ **Chain Rule:** $P(w_1^N) = \prod_{t=1}^{N} P(w_t \mid w_1^{t-1})$ — *exact*, no assumptions. - ✅ **Markov Assumption:** Approximate with $P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}^{t-1})$ to get the **n-gram model**. - ✅ **MLE Bigram:** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) / C(w_{i-1})$. - ✅ **Sparsity:** Vocabulary grows exponentially with $n$; unseen n-grams get $P = 0$. - ✅ **Laplace Smoothing:** Add 1 to numerator, $\vert V \vert$ to denominator. - ✅ **Perplexity:** $PPL = 2^{H(W)}$ — **lower is better**; interpret as effective branching factor. - ✅ **Five Generations:** Rule → Statistical → Neural Static → Transformer → LLM. - ✅ **Self-Attention Formula:** $\text{softmax}(Q K^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ — fully parallelizable, $O(1)$ path between any two positions. - ✅ **Multi-Head Attention:** $h$ parallel attention heads, concatenated — different linguistic relations per head. - ✅ **Pre-training + Fine-tuning + ICL:** The three-phase paradigm defining the modern LLM era. - ✅ **Key Architectural Milestones (Year-Model):** 2013-Word2Vec, 2014-Seq2Seq, 2017-Transformer, 2018-BERT, 2018-GPT, 2020-GPT-3. - ✅ **Two Killer Apps of Self-Attention:** (1) Parallelism (10–100× training speedup vs. RNN); (2) Direct long-range connections (no vanishing gradient over distance). - ✅ **Emergent Abilities of LLMs:** In-Context Learning, Chain-of-Thought Reasoning, Code Generation — all absent in pre-2020 models.

topics.title: The Era of Large Language Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Era of Large Language Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a deep neural network, predominantly built upon the **Transformer architecture**, that is trained on massive corpora of textual data (often hundreds of billions of tokens) using self-supervised learning objectives. The defining property of an LLM is its ability to perform a wide spectrum of natural language understanding and generation tasks via **in-context learning**, **instruction following**, and **zero/few-shot generalization**, without requiring task-specific gradient updates (i.e., no fine-tuning of parameters is needed at inference time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECMT862):** A Large Language Model is a *foundation model* trained on broad text data at scale, exhibiting *emergent abilities* (reasoning, in-context learning, chain-of-thought) that are not present in smaller models and arise unpredictably as parameters, data, and compute scale beyond critical thresholds.

The "Era of LLMs" refers to the historical window (post-2017) beginning with the publication of **"Attention Is All You Need"** (Vaswani et al., Google Brain, 2017), which introduced the Transformer, and culminating in the present generation of trillion-parameter models (GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, LLaMA, Mistral, etc.).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Universal Autocomplete" on Steroids

Imagine a child who has read **every book in every library in the world** — novels, encyclopedias, scientific journals, code repositories, legal documents, and social media. This child does not "understand" in a philosophical sense, but has built such a deep statistical map of how words, concepts, and reasoning chains follow one another that they can:

- Complete your unfinished sentence (next-token prediction)
- Translate between languages they have never formally studied
- Explain quantum mechanics in the style of a pirate
- Write functional code from a single English description
- Debate ethics, summarize legal contracts, and solve Olympiad-level mathematics

That is, in essence, what an LLM is — a **probabilistic engine for next-token prediction** trained at internet scale. The "Large" in LLM is not just a marketing term; it is a precise engineering observation: **scale unlocks qualitatively new capabilities** that smaller models fundamentally cannot achieve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Students Must Internalize**
> - **Token**: A discrete unit of text (a word, sub-word, or punctuation) — LLMs do not see "characters" or "words" but *tokens* (e.g., GPT uses BPE — Byte Pair Encoding).
> - **Parameter (Weight)**: A learnable number in the neural network. Modern LLMs have **7B, 13B, 70B, 405B, 1T+ parameters**.
> - **Context Window**: The maximum number of tokens the model can attend to in a single forward pass (modern models: 128K → 1M → 2M tokens).
> - **Pre-training**: The initial, compute-intensive training phase on raw text.
> - **Fine-tuning / RLHF**: Subsequent alignment phases (Supervised Fine-Tuning, Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback).

## 1.3 Why "The Era" Matters — The Paradigm Shift

Before LLMs, NLP was a **task-specific** discipline: you built one model for sentiment analysis, another for machine translation, another for named entity recognition. The LLM era replaced this with a **single general-purpose model** that can be prompted (or lightly fine-tuned) to perform any text task. This is the **"foundation model paradigm"** — a true Copernican revolution in artificial intelligence.

| Era | Dominant Paradigm | Typical Model Size | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-2013** | Statistical / N-gram | KB of parameters | Data sparsity, no semantics |
| **2013–2017** | Static Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) | Few hundred MB | Context-independent vectors |
| **2017–2019** | RNN / LSTM / Seq2Seq | Tens of millions | Sequential bottleneck, vanishing gradient |
