| **2017–Present** | **Transformer / LLM** | **Billions to trillions** | Compute, hallucination, alignment |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars That Enabled the LLM Era

The LLM revolution did not emerge from a single breakthrough. It rests on **three independent exponential curves** that reached critical mass simultaneously around 2017–2020. A KTU student must understand this as a *systems-level* phenomenon, not just an algorithmic one.

### Pillar 1: Compute (Hardware)

Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and later Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) delivered roughly a **10x increase in floating-point operations per second (FLOPs) every 4 years** — a continuation of Moore's Law extended through parallelism. Training GPT-3 (175B parameters) required an estimated **3.14 × 10²³ FLOPs** on thousands of A100 GPUs over weeks.

### Pillar 2: Data (Internet-Scale Text Corpora)

Common Crawl, Wikipedia, GitHub, arXiv, books (Books3, Project Gutenberg), and curated datasets like **The Pile** (825 GB) provided training corpora exceeding **trillions of tokens**. A widely cited internal data-quality rule states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chinchilla Scaling Hypothesis (Hoffmann et al., DeepMind, 2022):** For optimal compute-efficient training, a model should be trained on approximately **20 tokens per parameter**. Therefore, a 70B-parameter model should be trained on ~**1.4 trillion tokens**. Earlier models (GPT-3) were *undertrained* by this rule.

### Pillar 3: Algorithms (The Transformer + Self-Supervised Learning)

The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) replaced recurrence with **parallelizable self-attention**, making it feasible to train much deeper, wider networks on much longer sequences. Simultaneously, the insight that *raw text itself is a supervision signal* (predict the next token) eliminated the need for expensive human-labeled datasets.

## 2.2 The Transformer At A Glance

The Transformer is an **encoder-decoder** architecture built from stacked **self-attention** and **feed-forward** layers, with **residual connections** and **layer normalization**. For language modeling (GPT-style *decoder-only* models), only the decoder stack is used with **masked self-attention** to prevent the model from "looking ahead" at future tokens during training.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in derivations, numericals, and viva questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ is the key dimensionality; $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents softmax saturation. |
| 2 | Softmax Function | $\sigma(x_i) = \dfrac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{x_j}}$ | Converts logits into a probability distribution. |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss (Token-level) | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ | Standard pre-training objective for autoregressive LLMs. |
| 4 | Perplexity (Evaluation Metric) | $\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i)\right)$ | Lower is better; geometric mean of the inverse probability of the corpus. |
| 5 | Chinchilla Optimal Tokens | $D_{\text{opt}} \approx 20 \times N$ | $N$ = number of parameters; governs data-optimal training. |
| 6 | Transformer Forward FLOPs (approx.) | $C_{\text{forward}} \approx 2ND$ | $N$ = parameters, $D$ = training tokens; backward pass is ~2x. |
| 7 | Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Precision of n-gram overlap with reference translations; BP = brevity penalty. |
| 8 | ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence F-score) | $F_{lcs} = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2)\,R_{lcs}\,P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}}$ | Standard summarization evaluation metric. |
| 9 | Parameter Count Scaling (Chinchilla Loss) | $L(N) = E + \dfrac{A}{N^\alpha} + \dfrac{B}{D^\beta}$ | $E$ = irreducible loss; $A,B,\alpha,\beta$ are fitted constants. |
| 10 | Temperature-Scaled Softmax | $p_i = \dfrac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}$ | $T \to 0$ → greedy; $T \to \infty$ → uniform; controls generation randomness. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LLMs are not academic curiosities; they are **industrial infrastructure**. They power:

- **Search engines** (Google SGE, Bing Chat) — retrieval-augmented generation over web indexes
- **Code assistants** (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Codeium) — fine-tuned on permissively licensed code
- **Customer support automation** — intent classification + response generation
- **Scientific research** — protein structure prediction (ESM, AlphaFold), materials discovery
- **Edge / on-device inference** — quantized 1B–3B models running on phones via llama.cpp, ONNX Runtime, Core ML

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industry-Relevance Note:** The B.Tech (Hons.) PECMT862 syllabus explicitly links LLM engineering to the **"AI Capstone Project"** in Semester 8. Mastery of the architecture and training pipeline is therefore directly examinable and project-relevant.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\sqrt{d_k}$ in Scaled Dot-Product Attention

The scaling factor is not arbitrary — it is a variance-correction term. The following is a complete derivation that may appear in a KTU Part B (14-mark) question.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Assume query and key vectors $q, k \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ are drawn independently with each component having **zero mean and unit variance**:

$$
\mathbb{E}[q_i] = \mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(q_i) = \text{Var}(k_i) = 1.
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inner product.** The dot product is

$$
s = q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i.
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean of $s$.** Since the components are independent and zero-mean:

$$
\mathbb{E}[s] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i k_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0.
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance of $s$.** Using independence:

$$
\text{Var}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[q_i]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i])^2.
$$

Substituting unit variance and zero mean:

$$
\text{Var}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} (1)(1) - 0 = d_k.
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize.** To restore unit variance, we divide the dot product by $\sqrt{d_k}$. The scaled score $s' = s / \sqrt{d_k}$ now has $\text{Var}(s') = 1$ and is well-conditioned for the softmax.

**Step 6 — Consequence.** Without scaling, as $d_k$ grows (e.g., 64, 128 in modern LLMs), the dot product magnitudes explode, pushing the softmax into regions of near-zero gradient → the **vanishing-gradient problem in attention**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for students:** The $\sqrt{d_k}$ term is a *variance-stabilizing transformation* analogous to Batch Normalization. It is the single most asked derivation in Transformer-related KTU questions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Perplexity from Cross-Entropy

Perplexity is the **exponential of the average negative log-likelihood** of a sequence. The derivation follows directly from the definition of cross-entropy.

**Step 1.** Let a tokenized corpus be $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$ with model probability $p(x_i \mid x_{<i})$.

**Step 2.** The average cross-entropy (in *nats*, i.e., natural logarithm) is:

$$
H(p, X) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i}).
$$

**Step 3.** Perplexity is the *exponential* of cross-entropy, converting nats into a more interpretable "branching factor":

$$
\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\!\left(H(p, X)\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right).
$$

**Step 4 — Intuition.** $\text{PPL} = 50$ means the model is, on average, as "perplexed" as if it had to choose uniformly among 50 tokens at every step. State-of-the-art LLMs achieve $\text{PPL} \approx 3$ to $7$ on standard benchmarks.

$$
\boxed{\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Chinchilla Compute-Optimal Frontier

The DeepMind Chinchilla paper empirically fitted a parametric loss model. The derivation is conceptually straightforward.

**Step 1.** Assume the loss decomposes additively into a *parameter-scarce* term and a *data-scarce* term, plus an irreducible entropy floor $E$:

$$
L(N, D) = E + \frac{A}{N^\alpha} + \frac{B}{D^\beta}.
$$

**Step 2.** Impose the compute budget constraint. The training compute $C$ is proportional to $N \cdot D$ (parameters × tokens):

$$
C \approx 6 \, N \, D \quad \text{(factor 6 from forward + backward FLOPs).}
$$

**Step 3.** Eliminate $D$ using $D = C / (6N)$ and substitute:

$$
L(N) = E + \frac{A}{N^\alpha} + \frac{B (6N)^\beta}{C^\beta}.
$$

**Step 4.** Minimize w.r.t. $N$ by setting $dL/dN = 0$. Solving gives the optimal ratio:

$$
\boxed{\frac{D_{\text{opt}}}{N_{\text{opt}}} \approx 20 \quad \text{tokens per parameter (for the empirically fitted } \alpha \approx \beta \approx 0.34\text{).}}
$$

**Step 5 — Implication.** A 70B model should ideally train on **1.4T tokens**; a 405B model on **8.1T tokens**. This directly contradicts GPT-3's 175B / 300B (only ~1.7 tokens/param), which was *undertrained* by a factor of ~10.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Foundational Architectures

| Architecture | Year | Core Mechanism | Parallelizable? | Max Practical Context | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **N-gram / HMM** | Pre-2010 | Counting + Markov assumption | Yes | 5–10 tokens | Probabilistic foundations |
| **Word2Vec / GloVe** | 2013–2014 | Shallow skip-gram / co-occurrence | Yes | N/A (static) | Distributed semantics |
| **RNN (Elman)** | 1990 | Recurrent hidden state | No | ~50 tokens | Variable-length memory |
| **LSTM / GRU** | 1997 / 2014 | Gated recurrent cells | No | ~200 tokens | Mitigated vanishing gradient |
| **Seq2Seq + Attention** | 2014–2015 | Encoder–decoder + additive attention | Partial | ~500 tokens | Soft alignment |
| **Transformer** | 2017 | Self-attention, fully parallel | **Yes** | 512 → 2M tokens | **Quadratic-attention + positional encodings** |
| **GPT (Decoder-only)** | 2018+ | Masked self-attention | Yes | 2K → 2M | Pure autoregressive language modeling |
| **BERT (Encoder-only)** | 2018 | Bidirectional self-attention | Yes | 512 tokens | Masked language modeling |
| **Mixture of Experts (MoE)** | 2017 / 2023+ | Sparse routing over FFN experts | Yes | 128K+ | Conditional computation for trillion-param models |
| **State-Space Models (Mamba)** | 2023+ | Selective state-space recurrence | Yes | 1M+ | Sub-quadratic alternative to attention |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECMT862 Module 1]**

### **Q1. Define a Large Language Model. List any four emergent properties observed in modern LLMs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a Transformer-based neural network containing billions to trillions of parameters, pre-trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised next-token prediction, capable of performing diverse NLP tasks via in-context learning.

**Four emergent properties (½ mark each):**

1. **In-context learning (ICL):** Performing new tasks from examples provided in the prompt without weight updates.
2. **Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning:** Solving multi-step problems by generating intermediate reasoning steps.
3. **Instruction following:** Generalizing to novel natural-language instructions unseen during training.
4. **Cross-lingual transfer:** Performing tasks in low-resource languages after training primarily on English.

*(Acceptable alternatives: code generation, zero-shot classification, mathematical reasoning, hallucination-resistance at scale.)*

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between pre-training and fine-tuning in the context of LLMs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-training | Fine-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Massive unlabelled corpora (trillions of tokens) | Small, curated, task-specific labelled dataset |
| **Objective** | Self-supervised (next-token / masked-token prediction) | Supervised / RL-based (instruction following, preference) |
| **Compute** | Extremely high (weeks on thousands of GPUs) | Low (hours on a few GPUs) |
| **Parameter updates** | Updates all parameters from scratch | Updates all (SFT) or a subset (LoRA, adapters) |
| **Resulting model** | Base / foundation model | Specialized / aligned model |

*(1.5 marks for the comparison; 1.5 marks for correctly identifying the objectives and scale difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECMT862 Module 1, 14 marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Transformer-based decoder-only Large Language Model. Describe the role of **masked self-attention** in ensuring valid autoregressive generation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The architecture consists of the following sequential components — *(draw or describe the diagram from SECTION 4.2 of these notes)*:

1. **Tokenizer** (BPE/SentencePiece) → converts text to token IDs.
2. **Token + Positional Embeddings** → maps IDs to dense vectors and injects sequence order.
3. **Stack of $L$ identical Transformer blocks**, each containing:
   - **Layer Normalization** (pre-norm style in modern LLMs)
   - **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** ($h$ parallel heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention over a slice of the embedding)
   - **Residual Connection** around the attention sub-layer
   - **Layer Normalization**
   - **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network** (typically two linear layers with a SwiGLU or GELU nonlinearity, expanding to $4 \times d_{model}$)
   - **Residual Connection** around the FFN sub-layer
4. **Final Layer Norm**
5. **LM Head** (a linear layer projecting to vocabulary size $V$)
6. **Softmax** → probability distribution over the next token.

**Role of Masked Self-Attention (4 marks):**

In an autoregressive LLM, the model predicts $p(x_t \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{t-1})$. To enforce this causal structure during **training** (where the entire sequence is available in parallel for efficiency), the attention scores for future positions are *masked out* before the softmax.

Mathematically, the attention logits are modified as:

$$
\text{mask}_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \le i \text{ (allowed)} \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \text{ (forbidden)} \end{cases}
$$

The final attention is:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + \text{mask}\right) V.
$$

The $-\infty$ entries become $0$ after softmax, ensuring position $i$ attends **only** to positions $1$ through $i$. This preserves the autoregressive property while retaining full GPU parallelism during training (no recurrence is required).

**Why it matters:** Without masking, the model would "cheat" by peeking at future tokens, achieving zero training loss but failing completely at inference. Masking makes parallel training *equivalent* to sequential left-to-right generation.

---

#### **(b)** Derive the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling term in the Transformer attention mechanism. Explain what happens mathematically if this term is omitted when $d_k = 128$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*See SECTION 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The model answer must include:*

1. **Assumption** (1 mark): Components of $q$ and $k$ are independent with zero mean and unit variance.
2. **Mean of dot product** (1 mark): $\mathbb{E}[q \cdot k] = 0$.
3. **Variance of dot product** (2 marks): $\text{Var}(q \cdot k) = d_k$.
4. **Normalization argument** (1 mark): Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance.
5. **Consequence without scaling for $d_k = 128$** (2 marks):

If omitted, the dot products have standard deviation $\sqrt{128} \approx 11.31$. After softmax, such large logits push the output distribution toward a one-hot vector:

$$
\sigma(x_i) \approx \begin{cases} \approx 1 & \text{for the argmax} \\ \approx 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The gradient of softmax in this saturated regime is approximately zero, causing **vanishing gradients** and training stagnation. Empirically (Vaswani et al., 2017), learning without the $\sqrt{d_k}$ term requires learning-rate warm-up of $\sim 10\times$ longer and often fails to converge.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (b):**
> Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by:
> - Confusing the scaling term with **Layer Normalization** (it is *not* layer norm; it is a pre-softmax variance correction).
> - Failing to state the **independence assumption** explicitly.
> - Not connecting the missing scaling to a **practical failure mode** (gradient saturation). Always end a derivation question with a one-sentence engineering implication.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### **(a)** Explain the Chinchilla scaling law. A research lab plans to train a **70-billion-parameter** LLM. According to Chinchilla, how many training tokens are *compute-optimal*? Justify with the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (3 marks):** The Chinchilla scaling law (Hoffmann et al., DeepMind, 2022) is an empirically derived relationship that determines the *compute-optimal* allocation of model size $N$ and dataset size $D$ for a given compute budget $C$. The law states that for every doubling of model parameters, the number of training tokens should also be doubled — yielding a **constant ~20 tokens per parameter** ratio.

**Derivation (2 marks):** From the parametric loss model $L(N,D) = E + A N^{-\alpha} + B D^{-\beta}$ subject to the FLOPs constraint $C \approx 6ND$, minimizing $L$ with respect to $N$ yields:

$$
D_{\text{opt}} \approx 20 \times N_{\text{opt}}.
$$

**Numerical computation (2 marks):**

$$
D_{\text{opt}} = 20 \times 70 \times 10^9 = 1.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ tokens} = \mathbf{1.4\ trillion\ tokens}.
$$

**Practical implication:** LLaMA-2 70B was trained on **2T tokens** (slightly over-Chinchilla, trading inference cost for capability). GPT-3 175B was trained on only 300B tokens — **under-Chinchilla by ~10×**, leaving capability on the table.

---

#### **(b)** Compare encoder-only (BERT-style), decoder-only (GPT-style), and encoder-decoder (T5-style) Transformer architectures. For each, give **one canonical use case** and explain why the architecture is suited to it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Architecture | Attention Pattern | Pretraining Objective | Canonical Use Case | Why Suited |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder-only (BERT)** | **Bidirectional**, no mask | Masked Language Modeling (MLM) + Next Sentence Prediction | **Sentiment classification, NER, semantic search, embeddings** | Bidirectional context yields the richest *understanding* of each token; no generation is needed. |
| **Decoder-only (GPT, LLaMA)** | **Masked (causal)** self-attention | Next-token prediction (autoregressive) | **Open-ended text generation, chatbots, code synthesis** | The causal mask is the natural fit for left-to-right generation; the LM head is built into pretraining. |
| **Encoder–Decoder (T5, BART)** | Encoder: bidirectional; Decoder: causal + cross-attention | Span corruption, denoising | **Machine translation, summarization, structured I/O tasks** | Encoder compresses input bidirectionally; decoder generates output conditionally on that representation via cross-attention. |

**Mark allocation (7 marks):** 1 mark for correctly identifying the attention pattern of each; 1 mark for the pretraining objective; 1 mark for the use case; 1 mark for the justification (and 1 bonus for mentioning cross-attention in the encoder-decoder).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (b):**
> A frequent error: stating that BERT is "unidirectional" — it is **fully bidirectional** in its encoder. Another common error: claiming GPT cannot do classification — it can, but with a one-token prefix trick, not as naturally as BERT. Examiners reward students who mention **cross-attention** in the encoder-decoder description.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PECMT862 Module 1**

- ✅ **LLM = Transformer (decoder-only) + massive text + self-supervised next-token prediction.** This is the one-sentence definition.
- ✅ The LLM era was enabled by **three simultaneous exponentials**: compute (GP/TPUs), data (web-scale corpora), and algorithms (Transformer + self-supervision).
- ✅ **Transformer** replaced RNNs because self-attention is **parallelizable** and avoids the sequential bottleneck.
- ✅ The **scaling factor** in attention is $\sqrt{d_k}$, derived from variance stabilization, **not** arbitrary.
- ✅ **Emergent abilities** (in-context learning, CoT reasoning, instruction following) appear only beyond critical scale thresholds — they are *not* monotonic improvements.
- ✅ **Chinchilla rule:** 20 tokens per parameter for compute-optimal training. GPT-3 (175B / 300B) was undertrained; LLaMA-2 (70B / 2T) is closer to optimal.
- ✅ Training is **three-phase**: Pre-training → SFT → RLHF/DPO alignment. ChatGPT is a Phase-3 model, not a base model.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** Perplexity (for raw LM quality), BLEU (translation), ROUGE (summarization), Human Preference (alignment).
- ✅ Modern context windows: 128K (Claude, GPT-4 Turbo) → 1M–2M (Gemini 1.5, Magic). Long-context remains an active research frontier.
- ✅ **Key historical milestones to memorize with dates:**
  - 2013 — Word2Vec (Mikolov)
  - 2014 — Seq2Seq + Attention (Bahdanau)
  - 2017 — Transformer ("Attention Is All You Need")
  - 2018 — BERT (Google), GPT-1 (OpenAI)
  - 2019 — GPT-2, T5, RoBERTa
  - 2020 — GPT-3 (175B)
  - 2022 — Chinchilla, InstructGPT, ChatGPT launch (Nov 30)
  - 2023 — GPT-4, LLaMA, PaLM-2, Claude 2
  - 2024 — LLaMA-3, Gemini 1.5, Mamba, Agentic LLMs
- ✅ **For the KTU exam:** Always draw a block diagram (3–4 marks free), always define the *objective function*, and always end derivations with a one-line *engineering implication*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Language Models in Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Large Language Models in Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a transformer-based neural network trained on massive corpora of textual data using self-supervised learning objectives (typically next-token prediction / causal language modeling), containing **billions to hundreds of billions of trainable parameters**. When deployed "in practice," an LLM operates as a foundation model that can be adapted, prompted, or fine-tuned to perform a wide spectrum of downstream natural language processing tasks without task-specific architectural modifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An LLM in practice is a deployable, instruction-tuned, autoregressive transformer model (e.g., GPT-style decoder-only, LLaMA, Mistral) that ingests a tokenized prompt and emits a probabilistic distribution over its vocabulary to generate coherent, contextually relevant continuations at inference time.*

Key operational characteristics that distinguish practical LLMs from research prototypes include:

- **Parameter Count ($\theta$):** Ranging from **1B to 1T+** parameters.
- **Context Window ($C$):** Token span the model can attend to (typically **2K to 1M tokens**).
- **Token Vocabulary ($V$):** Usually **32K–256K** sub-word units (BPE/SentencePiece).
- **Inference Latency:** Time to First Token (TTFT) and Tokens Per Second (TPS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Apprentice

Imagine you hire a **brilliant but inexperienced apprentice** who has read **every book, newspaper, legal brief, coding repository, and poem ever published**. They cannot reason from first principles, but they have absorbed every pattern of human expression. When you give them a task phrased clearly, they can:

1. Recall the most likely continuation of your instruction (statistical pattern matching).
2. Re-arrange memorized knowledge to compose novel responses.
3. Follow explicit "directions" (prompts) to constrain their output style.

This apprentice is an **LLM in practice**. They are powerful, versatile, sometimes confidently wrong ("hallucinate"), and their performance depends heavily on **how well you phrase your question** (prompt engineering) and **what tools/context you give them** (RAG, function calling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "in Practice"?** Module 1 emphasizes the gap between *theoretical transformer math* (Modules 2–3) and the **engineering realities** of cost, latency, evaluation, safety, and deployment that a KTU engineer must handle when shipping LLM-powered products.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Practical LLM Usage

| Pillar | Description | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Prompting** | Crafting natural-language instructions | Prompt engineering, system messages |
| **Adaptation** | Modifying model behavior for a domain | Fine-tuning, LoRA, RLHF |
| **Augmentation** | Supplying external, verifiable knowledge | RAG, tool-use, function calling |

### 1.4 Core Metrics at a Glance

- **Perplexity (PPL):** Measures how "surprised" the model is by a held-out text sequence — lower is better.
- **Tokens Per Second (TPS):** Throughput metric for inference.
- **Cost per 1K Tokens ($/1K-tok):** Standard pricing unit in commercial APIs (e.g., **\$0.003–\$0.06 per 1K tokens** depending on tier).
- **Context Utilization Ratio:** $\frac{\text{used tokens}}{C_{\max}}$, where over-utilization truncates input.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says "in practice," think **engineering trade-offs** — cost vs. accuracy, latency vs. quality, open-source vs. proprietary, fine-tuning vs. prompting. Examiners love this triad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The LLM Inference Pipeline (Operational Mechanics)

When an LLM is called in production, the following sequence occurs. Each step has an engineering implication:

1. **Tokenization:** Input text $\rightarrow$ sequence of integer token IDs via BPE/SentencePiece.  
   *Engineering concern:* Tokenizer mismatch between training and inference corrupts outputs.

2. **Embedding Lookup:** Each token ID maps to a dense vector $\mathbf{e}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.  
   *Engineering concern:* Embedding table size $\approx V \times d$ must fit in VRAM.

3. **Transformer Forward Pass:** $L$ decoder layers apply causal self-attention + FFN.  
   *Engineering concern:* Quadratic memory in sequence length: $O(L \cdot n^2 \cdot d)$.

4. **Logit Projection:** Final hidden state $\rightarrow$ vector of size $V$ via tied/un-tied output matrix.

5. **Sampling Strategy:** Convert logits to token via Greedy, Top-$k$, Nucleus (Top-$p$), or Temperature-scaled softmax.

6. **Decoding Loop:** Append chosen token to context, repeat until `<eos>` or $n_{\max}$.

### 2.2 Why Sampling Strategies Matter in Practice

A production LLM rarely uses pure greedy decoding because it produces repetitive, degenerate text. The **temperature-scaled softmax** is the workhorse:

$$p(x_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

where $z_i$ is the logit for token $i$ and $\tau$ is the temperature. As $\tau \rightarrow 0$, behavior approaches greedy; as $\tau \rightarrow \infty$, the distribution becomes uniform.

- **Top-$k$ sampling:** Restrict the candidate pool to the $k$ highest-probability tokens.
- **Top-$p$ (nucleus) sampling:** Sample from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability $\geq p$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Token cost | $\text{Cost} = \frac{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}{1000} \times \text{price}_{1K}$ | USD |
| 2 | Perplexity | $PPL(X) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log p_{\theta}(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | Unitless (lower = better) |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_{CE} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Nats or bits-per-character |
| 4 | Inference VRAM (FP16) | $\text{Mem} \approx 2 \times \vert\theta\vert \text{ bytes}$ | e.g., 7B model $\approx$ 14 GB |
| 5 | KV-cache size | $\text{KV} \approx 2 \cdot L \cdot n \cdot d_h \cdot 2 \text{ bytes}$ | GB for long contexts |
| 6 | Attention complexity | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $n$ = seq length |
| 7 | BLEU score | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | ROUGE-L F1 | $F_{lcs} = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2)R_{lcs}P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs}+\beta^2 P_{lcs}}$ | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | Temperature sampling | $p_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i/\tau}}{\sum_j e^{z_j/\tau}}$ | $\tau \in [0.1, 2.0]$ |
| 10 | LoRA rank savings | $\Delta W \approx \dfrac{r(d_{in}+d_{out})}{d_{in} \cdot d_{out}}$ | $r \ll \min(d_{in},d_{out})$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** Examiners often ask *"How is perplexity different from accuracy?"* Answer: **Accuracy** is for closed-set classification; **Perplexity** measures the *inverse geometric mean* of the predicted probability of every correct token — it is a proper scoring rule for generative models.

### 2.4 The Cost–Latency–Quality Triangle

Every practical LLM deployment sits inside an irreducible trade-off triangle:

- **Quality** ↑ usually requires **larger models** → **cost** ↑ and **latency** ↑
- **Latency** ↓ requires **smaller models / quantization** → often **quality** ↓
- **Cost** ↓ requires **caching, batching, distillation** → engineering complexity ↑

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Most KTU viva questions on Module 1 revolve around *when to choose* Prompting vs. RAG vs. Fine-Tuning. The textbook answer: **Prompting** for style/format changes, **RAG** for knowledge freshness, **Fine-tuning** for domain-specific tone, vocabulary, or structured-output tasks where latency matters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Token Economics for an LLM API Call

**Problem Statement:** A startup uses an LLM API priced at **\$0.005 per 1,000 input tokens** and **\$0.015 per 1,000 output tokens**. A daily workload processes **12,000 customer-support tickets**. Each ticket averages **800 input tokens** (with retrieved context) and produces **200 output tokens**. Compute the daily and monthly (30-day) operational cost.

**Step 1 — Identify the per-ticket token counts.**

$$n_{\text{in}}^{\text{ticket}} = 800 \text{ tokens}, \quad n_{\text{out}}^{\text{ticket}} = 200 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 2 — Compute per-ticket cost.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{in}}^{\text{ticket}} = \frac{800}{1000} \times 0.005 = 0.004 \text{ USD}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{out}}^{\text{ticket}} = \frac{200}{1000} \times 0.015 = 0.003 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Sum per-ticket cost.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{ticket}} = 0.004 + 0.003 = 0.007 \text{ USD per ticket}$$

**Step 4 — Scale to daily volume.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{daily}} = 12{,}000 \times 0.007 = 84.00 \text{ USD/day}$$

**Step 5 — Scale to monthly volume.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = 84.00 \times 30 = 2520.00 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Generalized Formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{Cost}_{\text{period}} = N_{\text{calls}} \times \left(\frac{n_{\text{in}} \cdot p_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}} \cdot p_{\text{out}}}{1000}\right)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Inference Memory Footprint

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the GPU VRAM required to serve a **13-billion parameter** LLM in **FP16** precision, assuming a **2× overhead** for activations, optimizer-free inference state (KV-cache), and framework buffers.

**Step 1 — Compute the raw parameter memory in FP16.**

Each FP16 weight consumes **2 bytes**. Therefore:

$$M_{\text{params}} = 2 \times 13 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} = 26 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2 — Add the standard 2× runtime overhead (KV-cache, activations, CUDA workspace).**

$$M_{\text{total}} = 2 \times M_{\text{params}} = 2 \times 26 = 52 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret against common GPUs.**

| GPU | VRAM | Fits a 13B (FP16)? |
|---|---|---|
| NVIDIA A10 | 24 GB | No |
| NVIDIA A100 | 80 GB | Yes (with room for KV-cache) |
| NVIDIA H100 | 80 GB | Yes |
| 2 × A10 (tensor parallel) | 48 GB total | Yes with sharding |

**Result:** A single A100 80GB is the minimum for uncompressed FP16 serving of a 13B model in production.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Perplexity from Log-Likelihoods

**Problem Statement:** An LLM assigns the following log-probabilities to a 4-token ground-truth sequence: $\log p = [-2.1,\ -1.4,\ -3.2,\ -0.8]$. Compute the perplexity.

**Step 1 — Sum the log-probabilities.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i}) = (-2.1) + (-1.4) + (-3.2) + (-0.8) = -7.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy in nats).**

$$H = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i}) = -\frac{1}{4}(-7.5) = 1.875 \text{ nats}$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain perplexity.**

$$\boxed{PPL = e^{H} = e^{1.875} \approx 6.52}$$

**Interpretation:** The model is, on average, as uncertain as a uniform distribution over $\approx 6.52$ tokens — a moderately good fit (typical GPT-class models on Wikipedia achieve PPL ≈ 20–30).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Reformatting JSON to YAML | **Prompting** | Simple instruction-following |
| Company-internal Q&A on latest policy | **RAG** | Knowledge freshness, citation needed |
| Legal contract clause generation in house style | **Fine-Tuning (LoRA)** | Domain vocabulary + style consistency |
| Real-time translation of 100 languages | **Prompting + few-shot** | General capability, low setup cost |
| Math reasoning improvement | **Prompting + Chain-of-Thought** | Elicits step-by-step reasoning |
| Customer churn risk from chat logs | **Fine-Tuning + classifier head** | Specialized predictive task |

---

### 3.5 Derivation — LoRA Parameter Savings

**Problem Statement:** A transformer attention projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{in} \times d_{out}}$ where $d_{in} = d_{out} = 4096$. Using LoRA with rank $r = 8$, compute the fraction of *trainable* parameters relative to full fine-tuning.

**Step 1 — Full fine-tuning parameter count.**

$$N_{\text{full}} = d_{in} \times d_{out} = 4096 \times 4096 = 16{,}777{,}216$$

**Step 2 — LoRA parameter count.**

LoRA decomposes $\Delta W = B A$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d_{out}}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{in} \times r}$:

$$N_{\text{LoRA}} = r \times (d_{in} + d_{out}) = 8 \times (4096 + 4096) = 65{,}536$$

**Step 3 — Compute the savings ratio.**

$$\frac{N_{\text{LoRA}}}{N_{\text{full}}} = \frac{65{,}536}{16{,}777{,}216} \approx 0.0039 = 0.39\%$$

**Result:** LoRA fine-tunes only **~0.4%** of the parameters, dramatically reducing GPU memory and storage requirements while preserving near-full-finetuning quality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End LLM-Powered Application Architecture (RAG + Tool Use)

The following mermaid block renders the **production deployment topology** of a practical LLM application — the most common architectural diagram KTU examiners expect you to draw.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userApp["End User Client App"]
    apiGateway["API Gateway and Auth Layer"]
    orchestrator["LLM Orchestrator LangChain or LlamaIndex"]
    retriever["Vector Retriever FAISS or Pinecone"]
    llmCore["LLM Core API GPT or LLaMA or Mistral"]
    tools["External Tool APIs Calculator or SQL or Web"]
    cache["Response Cache Redis"]
    monitor["Observability Logs Traces Eval"]
    vectorDB[("Vector Store Embeddings")]
    docSrc["Document Source PDFs or Web or DB"]
    embedder["Embedding Model"]

    userApp --> apiGateway
    apiGateway --> orchestrator
    orchestrator --> retriever
    orchestrator --> llmCore
    orchestrator --> tools
    orchestrator --> cache
    orchestrator --> monitor
    retriever --> vectorDB
    docSrc --> embedder
    embedder --> vectorDB
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **End User** sends a query through the **API Gateway**.
2. The **Orchestrator** coordinates retrieval, LLM calls, and tool use.
3. The **Retriever** queries the **Vector Store** built from indexed documents.
4. The **LLM Core** generates a response using the retrieved context (RAG).
5. **External Tools** extend capability (math, DB lookup, web search).
6. **Cache** stores frequent answers for cost reduction.
7. **Observability** tracks latency, cost, hallucinations, and user feedback.

---

### 4.2 Prompting vs. RAG vs. Fine-Tuning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["New Practical LLM Task"]
    q1{"Need domain specific style or format"}
    q2{"Need fresh or private knowledge"}
    q3{"Latency budget below 200 ms"}
    prompt["Use Prompt Engineering"]
    rag["Use Retrieval Augmented Generation"]
    ft["Use Fine Tuning LoRA or QLoRA"]
    hybrid["Use Hybrid RAG plus Fine Tune"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- "No" --> q2
    q1 -- "Yes" --> q3
    q2 -- "Yes" --> rag
    q2 -- "No" --> prompt
    q3 -- "Yes" --> ft
    q3 -- "No" --> hybrid
```

---

### 4.3 LLM Inference Decoding Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["Prompt Tokens T1 T2 ... Tn"]
    b["Transformer Forward Pass"]
    c["Logits over Vocab V"]
    d["Sampling Strategy Greedy Top-k Top-p"]
    e["Next Token Tn plus 1"]
    f{"EOS Token Generated"}
    stop["Stop and Return Output"]
    loop["Append Token and Repeat"]

    a --> b --> c --> d --> e --> f
    f -- "No" --> loop
    loop --> b
    f -- "Yes" --> stop
```

---

### 4.4 Fine-Tuning Workflow (LoRA Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base["Pretrained Base Model Frozen"]
    loraA["LoRA Matrix A rank r"]
    loraB["LoRA Matrix B rank r"]
    data["Domain Specific Dataset"]
    train["Train Only A and B"]
    merge["Merge Weights for Inference"]
    deploy["Deploy Quantized Model INT4 or INT8"]

    base --> merge
    loraA --> train
    loraB --> train
    data --> train
    train --> merge --> deploy
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**  
*Define a Large Language Model (LLM). List any three practical applications of LLMs in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Large Language Model is a transformer-based deep neural network trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised next-token prediction, typically containing billions of parameters. Three practical industry applications are:

1. **Customer Support Automation** — LLM-powered chatbots (e.g., banking assistants) handle tier-1 queries, reducing human-agent load by up to 70%.
2. **Code Generation & Review** — Tools like GitHub Copilot accelerate developer productivity by 30–55% on boilerplate tasks.
3. **Document Summarization & Knowledge Mining** — Enterprise LLMs condense 100-page reports into executive briefings, used in legal, pharma, and consulting.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any three valid applications with brief justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
*Differentiate between **Perplexity** and **BLEU score** as LLM evaluation metrics. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Perplexity | BLEU |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Intrinsic (model-level) | Extrinsic (task-level) |
| Measures | How well the model predicts the next token | N-gram overlap with reference text |
| Range | 1 to ∞ (lower better) | 0 to 1 (higher better) |
| Use case | Pre-training, LM benchmarking | Translation, summarization |

**Perplexity** is preferred during **pre-training and language-model evaluation** because it directly measures probability assignment. **BLEU** is preferred for **generation tasks with reference outputs** (translation, summarization) because it compares surface-level n-gram matches against human gold standards.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1.5 marks for the comparison table, 1.5 marks for the use-case distinction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* 

**(a)** Explain the **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** architecture in detail. How does it differ from vanilla prompt engineering, and what are its practical advantages? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 7-billion parameter LLM is to be deployed on a single GPU. Compute the minimum VRAM required in **FP16**, **INT8**, and **INT4** precision. Assume a **20% overhead** for KV-cache and runtime allocations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** is an architectural pattern that augments an LLM's parametric memory with **non-parametric, external knowledge** retrieved at inference time.

**Components:**

1. **Document Ingestion Pipeline** — Domain documents (PDFs, HTML, databases) are chunked (typically 256–512 tokens), embedded by an embedding model (e.g., `text-embedding-3-small`), and stored in a **vector database** (FAISS, Pinecone, Chroma).
2. **Retriever** — At query time, the user query is embedded using the same embedding model; the retriever performs an **approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN)** search to fetch the top-$k$ most semantically similar chunks.
3. **Context Assembly** — Retrieved chunks are concatenated with the user prompt inside a templated prompt envelope (e.g., *"Use the following context to answer: {context}. Question: {query}"*).
4. **LLM Generation** — The LLM generates a response grounded in the retrieved evidence, drastically reducing hallucinations and enabling citation.

**Difference from Vanilla Prompting:**

| Aspect | Vanilla Prompting | RAG |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge source | Model's frozen weights | External, updatable index |
| Update cost | Re-train or fine-tune | Re-index documents |
| Hallucination risk | High | Low (grounded in retrieved text) |
| Citation ability | None | Inherently possible |
| Domain cost | High (fine-tune) | Low (index once) |

**Practical Advantages:**

- **Freshness:** Knowledge base can be re-indexed in minutes; no retraining needed.
- **Verifiability:** Citations are traceable to source documents — critical in legal, medical, and enterprise contexts.
- **Cost:** Avoids expensive domain fine-tuning for knowledge-heavy tasks.
- **Privacy:** Sensitive data stays in the controlled vector DB; the base LLM never sees it during training.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark per component, 2 marks for the comparison, 2 marks for practical advantages.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $\vert\theta\vert = 7 \times 10^9$ parameters. Overhead = **20%**.

**Step 1 — Base parameter memory per precision.**

- **FP16:** $2 \text{ bytes/param} \Rightarrow 7 \times 10^9 \times 2 = 14 \text{ GB}$
- **INT8:** $1 \text{ byte/param} \Rightarrow 7 \times 10^9 \times 1 = 7 \text{ GB}$
- **INT4:** $0.5 \text{ byte/param} \Rightarrow 7 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ GB}$

**Step 2 — Apply 1.20× overhead factor.**

$$M_{\text{FP16}} = 14 \times 1.20 = 16.8 \text{ GB}$$

$$M_{\text{INT8}} = 7 \times 1.20 = 8.4 \text{ GB}$$

$$M_{\text{INT4}} = 3.5 \times 1.20 = 4.2 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3 — Match against typical GPUs.**

| Precision | Min VRAM | Suitable Single GPU |
|---|---|---|
| FP16 | 16.8 GB | NVIDIA A100 40GB, RTX 4090 24GB |
| INT8 | 8.4 GB | NVIDIA A10 24GB, RTX 3090 24GB |
| INT4 | 4.2 GB | NVIDIA T4 16GB, RTX 3060 12GB |

**Engineering Insight:** Quantization from FP16 → INT4 yields a **4× memory reduction** with typically <2% quality loss on standard benchmarks — the primary lever for serving large models on commodity hardware.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 marks for correct FP16/INT8/INT4 calculations, 2 marks for the overhead multiplication, 2 marks for the GPU mapping.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare **Prompt Engineering**, **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**, and **Fine-Tuning** as three practical strategies for adapting LLMs. Provide a clear decision-making framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A customer-support chatbot uses an LLM API priced at **\$0.008 per 1K input tokens** and **\$0.024 per 1K output tokens**. It handles **8,500 chats/day**, averaging **1,200 input tokens** (with retrieved context) and **300 output tokens**. Additionally, 20% of queries hit a **cached-response path** that costs **\$0** per query. Calculate the monthly operational cost for 30 days. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**The Three Adaptation Strategies:**

1. **Prompt Engineering** — Crafting instructions, few-shot examples, system messages, and chain-of-thought scaffolds to elicit desired behavior from a frozen, off-the-shelf LLM.  
   *Cost:* Near-zero. *Latency:* Lowest. *Use when:* Task is general (translation, summarization, reformatting).

2. **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** — Injecting external, up-to-date knowledge into the prompt via a vector-retrieval step before generation.  
   *Cost:* Indexing + retrieval overhead (typically 2–10 ms). *Use when:* Knowledge must be fresh, private, or citable.

3. **Fine-Tuning (SFT / LoRA / QLoRA)** — Updating model weights on domain-specific labeled data so the model internalizes the desired behavior.  
   *Cost:* High GPU compute + dataset curation. *Use when:* Style, tone, structured-output format, or specialized reasoning is required.

**Decision Framework:**

```
IF task needs ONLY style/format change   →  Prompting
IF task needs external/fresh knowledge   →  RAG
IF task needs domain vocabulary/style    →  Fine-Tuning
IF task needs BOTH knowledge + style     →  RAG + Fine-Tuning (hybrid)
```

**Practical Examples:**

- *"Translate to Malayalam"* → Prompting.
- *"Answer questions about our 2025 product catalog"* → RAG.
- *"Generate SQL in our house dialect"* → Fine-Tuning.
- *"Generate legally compliant contracts using our clause library"* → RAG + Fine-Tuning.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks each for the three strategies, 1 mark for the decision framework.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $p_{\text{in}} = \$0.008$ per 1K tokens, $p_{\text{out}} = \$0.024$ per 1K tokens
- $N_{\text{daily}} = 8500$ chats/day, of which **20% are cached (free)** → **80% billed**.
- $n_{\text{in}} = 1200$, $n_{\text{out}} = 300$

**Step 1 — Compute effective daily billed chats.**

$$N_{\text{billed}} = 8500 \times (1 - 0.20) = 8500 \times 0.80 = 6800 \text{ chats/day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute per-chat cost.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{in}}^{\text{chat}} = \frac{1200}{1000} \times 0.008 = 0.0096 \text{ USD}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{out}}^{\text{chat}} = \frac{300}{1000} \times 0.024 = 0.0072 \text{ USD}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{chat}} = 0.0096 + 0.0072 = 0.0168 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Compute daily billed cost.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{daily}} = 6800 \times 0.0168 = 114.24 \text{ USD/day}$$

**Step 4 — Compute monthly cost (30 days).**

$$\boxed{\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = 114.24 \times 30 = 3427.20 \text{ USD/month}}$$

**Insight:** The 20% caching strategy saves the startup **\$0.0168 × 1700 × 30 = \$856.80/month** — a direct demonstration of why **prompt caching** is a high-leverage optimization in production LLM systems.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks for effective chat count, 2 marks for per-chat cost, 2 marks for daily/monthly scaling, 1 mark for the caching insight.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing tokens with words.** 1 token ≈ 0.75 English words. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you assume 1 word = 1 token.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply overhead** in VRAM calculations. Always state the **overhead factor** explicitly.
> 3. **Writing "RAG fine-tunes the model"** — RAG is retrieval at inference time; it does **not** update weights.
> 4. **Skipping units** in cost answers — always write **USD** or **\$/month**.
> 5. **Mixing up perplexity and cross-entropy.** Perplexity is the **exponential of cross-entropy**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Definition:** An LLM is a transformer-decoder trained on next-token prediction over massive corpora.
- ☐ **Three Pillars of Practical Use:** Prompting, RAG, Fine-Tuning — know the **decision flow**.
- ☐ **Inference Pipeline:** Tokenize → Embed → Transformer → Logits → Sample → Decode-loop.
- ☐ **Sampling Strategies:** Greedy, Top-$k$, Top-$p$, Temperature; never use pure greedy in production.
- ☐ **Perplexity Formula:** $PPL = \exp(\text{mean negative log-likelihood})$ — intrinsic quality metric.
- ☐ **BLEU/ROUGE:** Extrinsic metrics for translation/summarization.
- ☐ **VRAM Formula:** $\text{Mem} \approx 2 \times \vert\theta\vert$ bytes (FP16); INT8 halves; INT4 quarters.
- ☐ **Attention Cost:** $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ — quadratic in sequence length drives the KV-cache bottleneck.
- ☐ **RAG Architecture:** Ingestion → Embedding → Vector DB → Retriever → Context Assembly → LLM.
- ☐ **LoRA Math:** Trainable params $\approx r \times (d_{in}+d_{out})$, often <1% of full fine-tuning.
- ☐ **Cost Formula:** $\text{Cost} = \frac{n_{\text{in}} p_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}} p_{\text{out}}}{1000} \times N_{\text{calls}}$.
- ☐ **Caching is king:** 20% cache hit rate can save hundreds of dollars/month at scale.
- ☐ **Practical Limitations:** Hallucinations, context-length limits, cost, latency, bias, jailbreak risk.
- ☐ **Quantization Trade-off:** FP16 → INT8 → INT4 saves memory but slightly degrades quality.
- ☐ **Always cite** retrieved sources when using RAG for verifiability in production.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Models Pre-training: Encoder-Decoder Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Language Models Pre-training: Encoder-Decoder Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Encoder-Decoder Architecture (in LLM Pre-training)**: A sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) neural network framework built on stacked Transformer blocks, in which an **Encoder** consumes a corrupted/spanned input sequence and compresses it into a dense contextual representation, while a **Decoder** autoregressively reconstructs the original (or target) sequence by attending to both previously generated tokens and the encoder's contextual memory.

In the context of LLM pre-training, this architecture is the foundation of denoising autoencoders such as **T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer)**, **BART (Bidirectional and Auto-Regressive Transformers)**, **MASS (Masked Sequence to Sequence)**, and the original **Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017)**. The pre-training objective is **self-supervised**: a clean corpus is artificially corrupted (via masking, deletion, permutation, span-infilling), and the model is trained to recover the original sequence, forcing it to learn universal linguistic, syntactic, semantic, and world-knowledge representations.

**Key syllabus terminology to memorize:**
- **Pre-training**: Self-supervised learning on massive unlabeled corpora.
- **Fine-tuning**: Task-specific adaptation using small labeled datasets.
- **Transfer Learning**: Reusing pre-trained weights for downstream tasks.
- **Contextual Embedding**: A token's vector is a function of its surrounding tokens.
- **Span Corruption**: The dominant pre-training strategy in T5 (replacing contiguous spans with sentinel tokens).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bilingual Stenographer"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition**: Imagine a highly skilled bilingual stenographer sitting in a courtroom.
> 1. The stenographer **listens** to the entire witness testimony in English (this is the **Encoder**).
> 2. The stenographer forms a complete mental **summary** of the testimony — every name, date, and emotion is encoded into memory.
> 3. The stenographer then begins **typing the translation** in real-time into Malayalam (this is the **Decoder**).
> 4. Crucially, while typing each Malayalam word, the stenographer keeps **glancing back** at the English memory (cross-attention) and also at the **words already typed** (self-attention / masked self-attention).
> 5. If the judge suddenly **blanks out a name** ("____ ___" spans) and asks the stenographer to guess, the stenographer uses context to fill it in (this is the pre-training denoising objective).

This is exactly how an encoder-decoder LLM operates during pre-training: it encodes meaning into vectors, then decodes by attending jointly to its own past outputs and the encoded memory, while learning to reconstruct corrupted text.

### 1.3 Why Encoder-Decoder for Pre-training?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Bidirectional context** | The encoder sees the full sentence (left + right) — unlike GPT's left-only decoder. |
| **Generative capability** | The decoder can produce fluent text for translation, summarization, QA. |
| **Unified text-to-text** | All tasks (classification, translation, QA) can be cast as text generation (T5). |
| **Denoising objective** | Span-corruption forces the model to learn deep semantic, syntactic, and commonsense reasoning. |
| **Alignment with downstream tasks** | Many real-world tasks are naturally Seq2Seq (translation, summarization, dialogue). |

### 1.4 The Three Canonical Pre-training Objectives

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Denoising / Span Corruption (T5, BART)**: Random contiguous spans are replaced with sentinel tokens; the model generates the missing spans.
> 2. **Masked Language Modeling (BERT-style)**: ~15% tokens are masked; encoder predicts them (this is **encoder-only** pre-training — included here for contrast).
> 3. **Autoregressive Language Modeling (GPT-style)**: Predict the next token given previous tokens (decoder-only).
> The encoder-decoder hybrid uniquely combines objectives **(2)** in the encoder and **(3)** in the decoder, with cross-attention bridging them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Encoder-Decoder Pipeline

**Stage 1 — Input Tokenization & Embedding**
- Raw text $\to$ Subword tokens (BPE / SentencePiece / WordPiece).
- Each token $x_i$ maps to a learned vector $e_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}}$.
- Positional encoding $p_i$ is added: $\tilde{e}_i = e_i + p_i$.

**Stage 2 — Encoder Stack (N identical blocks)**
Each block contains two sub-layers:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — every token attends to every other token in the same sequence.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — a 2-layer MLP applied independently at each position.
- Each sub-layer is wrapped with **Residual Connection** + **Layer Normalization** (Post-LN) or Pre-LN variant.

**Stage 3 — Decoder Stack (N identical blocks)**
Each block contains three sub-layers:
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — prevents attending to future tokens (autoregressive).
2. **Multi-Head Cross-Attention** — queries come from decoder; keys/values come from the encoder output.
3. **Position-wise FFN**.
- Same residual + normalization pattern.

**Stage 4 — Linear + Softmax Output Head**
- Final hidden state $h_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}}$ is projected to vocabulary size $V$.
- Softmax produces a probability distribution over the next token.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following equations. They are **board-exam favorites** for derivations and numerical problems.

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}\!\left(Q W_i^Q,\; K W_i^K,\; V W_i^V\right)
$$

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O
$$

$$
\text{PE}(pos, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i / d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{PE}(pos, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i / d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, x W_1 + b_1)\,W_2 + b_2
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_\theta(y_t \mid y_{<t},\, x)
$$

$$
\text{Perplexity (PPL)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\log P_\theta(y_t \mid y_{<t})\right)
$$

### 2.3 Summary Table of Architectural Hyperparameters

| Hyperparameter | Notation | Typical Value (Base) | Typical Value (Large) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Model dimension | $d_{\text{model}}$ | **512** | **1024** | Embedding & hidden state size |
| Number of heads | $h$ | **8** | **16** | Parallel attention subspaces |
| Head dimension | $d_k = d_v$ | 64 | 64 | Per-head feature size |
| FFN hidden dim | $d_{ff}$ | 2048 | 4096 | Inner feed-forward width |
| Number of layers (enc & dec) | $N$ | 6 | 12 | Depth of stack |
| Dropout | — | **0.1** | 0.1 | Regularization |
| Vocabulary size | $V$ | ~30,000 | ~50,000 | Output projection size |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Encoder-decoder pre-training is the workhorse behind:
- **Google Translate** (original Transformer, 2017).
- **Search engines** (T5 powers document re-ranking and snippet generation).
- **Code intelligence** (CodeT5, PLBART for code summarization and bug-fixing).
- **Document AI** (LayoutLM, Donut — encoder processes image patches, decoder generates text).
- **Healthcare NLP** (BioBART, Clinical-T5 for medical report generation).
- **Speech & Audio** (Whisper uses encoder-decoder for speech-to-text translation).

### 2.5 Why the Scaling Factor $\sqrt{d_k}$?

> [!NOTE]
> When $d_k$ is large, the dot products $QK^{\top}$ grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients (saturation). Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the variance of the dot products $\approx 1$, preserving healthy gradient flow. This was empirically validated in Vaswani et al. (2017) as a critical stabilization trick.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scaled Dot-Product Attention

**Goal**: Show why the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary and how the attention distribution arises.

**Step 1 — Setup**
Let the query, key, and value matrices be:
- $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$
- $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_v}$

where $n$ is the number of queries and $m$ is the number of key-value pairs.

**Step 2 — Compute raw compatibility scores**
Take the dot product between each query and each key:
$$
S = Q K^{\top} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}
$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the variance of $S$**
Assume the components of $Q$ and $K$ are independent random variables with **zero mean** and **unit variance**:
$$
E[q_i] = E[k_i] = 0,\quad \text{Var}(q_i) = \text{Var}(k_i) = 1
$$
The dot product of one query with one key is:
$$
s = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i
$$
Its mean is **0** (product of zero-mean independent variables), and its variance is:
$$
\text{Var}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} E[q_i^2]\,E[k_i^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} 1 \cdot 1 = d_k
$$
Therefore, the variance of the compatibility scores grows **linearly** with the embedding dimension $d_k$.

**Step 4 — Apply the scaling trick**
Divide the scores by $\sqrt{d_k}$ so that the variance of the scaled score becomes:
$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1
$$
The scaled scores are:
$$
S' = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply softmax to obtain attention weights**
$$
A = \text{softmax}(S') = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}
$$
Each row of $A$ is a probability distribution (non-negative, sums to 1) over the keys.

**Step 6 — Weight the values**
$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A \cdot V = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

This is the **scaled dot-product attention** used in every Transformer block.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Head Attention Output Dimension

**Goal**: Verify that the concatenated output has dimension $d_{\text{model}}$ after the final projection.

**Step 1 — Per-head dimensions**
- Each head operates on queries, keys, values of dimension $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$.
- Each head output: $A_i V_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$.

**Step 2 — Concatenation**
$$
\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (h \cdot d_v)} = \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}
$$
(using the constraint $h \cdot d_v = d_{\text{model}}$).

**Step 3 — Final linear projection**
$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O
$$
with $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$. Output dimension: $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sinusoidal Positional Encoding

**Goal**: Show that the relative position $k - pos$ can be expressed as a linear function of the encoding.

The encoding uses wavelengths forming a geometric progression from $2\pi$ to $10000 \cdot 2\pi$:
$$
\lambda_i = 2\pi \cdot 10000^{2i / d_{\text{model}}}
$$
The angle for position $pos$ at frequency $i$ is $\theta_i \cdot pos$ where $\theta_i = 1 / 10000^{2i / d_{\text{model}}}$.

Using the sine/cosine addition identities:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PE}(pos + k,\, 2i) &= \sin(\theta_i (pos + k)) \\
&= \sin(\theta_i pos)\cos(\theta_i k) + \cos(\theta_i pos)\sin(\theta_i k) \\
&= \cos(\theta_i k) \cdot \text{PE}(pos,\, 2i) + \sin(\theta_i k) \cdot \text{PE}(pos,\, 2i+1)
\end{aligned}
$$
This shows that $\text{PE}(pos+k)$ is a **linear function of $\text{PE}(pos)$** with coefficients depending only on the offset $k$. Hence the model can learn to attend to **relative positions** easily.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pre-training Loss Across Model Families

| Model | Encoder | Decoder | Pre-training Objective | Loss Function | Cross-Attention |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BERT** | ✅ | ❌ | Masked LM + NSP | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}}$ | No |
| **GPT-2/3** | ❌ | ✅ | Next-token prediction | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}}$ | No |
| **Original Transformer** | ✅ | ✅ | Parallel corpora translation | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}$ (translation) | Yes |
| **T5** | ✅ | ✅ | Span Corruption (15%) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{denoise}}$ | Yes |
| **BART** | ✅ | ✅ | Text Infilling + Sentence Shuffling | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{denoise}}$ | Yes |
| **MASS** | ✅ | ✅ | Masked Seq-to-Seq | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MASS}}$ | Yes |
| **mBART** | ✅ | ✅ | Denoising on multilingual corpus | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{denoise}}$ | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Encoder-Decoder Pre-training Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenizer: BPE / SentencePiece]
    B --> C[Token IDs + Positional Encoding]
    C --> D[Input Embedding Matrix d_model]
    D --> E[Encoder Stack N blocks]
    E --> E1[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    E1 --> E2[Add and Norm]
    E2 --> E3[Feed-Forward Network]
    E3 --> E4[Add and Norm]
    E4 --> F[Encoder Output K and V Memory]
    F --> G[Decoder Stack N blocks]
    G --> G1[Masked Self-Attention]
    G1 --> G2[Add and Norm]
    G2 --> G3[Cross-Attention Q from Decoder K V from Encoder]
    G3 --> G4[Add and Norm]
    G4 --> G5[Feed-Forward Network]
    G5 --> G6[Add and Norm]
    G6 --> H[Linear Projection to Vocab Size]
    H --> I[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    I --> J[Predicted Token]
    K[Corrupted Input: Span Masked] --> C
    L[Original Target Sequence] --> M[Cross-Entropy Loss]
    J --> M
    M --> N[Backpropagation and Weight Update]
```

### 4.2 Multi-Head Attention Internal Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Input Queries Q] --> Q1[W_Q Linear]
    K[Input Keys K] --> K1[W_K Linear]
    V[Input Values V] --> V1[W_V Linear]
    Q1 --> H1[Head 1]
    K1 --> H1
    V1 --> H1
    Q1 --> H2[Head 2]
    K1 --> H2
    V1 --> H2
    Q1 --> Hn[Head h]
    K1 --> Hn
    V1 --> Hn
    H1 --> C[Concat]
    H2 --> C
    Hn --> C
    C --> WO[W_O Linear]
    WO --> OUT[Multi-Head Output]
```

### 4.3 T5 Span Corruption Pre-training Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Original Sentence: The cat sat on the mat] --> S2[Tokenize: The cat sat on the mat]
    S2 --> S3[Select Spans to Mask]
    S3 --> S4[Replace Spans with Sentinel Tokens X Y Z]
    S4 --> S5[Encoder Input: The cat X on Y Z]
    S5 --> ENC[Encoder]
    ENC --> MEM[Contextual Memory]
    S6[Decoder Input: X sat Y the Z] --> DEC[Decoder]
    MEM --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT[Output: cat on mat]
    OUT --> LOSS[Cross-Entropy vs Ground Truth]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Attention vs Self-Attention Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC_BLOCK[Encoder Block]
        EA[Input Sequence] --> EATT[Self-Attention: All Tokens See Each Other]
        EATT --> EFFN[Feed-Forward]
    end
    subgraph DEC_BLOCK[Decoder Block]
        DA[Shifted Output Sequence] --> DMASK[Masked Self-Attention: No Future Tokens]
        DMASK --> DCROSS[Cross-Attention: Q from Decoder, K and V from Encoder]
        DCROSS --> DFFN[Feed-Forward]
    end
    EFFN --> DCROSS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
*Explain the role of the cross-attention mechanism in an encoder-decoder Transformer. Why is it essential for sequence-to-sequence pre-training?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cross-attention allows the decoder to selectively focus on different parts of the encoder's output at every generation step. Its queries $(Q)$ come from the decoder's previous layer, while keys $(K)$ and values $(V)$ come from the encoder's final hidden states. This bridges the semantic gap between input and output sequences, enabling the model to align source tokens with target tokens — essential for translation, summarization, and span-corruption pre-training. Without cross-attention, the decoder would have no access to the input representation, collapsing the architecture into two independent stacks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
*Why is scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ necessary in the scaled dot-product attention formula? What happens if we omit it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As derived in Section 3.1, the variance of the dot product $QK^{\top}$ grows linearly with the embedding dimension $d_k$. Large dot products push the softmax into saturation regions where gradients are vanishingly small (close to 0), severely slowing or stalling training. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to 1, keeping gradients healthy. Without scaling, training becomes unstable, especially for large $d_k$ (e.g., 512, 1024).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Part B Module 1 questions offer **internal choice**. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the Transformer encoder-decoder used for pre-training language models. Discuss the role of each sub-layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the scaled dot-product attention formula and show mathematically why the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture Description (7 marks)**

The Transformer encoder-decoder consists of:

1. **Input Embedding + Positional Encoding**: Tokens are mapped to $d_{\text{model}}$-dimensional vectors and combined with sinusoidal positional encodings to inject order information.

2. **Encoder Stack** (N=6 blocks): Each block has
   - **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer where each position attends to all positions in the input.
   - **Position-wise FFN** — two linear transformations with ReLU: $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$.
   - **Residual connections** + **Layer Normalization** around each sub-layer.

3. **Decoder Stack** (N=6 blocks): Each block has three sub-layers:
   - **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — masks future positions to preserve autoregressive property.
   - **Multi-Head Cross-Attention** — queries from decoder, keys/values from encoder output.
   - **Position-wise FFN**.
   - Same residual + normalization wrapping.

4. **Output Head**: Linear projection to vocabulary size $V$ followed by softmax.

**Part (b) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Given $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$, with components having zero mean and unit variance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E\!\left[\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right] &= 0 \\
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) &= \frac{1}{d_k}\sum_{i=1}^{d_k} E[q_i^2]E[k_i^2] = \frac{d_k \cdot 1 \cdot 1}{d_k} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$
Without scaling, the variance would be $d_k$, leading to large logits that saturate the softmax. Hence:
$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$
This is mathematically justified and empirically essential for stable training.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the T5 span-corruption pre-training objective. How does it differ from BERT's masked language modeling and GPT's autoregressive pre-training? Why is it well-suited for the encoder-decoder architecture? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the dimensions of all learnable parameters in a single Transformer block (encoder side) with $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$, $d_{ff} = 2048$, and $V = 32000$. Show your work. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — T5 Span Corruption (7 marks)**

T5 masks roughly **15%** of input tokens by replacing contiguous random spans (average length 3) with unique sentinel tokens (e.g., `<extra_id_0>`, `<extra_id_1>`). The **encoder** sees the corrupted input with sentinels. The **decoder** must generate the dropped spans, each prefixed by its corresponding sentinel.

**Comparison:**
- **BERT MLM**: Predicts individual masked tokens; uses **encoder-only**; no generative capability.
- **GPT AR**: Predicts next token left-to-right; **decoder-only**; no bidirectional context.
- **T5 Span Corruption**: Predicts **multi-token spans** autoregressively; **encoder-decoder**; combines bidirectional encoding with generative decoding.

It suits encoder-decoder perfectly because the encoder must understand the corrupted context bidirectionally, while the decoder must generate fluent multi-token completions, mirroring the translation and summarization tasks the model will face downstream.

**Part (b) — Parameter Counting (7 marks)**

Per encoder block:

1. **Self-Attention projections** ($W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O$): each is $d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}$.
   $$4 \times (512 \times 512) = 4 \times 262144 = 1{,}048{,}576$$
2. **Feed-Forward Network**:
   - $W_1: 512 \times 2048 = 1{,}048{,}576$
   - $W_2: 2048 \times 512 = 1{,}048{,}576$
   - **Subtotal:** $2{,}097{,}152$
3. **Layer Normalization parameters** (2 per block, $\gamma$ and $\beta$): $2 \times 2 \times 512 = 2{,}048$

**Total per encoder block:**
$$
1{,}048{,}576 + 2{,}097{,}152 + 2{,}048 \approx 3{,}147{,}776 \text{ parameters}
$$

> (Note: The final output projection $d_{\text{model}} \times V$ is shared with the embedding matrix in T5; we count it separately only for the LM head.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor**: Students often write $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$ — KTU examiners will deduct **2 marks** for this critical omission.
> 2. **Confusing Q/K/V sources in cross-attention**: In cross-attention, **Q comes from the decoder** and **K, V come from the encoder**. Reversing this is a frequent error worth **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up Pre-LN vs Post-LN**: KTU 2024 expects you to know that the original Transformer uses **Post-LN**; T5/variants often use **Pre-LN** for stability.
> 4. **Skipping the variance derivation**: For derivations, you **must** show the assumption $E[q_i]=E[k_i]=0$ and $\text{Var}=1$. Omitting it loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the masking in decoder self-attention**: The "masked" part is what makes the decoder autoregressive — always mention it.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Encoder-Decoder = Two Transformer stacks** connected by **cross-attention**.
- **Pre-training objective** is typically **span corruption** (T5) or **text infilling** (BART).
- **Scaled dot-product attention**: $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$ — the $\sqrt{d_k}$ is non-negotiable.
- **Multi-head attention** projects $Q, K, V$ into $h$ subspaces of dimension $d_{\text{model}}/h$ each, then concatenates.
- **Positional encoding** uses sinusoidal $\sin$/$\cos$ at multiple wavelengths to encode absolute and (learnable) relative positions.
- **Cross-attention** is the *only* information pathway from encoder to decoder — without it, the decoder is blind to the input.
- **Masked self-attention** in the decoder uses an upper-triangular mask to prevent information leakage from future tokens.
- **Residual + LayerNorm** wrap every sub-layer to stabilize deep networks.
- **Loss** is standard cross-entropy over the target vocabulary at every decoding step.
- **Perplexity** is the standard evaluation metric: lower is better; $\text{PPL} = \exp(\text{avg. cross-entropy})$.
- **KTU favorites** for 14-mark derivations: scaling factor justification, multi-head parameter counting, sinusoidal relative-position property.
- **Distinguish clearly** between encoder-only (BERT), decoder-only (GPT), and encoder-decoder (T5, BART) pre-training.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attention Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Attention Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attention Mechanism (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The *Attention Mechanism* is a computational framework introduced in neural sequence modeling that enables a model to dynamically weigh the relevance of different elements in an input sequence when generating each element of an output sequence. Formally, it computes a weighted sum of *Value (V)* representations, where the weights (attention scores) are derived from a compatibility function between a *Query (Q)* and a set of *Keys (K)*.

In the context of **Large Language Models (LLMs)** built upon the **Transformer architecture** (Vaswani et al., 2017), attention replaces recurrence and convolution entirely, allowing **O(1) parallel computation** across sequence positions and full-context access without information degradation over long distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT862 / Module 1):**
> The Attention Mechanism is a *prerequisite* for understanding Multi-Head Attention, Positional Encoding, and the complete Transformer block — the foundational unit of all modern LLMs (GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Mistral).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a student reading a 500-page reference book to answer a single exam question. You do **not** read every page with equal intensity. Instead:

- Your brain formulates a **Query** ("What is backpropagation?")
- It scans the **Table of Contents (Keys)** to identify relevant chapters
- It assigns a **relevance score** to each chapter
- It then focuses more **attention (weight)** on highly relevant pages and skims irrelevant ones
- Finally, it extracts the **Value (information)** from those weighted pages to construct the answer.

This is *exactly* what the Attention Mechanism does inside an LLM — the Query is the current token being processed, the Keys are all tokens in the context, and the Values are the actual information content retrieved according to learned relevance scores.

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Role in LLM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Query (Q)** | The question you want to answer | Represents the current token's "information need" |
| **Key (K)** | Index labels of a library | Used to compute relevance with the Query |
| **Value (V)** | The actual book content | The information aggregated based on attention weights |
| **Score** | Relevance rating | A scalar indicating how much focus to place on each token |

> [!TIP]
> The standard embedding dimension for Keys/Queries in the original Transformer is **d_k = 64**, while the Value dimension is **d_v = 64**. The total model dimension is **d_model = 512**. These are critical parameters appearing frequently in numerical problems.

---

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Attention Mechanisms

| Type | Description | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Self-Attention** | Q, K, V all derived from the same input sequence | Encoder & Decoder of Transformer |
| **Cross-Attention** | Q from one sequence; K, V from another | Decoder attending to encoder output |
| **Causal (Masked) Self-Attention** | Prevents attending to future tokens | GPT-style autoregressive LLMs |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | Multiple attention operations in parallel | All modern Transformers |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | The base scoring function (Q·K / √d_k) | Universal in Transformer-based LLMs |
| **Additive Attention** | Uses a feed-forward network for scoring | Older RNN-based seq2seq models |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Attention Works

The attention operation unfolds in **five structured steps**:

1. **Linear Projection (Step 1)**: The input embedding matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ is multiplied by three learnable weight matrices to produce Queries, Keys, and Values.
2. **Score Computation (Step 2)**: A compatibility score is computed between each Query and every Key using dot product: $Q \cdot K^T$.
3. **Scaling (Step 3)**: Scores are divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent the softmax from saturating in regions of extremely small gradients (the *vanishing gradient* problem).
4. **Softmax Normalization (Step 4)**: The scaled scores are passed through a row-wise softmax to produce a probability distribution (attention weights summing to 1).
5. **Weighted Aggregation (Step 5)**: The attention weights are multiplied by the Value matrix to produce the final context-aware output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$?**
> As $d_k$ grows, the variance of $Q \cdot K^T$ grows linearly. Without scaling, the softmax outputs become near one-hot, causing vanishing gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance, keeping gradients in a trainable range.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **high-yield** — every formula here has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU university examinations for LLM / Deep Learning electives.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Shape |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = X W^Q$ | Query projection | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ |
| 2 | $K = X W^K$ | Key projection | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ |
