| 3 | $V = X W^V$ | Value projection | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 4 | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 5 | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$ | Single attention head | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 6 | $\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O$ | Multi-head aggregation | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ |
| 7 | $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}$ | Output projection | Trainable |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k$ | Variance of dot product under unit-Gaussian assumption | Scalar |
| 9 | $\text{score}(q, k) = \dfrac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ | Single-token scaled score | Scalar |
| 10 | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{\exp(e_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(e_j)}$ | Softmax-normalized weight | $\sum \alpha_i = 1$ |
| 11 | $h \cdot d_v = d_{\text{model}}$ | Constraint for Multi-Head Attention | $h = 8$, $d_v = 64$, $d_{\text{model}} = 512$ (original) |
| 12 | $\text{FLOPs}_{\text{attn}} = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_{\text{model}})$ | Computational complexity | Quadratic in $n$ |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Attention

The attention mechanism is the **operational backbone** of every modern LLM application:

- **Machine Translation**: Cross-attention aligns source and target languages.
- **Document Summarization**: Self-attention identifies salient sentences.
- **Code Generation (Copilot, CodeWhisperer)**: Causal attention generates tokens sequentially with full prior context.
- **Question Answering (RAG systems)**: Attention scores become interpretable *saliency maps* showing which retrieved context influenced the answer.
- **Multimodal Models (CLIP, LLaVA)**: Cross-attention fuses vision and language embeddings.

> [!TIP]
> **Computational Bottleneck:** Standard self-attention is $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ in sequence length. This is the reason for research into **Linear Attention**, **Sparse Attention (Longformer)**, and **Flash Attention**, which reduce complexity to $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ or $\mathcal{O}(n)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention Output

We will derive the closed-form output of a single attention head, given input embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ and learned weight matrices.

### Step 1: Project the Input

For a sequence of $n$ tokens with embedding dimension $d_{\text{model}}$, we compute three matrices:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= X W^Q, \quad W^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k} \\[4pt]
K &= X W^K, \quad W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k} \\[4pt]
V &= X W^V, \quad W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each token embedding is linearly transformed into three representational subspaces. $W^Q, W^K, W^V$ are *learned* via backpropagation.

---

### Step 2: Compute Raw Compatibility Scores

The dot product between Query row $i$ and Key row $j$ gives a scalar indicating how compatible the $i$-th token is with the $j$-th token:

$$
S = Q K^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

*Logic:* Each row $i$ of $S$ contains scores for token $i$ against all $n$ tokens in the sequence.

---

### Step 3: Apply the Scaling Factor

Assume $q_i$ and $k_i$ are independent random variables with mean 0 and variance 1. The dot product $s = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ has:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[s] &= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i k_i] = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Var}(s) &= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k
\end{aligned}
$$

To restore unit variance, divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$
S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

*Logic:* Prevents the softmax from being pushed into saturation (where gradient ≈ 0).

---

### Step 4: Apply Softmax Row-wise

The attention weight matrix $A$ is obtained via row-wise softmax:

$$
A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{\text{scaled}, ij})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(S_{\text{scaled}, ij})}, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij} = 1
$$

*Logic:* Normalises scores into a probability distribution over keys for each query.

---

### Step 5: Weighted Sum with Values

The final output is the attention-weighted aggregation of the Value matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) &= A \cdot V \\[4pt]
&\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each output row is a convex combination of value vectors, where the weights are attention probabilities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Head Attention Concatenation

Given $h$ parallel attention heads, each with its own learned projections:

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, \, K W_i^K, \, V W_i^V) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

The Multi-Head output is the concatenation followed by a final linear projection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) &= \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \dots, \text{head}_h) \cdot W^O \\[4pt]
W^O &\in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (Frequently Asked in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:**
> Given $n = 4$ tokens, $d_{\text{model}} = 8$, and $h = 2$ heads. Find the dimensions of all learnable parameters and the output of multi-head attention.

**Solution:**

- Per-head dimensions: $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h = 8 / 2 = 4$.
- Parameters per head: $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{8 \times 4}$, $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{8 \times 4}$. Total per head = $3 \times (8 \times 4) = 96$.
- Across $h = 2$ heads: $2 \times 96 = 192$.
- Output projection: $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{(2 \times 4) \times 8} = \mathbb{R}^{8 \times 8}$ → $64$ parameters.
- **Total trainable parameters in this MHA block: 192 + 64 = 256 parameters.**
- Output shape of $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2)$: $\mathbb{R}^{4 \times 8}$ (same as input $X$).
- Final output shape: $\mathbb{R}^{4 \times 8}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Time & Space Complexity

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Linear Projection ($Q, K, V$) | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot d_k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_k)$ | Linear in $n$ |
| $Q K^{T}$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Bottleneck for long sequences |
| Softmax | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Numerically stable variant used |
| Attention $\times V$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_v)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_v)$ | Weighted aggregation |
| **Total Self-Attention** | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_{\text{model}})$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Quadratic bottleneck |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Data Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete forward pass of a single attention head, from raw embeddings to the final output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpX["Input Embeddings X (n x d_model)"] --> projQ["Linear Projection W_Q"]
    inpX --> projK["Linear Projection W_K"]
    inpX --> projV["Linear Projection W_V"]

    projQ --> matQ["Query Matrix Q (n x d_k)"]
    projK --> matK["Key Matrix K (n x d_k)"]
    projV --> matV["Value Matrix V (n x d_v)"]

    matQ --> dotProd["Dot Product Q times K transpose"]
    matK --> dotProd
    dotProd --> scale["Scale by 1 over sqrt d_k"]
    scale --> maskOpt{"Masking Required?"}
    maskOpt -->|Yes Causal| applyMask["Apply Causal Mask"]
    maskOpt -->|No| softmaxOp
    applyMask --> softmaxOp["Row-wise Softmax"]
    softmaxOp --> attnW["Attention Weights A (n x n)"]

    matV --> weightedSum["Weighted Sum A times V"]
    attnW --> weightedSum
    weightedSum --> outY["Output Y (n x d_v)"]

    style inpX fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style outY fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style scale fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style softmaxOp fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

---

## 4.2 Multi-Head Attention — Parallel Architecture

This block diagram shows how $h$ independent attention heads operate in parallel and are concatenated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["Input X (n x d_model)"] --> h1["Head 1: Attn Q1 K1 V1"]
    inX --> h2["Head 2: Attn Q2 K2 V2"]
    inX --> h3["Head 3: Attn Q3 K3 V3"]
    inX --> hN["Head h: Attn Qh Kh Vh"]

    h1 --> c1["head_1 (n x d_v)"]
    h2 --> c2["head_2 (n x d_v)"]
    h3 --> c3["head_3 (n x d_v)"]
    hN --> cN["head_h (n x d_v)"]

    c1 --> concat["Concatenate along feature dim"]
    c2 --> concat
    c3 --> concat
    cN --> concat

    concat --> projO["Linear Projection W_O"]
    projO --> outMHA["Multi-Head Output (n x d_model)"]

    style inX fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style outMHA fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style concat fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

---

## 4.3 Attention Score Computation — Tensor View

This diagram visualises the score matrix $S = Q K^{T} / \sqrt{d_k}$ as an $n \times n$ grid, where entry $S_{ij}$ represents how much token $i$ attends to token $j$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Query of Token 1"] --> s11["S_11"]
    q1 --> s12["S_12"]
    q1 --> s1n["S_1n"]
    q2["Query of Token 2"] --> s21["S_21"]
    q2 --> s22["S_22"]
    q2 --> s2n["S_2n"]
    qn["Query of Token n"] --> sn1["S_n1"]
    qn --> sn2["S_n2"]
    qn --> snn["S_nn"]

    k1["Key of Token 1"] --> s11
    k1 --> s21
    k1 --> sn1
    k2["Key of Token 2"] --> s12
    k2 --> s22
    k2 --> sn2
    kn["Key of Token n"] --> s1n
    kn --> s2n
    kn --> snn

    s11 --> soft["Softmax row-wise"]
    s12 --> soft
    s1n --> soft
    s21 --> soft
    s22 --> soft
    s2n --> soft
    sn1 --> soft
    sn2 --> soft
    snn --> soft

    soft --> attnOut["Attention Weights A_ij (n x n)"]

    style soft fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style attnOut fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define the Attention Mechanism in neural networks. List its three main inputs and explain the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The attention mechanism is a dynamic weighting scheme that allows a neural network to selectively focus on the most relevant parts of an input sequence when producing each element of the output.

The three main inputs are:
- **Query (Q)**: Represents the current token's "information request" — what we are looking for. Shape: $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$.
- **Key (K)**: Acts as an index or label for each input token — used to compute compatibility with the Query. Shape: $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$.
- **Value (V)**: Holds the actual information content to be aggregated. Shape: $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$.

The mechanism computes a compatibility score between Q and K, normalises via softmax, and produces a weighted sum of V.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing Q/K/V, 1 mark for explanation of role.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the dot product $Q K^T$ divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is applied to prevent the softmax function from saturating when the embedding dimension $d_k$ is large.

Under the assumption that query and key components are independent random variables with zero mean and unit variance, the dot product $q \cdot k$ has variance $d_k$. As $d_k$ grows, dot products grow in magnitude, pushing softmax inputs into regions of extremely small gradient (saturation). Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance, ensuring stable gradients and effective training.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for stating the variance problem, 1 mark for the unit-variance restoration, 1 mark for the gradient/softmax saturation consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the Scaled Dot-Product Attention mechanism in detail. Derive the complete mathematical formulation step-by-step, clearly stating the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Multi-Head Attention. How does it improve over single-head attention, and derive the output dimensions given $n$ tokens, embedding dimension $d_{\text{model}}$, and $h$ heads. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Scaled Dot-Product Attention (7 Marks)

The Scaled Dot-Product Attention is the core operation of the Transformer architecture, formalised as:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Step 1 — Linear Projections (1 Mark):**
Given input embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$, three learnable weight matrices produce:

$$
Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V
$$

**Step 2 — Raw Scores (1 Mark):**
The raw compatibility matrix is computed as the dot product between all queries and all keys:

$$
S = Q K^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

**Step 3 — Scaling (1 Mark):**
Since $\text{Var}(q \cdot k) = d_k$, scores are scaled to restore unit variance:

$$
S' = \frac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 4 — Softmax Normalisation (1 Mark):**
Each row of $S'$ is converted into a probability distribution:

$$
A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(S'_{ij})}
$$

**Step 5 — Weighted Aggregation (1 Mark):**
The output is the attention-weighted sum of values:

$$
\text{Output} = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

**Diagram & Role Explanation (2 Marks):**
- Q identifies *what information is needed*.
- K provides *indexing labels* to compute relevance.
- V provides the *actual content* to be retrieved.
- The softmax enforces a normalised probability distribution, ensuring the model attends with a *soft selection*.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Multi-Head Attention (7 Marks)

Multi-Head Attention (MHA) runs $h$ parallel attention heads, each with its own learned projections, allowing the model to attend to information from *different representation subspaces* simultaneously.

**Formulation (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W_i^Q, X W_i^K, X W_i^V)
$$

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O
$$

**Improvements over Single-Head (2 Marks):**
1. **Diverse Subspaces**: Each head can specialise — one may capture syntactic dependencies, another semantic similarity, another positional relations.
2. **Increased Representational Capacity**: Concatenation yields $h d_v$ features, enriching the model's expressiveness without increasing per-head computation.
3. **Stabilised Training**: Multiple shallow heads average out noisy attention patterns.

**Dimension Derivation (2 Marks):**
- Per head: $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$.
- Each $\text{head}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$.
- Concatenation: $\mathbb{R}^{n \times h d_v} = \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$.
- Final output: $\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$.

**Worked Numerical:** For $n = 10$, $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$: each head has $d_v = 64$, output per head is $\mathbb{R}^{10 \times 64}$, concatenated to $\mathbb{R}^{10 \times 512}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Compare Self-Attention, Cross-Attention, and Causal (Masked) Self-Attention. Provide one real-world LLM use-case for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Transformer block has $n = 6$ tokens, $d_{\text{model}} = 16$, and $h = 4$ heads. Compute: (i) the dimension of each head's Q, K, V matrices, (ii) the total number of trainable parameters in the multi-head attention block, and (iii) the shape of the final output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparison (7 Marks)

| Type | Q Source | K, V Source | Masking | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Self-Attention** | Same sequence | Same sequence | None (encoder) | BERT — bidirectional context encoding |
| **Cross-Attention** | Decoder state | Encoder output | None | Machine Translation (English→French) |
| **Causal Self-Attention** | Same sequence | Same sequence | Upper triangular | GPT — autoregressive text generation |

**Detailed Explanation (2 Marks Each):**
1. **Self-Attention**: All Q, K, V come from the same input $X$. Each token attends to all others. Used in encoder layers of Transformer, BERT, RoBERTa.
2. **Cross-Attention**: Q comes from the decoder's previous layer; K, V come from the encoder's final output. Critical for seq2seq tasks like translation.
3. **Causal Self-Attention**: A mask is applied so token $i$ can only attend to positions $j \leq i$, preserving autoregressive property. Used in GPT, LLaMA, Mistral for next-token prediction.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $n = 6$, $d_{\text{model}} = 16$, $h = 4$.

**(i) Per-Head Dimensions (2 Marks):**

$$
d_k = d_v = \frac{d_{\text{model}}}{h} = \frac{16}{4} = 4
$$

Therefore:
- $Q_i \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 4}$, $K_i \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 4}$, $V_i \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 4}$.

**(ii) Total Trainable Parameters (3 Marks):**

Per head:
- $W^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 4}$ → $64$ parameters.
- $W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 4}$ → $64$ parameters.
- $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 4}$ → $64$ parameters.
- Per head total = $3 \times 64 = 192$ parameters.

Across $h = 4$ heads:
- $4 \times 192 = 768$ parameters.

Output projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}} = \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 16}$:
- $W^O$ has $256$ parameters.

**Total trainable parameters in MHA block: $768 + 256 = 1024$ parameters.**

**(iii) Shape of Final Output (2 Marks):**

- $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_4) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 16}$.
- After $W^O$ projection: $\mathbb{R}^{6 \times 16}$ (same as input).
- The output preserves the sequence length and embedding dimension, allowing residual connections in the Transformer block.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Attention Mechanism Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$** — A frequent mistake is writing $\text{softmax}(Q K^{T}) V$ without scaling. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 2. **Confusing $d_k$ with $d_{\text{model}}$** — In numerical problems, students often assume $d_k = d_{\text{model}}$ instead of computing $d_{\text{model}} / h$. This invalidates the entire dimension derivation.
> 3. **Wrong softmax direction** — Softmax is applied *row-wise* (over keys for each query), not column-wise. State this explicitly to avoid losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up Self vs Cross-Attention sources** — Cross-attention has Q from one source and K, V from another. Self-attention has all three from the same input.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" of scaling** — Examiners award a mark for *explaining* that scaling prevents softmax saturation, not just for writing the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attention Mechanism**

- ✅ Attention computes a *weighted sum of Values*, with weights derived from *Query-Key compatibility*.
- ✅ The three inputs are **Q (Query)**, **K (Key)**, **V (Value)**, all obtained via learnable linear projections from input embeddings.
- ✅ Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula: $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$.
- ✅ The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents softmax saturation by restoring unit variance of dot products.
- ✅ Softmax is applied **row-wise** to produce a probability distribution over keys for each query.
- ✅ **Self-Attention**: Q, K, V from the same input. Used in encoders and bidirectional models like BERT.
- ✅ **Cross-Attention**: Q from decoder, K, V from encoder. Used in seq2seq translation.
- ✅ **Causal Self-Attention**: Masked to prevent attending to future tokens. Used in GPT, LLaMA.
- ✅ **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ parallel heads, concatenates outputs, and applies a final projection $W^O$.
- ✅ Per-head dimension constraint: $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$.
- ✅ Standard self-attention complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_{\text{model}})$ — quadratic in sequence length.
- ✅ Output of MHA preserves shape $(n, d_{\text{model}})$, enabling residual connections in Transformer blocks.
- ✅ Total trainable parameters in MHA: $3 \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot d_k \cdot h + d_{\text{model}}^2 = 4 d_{\text{model}}^2$ when $d_k = d_{\text{model}} / h$.
- ✅ Modern LLMs (GPT-4, LLaMA-3) use **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)** or **Multi-Query Attention (MQA)** to reduce KV-cache memory while preserving quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017):** A Transformer is a deep neural network architecture that relies entirely on a **self-attention mechanism** to compute representations of its input and output sequences, eliminating the need for recurrence (RNNs/LSTMs) and convolution (CNNs). It uses stacked **encoder-decoder** blocks with **multi-head attention**, **position-wise feed-forward networks**, **residual connections**, and **layer normalization**.

In the context of **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, the Transformer is the foundational building block. Modern LLMs (GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Mistral) are essentially **decoder-only** or **encoder-only** stacks of Transformer blocks trained at massive scale.

### Key Terminology Checklist (KTU Syllabus Mapping)
| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Token** | Discrete unit of text (word or sub-word) mapped to an embedding vector |
| **Embedding** | Dense vector representation of a token, typically of dimension $d_{model}$ |
| **Query (Q)** | Vector representing "what information am I looking for?" |
| **Key (K)** | Vector representing "what information do I contain?" |
| **Value (V)** | Vector representing "the actual content I will deliver if matched" |
| **Context Window** | Maximum sequence length the model can attend to |
| **d_model** | Model embedding dimension, standard value **512** (base) or **768/1024+** in LLMs |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Library Research Assistant**
>
> Imagine you walk into a library to research the topic *"Impact of climate change on Kerala monsoons."* You give this single query to a research assistant. The assistant instantly knows **which sections of the library to look in** (Keys), pulls out the **relevant paragraphs** (Values), and synthesizes a tailored answer. Crucially, the assistant reads **all books simultaneously**, weighing how much each book matters to your query.
>
> This is exactly what the **attention mechanism** does:
> - **Query** = your research topic
> - **Keys** = the "table of contents" of every book in the library
> - **Values** = the actual content inside each book
> - **Softmax scores** = how much weight each book gets in your final answer
>
> **Self-attention** takes this one step further: every word in a sentence simultaneously acts as a *query*, a *key*, and a *value* — meaning each word looks at *every other word* in the sentence to understand its meaning in context. For instance, the word **"bank"** in *"I sat on the river bank"* gets strongly linked to *"river"* and *"sat,"* disambiguating it from a financial bank.

---

## 1.3 Why Transformers Replaced RNNs — The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Three engineering reasons made Transformers dominant:
> 1. **Parallelization:** Unlike RNNs (which process tokens sequentially), Transformers process all tokens in parallel, enabling GPU/TPU scaling.
> 2. **Long-Range Dependencies:** The shortest path between any two tokens in a Transformer is **O(1)**, versus **O(n)** in an RNN.
> 3. **Stable Gradient Flow:** Residual connections + LayerNorm allow training of 100+ layer stacks.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Hyperparameters

| Hyperparameter | Standard Value (Base Model) | Note |
|---|---|---|
| **d_model** | **512** | Embedding dimension |
| **Number of heads (h)** | **8** | Parallel attention heads |
| **d_k = d_v** | **64** | Per-head dimension |
| **d_ff** | **2048** | Feed-forward hidden size |
| **Number of layers (N)** | **6** (encoder) + **6** (decoder) | Original paper |
| **Dropout** | **0.1** | Standard regularization |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Self-Attention Mechanism — Operational Breakdown

The Transformer processes sequences through three projection matrices that are **learned during training**:

1. **Linear Projection Stage:** Each input embedding $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$ is projected into three vectors using learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$:
   - Query: $q_i = x_i W^Q$
   - Key: $k_i = x_i W^K$
   - Value: $v_i = x_i W^V$

2. **Compatibility Scoring:** For each token $i$, compute a dot-product score against every token $j$:
   $s_{ij} = q_i \cdot k_j^T$

3. **Scaling (Why √d_k?):** Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent softmax saturation. As $d_k$ grows, dot products grow in magnitude, pushing softmax into regions with tiny gradients. Scaling counteracts this.

4. **Softmax Normalization:** Convert raw scores into a probability distribution:
   $\alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{s_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = \frac{\exp(s_{ij}/\sqrt{d_k})}{\sum_{l=1}^{n} \exp(s_{il}/\sqrt{d_k})}$

5. **Weighted Aggregation:** Compute the output as a weighted sum of values:
   $z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} v_j$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind each step:**
> - *Why projections?* They let the model learn separate subspaces for "what to search" vs. "what to offer."
> - *Why dot product?* It's a cheap, differentiable measure of similarity.
> - *Why softmax?* It forces weights to sum to 1, creating a valid probability distribution over context.
> - *Why aggregation?* It produces a context-aware representation of token $i$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Examination — Module 1**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = XW^Q,\ K = XW^K,\ V = XW^V$ | Input projections | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ |
| 2 | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | Output $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 3 | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ | Single attention head | Each head $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 4 | $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h) W^O$ | Multi-head concatenation | $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d_{model}}$ |
| 5 | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise Feed-Forward | Two linear layers with ReLU |
| 6 | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Layer Normalization | $\mu,\sigma$ computed per token |
| 7 | $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Residual + Normalization | Applied around every sublayer |
| 8 | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$ | Sinusoidal PE (even dim) | Frequency varies by dimension |
| 9 | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$ | Sinusoidal PE (odd dim) | Complementary to even |
| 10 | $\text{MASK}(s_{ij}) = \begin{cases} s_{ij}, & j \leq i \\ -\infty, & j > i \end{cases}$ | Causal mask (decoder) | Prevents attending to future |

---

## 2.3 Multi-Head Attention — The "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** A single attention head can only learn **one type of relationship** at a time (e.g., syntactic). Multi-head attention runs **$h$ parallel attention heads**, each with its own learnable $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V$, allowing the model to attend to information from **different representation subspaces simultaneously** (e.g., one head tracks subject-verb agreement, another tracks coreference, another tracks positional proximity).

**Computational Equivalence (Key Insight for KTU):**
Although there are $h$ heads, the total parameter count and FLOPS are roughly equal to a single-head attention with full $d_{model}$ dimensionality — making multi-head attention essentially **free** in compute terms.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Transformer |
|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Original use case (Vaswani 2017): English↔German, English↔French WMT tasks |
| **LLMs (GPT, LLaMA)** | Decoder-only stacks generate text autoregressively |
| **BERT-family encoders** | Bidirectional context for classification, NER, search |
| **Vision Transformers (ViT)** | Image patches treated as tokens |
| **Speech (Whisper)** | Audio frames as tokens |
| **Code (Codex, Copilot)** | Code completion and synthesis |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

### Step 1 — Input Representation
Given an input sequence of $n$ tokens, embed each into a vector of dimension $d_{model}$. Stack into a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$.

### Step 2 — Learned Linear Projections
Multiply $X$ by three learnable weight matrices to produce queries, keys, and values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= X W^Q \quad \text{where } W^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k} \\
K &= X W^K \quad \text{where } W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k} \\
V &= X W^V \quad \text{where } W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* These three independent projections allow the model to **separate concerns** — the same token can act as a *search query* in one head and as *content to be retrieved* in another.

### Step 3 — Compute Raw Compatibility Scores
Take the matrix product $Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. Entry $(i,j)$ measures how much token $i$ should attend to token $j$.

$$
S = Q K^{\top}
$$

### Step 4 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$
Divide each entry by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$
\hat{S} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

*Logic:* Assuming $q_i, k_j$ have zero mean and unit variance with dimension $d_k$, their dot product has variance $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance, keeping softmax inputs in a numerically stable range (roughly $[-3, 3]$) where gradients are healthy.

### Step 5 — Optional Masking
For causal (decoder) attention, set $S_{ij} = -\infty$ for $j > i$ so post-softmax weights become zero:

$$
\hat{S}_{ij} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}, & j \leq i \\ -\infty, & j > i \end{cases}
$$

### Step 6 — Softmax Row-wise

$$
A = \text{softmax}(\hat{S}), \quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\hat{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{l=1}^{n} \exp(\hat{S}_{il})}
$$

### Step 7 — Weighted Sum of Values

$$
Z = A V \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

### Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Positional Encoding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Favorite:** Explain the sinusoidal positional encoding formula and prove that $PE_{pos+k}$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$.

The Transformer has **no built-in notion of order** — self-attention is permutation-equivariant. Positional encodings inject sequence order.

### Step 1 — Define the Frequency Schedule
For each dimension $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, d_{model}/2 - 1\}$, define a wavelength:

$$
\lambda_i = 2\pi \cdot 10000^{2i / d_{model}}
$$

### Step 2 — Define Sine (Even Dimensions) and Cosine (Odd Dimensions)

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} &= \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{\lambda_i / (2\pi)}\right) = \sin\!\left(pos \cdot 10000^{-2i/d_{model}}\right) \\
PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} &= \cos\!\left(pos \cdot 10000^{-2i/d_{model}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Proof of Relative-Position Linearity (KEY KTU STEP)

We want to show there exist matrices $M_k$ such that $PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos}$.

Using the angle-addition identities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{(pos+k,\, 2i)} &= \sin(\omega_i pos + \omega_i k) \\
&= \sin(\omega_i pos)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i pos)\sin(\omega_i k) \\
&= \cos(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} + \sin(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for the cosine component:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{(pos+k,\, 2i+1)} &= \cos(\omega_i pos + \omega_i k) \\
&= \cos(\omega_i pos)\cos(\omega_i k) - \sin(\omega_i pos)\sin(\omega_i k) \\
&= \cos(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} - \sin(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{(pos,\, 2i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\omega_i = 1 / 10000^{2i/d_{model}}$.

### Step 4 — Construct the Block-Diagonal Rotation Matrix

$$
M_k = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\omega_0 k) & \sin(\omega_0 k) & 0 & 0 & \cdots \\ -\sin(\omega_0 k) & \cos(\omega_0 k) & 0 & 0 & \cdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cos(\omega_1 k) & \sin(\omega_1 k) & \cdots \\ 0 & 0 & -\sin(\omega_1 k) & \cos(\omega_1 k) & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\;PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos}\;}
$$

**Engineering Significance:** Because $PE_{pos+k}$ is a **linear function** of $PE_{pos}$, the attention mechanism can learn to attend to **relative positions** easily, and the model can theoretically generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training.

---

## 3.3 Multi-Head Attention — Concat Math

For each head $i \in \{1, \dots, h\}$:

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\ K W_i^K,\ V W_i^V), \quad \text{head}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

Stack along the feature dimension:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O
$$

with $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h \cdot d_v \times d_{model}}$. Standard choice: $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$.

### Why Projections per Head?
Without per-head projections, every head would compute the **same attention pattern**. The independent projections let each head specialize in a different relational feature (e.g., syntactic vs. semantic).

---

## 3.4 Feed-Forward Sub-Layer

Each position passes through an **identical** (but parameter-shared across positions) two-layer MLP:

$$
\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\ x W_1 + b_1)\, W_2 + b_2
$$

with $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_{ff}}$, $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{ff} \times d_{model}}$, $d_{ff} = 2048$.

*Intuition:* Attention **mixes information across positions**, while FFN **mixes information across feature dimensions**. Together they cover both axes of representation.

---

## 3.5 Full Encoder Block (Analytical Recipe)

For input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(x + \text{MultiHeadAttention}(x, x, x)\right) \\
y &= \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(x' + \text{FFN}(x')\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The decoder block adds a **cross-attention** sublayer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(z_{\text{masked}} + \text{CrossAttention}(z_{\text{masked}}, \text{EncOut}, \text{EncOut})\right) \\
y &= \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(z + \text{FFN}(z)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Computational Complexity Comparison (KTU Board Favorite)

| Layer Type | Complexity (per layer) | Sequential Ops | Max Path Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-Attention** | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Recurrent (RNN)** | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Convolutional (CNN)** | $O(k \cdot n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log_k n)$ |

*Reading:* Self-attention is **quadratic in sequence length** but **constant-depth** in sequential ops — this is the architectural sweet spot that makes Transformers both powerful and parallelizable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Transformer Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputEmbed["INPUT TOKENS"] --> AddPos["ADD POSITIONAL ENCODING"]
    AddPos --> EncIn["ENCODER INPUT X"]
    EncIn --> MHSA["MULTI HEAD SELF ATTENTION"]
    MHSA --> AddNorm1["ADD AND LAYERNORM 1"]
    AddNorm1 --> FFN["FEED FORWARD NETWORK"]
    FFN --> AddNorm2["ADD AND LAYERNORM 2"]
    AddNorm2 --> EncOut["ENCODER OUTPUT"]
    EncOut --> XEnc["MEMORY KEYS AND VALUES"]

    DecIn["DECODER INPUT Y SHIFTED RIGHT"] --> DecEmb["DECODER EMBEDDING"]
    DecEmb --> DecPos["ADD POSITIONAL ENCODING"]
    DecPos --> MaskedAtt["MASKED MULTI HEAD SELF ATTENTION"]
    MaskedAtt --> DecNorm1["ADD AND LAYERNORM 1"]
    DecNorm1 --> CrossAtt["CROSS ATTENTION Q FROM DECODER K V FROM ENCODER"]
    CrossAtt --> DecNorm2["ADD AND LAYERNORM 2"]
    DecNorm2 --> DecFFN["FEED FORWARD NETWORK"]
    DecFFN --> DecNorm3["ADD AND LAYERNORM 3"]
    DecNorm3 --> Linear["LINEAR PROJECTION"]
    Linear --> Softmax["SOFTMAX OVER VOCAB"]
    Softmax --> Output["OUTPUT PROBABILITIES"]
```

## 4.2 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Internal Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q["QUERY MATRIX Q N X DK"] --> Mul1["MATRIX MULTIPLY"]
    K["KEY MATRIX K N X DK"] --> KT["TRANSPOSE K"]
    KT --> Mul1
    Mul1 --> Scale["DIVIDE BY SQRT DK"]
    Scale --> Mask["OPTIONAL MASK"]
    Mask --> Softmax["SOFTMAX ROW WISE"]
    Softmax --> MatMul["MATRIX MULTIPLY"]
    V["VALUE MATRIX V N X DV"] --> MatMul
    MatMul --> Out["OUTPUT Z N X DV"]
```

## 4.3 Multi-Head Attention — Parallel Heads Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InX["INPUT X"] --> ProjQ["LINEAR WQ"]
    InX --> ProjK["LINEAR WK"]
    InX --> ProjV["LINEAR WV"]
    ProjQ --> Split["SPLIT INTO H HEADS"]
    ProjK --> Split
    ProjV --> Split
    Split --> Head1["HEAD 1 ATTENTION"]
    Split --> Head2["HEAD 2 ATTENTION"]
    Split --> HeadH["HEAD H ATTENTION"]
    Head1 --> Concat["CONCATENATE ALONG FEATURE DIM"]
    Head2 --> Concat
    HeadH --> Concat
    Concat --> WO["LINEAR PROJECTION WO"]
    WO --> OutM["MULTI HEAD OUTPUT"]
```

## 4.4 Residual Connection & Layer Normalization Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Xin["SUBLAYER INPUT X"] --> Add["ADD"]
    SubOut["SUBLAYER OUTPUT"] --> Add
    Add --> LN["LAYER NORMALIZATION"]
    LN --> XinNext["NORMALIZED OUTPUT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Every sublayer (Multi-Head Attention, FFN, Cross-Attention) is wrapped in the *Add & LayerNorm* wrapper shown above. The "Add" creates the **residual/skip connection** that enables gradient flow across 100+ layers, and LayerNorm stabilizes activations per token.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define Self-Attention and explain how it differs from traditional RNN-based sequence modeling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Self-attention is a mechanism that allows each position in a sequence to attend to **all positions** in the same sequence by computing a weighted sum of value vectors, where the weights come from a softmax over scaled dot-products of query and key vectors.

**Key Differences from RNNs:**
| Aspect | RNN | Self-Attention |
|---|---|---|
| Sequential processing | Yes (token-by-token) | No (parallel over all tokens) |
| Long-range dependency path | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Parallelization on GPU | Limited | Full |
| Positional information | Inherent | Must be added explicitly |

*(Expected answer length: 8–10 lines. Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear comparison.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]**
**Why is the dot-product in attention scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$? What would happen if we omitted the scaling?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Assuming the components of $q$ and $k$ are independent random variables with zero mean and unit variance, their dot product has variance equal to $d_k$. As $d_k$ grows (typically 64–128), the dot product magnitudes grow, pushing the softmax into saturation regions where gradients are **extremely small** (vanishing gradient problem).

**Without scaling:** For large $d_k$, softmax outputs become near one-hot, gradients vanish, and training fails to converge. Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance, keeping softmax inputs in a numerically stable range.

*(Award 1 mark for variance argument, 1 mark for gradient/softmax explanation, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula. Start from the input embedding matrix $X$ and explain every step, including the role of the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Multi-Head Attention** mechanism. Why do we need multiple heads instead of one? Show the mathematical formulation and discuss how the per-head projection matrices enable subspace specialization. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. Start with input embeddings stacked as $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$. (0.5 mark)
2. Apply three learned projections to obtain $Q = XW^Q$, $K = XW^K$, $V = XW^V$, each $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$. (1 mark)
3. Compute raw scores via matrix product $S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. (1 mark)
4. **Variance argument:** Each entry of $S$ has variance $d_k$, so divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:
   $\hat{S} = QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k}$. (1.5 marks)
5. Apply row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights $A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\hat{S}_{ij})}{\sum_l \exp(\hat{S}_{il})}$. (1.5 marks)
6. Compute output $Z = AV \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$. (0.5 mark)
7. Final boxed formula:
   $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Multi-Head Attention (7 Marks):**

1. **Motivation:** A single attention head can only capture **one type of relationship**. (1 mark)
2. For each head $i = 1, \dots, h$, define separate projection matrices $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, where $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$. (1.5 marks)
3. Compute each head:
   $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$. (1.5 marks)
4. Concatenate all heads along the feature dimension and apply final projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d_{model}}$:
   $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)W^O$. (1.5 marks)
5. **Subspace specialization:** Different heads empirically learn different functions — syntactic relations, coreference, positional proximity, semantic similarity. (1 mark)
6. **Computational note:** Total FLOPS ≈ single-head with full $d_{model}$. (0.5 mark)

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **Transformer Encoder**. List all sub-layers, describe their functions, and draw a block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Sinusoidal Positional Encoding** in detail. Derive the formula and prove that $PE_{pos+k}$ is a linear function of $PE_{pos}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Encoder Architecture (7 Marks):**

The encoder consists of $N$ identical layers (typically $N=6$), each containing two sub-layers:

1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention sub-layer:** All queries, keys, values come from the same previous layer output. (1.5 marks)
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward sub-layer:** Two linear transformations with ReLU:
   $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$. (1.5 marks)

**Around each sub-layer:** Apply residual connection then LayerNorm:
$y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$. (1.5 marks)

**Additional components:**
- Input embeddings + positional encodings added to form encoder input. (1 mark)
- Output of final encoder layer passed to decoder via cross-attention. (1 mark)
- Block diagram should show: Input → Embed+PE → [MHA → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm] × N → Encoder Output. (0.5 mark)

**Part (b) — Positional Encoding (7 Marks):**

1. **Why needed:** Self-attention is permutation-equivariant — without PE, the model cannot distinguish *"dog bites man"* from *"man bites dog."* (1 mark)
2. **Formula:**
   $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$,
   $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$. (1 mark)
3. **Frequency schedule:** Each dimension has a different wavelength from $2\pi$ to $2\pi \cdot 10000$, enabling the model to attend to both fine and coarse positional patterns. (1 mark)
4. **Linearity proof (KEY STEP — award full marks only if derivation is shown):**
   Using angle-addition identities:
   $\sin(\omega_i(pos+k)) = \sin(\omega_i pos)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i pos)\sin(\omega_i k)$
   $= \cos(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{(pos,2i)} + \sin(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{(pos,2i+1)}$. (2 marks)
5. Similarly for cosine component. Construct the rotation matrix $M_k$. Conclude:
   $PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos}$. (1.5 marks)
6. **Significance:** Relative positions become linear combinations of absolute encodings, allowing length generalization. (0.5 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling:** Many students write $QK^{\top}V$ without scaling. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing self-attention with cross-attention:** Cross-attention takes Q from decoder and K, V from encoder. Self-attention uses the same source for all three. **−1.5 marks.**
> 3. **Skipping the linearity proof in PE:** KTU examiners specifically test the $PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos}$ derivation. A formula-only answer gets **at most 3/7 marks** for part (b) of Question B.
> 4. **Wrong masking direction:** In decoder self-attention, mask $j > i$ (future tokens), not the reverse. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Omitting LayerNorm/Residual in diagrams:** Every sub-layer block must show the Add & Norm wrapper. **−1 mark** per missing wrapper.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Transformers: 1-Page Rapid Revision**

- [ ] **Transformer = Encoder-Decoder stacks of self-attention + FFN blocks**, introduced by Vaswani et al. 2017 (*Attention Is All You Need*).
- [ ] **Three projections:** $Q = XW^Q$, $K = XW^K$, $V = XW^V$ — all learned.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- [ ] **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to fix variance explosion in softmax.
- [ ] **Multi-head:** $h$ parallel heads, each with its own $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V$, concatenated and projected via $W^O$.
- [ ] **Positional Encoding:** Sinusoidal, $PE_{(pos,2i)}=\sin$, $PE_{(pos,2i+1)}=\cos$, with $10000^{2i/d_{model}}$ frequency schedule.
- [ ] **Key PE property:** $PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos}$ (rotational linearity, learned relative position).
- [ ] **Residual + LayerNorm** wrap **every** sub-layer: $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$.
- [ ] **FFN:** $\max(0, xW_1+b_1)W_2 + b_2$ — same parameters shared across positions.
- [ ] **Causal mask** in decoder: set $S_{ij}=-\infty$ for $j > i$ before softmax.
- [ ] **Complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ per self-attention layer, $O(1)$ max path length.
- [ ] **LLM connection:** GPT/LLaMA = decoder-only Transformer stacks trained at massive scale.
- [ ] **Original hyperparameters:** $d_{model}=512$, $h=8$, $d_k=d_v=64$, $d_{ff}=2048$, $N=6$ enc + $N=6$ dec.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Data # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, **Data** refers to the massive, heterogeneous corpora of human-generated text (and increasingly multimodal signals) that serve as the *training substrate* from which a transformer-based neural network statistically learns the conditional probability distribution $P(\text{next token} \mid \text{previous tokens})$. The KTU 2024 scheme positions Data as the **foundational pillar of the LLM stack** — often summarized by the industry adage: *"the model is a compression of its training data."* Formally, a training corpus $\mathcal{D}$ for a causal language model is defined as: $$\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}), \dots, (x^{(N)})\}$$ where each $x^{(i)} = (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{L_i})$ is a sequence of discrete tokens, $N$ is the total number of training sequences, and $L_i$ is the length of the $i$-th sequence. The training objective minimizes the negative log-likelihood: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{L_i} \log P_\theta(t_j^{(i)} \mid t_{ [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT862 / Module 1):** Data is treated as the *first* component of the LLM lifecycle. The syllabus expects students to understand data sourcing, preprocessing, scaling laws, and quality — not merely "datasets." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are teaching a child to speak English. You do not give the child a *grammar rulebook*. Instead, you **immerse** the child in thousands of hours of natural conversation, books, and television. The child forms an internal statistical model of how words follow one another — what sounds right, what follows what, what is meaningful. **Data is the "immersion environment" of an LLM.** - A child raised only on sports commentary will speak like a sports commentator. - A child raised on poetry will speak poetically. - A child raised on toxic internet forums will adopt those patterns. Similarly, an LLM is a *reflection of its training data* — its strengths, blind spots, biases, knowledge, and even its failure modes are all encoded in $\mathcal{D}$. This is why the **quality, diversity, scale, and cleanliness** of data are arguably *more important* than the architecture itself in modern LLM research. ## 1.3 Taxonomy of LLM Data LLM training data is multi-tiered. The KTU syllabus groups it into the following canonical categories: | Tier | Category | Examples | Typical Scale | |------|----------|----------|---------------| | **Tier-1 (Generic Web)** | Web-crawled text | Common Crawl, C4, RefinedWeb | **Trillions of tokens** | | **Tier-2 (Curated Web)** | High-quality filtered web | FineWeb, RedPajama, Dolma | **Hundreds of billions** | | **Tier-3 (Curated Knowledge)** | Encyclopedic / reference | Wikipedia, Wikibooks, textbooks | **~10–20 billion tokens** | | **Tier-4 (Specialised)** | Code, math, scientific | GitHub, ArXiv, StackExchange | **~50–100 billion tokens** | | **Tier-5 (Instructional)** | Instruction-response pairs | FLAN, OpenAssistant, Alpaca | **~10–50 million pairs** | | **Tier-6 (Human Preference)** | Preference / alignment | HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback, HelpSteer | **~100K–1M samples** | > [!NOTE] > **Scale of Modern LLMs (as of 2024–2025):** > - **GPT-3 class:** $\approx 300$ billion tokens > - **LLaMA-3 class:** $\approx 15$ trillion tokens > - **Chinchilla optimal:** $D \approx 20 \times N_{\text{params}}$ tokens > - **Densing exponents (Kaplan 2020):** Loss $\propto N^{-0.076}$, Loss $\propto D^{-0.095}$ --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of LLM Data Modern LLM data engineering rests on **four fundamental pillars**. Mastery of these is critical for KTU board exam answers. ### Pillar 1: **Scale (Quantity)** More data generally improves model performance, following predictable **scaling laws**. The relationship between compute $C$, parameters $N$, and dataset size $D$ is approximately: $$C \approx 6 \, N \, D$$ (where 6 is the standard factor of forward+backward passes in transformer training). ### Pillar 2: **Quality (Curation)** Raw web data is **extremely noisy**. It contains: - HTML/CSS/JavaScript boilerplate - Spam, advertisements, navigation menus - Duplicated content - Toxic, hateful, or NSFW material - Low-information boilerplate (cookie banners, error pages) - Non-natural language (lorem ipsum, code-switching noise) Quality filters (heuristic + ML-based) typically **remove 60–90%** of raw crawled pages. ### Pillar 3: **Diversity (Coverage)** A model trained on English alone cannot speak Tamil, Malayalam, or Mandarin. A model trained only on news cannot write fiction. Data diversity ensures: - **Topical diversity** (news, science, dialogue, fiction, technical) - **Linguistic diversity** (multilingual coverage) - **Stylistic diversity** (formal, casual, narrative, instructional) ### Pillar 4: **Safety & Ethics** Data carries the **values, biases, and risks** of its producers. Key concerns: - **Demographic bias** (over-representation of certain dialects, regions, viewpoints) - **Copyright** (training on copyrighted books without permission) - **Privacy** (memorization of PII — personally identifiable information) - **Contamination** (test data leaking into training set, inflating benchmarks) ## 2.2 The Canonical Data Pipeline The end-to-end LLM data pipeline, in order, is: 1. **Raw Collection** → Web crawling (Common Crawl WARC files), licensed corpora, books (e.g., Books3 controversy), GitHub, ArXiv. 2. **Text Extraction** → WARC → WET (boilerplate stripped) → language detection (e.g., fastText langid). 3. **Quality Filtering** → Heuristic (length, word ratio, symbol-to-word ratio) + ML-based (e.g., GPT-2 quality classifier, FineWeb-Edu educational scorer). 4. **Deduplication** → Exact (SHA-256 hash) + fuzzy (MinHash, LSH) + semantic (embedding-based). 5. **PII / Toxicity Removal** → Regex + classifier-based scrubbing. 6. **Tokenization** → BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece, Unigram LM tokenizer. 7. **Mixing & Sampling** → Domain-weighted sampling (e.g., $\alpha$-weighted mixing). 8. **Packing & Batching** → Sequence packing to a fixed context length $L$ to maximize GPU utilization. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} \log P_\theta(t_i \mid t_{ [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for Tables:** In KTU answer sheets, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value notation, never the literal pipe character $\vert$ inside a markdown table cell. ## 2.4 Why Data Engineering Matters: Real-World Impact - **GPT-3 (2020):** Training on 570 GB of filtered Common Crawl produced dramatically better models than GPT-2's 40 GB. - **LLaMA-1 (2023):** Meta showed that **7B params trained on 1T tokens** outperformed much larger models — proving **data quality trumps parameter count**. - **Chinchilla (DeepMind, 2022):** A 70B model trained on **1.4T tokens** (20× params) beat a 280B model trained on 300B tokens — establishing the *Chinchilla law*. - **FineWeb-Edu (2024):** A 1.3T-token **educational subset** outperformed 5× larger unfiltered corpora on knowledge benchmarks. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Chinchilla Compute-Optimal Frontier The **Chinchilla scaling law** is one of the most tested concepts in KTU 2024 scheme assessments. We derive it from first principles. ### Step 1: Define the Loss as a Function of Parameters and Data Empirically (Hoffmann et al., DeepMind 2022), the cross-entropy loss of a transformer trained on $D$ tokens with $N$ parameters is well-fit by: $$L(N, D) = E + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + \frac{B}{D^{\beta}}$$ where: - $E$ is the **irreducible loss** (entropy of natural language), - $A, B$ are dataset-dependent constants, - $\alpha \approx 0.34, \quad \beta \approx 0.28$ are the **scaling exponents**. **Explanation:** The first term $E$ is the floor — even a perfect model cannot predict natural language below this entropy. The second term captures the *model capacity* gap, and the third captures the *data scarcity* gap. ### Step 2: Impose the Compute Constraint Training a transformer on $D$ tokens for one epoch costs approximately $6ND$ FLOPs (forward pass $\approx 2ND$, backward pass $\approx 4ND$). Therefore: $$C = 6ND \quad \Longrightarrow \quad D = \frac{C}{6N}$$ **Explanation:** We treat the total compute budget $C$ as fixed (determined by hardware and time). Substituting $D$ in terms of $N$ converts the 2-variable optimization into a 1-variable one. ### Step 3: Substitute and Differentiate Plug $D = C/(6N)$ into $L(N, D)$: $$L(N) = E + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + B \left(\frac{6N}{C}\right)^{\beta}$$ To find the optimal $N^*$ for a given $C$, take the derivative and set it to zero: $$\frac{dL}{dN} = -\frac{\alpha A}{N^{\alpha + 1}} + \frac{6\beta B \, N^{\beta - 1}}{C^{\beta}} = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{6\beta B \, N^{\beta - 1}}{C^{\beta}} = \frac{\alpha A}{N^{\alpha + 1}}$$ $$6\beta B \, N^{\beta - 1} \cdot N^{\alpha + 1} = \alpha A \, C^{\beta}$$ $$6\beta B \, N^{\alpha + \beta} = \alpha A \, C^{\beta}$$ $$\boxed{\,N^* = \left(\frac{\alpha A}{6\beta B}\right)^{\frac{1}{\alpha + \beta}} \cdot C^{\frac{\beta}{\alpha + \beta}}\,}$$ ### Step 4: Solve for the Optimal Data Substituting back: $$D^* = \frac{C}{6N^*}$$ With the empirical values $\alpha = 0.34, \beta = 0.28$: $$\frac{\alpha}{\beta} = \frac{0.34}{0.28} \approx 1.214$$ $$\frac{D^*}{N^*} = \frac{1}{6N^*} \cdot \frac{C}{N^*} \quad \text{(after algebra)} \quad \approx 20$$ $$\boxed{\,D_{\text{Chinchilla}}^{\text{optimal}} \approx 20 \times N_{\text{params}}\,}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** For a 70B-parameter model, the Chinchilla-optimal dataset is $\approx 1.4$ trillion tokens. This is the **"20 tokens per parameter" rule** that every KTU answer on scaling laws should mention. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Domain Mixing via Temperature Sampling Modern LLM training mixes data from $K$ domains with proportions $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_K$ where $\sum \alpha_i = 1$. The **temperature-$T$ resampling** formula controls the effective mixture ratio. ### Step 1: Original Domain Proportions Suppose a raw corpus has domain proportions $\alpha = (\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_K)$, where $\alpha_i$ is the fraction of tokens from domain $i$. ### Step 2: Apply Temperature Scaling Each proportion is raised to the power $1/T$: $$\hat{\alpha}_i^{(T)} = \frac{\alpha_i^{1/T}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \alpha_j^{1/T}}$$ **Explanation of the temperature parameter:** - $T \to 1$: No resampling — natural distribution preserved. - $T < 1$ (e.g., $T = 0.5$): **Sharpens** the distribution — high-resource domains dominate more. - $T > 1$ (e.g., $T = 2$): **Flattens** the distribution — low-resource domains get upweighted. ### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example Let $K = 3$ domains: Web ($\alpha_1 = 0.70$), Code ($\alpha_2 = 0.15$), Math ($\alpha_3 = 0.15$). Compute resampled proportions at $T = 0.5$: $$\alpha_1^{1/0.5} = 0.70^{2} = 0.49$$ $$\alpha_2^{1/0.5} = 0.15^{2} = 0.0225$$ $$\alpha_3^{1/0.5} = 0.15^{2} = 0.0225$$ $$\text{Sum} = 0.49 + 0.0225 + 0.0225 = 0.535$$ Therefore: $$\hat{\alpha}_1 = \frac{0.49}{0.535} \approx 0.916 \quad (91.6\%\ \text{Web})$$ $$\hat{\alpha}_2 = \frac{0.0225}{0.535} \approx 0.042 \quad (4.2\%\ \text{Code})$$ $$\hat{\alpha}_3 = \frac{0.0225}{0.535} \approx 0.042 \quad (4.2\%\ \text{Math})$$ **Interpretation:** A *low* temperature *further concentrates* mass on the already-dominant domain. LLM trainers use $T < 1$ to *amplify* high-quality domains like curated web, and $T > 1$ to *boost* low-resource domains like low-resource languages or code. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Open Datasets | Dataset | Year | Tokens | Key Innovation | Deduplication | License | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **C4** | 2020 | $\sim$750B | Cleaned CommonCrawl baseline | No (post-hoc) | ODC-BY | | **The Pile** | 2020 | $\sim$300B | 22 diverse sources | No | MIT | | **RedPajama-1T** | 2023 | $\sim$1.2T | LLaMA-1 reproduction | Optional | Apache 2.0 | | **Dolma** | 2024 | $\sim$3T | Public OLMo training data | Yes (MinHash) | ODC-BY | | **FineWeb** | 2024 | $\sim$15T | C4++ with aggressive filtering | Yes | ODC-BY | | **FineWeb-Edu** | 2024 | $\sim$1.3T | Educational-quality scored subset | Yes | ODC-BY | | **RedPajama-2** | 2024 | $\sim$30T | Massive, fully open | Yes | Apache 2.0 | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End LLM Data Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[RAW WEB CRAWL
CommonCrawl WARC] --> B[TEXT EXTRACTION
WARC to WET] C[LICENSED CORPUS
Books3 ArXiv GitHub] --> B B --> D[LANGUAGE IDENTIFICATION
fastText langid] D --> E[QUALITY FILTERING
Heuristic + ML classifier] E --> F[DEDUPLICATION
Exact hash + MinHash LSH] F --> G[PII SCRUBBING
Regex + NER removal] G --> H[TOKENIZATION
BPE SentencePiece] H --> I[DOMAIN MIXING
Temperature sampling] I --> J[PACKING + SHARDING
Fixed context length L] J --> K[TRAINING READY CORPUS D] K --> L[LLM TRAINING
Transformer] L --> M[FINAL LLM] ``` ## 4.2 Taxonomy of LLM Data Sources ```mermaid flowchart LR D[LLM TRAINING DATA] --> W[WEB TEXT] D --> B[BOOKS AND PAPERS] D --> C[CODE] D --> M[MULTILINGUAL] D --> X[CONVERSATIONAL] W --> W1[CommonCrawl] W --> W2[Wikipedia] W --> W3[News Archives] B --> B1[Books3] B --> B2[ArXiv] B --> B3[PubMed] C --> C1[GitHub] C --> C2[StackExchange] C --> C3[The Stack] M --> M1[MC4] M --> M2[CC100] M --> M3[ROOTS] X --> X1[ShareGPT] X --> X2[OpenAssistant] X --> X3[UltraChat] ``` ## 4.3 Data Quality Filtering — Two-Stage Funnel ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Raw Web Pages
~100 percent] --> Q[Stage 1 Heuristic Filter
Length, symbol ratio, lang] Q --> R[~30 percent retained] R --> S[Stage 2 ML Quality Scorer
Educational value classifier] S --> T[~10 percent high quality] T --> U[Final Curated Corpus] S --> V[~20 percent medium quality
used for diversity] V --> U ``` ## 4.4 Data–Parameter–Compute Triangle (Chinchilla Relationship) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[COMPUTE BUDGET C] -->|Allocated as| B[PARAMETERS N] A -->|Allocated as| C2[DATA D] B -->|20 to 1 ratio| C2 B --> D[MODEL CAPACITY] C2 --> E[KNOWLEDGE COVERAGE] D --> F[FINAL LOSS L] E --> F ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **Define "training data" in the context of Large Language Models. List any four major sources of LLM training data.** **Model Answer:** Training data $\mathcal{D}$ for an LLM is a large-scale corpus of tokenized text (and increasingly multimodal content) from which a transformer-based language model learns conditional probability distributions over token sequences by minimizing the cross-entropy loss: $$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log P_\theta(t \mid t_{ **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end pipeline of LLM training data preparation**, starting from raw web crawl to model-ready tokenized corpus. List **at least six distinct processing stages** and describe the role of each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Chinchilla scaling law** in detail. Derive the optimal data-to-parameter ratio from the empirical loss function $L(N, D) = E + A/N^{\alpha} + B/D^{\beta}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): **The LLM data preparation pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:** 1. **Raw Data Collection:** Aggregation from web (CommonCrawl WARC files), books, GitHub, Wikipedia, news, and licensed corpora. Outputs raw HTML/text in the petabyte range. 2. **Text Extraction & Boilerplate Removal:** Conversion of WARC → WET format. HTML tags, JavaScript, CSS, navigation menus, cookie banners, and ads are stripped. Trafilatura, jusText, and Resiliparse are common tools. 3. **Language Identification (LID):** fastText-based LID filters out pages not in target languages. Typically retains only the top $N$ languages with non-trivial representation. 4. **Quality Filtering:** Two-stage filtering: - *Heuristic filters:* document length, mean word length, symbol-to-word ratio, fraction of bullets, fraction of stopwords. - *ML-based filters:* GPT-2 perplexity threshold, educational-value classifier (FineWeb-Edu style), document coherence scorers. 5. **Deduplication:** Removal of exact (SHA-256) and near-duplicate (MinHash LSH, with $Jaccard \geq 0.8$ threshold) documents. Deduplication reduces memorization and contamination. 6. **PII & Toxicity Scrubbing:** Regex + NER-based removal of emails, phone numbers, SSNs, and other PII. Toxicity classifiers (Perspective API, Detoxify) flag and remove harmful content. 7. **Tokenization:** Conversion of text into subword token IDs via BPE, WordPiece, or Unigram LM tokenizers. Vocabulary size typically 32K–256K. 8. **Domain Mixing & Sequence Packing:** Weighted sampling across domains using temperature-$T$ resampling. Sequences are packed to a fixed context length $L$ (e.g., 4096, 8192) to maximize GPU throughput. **Final output:** A tokenized, packed, deduplicated training corpus $\mathcal{D}$ ready for consumption by a transformer trainer. #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Derivation of the Chinchilla law** (as derived in Section 3.1 above): 1. **Empirical loss function:** $$L(N, D) = E + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + \frac{B}{D^{\beta}}, \quad \alpha \approx 0.34,\ \beta \approx 0.28$$ 2. **Compute constraint:** $C = 6ND \Rightarrow D = C/(6N)$. 3. **Substitute and differentiate:** $$\frac{dL}{dN} = -\frac{\alpha A}{N^{\alpha+1}} + \frac{6\beta B N^{\beta-1}}{C^{\beta}} = 0$$ 4. **Solve for $N^*$ and $D^*$:** After algebraic manipulation, one obtains $N^* \propto C^{\beta/(\alpha+\beta)}$ and $D^* \propto C^{\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)}$. 5. **Take the ratio:** $$\frac{D^*}{N^*} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{N^*} \cdot \frac{C}{N^*}$$ Substituting the empirical constants and simplifying yields: $$\boxed{\,\frac{D^*}{N^*} \approx 20\,}$$ **Engineering takeaway:** Always train with approximately **20 tokens per parameter** for compute-optimal allocation. Going below wastes model capacity; going above wastes data and compute. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the **four pillars of LLM data engineering** — scale, quality, diversity, and safety. For each pillar, give one concrete technique used in modern LLM training pipelines. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With examples, explain the following data preprocessing techniques used in LLM corpora: (i) **MinHash LSH deduplication** (ii) **Quality classification using a small LM perplexity threshold** (iii) **Temperature-based domain mixing** **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): **The Four Pillars:** 1. **Scale (Quantity):** Modern LLMs are trained on **trillions of tokens** (LLaMA-3: 15T, RedPajama-2: 30T). Kaplan's scaling laws (2020) showed that loss decreases predictably as $D$ increases, following $L(D) \propto D^{-0.095}$. *Concrete technique:* **Chinchilla-optimal compute allocation** ($D \approx 20N$). 2. **Quality (Curation):** Raw web data is $\geq 90\%$ noise. Quality filtering removes low-information pages. *Concrete technique:* **Heuristic + ML filtering** — e.g., FineWeb-Edu uses a fine-tuned Snowflake-arctic-embed classifier to score educational value; pages below a threshold are discarded. 3. **Diversity (Coverage):** A model trained on a narrow domain (only news) fails at creative writing; a model trained on English only fails at Malayalam. Diversity must span **topics, languages, registers, and genres**. *Concrete technique:* **Multi-source aggregation** — combining CommonCrawl + GitHub + ArXiv + Wikipedia + Books + StackExchange to span web, code, science, encyclopedic, and dialogue data. 4. **Safety & Ethics:** Training data carries bias, copyright concerns, and PII. Without intervention, the model reproduces them. *Concrete technique:* **PII scrubbing + toxicity classification** — using regex + Named Entity Recognition (NER) to remove emails, phone numbers, and SSNs; using classifiers like Detoxify to filter hate speech and NSFW content. #### Model Solution — Part (b): **(i) MinHash LSH Deduplication:** Documents are converted to **shingles** (sets of $n$-grams, typically $n = 5$). Each document is represented by a **MinHash signature** of length $k$ (e.g., $k = 128$) — a compact fingerprint where similar documents produce similar signatures. **Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH)** then buckets similar signatures into the same hash bucket, enabling $O(1)$ lookup of near-duplicates with Jaccard similarity $\geq 0.8$. Documents in the same bucket are merged or one is removed. This technique, used in **Dolma, FineWeb, RedPajama-2**, removes $\sim$30–50% of near-duplicates that exact hashing misses. **(ii) Quality Classification via Perplexity Threshold:** A small reference language model (e.g., GPT-2 small, KenLM) is used to score each document by its **perplexity** $\text{PPL} = \exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_t \log P(t \mid t_{ [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data-Related Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing tokens with words.** Students frequently interchange *tokens* and *words*. Remember: 1 word ≈ 1.3–1.7 tokens (English), but **Malayalam has a much higher fertility (≈3.5 tokens/word)**. Marks lost for this slip. > > 2. **Writing "20 tokens per parameter" without context.** Always state: *"Chinchilla-optimal, compute-optimal allocation."* The number alone is insufficient. > > 3. **Forgetting the data pipeline is sequential, not parallel.** Deduplication must come *after* quality filtering, *before* tokenization. Random ordering loses 1–2 marks. > > 4. **Neglecting safety/ethics.** Even when not asked explicitly, a Data question in KTU expects at least *one sentence* on PII/toxicity/bias handling. This is the **most commonly missed** sub-point. > > 5. **Mismatched units in scaling law derivations.** Compute $C$ is in **FLOPs**, data $D$ is in **tokens**, parameters $N$ is dimensionless. A model with $N = 70$B and $D = 1.4$T satisfies $D \approx 20N$. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Data is the foundation of LLM training** — model behavior is bounded by corpus quality and diversity. - ✅ The four pillars of LLM data are **Scale, Quality, Diversity, and Safety**. - ✅ The canonical pipeline is: **Collect → Extract → LID → Filter → Deduplicate → Scrub → Tokenize → Mix → Pack**. - ✅ **Chinchilla law:** $D_{\text{opt}} \approx 20 \times N_{\text{params}}$ for compute-optimal training. - ✅ **Kaplan's exponents:** $L \propto N^{-0.076}$ and $L \propto D^{-0.095}$ — data has a slightly steeper exponent than parameters. - ✅ **Quality filters remove 60–90% of raw web data** — most crawled content is low-value. - ✅ **Deduplication uses MinHash + LSH** to catch near-duplicates missed by exact hashing. - ✅ **Temperature-$T$ resampling** controls the domain mixture: $T < 1$ sharpens, $T > 1$ flattens. - ✅ **Common Crawl** is the single largest source, but **Wikipedia + books + code** are critical for knowledge and reasoning. - ✅ **Multilingual data is challenging:** fertility varies widely across languages (English ≈ 1.4, Malayalam ≈ 3.5, Chinese ≈ 1.0 chars/token). - ✅ **Contamination** (benchmark leakage) can inflate evaluation scores by 10–30 percentage points — a critical concern. - ✅ Modern open datasets: **FineWeb (15T), FineWeb-Edu (1.3T), Dolma (3T), RedPajama-2 (30T)**. - ✅ **PII and toxicity scrubbing** are non-optional for responsible LLM development. - ✅ **Fertility = tokens/word; Compression Ratio = chars/token** — both are key tokenization efficiency metrics. - ✅ **Cross-Entropy Loss** is the LLM training objective; **Perplexity = exp(Loss)** is the standard evaluation metric.

topics.title: Pre-trained LLM Design Choices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pre-trained LLM Design Choices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Pre-trained LLM Design Choices* refer to the **deliberate architectural, data, objective, and compute hyperparameter decisions** made *before* the self-supervised pre-training phase of a Large Language Model. These decisions are **frozen at pre-training time** and govern the downstream capabilities, scaling behavior, context window, and alignment potential of the resulting model. They are distinct from fine-tuning or RLHF choices.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, the pre-training design space is partitioned into **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Tokenizer & Vocabulary Design** — how raw text becomes discrete integer IDs.
2. **Backbone Architecture** — the inductive bias of the network (decoder-only Transformer, encoder-only, encoder-decoder).
3. **Pre-training Objective** — the self-supervised loss that drives gradient updates.
4. **Data & Compute Schedule** — the Chinchilla/Kaplan scaling laws, data mixture, and learning rate schedule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECMT862 Module 1):**
> The course explicitly identifies *tokenization, position encoding, attention pattern, normalization, activation, and pre-training objective* as the canonical set of design choices. Mastery of the *trade-off matrix* between these choices is examinable.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Car Factory" Analogy

Imagine you are designing a **mass-production car** (your LLM) in a factory:

| Factory Decision | LLM Design Choice |
|---|---|
| Shape of the car's chassis | **Backbone architecture** (decoder-only Transformer) |
| Type of fuel it runs on | **Pre-training objective** (next-token prediction) |
| How fine-grained the fuel nozzle is | **Tokenizer** (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece) |
| How much total fuel and road-time you invest | **Compute budget & dataset size** |
| The size of the fuel tank | **Context window (sequence length)** |
| How the dashboard remembers gear position | **Position encoding** (RoPE, ALiBi, absolute) |
| Engine cooling system | **Normalization** (Pre-LN, RMSNorm) |

You make *all* of these decisions **on day zero**, before a single byte of training data has been seen. Changing any of them *after* pre-training is **catastrophically expensive** (it requires a fresh pre-training run costing millions of GPU-hours). This is why the design phase is the **single most consequential engineering decision** in the LLM lifecycle.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **standard benchmarks** universally cited in KTU-evaluated literature:
> - **FLOPs/token** ≈ **6 × N** (forward+backward), where *N* = non-embedding parameters.
> - **Chinchilla-optimal** ratio: **D ≈ 20 × N** tokens for *N* parameters.
> - **Context length** *L*: typically **2K → 128K → 1M** tokens across 2019 → 2024.
> - **Vocabulary size** *V*: typically **32K → 128K → 256K** subword pieces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Pre-training Design

### Pillar A — Tokenization Strategy

A tokenizer maps a Unicode string $s \in \Sigma^{\ast}$ to a sequence of integer IDs $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_L) \in \{1, \dots, V\}^{L}$. The three dominant families are:

- **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** — iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair. Used by GPT-2, GPT-3, LLaMA.
- **WordPiece** — greedy longest-match against a likelihood-based vocabulary. Used by BERT.
- **Unigram Language Model (SentencePiece)** — treats tokenization as a probabilistic segmentation and prunes tokens via EM. Used by T5, LLaMA-2, Mistral.
- **Byte-level BPE** — operates directly on UTF-8 bytes (256 base symbols) to guarantee **reversibility** and zero out-of-vocabulary tokens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked "Why is BPE preferred over word-level tokenization?", the answer must mention (1) **fixed vocabulary size** *V*, (2) **handling of OOV (out-of-vocabulary) tokens**, and (3) **compression ratio** = characters / tokens.

### Pillar B — Backbone Architecture

The modern consensus (post-2020) is the **decoder-only Transformer with causal (masked) self-attention**. The full attention mask $M_{ij} \in \{0, -\infty\}$ ensures position $i$ can only attend to positions $j \le i$:

$$
\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V
$$

where the mask $M$ is a strictly upper-triangular matrix of $-\infty$ values. This is in contrast to:
- **Encoder-only** (BERT-family): bidirectional, no causal mask → best for classification/embedding.
- **Encoder-decoder** (T5, BART): bidirectional encoder + autoregressive decoder → best for translation/summarization.

### Pillar C — Pre-training Objective

The dominant objective is **Causal Language Modeling (CLM)**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CLM}}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{L} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(x_t \mid x_{<t}\right)
$$

Alternatives include **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** (BERT, T5) and **Prefix/Span Corruption** (T5's "sentinel span" objective). For modern generative LLMs, CLM dominates because it unifies pre-training and inference-time generation.

### Pillar D — Position Encoding

Position encodings inject **order information** absent from the permutation-invariant attention mechanism:

- **Absolute position embeddings** (Vaswani et al., 2017): $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d})$.
- **RoPE (Rotary Position Embeddings)** — applied inside attention, multiplies $q$ and $k$ by a rotation matrix dependent on their relative offset. Enables **length extrapolation** via scaling factor $s$.
- **ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)** — adds a static linear penalty $-m \cdot (j-i)$ to attention scores. Strong length extrapolation.

### Pillar E — Compute & Data Schedule

The **Chinchilla scaling law** (Hoffmann et al., 2022) states that for a compute budget $C$ measured in FLOPs, the optimal $N$ and $D$ satisfy:

$$
N^{\ast}(C) = \left(\frac{C}{a}\right)^{\frac{1}{\alpha+1}}, \quad D^{\ast}(C) = \left(\frac{C}{b}\right)^{\frac{1}{\beta+1}}
$$

with empirically fitted constants giving the rule of thumb **$D \approx 20N$**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-portable**. Memorize the units in the rightmost column — KTU valuation often awards 1 mark for "stating units correctly".

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | Cross-entropy loss for CLM | nats per token |
| 2 | $C \approx 6 N D$ | Total pre-training compute (Kaplan) | FLOPs |
| 3 | $D^{\ast} \approx 20 N^{\ast}$ | Chinchilla-optimal tokens | tokens |
| 4 | $\text{FLOPs/token} \approx 6 N$ | Per-token forward+backward cost | FLOPs |
| 5 | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V)=\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k}+M)V$ | Scaled dot-product attention with mask $M$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{CR} = \vert s \vert_{\text{chars}} / \vert \mathbf{x} \vert$ | Tokenizer compression ratio | chars/token |
| 7 | $L_{\text{ctx}}$ | Maximum context length | tokens |
| 8 | $V$ | Vocabulary size | subword pieces |
| 9 | $\text{Perplexity} = \exp(\mathcal{L}/N_{\text{tok}})$ | Evaluation metric | dimensionless |
| 10 | $n_{\text{heads}} \cdot d_{\text{head}} = d_{\text{model}}$ | Multi-head split constraint | — |
