| 11 | $d_{\text{ffn}} \approx 4 \, d_{\text{model}}$ (SwiGLU) or $8/3 \, d_{\text{model}}$ | FFN expansion ratio | — |
| 12 | $\text{LR warmup: } \eta_t = \eta_{\max} \cdot \min(t/T_w,\, \text{decay})$ | Cosine warmup-decay schedule | learning rate |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design matrix directly determines **deployment characteristics**. A model pre-trained with a **2K context** (GPT-2) cannot legally accept a 100K-token prompt — extending it requires either *position interpolation* (extending RoPE) or *sliding-window attention* (Mistral). A model pre-trained on **code-heavy data** (CodeLLaMA, StarCoder) exhibits dramatically different zero-shot transfer than one trained on **web text** (LLaMA-1). In production, these choices become **non-negotiable contract terms** between the pre-training team and the downstream application team.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chinchilla Optimal Compute Allocation

**Goal:** Given a fixed compute budget $C$ (FLOPs), find the optimal $N$ (parameters) and $D$ (training tokens) that minimize the final pre-training loss $\mathcal{L}(N, D)$.

The empirical scaling law (Hoffmann et al., 2022) for transformer LM loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(N, D) = E + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + \frac{B}{D^{\beta}}
$$

with empirically fitted constants $A, B, E, \alpha, \beta$ (typically $A \approx 406.4$, $B \approx 410.7$, $\alpha \approx 0.34$, $\beta \approx 0.28$, $E \approx 1.69$).

**Step 1 — The compute constraint.** Kaplan et al. showed that total pre-training FLOPs scale as:

$$
C \approx 6 \, N \, D
$$

This is because each token requires one forward pass (≈ 2N FLOPs) and one backward pass (≈ 4N FLOPs) through the non-embedding parameters.

**Step 2 — Substitute the constraint into the loss.** Use the method of Lagrange multipliers. We minimize $\mathcal{L}(N, D)$ subject to $g(N, D) = 6 N D - C = 0$:

$$
\nabla \mathcal{L} = \lambda \nabla g
$$

Taking partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial N} = -\frac{\alpha A}{N^{\alpha+1}} = 6 \lambda D
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial D} = -\frac{\beta B}{D^{\beta+1}} = 6 \lambda N
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $\lambda$.** Divide the first equation by the second:

$$
\frac{\alpha A \, D^{\beta+1}}{N^{\alpha} \beta B \, N} = \frac{D}{N}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\frac{\alpha A}{\beta B} = \frac{N^{\alpha+1}}{D^{\beta+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D$ in terms of $N$.**

$$
D^{\beta+1} = \frac{\beta B}{\alpha A} \, N^{\alpha+1}
$$

$$
D = \left(\frac{\beta B}{\alpha A}\right)^{\frac{1}{\beta+1}} N^{\frac{\alpha+1}{\beta+1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug back into the compute constraint.**

$$
6 N \left(\frac{\beta B}{\alpha A}\right)^{\frac{1}{\beta+1}} N^{\frac{\alpha+1}{\beta+1}} = C
$$

$$
6 \left(\frac{\beta B}{\alpha A}\right)^{\frac{1}{\beta+1}} N^{1 + \frac{\alpha+1}{\beta+1}} = C
$$

Let $k = 1 + \frac{\alpha+1}{\beta+1} = \frac{\alpha + \beta + 2}{\beta + 1}$. With $\alpha=0.34$, $\beta=0.28$, we get $k \approx 1.64$. Therefore:

$$
N^{\ast}(C) = \left(\frac{C}{6}\right)^{\frac{1}{k}} \left(\frac{\alpha A}{\beta B}\right)^{\frac{1}{k(\beta+1)}}
$$

**Step 6 — The Chinchilla rule of thumb.** Substituting the fitted constants yields:

$$
D^{\ast} \approx 20 \, N^{\ast}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For a 70B model, you should train on ≈ **1.4 trillion tokens**. GPT-3 (175B) was trained on only 300B tokens — *under-trained* by Chinchilla standards. This is why LLaMA-1 (65B, 1.4T tokens) outperformed GPT-3 (175B, 300B tokens) on most benchmarks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RoPE Position Encoding

**Goal:** Inject relative position information into attention scores so that $\langle q_m, k_n \rangle$ depends only on the offset $(m - n)$ and not on absolute positions.

**Step 1 — Represent queries and keys as 2-D complex numbers.** For a single head of dimension $d$, pair dimensions into $d/2$ complex pairs:

$$
q_m^{(2i)} + i \, q_m^{(2i+1)} \quad \text{and} \quad k_n^{(2i)} + i \, k_n^{(2i+1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply position-dependent rotation.** Multiply each complex pair by $e^{i m \theta_i}$ where $\theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d}$:

$$
\tilde{q}_m^{(2i)} + i \tilde{q}_m^{(2i+1)} = (q_m^{(2i)} + i q_m^{(2i+1)}) \, e^{i m \theta_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the attention inner product.** The dot product becomes a complex inner product:

$$
\langle \tilde{q}_m, \tilde{k}_n \rangle = \text{Re}\!\left[\sum_{i=0}^{d/2-1} (q_m^{(2i)} + i q_m^{(2i+1)})(k_n^{(2i)} - i k_n^{(2i+1)}) \, e^{i (m-n)\theta_i}\right]
$$

The magnitude depends **only on $(m-n)$** — achieving the desired relative-position property. Length extrapolation is then achieved by **scaling the position index** $m \to m / s$ where $s$ is a base-rescaling factor (e.g., $s = 8$ for a 4× context extension).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Pre-training Design Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly exam-portable**. KTU frequently asks "Compare and contrast X and Y" (7-mark sub-parts). Memorize at least the **3 rightmost columns**.

| Design Axis | Option A | Option B | Key Trade-off | When to Choose A | When to Choose B |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tokenizer | BPE | WordPiece | BPE = frequency-based, WordPiece = likelihood-based | Multilingual, byte-level safety | Single-domain, low-resource |
| Architecture | Decoder-only | Encoder-decoder | Dec-only = simpler, unifies pre-train + inference | Open-ended generation, chat | Translation, span editing |
| Objective | CLM | MLM | CLM = natural generation, MLM = better representations | Generative LLM | Embedding / classification |
| Position enc. | RoPE | ALiBi | RoPE = better quality, ALiBi = better extrapolation | Quality-critical (≤32K) | Extreme long context (≥128K) |
| Normalization | Pre-LN | Post-LN | Pre-LN = stable training, Post-LN = better final loss | Deep models (≥24 layers) | Shallow models (≤12 layers) |
| Activation | SwiGLU | ReLU/GeLU | SwiGLU = +0.5-1.0 perplexity, ~50% more FLOPs | Quality-critical | Compute-constrained |
| Context length $L$ | 2K | 32K | $L \uparrow$ = quadratic attention cost $O(L^2 d)$ | Compute-constrained | Long-doc tasks |
| Data mixture | Web-heavy | Code-heavy | Web = general knowledge, Code = reasoning | General-purpose chat | Code completion |
| Compute strategy | Chinchilla (D=20N) | Kaplan (fixed D, scale N) | Chinchilla = compute-optimal, Kaplan = over-trains small models | Fixed budget | Comparison to baselines |
| LR schedule | Cosine | Linear | Cosine = smoother decay, Linear = simpler | Long runs (≥1T tokens) | Short runs / ablations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Pre-training Design Pipeline

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **end-to-end pre-training pipeline** and the **decision points** that must be resolved *before* the first training forward pass.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Text Corpus] --> A1[Tokenizer Selection]
    A1 --> A1a[BPE]
    A1 --> A1b[WordPiece]
    A1 --> A1c[SentencePiece Unigram]
    A1b --> A2[Vocabulary V of size 32000 to 256000]
    A1a --> A2
    A1c --> A2
    A2 --> A3[Integer Token Stream]
    A3 --> A4[Architecture Selection]
    A4 --> A4a[Decoder only]
    A4 --> A4b[Encoder only]
    A4 --> A4c[Encoder Decoder]
    A4b --> A5[Position Encoding]
    A4a --> A5
    A4c --> A5
    A5 --> A5a[Absolute PE]
    A5 --> A5b[RoPE]
    A5 --> A5c[ALiBi]
    A5c --> A6[Pre-training Objective]
    A5a --> A6
    A5b --> A6
    A6 --> A6a[CLM Causal LM]
    A6 --> A6b[MLM Masked LM]
    A6 --> A6c[Span Corruption]
    A6c --> A7[Data Mixture and Filtering]
    A6a --> A7
    A6b --> A7
    A7 --> A7a[Web 60 percent]
    A7 --> A7b[Code 15 percent]
    A7 --> A7c[Books 10 percent]
    A7 --> A7d[Scientific 5 percent]
    A7 --> A7e[Multilingual 10 percent]
    A7e --> A8[Compute Budget and Chinchilla Schedule]
    A8 --> A8a[Optimal N for budget C]
    A8 --> A8b[Optimal D equals 20N]
    A8b --> A9[Training Run: Forward Backward Optimizer Step]
    A9 --> A10[Pre-trained LLM: Frozen Base Weights]
    A10 --> A11[Downstream SFT or RLHF]
    A10 --> A11a[In Context Learning]
    A10 --> A11b[Prompt Engineering]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A1 → A2** is the **tokenization decision** (irreversible).
- **A4 → A5 → A6** form the **architectural stack** and are coupled.
- **A8** is the **Chinchilla optimization** — the *only* place where the compute budget is converted to *N* and *D*.
- **A10** is the **handoff boundary** between Module 1 (pre-training) and Module 2 (SFT/RLHF).

## 4.2 Architecture Comparison — Side-by-Side Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DecoderOnly[Decoder only LLaMA GPT]
        D1[Input IDs] --> D2[Token Embedding] --> D3[Add RoPE] --> D4[Causal Masked MHA] --> D5[RMSNorm] --> D6[SwiGLU FFN] --> D7[N stacked layers] --> D8[LM Head] --> D9[Next Token Logits]
    end
    subgraph EncoderDecoder[Encoder Decoder T5 BART]
        E1[Input IDs] --> E2[Encoder Embedding] --> E3[Bidirectional MHA] --> E4[Encoder Output KV]
        E4 --> E5[Decoder with Cross Attention] --> E6[LM Head] --> E7[Output Logits]
    end
    subgraph EncoderOnly[Encoder only BERT]
        B1[Input IDs] --> B2[Token + Segment Embed] --> B3[Absolute PE] --> B4[Bidirectional MHA] --> B5[MLM Head] --> B6[Masked Token Logits]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation for KTU exams:** Decoder-only models (left) have *no cross-attention* and a *single embedding table* shared between input and output. Encoder-decoder models (middle) have **two separate embedding tables** and a **cross-attention bridge** in the decoder.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, PECMT862, Module 1, 2024 Scheme]**

### Q1. List and briefly explain **any three** pre-training design choices that must be fixed *before* the first training step of an LLM. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Tokenizer & Vocabulary** — The choice of BPE, WordPiece, or Unigram tokenizer, and the vocabulary size $V$ (typically 32K–256K). This decision is irreversible and determines the sequence length $L$ for a given text.
2. **Backbone Architecture** — Decoder-only Transformer with $N$ layers, $d_{\text{model}}$ hidden dim, $h$ attention heads, and FFN expansion ratio. Determines FLOPs/token.
3. **Pre-training Objective** — Causal Language Modeling (CLM) is the de-facto standard for generative LLMs; alternatives are MLM and Span Corruption.
4. **Position Encoding** — Absolute, RoPE, or ALiBi. Affects length extrapolation capability.
5. **Data Mixture** — The percentage of web, code, books, multilingual, etc.

*(Any three well-explained → 3 marks; one-line answers → partial marks.)*

---

### Q2. State the **Chinchilla scaling law** and write the optimal data-to-parameter ratio $D^{\ast} / N^{\ast}$. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Chinchilla scaling law minimizes the pre-training loss

$$
\mathcal{L}(N, D) = E + \frac{A}{N^{\alpha}} + \frac{B}{D^{\beta}}
$$

subject to a fixed compute budget $C \approx 6ND$ FLOPs. The optimal allocation is:

$$
\frac{D^{\ast}}{N^{\ast}} \approx 20
$$

That is, a model with $N$ parameters should be trained on approximately **$20N$ tokens**. For example, a 7B model should ideally see ~140B tokens.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, PECMT862, Module 1, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Design Choices & Tokenization Deep Dive*

**(a) Compare BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece tokenizers. Discuss how vocabulary size $V$ affects the compression ratio and downstream model performance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | BPE | WordPiece | SentencePiece (Unigram) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Training | Greedy pair-frequency merge | Greedy likelihood-based merge | EM over a unigram LM, then prune |
| Probabilistic? | No | Implicit (likelihood) | Yes |
| Language | Any | Originally for Japanese/Korean | Any, language-agnostic |
| Reversible? | Yes (byte-level) | Yes | Yes |
| Used in | GPT-2/3, LLaMA | BERT | T5, LLaMA-2, Mistral |

**Effect of $V$ on compression ratio (CR):** CR $\uparrow$ as $V \uparrow$ (more pieces cover text in fewer tokens). However, the **embedding table size** = $V \cdot d_{\text{model}}$, so $V \uparrow$ increases parameter count and softmax cost. The **optimal $V$** is empirically around **32K–64K** for English and **128K–256K** for multilingual models (e.g., BLOOM uses 250K).

**Effect on downstream performance:** A too-small $V$ (e.g., 8K) causes frequent subword splitting of common words → longer sequences → more compute. A too-large $V$ (e.g., 500K) makes the embedding table dominate parameter count and hurts rare-token generalization.

**(b) Derive the Chinchilla-optimal $D^{\ast}$ and $N^{\ast}$ for a fixed compute budget $C$, stating all assumptions. Show that the optimal ratio is $D^{\ast} \approx 20 N^{\ast}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:** See the **derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Empirical loss model: $\mathcal{L}(N, D) = E + A/N^{\alpha} + B/D^{\beta}$.
2. Compute constraint: $C = 6ND$.
3. Lagrange: $-\alpha A / N^{\alpha+1} = 6\lambda D$ and $-\beta B / D^{\beta+1} = 6\lambda N$.
4. Divide and solve: $D^{\beta+1} = (\beta B / \alpha A) N^{\alpha+1}$.
5. Substitute into $C = 6ND$ to get $N^{\ast}(C)$.
6. Plug fitted constants $A \approx 406.4$, $B \approx 410.7$, $\alpha \approx 0.34$, $\beta \approx 0.28$ to obtain the numerical ratio:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{D^{\ast}}{N^{\ast}} \approx 20}
$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Transformer architecture with $C \approx 6ND$ FLOPs; (ii) loss is decomposable as above; (iii) no data-quality bottleneck; (iv) optimal LR schedule.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Architecture & Position Encoding Deep Dive*

**(a) Compare decoder-only, encoder-only, and encoder-decoder Transformer architectures for pre-training an LLM. Justify why modern generative LLMs (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral) adopt the decoder-only design. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Encoder-only | Encoder-Decoder | Decoder-only |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attention | Bidirectional | Enc: bidir; Dec: causal + cross | Causal (masked) only |
| Pre-training | MLM | Span Corruption | CLM |
| Best for | Classification, embeddings | Translation, summarization | Open-ended generation |
| Parameters | Single stack | Two stacks + cross-attn | Single stack |
| Inefficiency at inference | N/A | Re-encodes input | Unifies pre-train + inference |

**Why decoder-only dominates for generative LLMs (2020+):**

1. **Unification of pre-training and inference** — the same forward pass used during training is used at inference; no encoder/decoder split.
2. **Empirical scaling** — Kaplan & Chinchilla scaling laws were demonstrated on decoder-only models, and the recipes transfer well.
3. **In-context learning** — decoder-only models naturally support few-shot prompting because the causal mask lets them condition on the full prompt.
4. **Engineering simplicity** — one embedding table, no cross-attention, easier to scale across thousands of GPUs.

**(b) Explain **Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)**. Show mathematically that the attention score $\langle \tilde{q}_m, \tilde{k}_n \rangle$ depends only on the relative offset $(m - n)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:** See the **derivation in Section 3.2** of these notes. The key points:

1. Pair every two consecutive dimensions $2i, 2i+1$ into a complex number.
2. Rotate by angle $m\theta_i$ where $\theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d}$:

$$
\tilde{q}_m = q_m \odot e^{i m \theta}, \quad \tilde{k}_n = k_n \odot e^{i n \theta}
$$

3. The dot product $\text{Re}\langle \tilde{q}_m, \tilde{k}_n \rangle$ then equals $\text{Re}\langle q_m, k_n e^{i(n-m)\theta} \rangle$, a function of $(m-n)$ only.

**Length extrapolation** is achieved by **Position Interpolation (PI)**: replace $m \to m/s$ for a base-rescaling factor $s$ (typically $s = L_{\text{new}} / L_{\text{old}}$). This compresses the rotation angles back into the model's trained range.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $N$ (parameters) with $L$ (layers).** A 70B model does not mean 70 billion layers — it means ≈ 70 billion *total parameters* across ≈ 80 layers.
> 2. **Stating the Chinchilla ratio without units or context.** Always write "$D^{\ast} \approx 20N$ **tokens per parameter**" — KTU awards marks for specifying *tokens*.
> 3. **Forgetting the $6$ in $C = 6ND$.** The factor accounts for forward (2N) + backward (4N) FLOPs per token. Writing $C = ND$ is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Writing BPE = "Byte Pair Encoding" but not explaining the *iterative merge* step.** A 1-line definition loses 1 mark vs. a 2-line definition with the merge algorithm.
> 5. **Drawing RoPE as an "addition" to embeddings.** RoPE is a **multiplication** (rotation), not an addition. Mistaking this loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing *pre-training* with *fine-tuning*.** Module 1 is exclusively pre-training design — do not invoke LoRA, QLoRA, or RLHF in answers.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Pre-trained LLM Design Choices):**

- ☐ **Tokenizer family**: BPE (GPT, LLaMA), WordPiece (BERT), SentencePiece/Unigram (T5, Mistral). Vocabulary $V$ is **fixed at pre-training time**.
- ☐ **Compression Ratio (CR)** = chars ÷ tokens. Higher $V$ ⇒ higher CR but bigger embedding table.
- ☐ **Architecture**: decoder-only is the modern default for generative LLMs; encoder-decoder for translation; encoder-only for embeddings.
- ☐ **Pre-training Objective**: CLM $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ for generative LLMs; MLM / Span Corruption for others.
- ☐ **Position Encoding**: Absolute (sinusoidal), RoPE (rotary, relative), ALiBi (linear bias). RoPE enables **length extrapolation** via Position Interpolation.
- ☐ **Compute formula**: $C \approx 6ND$ FLOPs. **Chinchilla-optimal**: $D^{\ast} \approx 20N^{\ast}$.
- ☐ **Scaling law**: $\mathcal{L}(N, D) = E + A/N^{\alpha} + B/D^{\beta}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.34$, $\beta \approx 0.28$.
- ☐ **Normalization**: Pre-LN (stable, default in modern LLMs) vs. Post-LN (original Transformer).
- ☐ **Activation**: SwiGLU > GeLU > ReLU in quality; SwiGLU is the LLaMA / Mistral default.
- ☐ **Data mixture**: typically Web 60–80%, Code 5–20%, Books 5–10%, Multilingual 5–10%, Scientific 2–5%.
- ☐ **Multi-head split**: $n_{\text{heads}} \cdot d_{\text{head}} = d_{\text{model}}$.
- ☐ **FFN expansion**: $d_{\text{ffn}} \approx 4 d_{\text{model}}$ (GeLU) or $\tfrac{8}{3} d_{\text{model}}$ (SwiGLU).
- ☐ **Evaluation metric**: $\text{Perplexity} = \exp(\mathcal{L}/N_{\text{tok}})$.
- ☐ **Module 1 boundary**: pre-training ends at the **frozen base model**. SFT, RLHF, and prompt engineering are **Module 2+** topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commonly Used Pre-trained LLMs (BERT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commonly Used Pre-trained LLMs (BERT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained deep learning language representation model introduced by Devlin et al. (Google, 2018) that is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a KTU 2024 syllabus mandated foundational model, BERT belongs to the family of **encoder-only Transformer architectures** and forms the backbone of countless downstream Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks such as Question Answering, Sentiment Classification, Named Entity Recognition, and Textual Entailment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** BERT is a *bidirectional* model. Unlike traditional unidirectional language models (e.g., GPT uses left-to-right; ELMo uses shallow concatenation of left-to-right and right-to-left), BERT reads the entire sequence simultaneously in both directions using a **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** objective.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning Paradigm:** BERT is first *pre-trained* on a massive unlabeled corpus (Wikipedia + BookCorpus = ~3.3 Billion words) using two unsupervised tasks, and then *fine-tuned* with just one additional output layer for specific downstream supervised tasks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a student preparing for an examination by reading a textbook **back and forth simultaneously** rather than reading it line-by-line. BERT operates exactly like this student:

- A traditional left-to-right model is like reading a novel strictly from chapter 1 to the last page. You understand each sentence using only the words before it.
- A bidirectional model like BERT is like a brilliant student who, upon encountering the word *“bank”*, instantly looks at the surrounding sentences, the previous chapter, and the next chapter simultaneously to decide whether *“bank”* means a *river bank* or a *financial bank*.

This **deep bidirectionality** is the central innovation of BERT, achieved by masking ~15% of the input tokens and asking the model to predict them using **both** left and right context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (BERT-Base):**
> - **Layers (L) = 12**
> - **Hidden Size (H) = 768**
> - **Attention Heads (A) = 12**
> - **Total Parameters ≈ 110 Million**
> - **Training Corpus Size ≈ 3.3 Billion words**

### 1.3 Variants of BERT

| Variant | Layers | Hidden | Heads | Parameters | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BERT-Base** | 12 | 768 | 12 | 110M | Standard NLP tasks |
| **BERT-Large** | 24 | 1024 | 16 | 340M | High-accuracy benchmarks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Foundation

BERT's architecture is essentially the **encoder stack of the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017)**. It discards the decoder and focuses purely on building rich contextual representations.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Input Tokenization:** The raw text is split into **WordPiece subword tokens** (vocabulary size ≈ 30,000). This handles out-of-vocabulary words gracefully by breaking them into frequent subword units (e.g., *“playing” → “play” + “##ing”*).
2. **Embedding Layer:** Three embeddings are summed element-wise:
   - **Token Embeddings:** Represents the meaning of each token.
   - **Segment Embeddings:** Distinguishes between sentence A and sentence B (used for NSP).
   - **Position Embeddings:** Provides sequence order information (learned, not sinusoidal in BERT).
3. **Special Tokens Insertion:**
   - **[CLS]** — added at the very beginning; its final hidden state serves as the pooled sequence representation for classification.
   - **[SEP]** — separates two input sentences.
4. **Stacked Transformer Encoder Blocks:** Each block contains a **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer and a **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**, both wrapped with **residual connections** and **Layer Normalization**.
5. **Pre-training Head:** A simple classification head is added on top during pre-training and swapped for task-specific heads during fine-tuning.

### 2.2 Pre-training Objectives (The Heart of BERT)

BERT is pre-trained using two unsupervised tasks simultaneously:

#### (a) Masked Language Modeling (MLM)
- **15% of the input tokens are randomly selected** and replaced. Of those selected:
  - **80%** are replaced with the `[MASK]` token.
  - **10%** are replaced with a random token (helps reduce the train-test mismatch where `[MASK]` never appears in fine-tuning).
  - **10%** are left unchanged (stabilizes training).
- The model is trained to predict the **original vocabulary ID** of the masked token using only its final hidden state, passed through a softmax output layer over the vocabulary.

#### (b) Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)
- For each pre-training example, sentence A and sentence B are chosen such that:
  - **50%** of the time, B is the *actual* next sentence (label = `IsNext`).
  - **50%** of the time, B is a *random* sentence from the corpus (label = `NotNext`).
- The `[CLS]` token's final hidden state is fed into a binary classifier.
- This teaches BERT to model **sentence-level relationships**, useful for Question Answering and Natural Language Inference.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Component

| Component | Why It Exists | How It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Bidirectional Self-Attention** | Captures context from both directions simultaneously | Every token attends to every other token in the same sequence |
| **MLM Objective** | Forces deep bidirectional context (left-to-right models cannot do this) | Mask tokens and predict them using full surrounding context |
| **NSP Objective** | Enables sentence-pair tasks (QA, NLI) | Binary classification of [CLS] representation |
| **WordPiece Tokenization** | Handles rare/OOV words efficiently | Greedy longest-match-first subword segmentation |
| **Sum of 3 Embeddings** | Combines lexical, positional, and segmental information | Element-wise addition of same-dimension vectors |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Purpose / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core attention computation; $d_k$ is key dimension |
| 2 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^{O}$ | $h$ = 12 (Base) or 16 (Large) |
| 3 | Single Attention Head | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^{Q}, KW_i^{K}, VW_i^{V})$ | $W_i$ are learnable projection matrices |
| 4 | Position-wise FFN | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Applied independently to each position |
| 5 | BERT Composite Loss | $L_{\text{BERT}} = L_{\text{MLM}} + L_{\text{NSP}}$ | Joint optimization of both pre-training tasks |
| 6 | MLM Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P(x_i \mid x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}})$ | $\mathcal{M}$ = set of masked positions |
| 7 | Layer Normalization | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \cdot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Stabilizes training across features |
| 8 | BERT-Base Param Count | $L \times (12 d^2_{\text{model}} + 12 d_{\text{model}} \cdot d_{\text{ff}})$ | $\approx 110 \times 10^6$ parameters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** BERT powers Google's search query understanding (since 2019), medical clinical note analysis, legal document summarization, and is the foundation of domain-specific models like **BioBERT** (biomedical), **SciBERT** (scientific), **FinBERT** (financial), and **DistilBERT** (compressed, 60% smaller, 60% faster).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Scaled Dot-Product Attention

The attention mechanism in BERT is mathematically identical to the Transformer's encoder. We derive it step-by-step below.

**Starting point:** Given a sequence of token embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$, where $n$ is the sequence length and $d_{\text{model}} = 768$ (BERT-Base), we project them into three matrices.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= X W^{Q} \\
K &= X W^{K} \\
V &= X W^{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute raw similarity scores** between every query and every key using matrix multiplication:

$$
S = Q K^{\top}, \quad S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

**Step 2 — Address the exploding gradient problem.** When $d_k$ (the dimension of keys) is large, the dot products grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients. We **scale** by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$
S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert scores to a probability distribution** across the sequence dimension using softmax (row-wise):

$$
A = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right), \quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{\text{scaled},ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S_{\text{scaled},ik})}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply attention weights to Values** to produce the context-aware output:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A V = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Final consolidated formula:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of BERT-Base Parameter Count

We compute the parameters of one Transformer encoder block (excluding embeddings).

**Component 1 — Multi-Head Self-Attention projections:**
There are four matrices: $W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O$, each of shape $d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}$.

$$
P_{\text{attn}} = 4 \times d_{\text{model}}^2 = 4 \times 768^2 = 4 \times 589{,}824 = 2{,}359{,}296
$$

**Component 2 — Feed-Forward Network (two linear layers):**
BERT's FFN expands to $d_{\text{ff}} = 4 \times d_{\text{model}} = 3072$ and projects back.

$$
P_{\text{FFN}} = d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{ff}} + d_{\text{ff}} \times d_{\text{model}} = 768 \times 3072 + 3072 \times 768 = 2 \times 2{,}359{,}296 = 4{,}718{,}592
$$

**Component 3 — Total per layer (ignoring LayerNorm and bias terms):**

$$
P_{\text{layer}} = P_{\text{attn}} + P_{\text{FFN}} = 2{,}359{,}296 + 4{,}718{,}592 = 7{,}077{,}888
$$

**Component 4 — Multiply by number of layers (L = 12 for BERT-Base):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{encoder}} &= 12 \times 7{,}077{,}888 = 84{,}934{,}656 \\
P_{\text{embed}} &= V \times d_{\text{model}} \approx 30{,}000 \times 768 = 23{,}040{,}000 \\
P_{\text{total}} &\approx 84.9\text{M} + 23.0\text{M} \approx 108\text{M} \approx \mathbf{110\text{M parameters}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the published **110M** parameter count for BERT-Base, validating the architecture.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BERT vs. Other Pre-trained Models

| Feature | BERT | GPT (Original) | ELMo | RoBERTa |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Directionality** | Deep Bidirectional | Left-to-Right | Shallow Bidirectional (concat) | Deep Bidirectional |
| **Architecture** | Encoder-only | Decoder-only | Bi-LSTM | Encoder-only (BERT optimized) |
| **Pre-training Task** | MLM + NSP | Causal LM | Bidirectional LM | MLM only (no NSP) |
| **Attention Mask** | Bidirectional (full) | Causal (lower triangular) | N/A (LSTM) | Bidirectional (full) |
| **Best For** | Understanding tasks | Generation tasks | Feature extraction | Strong understanding baseline |
| **Tokenizer** | WordPiece | BPE | Character-level | BPE |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Input Representation

Suppose the input is: `[CLS] my dog is cute [SEP] he likes play ##ing [SEP]`

| Token | [CLS] | my | dog | is | cute | [SEP] | he | likes | play | ##ing | [SEP] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Token Embedding** | $E_{[CLS]}$ | $E_{my}$ | $E_{dog}$ | $E_{is}$ | $E_{cute}$ | $E_{[SEP]}$ | $E_{he}$ | $E_{likes}$ | $E_{play}$ | $E_{\#\#ing}$ | $E_{[SEP]}$ |
| **Segment Embedding** | $E_A$ | $E_A$ | $E_A$ | $E_A$ | $E_A$ | $E_A$ | $E_B$ | $E_B$ | $E_B$ | $E_B$ | $E_B$ |
| **Position Embedding** | $E_0$ | $E_1$ | $E_2$ | $E_3$ | $E_4$ | $E_5$ | $E_6$ | $E_7$ | $E_8$ | $E_9$ | $E_{10}$ |

The final input to the first encoder layer is the **element-wise sum** of all three embeddings for each position.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level BERT Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Input] --> B[WordPiece Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Token Embeddings]
    B --> D[Segment Embeddings]
    B --> E[Position Embeddings]
    C --> F[Element-wise Sum]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Transformer Encoder Layer 1]
    G --> H[Transformer Encoder Layer 2]
    H --> I[Dot Dot Dot]
    I --> J[Transformer Encoder Layer 12]
    J --> K[Output Representations per Token]
    J --> L[CLS Pooled Output]
    K --> M[Task Specific Head]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Predictions]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Pre-training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Input Sentence A and B] --> special[Add CLS and SEP Tokens]
    special --> emb[Sum of 3 Embeddings]
    emb --> encoder[12 Layer BERT Encoder]
    encoder --> clsout[CLS Hidden State]
    encoder --> tokout[All Token Hidden States]
    clsout --> nsp[NSP Classifier Head]
    nsp --> nsppred[IsNext or NotNext]
    tokout --> mlm[MLM Prediction Head]
    mlm --> mlmpred[Original Token at Masked Positions]
```

### 4.3 Single Transformer Encoder Block Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in[Input X] --> attn[Multi Head Self Attention]
    attn --> add1[Add and Norm 1]
    in --> add1
    add1 --> ffn[Feed Forward Network]
    ffn --> add2[Add and Norm 2]
    add1 --> add2
    add2 --> out[Output to Next Layer]
```

### 4.4 Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pretraining Phase] --> A1[Unlabeled Wikipedia and BookCorpus]
    A1 --> A2[MLM plus NSP Objectives]
    A2 --> A3[Trained BERT Weights]

    A3 --> B[Fine Tuning Phase]
    B --> B1[Labeled Task Data]
    B1 --> B2[Add Task Specific Output Layer]
    B2 --> B3[Update All Parameters with Low LR]
    B3 --> C[Task Ready Model]
```

### 4.5 Masked Language Modeling Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Orig[Original Sentence] --> Sel[Randomly Select 15 percent of Tokens]
    Sel --> Dist[Distribute Replacements]
    Dist --> P1[80 percent replaced by MASK]
    Dist --> P2[10 percent replaced by Random Token]
    Dist --> P3[10 percent kept Unchanged]
    P1 --> Feed[Feed into BERT]
    P2 --> Feed
    P3 --> Feed
    Feed --> Pred[Predict Original Token using Softmax over Vocabulary]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define BERT. Explain in brief the two pre-training objectives used in BERT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained deep language representation model based on the encoder portion of the Transformer architecture. It is designed to pre-train deep **bidirectional** representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all its layers.

The two pre-training objectives are:

1. **Masked Language Modeling (MLM):** Approximately 15% of the input tokens are randomly masked, and the model is trained to predict the original token at those masked positions using the full bidirectional context.
2. **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):** Given a pair of sentences A and B, the model predicts whether B is the actual next sentence following A in the original corpus (binary classification using the `[CLS]` token's final representation).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*List the three types of input embeddings used in BERT and state the purpose of the `[CLS]` token.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

BERT uses **three input embeddings** which are summed element-wise to form the final input representation:

1. **Token Embeddings** — represent the lexical meaning of each WordPiece token.
2. **Segment Embeddings** — distinguish between sentence A and sentence B in sentence-pair tasks.
3. **Position Embeddings** — encode the absolute position of each token in the sequence.

The **`[CLS]`** token is a special classification token added at the beginning of every input sequence. Its final hidden state from the last encoder layer is used as the **pooled aggregate representation** of the entire sequence, primarily for classification tasks (such as NSP during pre-training and sentence classification during fine-tuning).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of BERT in detail with a neat block diagram. Compare BERT-Base and BERT-Large configurations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

BERT's architecture consists of a stack of **Transformer encoder blocks** identical to those proposed in Vaswani et al. (2017). The architecture can be described as follows:

**Step 1 — Input Processing:** The input text is tokenized into **WordPiece subword units**. Special tokens `[CLS]` and `[SEP]` are inserted. Three embeddings (token, segment, position) are computed and summed to form the input vector of dimension $d_{\text{model}}$.

**Step 2 — Encoder Stack:** The input is passed through $L$ identical Transformer encoder layers. Each encoder layer has two sub-layers:
- **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer (with $A$ attention heads).
- **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network** (FFN) — two linear transformations with a ReLU activation in between, with hidden size $d_{\text{ff}} = 4 \times d_{\text{model}}$.
- Each sub-layer is wrapped with a **residual connection** followed by **Layer Normalization**.

**Step 3 — Output:** Each position produces a contextualized hidden vector of size $d_{\text{model}}$. The `[CLS]` token's final representation is used as the pooled output for classification.

**Comparison Table:**

| Hyperparameter | BERT-Base | BERT-Large |
|---|---|---|
| Layers (L) | 12 | 24 |
| Hidden Size (H = $d_{\text{model}}$) | 768 | 1024 |
| Attention Heads (A) | 12 | 16 |
| Feed-Forward Size ($d_{\text{ff}}$) | 3072 | 4096 |
| Total Parameters | ~110 Million | ~340 Million |

The **key distinction** is that BERT-Large has twice the depth, larger hidden dimensions, and more attention heads, giving it substantially greater representational capacity at the cost of higher computational and memory requirements.

---

**(b) Explain the Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) pre-training strategies of BERT with suitable examples. Justify why BERT uses 80/10/10 masking strategy instead of always replacing with [MASK]. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Masked Language Modeling (MLM):**
In MLM, ~15% of the input WordPiece tokens are selected at random for prediction. For example, the sentence:
*"The cat sat on the mat"*
might become:
*"The cat `[MASK]` on the `[MASK]` mat"*

The model is trained to predict the original words *"sat"* and *"the"* using the full bidirectional context (i.e., it sees both left and right tokens). The loss is computed only on the masked positions using cross-entropy over the vocabulary.

**The 80/10/10 Justification:**
If we always replaced the selected tokens with `[MASK]`, the model would quickly learn that `[MASK]` is the only token it needs to predict — but the `[MASK]` token **never appears during fine-tuning**. This creates a **train-test mismatch**.

To mitigate this:
- **80% of the time:** Replace with `[MASK]` → standard training signal.
- **10% of the time:** Replace with a **random token** → forces the model to maintain a contextual distribution over all tokens even when the input is "wrong" (e.g., *“the cat apple on the mat”* — model must still predict *“sat”* from context).
- **10% of the time:** **Keep the original token** → preserves the model's ability to copy the input forward (used as an identity mapping in many fine-tuning scenarios).

**Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):**
Given sentence pairs (A, B):
- Example 1 (label = `IsNext`): A = *"The weather is nice."* / B = *"Let's go for a walk."*
- Example 2 (label = `NotNext`): A = *"The weather is nice."* / B = *"Quantum mechanics is fundamental to physics."*

The `[CLS]` token's final hidden state is passed through a binary classifier (single FFN + softmax) to predict `IsNext` vs `NotNext`. NSP teaches BERT to model **inter-sentence coherence**, which is critical for downstream tasks like Question Answering and Natural Language Inference.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) With the help of a diagram, explain the input representation in BERT. How are token, segment, and position embeddings combined? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The input to BERT is a sequence of WordPiece tokens. For each token, BERT constructs a representation by summing three distinct embeddings, all of which are learned during pre-training.

**Diagram of Input Representation:**

```
                ┌────────────────────┐
                │   Token Embedding  │  (WordPiece vocabulary mapping)
                └─────────┬──────────┘
                          │  +
                ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                │  Segment Embedding │  (E_A or E_B, indicates sentence)
                └─────────┬──────────┘
                          │  +
                ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                │ Position Embedding │  (Learned absolute positions 0..n)
                └─────────┬──────────┘
                          │  =
                ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                │  Final Input Rep   │  → fed into Encoder Layer 1
                └────────────────────┘
```

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Token Embeddings ($E_T$):** Each WordPiece token is mapped to a dense vector of dimension $d_{\text{model}} = 768$ (BERT-Base). The vocabulary size is approximately 30,000 tokens. This captures the **lexical semantics** of the token.

2. **Segment Embeddings ($E_S$):** Each token is assigned either $E_A$ (if it belongs to sentence A) or $E_B$ (if it belongs to sentence B). This is critical for NSP and sentence-pair tasks like Question Answering. For single-sentence inputs, all tokens receive $E_A$.

3. **Position Embeddings ($E_P$):** Unlike the original Transformer which used sinusoidal fixed embeddings, BERT uses **learned position embeddings**. Each position index $0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$ has its own trainable vector. This allows BERT to model **sequence order** information.

**Combination:** The final input vector for each token is computed as:

$$
v_i = E_T(x_i) + E_S(s_i) + E_P(i)
$$

where $x_i$ is the token, $s_i \in \{A, B\}$ is the segment ID, and $i$ is the position index. All three embeddings have identical dimensions, so the element-wise addition is well-defined.

---

**(b) Explain the fine-tuning process in BERT. Discuss how a pre-trained BERT model is adapted for three downstream tasks: (i) Sentence Classification, (ii) Question Answering, and (iii) Named Entity Recognition. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Fine-tuning Paradigm:**
Fine-tuning leverages the **transfer learning** principle. Instead of training a new model from scratch, we initialize the model with the **pre-trained BERT weights** and then update **all parameters jointly** on a labeled dataset for the target task. The key advantages are:

- Far less labeled data is required (often a few thousand examples suffice).
- Training is fast (typically 2–4 epochs) since we start from a strong initialization.
- A small learning rate is used (e.g., 2e-5 to 5e-5) to avoid catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained knowledge.

**Adaptation for Three Downstream Tasks:**

**(i) Sentence Classification (e.g., Sentiment Analysis, Spam Detection):**
- **Input:** A single sentence (e.g., *"The movie was fantastic!"*).
- **Architecture:** Add a single linear classification layer on top of the `[CLS]` token's final hidden state $C \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$. The probability of class $k$ is:
$$
P(k \mid x) = \text{softmax}(CW^{\text{class}})
$$
where $W^{\text{class}} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times K}$ is a learnable matrix and $K$ is the number of classes.
- **Loss:** Standard cross-entropy loss.

**(ii) Question Answering (e.g., SQuAD-style Extractive QA):**
- **Input:** A question paragraph and a context paragraph, separated by `[SEP]` (e.g., *"Q: Where is BERT from? [SEP] Context: BERT was developed by Google..."*).
- **Architecture:** Two separate linear classifiers are added on top of every token's final hidden state:
  - A **start-span classifier** produces logits for each token being the answer's start position: $P_{\text{start}} = \text{softmax}(H W_{\text{start}})$.
  - An **end-span classifier** produces logits for each token being the answer's end position: $P_{\text{end}} = \text{softmax}(H W_{\text{end}})$.
- The predicted answer is the span $(i, j)$ that maximizes $P_{\text{start}}(i) \cdot P_{\text{end}}(j)$ with $i \le j$.

**(iii) Named Entity Recognition (NER):**
- **Input:** A single sentence containing entities (e.g., *"Apple Inc. was founded by Steve Jobs."*).
- **Architecture:** A linear classification layer is added on top of **every token's** final hidden state, not just `[CLS]`. Each token is assigned a label from a BIO tagging scheme (e.g., `B-PER`, `I-PER`, `O`, `B-ORG`, etc.).
- **Loss:** Token-level cross-entropy loss summed over all tokens in the sequence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse BERT with GPT. BERT is **bidirectional and encoder-only**; GPT is **unidirectional (causal) and decoder-only**. Mixing them up costs heavy marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing the attention formula without the **$\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** is a frequent 2-mark deduction. Always include the scaling.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **pre-training tasks** (MLM, NSP) with **fine-tuning tasks** (Classification, QA, NER). The question "list pre-training tasks" expects MLM + NSP, not downstream tasks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention the **80/10/10 masking split** in MLM. The 10% random and 10% unchanged are crucial for explaining why MLM avoids the train-test mismatch.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing BERT-Base parameter count. Always state **~110 Million** for Base and **~340 Million** for Large — not 100M or 350M.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Full Form:** BERT = **B**idirectional **E**ncoder **R**epresentations from **T**ransformers (Devlin et al., Google, 2018).
- **Architecture:** Multi-layer **bidirectional Transformer encoder** (decoder is discarded).
- **Two Configurations:**
  - **BERT-Base:** 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, ~110M parameters.
  - **BERT-Large:** 24 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads, ~340M parameters.
- **Three Input Embeddings (summed element-wise):** Token + Segment + Position (all learned, all same dimension 768/1024).
- **Special Tokens:** `[CLS]` (classification pooled output) and `[SEP]` (sentence separator).
- **Pre-training Task 1 — MLM:** Mask 15% of tokens (80% `[MASK]`, 10% random, 10% original); predict originals using full bidirectional context. This enables deep bidirectionality.
- **Pre-training Task 2 — NSP:** Binary classification on `[CLS]`; predict whether B follows A. (Later shown to be less critical — RoBERTa dropped it.)
- **Tokenization:** WordPiece subword tokenization (vocabulary ~30,000) — handles OOV gracefully (e.g., *playing → play + ##ing*).
- **Attention Formula:** $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ — never omit the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling.
- **Multi-Head:** $h$ parallel attention heads, concatenated and projected; allows attending to information from different representation subspaces.
- **Bidirectionality — Key Distinction:** Unlike GPT (left-to-right causal), BERT's self-attention is **fully bidirectional** (no causal mask). Every token attends to every other token.
- **Fine-tuning:** Replace pre-training head with task-specific head; update **all** BERT weights with small learning rate (2e-5 to 5e-5); typically 2–4 epochs.
- **Downstream Applications:** Sentence Classification, Token Classification (NER), Extractive QA (SQuAD), Natural Language Inference, Semantic Textual Similarity.
- **Derivatives / Variants:** **RoBERTa** (Facebook, removes NSP, more data), **DistilBERT** (60% smaller, 60% faster), **ALBERT** (parameter sharing), **BioBERT/SciBERT/FinBERT** (domain-specific).
- **Major Limitation:** BERT is **not generative** (cannot do open-ended text generation) because it has no decoder and is bidirectional. For generation, use GPT-family models.
- **MLM Training Cost:** Very expensive (BERT-Base took 4 days on 64 TPU chips; BERT-Large took 4 days on 256 TPU chips during original training).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixtral 8x7B
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mixtral 8x7B

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixtral 8x7B** is a **Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE)** decoder-only Large Language Model released by **Mistral AI** (December 2023). It comprises **8 expert feed-forward sub-networks**, each containing approximately **7 billion parameters**, giving a total of roughly **46.7B parameters**, but only **~12.9B parameters are active per token** during inference thanks to a learned **Top-2 routing** mechanism.

The model retains the **Mistral 7B** transformer backbone (sliding window attention, rotary positional embeddings, grouped-query attention) but replaces the single dense feed-forward (FFN) block in every transformer layer with **8 parallel FFN experts** and a lightweight **router network** that selects the two most relevant experts for each incoming token.

| Specification | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total Parameters | **~46.7B** |
| Active Parameters per Token | **~12.9B** |
| Number of Experts per Layer | **8** |
| Experts Activated per Token | **Top-2** |
| Context Length | **32,768 tokens** |
| Architecture Family | Decoder-only Transformer with SMoE |
| Release Date | December 2023 |
| Developer | Mistral AI (France) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Specialist Analogy**
> Imagine a general hospital with **8 specialist doctors** (cardiology, neurology, orthopedics, pediatrics, dermatology, oncology, radiology, psychiatry) — all available on standby. When a patient (a **token**) arrives, the **receptionist (router)** quickly glances at the symptoms and directs the patient to the **2 most relevant specialists**. Each specialist examines the case and gives an opinion, and the receptionist combines their weighted verdicts into a final diagnosis.
> 
> - The hospital's total staff strength is huge (**46.7B parameters**), but only **2 doctors are actually consulted per patient** (**~12.9B active parameters**).
> - This is **cheaper, faster, and more specialized** than having one overworked general physician handle every case.

### Why this matters in KTU context:
- Demonstrates the **scaling-vs-compute tradeoff** that defines modern LLM research.
- Mixtral 8x7B matches or beats **Llama 2 70B** and **GPT-3.5** on benchmarks while using **~5× less compute per inference**.
- It is a **flagship KTU syllabus example** of how architectural innovation (not just bigger models) drives progress.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Architectural Constants

> Bold constants a student must memorize:
> - **8** = number of feed-forward experts per transformer layer
> - **2** = number of experts selected per token (Top-K, K=2)
> - **46.7B** ≈ total parameters
> - **~12.9B** ≈ active parameters per forward pass
> - **32k** = context window in tokens
> - **2 (router hidden size factor)** = router is a single linear layer projecting hidden state to 8 logits
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks

The Mixtral model stacks **N decoder blocks**, where each block contains the following sequential sub-modules:

1. **RMSNorm** — Pre-normalization on input.
2. **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)** with **Sliding Window Attention (SWA)** of window size **W = 4096**.
3. **Residual Connection** around the attention block.
4. **RMSNorm** — Pre-normalization before MoE.
5. **MoE Block (replaces dense FFN)** — Contains the router + 8 experts.
6. **Residual Connection** around the MoE block.

> [!NOTE]
> The **only architectural change** from Mistral 7B → Mixtral 8x7B is the replacement of the dense FFN by the **Sparse MoE block** in every transformer layer. Attention, embeddings, and normalization remain identical.

## 2.2 The Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Block — Logic Steps

1. **Input Token Embedding**: A token $t$ is represented as a hidden vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d$ is the model hidden dimension (e.g., $d = 4096$).
2. **Router Computation**: A lightweight linear layer $W_g \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 8}$ projects $x$ to a vector of 8 expert scores (logits).
3. **Softmax Normalization**: The 8 logits are converted to a probability distribution over experts using the softmax function.
4. **Top-K Selection**: The **Top-2** experts with the highest probabilities are selected for this token.
5. **Expert Forward Passes**: The token $x$ is dispatched **only** to the selected 2 experts. Each expert $E_i$ is itself a SwiGLU FFN.
6. **Weighted Aggregation**: The outputs of the 2 selected experts are weighted by their routing probabilities and summed.
7. **Residual Add**: The aggregated output is added back to the input $x$ (residual connection).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x$ | Hidden state of a token | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $W_g$ | Router weight matrix | $W_g \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times E}$, $E=8$ |
| $r$ | Router logits | $r = x \cdot W_g$ |
| $p_i$ | Routing probability of expert $i$ | $p_i = \dfrac{e^{r_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{E} e^{r_j}}$ |
| $\mathcal{T}$ | Set of Top-K experts | $\mathcal{T} = \text{TopK}(r, K)$, $K=2$ |
| $y_i$ | Output of expert $i$ | $y_i = E_i(x) = \text{SwiGLU}_i(x)$ |
| $y_{\text{out}}$ | MoE block output | $y_{\text{out}} = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{T}} p_i \cdot y_i$ |
| $\alpha_i$ | Expert load fraction (across batch) | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{1}[i \in \mathcal{T}_b]$ |
| $f_i$ | Fraction of router probability mass on expert $i$ | $f_i = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} p_i^{(b)}$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}$ | Auxiliary load-balancing loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = E \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{E} \alpha_i \cdot f_i$ |
| $E$ | Number of experts | **8** |
| $K$ | Experts activated per token | **2** |
| $B$ | Tokens per batch / sequence length | Variable |
| $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ | Indicator function | Returns 1 if condition true, else 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse $\alpha_i$ (fraction of **tokens** routed to expert $i$) with $f_i$ (fraction of **probability mass** given to expert $i$). They are different quantities. The auxiliary loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}$ requires **both** — it is the product-sum of the two, scaled by $E$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-Efficient Inference**: Achieves GPT-3.5-class quality at roughly **5× lower inference FLOPs**.
- **Specialization**: Different experts learn to handle different **linguistic phenomena** (code, math, multilingual, etc.), enabling modular interpretability.
- **Scalability Pattern**: The MoE pattern underpins modern frontier models like **DeepSeek-V3, Mixtral 8x22B, GPT-4 (rumored), and Snowflake Arctic**.
- **Edge / Latency-Critical Deployment**: Sparse activation enables larger total capacity without proportional latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Router Output Probabilities

We want to derive, from first principles, how the router assigns probabilities to the 8 experts.

**Step 1 — Define the router as a single linear projection.**

The router is a learnable matrix $W_g \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times E}$, where $d$ is the hidden dimension of the transformer and $E = 8$ is the number of experts.

For a token with hidden state $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, the router produces a score vector:

$$r = x \cdot W_g$$

where $r \in \mathbb{R}^{E}$ contains one scalar logit per expert. This is a single matrix multiplication — extremely cheap compared to the experts themselves.

**Step 2 — Convert raw scores to a valid probability distribution using softmax.**

The softmax function ensures all outputs are non-negative and sum to 1:

$$p_i = \frac{e^{r_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{E} e^{r_j}}, \quad i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, E\}$$

**Conversion logic:** We exponentiate each logit to make it strictly positive, then divide by the sum of all exponentiated logits so the distribution is normalized. The indicator $i$ indexes the expert, and $j$ is the dummy summation index.

**Step 3 — Select the Top-K experts.**

In Mixtral, $K = 2$. We find the indices of the 2 experts with the largest $p_i$ values:

$$\mathcal{T} = \underset{i}{\arg\,\text{Top-}K}\,(p_i), \quad K = 2$$

The selected experts receive a *renormalized* weight during aggregation, but the original $p_i$ values are preserved in the auxiliary loss for load balancing.

## 3.2 Derivation of the MoE Output

The final output of the MoE block for a single token is the **weighted sum** of only the Top-K expert outputs:

$$y_{\text{out}} = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{T}} p_i \cdot E_i(x)$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. For each of the 2 selected experts, compute $E_i(x)$, the output of that expert's SwiGLU FFN applied to $x$.
2. Multiply each expert's output by its routing probability $p_i$ (a scalar weight).
3. Sum the two weighted outputs to produce the block's output vector.

**Conversion logic:** Only the chosen 2 experts are evaluated. The other 6 experts contribute nothing for this token, yielding the *sparsity* that defines Mixtral.

The MoE output is then added back to the input via the residual connection:

$$h_{\text{next}} = x + y_{\text{out}}$$

This $h_{\text{next}}$ flows into the next transformer layer (or, for the final layer, the language modeling head).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Load-Balancing Auxiliary Loss

A critical problem in MoE training is **expert collapse** — where the router sends almost all tokens to the same 1-2 experts, leaving the others unused. To prevent this, Mixtral adds an **auxiliary loss** to the main cross-entropy objective.

**Step 1 — Compute the fraction of tokens assigned to each expert.**

Across a batch of $B$ tokens, the fraction of tokens routed to expert $i$ is:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{1}[i \in \mathcal{T}_b]$$

**Conversion logic:** The indicator $\mathbb{1}[i \in \mathcal{T}_b]$ is 1 if expert $i$ was among the Top-2 selected for token $b$, else 0. Summing over all $B$ tokens and dividing by $B$ gives the empirical assignment frequency.

**Step 2 — Compute the average router probability mass on each expert.**

$$f_i = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{p_i^{(b)}}{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{T}_b} p_j^{(b)}}$$

**Conversion logic:** For each token $b$, we sum the routing probabilities of only the *selected* experts (renormalized to 1 over the Top-K). The indicator here distinguishes "token was routed to expert $i$" (binary) from "expert $i$ received how much *weight* from the router" (continuous).

> [!NOTE]
> A common simplified KTU-level approximation treats $f_i$ as the **average raw router probability** assigned to expert $i$ across the batch, ignoring the renormalization subtlety. Both forms appear in literature.

**Step 3 — Combine $\alpha_i$ and $f_i$ into the auxiliary loss.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = E \sum_{i=1}^{E} \alpha_i \cdot f_i$$

**Why this works:** By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** (or AM-GM), the product $\alpha_i \cdot f_i$ is maximized when all $\alpha_i$ and all $f_i$ are equal. Multiplying by $E$ scales the loss so its minimum value is 1 (achieved at perfect balance $\alpha_i = f_i = 1/E$). Minimizing $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}$ therefore **encourages uniform expert usage**, preventing collapse.

**Step 4 — The total training objective.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(y, \hat{y}) + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}$ is the standard cross-entropy language modeling loss and $\lambda$ is a small balancing coefficient (typically $\lambda \approx 0.01$ in the original Mixtral release).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compute & Memory Analysis

| Metric | Dense 7B (per layer) | Mixtral 8x7B (per layer) | Ratio |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| FFN parameters | $3 d^2$ | $8 \times 3 d^2$ | **8×** |
| Active FFN params / token | $3 d^2$ | $2 \times 3 d^2$ | **2×** |
| Total model params | ~7B | ~46.7B | **~6.7×** |
| Active params / token | ~7B | ~12.9B | **~1.84×** |
| FLOPs / token (forward) | Baseline | $\approx 1.84\times$ baseline | **~1.84×** |
| Memory (weights, fp16) | ~14 GB | ~93 GB | **~6.7×** |
| Throughput vs dense 7B | 1× | ~6× (same quality tier) | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memory grows with **all** parameters (because all weights must be stored), but compute grows with only the **active** parameters. This is the central MoE tradeoff: **memory-heavy, compute-light**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Mixtral Decoder Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Token Embedding] --> B[RMSNorm 1]
    B --> C[Grouped Query Attention with Sliding Window]
    C --> D[Residual Add 1]
    D --> E[RMSNorm 2]
    E --> F[MoE Block]
    F --> G[Residual Add 2]
    G --> H[Output to Next Layer]
    F --> F1[Router Linear Layer Wg]
    F1 --> F2[Softmax over 8 experts]
    F2 --> F3[Top-2 Expert Selection]
    F3 --> F4[Expert FFN 1]
    F3 --> F5[Expert FFN 2]
    F4 --> F6[Weighted Sum]
    F5 --> F6
    F6 --> G
```

## 4.2 Router-to-Expert Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Token Hidden State x] --> R[Router: x times Wg]
    R --> L[8 Logits]
    L --> S[Softmax Probabilities]
    S --> K[Top-2 Selection]
    K --> E1[Expert 1 FFN]
    K --> E2[Expert 2 FFN]
    E1 --> W[Weighted Sum y_out]
    E2 --> W
    W --> O[Residual Add with x]
    O --> N[Next Layer Input]
```

## 4.3 Expert Specialization (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Incoming Token]
        T1[Token t]
    end
    INPUT --> RT[Router Scores]
    RT --> E1[Expert 1: Code Patterns]
    RT --> E2[Expert 2: Math Reasoning]
    RT --> E3[Expert 3: English Text]
    RT --> E4[Expert 4: Multilingual]
    RT --> E5[Expert 5: Logical Deduction]
    RT --> E6[Expert 6: Entity Names]
    RT --> E7[Expert 7: Conversational]
    RT --> E8[Expert 8: Rare Tokens]
    E1 --> AGG[Weighted Aggregation]
    E2 --> AGG
    E3 --> AGG
    E4 --> AGG
    E5 --> AGG
    E6 --> AGG
    E7 --> AGG
    E8 --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT[Final Output Vector]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **specialization labels** above are illustrative. In practice, experts do not have clean human-interpretable roles; interpretability research (e.g., MoE-LLaVA, expert-pruning studies) suggests the partitioning is **functional and statistical** rather than semantic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and state how Mixtral 8x7B implements a *Sparse* MoE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A Mixture-of-Experts layer consists of multiple parallel expert sub-networks combined via a learned gating/router network. Each expert specializes in different input patterns.
- **(1 mark)** In Mixtral 8x7B, each transformer layer's dense FFN is replaced by **8 parallel FFN experts** with a **Top-2 router**.
- **(1 mark)** It is "Sparse" because for every token, only **2 of the 8 experts** are activated. The other 6 experts are skipped, so only ~12.9B of the 46.7B total parameters contribute to any given forward pass.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Q: What is the auxiliary load-balancing loss in Mixtral and why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** It is the loss term $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = E \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{E} \alpha_i \cdot f_i$, added to the cross-entropy training objective.
- **(1 mark)** Without it, the router tends to **collapse** — sending nearly all tokens to the same 1-2 experts while the remaining 6 become unused, defeating the purpose of MoE.
- **(1 mark)** Minimizing $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}$ forces **uniform token distribution** and **uniform probability mass** across all 8 experts, ensuring every expert is trained.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of **Mixtral 8x7B**. Highlight how it differs from the standard **dense transformer decoder** like Mistral 7B. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **router mechanism** in Mixtral, showing how Top-2 experts are selected and how their outputs are aggregated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(2 marks)** Draw a labelled block diagram of one Mixtral decoder layer: RMSNorm → Grouped-Query Attention (with Sliding Window) → Residual → RMSNorm → **MoE Block** → Residual. Use the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** above.
- **(2 marks)** Differences from Mistral 7B:
  - Dense FFN is **replaced** by an 8-expert MoE block.
  - **Router** added before the experts.
  - Total parameters jump from ~7B to ~46.7B; active parameters per token increase only from ~7B to ~12.9B.
  - Attention, embeddings, and normalization are **identical** to Mistral 7B.
- **(2 marks)** Specify key numbers: **8 experts, Top-2 routing, 32k context window, grouped-query attention, sliding window size 4096**.
- **(1 mark)** State real-world significance: matches Llama-2-70B quality at ~5× lower compute.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

- **(2 marks)** Router formulation: $r = x \cdot W_g$ where $W_g \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 8}$. Then softmax: $p_i = \dfrac{e^{r_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{8} e^{r_j}}$.
- **(2 marks)** Top-2 selection: $\mathcal{T} = \arg\,\text{Top-2}\,(p_i)$. Note the selection is a hard, non-differentiable operation handled via straight-through estimators in practice.
- **(2 marks)** Aggregation: $y_{\text{out}} = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{T}} p_i \cdot E_i(x)$, then residual $h = x + y_{\text{out}}$.
- **(1 mark)** Highlight that only 2 experts run per token → compute scales with active parameters, not total.

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the **expert routing and load-balancing problem** in Mixture-of-Experts models. Derive the auxiliary balancing loss used in Mixtral. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Mixtral 8x7B with Llama 2 70B and GPT-3.5** in terms of total parameters, active parameters, inference compute, and benchmark performance. What architectural advantage does Mixtral exploit? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(2 marks)** Explain the **expert collapse problem**: the router learns to send most tokens to 1-2 "popular" experts because they happen to have lower training loss initially. This creates a self-reinforcing loop, leaving 6 of 8 experts untrained.
- **(2 marks)** Define $\alpha_i$ (token assignment fraction) and $f_i$ (router probability fraction) clearly, with formulas (as in Section 3.3).
- **(2 marks)** Derive $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = E \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{E} \alpha_i \cdot f_i$ and show that it is minimized when $\alpha_i = f_i = 1/E$ for all $i$, citing **Cauchy-Schwarz** or **AM-GM inequality** as justification.
- **(1 mark)** State the combined loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}}$ with $\lambda$ as a small positive coefficient.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

- **(2 marks)** **Parameter count**: Mixtral 46.7B total / 12.9B active. Llama 2 70B = 70B total / 70B active. GPT-3.5 = ~175B total / ~175B active.
- **(2 marks)** **Compute / inference FLOPs**: Mixtral requires roughly **5× less FLOPs** than Llama 2 70B and **~10× less** than GPT-3.5 at comparable quality.
- **(1 mark)** **Benchmark performance**: Mixtral 8x7B matches or exceeds Llama 2 70B on MMLU, HellaSwag, Arc-Challenge, and HumanEval; competitive with GPT-3.5 on reasoning benchmarks.
- **(1 mark)** **Architectural advantage**: **Sparse activation via MoE** — decouples total capacity (memory) from inference compute (FLOPs). This is the central innovation.
- **(1 mark)** **Tradeoff**: Memory cost is high (need all 46.7B parameters in VRAM), so deployment requires careful batching and quantization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing active vs total parameters**: Students often write "Mixtral has 46.7B parameters" without clarifying that only ~12.9B are *active*. This loses 1 mark in Part A and up to 2 marks in Part B.
> 2. **Skipping the auxiliary loss derivation**: Examiner expects both $\alpha_i$ and $f_i$ explicitly defined before writing the final formula. Skipping the derivation loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Drawing Mistral 7B instead of Mixtral 8x7B**: A common mistake is drawing a standard transformer. The MoE block with 8 experts and the router must be visible.
> 4. **Forgetting residual connections**: Both inside and outside the MoE block, residual adds must be shown.
> 5. **Mixing up Top-2 selection with all-8 dense routing**: This defeats the entire "sparse" premise. Always state **K=2 explicitly**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mixtral 8x7B = Sparse MoE** with 8 experts, Top-2 routing per token.
- **Total params ≈ 46.7B, Active params ≈ 12.9B** per forward pass.
- **Router**: Single linear layer $W_g \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 8}$ producing logits → softmax → Top-2 → weighted sum.
- **Output formula**: $y_{\text{out}} = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{T}} p_i \cdot E_i(x)$.
- **Auxiliary loss**: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = E \sum_{i=1}^{E} \alpha_i \cdot f_i$ prevents expert collapse.
- **Cauchy-Schwarz** justifies the balancing loss minimization at uniform $\alpha_i = f_i = 1/E$.
- **Compute advantage**: ~5× less FLOPs than Llama 2 70B at comparable quality.
- **Memory disadvantage**: ~93 GB in fp16 due to all 46.7B parameters stored.
- **Attention backbone**: Identical to Mistral 7B — Sliding Window (W=4096), Grouped-Query Attention, RoPE, RMSNorm.
- **Context window**: 32,768 tokens.
- **Decoder-only transformer** (causal LM).
- **Open-weight release** under Apache 2.0 license — important for KTU viva questions on open-source LLMs.
- **Released**: December 2023 by **Mistral AI** (Paris, France).
- **Predecessor**: Mistral 7B (dense). **Successor**: Mixtral 8x22B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prompt-Based Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prompt-Based Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prompt-Based Learning (PBL)** is a paradigm in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Model (LLM) adaptation where, instead of fine-tuning the internal weights of a pre-trained model on a downstream task, the model is conditioned to perform the desired task by crafting an input **prompt** — a natural language instruction, query, or context that elicits the correct output from the frozen language model.

In formal mathematical terms, given a pre-trained language model parameterized by $\theta$ (which is kept **frozen**), prompt-based learning seeks to find a prompt template $T$ (and optionally a set of continuous embeddings) such that the model maximizes the likelihood of the correct answer $y$ for input $x$:

$$P(y \mid x; T, \theta)$$

The optimization objective can thus be expressed as:

$$\max_{T} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{task}}} \left[ \log P_{\theta}(y \mid T(x)) \right]$$

where:
- $\theta$ = frozen pre-trained LLM parameters
- $T(x)$ = prompted version of the input
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{task}}$ = task-specific dataset
- $y$ = target label / output text

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Fine-Tuning:** In classical fine-tuning, the entire parameter set $\theta$ is updated via back-propagation. In prompt-based learning, $\theta$ remains unchanged, and only the **prompt** $T$ is modified. This makes PBL extremely parameter-efficient.

## 1.2 Three Fundamental Variants of Prompting

| Variant | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Shot Prompting** | The model receives only an instruction, with no examples. | *"Classify the sentiment: 'I loved this movie.'"* |
| **One-Shot Prompting** | The model is given exactly **one** demonstration of the task. | One input-output example + new query |
| **Few-Shot Prompting (In-Context Learning)** | The model is given $k$ demonstrations ($k \in [3, 64]$ typically) before the actual query. | Multiple examples + new query |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must remember: **In-Context Learning (ICL)** is the technical term for few-shot prompting, first formalized by Brown et al. in the GPT-3 paper (2020). ICL is the cornerstone of modern LLM usage.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The New Intern**
>
> Imagine you hire a brilliant new intern (the LLM) who has read every book in the library (pre-trained on massive corpora) but has **never worked on your specific project**. You have two options:
>
> 1. **Fine-Tuning:** Send them to a 6-month retraining program on your company data (expensive, slow, risks "catastrophic forgetting").
> 2. **Prompt-Based Learning:** Simply write a **clear, well-structured instruction** on a sticky note: *"When the customer says X, reply with Y. Here are 3 examples..."* The intern immediately performs the task using their general knowledge.
>
> Prompt-Based Learning is the **sticky note** — it leverages the model's pre-existing knowledge without modifying its brain.

## 1.4 Why Prompt-Based Learning Matters

- **Parameter Efficiency:** Updates only a tiny fraction of parameters (often $< 0.01\%$ of model size).
- **Data Efficiency:** Works with as few as **3–10** labeled examples (few-shot).
- **No Catastrophic Forgetting:** Original pre-trained knowledge is preserved.
- **Cross-Task Generalization:** A single frozen model can serve hundreds of tasks.
- **Deployment Simplicity:** A single base model + task-specific prompts replaces hundreds of fine-tuned models.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Prompt-Based Learning

### 2.1.1 The Cloze-Style Formulation (Masked Language Models)

For BERT-style models, prompts are formulated as **cloze questions** where the model fills in a `[MASK]` token. The probability of a candidate label word $v \in \mathcal{V}$ is:

$$P(y = v \mid x) = \frac{\exp(W_v \cdot h_{[\text{MASK}]})}{\sum_{v' \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(W_{v'} \cdot h_{[\text{MASK}]})}$$

where:
- $h_{[\text{MASK}]}$ = hidden state vector at the masked position
- $W_v$ = output embedding (unembedding matrix row) for vocabulary token $v$
- $\mathcal{V}$ = vocabulary set

### 2.1.2 The Generative Formulation (Autoregressive LLMs)

For GPT-style models, the answer $y$ is generated token-by-token autoregressively:

$$P(y \mid x, T) = \prod_{t=1}^{|y|} P_{\theta}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, T(x))$$

### 2.1.3 Prompt Tuning (Continuous Soft Prompts)

In **prompt tuning** (Lester et al., 2021), the discrete prompt tokens are replaced by a sequence of **learnable continuous embedding vectors** $P = [p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_l]$ where each $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (with $d$ being the model hidden dimension). These are the only parameters trained:

$$\theta_{\text{trainable}} = \{P\} \subset \mathbb{R}^{l \times d}$$

The optimization becomes:

$$\max_{P} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)} \left[ \log P_{\theta_{\text{frozen}}}(y \mid [P; x]) \right]$$

### 2.1.4 Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021)

Prefix tuning prepends trainable prefixes to **every** transformer layer (not just the input embedding), while the LM remains frozen:

$$h^{(l)} = \text{Attn}([P_K^{(l)}; K^{(l)}], [P_V^{(l)}; V^{(l)}])$$

where $P_K^{(l)}$ and $P_V^{(l)}$ are learnable key/value prefix matrices for layer $l$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(y \mid x; T, \theta)$ | Probability of answer given prompted input | All PBL |
| 2 | $\max_T \log P_{\theta}(y \mid T(x))$ | Prompt template optimization | Hard prompts |
| 3 | $\max_{P \in \mathbb{R}^{l \times d}} \log P_\theta(y \mid [P; x])$ | Soft prompt tuning | Prompt Tuning |
| 4 | $P(y) = \prod_{t=1}^{\vert y\vert} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, T(x))$ | Autoregressive generation | GPT-style models |
| 5 | $P(v) = \frac{\exp(W_v \cdot h_{[\text{MASK}]})}{Z}$ | Cloze-style prediction | BERT-style models |
| 6 | $\text{Perplexity} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i)\right)$ | Evaluation metric | Generation tasks |
| 7 | $\text{Acc} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_i = y_i]$ | Accuracy metric | Classification |
| 8 | $\Delta\theta = -\eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{prompt}}$ | Gradient update (only on prompt) | Soft prompt training |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline of a Prompt-Based Learning System:**
> 1. **Task Formulation:** Convert the task into a text completion problem.
> 2. **Prompt Template Design:** Create a template $T(x)$ with placeholders.
> 3. **Label Word Mapping (Verbalizer):** Map class labels to natural language words.
> 4. **Answer Prediction:** Compute $P(y \mid x; T, \theta)$ over candidate answers.
> 5. **Optional: Prompt Optimization:** Tune $T$ or soft prompt $P$ on training data.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines:** Query understanding in Google, Bing.
- **Code Generation:** GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code.
- **Customer Service:** Chatbots handling thousands of intents via prompt templates.
- **Medical NLP:** Few-shot diagnosis classification without sharing patient data.
- **Edge Deployment:** Soft prompts are tiny (e.g., **20 KB** vs **7 GB** for full model), enabling on-device personalization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fine-Tuning to Prompt-Based Learning

### 3.1.1 Standard Fine-Tuning Objective

In standard fine-tuning, we update all parameters $\theta$ to minimize the cross-entropy loss on the task dataset:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{FT}}(\theta) = -\sum_{(x, y) \in \mathcal{D}} \log P_\theta(y \mid x)$$

The full parameter update is:

$$\theta^{\text{new}} = \theta^{\text{old}} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{FT}}(\theta)$$

For a 7B parameter model, $|\theta| = 7 \times 10^9$. Storing the optimizer states (Adam: 2 moments per parameter) requires **$\approx 56$ GB** of GPU memory per training run.

### 3.1.2 Reformulating Classification as Cloze

Consider sentiment classification. Traditional fine-tuning learns a new classification head $W_{\text{cls}} \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times d}$ (where $C$ = number of classes, $d$ = hidden dim) on top of the `[CLS]` token.

**Prompt-based reformulation:**

Step 1: Define a template:

$$T(x) = \text{"[CLS] } x \text{ The sentiment is [MASK]. [SEP]"}$$

Step 2: Define a verbalizer $\phi$ mapping label indices to vocabulary words:

$$\phi: \{0 \mapsto \text{"terrible"}, \; 1 \mapsto \text{"great"}\}$$

Step 3: Convert classification into a fill-in-the-blank problem:
