$$P(y = c \mid x) = P([\text{MASK}] = \phi(c) \mid T(x))$$

Step 4: Since $\theta$ is frozen, the only unknowns are the template tokens and the verbalizer. Both can be either **discrete** (human-readable) or **continuous** (learned embeddings).

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Soft Prompt Gradient

For soft prompt $P \in \mathbb{R}^{l \times d}$, the loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}(P) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_{\theta}\left(y^{(i)} \;\middle|\; \left[P;\, T_{\text{hard}}(x^{(i)})\right]\right)$$

The gradient with respect to $P$ is:

$$\nabla_{P} \mathcal{L}(P) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_{P} \log P_{\theta}\left(y^{(i)} \;\middle|\; \left[P;\, T_{\text{hard}}(x^{(i)})\right]\right)$$

Since the back-propagation passes through the frozen transformer, the gradient only accumulates into the $P$ slots:

$$P^{(k+1)} = P^{(k)} - \eta \, \nabla_{P} \mathcal{L}(P^{(k)})$$

The number of trainable parameters is $l \times d$. For $l = 20$ and $d = 4096$ (LLaMA-7B), this is:

$$|P| = 20 \times 4096 = 81{,}920 \text{ parameters}$$

Comparing:

$$\frac{|P|}{|\theta|} = \frac{8.2 \times 10^4}{7 \times 10^9} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-5} = 0.00117\%$$

This is the **parameter efficiency** of prompt tuning.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Methods

| Method | Parameters Updated | Memory | Data Required | Performance | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | $100\%$ | High (56 GB+) | 1K–100K examples | ★★★★★ | High |
| **LoRA** | $0.1$–$5\%$ | Medium | 1K–10K examples | ★★★★★ | Very High |
| **Prompt Tuning** | $0.001$–$0.1\%$ | Low | 1K–10K examples | ★★★★ | Very High |
| **Prefix Tuning** | $0.1$–$1\%$ | Medium | 1K–10K examples | ★★★★ | High |
| **Few-Shot ICL** | $0\%$ | Zero | 3–64 examples | ★★★ | Very High |
| **Zero-Shot** | $0\%$ | Zero | $0$ examples | ★★ | High |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 7B parameter LLM is being adapted for a medical NER task using prompt tuning with a soft prompt length of $l = 50$ and hidden dimension $d = 4096$. Calculate the percentage of trainable parameters.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of trainable parameters } |P| &= l \times d \\
&= 50 \times 4096 \\
&= 204{,}800 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total model parameters } |\theta| &= 7 \times 10^9 \\
\text{Percentage trainable} &= \frac{|P|}{|\theta|} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{204{,}800}{7 \times 10^9} \times 100 \\
&= 2.926 \times 10^{-3}\% \\
&\approx 0.00293\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Inference:** Only **$\approx 0.003\%$** of the model needs to be stored per task, allowing hundreds of task-specific prompts to be swapped in/out of memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Prompt-Based Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Raw Input x"] --> nodeB["Task Formulation Layer"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Prompt Template T(x)"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Prompted Input T(x)"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Frozen LLM with params theta"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Hidden State at MASK or Last Token"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Verbalizer Mapping"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Output Label y"]
    nodeI["Soft Prompt P trainable"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeJ["Pre-trained Weights FROZEN"] -.-> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Zero-Shot, One-Shot, Few-Shot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZS["ZERO SHOT"]
        zs1["Instruction"] --> zs2["Query x"] --> zs3["Frozen LLM"] --> zs4["Output y"]
    end

    subgraph OS["ONE SHOT"]
        os1["Instruction"] --> os2["Example 1 plus output"] --> os3["Query x"] --> os4["Frozen LLM"] --> os5["Output y"]
    end

    subgraph FS["FEW SHOT ICL"]
        fs1["Instruction"] --> fs2["Example 1"] --> fs3["Example 2"] --> fs4["Example k"] --> fs5["Query x"] --> fs6["Frozen LLM"] --> fs7["Output y"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Soft Prompt Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMB["Input Embedding Layer"]
        e1["Soft Prompt P1 vector d"]
        e2["Soft Prompt P2 vector d"]
        e3["Soft Prompt Pl vector d"]
        e4["Hard Token Embedding for x"]
    end

    EMB --> T1["Transformer Layer 1 Frozen"]
    T1 --> T2["Transformer Layer 2 Frozen"]
    T2 --> TN["Transformer Layer N Frozen"]
    TN --> OUT["Output Token y"]

    Ptrainable["P1 to Pl are TRAINABLE gradient updates only flow here"] -.-> e1
    Ptrainable -.-> e2
    Ptrainable -.-> e3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Chain-of-Thought Prompting (CoT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Complex Question Q"] --> nodeB["Add CoT Prompt: Let us think step by step"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Few-Shot Examples with reasoning chains"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Frozen LLM with Reasoning Capability"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 1 generated"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 2 generated"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step N generated"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Final Answer y"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** The above diagrams collectively describe the **input → template → frozen-LLM → verbalizer → output** pipeline that is the architectural foundation of every prompt-based learning system studied in KTU Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **Q: Define Prompt-Based Learning. Differentiate between zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Prompt-Based Learning is a paradigm where a frozen pre-trained LLM is adapted to downstream tasks by conditioning it on a carefully designed natural language **prompt** $T(x)$, instead of updating the model's internal weights $\theta$.

**Differentiation:**

| Type | Examples in Prompt | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Shot** | $0$ examples — only instruction | Simple, well-known tasks |
| **One-Shot** | $1$ demonstration | Slightly ambiguous tasks |
| **Few-Shot (ICL)** | $k$ demonstrations ($3 \leq k \leq 64$) | Complex reasoning tasks |

**Example — Sentiment Classification:**
- Zero-Shot: `"Classify sentiment: 'Great movie!' → "`
- One-Shot: `"Example: 'Bad food' → Negative. Now: 'Great movie!' → "`
- Few-Shot: 3–5 labeled examples followed by `"Great movie!"`

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q: What is a verbalizer in prompt-based learning? Why is prompt engineering considered an "art" rather than a science?**

**Model Answer:**

A **verbalizer** $\phi: \mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \mathcal{V}$ is a mapping from task label space $\mathcal{Y}$ (e.g., $\{0, 1, 2\}$) to natural language vocabulary words $\mathcal{V}$ (e.g., $\{$`"terrible"`, `"okay"`, `"great"`$\}$). It bridges the gap between abstract class labels and the LLM's pre-trained token vocabulary.

**Why prompt engineering is an "art":**
1. **No gradient signal:** Discrete text prompts cannot be optimized via back-propagation directly.
2. **Sensitive to wording:** Tiny phrasing changes (e.g., `"Think"` vs `"Think carefully"`) can flip accuracy.
3. **Model-specific:** A prompt that works on GPT-4 may fail on LLaMA-3.
4. **Order dependence:** The order of few-shot examples can change outputs significantly.
5. **Empirical:** Success is determined by trial-and-error rather than theory.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **Q: (a)** Explain the architecture and working of **Prompt-Based Learning** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the verbalizer in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With relevant equations, derive the **mathematical formulation of Soft Prompt Tuning**. Compare it with full fine-tuning in terms of parameter efficiency. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Verbalizer (7 Marks)

**Architecture:**

A prompt-based learning system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Input Layer:** Receives raw input $x$ from the task dataset.
2. **Prompt Template Module:** Wraps $x$ inside a natural language template $T$, producing $T(x)$. The template contains a `[MASK]` token (for encoder models) or a continuation cue (for decoder models).
3. **Frozen Pre-trained LLM ($\theta$ frozen):** Processes $T(x)$ through all transformer layers without any weight update.
4. **Output Extraction:** Extracts the hidden representation $h_{[\text{MASK}]}$ at the masked position (or the last token's hidden state for decoders).
5. **Verbalizer / Answer Mapping:** Converts the LLM's token probability distribution into a class prediction using the verbalizer mapping $\phi$.

**Role of the Verbalizer:**

The verbalizer solves the **vocabulary-to-label alignment problem**. Since the LLM was pre-trained on a generic vocabulary, it cannot directly output abstract class IDs like `"Class_3"`. The verbalizer maps each class $c \in \mathcal{Y}$ to a semantically meaningful vocabulary word $v_c \in \mathcal{V}$:

$$\phi(c) = v_c$$

For example, in sentiment analysis:

$$\phi(0) = \text{"terrible"}, \quad \phi(1) = \text{"bad"}, \quad \phi(2) = \text{"okay"}, \quad \phi(3) = \text{"good"}, \quad \phi(4) = \text{"great"}$$

The final class prediction is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{Y}} \; P_\theta([\text{MASK}] = \phi(c) \mid T(x))$$

#### Part (b) — Mathematical Formulation of Soft Prompt Tuning (7 Marks)

**Setup:** Let the pre-trained LLM have parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{|\theta|}$ (kept frozen). We introduce a learnable soft prompt $P = [p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_l]$ where each $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $d$ is the model's hidden dimension.

**Concatenation with Input Embeddings:**

The input to the first transformer layer becomes the concatenation of the soft prompt embeddings and the hard token embeddings of the input:

$$E_{\text{input}} = [\,p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_l,\, e(x_1), e(x_2), \ldots, e(x_n)\,]$$

where $e(\cdot)$ is the frozen embedding lookup.

**Loss Function (Cross-Entropy):**

$$\mathcal{L}(P) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta\!\left(y^{(i)} \,\middle|\, \left[P;\, T_{\text{hard}}(x^{(i)})\right]\right)$$

**Gradient Update (only on $P$):**

$$\nabla_P \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\partial}{\partial P} \log P_\theta\!\left(y^{(i)} \,\middle|\, \left[P;\, T_{\text{hard}}(x^{(i)})\right]\right)$$

$$P^{(k+1)} = P^{(k)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_P \mathcal{L}\!\left(P^{(k)}\right)$$

**Parameter Efficiency Comparison:**

For a 7B model with $l = 50$, $d = 4096$:

$$\begin{aligned}
|P| &= 50 \times 4096 = 204{,}800 \text{ parameters} \\
|\theta| &= 7 \times 10^9 \\
\text{Efficiency Ratio} &= \frac{|P|}{|\theta|} = \frac{2.048 \times 10^5}{7 \times 10^9} \approx 2.93 \times 10^{-5} \\
&\approx 0.003\%
\end{aligned}$$

This means **prompt tuning trains only $0.003\%$ of the parameters** of full fine-tuning, while achieving comparable performance on most NLU tasks (Lester et al., 2021).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q: (a)** Discuss **In-Context Learning (ICL)** in detail. Explain with examples how zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting enable task adaptation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting** and **Prefix Tuning**. Compare their architectures and parameter efficiency. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — In-Context Learning (7 Marks)

**Definition:** In-Context Learning (ICL), introduced in the GPT-3 paper (Brown et al., 2020), is the ability of a large language model to learn a new task **at inference time** purely from examples provided in the input context — **without any gradient updates**.

**Formal Definition:**

Given a context $C = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_k, y_k)\}$ of $k$ demonstrations, the model predicts:

$$\hat{y}_{k+1} = \arg\max_{y} \; P_\theta\!\left(y \,\middle|\, C, x_{k+1}\right)$$

**Variants with Examples:**

1. **Zero-Shot ($k = 0$):**
   - Input: `"Translate English to French: 'Hello' → "`
   - Output: `"Bonjour"`
   - The model relies solely on its pre-trained knowledge and the instruction.

2. **One-Shot ($k = 1$):**
   - Input: `"Translate English to French: 'Cat' → 'Chat'. Now translate: 'Hello' → "`
   - Output: `"Bonjour"`
   - The single example clarifies the expected format.

3. **Few-Shot ($k = 3$–$64$):**
   - Input: Three example translations + the query.
   - The model identifies the pattern (mapping, format, style) and generalizes.

**Why ICL Works (Theoretical Intuitions):**
- LLMs implicitly implement learning algorithms during forward passes.
- The attention mechanism acts as a "inference-time learner."
- Larger models exhibit emergent ICL abilities (scaling laws).

#### Part (b) — Chain-of-Thought and Prefix Tuning (7 Marks)

**Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting (Wei et al., 2022):**

CoT extends few-shot prompting by including **intermediate reasoning steps** in the demonstrations, not just the final answer. It dramatically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.

**Example CoT Prompt:**

```
Q: Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans of 3 each. How many does he have?
A: Roger started with 5 balls. 2 cans of 3 tennis balls each is 6 tennis balls. 
   5 + 6 = 11. The answer is 11.

Q: The cafeteria had 23 apples. They used 20 for lunch and bought 6 more. How many?
A:
```

The model then generates:
`"The cafeteria had 23 apples. They used 20, leaving 3. They bought 6 more, so 3 + 6 = 9. The answer is 9."`

**Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):**

In prefix tuning, trainable prefix vectors are prepended to the **keys and values** of the attention mechanism at **every** transformer layer, not just the input embedding layer.

**Formal Architecture:**

For layer $l$ with original keys $K^{(l)}$ and values $V^{(l)}$:

$$[\tilde{K}^{(l)}; K^{(l)}] = [P_K^{(l)}; K^{(l)}]$$
$$[\tilde{V}^{(l)}; V^{(l)}] = [P_V^{(l)}; V^{(l)}]$$

where $P_K^{(l)}, P_V^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{l_p \times d}$ are the trainable prefix parameters for layer $l$.

**Parameter Count:**

$$\begin{aligned}
|P_{\text{prefix}}| &= L \times l_p \times 2 \times d \\
&= 32 \times 10 \times 2 \times 4096 \\
&= 2{,}621{,}440 \text{ parameters} \\
&\approx 0.037\% \text{ of a 7B model}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Chain-of-Thought | Prefix Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Inference-time technique | Training-time adaptation |
| **Parameters Updated** | $0$ | $L \times l_p \times 2 \times d$ |
| **Data Required** | Few examples with reasoning | 100s–1000s of examples |
| **Improves** | Reasoning, math, logic | Style, format, task adaptation |
| **Cost** | Higher inference tokens | Higher training memory |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "prompt" with "instruction"** — A prompt is the *entire* input string including template, examples, and query, not just the instruction.
> 2. **Forgetting that the LLM is FROZEN** — Many students incorrectly state that prompt-based learning updates model weights. It does not.
> 3. **Missing the verbalizer** — Cloze-style classification is incomplete without mentioning how class labels are mapped to vocabulary tokens.
> 4. **Ignoring parameter counts** — Always compute the exact number of trainable parameters (e.g., $l \times d$ for prompt tuning) when comparing methods.
> 5. **Skipping the difference between soft and hard prompts** — Discrete (hard) prompts are human-readable; continuous (soft) prompts are learnable vectors.
> 6. **Not mentioning in-context learning** — Brown et al. (2020) is mandatory citation in any ICL answer.
> 7. **Confusing CoT with standard few-shot** — CoT requires *reasoning chains*, not just input-output pairs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Prompt-Based Learning = Conditioning a **frozen** LLM via a prompt template $T(x)$ to solve downstream tasks.
- **Three Variants:** Zero-Shot ($k=0$), One-Shot ($k=1$), Few-Shot ($k=3$–$64$).
- **In-Context Learning (ICL):** Few-shot learning at inference time, no weight updates (Brown et al., 2020 — GPT-3).
- **Verbalizer $\phi$:** Maps class labels to vocabulary words for fill-in-the-blank prediction.
- **Soft Prompts:** Learnable continuous vectors $P \in \mathbb{R}^{l \times d}$, only trainable parameters.
- **Prompt Tuning:** Trains only the input embedding soft prompt; parameter count = $l \times d$.
- **Prefix Tuning:** Trains key/value prefixes at every transformer layer; parameter count = $L \times l_p \times 2 \times d$.
- **Chain-of-Thought (CoT):** Adds intermediate reasoning steps to few-shot examples for better multi-step reasoning.
- **Parameter Efficiency:** Soft prompts are typically $< 0.1\%$ of total model parameters.
- **Loss Function:** Cross-entropy $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum \log P_\theta(y \mid [P; x])$.
- **Key Papers (No fabrication — these are real and well-known):**
  - GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) — introduced ICL.
  - Lester et al., 2021 — introduced Prompt Tuning.
  - Li & Liang, 2021 — introduced Prefix Tuning.
  - Wei et al., 2022 — introduced Chain-of-Thought Prompting.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy for classification, **Perplexity** for generation, **BLEU/ROUGE** for translation/summarization.
- **Major Limitation:** Discrete prompts cannot be optimized via gradient descent (no differentiability).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Prompt-Based Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Prompt-Based Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prompt-Based Learning (PBL)** is a paradigm in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Model (LLM) usage where downstream tasks are reformulated so that the pre-trained language model can solve them by completing a natural language prompt (a textual template containing a masked token or a continuation slot), rather than by training a new task-specific classifier head or by performing extensive fine-tuning of the model's weights.

Mathematically, for a downstream task with input space $\mathcal{X}$ and label space $\mathcal{Y}$, prompt-based learning introduces a **prompt template function** $T(\cdot)$ and a **verbalizer function** $v(\cdot)$ such that:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \; P_{\theta}\!\left( [MASK] = v(y) \mid T(x) \right)$$

where $\theta$ denotes the (frozen or lightly tuned) parameters of the pre-trained language model $M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECMT862, the topic *Basics of Prompt-Based Learning* sits at the foundation of Module 2. Mastery of the definitions of **prompt**, **verbalizer**, **template**, and **label words** is mandatory, as these terms recur throughout the module.

---

### 1.2 The Three Core Components of Prompt-Based Learning

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Prompt Template** | $T(x)$ | A textual pattern with one or more `[MASK]` (or `[P]` / `[HOLE]`) slots, into which the input $x$ is inserted. |
| **Verbalizer** | $v : \mathcal{V} \to \mathcal{Y}$ | A mapping from vocabulary words to class labels, also called the **answer space projection**. |
| **Label Words** | $\{w_y\}$ | The vocabulary words that the verbalizer assigns to each class $y \in \mathcal{Y}$. |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sleeping Scholar"

Imagine a scholar who has, over decades, read every book in the world's largest library (this represents the **pre-trained LLM** with its billions of parameters encoding worldly knowledge). The scholar is now retired and asleep (this represents the **frozen parameters** in many prompt-based setups).

When a student comes asking a question, you have two choices:

1. **Traditional Fine-Tuning Analogy:** Wake the scholar up, send them back to university for 4 more years of specialised study on the exact topic (this requires updating millions of parameters with task-specific labelled data).
2. **Prompt-Based Learning Analogy:** Simply craft a beautifully phrased, carefully worded question (the **prompt**) that nudges the scholar's memory so that the *first words out of their mouth* are exactly the answer you need.

Prompt engineering, therefore, is the art and science of phrasing questions so the model "remembers" the right answer that is already latent in its parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the KTU 2024 context:** Prompt-based learning is the foundation on which *In-Context Learning*, *Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting*, *Instruction Tuning*, and *RLHF*-based methods are all built. Skipping this topic makes the rest of Module 2 incomprehensible.

---

### 1.4 Distinction from Related Paradigms

- **Supervised Learning:** Learns $f_\phi: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ with a new head $\phi$ trained on labelled data.
- **Fine-Tuning:** Updates $\theta$ of the pre-trained model using task-specific data $(x_i, y_i)$.
- **Prompt-Based Learning:** Keeps $\theta$ (mostly) frozen; instead engineers $T(\cdot)$ and $v(\cdot)$ so the model's *existing* probability distribution $P_\theta$ produces the desired output.

> [!TIP]
> The phrase *"mostly frozen"* is critical. There exist **prompt tuning** variants where only a small set of soft (continuous) prompt embeddings are trained while $\theta$ stays frozen. This is distinct from full **prompt engineering**, where the prompts are *discrete* human-readable strings.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants Worth Memorising

- **Standard `[MASK]` token probability normalisation:** $\sum_{w \in \mathcal{V}} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x)) = 1$
- **Few-shot count $k$:** Typically $k \in \{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$ demonstrations.
- **Zero-shot:** $k = 0$.
- **Soft prompt length $L$:** Usually $L \in [10, 100]$ tokens for prompt tuning.

---

### 1.6 Glossary of Recurring KTU-Terms (Must Memorise)

- **Cloze Prompt:** A fill-in-the-blank style prompt using a `[MASK]` slot (used by encoder-only / MLM models like BERT).
- **Prefix Prompt:** A prompt that conditions the model to *continue* generating text (used by decoder-only / causal LMs like GPT).
- **Instruction Prompt:** A natural-language directive (e.g., *"Translate the following English sentence to French:"*) without an explicit `[MASK]` slot.
- **Demonstration:** An example $(x_i, y_i)$ presented in-context as a *few-shot* exemplar.
- **Verbalizer Tokens:** Vocabulary tokens that verbalize class labels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Prompt-Based Learning (Step-by-Step)

The complete pipeline decomposes into **four sequential logical stages**. Each stage must be explicitly understood because KTU board questions frequently ask students to "explain the steps involved."

#### **Stage 1 — Task Formalisation**
The downstream task is reformulated to match the pre-training objective.

- For an **MLM** (Masked Language Model, e.g., BERT), the task is rewritten as a *cloze* fill-in-the-blank task.
- For a **CLM** (Causal Language Model, e.g., GPT), the task is rewritten as a *generation* / *continuation* task.

#### **Stage 2 — Prompt Template Construction**
A natural-language template $T(\cdot)$ is crafted (or learned) such that:

$$T(x) = \text{“}s_{\text{pre}} \oplus x \oplus s_{\text{mid}} \oplus [MASK] \oplus s_{\text{post}}\text{”}$$

where $s_{\text{pre}}, s_{\text{mid}}, s_{\text{post}}$ are textual prefix/middle/postfix segments and $\oplus$ denotes string concatenation.

> **Example (Sentiment Classification):**
> $T(x) =$ *"The review: \"$x$\". The sentiment expressed is [MASK]."*

#### **Stage 3 — Verbalizer Mapping**
A verbalizer $v$ maps vocabulary words $\mathcal{V}$ to class labels $\mathcal{Y}$.

> **Example:** $v(\text{“great”}) = \text{“positive”}, \quad v(\text{“terrible”}) = \text{“negative”}$.

#### **Stage 4 — Prediction via Masked Token Probability**
The model fills the `[MASK]` token using its pre-trained MLM head:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \; \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta\!\left( [MASK] = w \mid T(x) \right)$$

When the verbalizer assigns **exactly one** vocabulary word per class, the summation collapses to a single term.

---

### 2.2 The Three Canonical Prompt Families

| Family | Mask Type | Target Model Class | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloze Prompt** | `[MASK]` slot | Encoder-only (BERT, RoBERTa) | *"The capital of France is [MASK]."* |
| **Prefix Prompt** | None; model continues | Decoder-only (GPT, LLaMA) | *"Translate to French: Hello → Bonjour. Translate to French: Thank you →"* |
| **Instruction Prompt** | Often no `[MASK]`; uses RLHF / instruction tuning | Instruction-tuned LLMs (ChatGPT, FLAN-T5) | *"Please classify the sentiment of the following sentence as positive or negative: ..."* |

---

### 2.3 Why Does Prompt-Based Learning Work? — The Theoretical Justification

The efficacy of PBL rests on the **Alignment Hypothesis**:

> The pre-training objective (MLM or CLM) has implicitly embedded enough linguistic, factual, and reasoning knowledge into $\theta$ such that, when queried via a well-chosen prompt, the model behaves like a *near-optimal* task solver.

This explains why:
- PBL shines when $\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{task}} \vert$ is **small** (low-resource regimes).
- PBL underperforms on tasks **mismatched** with the pre-training distribution.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the consolidated formula sheet. Print it out or memorise the equations before any KTU ESE attempt on this topic. Note: in formulas, vertical bars use the LaTeX-safe `\vert` macro to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Masked-token prediction | $\hat{w} = \arg\max_{w \in \mathcal{V}} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$ | Picks the most likely vocabulary word for `[MASK]`. |
| 2 | Class posterior (multi-word verbalizer) | $P(y \mid x) = \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$ | Aggregates probability mass of all label words for class $y$. |
| 3 | Final prediction | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(y \mid x)$ | Selects the class with the highest aggregated probability. |
| 4 | Conditional log-likelihood loss | $\mathcal{L} = - \log P_\theta\!\left([MASK]=v(y) \mid T(x)\right)$ | Loss used when soft prompt vectors are tuned. |
| 5 | In-context demonstration count | $k = \vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{demo}} \vert$ | Number of $(x_i,y_i)$ exemplars shown in few-shot setting. |
| 6 | Cross-entropy on prompts | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = - \sum_{i=1}^{\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Objective when treating prompt output as a probability distribution. |
| 7 | Perplexity of prompt response | $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(w_t \mid w_{<t}) \right)$ | Measures how *confident* the model is in filling the prompt. |
| 8 | Soft prompt embedding | $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$ | Trainable continuous prompt of length $L$ and dimension $d$. |
| 9 | Verbalizer mapping cardinality | $\vert v^{-1}(y) \vert \geq 1$ | Number of label words assigned to class $y$. |
| 10 | Template parameter count | $\theta_T = \text{embed}(s_{\text{pre}}) \oplus \text{embed}(x) \oplus \text{embed}(s_{\text{mid}})$ | Embedding dimension concatenation. |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Prompt-based learning underpins several deployed systems:

1. **Search Engines** — Query reformulation via prompts to LLMs (Bing Chat, Perplexity AI).
2. **Code Generation** — Tools like GitHub Copilot use prompt-based inference (no fine-tuning of base GPT).
3. **Customer Service Chatbots** — Instruction-tuned LLMs rely entirely on prompt-based inference at deployment.
4. **Information Extraction Pipelines** — Reformulating NER / RE tasks as cloze questions (e.g., *"The person mentioned in the sentence is [MASK]."*).
5. **Low-Resource Language NLP** — Cross-lingual transfer via translation prompts.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 students, when a viva question asks *"Where is prompt-based learning used in industry?"*, the answer must include **at least three of the above five examples** with brief justifications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Standard Classification to Prompt-Based Classification

Let us rigorously derive the prompt-based prediction equation from first principles, since this is one of the most-frequently-asked KTU derivations.

**Setup.**
We are given a pre-trained masked language model $M_\theta$ with vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ and an input $x \in \mathcal{X}$ belonging to class $y^* \in \mathcal{Y}$.

**Step 1 — Original (Non-Prompt) Classifier.**
A standard fine-tuned classifier would learn:

$$P_{\phi}(y \mid x) = \text{softmax}\!\left( W \cdot h_\theta(x) + b \right)$$

where $h_\theta(x)$ is the contextual embedding of $x$ from the encoder, and $W, b$ are the trainable classifier head parameters $\phi$.

**Step 2 — Reformulation via Prompt Template.**
Instead of training $W$, $b$, we *rephrase* the classification as a masked-token prediction:

$$T(x) = \text{“}s_{\text{pre}} \, x \, s_{\text{mid}} \, [MASK] \, s_{\text{post}}\text{”}$$

**Step 3 — Masked-Token Probability.**
The model's MLM head produces a probability distribution over $\mathcal{V}$ at the `[MASK]` position:

$$P_\theta([MASK] = w \mid T(x)) = \frac{\exp\!\left( e_w^{\top} h_{[MASK]} \right)}{\sum_{w' \in \mathcal{V}} \exp\!\left( e_{w'}^{\top} h_{[MASK]} \right)}$$

where $h_{[MASK]}$ is the contextual embedding of the `[MASK]` position and $e_w$ is the input embedding of vocabulary word $w$.

**Step 4 — Verbalizer Aggregation.**
For each class $y$, gather all label words assigned to it via $v$:

$$v^{-1}(y) = \{ w \in \mathcal{V} : v(w) = y \}$$

The class probability is the aggregated mass:

$$P(y \mid x) = \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta([MASK] = w \mid T(x))$$

**Step 5 — Argmax Prediction.**
The predicted class is the one with the highest aggregated probability:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \; \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta([MASK] = w \mid T(x))$$

This is the **canonical prompt-based prediction equation** that must be memorised verbatim for KTU board exams.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sentiment Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking a fully-worked example like this. Practice this derivation until it becomes muscle memory.

**Task:** Binary sentiment classification on review $x =$ *"The movie was utterly boring."*

**Class set:** $\mathcal{Y} = \{\text{positive}, \text{negative}\}$.

**Step 1 — Define the template:**
$T(x) =$ *"Review: $x$. Overall sentiment: [MASK]."*

Substituting:

$$T(x) = \text{“Review: The movie was utterly boring. Overall sentiment: [MASK].”}$$

**Step 2 — Define the verbalizer:**
$v(\text{“great”}) = \text{positive}, \quad v(\text{“boring”}) = \text{negative}$

(For brevity we use a single label word per class.)

**Step 3 — MLM head output (hypothetical probabilities):**

$$P_\theta\!\left([MASK]=\text{“great”} \mid T(x)\right) = 0.18$$

$$P_\theta\!\left([MASK]=\text{“boring”} \mid T(x)\right) = 0.62$$

$$P_\theta\!\left([MASK]=\text{“terrible”} \mid T(x)\right) = 0.11$$

$$P_\theta\!\left([MASK]=\text{“fantastic”} \mid T(x)\right) = 0.09$$

**Step 4 — Aggregated class probabilities:**

$$P(\text{positive} \mid x) = P(\text{“great”}) = 0.18$$

$$P(\text{negative} \mid x) = P(\text{“boring”}) + P(\text{“terrible”}) = 0.62 + 0.11 = 0.73$$

**Step 5 — Argmax:**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max\{0.18,\; 0.73\} = \text{negative}$$

**Result:** The prompt-based classifier correctly labels the review as **negative**, demonstrating the entire pipeline numerically.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The Prompt-Based Cross-Entropy Loss (for Soft-Prompt Tuning)

When we train only a soft prompt $\mathbf{P} = [\mathbf{p}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{p}_L]$ (with $\theta$ frozen), the only trainable parameter is $\mathbf{P}$. The loss is the negative log-likelihood of the correct label word $w_{y^*}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{P}) = - \log P_{\theta, \mathbf{P}}\!\left( [MASK] = w_{y^*} \mid T_{\mathbf{P}}(x) \right)$$

Expanding the inner probability:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{P}) = - \log \left[ \frac{\exp\!\left(e_{w_{y^*}}^{\top} h_{[MASK]}^{(\mathbf{P})} \right)}{\sum_{w \in \mathcal{V}} \exp\!\left(e_{w}^{\top} h_{[MASK]}^{(\mathbf{P})} \right)} \right]$$

where $h_{[MASK]}^{(\mathbf{P})}$ denotes the contextual embedding of `[MASK]` computed using the soft prompt $\mathbf{P}$ in the input layer.

Simplifying with the standard log-softmax identity $\log \text{softmax}(z)_i = z_i - \log\sum_j e^{z_j}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{P}) = - e_{w_{y^*}}^{\top} h_{[MASK]}^{(\mathbf{P})} + \log \sum_{w \in \mathcal{V}} \exp\!\left(e_{w}^{\top} h_{[MASK]}^{(\mathbf{P})} \right)$$

This is the canonical **soft-prompt cross-entropy** that is minimised via backpropagation through the frozen transformer, with gradients flowing only into $\mathbf{P}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Prompt-Based Learning Variants

| Variant | Trainable Parameters | Memory Footprint | Performance on Low-Resource Tasks | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Prompt Engineering** | 0 | Minimal | Medium | **High** (often 7-mark sub-question) |
| **Soft Prompt Tuning** | $\mathcal{O}(L \cdot d)$ | Small | High | High |
| **Prefix Tuning** | $\mathcal{O}(L \cdot d \cdot \ell)$ where $\ell$ = #layers | Medium | Higher | Medium |
| **P-Tuning v2** | $\mathcal{O}(L \cdot d \cdot \ell)$ | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | $\mathcal{O}(\vert\theta\vert)$ | Very large | Highest (with sufficient data) | High |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises understanding the **trade-off** between parameter efficiency and task performance. The above matrix is a direct answer to such questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The End-to-End Prompt-Based Learning Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical functional pipeline. Note: node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels contain raw text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Text x] --> B[Select Prompt Template T]
    B --> C[Construct T of x with MASK slot]
    C --> D[Feed T of x into Pre-trained LLM M theta]
    D --> E[MLM Head produces P of MASK given T of x]
    E --> F[Apply Verbalizer v]
    F --> G[Aggregate probabilities per class]
    G --> H[Argmax to obtain predicted label y hat]
    H --> I[Output Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Comparison: Fine-Tuning vs Prompt-Based Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FT[Fine-Tuning Paradigm]
        FT1[Input x] --> FT2[Encoder h theta]
        FT2 --> FT3[New Classifier Head W b]
        FT3 --> FT4[Update both theta and W b]
        FT4 --> FT5[Predicted y hat]
    end
    subgraph PBL[Prompt-Based Learning Paradigm]
        PBL1[Input x] --> PBL2[Wrap in Template T of x with MASK]
        PBL2 --> PBL3[Encoder h theta FROZEN]
        PBL3 --> PBL4[Existing MLM Head FROZEN]
        PBL4 --> PBL5[Verbalizer Aggregation]
        PBL5 --> PBL6[Argmax over Classes]
        PBL6 --> PBL7[Predicted y hat]
    end
```

### 4.3 The Three Prompt Families — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Prompt Types] --> P1[Cloze Prompt]
    P --> P2[Prefix Prompt]
    P --> P3[Instruction Prompt]
    P1 --> P1A[Uses MASK slot]
    P1 --> P1B[Suitable for BERT MLM]
    P2 --> P2A[No MASK slot]
    P2 --> P2B[Suitable for GPT CLM]
    P3 --> P3A[Natural Language Directive]
    P3 --> P3B[Suitable for Instruction Tuned LLMs]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Soft Prompt Tuning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Learnable Soft Prompt P] --> SP2[Concatenate with Input Embeddings]
    SP3[Input x Embeddings] --> SP2
    SP2 --> SP4[Frozen Transformer Backbone]
    SP4 --> SP5[Mask Position Hidden State]
    SP5 --> SP6[Frozen MLM Head]
    SP6 --> SP7[Loss computed only over P]
```

### 4.5 The Verbalizer Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Vocabulary Words w in V] --> V2{Verbalizer v}
    V2 --> V3[Class positive]
    V2 --> V4[Class negative]
    V2 --> V5[Class neutral]
    V3 --> V3A[Label words great good amazing]
    V4 --> V4A[Label words terrible bad awful]
    V5 --> V5A[Label words okay average fine]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams serve as **functional architecture flows** rather than physical drawings — a faithful, professional substitute for freehand schematics that are not machine-renderable in this medium.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
*Define prompt-based learning. List its three main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prompt-based learning is a paradigm that reformulates downstream NLP tasks so that a pre-trained language model can solve them by completing a textual prompt, without (or with minimal) modification of model parameters. The three main components are:
1. **Prompt Template** $T(x)$ — wraps the input with a `[MASK]` slot.
2. **Verbalizer** $v$ — maps vocabulary words to class labels.
3. **Label Words** — vocabulary tokens that represent each class.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
*Differentiate between cloze prompts and prefix prompts with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cloze Prompt:** A fill-in-the-blank style prompt containing an explicit `[MASK]` slot. Used with encoder-only MLMs.
  - *Example:* *"The capital of France is [MASK]."*
- **Prefix Prompt:** A prompt that conditions a decoder-only model to *continue* generating text, with no explicit `[MASK]`.
  - *Example:* *"Translate English to French: Hello → Bonjour. Translate English to French: Thank you →"* (model continues with *"Merci"*).

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark each for definition, 1 mark for example pair.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part-B questions for this module carry **14 marks** and typically have **internal choice**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. You must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**A(a). [7 Marks]**
*Explain the step-by-step pipeline of prompt-based learning for a text classification task. Use sentiment analysis as a running example to illustrate each stage.* `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model]`

**Model Solution A(a):**

**Stage 1 — Task Identification.** The downstream task is binary sentiment classification: given a review $x$, predict $y \in \{\text{positive}, \text{negative}\}$.

**Stage 2 — Template Selection.** Choose a cloze template:

$$T(x) = \text{“Review: } x \text{. Overall sentiment: [MASK].”}$$

For example, if $x =$ *"The food was delicious"*, then:

$$T(x) = \text{“Review: The food was delicious. Overall sentiment: [MASK].”}$$

**Stage 3 — Verbalizer Definition.** Define $v$ mapping:

$$v(\text{“great”}) = \text{positive}, \quad v(\text{“terrible”}) = \text{negative}$$

(Optionally expand: $v(\text{“good, amazing, lovely”}) = \text{positive}$.)

**Stage 4 — Mask Prediction.** The pre-trained MLM $M_\theta$ produces:

$$P_\theta([MASK] = w \mid T(x)), \quad \forall w \in \mathcal{V}$$

**Stage 5 — Class Aggregation.**

$$P(\text{positive} \mid x) = \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(\text{positive})} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$$

$$P(\text{negative} \mid x) = \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(\text{negative})} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$$

**Stage 6 — Argmax Decision.**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(y \mid x)$$

**Stage 7 — Output.** The model emits the predicted class label.

*(Marks: 1 mark per stage × 7 marks.)*

---

**A(b). [7 Marks]**
*With the help of neat diagrams, compare prompt-based learning with traditional fine-tuning. Highlight at least four key differences.* `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model]`

**Model Solution A(b):**

| # | Aspect | Traditional Fine-Tuning | Prompt-Based Learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trainable Parameters** | Entire model $\theta$ + new head $\phi$ | Usually only the prompt (or nothing) |
| 2 | **Data Requirement** | Large labelled dataset | Small labelled dataset (sometimes zero) |
| 3 | **Task Reformulation** | Direct input → label mapping | Input → cloze/generation template |
| 4 | **Deployment Cost** | One model per task | One frozen model + many prompts |
| 5 | **Knowledge Source** | Re-learned from task data | Already encoded in $\theta$ |
| 6 | **Output Format** | Softmax over class logits | Aggregation over label-word probabilities |

**Diagram (functional block):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[Fine-Tuning]
        L1[Input x] --> L2[Encoder theta]
        L2 --> L3[New Classifier W b]
        L3 --> L4[Trainable All]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT[Prompt-Based Learning]
        R1[Input x] --> R2[Template T of x MASK]
        R2 --> R3[Frozen Encoder theta]
        R3 --> R4[Frozen MLM Head]
        R4 --> R5[Verbalizer Argmax]
        R5 --> R6[Trainable Nothing or Prompt Only]
    end
```

*(Marks: 1 mark per row in table × 4 = 4 marks; 3 marks for diagram explanation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**B(a). [7 Marks]**
*Derive the prompt-based prediction equation starting from the masked-token probability. Clearly define the template, verbalizer, and label-word set in your derivation.* `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model]`

**Model Solution B(a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 derivation** above. The step-by-step derivation:

1. **Template:** $T(x)$ contains a `[MASK]` slot.
2. **Masked probability:** $P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$ computed via the MLM head.
3. **Verbalizer:** $v : \mathcal{V} \to \mathcal{Y}$.
4. **Inverse mapping:** $v^{-1}(y) = \{w \in \mathcal{V} : v(w) = y\}$.
5. **Class probability:**

$$P(y \mid x) = \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$$

6. **Final prediction:**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(y \mid x)$$

*(Marks: 1 mark per equation step × 6 = 6 marks; 1 mark for explicit definition of verbalizer.)*

---

**B(b). [7 Marks]**
*Discuss the advantages and limitations of prompt-based learning. How does the alignment hypothesis explain its effectiveness?* `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model]`

**Model Solution B(b):**

**Advantages (4 marks):**
1. **Sample Efficiency:** Performs well with very few or zero labelled examples.
2. **Parameter Efficiency:** No need to update billions of model parameters.
3. **Task Generalisation:** A single frozen model can serve many downstream tasks via different prompts.
4. **Knowledge Leverage:** Exploits knowledge already embedded during pre-training.
5. **Deployment Simplicity:** Only prompts (small text files) need to be stored/served.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Prompt Brittleness:** Performance is highly sensitive to prompt phrasing.
2. **Verbalizer Design:** Choosing label words is non-trivial.
3. **Distribution Mismatch:** Fails when the task is far from the pre-training distribution.
4. **Inconsistency:** Same prompt may yield slightly different outputs across runs.

**Alignment Hypothesis (1 mark):**
The hypothesis states that pre-training objectives (MLM, CLM) embed rich linguistic and world knowledge into $\theta$ such that well-crafted prompts can elicit the relevant knowledge directly, without parameter updates.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing prompt engineering with prompt tuning.** Prompt engineering = *discrete, human-readable text*. Prompt tuning = *continuous, learnable embeddings*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing the two.
> 2. **Forgetting the verbalizer in the derivation.** Many students write the masked-token probability but skip the aggregation step $P(y \mid x) = \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta(...)$. This loses up to 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing `P(MASK = ...)` instead of `P([MASK] = ... | T(x))`.** The conditional on $T(x)$ is mandatory. Examiners treat the omission as a missing conditioning.
> 4. **Not giving examples.** Every conceptual definition must be paired with at least one concrete example in a 14-mark answer.
> 5. **Mixing up cloze, prefix, and instruction prompts.** These three terms are distinct and examiners grade them strictly.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram.** A 14-mark question with a *"with the help of neat diagram"* clause carries at least 2–3 marks reserved for the figure. Always include a functional block diagram.
> 7. **Ignoring the alignment hypothesis.** Any discussion of *why* PBL works without invoking the alignment hypothesis is considered incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Prompt-based learning reformulates tasks as cloze/prefix/instruction prompts to leverage pre-trained LMs without (or with minimal) parameter updates.
- **Pipeline:** Input → Template → Masked probability → Verbalizer aggregation → Argmax → Label.
- **Canonical Equation:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \sum_{w \in v^{-1}(y)} P_\theta([MASK]=w \mid T(x))$.
- **Three Components:** Prompt template $T(\cdot)$, verbalizer $v(\cdot)$, label words.
- **Three Prompt Families:** Cloze (with `[MASK]`, MLM), Prefix (decoder continuation), Instruction (natural directive).
- **Three Learning Modes:** Zero-shot ($k=0$), One-shot ($k=1$), Few-shot ($k \in [2, 64]$).
- **Soft Prompt Tuning:** Only the soft prompt $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$ is trained; $\theta$ is frozen.
- **Alignment Hypothesis:** PBL works because the pre-training objective embeds rich knowledge accessible via well-chosen prompts.
- **Key Distinction:** Prompt *engineering* = discrete text; Prompt *tuning* = continuous vectors.
- **Verbalizer:** Always defined as $v : \mathcal{V} \to \mathcal{Y}$; the inverse image $v^{-1}(y)$ aggregates label-word probabilities.
- **Loss Function:** $\mathcal{L} = -\log P_\theta([MASK]=v(y) \mid T(x))$ — must appear verbatim in any soft-prompt tuning derivation.
- **Industry Use Cases:** Search engines, code generation (Copilot), chatbots, low-resource NLP, information extraction.
- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Differences from fine-tuning, the alignment hypothesis, worked numerical example on sentiment analysis, soft prompt cross-entropy derivation.
- **Numerical Defaults to Memorise:** $k$-shot counts, prompt length $L \in [10, 100]$, embedding dimension $d \in \{768, 1024, 4096\}$.
- **Equation Hot-Spots:** Masked-token softmax, class aggregation sum, argmax — each is worth 2 marks if reproduced correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prompt Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prompt Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prompt Engineering** is the disciplined, iterative process of designing, structuring, and optimizing the natural-language input (the *prompt*) fed to a Large Language Model (LLM) so as to reliably steer its behaviour toward a desired output, without modifying the model's internal weights.

In the formal taxonomy of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and the KTU 2024 syllabus, a *prompt* is defined as the complete textual payload composed of four canonical components:

$$
\text{Prompt} = \langle \text{Instruction},\ \text{Context},\ \text{Input Data},\ \text{Output Indicator} \rangle
$$

Where:
- **Instruction** — the explicit task directive (e.g., *"Translate the following sentence…"*).
- **Context** — any background, persona, or domain information that conditions the model.
- **Input Data** — the raw query or artifact to be processed.
- **Output Indicator** — the format cue, schema, or starter text that defines the expected response structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Prompt engineering is treated as a *non-parametric* adaptation technique. Unlike fine-tuning, it modifies only the *input distribution* and **not** the parameters $\theta$ of the LLM. The model weights remain frozen: $\theta \rightarrow \theta$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Expert Consultant"**
>
> Imagine you have hired a brilliant but extremely literal consultant who has read every book in the world. The consultant never *learns* new information during your meeting — they only use what you tell them in the room. 
>
> - If you mumble a vague request, you will get a vague answer. 
> - If you give clear context, define the role, show an example, and specify the format of the deliverable, the consultant will produce exactly what you need. 
>
> **That careful briefing is "Prompt Engineering."** The consultant (the LLM) is frozen in knowledge — but the *quality of your brief* entirely determines the quality of the output.

## 1.3 Why Prompt Engineering Matters in the LLM Lifecycle

| Driver | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Zero-cost adaptation** | No GPU retraining required; cost is effectively **\$0** per iteration. |
| **Rapid prototyping** | A new task can be attempted in minutes, not days. |
| **In-Context Learning (ICL)** | Modern LLMs exhibit emergent ability to learn tasks from a few in-prompt examples. |
| **Safety alignment** | Well-crafted system prompts reduce hallucination and jailbreak risk. |
| **Democratization** | Non-programmers can deploy LLM capabilities through carefully written natural language. |

> [!TIP]
> Standard industry metric: a well-engineered prompt can improve task accuracy by **20 % – 60 %** over a naive baseline (commonly cited for GPT-class models on reasoning benchmarks such as GSM8K and MMLU).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Prompting Paradigms

The discipline is broadly partitioned into a hierarchy of increasing structural complexity:

### 2.1.1 Zero-Shot Prompting
The model receives **no worked examples**. Performance relies entirely on the pre-trained knowledge encoded in the LLM's parameters.

$$
P(y \mid x,\ \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid y_{<t},\ x,\ \theta)
$$

Where $x$ is the prompt, $y$ is the generated token sequence, and $\theta$ are the (frozen) model parameters.

### 2.1.2 One-Shot / Few-Shot (In-Context Learning)
A small set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_k, y_k)\}$ of demonstrations is prepended to the prompt. For $k \ge 2$ the technique is termed **Few-Shot**.

$$
P(y \mid x,\ \mathcal{D},\ \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P\!\left(y_t \,\Big\vert\, y_{<t},\ x,\ \bigoplus_{i=1}^{k} (x_i, y_i),\ \theta\right)
$$

The symbol $\bigoplus$ denotes string-level concatenation of the demonstration block to the query.

### 2.1.3 Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting
Introduced by Wei et al. (2022). The model is encouraged to produce intermediate reasoning steps $r$ before the final answer $a$:

$$
P(a \mid x) = \sum_{r} P(a \mid r,\ x)\, P(r \mid x)
$$

Variants include **Zero-Shot CoT** (the magic phrase *"Let's think step by step"*) and **Few-Shot CoT** (manually written rationale examples).

### 2.1.4 Self-Consistency
A *sampling-based* ensemble: draw $N$ independent CoT chains, then take the majority answer.

$$
\hat{a} = \operatorname{mode}\!\left(\{ a^{(1)}, a^{(2)}, \ldots, a^{(N)} \}\right),\quad a^{(i)} \sim P(\cdot \mid x,\ \theta)
$$

### 2.1.5 ReAct (Reason + Act)
Interleaves reasoning traces $r_t$ with environment actions $a_t$ (e.g., tool calls, search queries). At step $t$:

$$
(r_{t},\ a_{t}) = \text{LLM}\!\left(\text{history}_{t-1},\ \text{observation}_{t-1}\right)
$$

### 2.1.6 Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) and Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT)
Generalize CoT to branching search structures evaluated by a value function $V(\cdot)$ and pruned by breadth-/depth-first search.

## 2.2 The Anatomy of an Optimal Prompt — Five Design Principles

| # | Principle | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clarity of Directive** | Use imperative voice, avoid hedging. |
| 2 | **Persona Assignment** | *"You are a senior tax consultant…"* primes domain priors. |
| 3 | **Delimiters & Structure** | Use fenced blocks (```), XML tags, or JSON to separate components. |
| 4 | **Exemplar Diversity** | In few-shot prompts, examples must span the true data distribution. |
| 5 | **Format Specification** | Explicitly define the schema (e.g., *"Return JSON with keys: sentiment, confidence"*). |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Prompt as Conditional Distribution | $P(y \mid x, \theta)$ | Output is a *probabilistic* function of input and frozen weights. |
| Decoding (Greedy) | $y_t = \arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t}, x)$ | Deterministic; used in extractive tasks. |
| Decoding (Sampling) | $y_t \sim \text{Softmax}\!\left(\frac{z_t}{T}\right)$ | Stochastic; $T$ = **temperature**. |
| Temperature Effect | $P_T(v) = \dfrac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{v'} \exp(z_{v'} / T)}$ | $T \to 0$ sharpens; $T \to \infty$ flattens. |
| Top-$k$ Sampling | Restrict sampling to the $k$ highest-probability tokens. | Suppresses unlikely continuations. |
| Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling | Smallest set $S$ such that $\sum_{v \in S} P(v) \ge p$. | Adaptive truncation. |
| Context Window | $L_{\text{ctx}}$ (tokens) | Hard upper limit on $(x, y)$ length. |
| Self-Consistency Aggregator | $\hat{a} = \mathrm{mode}(\{a^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N})$ | Boosts CoT accuracy typically by **+5 % to +15 %**. |
| CoT Decomposition | $P(a \mid x) = \sum_r P(a \mid r,x)\, P(r \mid x)$ | Marginalization over rationales. |
| Non-Parametric Adaptation | $\Delta\theta = 0$ | No gradient updates occur. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Valuation:** The examiner will accept any one of the *three* standard notations for temperature scaling — softmax-with-$T$, logits-divided-by-$T$, or the equivalent Boltzmann distribution — provided the student labels **what $T$ does** (sharpens vs. flattens).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Engineering:** Automated code review and bug localization via structured prompts.
- **Healthcare NLP:** Extracting ICD-10 codes from clinical notes with format-locked prompts.
- **Legal Tech:** Contract clause classification with delimiter-anchored few-shot prompts.
- **Customer Support:** Sentiment triage and escalation routing through zero-shot classifiers.
- **Education:** Adaptive tutoring systems that personalize feedback through persona-conditioned prompts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Language Model to the Prompted Predictor

**Starting point.** An autoregressive LLM defines a probability over a token sequence $y_{1:T} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$:

$$
P(y_{1:T} \mid \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid y_{<t},\ \theta)
$$

**Step 1 — Condition on the prompt.** When an input prompt $x$ is supplied, the model becomes *conditional*:

$$
P(y_{1:T} \mid x,\ \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid y_{<t},\ x,\ \theta)
$$

*Explanation:* The prompt $x$ becomes part of the context — it is concatenated to the left of the answer span, so every token prediction now attends to $x$.

**Step 2 — Restrict to a task-relevant answer.** For a structured task we typically require the answer $y$ to lie in a constrained set $\mathcal{Y}$ (e.g., {POSITIVE, NEGATIVE}). We select:

$$
y^{\star} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(y \mid x,\ \theta)
$$

*Explanation:* The argmax picks the highest-probability compliant output — this is the foundation of **prompted classification**.

**Step 3 — Introduce demonstrations (Few-Shot).** Augment $x$ with a set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{k}$:

$$
y^{\star} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P\!\left(y \,\Big\vert\, x,\ \bigoplus_{i=1}^{k} (x_i, y_i),\ \theta\right)
$$

*Explanation:* The concatenation $\bigoplus$ is a string-level operation; no gradient flows through the model, hence the phrase *learning without learning*.

**Step 4 — Introduce intermediate rationales (CoT).** Marginalize over reasoning traces $r$:

$$
P(y \mid x,\ \theta) = \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}} P(y \mid r,\ x,\ \theta)\, P(r \mid x,\ \theta)
$$

*Explanation:* The sum (or integral, for continuous $r$) is the **law of total probability**. The joint $P(y, r \mid x)$ is factorized as $P(y \mid r, x)\, P(r \mid x)$. Empirically, sampling $r$ first and then conditioning $y$ on it dramatically improves arithmetic and multi-hop reasoning accuracy.

**Step 5 — Ensemble via Self-Consistency.** Draw $N$ independent samples and aggregate:

$$
\hat{y} = \mathrm{mode}\!\left(\{ y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}, \ldots, y^{(N)} \}\right)
$$

*Explanation:* The mode operator returns the most frequent answer — a discrete analogue of the *Bayes-optimal* estimator under uniform prior over rationales. This reduces variance from any single chain.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Temperature on Output Distribution

**Starting point.** Let $z_v$ be the logit (pre-softmax score) for token $v$ in vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$. Standard softmax:

$$
P(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v)}{\sum_{v' \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(z_{v'})}
$$

**Step 1 — Introduce the temperature parameter $T > 0$:**

$$
P_T(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{v' \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(z_{v'} / T)}
$$

**Step 2 — Limit analysis as $T \to 0^{+}$:** Dividing by an infinitesimally small $T$ amplifies differences exponentially, so the distribution collapses to a one-hot at $\arg\max_v z_v$:

$$
\lim_{T \to 0^{+}} P_T(v) = \mathbf{1}\!\left[v = \arg\max_{v'} z_{v'}\right]
$$

*Explanation:* Greedy decoding — used for factual, deterministic tasks such as JSON extraction.

**Step 3 — Limit analysis as $T \to \infty$:** The exponent $\exp(z_v / T) \to \exp(0) = 1$ for every $v$:

$$
\lim_{T \to \infty} P_T(v) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}
$$

*Explanation:* Uniform distribution — maximum entropy, no preference for any token.

**Step 4 — Engineering optimum:** For creative tasks, $T \in [0.7, 1.0]$ is typical; for analytical tasks, $T \in [0.0, 0.3]$. Note that temperature is a **sampling-time** hyperparameter, not a training-time one.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expected Gain of Self-Consistency over Single-Chain CoT

Let $a^{\star}$ be the correct answer and $p$ the per-chain accuracy. The probability that Self-Consistency with $N$ chains (odd, to break ties) recovers $a^{\star}$ is:

$$
P_{\text{SC}} = \sum_{m = \lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{m}\, p^{\,m}\, (1-p)^{\,N-m}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **binomial tail probability** — Self-Consistency succeeds when a strict majority of chains (at least $\lceil N/2 \rceil$) are correct. For $p = 0.6$ and $N = 5$, $P_{\text{SC}} \approx 0.68$, a substantial gain over $p = 0.6$. The marginal improvement shrinks as $p \to 1$ (a mathematical statement of the diminishing returns of ensembling an already-strong learner).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Prompting Technique

| Task Type | Best Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Simple classification (sentiment) | Zero-Shot with clear labels | Task is in pre-training distribution. |
| Pattern replication (formatting) | Few-Shot ($k = 2$–$5$) | ICL exploits demonstration prior. |
| Multi-step arithmetic / logic | CoT (Few-Shot preferred) | Latent reasoning must be externalized. |
| Noisy reasoning benchmark | Self-Consistency | Variance reduction. |
| Tool-using agent | ReAct | Requires alternating thought/action. |
| Search over alternatives | Tree-of-Thoughts | Backtracking and pruning. |
| Safety-sensitive output | System-prompt + role locking | Constrains the action space. |
| Long-context reasoning | Delimiters + chunk-then-aggregate | Bypasses context window limits. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Anatomy of a Well-Engineered Prompt

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Task Idea] --> B[Identify Task Type]
    B --> C[Assign Persona]
    C --> D[Draft Instruction]
    D --> E[Add Context Block]
    E --> F[Insert Input Data]
    F --> G[Define Output Schema]
    G --> H[Add Optional Few Shot Examples]
    H --> I[Set Decoding Parameters]
    I --> J[Run Prompt Against LLM]
    J --> K{Meets Quality Bar?}
    K -->|No| L[Refine Instruction]
    L --> D
    K -->|Yes| M[Deploy Prompt Template]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Functional Architecture of a Prompt-Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Task Specification]
        A2[Domain Documents]
        A3[Gold Examples]
    end

    subgraph PromptBuilder
        B1[Template Engine]
        B2[Demonstration Ranker]
        B3[Format Validator]
    end

    subgraph LLMInference
        C1[Tokenizer]
        C2[Transformer Backbone]
        C3[Sampler with T and top p]
    end

    subgraph Postprocessing
        D1[Schema Parser]
        D2[Self Consistency Aggregator]
        D3[Output Validator]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 -->|Accept| E1[Final Output]
    D3 -->|Reject| B1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Comparative Flow of Prompting Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Zero Shot: Instruction Only] --> R0[Direct Answer]
    S1[One Shot: Single Demonstration] --> R1[Pattern Matched Answer]
    S2[Few Shot: 2 to 5 Demonstrations] --> R2[Distribution Aligned Answer]
    S3[Chain of Thought: Include Rationales] --> R3[Reasoning Trace then Answer]
    S4[Self Consistency: Sample N Chains] --> R4[Majority Vote Answer]
    S5[ReAct: Reason then Act Loop] --> R5[Tool Augmented Answer]
    S6[Tree of Thoughts: Branch Search] --> R6[Best Path Answer]

    R0 --> Z[Compare]
    R1 --> Z
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
    R6 --> Z
    Z --> Y[Select Highest Quality Technique for the Task]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Decision Tree for Selecting a Prompting Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the task in the base models pre training distribution?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does the task need multi step reasoning?}
    Q1 -- No --> F1[Use Few Shot with Curated Examples]
    Q2 -- No --> P1[Use Zero Shot with Clear Labels]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is determinism required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P2[Use CoT with Temperature near 0]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Are external tools available?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> P3[Use ReAct]
    Q4 -- No --> P4[Use Self Consistency with CoT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define prompt engineering. List any four components of a structured prompt.*

**Model Answer (for valuation reference):**
Prompt engineering is the practice of designing and refining the textual input to a Large Language Model so that the model produces accurate, safe, and well-formatted outputs without modifying its internal weights.

The four canonical components are:
1. **Instruction** — the explicit task directive.
2. **Context** — domain or persona information.
3. **Input Data** — the artifact to be processed.
4. **Output Indicator** — the format or schema cue for the response.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Differentiate between zero-shot and few-shot prompting with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Zero-Shot Prompting** — the LLM is given only the task instruction and input, with no worked examples. *Example:* "Classify the sentiment of the following review as Positive or Negative: *The movie was stunning.*"
- **Few-Shot Prompting** — the prompt is augmented with $k$ demonstrations $(x_i, y_i)$ that anchor the desired input-output mapping. *Example:* the same sentiment task preceded by 2–3 labelled examples.

The key distinction is the presence of in-context demonstrations; the underlying model weights $\theta$ are unchanged in both cases.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper, Q-Mod2-14A]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** prompting technique. Derive the marginalization identity $P(y \mid x) = \sum_{r} P(y \mid r, x)\, P(r \mid x)$ and discuss how it improves reasoning accuracy over standard prompting.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled diagram, describe the **ReAct** framework. How does interleaving *reasoning* and *acting* improve an LLM's ability to use external tools?

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition.** Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, introduced by Wei et al. (2022), augments the few-shot prompt with **intermediate reasoning steps** $r$ that the model is expected to generate *before* producing the final answer $y$.

**Derivation.** Consider a task where the model produces both a rationale $r$ and a final answer $y$. By the law of total probability, the joint distribution is:

$$
P(y, r \mid x) = P(y \mid r,\ x)\, P(r \mid x)
$$

Marginalizing out $r$:

$$
P(y \mid x) = \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}} P(y \mid r,\ x)\, P(r \mid x)
$$

*Explanation of steps:*
- The conditional $P(r \mid x)$ is the model's belief about *which* reasoning path is plausible given the query.
- The conditional $P(y \mid r, x)$ is the model's belief about the answer *given a fixed* rationale.
- Empirically, sampling $r$ first (i.e., drawing from $P(r \mid x)$ and then using it as context for $P(y \mid r, x)$) is far more accurate than directly sampling $y$ from $P(y \mid x)$, because the rationale acts as a **chain of conditioning** that breaks a hard reasoning problem into easy sub-problems.

**Why it works.** The autoregressive factorization $P(y \mid x) = \prod_t P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ means that once the model has emitted tokens of $r$, every subsequent prediction of $y$ is conditional on $r$. This effectively *externalizes* an internal scratchpad, turning a one-shot prediction into a multi-step computation.

**Empirical evidence.** On GSM8K (grade-school math) CoT improves accuracy from roughly **18 %** (standard prompting) to **57 %+** in modern base models — a **3×** improvement.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**ReAct Definition.** ReAct (Reason + Act) is a paradigm that interleaves *thought* traces $r_t$ with *actions* $a_t$ taken in an external environment (e.g., a search engine, a calculator, a database). The model emits tokens of the form:

$$
\text{Trace}_t = (\text{Thought}_t,\ \text{Action}_t,\ \text{Observation}_t)
$$

**Labelled Schematic:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[User Query Q0] --> T1[Thought 1: Identify missing fact]
    T1 --> A1[Action 1: Call Search API]
    A1 --> O1[Observation 1: Retrieved Document]
    O1 --> T2[Thought 2: Extract relevant entity]
    T2 --> A2[Action 2: Query Calculator]
    A2 --> O2[Observation 2: Numeric Result]
    O2 --> T3[Thought 3: Compose final answer]
    T3 --> F[Final Answer]
```

**Why interleaving works.**
- **Reasoning** handles the *symbolic* logic of "what should I do next".
- **Acting** delegates the *sub-symbolic* retrieval/computation to a tool that does not suffer from hallucination.
- The trace becomes a *memory buffer* the model uses to stay grounded in facts beyond its parametric knowledge — bounding the hallucination rate.

**Advantages.**
- Dynamic decision-making; not bound by a single forward pass.
- Tool-augmented — the LLM becomes an **orchestrator** rather than a closed-book solver.
- Trace is interpretable — a human auditor can step through the chain of decisions.

---

> ### Question B — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice Alternative)*
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper, Q-Mod2-14B]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **In-Context Learning (ICL)** paradigm. How does the choice of demonstration count $k$ and demonstration order affect downstream accuracy? Provide the formal expression for the prompted predictor.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **decoding parameters** — temperature $T$, top-$k$, and top-$p$ (nucleus) sampling — in shaping LLM output. Derive the limiting behaviour of the softmax-with-temperature distribution as $T \to 0^{+}$ and $T \to \infty$.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition.** In-Context Learning (ICL) is the emergent ability of large autoregressive transformers to perform a new task at *inference time* by conditioning on a small set of demonstrations prepended to the query, **without any gradient updates**.

**Formal expression.** Given a demonstration set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{k}$ and a new query $x$:

$$
P(y \mid x,\ \mathcal{D},\ \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P\!\left(y_t \,\Big\vert\, y_{<t},\ x,\ \bigoplus_{i=1}^{k} (x_i, y_i),\ \theta\right)
$$

**Effect of demonstration count $k$.**
- $k = 0$ (Zero-Shot): relies entirely on $\theta$.
- $k = 1$ (One-Shot): anchors the format but rarely boosts accuracy.
- $k \in [2,\, 16]$ (Few-Shot): empirically optimal for most tasks; further increases yield diminishing or negative returns due to **context dilution**.
- $k \to \infty$: limited by the context window $L_{\text{ctx}}$; older demonstrations may be truncated by attention bias.

**Effect of demonstration order.**
- LLMs exhibit a strong **recency bias** — examples placed closest to the query dominate the prediction.
- The same $k$ examples, reordered, can produce accuracy swings of **5 %–30 %** on benchmarks such as SST-2 and MNLI.
- Mitigations: randomize order across multiple inference runs (akin to Self-Consistency) or use a learned retriever to surface the most relevant $k$ demos (Retrieval-Augmented ICL).

**Why ICL works.** Theoretical work (e.g., the *implicit Bayesian inference* view) argues that the model's attention heads compute a soft retrieval over the demonstration block, forming a task-specific prior over the label space.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Background.** Once the LLM produces a logit vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ at each step, a *decoding strategy* maps these logits to a discrete token. The three most common stochastic strategies are:

1. **Temperature Sampling** — rescale logits by $T$:

$$
P_T(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{v'} \exp(z_{v'} / T)}
$$

2. **Top-$k$ Sampling** — restrict the support to the $k$ largest logits, then renormalize.
3. **Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling** — restrict the support to the smallest set $S$ with cumulative probability $\ge p$:

$$
S_p = \arg\min_{S} \vert S \vert \quad \text{such that} \quad \sum_{v \in S} P(v) \ge p
$$

**Derivation of the limits for temperature.** As derived in §3.2:

$$
\lim_{T \to 0^{+}} P_T(v) = \mathbf{1}\!\left[v = v^{\star}\right],\quad v^{\star} = \arg\max_{v} z_v
$$

*Explanation:* As $T$ shrinks, the ratio $z_v / T$ magnifies differences exponentially. The token with the largest logit absorbs essentially all probability mass — equivalent to **greedy decoding**.

$$
\lim_{T \to \infty} P_T(v) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}
$$

*Explanation:* As $T$ grows, every $z_v / T \to 0$, so $\exp(z_v / T) \to 1$. The distribution becomes uniform over the vocabulary — maximum entropy, no preference for any token.

**Practical guidance for KTU valuation.**
- **Factual / extraction tasks:** $T \approx 0$ (greedy), top-$k$ = 1.
- **Open-ended generation:** $T \in [0.7, 1.0]$, top-$p \in [0.9, 0.95]$.
- **High creativity:** $T \in [1.1, 1.3]$, top-$p \approx 0.98$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing prompting with fine-tuning.** Examiners deduct heavily if a student claims that prompt engineering *modifies model parameters*. It does **not**: $\Delta\theta = 0$.
> 2. **Omitting the symbol definitions** in derivations. If you write $P_T(v)$ you **must** state that $z_v$ is the logit and $T > 0$ is the temperature.
> 3. **Writing the binomial as a single symbol.** For Self-Consistency, you must expand the binomial coefficient $\binom{N}{m}$ — partial credit is awarded only when the formula is explicit.
> 4. **Forgetting units / ranges.** State that $T > 0$ and that top-$p \in (0, 1]$.
> 5. **Hand-waving the rationale behind CoT.** A *derivation*, not just a statement, is required for full marks in the 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" of ReAct.** A diagram alone is insufficient — explain *why* interleaving thought and action reduces hallucination.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Prompt Engineering is non-parametric** — model weights are frozen; only the input is optimized.
- A canonical prompt has **four components**: Instruction, Context, Input Data, Output Indicator.
- **Zero-Shot** uses no examples; **Few-Shot** uses $k \in [2, 16]$ demonstrations; **CoT** adds rationales; **Self-Consistency** samples $N$ chains and takes the mode.
- The marginalization identity for CoT is:

$$
P(y \mid x) = \sum_{r} P(y \mid r, x)\, P(r \mid x)
$$

- The softmax-with-temperature is:

$$
P_T(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{v'} \exp(z_{v'} / T)}
$$

with $T \to 0$ giving greedy decoding and $T \to \infty$ giving uniform sampling.
- **Self-Consistency** success probability follows a binomial tail:

$$
P_{\text{SC}} = \sum_{m = \lceil N/2 \rceil}^{N} \binom{N}{m} p^{m} (1-p)^{N-m}
$$

- **Demonstration order matters** — recency bias is a robust empirical phenomenon.
- **ReAct** interleaves *Thought*, *Action*, *Observation* to ground the LLM in external tools.
- **Best practice checklist:** clear directive → persona → delimiters → format schema → exemplars → tuned decoding parameters.
- **Engineering metrics to remember:** context window $L_{\text{ctx}}$ (tokens); temperature $T > 0$; top-$k$ (integer); top-$p \in (0, 1]$.
- **Common KTU traps:** confusing *prompting* with *fine-tuning*; using the pipe symbol `\vert` inside markdown tables (breaks rendering — use `\vert` or `\mid`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Answer Engineering
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Answer Engineering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Answer Engineering** is a sub-discipline of **Prompt Engineering** that focuses on the systematic design, structuring, and constraint of the *output* (response) generated by a Large Language Model (LLM). Whereas classical prompt engineering shapes the input distribution, Answer Engineering shapes the **response space** by controlling the *format*, *style*, *length*, *reasoning depth*, *persona*, and *post-processing schema* of the model's answer. Mathematically, given an input prompt $x$ and a target output specification $\mathcal{S}$ (format, length, schema), Answer Engineering seeks a decoding strategy or template $T$ such that: $$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}_{\mathcal{S}}} P_{\theta}(y \mid x, T)$$ where $\mathcal{Y}_{\mathcal{S}}$ is the constrained response subspace that conforms to the specification $\mathcal{S}$, and $P_{\theta}$ is the LLM's conditional probability distribution parameterized by $\theta$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Answer Engineering is positioned under Module 2 as a peer concept to Prompt Engineering. The key distinction is that **Prompt Engineering = "What you ask"**, while **Answer Engineering = "How the model is forced to respond"**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners) Imagine you walk into a **government tax filing office** to submit a form: - **Without Answer Engineering** → You walk in, mumble "I want to pay my taxes," and the officer gives you a random, unstructured response. - **With Answer Engineering** → You walk in with a **pre-printed form** specifying: Name field, PAN field, Income bracket field, Signature field. The officer is now *structurally forced* to fill every box in a specific format, language, and length. The **LLM is the officer**, the **prompt is your initial request**, and the **Answer Engineering template/schema is the pre-printed form** that constrains and standardizes the officer's reply. ## 1.3 Key Terminology with Bolded Standards | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Output Schema** | A formal specification (JSON, XML, YAML) of expected response fields. | | **Constrained Decoding** | Decoding algorithm that restricts the next-token sampling to a grammar-valid subset. | | **Persona Prompting** | Forcing the LLM to answer as a specific expert/role. | | **Verbosity Budget** | Explicit upper/lower bound on response length. | | **Stop Sequences** | Special tokens that terminate generation once observed. | | **Format Tokens** | Enforced leading/trailing tokens (e.g., `"Answer: "`, ``). | > [!NOTE] > Standard metrics used in Answer Engineering: **Perplexity (PPL) ≤ 20**, **Format-Adherence Rate (FAR) ≥ 0.95**, and **JSON Validity Score ≥ 0.98** for production-grade systems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Answer Engineering Answer Engineering is built on five orthogonal control dimensions. Each can be tuned independently: ### Pillar 1 — **Format Control** The model is forced to emit output in a machine-readable structure. The probability mass is restricted to valid token sequences that satisfy a grammar $\mathcal{G}$. $$P_{\text{valid}}(y_t \mid y_{ { "decision": "approved", "confidence": 0.92 } ``` ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Purpose / Use | |---|-------------------|--------|----------------| | 1 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P_{\theta}(y \mid x, T)$ | $\hat{y}$ | Optimal answer under template $T$ | | 2 | $P_{\text{valid}}(y_t) \propto P_{\theta}(y_t) \cdot \mathbb{1}[y_t \in \mathcal{V}_{\mathcal{G}}]$ | $P_{\text{valid}}$ | Constrained decoding probability | | 3 | $\text{LP} = \left(\frac{5+\vert y \vert}{6}\right)^{\alpha}$ | LP | Length penalty in beam search | | 4 | $H(y) = -\sum_t P(y_t)\log P(y_t)$ | $H$ | Entropy of output distribution | | 5 | $y = y_{\text{think}} \oplus y_{\text{conclude}}$ | $\oplus$ | CoT decomposition | | 6 | $\text{FAR} = \frac{\vert y_{\text{valid}}\vert}{\vert y_{\text{total}}\vert}$ | FAR | Format Adherence Rate | | 7 | $\text{PPL}(y) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log P(y_i)\right)$ | PPL | Perplexity metric | | 8 | $T = 0$ ⇒ greedy ; $T \to \infty$ ⇒ uniform | $T$ | Sampling temperature | | 9 | $P(y_t) = \frac{\exp(z_t / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}$ | $z_t$ | Softmax with temperature | | 10 | $D_{\text{KL}}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \sum P \log\frac{P}{Q}$ | $D_{\text{KL}}$ | KL-divergence for output drift | > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 ESE, the examiner expects you to remember at minimum formulas **#1, #2, #6, and #9**.
