These are the *favourite* short-answer and 7-mark sub-questions on Answer Engineering. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare NLP** → Constrain LLM answers to ICD-10 codes only. - **Legal Tech** → Force output as `{clause_id, risk_level, recommendation}` JSON. - **Customer Support** → Persona + verbosity budget + escalation triggers. - **Code Generation** → Stop sequences at ``; format as fenced markdown. - **RAG Pipelines** → Wrap answers in `` and `` tags for re-ranking. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Constrained Decoding Probability **Problem statement:** Given an LLM with vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$, derive the renormalized probability of a next token $y_t$ when the output is constrained to follow a context-free grammar $\mathcal{G}$ that is currently in state $s_t$. **Step 1 — Start from the unconstrained conditional.** The LLM defines the next-token distribution directly from logits $z_t$: $$P_{\theta}(y_t = v \mid y_{ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Answer Engineering Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Query x] --> B[Prompt Constructor] B --> C[Combined Prompt x plus T] C --> D[LLM Forward Pass] D --> E[Raw Logits z_t] E --> F{Constraint Layer} F -->|Grammar Check| G[Valid Token Mask] F -->|Length Budget| H[Stop Sequence Logic] F -->|Persona Filter| I[Style Reweighting] G --> J[Renormalized Distribution] H --> J I --> J J --> K[Token Sampler] K --> L[Generated Token y_t] L --> M{Stop or Budget Hit} M -->|No| D M -->|Yes| N[Final Answer y hat] N --> O[Post Processing Extractor] O --> P[Structured Output Schema] ``` ## 4.2 Five-Pillar Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Format Control] --> CORE[Answer Engineering Core] P2[Length Control] --> CORE P3[Persona Control] --> CORE P4[Reasoning Control] --> CORE P5[Schema Wrapping] --> CORE CORE --> OUT[Constrained Response y hat] OUT --> VAL[Validator FAR PPL] VAL -->|Feedback| CORE ``` ## 4.3 Constrained Decoding State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> start start --> emit: valid token chosen emit --> start: state advances start --> endGrammar: terminal reached start --> reject: invalid token attempted reject --> start: token masked with minus infinity endGrammar --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Answer Engineering in a RAG Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD U[User Question] --> RET[Retriever top k Docs] RET --> CTX[Context Block] U --> PE[Prompt Engineer] CTX --> PE PE --> AE[Answer Engineering Module] AE --> SYS[System Constraints Format Persona Length] SYS --> LLM[LLM Inference Engine] LLM --> RAW[Raw Text Response] RAW --> PP[Post Processor Regex JSON Parser] PP --> API[Structured API Response] API --> CLIENT[Client Application] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define Answer Engineering and distinguish it from Prompt Engineering. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Answer Engineering is the discipline of designing the *output* specification, format, persona, length, and reasoning structure that an LLM must follow while generating a response. - **(1 mark)** Prompt Engineering controls the *input* side (what is asked); Answer Engineering controls the *output* side (how the model is forced to answer). - **(1 mark)** Example: A prompt template injecting *"Respond strictly in valid JSON with keys {label, score}"* is Answer Engineering, whereas *"Translate the following text"* is Prompt Engineering. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is a stop sequence? How does it serve as a length-control mechanism in Answer Engineering? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** A stop sequence is a special string of tokens (e.g., ``, `\n\nUser:`) that, when emitted by the LLM, halts generation immediately. - **(1 mark)** It functions as a *hard terminator* for the decoder loop, guaranteeing the response ends at a precise boundary irrespective of token count. - **(1 mark)** This is essential in chat-bots and RAG systems where unbounded generation causes cost overruns and hallucinated turns. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model]** > **(a)** Explain in detail the **five pillars of Answer Engineering**, with one real-world example per pillar. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the **constrained decoding probability** for an LLM restricted by a context-free grammar $\mathcal{G}$. Show how an invalid token's probability is forced to zero. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] The five pillars are: 1. **Format Control** — Output must be JSON / XML / Markdown table. *Example:* In a medical triage system, the LLM is forced to output `{"severity": "high", "action": "ER"}`. 2. **Length / Verbosity Control** — Hard cap `max_tokens=150` plus a stop sequence. *Example:* Customer-support chat-bot limited to 2-sentence replies. 3. **Persona Control** — System prompt: *"You are a senior tax consultant."* *Example:* Finance advisor app. 4. **Reasoning Control** — Trigger CoT: *"Think step-by-step before answering."* *Example:* Math word problems (GSM8K benchmark). 5. **Post-Processing Schema** — Wrap output in `...` tags. *Example:* RAG pipeline using regex extractors. *(Each pillar with example = ~1.4 marks; 5 × 1.4 ≈ 7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] Start with the standard LLM softmax over the full vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$: $$P_{\theta}(y_t = v \mid y_{ **(a)** Discuss the role of **temperature and length penalty** in shaping the LLM's answer. Show mathematically how $T \to 0$ leads to greedy decoding. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A company wants its LLM to **always reply in JSON** with keys `{"decision", "confidence", "reasoning"}`. Design an Answer Engineering strategy covering format, validation, and fall-back. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] The softmax with temperature $T$ is: $$P_T(y_t = v) = \frac{\exp(z_{t,v}/T)}{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(z_{t,j}/T)}$$ **Limit case $T \to 0$:** The exponent dominates — the token with the highest logit wins. Formally: $$\lim_{T \to 0} P_T(y_t = v) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } v = \arg\max_j z_{t,j} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This is **greedy decoding**, the most deterministic form of Answer Engineering. **(2 marks)** **Limit case $T \to \infty$:** All $z_{t,j}/T \to 0$, so $\exp(0) = 1$ for every $j$, yielding a uniform distribution. **(1 mark)** **Length penalty** in beam search rescores candidates to discourage overly short or long outputs: $$\text{score}(y) = \frac{\log P(y)}{\text{LP}(y)}, \quad \text{LP}(y) = \left(\frac{5+\vert y \vert}{6}\right)^{\alpha}$$ For $\alpha = 0$, the penalty vanishes (no length preference). For $\alpha > 0$, longer outputs are favored. **(2 marks)** **Application:** Legal-document summarization uses $\alpha = 0.6$ and $T = 0.2$ to balance fluency and conciseness. **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Strategy components:** 1. **System Prompt (1 mark)** — *"You are an API that always returns valid JSON. Never add prose."* 2. **Few-shot Exemplar (1 mark)** — Provide 2–3 examples in the prompt showing the exact JSON shape. 3. **Grammar-Constrained Decoding (2 marks)** — Define a JSON schema in the runtime (e.g., via `outlines`, `guidance`, or `lm-format-enforcer` libraries) so the LLM *cannot* emit non-JSON tokens. 4. **Stop Sequence (1 mark)** — Set `` or `"` as the stop token. 5. **Validator + Fallback (2 marks)** — Parse the response with a JSON parser; if invalid, retry with a corrective prompt: *"Your previous output was malformed. Re-emit valid JSON only."* If retries exceed $N = 2$, fall back to a deterministic regex or a fine-tuned smaller model. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > - Writing *only* about prompts and forgetting the **output-side** framing → loses 2–3 marks. > - Confusing Answer Engineering with fine-tuning → examiner deducts 2 marks. > - Omitting the **indicator function / mask** in the constrained-decoding derivation → -1.5 marks. > - Not mentioning **fallback / re-prompting** in design questions → -1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition** — Answer Engineering = controlling the *output* of an LLM via format, length, persona, reasoning, and schema. - **Core equation** — $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}_{\mathcal{S}}} P_{\theta}(y \mid x, T)$. - **Constrained decoding** — invalid tokens receive $z' = -\infty$ and probability $0$ after renormalization by $Z_t$. - **Five pillars** — Format, Length, Persona, Reasoning (CoT), Post-processing schema. - **Temperature** — $T \to 0$ is greedy; $T \to \infty$ is uniform; $T = 1$ is default. - **Length penalty** — $\text{LP} = \left(\frac{5+\vert y \vert}{6}\right)^{\alpha}$ controls verbosity bias. - **Metrics** — Format Adherence Rate (FAR), Perplexity (PPL), JSON Validity Score. - **Grammar engines** — `outlines`, `guidance`, `lm-format-enforcer`, `instructor` (Python libraries). - **Common confusions** — Answer Engineering is *not* fine-tuning; it is *runtime* control. - **CoT gain** — Average accuracy boost of **+18% to +42%** on math/logic tasks. - **Stop sequence** — Hard terminator, used for length & format control simultaneously. - **Validator loop** — Always pair Answer Engineering with a parser + retry/fallback. - **2024 Scheme tip** — KTU examiners love questions linking Answer Engineering to RAG pipelines and JSON-schema enforcement.

topics.title: Multi-Prompt Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi-Prompt Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Prompt Inference** is a structured inference paradigm in Large Language Models (LLMs) in which *two or more distinct prompts* — sharing the same underlying intent — are simultaneously or sequentially submitted to a model, and their corresponding outputs are *aggregated, compared, or composed* to produce a single, more reliable, accurate, and robust final response.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PECMT862 – Module 2), Multi-Prompt Inference is treated as a **higher-order prompting strategy** that operates *on top of* basic prompt engineering. Instead of relying on a single "best guess" prompt, the system treats the LLM as a *probabilistic black box* and uses *diversity in prompt formulation* to exploit the variance in the model's output distribution, mitigating stochasticity and prompt sensitivity.

Mathematically, given a user query $Q$, instead of computing:

$$ y = \arg\max_{y} P_{\theta}(y \mid P, Q) $$

Multi-Prompt Inference computes an aggregated response:

$$ \hat{y} = f_{\text{agg}}\Big( P_{\theta}(y \mid P_1, Q),\ P_{\theta}(y \mid P_2, Q),\ \dots,\ P_{\theta}(y \mid P_n, Q) \Big) $$

where $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ are $n$ semantically-equivalent prompt variants, and $f_{\text{agg}}$ is an aggregation function (e.g., majority vote, weighted average of logprobs, LLM-based judge, or self-consistency check).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multiple Doctors" Intuition

Imagine you have a persistent headache and visit a *single doctor*. The diagnosis is one opinion, possibly biased, possibly wrong. Now imagine you visit **three different doctors**, each of whom asks a slightly *different set of questions* (analogous to multiple prompts), examines you independently, and gives a diagnosis. If all three agree, you can be highly confident. If two say "migraine" and one says "tension headache," the majority view (with some weighting) is likely correct. If they disagree wildly, a senior consultant (the *meta-judge*) is asked to arbitrate.

**That is exactly Multi-Prompt Inference:**
- Each **doctor** = one prompt variant $P_i$
- The **patient (query)** $Q$ is identical
- The **diseases** = candidate outputs $y_i$
- The **majority vote / senior consultant** = aggregation function $f_{\text{agg}}$
- The **final diagnosis** = $\hat{y}$

The central insight: a *single* LLM call is opinionated, stochastic, and prompt-sensitive. *Multiple* LLM calls with diverse prompts give the system a statistical basis to converge on the correct answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** Multi-Prompt Inference bridges **Prompt Engineering** (covered earlier) and **Reasoning Strategies** (covered later in the module). It is foundational to techniques such as *Self-Consistency*, *Tree-of-Thoughts*, *Prompt Chaining*, and *Mixture-of-Prompts (MoP)*.

## 1.3 Why Multi-Prompt Inference is Necessary — The Three Core Motivations

A single LLM call suffers from three well-documented weaknesses:

| Weakness | Description | How Multi-Prompt Helps |
|---|---|---|
| **Stochasticity** | Sampling-based decoding ($T > 0$) yields different outputs for the same prompt | Aggregating over multiple samples smooths variance |
| **Prompt Sensitivity** | Tiny rewordings (e.g., "Explain" vs "Describe") can flip answers | Diverse prompt formulations reduce dependency on phrasing |
| **Single-Point-of-Failure** | One erroneous reasoning chain derails the final answer | Multiple chains offer redundancy and self-correction |

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Familiarize yourself with these exact terms — KTU exam questions use them verbatim.

- **Prompt Variant** $P_i$: A semantically equivalent reformulation of the base prompt.
- **Inference Call**: A single forward pass through the LLM.
- **Aggregation Function** $f_{\text{agg}}$: Maps a set of outputs $\{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n\}$ to a single output $\hat{y}$.
- **Sampling Temperature** $T$: Controls output randomness; higher $T$ → more diversity.
- **Log-Probability** $\log P_{\theta}(y \mid P, Q)$: Confidence score the model assigns to a token/output.
- **Self-Consistency**: Majority-vote aggregation across sampled reasoning paths.
- **Prompt Chaining**: Sequential multi-prompt where each output feeds the next prompt.
- **Ensemble Inference**: Parallel multi-prompt where outputs are combined after the fact.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Multi-Prompt Inference

The full pipeline decomposes into **six discrete stages**. Each stage is a potential KTU exam question.

### Stage 1 — Query Reception
The user query $Q$ arrives. It is a single piece of natural language text, optionally carrying context, history, and constraints.

### Stage 2 — Prompt Variant Generation
The system generates $n$ prompt variants $\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n\}$. Variant generation can be:

- **Manual / Hand-crafted**: A human prompt engineer writes $n$ different phrasings.
- **Template-based**: A single template with slots that are filled with synonyms/paraphrases.
- **LLM-generated**: An auxiliary LLM call paraphrases the base prompt $n$ times.
- **Adversarial / Perturbative**: Minor perturbations (typos, re-orderings) are injected.

Formally, given base prompt $P_0$:

$$ \{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n\} = G(P_0,\ n) $$

where $G$ is the variant-generation function.

### Stage 3 — Parallel / Sequential Execution
Each $(P_i, Q)$ pair is fed to the LLM. This can be:

- **Parallel**: All $n$ calls executed concurrently → low latency.
- **Sequential**: Calls executed one after another → preserves order dependency, needed for prompt chaining.

The output set is:

$$ \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n\} = \{ M_{\theta}(P_i, Q) \}_{i=1}^{n} $$

where $M_{\theta}$ denotes the LLM parameterized by weights $\theta$.

### Stage 4 — Output Collection & Alignment
Raw outputs may differ in length, format, or surface form. They are *normalized* — e.g., extracting the final answer from a chain-of-thought trace, parsing JSON, lowercasing, or mapping free text to a categorical label.

### Stage 5 — Aggregation
The aggregation function $f_{\text{agg}}$ combines $\{y_i\}$ into $\hat{y}$. Common choices are detailed in §2.3.

### Stage 6 — Verification (Optional)
A final LLM-as-a-judge call verifies $\hat{y}$ against $Q$, or the system returns $\hat{y}$ directly with a confidence score.

## 2.2 Decomposition — The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why It Matters | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Variant Generation | Diversity is the fuel of multi-prompt inference | Quality vs. cost of generation |
| Parallel Execution | Latency = max of individual calls (not sum) | Throughput, API rate limits |
| Output Alignment | Apples-to-apples comparison requires normalization | Regex, schema, parser reliability |
| Aggregation | The single point of truth | Voting ties, weighting, judge bias |
| Verification | Last line of defense | Extra compute cost |

## 2.3 Aggregation Strategies — The KTU Core

> [!NOTE]
> **This is one of the most heavily tested sub-topics.** KTU frequently asks: *"Compare self-consistency, weighted voting, and LLM-as-judge aggregation."*

### 2.3.1 Majority Voting (Hard Vote)

Each $y_i$ casts one vote. The output with the highest vote count wins.

$$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y] $$

where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function.

### 2.3.2 Weighted Voting (Soft Vote)

Each $y_i$ is weighted by the model's confidence (log-probability):

$$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[y_i = y] $$

with weights $w_i$ typically chosen as the normalized log-probability:

$$ w_i = \frac{\exp\!\big( \frac{1}{L_i} \sum_{t=1}^{L_i} \log P_{\theta}(y_{i,t} \mid P_i, Q, y_{i,<t}) \big)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp\!\big( \frac{1}{L_j} \sum_{t=1}^{L_j} \log P_{\theta}(y_{j,t} \mid P_j, Q, y_{j,<t}) \big)} $$

### 2.3.3 Self-Consistency (Reasoning-Aware Voting)

Used in conjunction with Chain-of-Thought (CoT). The model produces $n$ different *reasoning paths*, each ending in a final answer. The answer with the highest frequency across paths is selected.

This was introduced by **Wang et al., 2022** as a powerful method to boost arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning accuracy.

### 2.3.4 Best-of-N (Rejection Sampling)

Generate $n$ candidates, score each with a reward model $R_{\phi}$, and select the highest-scoring one:

$$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{y_i} R_{\phi}(P_0, Q, y_i) $$

This is the foundation of **RLHF fine-tuning** and inference-time scaling laws.

### 2.3.5 LLM-as-a-Judge

A second LLM call (the *judge*) is presented with all $\{y_i\}$ and asked to pick the best:

$$ \hat{y} = M_{\theta}\!\Big( \text{prompt}_{\text{judge}}\big(Q,\ \{y_1, \dots, y_n\}\big) \Big) $$

Strength: handles open-ended, qualitative tasks where voting is ill-defined.
Weakness: introduces judge bias and extra latency.

### 2.3.6 Prompt Chaining (Sequential Aggregation)

Here aggregation is *not* voting — the outputs are **concatenated or fed forward**:

$$ P_{i+1} = P_i \oplus y_i \oplus \text{next instruction} $$

Each prompt's output becomes the context for the next, enabling decompositional reasoning.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-Prompt Inference | $y = \arg\max_{y} P_{\theta}(y \mid P, Q)$ | Baseline |
| 2 | Multi-Prompt Inference (General) | $\hat{y} = f_{\text{agg}}\big( \{P_{\theta}(y \mid P_i, Q)\}_{i=1}^{n} \big)$ | Core equation |
| 3 | Majority Vote | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$ | Hard vote |
| 4 | Weighted Vote Weight | $w_i = \frac{\exp(\bar{\ell}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(\bar{\ell}_j)}$ | $\bar{\ell}_i$ = avg log-prob |
| 5 | Best-of-N Selection | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y_i} R_{\phi}(P_0, Q, y_i)$ | $R_{\phi}$ = reward model |
| 6 | Sampling Temperature | $P_T(y_t) = \frac{\exp(\ell_t / T)}{\sum_{v} \exp(\ell_v / T)}$ | $T=1$ default |
| 7 | Pass@$k$ Metric | $\text{Pass@}k = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$ | $c$ = correct, $n$ = total |
| 8 | Inference Cost | $C_{\text{total}} = n \cdot C_{\text{single}} + C_{\text{agg}}$ | Linear in $n$ |
| 9 | Latency (Parallel) | $L_{\text{parallel}} = \max_i L_i$ | vs sequential sum |
| 10 | Self-Consistency Accuracy | $A_{sc} = A_{\text{majority}}(\{y_i\})$ | Empirical boost over greedy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Notice that all absolute-value / magnitude notations in the table above are written using $\vert$ and parenthesized expressions — never the bare pipe `|` — to prevent breaking the markdown table. Memorize the $\binom{\cdot}{\cdot}$ binomial coefficient notation for the **Pass@$k$** formula.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Medical AI Triage** | Multiple symptom-extraction prompts → majority diagnosis for safety |
| **Legal Document QA** | Multiple clause-paraphrasing prompts → consensus answer |
| **Code Generation** | Multiple "implement this function" prompts → pick the one passing unit tests (Best-of-N) |
| **Customer Support** | Multi-intent classification prompts → robust routing |
| **Math Reasoning** | Self-consistency decoding boosts GSM8K accuracy by 10–20% |
| **Hallucination Mitigation** | LLM-as-judge detects fabricated claims via cross-prompt agreement |

## 2.6 Trade-offs — The Engineering Reality

Multi-Prompt Inference is **not free**. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate the cost-benefit:

| Benefit | Cost |
|---|---|
| ↑ Accuracy | ↑ Compute (n× calls) |
| ↑ Robustness | ↑ Latency (if sequential) |
| ↓ Prompt sensitivity | ↑ API cost |
| ↓ Hallucination rate | ↑ Engineering complexity |
| ↑ Confidence calibration | ↑ Token usage (higher carbon footprint) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Majority Voting Works — A Probabilistic Justification

**Goal:** Show that aggregating over multiple sampled outputs can *provably* improve the probability of producing a correct answer.

### Setup

Let the *true* correct answer be $y^*$. For each prompt variant $P_i$, the LLM produces $y_i$ with some probability of being correct:

$$ p_i = P(y_i = y^* \mid P_i, Q) $$

Assume — for the purposes of this derivation — that:
1. The LLM is *better than random*: $p_i > 0.5$ for each $i$ (this is a *local* condition, achievable by good prompting).
2. The errors made by different prompt variants are *approximately independent* (this is a *diversity* assumption — satisfied when prompts are sufficiently different).

### Step 1 — Probability a Single Sample is Correct

By assumption 1, each individual sample is more likely correct than not:

$$ p_i > 0.5 \quad \forall\, i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\} $$

### Step 2 — Majority Vote Output

Let $C$ = number of correct votes in $\{y_1, \dots, y_n\}$. The majority vote is correct iff $C > n/2$.

### Step 3 — Compute $P(C > n/2)$

Under independence and identical $p_i = p$ (simplification), $C \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{majority correct}) &= P\!\left(C > \frac{n}{2}\right) \\
&= \sum_{k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare to Single-Prompt

We want to show that $P(\text{majority correct}) > p$ for $n \ge 3$ and $p > 0.5$.

This follows from the **Condorcet Jury Theorem** (1785), restated for LLMs:

> [!NOTE]
> **Condorcet Jury Theorem (LLM adaptation):** If each member of a group has independent probability $p > 0.5$ of being correct, the probability that the *majority vote* is correct approaches **1** as $n \to \infty$.

### Step 5 — Numerical Illustration

For $p = 0.6$ and $n = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{majority correct}) &= \sum_{k=3}^{5} \binom{5}{k} (0.6)^{k} (0.4)^{5-k} \\
&= \binom{5}{3}(0.6)^3(0.4)^2 + \binom{5}{4}(0.6)^4(0.4)^1 + \binom{5}{5}(0.6)^5 \\
&= 10(0.216)(0.16) + 5(0.1296)(0.4) + 1(0.07776) \\
&= 0.3456 + 0.2592 + 0.07776 \\
&= 0.68256
\end{aligned}
$$

So $P(\text{majority correct}) = 0.683 > 0.6 = p$. The improvement is $\Delta = 0.083$, or **8.3 percentage points** with just 5 samples. With $n = 20$, the majority-correct probability approaches 0.97.

### Step 6 — Key Insight

The benefit of multi-prompt inference is *not* that any single prompt is better, but that **diverse, independent errors can be outvoted by a correct consensus**. This is the mathematical backbone of Self-Consistency, Best-of-N, and Ensemble Decoding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pass@$k$ Metric (Code Generation Evaluation)

When we have $n$ candidate code solutions generated by $n$ prompts, and $c$ of them are correct, the expected Pass@$k$ — the probability that *at least one* of $k$ randomly chosen candidates is correct — is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pass@}k &= 1 - P(\text{all } k \text{ chosen candidates are wrong}) \\
&= 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of the second line:**
- Total ways to choose $k$ candidates from $n$: $\binom{n}{k}$
- Ways to choose $k$ candidates that are *all wrong*: choose $k$ from the $(n-c)$ wrong ones: $\binom{n-c}{k}$
- Therefore, the probability all $k$ are wrong is $\binom{n-c}{k} / \binom{n}{k}$
- Complement gives Pass@$k$.

**Example:** $n = 10$ candidates, $c = 3$ correct, $k = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pass@}1 &= 1 - \frac{\binom{7}{1}}{\binom{10}{1}} = 1 - \frac{7}{10} = 0.30
\end{aligned}
$$

For $k = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pass@}5 &= 1 - \frac{\binom{7}{5}}{\binom{10}{5}} = 1 - \frac{21}{252} = 1 - 0.0833 = 0.9167
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the *scaling benefit* of multi-prompt inference in code generation: 5 samples yields a 91.7% pass rate even when individual pass rate is only 30%.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Aggregation Strategy Selection

| Aggregation | Best For | Open-Ended OK? | Needs Reward Model? | Compute Cost | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Majority Vote** | MCQ, classification, math | No | No | $n \times$ | Frequently asked |
| **Weighted Vote** | When logprobs available | No | No | $n \times$ | Frequently asked |
| **Self-Consistency** | CoT reasoning tasks | No | No | $n \times$ | Frequently asked |
| **Best-of-N** | Generative, quality tasks | Yes | **Yes** | $n \times$ | Moderately asked |
| **LLM-as-Judge** | Open-ended, subjective | Yes | No (uses LLM) | $(n+1) \times$ | Frequently asked |
| **Prompt Chaining** | Multi-step tasks | Yes | No | $n \times$ (sequential) | Frequently asked |

## 3.4 Derivation: Sampling Temperature Effect on Multi-Prompt

The temperature-scaled softmax is:

$$ P_T(y_t \mid y_{<t}) = \frac{\exp(\ell_{y_t} / T)}{\sum_{v \in V} \exp(\ell_v / T)} $$

where $\ell_{y_t}$ is the logit of token $y_t$, and $V$ is the vocabulary.

**Three regimes:**

- $T \to 0$: Greedy decoding. Output is deterministic; multi-prompt aggregation offers *no* benefit.
- $T = 1$: Standard sampling. Outputs are diverse; aggregation is highly beneficial.
- $T \to \infty$: Uniform sampling. Outputs are essentially random; aggregation reverts to random guess.

**Optimal choice for multi-prompt:** $T \in [0.5,\ 1.0]$ — high enough to induce diversity, low enough to keep the model "on distribution."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question asks "Why use $T = 0.7$ in Self-Consistency?", answer: *"To introduce controlled stochasticity so multiple sampled reasoning paths differ, enabling the majority-vote aggregation to exploit diversity. $T=0$ would yield identical outputs, and $T=\infty$ would yield noise."*

## 3.5 The Self-Consistency Algorithm — Full Pseudocode (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the **NO PYTHON rule for non-CS courses**: PECMT862 is a CS elective, so pseudocode in a clean format is permitted for clarity. The actual KTU exam expects *English explanation*, not code.

**Input:** Query $Q$, base prompt $P_0$, number of samples $n$, temperature $T$.

**Steps:**
1. Construct $n$ prompt variants $\{P_1, \dots, P_n\}$ by appending a CoT instruction (e.g., "Let's think step by step") with slight paraphrasing.
2. For each $i = 1$ to $n$:
   - Call the LLM with $(P_i, Q)$ at temperature $T$.
   - Receive full reasoning trace $r_i$ and final answer $a_i$.
3. Extract the final answer $a_i$ from each $r_i$ via answer-parsing regex.
4. Count the frequency of each unique answer: $\text{count}(a) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[a_i = a]$.
5. Return $\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} \text{count}(a)$ (the modal answer).

**Output:** $\hat{a}$ — the most consistent answer across $n$ reasoning paths.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Multi-Prompt for a Math Problem

**Query:** "What is 17 × 24?"

**Three CoT prompts (variants):**
- $P_1$: "Solve step by step."
- $P_2$: "Show your reasoning, then give the final number."
- $P_3$: "Think carefully and compute the product."

**LLM outputs (with $T = 0.7$):**
- $y_1$: "17 × 24 = 17 × 20 + 17 × 4 = 340 + 68 = **408**"
- $y_2$: "Let me compute: 17 × 24. 10 × 24 = 240, 7 × 24 = 168. Sum = 240 + 168 = **408**"
- $y_3$: "17 × 24. I think 17 × 25 = 425, minus 17 = **408**"

**Aggregation:** All three give 408 → unanimous → $\hat{y} = 408$ with very high confidence.

**Failure case (single prompt):** If only $P_1$ was used and the model made an arithmetic slip, e.g., 416, no recovery is possible. Multi-prompt *catches* this.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]

**Define Multi-Prompt Inference. List any two advantages over single-prompt inference.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Multi-Prompt Inference is an inference strategy in which multiple semantically equivalent prompt variants $\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n\}$ are submitted to a Large Language Model for the same query $Q$, and their outputs are aggregated via a function $f_{\text{agg}}$ to produce a single, more robust final response $\hat{y}$. Formally, $\hat{y} = f_{\text{agg}}\big( \{M_{\theta}(P_i, Q)\}_{i=1}^{n} \big)$.

**Any two advantages (1 mark each):**
1. **Reduced prompt sensitivity** — diverse prompts mitigate the model's brittleness to phrasing.
2. **Higher accuracy** — voting/aggregation exploits the Condorcet Jury Theorem effect.
3. **Lower hallucination rate** — cross-prompt agreement acts as a sanity check.
4. **Better confidence calibration** — agreement frequency yields a usable confidence score.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between Self-Consistency and Best-of-N sampling in multi-prompt inference.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Self-Consistency | Best-of-N |
|---|---|---|
| **Aggregation** | Majority vote on final answers | Reward model scores; pick max |
| **Requires** | $n$ sampled reasoning paths | $n$ candidates + reward model $R_{\phi}$ |
| **Best For** | Math, logical reasoning | Open-ended generation, code |
| **Diversity Source** | Sampling temperature | Prompt variants + sampling |
| **Cost** | $n$ LLM calls | $n$ LLM calls + reward model calls |
| **Output Type** | Discrete / categorical | Continuous quality score |

(3 marks — 1.5 marks for each column's distinctive feature, 0.5 for the contrast itself)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing "multi-prompt" with "multi-modal" — they are different. Multi-prompt = multiple text prompts; multi-modal = multiple input types (text + image).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing pseudocode in the exam — KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) deducts marks for "implementation-only" answers. Always explain the *concept* and *math* first.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to mention the **diversity assumption** when justifying voting. Voting only works if errors are *approximately independent* across prompts.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping the cost-benefit analysis. Every multi-prompt answer must include the *trade-off table* (cost ↑, latency ↑, accuracy ↑).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Theory + Self-Consistency

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the Self-Consistency decoding method in detail. State the assumptions required for it to work, derive the formula for the majority-correct probability under the binomial model, and show numerically how accuracy improves from $n=1$ to $n=5$ when $p=0.6$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Self-Consistency with Prompt Chaining. Construct a 4-step prompt chain for a document-summarization task, identifying what each prompt's role is, and explain when chaining is preferable to parallel ensembling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Definition of Self-Consistency (2 marks)**

Self-Consistency is a multi-prompt inference technique introduced by **Wang et al. (2022)**. It works as follows:
- The base prompt $P_0$ is augmented with a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trigger such as "Let's think step by step."
- The LLM is sampled $n$ times at temperature $T \in [0.5, 1.0]$, producing $n$ distinct reasoning paths $\{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}$ and final answers $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$.
- The most frequent final answer (the **mode**) is selected: $\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[a_i = a]$.

**Step 2 — Assumptions (1.5 marks)**

Two key assumptions:
1. **Local competence:** $P(a_i = a^* \mid P_i, Q) > 0.5$ for each individual sample — the model is *better than random* on this task.
2. **Independence of errors:** the reasoning paths $\{r_i\}$ fail on *different* problems, so their errors are not perfectly correlated.

**Step 3 — Derivation (2.5 marks)**

Let $C$ = number of correct answers in $\{a_1, \dots, a_n\}$. Under i.i.d. assumption, $C \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$. The majority is correct iff $C > n/2$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{majority correct}) &= \sum_{k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Demonstration (1 mark)**

For $p = 0.6$, $n = 1$: $P = 0.6$.
For $p = 0.6$, $n = 5$ (computed in §3.1): $P = 0.683$.

**Improvement:** $\Delta = 0.683 - 0.600 = 0.083$, i.e., **+8.3 percentage points** with just 5 samples.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Self-Consistency | Prompt Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Parallel | Sequential |
| Aggregation | Voting | Concatenation / forward-feeding |
| Use Case | Single-step reasoning | Multi-step decomposition |
| Latency | $\max_i L_i$ | $\sum_i L_i$ |
| Dependency | Independent samples | Each step depends on previous |

**4-Step Document Summarization Chain (3 marks):**

1. **$P_1$ (Topic Extraction):** *"Read the document and list the top 5 main topics covered."* → $y_1$ = topic list.
2. **$P_2$ (Key Sentence Selection):** *"Given the topics $\langle y_1 \rangle$, extract the most important sentence for each topic."* → $y_2$ = key sentences.
3. **$P_3$ (Drafting):** *"Combine the key sentences $\langle y_2 \rangle$ into a coherent 150-word draft summary."* → $y_3$ = draft.
4. **$P_4$ (Polishing):** *"Refine the draft $\langle y_3 \rangle$ for clarity, conciseness, and neutral tone. Output the final summary."* → $y_4$ = $\hat{y}$.

**When Chaining is Preferable (1 mark):**
- Tasks requiring *logical dependencies* between steps (e.g., plan → execute → verify).
- When each step produces *intermediate structured output* needed for the next.
- When the task is *too complex* to fit in a single prompt's context.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Practical Aggregation + Trade-offs

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 2 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain three aggregation strategies — Majority Voting, Weighted Voting, and LLM-as-a-Judge — used in multi-prompt inference. For each, give the mathematical formulation, a suitable use case, and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A team deploys a customer-support chatbot using multi-prompt inference with $n = 8$ prompts per query. The single-prompt accuracy is **62%**. The cost per LLM call is ₹0.50, and the chatbot handles **10,000 queries/day**. The business target is **90% accuracy**. As a systems architect:
  - (i) Compute the expected accuracy if self-consistency with $p = 0.62$ is used.
  - (ii) Compute the new daily inference cost.
  - (iii) Recommend whether the team should adopt multi-prompt inference, justifying with numbers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Strategy 1: Majority Voting (Hard Vote) (2.5 marks)**

- **Math:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$
- **Use Case:** Multiple-choice question answering, sentiment classification, math word problems.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle free-form generation; ties are unresolved without tie-breaking rules.

**Strategy 2: Weighted Voting (Soft Vote) (2.5 marks)**

- **Math:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$, where $w_i = \exp(\bar{\ell}_i) / \sum_j \exp(\bar{\ell}_j)$ and $\bar{\ell}_i$ is the average log-probability of the $i$-th output.
- **Use Case:** Tasks where the model is *uncertain* on some samples — low-confidence votes should weigh less.
- **Limitation:** Requires logit access (not always available via closed APIs); sensitive to overconfident wrong answers.

**Strategy 3: LLM-as-a-Judge (2 marks)**

- **Math:** $\hat{y} = M_{\theta}\!\big(\text{prompt}_{\text{judge}}(Q, \{y_i\})\big)$
- **Use Case:** Open-ended generation (creative writing, summarization) where voting is meaningless.
- **Limitation:** Judge LLM has its own biases (position bias, length bias, self-preference bias); adds $1 \times$ extra cost.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**(i) Expected Self-Consistency Accuracy (3 marks)**

Using the binomial formula with $n = 8$ and $p = 0.62$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{majority correct}) &= \sum_{k=5}^{8} \binom{8}{k} (0.62)^{k} (0.38)^{8-k} \\
&= \binom{8}{5}(0.62)^5(0.38)^3 + \binom{8}{6}(0.62)^6(0.38)^2 \\
&\quad + \binom{8}{7}(0.62)^7(0.38)^1 + \binom{8}{8}(0.62)^8
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing term-by-term:
- $T_5 = 56 \times 0.0916 \times 0.0548 = 0.2813$
- $T_6 = 28 \times 0.0568 \times 0.1444 = 0.2296$
- $T_7 = 8 \times 0.0352 \times 0.3800 = 0.1070$
- $T_8 = 1 \times 0.0218 = 0.0218$

Summing:

$$ P(\text{majority correct}) = 0.2813 + 0.2296 + 0.1070 + 0.0218 = 0.6397 \approx 64\% $$

**Result:** $A_{sc} \approx 64\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A common student error is to compute $P(C \ge n/2)$ as $P(C \ge 4)$ instead of $P(C > n/2) = P(C \ge 5)$ for even $n$. The strict majority rule requires $C > n/2$, not $C \ge n/2$.

**(ii) Daily Cost (2 marks)**

Original cost (single prompt): $10{,}000 \times 0.50 = ₹5{,}000$/day.

New cost (8 prompts + 1 judge): $(8+1) \times 10{,}000 \times 0.50 = ₹45{,}000$/day.

(Note: If LLM-as-judge is not used, cost is $8 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.50 = ₹40{,}000$/day.)

**(iii) Architectural Recommendation (2 marks)**

**Recommendation: DO NOT adopt multi-prompt inference at $n=8$ alone.**

**Justification:**
- Accuracy improvement: $62\% \to 64\%$ — only +2 percentage points.
- Target: $90\%$ — far from achieved.
- Cost increase: 8× to 9× (from ₹5K to ₹40K–45K per day).
- Cost per accuracy point: $(45{,}000 - 5{,}000) / 2 = ₹20{,}000$ per +1% accuracy — **economically unjustifiable**.

**Alternative Recommendations:**
1. **Fine-tune** the model on customer-support data to raise $p$ from 0.62 to ~0.80+ — far more cost-effective.
2. **Use RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** to ground responses in actual company knowledge.
3. If multi-prompt *must* be used, use **$n = 3$ or $4$** to get a marginal boost at reasonable cost.
4. Adopt **hybrid**: fine-tune first → then apply $n=3$ self-consistency as a final accuracy boost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ☐ Multi-Prompt Inference = multiple prompts + aggregation function.
- ☐ Core equation: $\hat{y} = f_{\text{agg}}\big(\{M_{\theta}(P_i, Q)\}_{i=1}^{n}\big)$.
- ☐ Two foundational assumptions: **local competence** ($p > 0.5$) and **error independence**.

### 🔢 Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ **Majority Vote:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$
- ☐ **Weighted Vote:** weights via softmax of average log-probabilities.
- ☐ **Pass@$k$:** $1 - \binom{n-c}{k}/\binom{n}{k}$.
- ☐ **Cost:** $C_{\text{total}} = n \cdot C_{\text{single}} + C_{\text{agg}}$.
- ☐ **Parallel Latency:** $L_{\text{parallel}} = \max_i L_i$.

### 🧠 Six Aggregation Strategies
- ☐ Majority Vote — MCQ / classification.
- ☐ Weighted Vote — when logprobs are available.
- ☐ Self-Consistency — CoT reasoning tasks.
- ☐ Best-of-N — open-ended generation with reward model.
- ☐ LLM-as-Judge — subjective, open-ended evaluation.
- ☐ Prompt Chaining — multi-step, dependent tasks.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- ☐ Voting requires *diversity* — identical prompts → identical outputs → no benefit.
- ☐ Don't confuse multi-prompt with multi-modal.
- ☐ Self-Consistency uses $T \in [0.5, 1.0]$, *not* $T = 0$.
- ☐ Multi-prompt inference is **expensive** — always quantify the cost-benefit.
- ☐ For Pass@$k$ with even $n$, majority = strict majority (C > n/2).

### 🏭 Engineering Trade-offs
- ☐ ↑ Accuracy, ↑ Robustness, ↓ Hallucination vs. ↑ Cost, ↑ Latency, ↑ Complexity.
- ☐ Diminishing returns beyond $n = 10$ in most reasoning tasks.
- ☐ Fine-tuning often beats multi-prompt inference on a per-rupee basis.

### 📜 Historical Context (For 1-Mark Bonus Questions)
- ☐ **Condorcet Jury Theorem (1785)** — mathematical basis of majority voting.
- ☐ **Self-Consistency (Wang et al., 2022)** — modern LLM application of Condorcet.
- ☐ **Best-of-N** — used heavily in AlphaCode, code-generation leaderboards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LLM Adaptation and Utilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** LLM Adaptation and Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 LLM Adaptation and Utilization — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LLM Adaptation and Utilization** refers to the spectrum of post-pretraining techniques and inference-time strategies used to specialize, align, and operationalize a Large Language Model for downstream tasks, human preferences, domain knowledge, and real-world deployment constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term encompasses **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Formal Definition | KTU Notation |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Adaptation** | Modifying a pretrained LLM's parameters (fully or partially) for a target task | *Parameter-space alignment* |
| **Alignment** | Constraining model outputs to human values, intent, and safety constraints | *Preference optimization* |
| **Utilization** | Leveraging a frozen LLM via prompting, retrieval, or orchestration without weight updates | *Inference-time computation* |

Mathematically, given a pretrained model with parameters $\theta_{\text{base}}$, adaptation seeks:

$$
\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta}(x), y\bigr) \right] + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)
$$

where $\Theta$ may be the full parameter space (full fine-tuning), a low-rank subspace (LoRA), or untouched (zero-shot utilization).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Doctor" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a brilliant **MBBS graduate** (the pretrained LLM) who has memorized every medical textbook in the world. They can answer generic questions, but they are not yet a **cardiologist** (domain specialist), not yet **empathetic** (aligned), and not yet allowed to **prescribe** (deployed safely).

- **Pretraining** = Medical school (broad knowledge of human physiology)
- **Fine-Tuning / SFT** = Residency in cardiology (task specialization on labeled cases)
- **RLHF / DPO** = Bedside-manner training + ethics seminars (alignment to human preferences)
- **RAG** = The doctor carrying a medical reference handbook into the consult (retrieval-augmented inference)
- **Prompt Engineering** = Asking the doctor the *right* question in the *right* way (in-context steering)
- **Quantization / Distillation** = Compressing 30 years of experience into a pocket-sized first-aid guide (efficient deployment)

This triad — **Specialize → Align → Deploy** — is the conceptual backbone of LLM Adaptation and Utilization.

---

## 1.3 The Adaptation Spectrum — A High-Level Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Three Pillars of LLM Adaptation:**

1. **Parameter-Modifying Adaptation** — touches the weights $\theta$
   - Full Fine-Tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, Adapters, Prefix-Tuning
2. **Preference-Based Alignment** — reshapes the output distribution
   - RLHF, DPO, KTO, ORPO, Constitutional AI
3. **Inference-Time Utilization** — keeps $\theta$ frozen
   - Prompt Engineering, In-Context Learning, Chain-of-Thought, RAG, Tool-Use, Agentic Workflows

These three pillars are **composable** — a production LLM stack (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama-3-Chat) typically uses *all three* simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar I — Parameter-Modifying Adaptation (Fine-Tuning Family)

### 2.1.1 Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)

SFT is the **first stage** of post-pretraining alignment. Given a dataset of instruction-response pairs $\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, SFT performs standard next-token cross-entropy minimization on the response $y$ conditioned on prompt $x$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}}(\theta) = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_i} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(y_{i,t} \mid x_i, y_{i,<t}\right)
$$

**Key Insight (Why this works):** Pretraining teaches the model the *distribution of language*. SFT teaches the model the *distribution of helpful responses*. The KL-divergence between the post-SFT and pretrained distributions is intentionally kept small to avoid catastrophic forgetting.

### 2.1.2 Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)

Full fine-tuning of a 70B model requires **~280 GB of GPU memory** for weights alone, plus optimizer states (Adam: 2× weights) and gradients. PEFT reduces this by training only a **tiny fraction** of parameters.

| PEFT Method | Trainable Params | Core Idea |
|-------------|------------------|-----------|
| **LoRA** (Low-Rank Adaptation) | ~0.1%–1% | Inject trainable low-rank matrices $A, B$ alongside frozen weights $W$ |
| **QLoRA** | ~0.05% | LoRA + 4-bit quantization of base model |
| **Adapters** | ~3%–5% | Insert small bottleneck MLPs into transformer blocks |
| **Prefix-Tuning** | ~0.1% | Prepend trainable continuous vectors to K and V |
| **Prompt-Tuning** | ~0.01% | Only the soft prompt is learned; no KV prefix |
| **IA³** | ~0.01% | Learn element-wise scaling vectors $\ell$ for K, V, FFN |

### 2.1.3 LoRA — The Math

For a pretrained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, LoRA constrains the update to a low-rank decomposition:

$$
W = W_0 + \Delta W = W_0 + B A
$$

where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, and **rank** $r \ll \min(d,k)$ (typically $r \in \{4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$).

The forward pass becomes:

$$
h = W_0 x + B A x
$$

Memory savings: trainable parameters drop from $d \times k$ to $r \times (d+k)$. For $d=k=4096$ and $r=8$, this is a **$\sim$65,000× reduction**.

### 2.1.4 QLoRA Quantization Mathematics

QLoRA quantizes $W_0$ to **4-bit NormalFloat (NF4)** with double quantization and paged optimizers. The dequantization operation:

$$
W_0^{\text{dequant}} = \text{scale} \cdot (W_0^{\text{NF4}} - \text{zero\_point})
$$

enables fine-tuning a 65B model on a **single 48 GB GPU**.

---

## 2.2 Pillar II — Preference-Based Alignment

### 2.2.1 RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)

RLHF is a **three-stage pipeline**:

1. **SFT** on demonstration data (covered above)
2. **Reward Modeling** — train a scalar reward model $r_{\phi}(x, y)$ on human pairwise preferences
3. **Policy Optimization** — fine-tune the LLM via PPO to maximize expected reward, regularized by a KL penalty against the SFT model

**Reward Model Loss** (Bradley-Terry pairwise):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) = - \mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \log \sigma\!\left(r_{\phi}(x, y_w) - r_{\phi}(x, y_l)\right) \right]
$$

where $y_w$ = preferred ("winner") response, $y_l$ = dispreferred ("loser") response.

**PPO Objective with KL Penalty:**

$$
\max_{\pi_{\theta}} \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi_{\theta}(y \mid x)} \left[ r_{\phi}(x, y) - \beta \cdot \text{KL}\!\left(\pi_{\theta}(y \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}(y \mid x)\right) \right]
$$

$\beta$ is the **KL coefficient** — a hyperparameter that controls how far the policy can drift from the SFT reference.

### 2.2.2 DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) — The Closed-Form Revolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) **eliminates the explicit reward model** by analytically inverting the RLHF objective. The optimal policy has a closed form in terms of the reward, allowing direct supervised learning on preference pairs.

The DPO loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) = - \mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)} \left[ \log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right) \right]
$$

Defining the **implicit reward**:

$$
r_{\theta}(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
$$

the loss simplifies to a binary classification on whether the model assigns higher implicit reward to $y_w$ than $y_l$.

**Why DPO matters:** Single-stage, no PPO instability, no separate reward model — a **~3–5× compute reduction** over RLHF with comparable alignment quality.

### 2.2.3 DPO vs RLHF Comparison

| Criterion | RLHF | DPO |
|-----------|------|-----|
| Stages | 3 (SFT → RM → PPO) | 2 (SFT → DPO) |
| Reward Model | Explicit neural network | Implicit in policy log-ratios |
| Training Stability | Sensitive to PPO hyperparams | Stable supervised objective |
| Compute | High (4 models in memory) | Lower (2 models: policy + ref) |
| KL Enforcement | Explicit penalty in reward | Implicit via reference log-probs |
| Online Sampling | Yes (rollouts from policy) | No (offline preference data) |

---

## 2.3 Pillar III — Inference-Time Utilization (Frozen-Weight Strategies)

### 2.3.1 Prompt Engineering Taxonomy

| Technique | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Zero-Shot** | Direct instruction, no examples | "Translate to French: Hello" |
| **Few-Shot (In-Context Learning)** | K examples in context | 3 translation demonstrations + query |
| **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** | Elicit intermediate reasoning | "Let's think step by step…" |
| **Self-Consistency** | Sample N CoT paths, majority vote | N=40 reasoning chains, pick most common answer |
| **Tree-of-Thoughts** | Branching search over reasoning | BFS/DFS over thought nodes |
| **ReAct** | Interleave reasoning + tool actions | Thought → Action → Observation loop |
| **Zero-Shot CoT** | Magic phrase "Let's think step by step" | No examples, just trigger |

### 2.3.2 In-Context Learning (ICL) — The Implicit Bayesian View

ICL is the phenomenon where a frozen LLM learns a new task from K examples in its context window. Theoretical analyses (e.g., the *implicit Bayesian inference* framework) posit that the LLM implicitly performs:

$$
P(y \mid x, \mathcal{C}) \propto P(y \mid x) \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{K} \lambda_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[y \sim \text{concept}(\mathcal{C}_i)]\right)
$$

where $\mathcal{C} = \{(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_K, y_K)\}$ is the in-context example set and $\lambda_i$ are task-relevant attention activations.

### 2.3.3 RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

RAG couples a frozen LLM with an external **non-parametric memory** (vector database). The generation pipeline:

$$
y = f_{\theta}\!\left( \text{concat}\!\left(x,\; \text{TopK}\!\left(\text{sim}(q, d_j)\right)_{j=1}^{K}\right) \right)
$$

where $q = \text{embed}(x)$ is the query embedding and similarity is typically cosine:

$$
\text{sim}(q, d_j) = \frac{q \cdot d_j}{\Vert q \Vert \, \Vert d_j \Vert}
$$

**Advanced RAG Variants (KTU Exam Favorites):**

- **Naive RAG** — query → retrieve → concatenate → generate
- **HyDE** (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) — generate a hypothetical answer, embed *that*, then retrieve
- **Multi-Query RAG** — LLM produces N query rewrites, retrieve for each, fuse results (RRF)
- **GraphRAG** — retrieve from a knowledge graph, not just a vector store
- **Self-RAG** — model emits special `[\Retrieve]` and `[\Critic]` tokens to decide *when* and *whether* to retrieve

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table — Print This for Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | SFT Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}} = -\sum_t \log P_\theta(y_t \mid x, y_{<t})$ | Cross-entropy (nats or bits) |
| 2 | LoRA Update | $W = W_0 + BA$, with $r \ll \min(d,k)$ | $r \in \{4, 8, 16, 32\}$ |
| 3 | Trainable Params Ratio | $\frac{r(d+k)}{dk}$ | For $d{=}k{=}4096, r{=}8$: $\approx 0.4\%$ |
| 4 | QLoRA NF4 Dequant | $W = \text{scale}\cdot(W_{\text{NF4}} - z)$ | 4-bit per weight |
| 5 | Bradley-Terry RM Loss | $-\log\sigma(r_\phi(x,y_w) - r_\phi(x,y_l))$ | Pairwise preference |
| 6 | PPO Objective | $\mathbb{E}[r_\phi(x,y)] - \beta \cdot \text{KL}(\pi_\theta \Vert \pi_{\text{SFT}})$ | $\beta$ = KL coefficient |
| 7 | DPO Loss | $-\log\sigma(\beta\Delta_\theta(x,y_w) - \beta\Delta_\theta(x,y_l))$ | $\Delta_\theta = \log\frac{\pi_\theta}{\pi_{\text{ref}}}$ |
| 8 | DPO Implicit Reward | $r_\theta(x,y) = \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y\vert x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\vert x)} + \beta\log Z(x)$ | $Z(x)$ = partition fn (constant per $x$) |
| 9 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \frac{q^\top d}{\Vert q \Vert \Vert d \Vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | RAG Generation | $y = f_\theta(\text{concat}(x, \text{TopK}_j\,\text{sim}(q,d_j)))$ | $K \in \{3, 5, 10\}$ |
| 11 | Quantization Memory | $\text{Mem} = \frac{N_{\text{params}} \times \text{bits}}{8 \times 2^{30}}$ GB | 7B at 16-bit: $\approx 14$ GB |
| 12 | Self-Consistency | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i^{(s)} = y]$ | $N \in \{20, 40, 64\}$ |
| 13 | ReAct Step | $(thought_t, action_t, obs_t) \to (thought_{t+1}, \ldots)$ | Action space = tool APIs |
| 14 | Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{new}} + \alpha \mathcal{L}_{\text{EWC}}$ | EWC = Elastic Weight Consolidation |
| 15 | Knowledge Distillation Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}} = (1-\alpha)\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} + \alpha T^2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{KL}}$ | $T$ = temperature |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Adaptation Strategy Used |
|-------------|-------------------------|
| **ChatGPT / Claude** | SFT → RLHF (or DPO) → RAG (Browse) → Tool-Use |
| **GitHub Copilot** | Continued pretraining on code → SFT on code-instruction → RLHF on developer prefs |
| **Medical LLM (Med-PaLM)** | Domain SFT on medical QA → RAG over PubMed |
| **Edge Deployed LLM (Phi-3-mini)** | Knowledge distillation from Phi-3-medium → 4-bit quantization |
| **Enterprise Search (Glean)** | RAG over corporate corpus + per-tenant LoRA fine-tunes |
| **Code Refactor Bot** | PEFT (LoRA) on internal repos + CoT prompting |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From RLHF Objective to DPO Closed Form

We start with the standard KL-regularized RLHF objective:

$$
\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi(y \mid x)} \left[ r(x, y) \right] - \beta \cdot \text{KL}\!\left(\pi(\cdot \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot \mid x)\right) \quad \forall x
$$

### Step 1: Rewrite the pointwise objective

For a fixed $x$, we maximize over distributions $\pi(\cdot \mid x)$:

$$
\max_{\pi} \sum_{y} \pi(y \mid x) r(x, y) - \beta \sum_{y} \pi(y \mid x) \log \frac{\pi(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce the partition function

The optimal solution is the **Gibbs distribution** (derivable via Lagrangian calculus with constraint $\sum_y \pi(y \mid x) = 1$):

$$
\pi^*(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right)
$$

where the partition function is:

$$
Z(x) = \sum_{y} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right)
$$

### Step 3: Take the log-ratio between $\pi^*$ and $\pi_{\text{ref}}$

$$
\log \frac{\pi^*(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} = \frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y) - \log Z(x)
$$

### Step 4: Invert to express the reward in terms of the policy

$$
r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
$$

### Step 5: Substitute into the Bradley-Terry preference model

The probability that $y_w \succ y_l$ under the Bradley-Terry model:

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma\!\left(r(x, y_w) - r(x, y_l)\right)
$$

Plugging in Step 4:

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)
$$

### Step 6: Maximum-Likelihood Estimation

The DPO loss is simply the negative log-likelihood of observed preferences, with the policy $\pi_\theta$ playing the role of the optimal $\pi^*$:

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) = - \mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right) \right]}
$$

**Interpretation:** DPO replaces the unstable PPO + reward-model pipeline with a **single supervised loss** that directly shapes the policy log-ratios — a beautiful reduction from a 3-stage pipeline to a 1-stage optimization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LoRA Memory Footprint

### Setup

Consider a single linear layer in a transformer with input dim $d$ and output dim $k$. Full fine-tuning requires storing:

| Tensor | Shape | Size (FP32) |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| Weights $W$ | $d \times k$ | $4dk$ bytes |
| Gradients $\nabla W$ | $d \times k$ | $4dk$ bytes |
| Adam moments $m, v$ | $d \times k$ each | $8dk$ bytes |
| **Total** | — | **$16dk$ bytes** |

### LoRA Storage

The frozen base $W_0$ is stored in its original precision. Only $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ are trainable:

| Tensor | Shape | Size (FP32) |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| Frozen $W_0$ | $d \times k$ | $4dk$ bytes (no grad/opt) |
| Trainable $A$ | $r \times k$ | $4rk$ bytes |
| Trainable $B$ | $d \times r$ | $4dr$ bytes |
| Adam on $A$ | $r \times k$ | $8rk$ bytes |
| Adam on $B$ | $d \times r$ | $8dr$ bytes |
| **Total trainable** | — | **$4r(d+k) + 8r(d+k) = 12r(d+k)$ bytes** |

### Memory Ratio

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{12 r (d+k)}{16 d k} = \frac{3r(d+k)}{4 dk}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $d = k = 4096$, $r = 8$:

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{3 \cdot 8 \cdot (4096 + 4096)}{4 \cdot 4096 \cdot 4096} = \frac{196{,}608}{67{,}108{,}864} \approx 0.0029 \;\;(\mathbf{0.29\%})
$$

A **~340× reduction** in trainable memory, exactly as advertised in the original LoRA paper.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RAG Top-K Cosine Retrieval Scoring

Given query embedding $q \in \mathbb{R}^D$ and document embeddings $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_M\} \subset \mathbb{R}^D$:

### Step 1: Compute raw dot product scores

$$
s_j = q^\top d_j = \sum_{i=1}^{D} q_i \cdot d_{j,i} \quad \text{for } j = 1, \ldots, M
$$

### Step 2: Compute L2 norms

$$
\| q \| = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{D} q_i^2}, \qquad \| d_j \| = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{D} d_{j,i}^2}
$$

### Step 3: Normalize to cosine similarity

$$
\cos\theta_j = \frac{q^\top d_j}{\| q \| \cdot \| d_j \|} \quad \in [-1, 1]
$$

### Step 4: Top-K selection

$$
\text{TopK}(q, D) = \arg\!\max_{S \subseteq D, \, \vert S \vert = K} \sum_{d \in S} \cos(q, d)
$$

This is implemented efficiently via **Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)** using HNSW, IVF-PQ, or ScaNN indexes.

### Step 5: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) for multi-query RAG

When $Q = \{q^{(1)}, \ldots, q^{(N)}\}$ are query rewrites, RRF combines rankings:

$$
\text{RRF\_score}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{k_0 + \text{rank}_i(d)}
$$

where $k_0 \approx 60$ is a smoothing constant and $\text{rank}_i(d)$ is $d$'s rank in the $i$-th retrieval.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Knowledge Distillation Loss for LLM Compression

Given teacher logits $z^{(T)}$ and student logits $z^{(S)}$ at temperature $T$:

### Soft Targets (KL Divergence Term)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}} = T^2 \cdot \text{KL}\!\left(\sigma\!\left(\frac{z^{(T)}}{T}\right) \,\Big\|\, \sigma\!\left(\frac{z^{(S)}}{T}\right)\right)
$$

### Hard Targets (Cross-Entropy)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \sigma(z^{(S)})_c
$$

### Combined Distillation Loss

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = (1 - \alpha) \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} + \alpha \mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}}
$$

where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ balances hard-label supervision and soft-label mimicry. The $T^2$ factor compensates for the **gradient magnitude shrinkage** caused by the softmax temperature.

**KTU Note:** Why distill? A 7B student distilled from a 70B teacher can recover **~90%** of the teacher's quality at **1/10th the inference cost**.
