\begin{aligned}
\frac{M_{\text{LoRA}}}{M_{\text{full}}} &= \frac{2N + 10 P_{\text{LoRA}}}{12 N} \\
&\approx \frac{2N}{12N} \quad \text{(since } P_{\text{LoRA}} \ll N\text{)} \\
&\approx \frac{1}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **~6× reduction** in VRAM.

### 3.3.3 QLoRA Memory (NF4-quantized base + FP16 LoRA)

QLoRA quantizes the base model to **4-bit NormalFloat (NF4)**, reducing weight memory by 4×:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{model, QLoRA}} &= 0.5 N \quad &\text{(4-bit weights, frozen)} \\
M_{\text{LoRA params}} &= 2 P_{\text{LoRA}} \quad &\text{(LoRA in FP16)} \\
M_{\text{LoRA gradients}} &= 2 P_{\text{LoRA}} \\
M_{\text{LoRA optimizer}} &= 8 P_{\text{LoRA}} \\
\hline
M_{\text{total (QLoRA)}} &\approx 0.5 N + 12 P_{\text{LoRA}} + M_{\text{act}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For GPT-3 175B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &\approx 0.5 \times 175 \times 10^9 + 12 \times 75 \times 10^6 \\
&= 87.5 \times 10^9 + 0.9 \times 10^9 \\
&\approx \mathbf{88.4\ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction factor vs full fine-tuning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M_{\text{QLoRA}}}{M_{\text{full}}} &= \frac{0.5 N}{12 N} = \frac{1}{24}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QLoRA achieves a ~24× memory reduction** compared to full fine-tuning. This is why QLoRA made it possible to fine-tune a 65B-parameter LLaMA model on a **single 48 GB GPU** (e.g., a single A6000 or A40) — a feat previously thought impossible.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Adapter Module Forward Pass

The Houlsby adapter is a bottleneck module inserted after the attention and FFN sub-layers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{down}} &= f(W_{\text{down}} \cdot h) \quad &\in \mathbb{R}^{r_a} \\
h_{\text{up}} &= W_{\text{up}} \cdot h_{\text{down}} \quad &\in \mathbb{R}^{d} \\
h' &= h + h_{\text{up}} \quad &\text{(residual connection)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $W_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{r_a \times d}$ (down-projection)
- $W_{\text{up}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r_a}$ (up-projection)
- $f$ is a non-linearity (e.g., ReLU, GeLU, or SwiGLU)
- $r_a \ll d$ (typically $r_a = 8$ or $r_a = 64$)

**Trainable parameters per adapter module:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{adapter}} &= r_a \cdot d + d \cdot r_a \\
&= 2 d r_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**For LLaMA-7B with $d = 4096$ and $r_a = 8$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{adapter}} &= 2 \times 4096 \times 8 \\
&= 65{,}536 \text{ parameters per module}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Initialization Convention:** The up-projection $W_{\text{up}}$ is initialized to **zero** so that the adapter starts as an identity function: $h' = h$ at training step 0. This mirrors the LoRA initialization philosophy — start from the pre-trained model, learn deltas.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Prefix Tuning Trainable Parameter Count

Prefix tuning prepends trainable vectors to the **Key** and **Value** matrices at **every transformer layer**. For a prefix of length $n_p$ at layer $l$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_l &\in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d} \\
P_l^{(k)}, P_l^{(v)} &\in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d} \quad \text{(key and value prefixes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full prefix parameters across $L$ layers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{prefix}} &= L \times n_p \times 2d
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a 24-layer model with $d = 1024$ and $n_p = 20$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{prefix}} &= 24 \times 20 \times 2 \times 1024 \\
&= 983{,}040 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

The original Li & Liang (2021) paper **reparameterizes** the prefix through a small MLP for training stability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_l &= \text{MLP}_l(E_l)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E_l \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d'}$ is a smaller embedding (e.g., $d' = d$), and $\text{MLP}_l: \mathbb{R}^{d'} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a 2-layer feed-forward network. After training, the MLP is **absorbed** into $P_l$ for inference.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — BitFit Parameter Count

BitFit updates only the bias terms of the network. The biases present in a transformer layer are:

- $b_q, b_k, b_v, b_o$ — attention biases (4 vectors of dimension $d$)
- $b_{ffn,1}, b_{ffn,2}$ — FFN biases (2 vectors of dimension $d$ or $d_{ffn}$)

**Total biases per layer (typical configuration):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{bias, layer}} &= 4d + 2d \\
&= 6d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total across $L$ layers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{BitFit}} &= 6 L d
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 24 layers, $d = 1024$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{BitFit}} &= 6 \times 24 \times 1024 \\
&= 147{,}456 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison to full model ($\approx 6 \times 10^8$ params for a 350M model):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{\text{BitFit}}}{P_{\text{full}}} &= \frac{147{,}456}{600{,}000{,}000} \\
&\approx 2.46 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.0246\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Finding:** Despite training only **0.02%** of the parameters, BitFit achieves competitive performance on small-to-medium-sized GLUE tasks, demonstrating that bias terms carry significant task-specific information.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Decision Criterion | Recommended PEFT | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single-task, max accuracy needed | LoRA ($r = 16$ or $r = 32$) | Best accuracy / parameter trade-off |
| Single GPU (< 24 GB VRAM) | QLoRA + NF4 | 4-bit base + LoRA fits 7B – 13B models |
| Multi-task learning, modular | Adapter Layers | Stacking adapters enables task isolation |
| Generative task, few-shot style | Prefix Tuning | Designed for autoregressive generation |
| Absolute minimum parameters | BitFit / IA³ | Bias-only or activation scaling |
| Production deployment, zero latency | LoRA (with merged weights) | $W_{\text{merged}} = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$ at deployment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — LoRA Architecture: Side-by-Side Comparison

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how LoRA injects a **parallel low-rank path** alongside the frozen pre-trained weight matrix.

```mermaid
graph TB
    inputX[Input Vector X d-dim]
    inputX --> frozenW[Frozen Weight Matrix W d x k]
    inputX --> matrixA[Trainable Matrix A r x k initialized Gaussian]
    matrixA --> matmul1[Matrix Multiply A times X yields r-dim]
    matrixB[Trainable Matrix B d x r initialized Zeros] --> matmul2[Matrix Multiply B times A times X yields d-dim]
    matmul1 --> matmul2
    matmul2 --> scalePath[Scale by alpha divided by r]
    frozenW --> addOp[Element-wise Addition]
    scalePath --> addOp
    addOp --> outputH[Output Vector H d-dim]

    style frozenW fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style matrixA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style matrixB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style outputH fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The frozen path (gray) and the LoRA path (blue) are computed **in parallel**. The two outputs are summed at the `addOp` node. After training, the LoRA matrices can be merged into the frozen weight for zero-overhead inference.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — PEFT Method Taxonomy (Classification Tree)

```mermaid
graph TB
    rootPEFT[PEFT Compute-Efficient Fine-Tuning]
    rootPEFT --> additive[Additive Methods]
    rootPEFT --> selective[Selective Methods]
    rootPEFT --> reparam[Reparameterization Methods]

    additive --> adapter[Adapter Layers Houlsby 2019]
    additive --> prefix[Prefix Tuning Li Liang 2021]
    additive --> prompt[Prompt Tuning Lester 2021]
    additive --> ptv2[P-Tuning v2 Liu 2022]

    selective --> bitfit[BitFit Ben-Zaken 2021]
    selective --> toplayers[Top Layer Tuning]

    reparam --> lora[LoRA Hu 2021]
    lora --> qlora[QLoRA Dettmers 2023]
    reparam --> krona[KronA Edalati 2022]
    reparam --> ia3[IA3 Liu 2022]

    style rootPEFT fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:3px
    style additive fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:2px
    style selective fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px
    style reparam fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px
    style lora fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy explanation:** PEFT is the parent category. LoRA (highlighted in gold) is the most popular method and forms the foundation for QLoRA, the most memory-efficient method in current production use.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — LoRA Deployment Lifecycle (Training → Merge → Inference)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stage1[Stage 1 Training Phase] --> stage2[Stage 2 Optional Merge]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Inference Phase]

    stage1 --> detail1[Frozen W0 kept in VRAM]
    stage1 --> detail2[LoRA A and B trainable]
    stage1 --> detail3[Optimizer states only for A and B]

    stage2 --> mergeOp[Compute Wmerged = W0 + alpha over r times B times A]
    mergeOp --> discardA[Discard A and B]
    discardA --> deployW[Deploy only Wmerged of shape d times k]

    stage3 --> inferOp[Inference: H = Wmerged times X identical to original model]
    inferOp --> zeroOver[Zero additional latency]

    style stage1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style stage2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px
    style stage3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style zeroOver fill:#28a745,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The merge step is what makes LoRA a **zero-cost deployment** technique. After training, you can either (a) keep the small LoRA files for hot-swapping between tasks, or (b) merge and deploy as a single standard model. Many production systems choose (a) for multi-task serving.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Memory Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph fullFT[Full Fine-Tuning Memory ~ 12N]
        fw1[Model Weights 2N]
        fg1[Gradients 2N]
        fo1[Optimizer 8N]
    end

    subgraph loraM[LoRA Memory ~ 2N + 10P]
        lw1[Model Weights 2N Frozen]
        lg1[LoRA Gradients 2P]
        lo1[LoRA Optimizer 8P]
    end

    subgraph qloraM[QLoRA Memory ~ 0.5N + 12P]
        qw1[NF4 Quantized Weights 0.5N]
        qg1[LoRA Gradients 2P]
        qo1[LoRA Optimizer 8P]
        qd1[Double Quantization Overhead]
        qp1[Paged Optimizer Memory]
    end

    fullFT -->|6x reduction| loraM
    loraM -->|4x further reduction| qloraM

    style fullFT fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px
    style loraM fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px
    style qloraM fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**
> **"Differentiate between full fine-tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). List any two PEFT techniques with one-line descriptions."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Full Fine-Tuning | PEFT |
|---|---|---|
| Trainable Parameters | 100% of model weights | 0.1% – 5% of model weights |
| GPU Memory Required | $\approx 12N$ bytes (weights + grads + optimizer) | $\approx 2N$ to $0.5N$ bytes |
| Storage per Task | Full model copy (hundreds of GB) | Adapter file ($\sim$10 – 100 MB) |
| Risk of Catastrophic Forgetting | High | Low |
| Inference Latency Overhead | None | None (after LoRA merge) |

**Two PEFT Techniques:**

1. **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation):** Approximates the weight update $\Delta W$ as a low-rank product $\frac{\alpha}{r} B A$, with $r \ll \min(d, k)$.
2. **Adapter Layers:** Inserts small bottleneck feed-forward modules (down-projection → non-linearity → up-projection) sequentially within each transformer layer, while keeping the original weights frozen.

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**
> **"Explain the role of the scaling factor $\alpha / r$ in LoRA. Why is matrix $B$ initialized to zero while matrix $A$ is initialized with a Gaussian distribution?"**

**Model Answer:**

The scaling factor $\frac{\alpha}{r}$ in the update rule $W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$ serves two purposes:

1. **Magnitude Normalization:** It ensures that the magnitude of $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$ does not depend on the chosen rank $r$. This means that increasing $r$ adds *capacity* (more directions to learn) but does **not** inflate the initial magnitude of the weight update — keeping training stable across different rank choices.

2. **Hyperparameter Control:** $\alpha$ acts as a "learning rate" for the LoRA path. A common heuristic is $\alpha = 2r$, which empirically performs well across tasks.

**Initialization Convention:**

- **Matrix $A$ is initialized from a Gaussian distribution** (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with small $\sigma$): this breaks the symmetry between input dimensions and ensures that $A x$ produces a non-zero, non-trivial intermediate representation.
- **Matrix $B$ is initialized to zero:** this ensures that $\Delta W = B A = 0$ at training step 0. Therefore, the model output $h = W_0 x + 0 = W_0 x$ is **identical** to the pre-trained model output at the start of training — preventing any random initial perturbation from degrading the model's pre-trained capabilities.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake students make in PEFT questions is **(a)** forgetting the $\alpha/r$ scaling factor when computing $\Delta W$, **(b)** confusing trainable parameter count with total parameter count, and **(c)** writing the adapter forward pass without the residual connection. Marks are deducted for: missing scaling factors, incorrect initialization, omitting the residual path, and not stating the merging step in LoRA. Always draw the architecture diagram — a labeled diagram alone can earn you 2–3 marks even if your math has errors.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style, Module 2]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)** method in detail. Derive the trainable parameter count for a transformer attention layer (with $W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$) and compare it with full fine-tuning for $d = 4096$ and rank $r = 8$. Show the forward pass equation and explain the initialization strategy.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **QLoRA** method. Explain **NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat) quantization**, **double quantization**, and **paged optimizers**. Show how QLoRA achieves a ~24× memory reduction over full fine-tuning for a 175B-parameter model.

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**LoRA Method Explanation:**

LoRA freezes the pre-trained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ and constrains its update $\Delta W$ to be a low-rank decomposition. The forward pass becomes:

$$h = W' x = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x$$

where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, and $r \ll \min(d, k)$.

**Parameter Count Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{full, attn}} &= 4 \times d \times d = 4 d^2 \\
P_{\text{LoRA, attn}} &= 4 \times r(d + k) = 8 r d \quad \text{(since } d = k\text{)} \\
\frac{P_{\text{LoRA}}}{P_{\text{full}}} &= \frac{8 r d}{4 d^2} = \frac{2 r}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Substitution ($d = 4096$, $r = 8$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{full}} &= 4 \times 4096^2 = 67{,}108{,}864 \\
P_{\text{LoRA}} &= 8 \times 8 \times 4096 = 262{,}144 \\
\frac{P_{\text{LoRA}}}{P_{\text{full}}} &= \frac{2 \times 8}{4096} = \frac{1}{256}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initialization Strategy:**
- $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ — breaks symmetry
- $B = 0$ — ensures $\Delta W = 0$ at step 0

**Deployment Trick:** After training, $W_{\text{merged}} = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$ can be precomputed, yielding **zero inference overhead**.

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**QLoRA Components:**

1. **NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat) Quantization:** A custom 4-bit data type optimized for the normal distribution of neural network weights. It uses **quantile-based binning** so that each quantization bin contains an equal number of weights under a $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ distribution. This preserves more information than uniform 4-bit quantization.

2. **Double Quantization:** The **quantization constants** (block-wise scaling factors) used in the first quantization are themselves **quantized again**, saving an additional ~3 bits per parameter on average.

3. **Paged Optimizers:** Uses **NVIDIA's unified memory** to page optimizer states to CPU RAM when GPU VRAM is saturated, preventing out-of-memory errors during gradient checkpointing spikes.

**Memory Analysis for 175B model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{full}} &\approx 12 N = 12 \times 175 \times 10^9 = 2.1 \text{ TB} \\
M_{\text{QLoRA}} &\approx 0.5 N + 12 P_{\text{LoRA}} = 87.5 \text{ GB} + 0.9 \text{ GB} \approx 88.4 \text{ GB} \\
\frac{M_{\text{QLoRA}}}{M_{\text{full}}} &= \frac{0.5 N}{12 N} = \frac{1}{24}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2025 Style, Module 2]**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Adapter Layers** in detail. Derive the trainable parameter count and the forward pass equation. Compare Houlsby adapters with **Lin et al. (Compacter)** adapters.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe **Prefix Tuning** and **Prompt Tuning**. Derive the trainable parameter count for each. Compare them with adapter-based methods in terms of accuracy and inference cost.

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Houlsby Adapter Module:**

A bottleneck FFN inserted after both the attention and FFN sub-layers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{down}} &= f(W_{\text{down}} h) \in \mathbb{R}^{r_a} \\
h_{\text{up}} &= W_{\text{up}} h_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \\
h' &= h + h_{\text{up}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trainable parameters per adapter module:**

$$P_{\text{adapter}} = r_a \cdot d + d \cdot r_a = 2 d r_a$$

**For $d = 4096$ and $r_a = 8$:**

$$P_{\text{adapter}} = 2 \times 4096 \times 8 = 65{,}536 \text{ parameters per module}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Houlsby Adapter | Compacter (Lin et al.) |
|---|---|---|
| Insertion Point | After attention **and** FFN | After FFN only |
| Decomposition | Standard bottleneck | Kronecker product (Kronecker Adapter) |
| Parameter Count | $2 d r_a$ | $d + r_a$ (much smaller) |
| Task Performance | Strong baseline | Comparable with fewer params |
| Inference Overhead | Slight (sequential add) | Slight (sequential add) |

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Prefix Tuning:**

Trainable prefix vectors are prepended to the **Key** and **Value** matrices at every transformer layer. For prefix length $n_p$ and $L$ layers:

$$P_{\text{prefix}} = L \cdot n_p \cdot 2 d$$

**Reparameterization for training stability:**

$$P_l = \text{MLP}_l(E_l), \quad E_l \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}$$

**Prompt Tuning:**

Trainable soft prompt embeddings are prepended **only at the input layer**:

$$P_{\text{prompt}} = n_p \cdot d$$

**Comparison with Adapter Methods:**

| Metric | Prefix Tuning | Prompt Tuning | Adapter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trainable Params | High (across all layers) | Very Low (input only) | Medium |
| Accuracy on Generation | **Best** (controls all layers) | Weaker (input only) | Good |
| Accuracy on Understanding | Good | Weak | **Best** |
| Inference Latency | Slight (longer K, V) | None (merged) | Slight |

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway for exam:** Prefix Tuning is best for **generative tasks** (summarization, translation). Prompt Tuning is best for **scale** (works well only at very large model sizes, e.g., > 10B). Adapters are best for **classification** and multi-task learning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning)** trains **0.1% – 5%** of model parameters, vs. 100% for full fine-tuning.
- [x] **LoRA** update rule: $W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$ with $A$ Gaussian-initialized, $B$ zero-initialized.
- [x] **LoRA trainable params per matrix:** $r(d + k)$ — about **$2r/d$** of full fine-tuning.
- [x] **LoRA forward pass:** $h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x$ — can be **merged** for zero inference overhead.
- [x] **Adapter module:** Bottleneck FFN $h' = h + W_{\text{up}} f(W_{\text{down}} h)$ with $2 d r_a$ parameters.
- [x] **Prefix Tuning:** Adds trainable $n_p \times d$ vectors to K and V at **every layer**.
- [x] **Prompt Tuning:** Adds trainable $n_p \times d$ soft prompts **only at the input**.
- [x] **BitFit:** Updates only bias terms — roughly $6 L d$ parameters.
- [x] **IA³:** Element-wise scales attention and FFN activations with learnable vectors.
- [x] **QLoRA:** LoRA on a **4-bit NF4 quantized** base model — achieves ~24× memory reduction.
- [x] **QLoRA components:** NF4 quantization, double quantization, paged optimizers.
- [x] **Memory hierarchy (175B model):** Full = 2.1 TB → LoRA = 350 GB → QLoRA = 88 GB.
- [x] **Parameter reduction hierarchy (r=8, d=4096):** LoRA = 1/256 of full; BitFit = ~0.02% of full.
- [x] **Three pillars of PEFT:** Additive (Adapter, Prefix, Prompt), Selective (BitFit), Reparameterization (LoRA, QLoRA, KronA).
- [x] **Decision rubric:** LoRA for general use, QLoRA for low VRAM, Adapter for multi-task, Prefix for generation, BitFit for minimum parameters.
- [x] **LoRA ranks used in practice:** $r \in \{4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128\}$, with $r = 8$ being the most common default.
- [x] **Why $B = 0$ at initialization:** Ensures $\Delta W = 0$ at step 0, preserving pre-trained behavior.
- [x] **Why $\alpha = 2r$ is common:** Decouples learning rate scaling from rank; empirically robust.
- [x] **LoRA is applied to:** Attention matrices $W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o$ and sometimes FFN matrices $W_{up}, W_{down}$ in modern implementations.
- [x] **NF4 (NormalFloat-4):** A 4-bit data type with 16 quantile-based bins matched to a $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any PEFT numerical question, always **state the assumptions** ($d = k$, rank values, layer counts), **show the substitution step-by-step**, **include the unit** (parameters, bytes, GB), and **conclude with a comparative statement** ("therefore LoRA achieves a $X\times$ reduction"). Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the comparative conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: End-User Prompting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** End-User Prompting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**End-User Prompting** is the practice wherein non-developer users (end users) interact with Large Language Models (LLMs) by crafting natural language inputs—known as **prompts**—to elicit desired, task-specific outputs without requiring direct access to the model's internal parameters, application programming interfaces (APIs), or fine-tuning procedures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance aligned with **Course Outcome CO2** (Analyse prompt design strategies for downstream LLM applications), end-user prompting is positioned as the *primary interaction surface* between human intent and model behaviour. It leverages the model's emergent **In-Context Learning (ICL)** capability, where the LLM infers the task from a small number of demonstrations or natural language instructions embedded directly in the input sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECMT862 Module 2):**
> End-user prompting is the foundational skill upon which Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), agents, and tool-use patterns are constructed. Mastery of this topic is **directly examinable** in Part A (3 marks) and forms the conceptual base for Part B (14 marks) questions on prompt engineering and evaluation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just hired a **brilliant but extremely literal-minded personal assistant** named *LLM-Bot*. This assistant:

- Has read nearly every book in the library, but has **no memory of yesterday's conversation** (stateless).
- Will interpret your words **exactly as written**, with no mind-reading.
- Performs dramatically better when you give **clear role + clear task + clear examples + clear format**.

**End-User Prompting is the art and science of giving LLM-Bot the right "briefing memo"** so it produces the precise answer you need. A vague request ("Tell me about dogs") yields a generic essay; a well-engineered prompt ("Act as a veterinarian. List the top 3 health risks for a 2-year-old Labrador in bullet form, citing breed-specific statistics") yields a focused, structured, useful response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The quality of an LLM's output is bounded by the quality of the prompt. This is captured informally in the mantra: *"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)"*—but in the LLM era, we refine it to: *"Precision In, Performance Out."*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Prompt** | The complete natural language input supplied to the LLM, including instructions, context, examples, and the actual query |
| **Token** | The atomic unit of text processed by an LLM (≈ 0.75 words in English); governed by the model's **context window** |
| **Context Window** | The maximum number of tokens the model can process in a single forward pass (e.g., **8K**, **128K**, **1M** tokens) |
| **Zero-Shot Prompting** | A prompt containing **no task demonstrations**, relying solely on the model's pre-trained knowledge |
| **Few-Shot Prompting** | A prompt containing **K examples** (typically K = 2 to 16) of input→output pairs before the actual query |
| **In-Context Learning (ICL)** | The emergent ability of LLMs to learn new tasks from examples provided *inside* the prompt at inference time, **without weight updates** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a High-Quality Prompt

A well-engineered prompt consists of **five interlocking components**, each serving a distinct purpose. We call this the **P.R.I.D.E. Framework** (mnemonic for KTU recall):

- **P — Persona / Role**: Assigning the LLM an identity (e.g., *"You are a senior data scientist…"*). This primes the model's distribution towards domain-specific vocabulary and reasoning patterns.
- **R — Request / Instruction**: A clear, unambiguous statement of the task (e.g., *"Summarize the following text in three sentences."*).
- **I — Input Data**: The actual content to be processed (e.g., the article, code snippet, or question).
- **D — Demonstrations (Optional)**: Example input-output pairs for few-shot prompting.
- **E — Expected Output Format / Constraints**: Specifications such as JSON schema, bullet points, word limit, tone, or "step-by-step reasoning."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** LLMs are next-token predictors trained on vast corpora. By providing role + instruction + format, the user **conditions the probability distribution** $P(y \mid x)$ of the output $y$ given input $x$, steering generation toward the desired region of output space.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of End-User Prompting Techniques

The following structured breakdown represents the core prompting paradigms covered in **Module 2 of PECMT862**:

- **Direct (Zero-Shot) Prompting**
  - Simplest form: only the instruction is given.
  - Relies entirely on the model's pre-trained instruction-following ability.
  - Best for: simple classification, factual recall, rewriting.

- **One-Shot Prompting**
  - A single demonstration is provided before the query.
  - Useful when the desired output format is unusual.

- **Few-Shot Prompting**
  - Multiple (typically 2–16) demonstrations are provided.
  - Empirically shown to drastically improve performance on tasks requiring pattern recognition, format adherence, and reasoning chains.
  - Original formalization: Brown et al., 2020, "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners."

- **Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting**
  - Demonstrations include **intermediate reasoning steps** before the final answer.
  - Enables the model to "think aloud," improving performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.

- **Role / Persona Prompting**
  - Assigning a specific expert identity to influence tone, vocabulary, and domain depth.

- **ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) Prompting**
  - Interleaves *Thought*, *Action*, and *Observation* steps, enabling tool use and agentic behaviour.

- **Self-Consistency / Self-Refine**
  - Generate multiple candidate outputs, then select the most consistent or iteratively critique and revise.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Formal Representation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **LLM Objective (Training)** | $\max_{\theta} \sum_{i} \log P_{\theta}(y_i \mid x_1, \dots, x_{i-1})$ | Standard autoregressive language modelling loss |
| **Conditional Generation** | $P(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P_{\theta}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ | Probability of output $y$ given prompt $x$ |
| **In-Context Learning** | $P(y \mid x, \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{K})$ | $K$ demonstrations augment the conditioning context |
| **Effective Context Budget** | $B_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{window}} - T_{\text{response}}$ | Where $C_{\text{window}}$ is total context and $T_{\text{response}}$ is reserved output tokens |
| **Token Approximation** | $1 \text{ token} \approx 0.75 \text{ English words} \approx 4 \text{ characters}$ | Useful for prompt length estimation |
| **Temperature Effect** | $P_{\text{soft}}(y_t) = \frac{\exp(z_t / \tau)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$ | $\tau \to 0$ deterministic; $\tau \to \infty$ uniform; default $\tau = 1$ |
| **Perplexity (Quality Proxy)** | $\text{PPL}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | Lower PPL ⇒ more confident, fluent predictions |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, the absolute value bars have been rendered using LaTeX `\vert` to comply with KTU Markdown Table Safety Standards. The vertical pipe `\vert` symbol must **never** appear inside a Markdown table cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

End-user prompting is the **central interaction layer** for:

- **Customer support automation** (chatbots, ticket triage)
- **Code generation assistants** (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Tabnine)
- **Content creation pipelines** (marketing copy, technical documentation)
- **Educational tutoring systems** (Socratic math tutors, language learning bots)
- **Data extraction from unstructured text** (invoice parsing, contract analysis)
- **Scientific research acceleration** (literature summarization, hypothesis generation)

In the KTU 2024 curriculum, this is the gateway concept to **RAG, agents, and tool calling** in higher modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Information-Theoretic Basis of Few-Shot Prompting

We derive, step by step, *why* few-shot examples improve LLM performance. This is a high-yield analytical exercise commonly asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

**Step 1 — Formal Setup.**

Let $f_{\theta}$ denote a pre-trained autoregressive LLM with parameters $\theta$. The model defines a probability distribution over output token sequences conditioned on a context $c$:

$$
P_{\theta}(y \mid c) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P_{\theta}\!\left(y_t \mid y_{<t},\, c\right)
$$

where $T$ is the length of the output sequence.

**Step 2 — Zero-Shot Conditioning.**

In zero-shot prompting, the context $c$ contains only the task instruction $I$ and the query $q$:

$$
c^{(0)} = (I, q)
$$

The model produces:

$$
\hat{y}^{(0)} = \arg\max_{y} \, P_{\theta}\!\left(y \mid c^{(0)}\right)
$$

This corresponds to a single peak in a vast, high-dimensional probability space.

**Step 3 — Few-Shot Conditioning.**

In few-shot prompting, we prepend $K$ demonstrations $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{K}$ to the context:

$$
c^{(K)} = \big(I,\; (x_1, y_1),\; (x_2, y_2),\; \dots,\; (x_K, y_K),\; q \big)
$$

The model now optimises:

$$
\hat{y}^{(K)} = \arg\max_{y} \, P_{\theta}\!\left(y \mid c^{(K)}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Information-Theoretic Interpretation.**

Let $H(Y \mid C)$ denote the conditional entropy of the output given context. Adding demonstrations **reduces the entropy** of the output distribution by providing statistical evidence about the desired mapping $x \mapsto y$:

$$
H\!\left(Y \mid c^{(K)}\right) \;\leq\; H\!\left(Y \mid c^{(0)}\right)
$$

In other words, the examples **collapse** the probability mass onto the correct region of the output space.

**Step 5 — Cross-Entropy Reduction (Quantitative).**

For a target output $y^{\star}$, the cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(K)} = -\log P_{\theta}\!\left(y^{\star} \mid c^{(K)}\right) \;\leq\; \mathcal{L}^{(0)} = -\log P_{\theta}\!\left(y^{\star} \mid c^{(0)}\right)
$$

Empirically, this manifests as **higher accuracy, better format adherence, and reduced hallucination** when demonstrations are well-chosen.

**Step 6 — Practical Bound (Context Window Limit).**

However, the benefit saturates because $K$ demonstrations consume tokens:

$$
T_{\text{prompt}} \;=\; T_I + \sum_{i=1}^{K} (T_{x_i} + T_{y_i}) + T_q
$$

The constraint is:

$$
T_{\text{prompt}} \;\leq\; C_{\text{window}} - T_{\text{reserve}}
$$

Therefore, in practice one must balance demonstration richness against context economy.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Prompting Technique Selection

| Task Characteristic | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Simple factual recall | Zero-Shot | Instruction-following is sufficient; saves tokens |
| Unusual output format | One-Shot / Few-Shot | Demonstrations teach the format by example |
| Multi-step arithmetic | Chain-of-Thought | Explicit reasoning unlocks latent reasoning ability |
| Domain-specific expertise | Role Prompting | Persona primes domain vocabulary |
| Real-time data access | ReAct + Tool Use | Model must call external APIs/databases |
| High-stakes accuracy | Self-Consistency | Multiple samples reduce variance and error rate |
| Customer-facing chat | Role + Few-Shot + Constraints | Combines persona, examples, and safety guardrails |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Designing a Prompt (Analytical Walk-Through)

**Scenario:** A KTU student wants the LLM to extract structured JSON from a job description.

**Naïve Prompt (Zero-Shot):**
> "Extract the skills from this job description."

**Engineered Prompt (Following P.R.I.D.E.):**
> *"You are an expert technical recruiter. Extract skills from the job description below. Return a valid JSON object with two keys: `technical_skills` (array of strings) and `soft_skills` (array of strings). If a category is empty, return an empty array.*
>
> *Example:*
> *Input: 'Looking for a Python developer with teamwork skills.'*
> *Output: `{"technical_skills": ["Python"], "soft_skills": ["teamwork"]}`*
>
> *Actual Input: [job description text here]"*

**Analytical Commentary:** The engineered prompt adds **persona** (recruiter), **explicit output schema** (JSON structure), and **a demonstration** (one-shot). This collapses the entropy of the output distribution and produces a parseable, reliable result suitable for downstream automation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Prompt Flow Architecture

The following block diagram depicts the end-to-end flow of information from the end user through the LLM and back.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[End User Intent] --> B[Prompt Construction]
    B --> C[Add Persona]
    B --> D[Add Instruction]
    B --> E[Add Demonstrations]
    B --> F[Add Input Data]
    B --> G[Add Output Constraints]
    C --> H[Assembled Prompt String]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Tokenization Layer]
    I --> J[LLM Inference Engine]
    J --> K[Decoding Strategy]
    K --> L[Generated Tokens]
    L --> M[Final Response to User]
    M --> N{User Satisfied?}
    N -- No --> O[Prompt Refinement Loop]
    O --> B
    N -- Yes --> P[Task Complete]
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Prompting Techniques

The diagram below visualises the hierarchy of prompting strategies end users may employ.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[End User Prompting Paradigms] --> B[Direct Prompting]
    A --> C[Example Based Prompting]
    A --> D[Reasoning Enhanced Prompting]
    A --> E[Tool Augmented Prompting]
    B --> B1[Zero Shot]
    B --> B2[Instruction Only]
    C --> C1[One Shot]
    C --> C2[Few Shot]
    C --> C3[Role Prompting]
    D --> D1[Chain of Thought]
    D --> D2[Self Consistency]
    D --> D3[Self Refine]
    E --> E1[ReAct Pattern]
    E --> E2[Function Calling]
    E --> E3[Retrieval Augmented]
```

### 4.3 The P.R.I.D.E. Component Diagram

The following schematic illustrates how the five components of the P.R.I.D.E. framework are assembled into a final prompt.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Persona: Who is the AI] --> X[Final Prompt Buffer]
    R[Request: What to do] --> X
    I[Input Data: Subject material] --> X
    D[Demonstrations: Few shot examples] --> X
    E[Expected Format: JSON bullets text] --> X
    X --> Y[Tokenized Sequence]
    Y --> Z[LLM Forward Pass]
```

### 4.4 Iterative Refinement Loop (End-User Workflow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as End User
    participant P as Prompt Builder
    participant L as LLM Service
    participant E as Evaluation Step
    U->>P: Define Goal
    P->>L: Send Initial Prompt
    L-->>E: Raw Output
    E->>U: Quality Score
    alt Score meets threshold
        U-->>U: Accept and Use
    else Score below threshold
        U->>P: Refine Prompt
        P->>L: Send Revised Prompt
        L-->>E: New Output
        E->>U: New Quality Score
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*Define end-user prompting. List any two advantages of using a well-structured prompt when interacting with a Large Language Model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** End-user prompting is the technique of providing natural language instructions, context, and/or examples as input to a pre-trained LLM in order to guide it to produce a desired output, without modifying the model's weights. It exploits the model's in-context learning capability to interpret and execute the task described in the prompt.

**Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
- Reduces ambiguity and improves response accuracy.
- Enables non-technical users to leverage LLMs without coding knowledge.
- Lowers hallucination by constraining format and scope.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**
*Distinguish between zero-shot prompting and few-shot prompting with a suitable example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Zero-Shot | Few-Shot |
|---|---|---|
| Examples provided | None | 2 to 16 examples |
| Token cost | Lowest | Higher |
| Best suited for | Simple, well-known tasks | Novel or format-specific tasks |
| **Example** | *"Classify the sentiment of: 'I love this phone.'"* → Positive | *"Apple → Fruit, Carrot → Vegetable, Rose → ?"* → Flower |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **P.R.I.D.E. framework** for prompt construction. Describe each component with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting** and **ReAct prompting** in detail. Compare their use cases and provide a sample prompt for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (a):**

The **P.R.I.D.E. framework** is a structured approach to designing effective prompts. Each letter represents a critical component:

- **P — Persona:** Assigning an identity to the LLM to prime its domain knowledge.
  - *Example:* "You are a senior Kubernetes administrator with 10 years of experience."

- **R — Request:** A clear, unambiguous task statement.
  - *Example:* "Explain the difference between StatefulSets and Deployments."

- **I — Input Data:** The raw material to be processed.
  - *Example:* The actual YAML manifests or configuration files.

- **D — Demonstrations:** Few-shot examples illustrating the expected transformation.
  - *Example:* "Input: '2+2' → Output: '4'. Input: '5+3' → Output: '8'."

- **E — Expected Output:** Constraints on format, length, or structure.
  - *Example:* "Respond in markdown with a table containing two columns: Feature and Description."

Using all five components produces prompts that are unambiguous, format-compliant, and high-quality. **(7 marks — 1 mark per component + 2 marks for synthesis)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (b):**

**Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting:**
CoT prompting, introduced by Wei et al. (2022), elicits explicit intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer. The model is shown demonstrations that include "thinking out loud" traces. This unlocks emergent reasoning capabilities, particularly for arithmetic, commonsense, and multi-hop logic tasks.

*Sample CoT Prompt:*
> *"Q: Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans of 3 balls each. How many does he have now?*
> *A: Roger started with 5 balls. 2 cans of 3 balls each = 6 balls. 5 + 6 = 11. The answer is 11.*
> *Q: The cafeteria had 23 apples. They used 20 for lunch and bought 6 more. How many now?"*
> *A: Started with 23, used 20 → 3 remain, bought 6 more → 3 + 6 = 9. The answer is 9.*

**ReAct Prompting:**
ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) interleaves three step types: *Thought* (reasoning), *Action* (calling a tool or API), and *Observation* (the result of the action). This enables LLMs to interact with external systems, perform multi-step research, and recover from intermediate errors.

*Sample ReAct Trace:*
> *Thought: I need to find the current weather in Kochi.*
> *Action: search_weather(location="Kochi")*
> *Observation: 28°C, partly cloudy.*
> *Thought: I now have the weather data.*
> *Final Answer: The current temperature in Kochi is 28°C with partly cloudy skies.*

**Comparison:**
- CoT is **internal**, reasoning only within the model's knowledge.
- ReAct is **external**, extending reasoning with tool use and real-time data.
- CoT is preferred for math/logic; ReAct is preferred for agentic workflows. **(7 marks — 3 marks for CoT, 3 marks for ReAct, 1 mark comparison)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024]**

**(a)** Define **In-Context Learning (ICL)**. Derive mathematically how the inclusion of demonstrations reduces the conditional entropy of the LLM's output distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **self-consistency prompting**. How does it differ from greedy decoding? Design a prompt for a math word problem and apply the self-consistency strategy to obtain a robust answer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (a):**

**Definition of ICL (2 marks):** In-Context Learning is the ability of a pre-trained LLM to perform a new task solely by conditioning on a few input-output demonstrations provided inside the prompt, **without any gradient updates or parameter modification**.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider an LLM parameterised by $\theta$. The model defines an autoregressive distribution:

$$
P_{\theta}(y \mid c) \;=\; \prod_{t=1}^{T} P_{\theta}\!\left(y_t \mid y_{<t}, c\right)
$$

In the zero-shot setting, the context is $c^{(0)} = (I, q)$, where $I$ is the instruction and $q$ is the query. The corresponding output entropy is:

$$
H\!\left(Y \mid c^{(0)}\right) \;=\; -\sum_{y} P_{\theta}\!\left(y \mid c^{(0)}\right) \log P_{\theta}\!\left(y \mid c^{(0)}\right)
$$

In the few-shot setting, the context is augmented with $K$ demonstrations:

$$
c^{(K)} = \big(I,\; (x_1, y_1),\; \dots,\; (x_K, y_K),\; q \big)
$$

Since the demonstrations provide statistical evidence about the desired input-output mapping, the model's posterior becomes more peaked, reducing entropy:

$$
H\!\left(Y \mid c^{(K)}\right) \;\leq\; H\!\left(Y \mid c^{(0)}\right)
$$

Equivalently, for any target $y^{\star}$, the cross-entropy loss decreases:

$$
-\log P_{\theta}\!\left(y^{\star} \mid c^{(K)}\right) \;\leq\; -\log P_{\theta}\!\left(y^{\star} \mid c^{(0)}\right)
$$

This is the formal information-theoretic explanation for why few-shot prompting improves task performance.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (b):**

**Self-Consistency Prompting (3 marks):**
Self-Consistency, introduced by Wang et al. (2022), is a decoding strategy that samples **multiple diverse reasoning paths** from the LLM (using a non-zero sampling temperature), and then selects the **most frequent final answer** (majority vote). This exploits the intuition that complex problems have many valid reasoning paths but a single correct answer; aggregating across paths reduces variance and random errors.

**Difference from Greedy Decoding (2 marks):**
- *Greedy decoding* selects the single highest-probability token at each step → one deterministic output.
- *Self-consistency* samples multiple stochastic outputs and aggregates them → higher accuracy at the cost of $N$ times the compute.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**

*Problem:* "A bakery sells cupcakes at Rs. 50 each. In the morning they sold 12, and in the evening they sold 18. How much revenue did they make?"

*Self-Consistency Procedure:*
1. Sample $N = 5$ different CoT completions using temperature $\tau = 0.7$.
2. Each completion may reason slightly differently but should yield the same final number.
3. Final answers: 1500, 1500, 1500, 1500, 1500.
4. **Majority vote → 1500** (the robust answer).

If one path produced 1300 (an arithmetic slip), it is outvoted by the four correct samples, making the final answer robust.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse "prompt engineering" (designing prompts) with "fine-tuning" (updating model weights). Many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing the two.
> - When asked to "list prompting techniques," **always name the technique AND give a one-line example**. Vague lists without examples receive partial credit only.
> - In derivation questions, **every line of math must be justified with a sentence**. Writing equations without explanation is penalised.
> - For Part B sub-parts, students often exceed the word limit by writing generic LLM essays. **Stay focused on the exact sub-question asked.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **End-user prompting** is the natural language interface between humans and LLMs; it does **not** alter model weights.
- The **P.R.I.D.E. framework** (Persona, Request, Input, Demonstrations, Expected format) is the most reliable scaffold for high-quality prompts.
- **Zero-shot** = no examples; **Few-shot** = 2–16 demonstrations; both leverage in-context learning.
- **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** adds explicit intermediate reasoning steps to unlock multi-step inference.
- **ReAct** interleaves *Thought, Action, Observation* for tool use and agentic behaviour.
- **Role prompting** conditions the model on a persona to influence domain vocabulary and tone.
- **Self-consistency** samples multiple outputs and takes a majority vote to reduce variance.
- Prompting is bounded by the **context window**: $T_{\text{prompt}} + T_{\text{response}} \leq C_{\text{window}}$.
- The **information-theoretic basis** of few-shot prompting is the reduction of conditional entropy $H(Y \mid c)$ when demonstrations are added.
- Always specify **output format** explicitly (JSON, bullets, table, word limit) to reduce post-processing.
- For KTU examinations, **define → explain → example** is the safest answer structure for full marks.
- Prompting is a **prerequisite** for RAG, agents, and function-calling in higher modules of PECMT862.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning for LLM Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tuning for LLM Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **LLM Alignment** is the process of steering a pretrained Large Language Model (LLM) to generate outputs that are *helpful, honest, and harmless (HHH)*, while adhering to human intentions, values, preferences, and societal norms. **Alignment Tuning** refers to the parameter optimization procedures applied post-pretraining that modify the model's behavior using curated datasets, reward signals, or preference comparisons.

In the formal KTU 2024 framework, *Tuning for LLM Alignment* encompasses three principal paradigms:

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** — Teacher-forcing the model on instruction-response pairs.
2. **Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)** — Optimizing a policy using a learned reward model trained on human preference rankings.
3. **Preference Optimization without RL** — Methods like **Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)**, **ORPO**, and **KTO** that bypass the explicit reward model.

### 1.2 The Core Alignment Objective

The mathematical purpose of alignment tuning is to solve:

$$
\max_{\pi_{\theta}} \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, \, y \sim \pi_{\theta}(y \mid x)}\big[\,r_{\phi}(x, y)\,\big] - \beta \cdot \mathbb{D}_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\big(\pi_{\theta}(y \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)\big)
$$

where:
- $\pi_{\theta}$ = aligned policy (the LLM we are tuning)
- $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ = reference (frozen SFT) policy
- $r_{\phi}$ = learned reward model
- $\beta$ = KL penalty coefficient (**divergence control hyperparameter**)
- $\mathbb{D}_{\mathrm{KL}}$ = **Kullback–Leibler Divergence**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Training a Sophisticated Service Dog**
>
> Imagine a brilliant but untrained **Alaskan Malamute** (the pretrained LLM). It knows every word in the dictionary and can technically fetch a newspaper, but it might also chew your shoes, bark at guests, or run into traffic.
>
> - **Pretraining** = Teaching the dog general vocabulary and world facts.
> - **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** = Showing the dog 5,000 examples of "good behavior" (sit, stay, roll-over on command).
> - **Reward Model** = A panel of dog-judges scoring the dog's behavior on a 1–10 scale.
> - **RLHF (PPO)** = The dog repeatedly tries behaviors, gets a score, and adjusts its strategy using reinforcement learning to maximize scores.
> - **DPO** = Instead of a judge, the dog directly learns "this trick is BETTER than that trick" from comparative feedback, no scores needed.
>
> The **KL penalty** is like a leash — it prevents the dog from becoming a completely different (potentially dangerous) animal while it learns the new tricks.

### 1.4 Why Alignment is Necessary

| Failure Mode | Pretrained LLM Behavior | After Alignment |
|---|---|---|
| Toxicity | Generates slurs, harmful stereotypes | Refuses or redirects |
| Hallucination | Confidently invents facts | Expresses uncertainty |
| Instruction-following | Ignores user intent | Follows multi-step instructions |
| Bias | Mirrors internet-scale biases | Mitigates demographic bias |
| Refusal calibration | Over- or under-refuses | Calibrated helpfulness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pretraining Objective ≠ User Intent.** Pretraining maximizes next-token likelihood over web data, which optimizes for *plausibility*, not *usefulness*, *truth*, or *safety*. Alignment closes this gap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Alignment Pipeline (InstructGPT Paradigm)

The canonical RLHF pipeline proceeds in three discrete stages:

**Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):**
- Collect demonstrations $(x_i, y_i)$ from human labelers.
- Maximize $\log \pi_{\theta}(y_i \mid x_i)$ via standard cross-entropy.
- Result: $\pi_{\text{SFT}}$, the reference model $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.

**Stage 2 — Reward Modeling:**
- For each prompt $x$, sample $K$ completions $\{y_1, \dots, y_K\}$ from $\pi_{\text{SFT}}$.
- Humans rank them: $y_w \succ y_l$ (winner preferred over loser).
- Train $r_{\phi}(x, y)$ using the **Bradley-Terry pairwise preference model**:

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \frac{\exp\big(r_{\phi}(x, y_w)\big)}{\exp\big(r_{\phi}(x, y_w)\big) + \exp\big(r_{\phi}(x, y_l)\big)} = \sigma\big(r_{\phi}(x, y_w) - r_{\phi}(x, y_l)\big)
$$

- Loss: $\mathcal{L}_{R} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l) \sim \mathcal{D}} \big[\log \sigma(r_{\phi}(x, y_w) - r_{\phi}(x, y_l))\big]$

**Stage 3 — Policy Optimization (PPO):**
- Sample completions from $\pi_{\theta}$.
- Score using $r_{\phi}$ and penalize divergence from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.
- Update $\theta$ via **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}} = -\sum_t \log \pi_{\theta}(y_t \mid x, y_{<t})$ | Cross-entropy on demonstrations | SFT |
| 2 | $P(y_w \succ y_l) = \sigma(r_w - r_l)$ | Bradley-Terry preference | Reward Model |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L}_{R} = -\log \sigma(r_{\phi}(x, y_w) - r_{\phi}(x, y_l))$ | Reward model loss | Reward Model |
| 4 | $\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}(\pi_\theta \mid\mid \pi_{\text{ref}}) = \mathbb{E}_y \big[\log \frac{\pi_\theta(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)}\big]$ | Divergence penalty | All RL methods |
| 5 | $\text{reward} = r_{\phi}(x, y) - \beta \cdot \log \frac{\pi_{\theta}(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)}$ | KL-regularized reward | PPO |
| 6 | $r^{\ast}(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^{\ast}(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)$ | Optimal reward decomposition | DPO derivation |
| 7 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\log \sigma\big(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\big)$ | DPO loss | DPO |
| 8 | $r_{\text{impl}}(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}$ | Implicit reward | DPO |
| 9 | $\text{clip}(r_t, 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \cdot A_t$ | PPO clipped surrogate | PPO |
| 10 | $A_t = \delta_t + (\gamma \lambda) \delta_{t+1} + \dots$ | GAE advantage | PPO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formula 6 — the *optimal reward decomposition* — is the **most important equation in the entire alignment module**. Memorize it. It bridges RLHF and DPO and is asked almost every semester.

### 2.3 Why KL Divergence is the Alignment Anchor

The KL term $\beta \cdot \mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}$ serves three engineering purposes:
1. **Prevents reward hacking** — the policy cannot game the reward model by drifting into degenerate high-reward regions.
2. **Preserves pretraining knowledge** — catastrophic forgetting of factual capability is suppressed.
3. **Stabilizes optimization** — PPO becomes tractable when the policy stays in a neighborhood of $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.

The coefficient $\beta$ trades off:
- **Large $\beta$** → Conservative, high-fidelity to SFT model, low alignment.
- **Small $\beta$** → Aggressive, high reward, high risk of mode-collapse / jailbreaks.

### 2.4 Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) — The Closed-Form Revolution

Rafailov et al. (2023) showed that the RLHF objective has a **closed-form optimal solution** for the policy:

$$
\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right)
$$

Inverting this gives the reward in terms of the policy, which when substituted back into the Bradley-Terry loss yields **Formula 7** in the table. Result: **No reward model, no RL loop, no PPO instability** — just supervised learning on preference pairs.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Alignment Method Used |
|---|---|
| ChatGPT / GPT-4 | SFT + RLHF (PPO) |
| Llama-2-Chat | SFT + RLHF + Iterative DPO |
| Mistral-Instruct | SFT + DPO |
| Anthropic Claude | Constitutional AI (RLAIF) + RLHF |
| Google Gemini | SFT + RLHF + Rejection Sampling |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From RLHF Objective to DPO Loss

We start from the KL-regularized RLHF objective:

$$
\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi(y \mid x)}\big[r(x, y)\big] - \beta \, \mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\big(\pi(\cdot \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot \mid x)\big)
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the KL term explicitly:**

$$
= \max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{x}\mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi}\big[r(x, y)\big] - \beta \, \mathbb{E}_{x}\mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi}\big[\log \tfrac{\pi(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Combine into a single expectation with partition function $Z(x)$:**

$$
= \max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{x}\mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi}\!\left[\,r(x, y) - \beta \log \tfrac{\pi(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}\,\right]
$$

$$
= \max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{x}\mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi}\!\left[\,r(x, y) - \beta \log \pi(y \mid x) + \beta \log \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)\,\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the structure of an exponential-tilting distribution. The optimal $\pi^{\ast}$ satisfies the first-order KKT condition. Apply Lagrange multiplier $\beta \log Z(x)$ to enforce $\sum_y \pi(y \mid x) = 1$:**

$$
r(x, y) - \beta \log \pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) + \beta \log \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) = \beta \log Z(x)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x)$:**

$$
\beta \log \pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) = r(x, y) + \beta \log \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) - \beta \log Z(x)
$$

$$
\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \, \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \, \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \, \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \, \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right)\;}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The partition function $Z(x) = \sum_y \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \exp\!\big(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\big)$ normalizes the distribution over all possible completions. It is intractable in practice but cancels out in the final DPO loss.

**Step 5 — Invert to express the reward in terms of the policy:**

$$
r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the critical step — we have converted an *unobservable* reward into a function of two *observable* policies. The constant $\beta \log Z(x)$ depends only on $x$, so it cancels in pairwise comparisons.

**Step 6 — Substitute into the Bradley-Terry preference loss (Formula 2):**

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma\!\big(r(x, y_w) - r(x, y_l)\big)
$$

$$
r(x, y_w) - r(x, y_l) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^{\ast}(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi^{\ast}(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}
$$

**Step 7 — Final DPO loss (this is what we actually optimize):**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l) \sim \mathcal{D}} \log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)\;}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** DPO requires only: (i) the *current* policy $\pi_\theta$ and (ii) the *frozen reference* $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ — both obtained from a single forward pass. No reward model, no PPO, no value function, no rollouts.

### 3.2 Derivation: PPO Clipped Surrogate Objective

The PPO objective prevents destructively large policy updates:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_t \Big[\min\big(r_t(\theta) A_t,\; \text{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) A_t\big)\Big]
$$

where $r_t(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t \mid s_t)}$ is the probability ratio, and $A_t$ is the GAE advantage estimate.

**Step 1 — The unclipped surrogate $L^{\text{CPI}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[r_t(\theta) A_t]$ allows unbounded updates.**

**Step 2 — The clip operator truncates $r_t$ to $[1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon]$, where $\epsilon \approx 0.2$.**

**Step 3 — The $\min$ of clipped and unclipped makes the objective *pessimistic*: if the update moves the policy outside the trust region *and* the advantage is favorable, we take the clipped (lower) value, killing the gradient.**

**Step 4 — The advantage $A_t$ is estimated via Generalized Advantage Estimation:**

$$
A_t = \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} (\gamma \lambda)^l \, \delta_{t+l}, \quad \delta_t = r_t + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RLHF vs. DPO vs. SFT

| Dimension | SFT | RLHF (PPO) | DPO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Demonstrations | Pairwise preferences | Pairwise preferences |
| **Reward model** | ❌ Not needed | ✅ Required | ❌ Not needed |
| **RL loop** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (rollouts) | ❌ No |
| **Stability** | High | Low (reward hacking) | High |
| **Compute cost** | Low | Very high (4 models in memory) | Low (2 models) |
| **Reward hacking risk** | None | High | Low |
| **Performance ceiling** | Lower | Higher (in theory) | ≈ PPO empirically |
| **Hyperparameter sensitivity** | Low | Very high | Moderate ($\beta$ only) |
| **KTU exam weight** | 2 marks | 7 marks | 5 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The RLHF Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Pretrained LLM] --> B[Stage 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning SFT]
    B --> C[pi SFT Reference Model]
    B --> D[Demo Dataset x y pairs]
    D --> B
    C --> E[Stage 2: Reward Model Training]
    E --> F[Pairwise Preference Dataset y_w beats y_l]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Trained Reward Model r phi]
    C --> H[Stage 3: PPO Policy Optimization]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Rollout Generation]
    I --> J[Score with r phi]
    J --> K[Compute KL Penalty vs pi ref]
    K --> L[PPO Clipped Update]
    L --> M[Aligned Policy pi theta]
    M --> N{Safety Eval}
    N -->|Pass| O[Deploy]
    N -->|Fail| P[Reiterate with RLAIF or Constitutional AI]
```

### 4.2 DPO Architecture: Direct Preference Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prompt x] --> B[Sample y_w and y_l from pi SFT]
    B --> C[Human Ranks: y_w beats y_l]
    C --> D[Preference Dataset]
    D --> E[Stage 1 DPO: Initialize pi theta from pi SFT]
    E --> F[Forward pass y_w through pi theta]
    E --> G[Forward pass y_w through pi ref]
    E --> H[Forward pass y_l through pi theta]
    E --> I[Forward pass y_l through pi ref]
    F --> J[Compute Log Ratios]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Apply beta scaled log sigma]
    K --> L[Backprop through pi theta only]
    L --> M[Aligned pi theta No Reward Model No RL Loop]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: SFT vs RLHF vs DPO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SFT_BLOCK["SFT Path"]
        S1[Prompt + Demo y] --> S2[Cross Entropy Loss] --> S3[Updated pi theta]
    end
    subgraph RLHF_BLOCK["RLHF PPO Path"]
        R1[Prompt] --> R2[Rollout y from pi theta]
        R2 --> R3[Score r phi]
        R3 --> R4[KL Penalty vs pi ref]
        R4 --> R5[GAE Advantage A_t]
        R5 --> R6[PPO Clipped Surrogate]
        R6 --> R7[Updated pi theta]
    end
    subgraph DPO_BLOCK["DPO Path"]
        D1[Prompt + y_w + y_l] --> D2[Compute 4 log probs]
        D2 --> D3[Log sigma of beta scaled difference]
        D3 --> D4[Updated pi theta]
    end
```

### 4.4 Reward Hacking Failure Mode (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Poorly Calibrated Reward Model] --> B[Policy Discovers High r phi Region]
    B --> C[KL Penalty Insufficient beta too small]
    C --> D[Policy Drift to Degenerate Behavior]
    D --> E1[Mode Collapse repeating tokens]
    D --> E2[Reward Model Exploit fixed phrases]
    D --> E3[Loss of Fluency gibberish]
    E1 --> F[Alignment Failure]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block flow shows why the KL coefficient $\beta$ is the single most important hyperparameter in RLHF. Setting $\beta$ too small allows *reward hacking* — the policy moves far from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ into regions where $r_{\phi}$ is spuriously high but actual generation quality collapses.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define LLM alignment. List any three properties an aligned LLM should satisfy.*

**Model Answer:**
LLM alignment is the process of steering a pretrained LLM to produce outputs consistent with human intentions, values, and intentions, typically involving instruction-following, safety, and truthfulness.
An aligned LLM should satisfy the **3H properties**:
1. **Helpful** — efficiently assists the user in completing the task.
2. **Honest** — avoids fabricating information and expresses calibrated uncertainty.
3. **Harmless** — refuses or redirects prompts that could lead to physical, psychological, or societal harm.

> **[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for 3H = 0.66×3 ≈ 2 marks + 1 mark]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the role of the KL-divergence term in the RLHF objective function?*

**Model Answer:**
The KL-divergence term $\beta \cdot \mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}(\pi_\theta \,\|\, \pi_{\text{ref}})$ acts as a *regularizer* that penalizes the aligned policy $\pi_\theta$ for drifting too far from the SFT reference model $\pi_{\text{ref}}$. It serves three roles:
1. **Prevents reward hacking** — stops the policy from exploiting weaknesses in the learned reward model.
2. **Preserves pretraining knowledge** — mitigates catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities.
3. **Stabilizes PPO optimization** — keeps policy updates within a tractable trust region.

> **[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark for the role statement + 2 marks for the three sub-points]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the three stages of the RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) pipeline used in aligning large language models. Describe the role of each stage with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) loss starting from the KL-regularized RLHF objective, showing the step where the partition function $Z(x)$ cancels out. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Stages of RLHF — 7 marks**

**Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):**
- Human annotators write high-quality demonstrations $(x_i, y_i)$ for diverse prompts.
- The pretrained LLM is fine-tuned via standard cross-entropy:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}}(\theta) = -\sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D}} \log \pi_\theta(y_i \mid x_i)
$$

- Result: $\pi_{\text{SFT}} \equiv \pi_{\text{ref}}$, the reference policy for subsequent stages.

**Stage 2 — Reward Modeling:**
- For each prompt $x$, $K$ completions are sampled from $\pi_{\text{SFT}}$.
- Human labelers rank them producing pairs $(y_w, y_l)$ with $y_w \succ y_l$.
- A reward model $r_\phi(x, y)$ is trained using the Bradley-Terry model:

$$
\mathcal{L}_R(\phi) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)} \log \sigma\big(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)\big)
$$

- Typically $r_\phi$ is initialized from $\pi_{\text{SFT}}$ with a scalar value head.

**Stage 3 — Policy Optimization with PPO:**
- The policy $\pi_\theta$ is optimized to maximize the KL-regularized reward:

$$
\max_\theta \; \mathbb{E}_{x, y \sim \pi_\theta}\big[r_\phi(x, y)\big] - \beta \, \mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\big(\pi_\theta \,\|\, \pi_{\text{ref}}\big)
$$

- PPO's clipped surrogate objective ensures stable updates:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_t \min\big(r_t(\theta) A_t,\; \text{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) A_t\big)
$$

- Four models held in memory: policy $\pi_\theta$, reference $\pi_{\text{ref}}$, reward $r_\phi$, value $V$.

> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks Stage 1 + 2 marks Stage 2 + 3 marks Stage 3]**

**(b) Derivation of DPO Loss — 7 marks**

Starting from:

$$
\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{x, y \sim \pi}\big[r(x,y)\big] - \beta \mathbb{E}_{x, y \sim \pi}\big[\log \tfrac{\pi(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}\big]
$$

Introduce Lagrange multiplier $\beta \log Z(x)$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$
r(x, y) - \beta \log \pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) + \beta \log \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) = \beta \log Z(x)
$$

Solving:

$$
\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right)
$$

Invert for reward:

$$
r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
$$

Substituting into Bradley-Terry:

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l) = \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)
$$

The $Z(x)$ term cancels because it appears identically in both $r(x, y_w)$ and $r(x, y_l)$ and subtracts to zero.

Final loss:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)\;}
$$

> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks setup + 2 marks solving for π* + 2 marks inversion + 1 mark final boxed loss]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) forgetting to state that $Z(x)$ is the partition function that *normalizes* the distribution, or (b) omitting the cancellation step of $\log Z(x)$ in the pairwise comparison. **Always explicitly write the cancellation** — examiners award marks for that single line.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare and contrast RLHF (with PPO) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for LLM alignment, with respect to: data requirements, computational cost, stability, and the role of the reward model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of *reward hacking* in the context of RLHF. Discuss two mitigation strategies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison: RLHF vs DPO — 7 marks**

| Dimension | RLHF (PPO) | DPO |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Requires demonstrations (SFT) + pairwise preferences (reward model) + rollouts (PPO) | Only pairwise preferences after SFT |
| **Reward Model** | Explicit, separately trained neural network $r_\phi$ | Implicit, encoded in the log-ratio $\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta}{\pi_{\text{ref}}}$ |
| **Computation** | High: 4 models in GPU memory ($\pi_\theta, \pi_{\text{ref}}, r_\phi, V$) + rollouts | Low: 2 models ($\pi_\theta, \pi_{\text{ref}}$), 4 forward passes per step |
| **Stability** | Low — sensitive to reward model errors, KL coefficient, learning rate | High — supervised loss landscape, no bootstrapping |
| **Reward hacking risk** | High — policy can exploit $r_\phi$ | Low — no explicit reward function to exploit |
| **Implementation** | Complex (RL infrastructure, value heads, GAE) | Simple (standard PyTorch training loop) |
| **Empirical quality** | Slight edge in long-horizon reasoning | Comparable or better in chat benchmarks |

> **[7 marks breakdown: 1 mark intro + 6 marks table content — examiners pick 4–5 rows for credit]**

**(b) Reward Hacking — 7 marks**

**Definition:** *Reward hacking* (also called *reward tampering* or *specification gaming*) occurs when the policy $\pi_\theta$ discovers a high-reward region in the learned reward model $r_\phi$ that does **not** correspond to genuinely good outputs.

**Symptoms:**
- Mode collapse (repeating high-scoring tokens).
- Excessive use of flattering phrases the annotators unconsciously rewarded.
- Loss of factual accuracy despite high reward scores.
- "Sycophancy" — agreeing with the user even when wrong.

**Mitigation Strategy 1 — Strong KL Regularization (β-tuning):**
Increase $\beta$ in the regularizer to restrict how far $\pi_\theta$ can drift from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$. This caps the policy in a neighborhood where $r_\phi$ is well-calibrated.

$$
\text{Reward}_{\text{eff}} = r_\phi(x, y) - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}
$$

**Mitigation Strategy 2 — Reward Model Ensembling and Uncertainty:**
Train an *ensemble* of $K$ reward models $\{r_{\phi_1}, \dots, r_{\phi_K}\}$ and use the *minimum* (or a pessimistic percentile) as the effective reward. This prevents exploiting the idiosyncrasies of any single model.

**Mitigation Strategy 3 — Iterative RLHF (Llama-2 style):**
Re-collect preference data using the current aligned policy $\pi_{\theta}^{(t)}$ as the sampling model, then retrain the reward model. This moves the data distribution closer to $\pi_\theta$, reducing out-of-distribution reward exploitation.

> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks definition + symptoms + 5 marks across 2–3 mitigation strategies]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When asked about reward hacking, students often describe it but *fail to give concrete symptoms* (mode collapse, sycophancy). Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for the symptom description specifically. Also avoid generic answers like "use better data" — name the *technical* mechanism (KL, ensembling, iterative sampling).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tuning for LLM Alignment**

- **Alignment** bridges the gap between *pretraining likelihood* and *user intent* (the 3H: Helpful, Honest, Harmless).
- **RLHF** is a *three-stage pipeline*: SFT → Reward Modeling → PPO.
- **SFT** uses standard cross-entropy on human-written demonstrations: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum \log \pi_\theta(y \mid x)$.
- **Bradley-Terry model** defines preference probability: $P(y_w \succ y_l) = \sigma(r_w - r_l)$.
- **Reward Model loss**: $\mathcal{L}_R = -\log \sigma(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l))$.
- **RLHF objective** is a KL-regularized expected reward maximization.
- **PPO clipped surrogate** with $\epsilon \approx 0.2$ prevents destructive updates.
- **The KL penalty** has coefficient $\beta$ that trades alignment strength vs. fidelity to $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.
- **DPO** = closed-form solution of the RLHF objective; no reward model, no RL loop.
- **Optimal policy form** (THE equation): $\pi^{\ast}(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x) \exp(r(x,y)/\beta)$.
- **Implicit reward** in DPO: $r_{\text{impl}}(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)}$.
- **Reward hacking** is the central failure mode of RLHF; mitigated by KL control, ensembles, and iterative data collection.
- **Constitutional AI (RLAIF)** replaces human preferences with AI-generated critiques based on a written "constitution."
- **Examination focus**: KTU 2024 weights DPO derivation at ~5 marks, RLHF pipeline at ~5–7 marks, and KL-reward comparison at ~3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Reinforcement Learning Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Reinforcement Learning Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL) Framework Definition**
> The Reinforcement Learning Framework is a computational paradigm in which an **autonomous agent** learns an optimal **policy** (behavioural strategy) by **interacting with a dynamic environment** through a sequential decision-making process. At each discrete time step, the agent observes the current **state**, selects an **action** from a permissible set, transitions to a new **state** governed by the environment's transition dynamics, and receives a scalar **reward** signal. The agent's objective is to maximize the **expected cumulative discounted reward** (the *return*) over the long run.

Formally, the RL framework is mathematically grounded in the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, which is the canonical tuple:

$$
\mathcal{M} \;=\; \langle \, \mathcal{S}, \; \mathcal{A}, \; P, \; R, \; \gamma \, \rangle
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ is the finite (or infinite) set of **states**.
- $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of **actions** available to the agent.
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ is the **state transition probability function**.
- $R(s, a, s')$ is the **reward function** (sometimes written as $R(s, a)$).
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor**, controlling the present value of future rewards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Training a Pet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Teaching a Dog New Tricks**
> Think of the RL agent as a **puppy** and the environment as a **living room**. Every time the puppy sits on command, you give it a **biscuit (reward)**. Every time it chews your slippers, you say "No!" (a **negative reward / penalty**). The puppy doesn't know the rules in advance; it learns by *trial and error* which behaviours earn biscuits. Over time, the puppy builds an internal **mental map (policy)** — "if I hear 'sit', I should sit, because sitting in the past gave me treats."
>
> | RL Concept | Puppy Analogy |
> |---|---|
> | Agent | The puppy |
> | Environment | The house (you + the room) |
> | State | Where the puppy is and what it sees/hears |
> | Action | Sit, bark, run, chew |
> | Reward | Biscuit (positive) or scolding (negative) |
> | Policy | The puppy's internal decision rule |
> | Return | Total biscuits earned over the afternoon |

This analogy makes one crucial point clear: **the agent is never told *what* to do — it must discover the optimal behaviour by exploring actions and observing the consequences.**

## 1.3 The Three Distinctive Pillars of RL

Unlike **supervised learning** (which needs labelled input–output pairs) and **unsupervised learning** (which finds hidden structure in unlabelled data), RL is built on three unique pillars:

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Trial-and-Error Search:** The agent must probe different actions to discover which ones yield high rewards.
>
> **Pillar 2 — Delayed Reward:** A current action may influence a reward many time-steps later (e.g., a chess move that wins the game after 40 plies).
>
> **Pillar 3 — Environmental Non-Stationarity:** The environment's response is stochastic and may depend on the agent's prior actions, so past data is *not* i.i.d.

## 1.4 Core Terminology — KTU One-Liner Bank

- **Policy ($\pi$):** A mapping from states to a probability distribution over actions, $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$.
- **Return ($G_t$):** The cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ onward, $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- **State-Value Function ($V_\pi(s)$):** Expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$.
- **Action-Value Function ($Q_\pi(s, a)$):** Expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$, then following $\pi$.
- **Model of the Environment:** Any representation of transition probabilities $P$ and rewards $R$ — used in *model-based* RL.
- **Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off:** The fundamental dilemma of choosing between trying new actions (exploration) and repeating known good actions (exploitation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The course explicitly distinguishes between the *prediction* problem (evaluate a given policy) and the *control* problem (find the optimal policy). These are answered by *policy evaluation* and *policy improvement* respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
