# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

The RL framework operates as a **closed feedback loop** that repeats at every discrete time step $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots, T$. The sequence unfolds as follows:

1. **Observation:** The agent perceives the current environment state $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. **Action Selection:** Using its current policy $\pi$, the agent samples an action $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$.
3. **Environment Response:** The environment, governed by $P$ and $R$, produces:
   - The next state $S_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$.
   - A scalar reward $R_{t+1} = R(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$.
4. **Learning Update:** The agent updates its internal estimates (e.g., $V$, $Q$, or $\pi$) using the tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$.
5. **Increment:** $t \leftarrow t + 1$, and the loop continues until a terminal state or a maximum horizon is reached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this called a "framework"?** Because the *same* loop underlies vastly different problems — from training a robotic arm to grasp objects, to fine-tuning a Large Language Model with human feedback (RLHF), to game-playing agents like AlphaGo.

## 2.2 The Markov Property — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markov Property Definition**
> A state $S_t$ is said to possess the **Markov property** if and only if the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present:
> $$P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a) \;=\; P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_0, A_0, S_1, A_1, \dots, S_t, A_t)$$
> In other words, $S_t$ is a **sufficient statistic** of all history. This single assumption is what makes the MDP tractable — it allows the entire trajectory to be summarized by the current state.

**Why does it matter for LLM context?** In modern RLHF for large language models, the "state" is typically the entire prompt-and-response-so-far (i.e., the context window). Whether that state is "Markovian" depends on the architecture — modern transformer-based policies can act on it as if it were.

## 2.3 Discount Factor ($\gamma$) — The Time-Value of Reward

The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ answers the question: *"How much is a future reward worth today?"*

- If $\gamma = 0$ → the agent is **myopic** (greedy), caring only about immediate reward.
- If $\gamma \to 1$ → the agent is **far-sighted**, valuing future rewards almost as much as immediate ones.
- $\gamma$ also **guarantees mathematical convergence** of the infinite-horizon return, since the geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k$ converges to $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ when $\vert \gamma \vert < 1$.

## 2.4 The Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma

> [!NOTE]
> **The Famous k-Armed Bandit Intuition**
> Imagine you walk into a casino with $k$ slot machines, each with an unknown payout distribution. You have $N$ pulls. **Exploitation** = keep pulling the arm that has paid you best so far. **Exploration** = occasionally try a less-played arm, because it *might* be even better. RL generalises this dilemma to a **sequential** setting where actions affect future states.

Common strategies to balance this trade-off:

| Strategy | Mechanism | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon$-greedy | With probability $\varepsilon$ pick a random action; otherwise pick the best-known one | High — frequently examined |
| Softmax / Boltzmann | Sample action $a$ with probability $\frac{\exp(Q(s,a)/\tau)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(Q(s,a')/\tau)}$ | Moderate |
| UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) | Optimism in the face of uncertainty; adds an exploration bonus | Moderate |
| Thompson Sampling | Sample from posterior over action-values, then act greedily | Moderate |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-memorize equations** for the RL framework. Every KTU 2024 question on this module will be derivable from these.

| # | Name | Equation | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Return (cumulative discounted reward) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \, R_{t+k+1}$ | Defines the agent's objective |
| 2 | State-Value Function | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | "How good is state $s$ under $\pi$?" |
| 3 | Action-Value Function | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | "How good is taking action $a$ in $s$ under $\pi$?" |
| 4 | Bellman Expectation Equation for $V$ | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma \, V_\pi(s') \big]$ | Recursive decomposition |
| 5 | Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q$ | $Q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q_\pi(s', a') \big]$ | Q-form of recursion |
| 6 | Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$ | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma \, V^*(s') \big]$ | Defines optimal value |
| 7 | Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*$ | $Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \big]$ | Foundation of Q-learning |
| 8 | Policy Improvement Theorem | $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q_\pi(s, a)$ | Greedy update guaranteed $\geq \pi$ |
| 9 | Optimal Policy from $Q^*$ | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^*(s, a)$ | Always deterministic at the optimum |
| 10 | TD(0) Update Rule | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \big]$ | Bootstrapping estimate |
| 11 | Q-Learning Update Rule | $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \big]$ | Off-policy, model-free |
| 12 | Discounted Geometric Sum | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k = \frac{1}{1 - \gamma}$ for $\vert \gamma \vert < 1$ | Convergence guarantee |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Distinction (KTU Examiners Test This):**
> - $V_\pi$ is *on-policy* — it depends on the policy $\pi$ being followed.
> - $V^*$ is the *optimal* value, unique, and is the **fixed point** of the Bellman optimality operator.
> - The Bellman **expectation** equation uses the *actual* policy's action distribution; the Bellman **optimality** equation uses a $\max$ operator.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** The agent learns joint-torque policies to walk or grasp.
- **LLM Fine-Tuning (RLHF):** A reward model (often a human-preference classifier) provides the scalar reward, and PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) updates the LLM's weights.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Lane-keeping policies are trained in simulation.
- **Resource Management:** Google's DeepMind used RL to cool data-centre fans, reducing energy usage by **~40%**.
- **Game AI:** Atari, Go, StarCraft II, Dota 2 — all state-of-the-art agents are RL-based.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in the Era of LLMs**
> The Transformer-RL hybrid is the heart of modern alignment: ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are all fine-tuned using a variant of RL (typically PPO or DPO) on top of human feedback. Understanding the underlying framework is *essential* for any LLM engineer.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the *exhaustive* derivations of the most important equations in the RL framework, since KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to "show that…" or "derive the Bellman equation."

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Return $G_t$ in Terms of $G_{t+1}$

We begin from the *definition* of the return and apply the recursive structure of the trajectory.

**Step 1 — Write the definition expanded:**

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma R_{t+2} \;+\; \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} \;+\; \gamma^{3} R_{t+4} \;+\; \cdots
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $\gamma$ from the second term onwards:**

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma \big[ R_{t+2} \;+\; \gamma R_{t+3} \;+\; \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} \;+\; \cdots \big]
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the bracketed expression as $G_{t+1}$:**

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} \;+\; \gamma \, G_{t+1}
$$

**Conclusion:** This *recursive* form, $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$, is the workhorse identity used in nearly every RL proof.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi(s)$

**Starting Point:** The definition of the state-value function is

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_t \;\big|\; S_t = s \,\big]
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the recursive form of $G_t$:**

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \;\big|\; S_t = s \,\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Use the linearity of expectation, $\mathbb{E}[X + Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$:**

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, R_{t+1} \;\big|\; S_t = s \,\big] \;+\; \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_{t+1} \;\big|\; S_t = s \,\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Condition on the action and the next state.** The agent picks $A_t$ according to $\pi(\cdot \mid s)$ and the environment transitions to $S_{t+1}$:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}} p(s', r \mid s, a) \Big[\, r \;+\; \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \,\big] \,\Big]
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the inner expectation as $V_\pi(s')$ by definition:**

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \Big[\, r \;+\; \gamma \, V_\pi(s') \,\Big]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi$**. It expresses the value of a state as the *one-step* reward plus the discounted value of the successor state, averaged over the stochasticity of the policy and the environment.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q_\pi(s, a)$

**Starting Point:**

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_t \;\big|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\big]
$$

**Step 1 — Apply $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$:**

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \;\big|\; S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Expand over the next state $s'$ and reward $r$:**

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \Big[\, r \;+\; \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \,\big] \,\Big]
$$

**Step 3 — Note that after reaching $s'$, the agent's actions are now drawn from $\pi(\cdot \mid s')$. Expand over $a'$:**

$$
\mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \,\big] \;=\; \sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q_\pi(s', a')
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \Big[\, r \;+\; \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q_\pi(s', a') \,\Big]
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: From Bellman Expectation to Bellman Optimality

**Step 1 — Recall the optimal state-value function:**

$$
V^*(s) \;=\; \max_{\pi} V_\pi(s) \quad \forall \, s \in \mathcal{S}
$$

**Step 2 — A useful identity (relationship between $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$):**

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

This follows directly by the law of total expectation: the value of a state equals the expected action-value over the policy's action distribution.

**Step 3 — At the optimum, the optimal policy is the **greedy** policy with respect to $Q^*$:**

$$
\pi^*(a \mid s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q^*(s, a') \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute this degenerate distribution (one-hot at the best action) into the $V_\pi$–$Q_\pi$ relationship:**

$$
V^*(s) \;=\; \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q^*(s, a)
$$

**Step 5 — Use the Bellman expectation form of $Q^*$ and replace the inner sum by a max:**

$$
Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \Big[\, r \;+\; \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\Big]
$$

**Step 6 — Plug this back into Step 4 to get the Bellman optimality equation for $V^*$:**

$$
V^*(s) \;=\; \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \Big[\, r \;+\; \gamma \, V^*(s') \,\Big]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Optimality" vs "Expectation"?** The expectation equation uses the *given* policy's action probabilities. The optimality equation uses a $\max$ because the *best* policy will deterministically pick the best action in every state it visits.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: The Policy Improvement Theorem (Sketch)

**Step 1 — Define a new policy $\pi'$ that is greedy with respect to $Q_\pi$:**

$$
\pi'(s) \;=\; \arg\max_{a} Q_\pi(s, a) \quad \forall \, s
$$

**Step 2 — By the definition of $Q_\pi$, when we take the action $\pi'(s)$ in state $s$, we obtain an expected return of at least that of the original policy's action distribution:**

$$
Q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \;\geq\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; V_\pi(s)
$$

The inequality holds because $\pi'(s)$ is the argmax, so its $Q$-value is at least the average under $\pi$.

**Step 3 — Iterating this argument along the trajectory proves (by the chain of inequalities):**

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;\geq\; V_\pi(s) \quad \forall \, s
$$

**Conclusion:** Acting greedily with respect to $Q_\pi$ produces a policy that is *no worse* than $\pi$. Applying this iteratively yields the **policy iteration** algorithm, which is provably convergent to $\pi^*$.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — On-Policy vs Off-Policy

| Feature | On-Policy (e.g., SARSA) | Off-Policy (e.g., Q-Learning) |
|---|---|---|
| Behaviour policy | Same as target policy | Different (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy behaviour, greedy target) |
| Update uses | Action *actually taken* in $S_{t+1}$ | $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$ |
| Convergence guarantee | Yes (under GLIE + tabular) | Yes (under Robbins-Monro + tabular) |
| Exploration built-in | Via the same stochastic policy | Behaviour policy explores, target exploits |
| Variance | Lower (uses real samples) | Higher (uses the *max*, which is biased but low-variance) |
| Typical use case | Real-time control, robotics | Batch learning from replay buffer |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — A 3-State MDP

> [!NOTE]
> **Toy MDP Setup** (frequently appears in KTU problems)
>
> - States: $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ where $s_3$ is terminal.
> - From $s_1$, action $a$ leads to $s_2$ with reward $+1$.
> - From $s_1$, action $b$ leads to $s_2$ with reward $+0$.
> - From $s_2$, action $a$ leads to $s_3$ with reward $+10$.
> - From $s_2$, action $b$ leads to $s_1$ with reward $-1$.
> - $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Question:** Compute $V_\pi(s_1)$ and $V_\pi(s_2)$ for the equiprobable policy $\pi(a \mid s) = \pi(b \mid s) = 0.5$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the Bellman expectation equations.**

For $s_1$:

$$
V_\pi(s_1) = 0.5 \big[ 1 + 0.9 \, V_\pi(s_2) \big] + 0.5 \big[ 0 + 0.9 \, V_\pi(s_2) \big] = 0.5 + 0.9 \, V_\pi(s_2)
$$

For $s_2$:

$$
V_\pi(s_2) = 0.5 \big[ 10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 \big] + 0.5 \big[ -1 + 0.9 \, V_\pi(s_1) \big] = 4.5 - 0.5 + 0.45 \, V_\pi(s_1) = 4.0 + 0.45 \, V_\pi(s_1)
$$

(Note: $V_\pi(s_3) = 0$ since it is terminal.)

**Step 2 — Solve the linear system.**

From the first equation: $V_\pi(s_1) = 0.5 + 0.9 \, V_\pi(s_2)$. Substitute into the second:

$$
V_\pi(s_2) = 4.0 + 0.45 \big( 0.5 + 0.9 \, V_\pi(s_2) \big)
$$

$$
V_\pi(s_2) = 4.0 + 0.225 + 0.405 \, V_\pi(s_2)
$$

$$
0.595 \, V_\pi(s_2) = 4.225 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_\pi(s_2) \approx 7.10
$$

**Step 3 — Back-substitute:**

$$
V_\pi(s_1) = 0.5 + 0.9 \times 7.10 \approx 6.89
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_\pi(s_1) \approx 6.89$, $V_\pi(s_2) \approx 7.10$, $V_\pi(s_3) = 0$.

This example is a **standard KTU-style computation** and tests the student's ability to translate the framework's equations into numerical solutions.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Core Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation guidance:** Read the loop in the clockwise direction starting from the top. At every time-step $t$, the agent and the environment exchange a state, an action, and a reward.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AGENT<br/>Policy pi and Value V"]
    B["ENVIRONMENT<br/>Transition P and Reward R"]
    C["At time t<br/>Agent observes state S_t"]
    D["Agent selects action<br/>A_t from pi"]
    E["Environment transitions<br/>to new state S_t1"]
    F["Environment emits<br/>scalar reward R_t1"]
    G["Agent updates<br/>policy or value estimates"]
    H["Time increments<br/>t becomes t+1"]
    I["Loop continues<br/>until terminal state or horizon T"]

    A -- "1. State observation S_t" --> C
    C --> D
    D -- "2. Action A_t" --> B
    B --> E
    B --> F
    E -- "3. New state S_t1" --> A
    F -- "4. Reward R_t1" --> A
    A --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -. "next iteration" .-> C
```

**How to read this:** The arrows leaving the *AGENT* node are *action* signals; the arrows returning to the *AGENT* node are *state* and *reward* signals. This two-way coupling is what makes RL fundamentally different from supervised learning (which has a one-way data flow).

## 4.2 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an RL System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PerceptionLayer["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        S1["State Encoder<br/>f_obs : O to S"]
    end

    subgraph DecisionLayer["DECISION LAYER"]
        P1["Policy Network<br/>pi_theta of S to A"]
        V1["Value Network<br/>V_phi of S to R"]
        Q1["Q-Network<br/>Q_psi of S x A to R"]
    end

    subgraph LearningLayer["LEARNING LAYER"]
        L1["Replay Buffer<br/>D of past transitions"]
        L2["Loss Computation<br/>TD error or policy gradient"]
        L3["Optimizer<br/>SGD or Adam update"]
    end

    subgraph EnvironmentLayer["ENVIRONMENT LAYER"]
        E1["World Model<br/>P and R functions"]
        E2["Interaction Interface<br/>apply action read state reward"]
    end

    S1 --> P1
    S1 --> V1
    S1 --> Q1
    P1 --> E2
    E2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> S1
    E1 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    V1 --> L2
    Q1 --> L2
    P1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> P1
    L3 --> V1
    L3 --> Q1
```

**Block-Level Reading Guide:**

| Block | Function | Maps to KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| PerceptionLayer | Transforms raw observations $\mathcal{O}$ into Markovian states $\mathcal{S}$ | State representation |
| DecisionLayer (Policy) | Decides which action to take | Policy $\pi_\theta$ |
| DecisionLayer (Value) | Estimates expected return | $V_\phi$ or $Q_\psi$ |
| LearningLayer (Buffer) | Stores $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ tuples | Experience replay |
| LearningLayer (Loss) | Computes TD error or policy gradient | Update target |
| EnvironmentLayer | Simulates or interfaces with the real world | MDP |

## 4.3 RL Framework Categorisation Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["Reinforcement Learning Framework"]
    R1["Model Free<br/>No knowledge of P or R"]
    R2["Model Based<br/>Learns or is given P and R"]

    Root --> R1
    Root --> R2

    R1 --> R1a["Value Based<br/>Learn V or Q"]
    R1 --> R1b["Policy Based<br/>Learn pi directly"]
    R1 --> R1c["Actor Critic<br/>Combine both"]

    R2 --> R2a["Pure Planning<br/>Use model to plan"]
    R2 --> R2b["Model Augmented<br/>Real samples + model rollouts"]

    R1a --> R1a1["Q Learning"]
    R1a --> R1a2["SARSA"]
    R1a --> R1a3["Deep Q Network DQN"]
    R1b --> R1b1["REINFORCE"]
    R1b --> R1b2["Trust Region Policy Optimization"]
    R1b --> R1b3["Proximal Policy Optimization PPO"]
    R1c --> R1c1["Advantage Actor Critic A2C"]
    R1c --> R1c2["Soft Actor Critic SAC"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this tree in an exam:** When a question asks "Classify PPO within the RL framework," the correct chain is *RL → Model-Free → Policy-Based → PPO*. This is a frequently asked KTU sub-question.

## 4.4 Trajectory Visualisation — Rollout Over Time

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ag as Agent
    participant En as Environment
    participant Tb as Time Index

    Tb->>Ag: t = 0
    Ag->>En: A_0
    En-->>Ag: R_1, S_1
    Tb->>Ag: t = 1
    Ag->>En: A_1
    En-->>Ag: R_2, S_2
    Tb->>Ag: t = 2
    Ag->>En: A_2
    En-->>Ag: R_3, S_3
    Tb->>Ag: t = 3 (terminal)
```

**Interpretation:** This sequence diagram represents one *episode* (or *trajectory*) of length $T = 3$. The total return is $G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^2 R_3$.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Reinforcement Learning Framework. List its four primary components. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework is a goal-directed computational paradigm in which an autonomous **agent** learns an optimal **policy** by **interacting with a dynamic environment** through trial-and-error and receiving scalar **rewards** as feedback.

The four primary components are:

1. **Policy ($\pi$):** The agent's behavioural rule mapping states to actions.
2. **Reward Signal ($R$):** The scalar feedback indicating how well the agent is doing at each step.
3. **Value Function ($V$ or $Q$):** The expected long-term return, used to evaluate states or state-action pairs.
4. **Model of the Environment (optional):** An internal representation of transition dynamics $P$ and rewards $R$ — present in *model-based* RL, absent in *model-free* RL.

*(3 marks — full credit requires all four components explicitly named and briefly explained.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between *exploration* and *exploitation* in reinforcement learning. Why is this trade-off fundamental? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Exploration:** Trying out novel or randomly chosen actions to discover their consequences. The agent "explores" the action space to gather information.
- **Exploitation:** Choosing the action that, based on current knowledge, yields the highest expected reward. The agent "exploits" what it has already learned.

**Why fundamental:** A purely exploitative agent will be stuck in a locally optimal but globally suboptimal behaviour (it will never discover the truly best action). A purely exploratory agent will never settle on a good policy and will continue to act randomly. The dilemma is fundamental because *every* action the agent takes is, implicitly, a balance between these two objectives. Common resolution strategies include $\varepsilon$-greedy, softmax/Boltzmann, UCB, and Thompson sampling.

*(3 marks — awarding scheme: 1 mark for each correct definition + 1 mark for explaining why the trade-off is fundamental.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Note**
> Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks**, split as 7 marks for sub-part (a) and 7 marks for sub-part (b). Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation and Use It to Compute the Optimal Policy

> **\[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024\]**

#### Part (a) — Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*(s)$ from First Principles. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State the goal:** We want a recursive expression for $V^*(s) = \max_\pi V_\pi(s)$.

**Step 2 — Recall the Bellman expectation equation for $V_\pi(s)$ (already proven in Section 3.2):**

$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \big]
$$

**Step 3 — Note the identity linking $V$ and $Q$:**

$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

**Step 4 — At the optimum, the policy is greedy:**

$$
\pi^*(a \mid s) = \mathbf{1}\big[ a = \arg\max_{a'} Q^*(s, a') \big]
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting this one-hot distribution into the $V$–$Q$ relationship yields:**

$$
V^*(s) = \max_{a} Q^*(s, a)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Bellman expectation form of $Q^*(s, a)$:**

$$
Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \big]
$$

**Step 7 — Combine Steps 5 and 6:**

$$
\boxed{\, V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \big] \,}
$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the state-value function.

#### Part (b) — Consider the following 2-state MDP and compute the optimal policy using the Bellman Optimality Equation. **(7 marks)**

> **MDP Specification:**
> - States: $\{s_1, s_2\}$, $s_2$ terminal.
> - Actions in $s_1$: $\{a, b\}$.
> - Transitions:
>   - $a$: $s_1 \to s_2$ with reward $+5$, probability $1.0$.
>   - $b$: $s_1 \to s_1$ with reward $+1$, probability $1.0$.
> - $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Terminal-state boundary condition:** $V^*(s_2) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Write the Bellman optimality equation for $V^*(s_1)$:**

$$
V^*(s_1) = \max_a \big[ R(s_1, a) + \gamma \cdot V^*(s_1') \big]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the value of each action in $s_1$:**

$$
Q^*(s_1, a) = 5 + 0.9 \cdot V^*(s_2) = 5 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 5
$$

$$
Q^*(s_1, b) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot V^*(s_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Set up the Bellman equation:**

$$
V^*(s_1) = \max \{ 5,\; 1 + 0.9 \, V^*(s_1) \}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the two candidate cases:**

- If $\pi^*(s_1) = a$: $V^*(s_1) = 5$.
- If $\pi^*(s_1) = b$: $V^*(s_1) = 1 + 0.9 \, V^*(s_1) \Rightarrow 0.1 \, V^*(s_1) = 1 \Rightarrow V^*(s_1) = 10$.

Since $10 > 5$, the max picks $b$.

**Step 6 — Conclude:**

$$
\boxed{\, V^*(s_1) = 10, \quad \pi^*(s_1) = b, \quad V^*(s_2) = 0 \,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although action $a$ gives a larger *immediate* reward ($+5$ vs $+1$), action $b$ keeps the agent in a non-terminal state where it can keep collecting reward indefinitely. With $\gamma < 1$, the geometric series of $b$'s rewards converges to $10 > 5$, so the optimal policy is to *never* terminate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Model-Based vs Model-Free RL, with Focus on Q-Learning

> **\[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023\]**

#### Part (a) — Compare Model-Based and Model-Free RL Frameworks with Examples. **(7 marks)**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Model-Based RL | Model-Free RL |
|---|---|---|
| Environment model | Learns or is given $P$ and $R$ | Does *not* use $P$ or $R$ explicitly |
| Sample efficiency | High (reuses simulated rollouts) | Low (needs many real samples) |
| Computational cost | Planning is expensive | Updates are cheap |
| Robustness to model error | Vulnerable to model bias | Robust (no model errors) |
| Examples | AlphaZero, Dyna-Q, MuZero | Q-Learning, SARSA, PPO, DQN |
| When to prefer | When environment is expensive or dangerous to sample | When environment is complex and hard to model |

**Examples:**
- **Model-Based:** AlphaZero (Chess/Go/Shogi) — uses an internal world model and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
- **Model-Free:** Deep Q-Network (DQN) — learned directly from Atari pixels with no world model.

#### Part (b) — Derive the Q-Learning Update Rule and Explain Why It Is Off-Policy. **(7 marks)**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Start with the Bellman optimality equation for $Q^*$:**

$$
Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \big]
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a *sample-based* update.** The expectation over $s'$ and $r$ is replaced by a single observed sample $(s', r)$:

$$
\text{Target} = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Temporal-Difference (TD) error $\delta_t$:**

$$
\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Perform a stochastic gradient step with learning rate $\alpha$:**

$$
\boxed{\, Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \big] \,}
$$

**Why it is off-policy:** The *behaviour* policy (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy) is the one used to generate the action $A_t$ that produced the transition. However, the *target* inside the max operator is computed *as if* the agent were following the *greedy* policy (the argmax). The update thus *learns* the value of the optimal policy while *behaving* according to a different (more exploratory) policy. This decoupling of behaviour from target is the definition of *off-policy* learning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on RL Framework Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_\pi$ with $V^*$:** $V_\pi$ is policy-specific; $V^*$ is universal. Mixing them up in a derivation loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor in infinite-horizon problems:** Without $\gamma < 1$, infinite sums diverge. Examiners always test this.
> 3. **Using the wrong form of the Bellman equation:** Bellman *expectation* uses the policy's action probabilities; Bellman *optimality* uses $\max$. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Mis-stating the Q-learning update:** The classic error is putting the max *outside* the $\alpha$ multiplication. The correct form is $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha[\cdot]$.
> 5. **Ignoring the Markov property:** If the state is not Markovian, the entire MDP framework breaks down. Examiners occasionally give a counter-example state definition to test this.
> 6. **Not specifying whether a method is on-policy or off-policy:** SARSA vs. Q-Learning. Always state the policy type.
> 7. **Arithmetic errors in MDP solutions:** The KTU valuation pattern is strict — even a 0.1 error in a numerical problem will reduce a 7-mark sub-question to 4 or 5.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — The Reinforcement Learning Framework**
>
> ✅ **The Agent–Environment Loop** is the *defining* structure of RL: state → action → reward → next state → update.
>
> ✅ **MDP Tuple** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ is the canonical mathematical model.
>
> ✅ **Markov Property:** $P(s' \mid s, a) = P(s' \mid \text{all history})$ — without it, RL theory breaks.
>
> ✅ **Return:** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots$ — the agent's *objective*.
>
> ✅ **Discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$:** Controls the time-value of reward and guarantees convergence.
>
> ✅ **Bellman Expectation Equations** express $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$ recursively under a *fixed* policy.
>
> ✅ **Bellman Optimality Equations** use a $\max$ operator and define $V^*$ and $Q^*$.
>
> ✅ **Policy Improvement Theorem:** Acting greedily w.r.t. $Q_\pi$ is monotonically non-decreasing in policy value.
>
> ✅ **On-Policy vs Off-Policy:** SARSA is on-policy; Q-Learning is off-policy.
>
> ✅ **Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off** is fundamental; $\varepsilon$-greedy, UCB, and softmax are standard resolutions.
>
> ✅ **Model-Free vs Model-Based:** Q-Learning, SARSA, PPO are model-free; AlphaZero, Dyna-Q are model-based.
>
> ✅ **Connection to LLMs:** Modern LLM alignment uses RLHF — a direct application of this framework, where the reward model is trained on human preferences and the LLM is the policy updated via PPO or DPO.
>
> ✅ **For KTU numerical problems:** Always set $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$ and use Bellman equations as a *system of linear equations* to solve for the value function.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: The Reinforcement Learning Framework.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping the RL Framework to LLMs with Human Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mapping the RL Framework to LLMs with Human Feedback # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)** is a fine-tuning paradigm that adapts the classical **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** formulation of Reinforcement Learning to the discrete, token-generation task of Large Language Models. In this framework, the pre-trained LLM is recast as a **stochastic policy** $\pi_\theta$, and a separate **reward model** $r_\phi$, trained on pairwise human preference data, supplies the scalar reward signal that drives policy optimisation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic is a direct extension of Module 3 (Alignment & Fine-tuning). Examiners test whether students can (i) translate each RL component (agent, state, action, reward, policy) into its LLM equivalent, and (ii) articulate why a learned reward model is needed instead of using a hand-coded one. The five-tuple MDP $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, r, \gamma \rangle$ used in classical RL is preserved, but every element is reinterpreted as a token-level construct over the vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$: | MDP Concept | Standard RL | LLM Counterpart | |---|---|---| | Agent | Robot / Game-player | The LLM (parameters $\theta$) | | Environment | Physical / simulated world | Text context window + user prompt | | State $s_t$ | Sensor readings | Token sequence $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_t)$ | | Action $a_t$ | Motor command | Next token drawn from $\mathcal{V}$ | | Reward $r_t$ | Numerical feedback | Scalar from reward model $r_\phi$ | | Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ | Action distribution | Categorical over vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ | | Episode | Full trajectory | Generation until `` | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Dog and the Treat Trainer"** > > Imagine training a **highly intelligent dog** (the LLM) to write essays: > - The **dog** is the *agent* — it does the writing. > - The **living room** it sits in is the *environment* (the prompt + everything written so far). > - Each **single word** it barks or whines is an *action*. > - You, the **trainer**, do *not* hand the dog a numeric score every word; instead, after the dog finishes a paragraph, you read two versions and say *"I liked paragraph A more than B."* > - A junior trainer (the **reward model**) watches your preferences, learns what *you* mean by "good," and starts handing out automatic treats ($r_t$). > - The dog then learns (via **PPO**) to maximise these treats, while a leash (the **KL penalty**) prevents it from drifting into nonsense. > > This is precisely RLHF: humans give *preferences*, a model converts those preferences to *rewards*, and RL maximises the rewards. ## 1.3 Why Human Feedback Is Needed Pure supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on $(x, y^\star)$ pairs teaches the model to imitate, but it cannot capture nuanced qualities such as **helpfulness, harmlessness, or honesty** — because such qualities are not enumerable as label tokens. Human preferences, by contrast, are *comparative* and easier for annotators to produce, making them scalable. > [!TIP] > **One-line answer for KTU 2-mark question:** *RLHF replaces the unobservable "true" reward function with a learned proxy reward model derived from pairwise human comparisons, enabling optimisation of qualities that cannot be expressed as a single label.* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Walk-Through of the Mapping 1. **State construction** — At decoding step $t$, the state $s_t$ is the concatenation of the prompt $x$ and the tokens generated up to $t-1$: $$s_t = (x, y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{t-1})$$ 2. **Action selection** — Token $a_t \in \mathcal{V}$ is sampled from the categorical distribution: $$a_t \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t)$$ 3. **Reward assignment** — A *sparse*, *delayed* reward is computed only at the end of the response: $$r_T = r_\phi(x, y_{1:T})$$ During generation, a per-token **KL penalty** is applied to anchor the policy to a reference model $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ (the SFT model). 4. **Policy update** — The parameters $\theta$ are updated using **Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO)**, a first-order trust-region method. 5. **Iterative loop** — Generation → reward → update → repeat for many prompts. ## 2.2 The Three-Stage RLHF Pipeline | Stage | Objective | Loss / Update Rule | |---|---|---| | **Stage 1 — SFT** | Teach format & instruction-following | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}} = -\sum_t \log \pi_\theta(y_t^\star \mid x, y_{ [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise this table verbatim — every symbol, every constant. KTU examiners award full marks only when both the **formula** and the **boundary conditions** are written correctly. | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t} \gamma^k \, r_{t+k}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$; typically $\gamma = 1$ for LLMs | | 2 | Policy (token) | $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = P_\theta(y_t \mid x, y_{ [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This exact pipeline is what produced **InstructGPT, ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini**. Without mapping RL onto token generation, modern conversational alignment would be impossible. The KL term is the safety leash that prevents **reward hacking** (the model generating fluent but vacuous text that fools the reward model). ## 2.4 Why PPO and not Vanilla Policy Gradient? Vanilla REINFORCE has **unbounded updates**, causing the LLM to collapse (mode-collapse to a single high-reward token). PPO's *clipping* mechanism guarantees that the policy ratio $\rho_t$ stays in $[1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon]$, providing stable, sample-efficient updates that scale to billions of parameters. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bradley-Terry Preference Model **Goal:** Given two candidate responses $y_w$ (winner) and $y_l$ (loser) for prompt $x$, model the probability that a human prefers $y_w$ over $y_l$ using the unknown reward function $r$. **Step 1 — Assumption of Plackett–Luce family.** Assume the probability that $y_w$ beats $y_l$ is a function only of the difference of their latent utilities (rewards): $$P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) \;=\; f\!\big(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l)\big)$$ **Step 2 — Choose a logistic link function.** The Bradley-Terry model chooses $f$ to be the standard logistic sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$: $$P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) \;=\; \sigma\!\big(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l)\big)$$ **Step 3 — Expand the sigmoid.** Substituting the definition of $\sigma$: $$P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\big(-\big[r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l)\big]\big)}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum-likelihood loss.** The dataset $\mathcal{D}$ contains $N$ triplets $(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)})$. The log-likelihood is: $$\log \mathcal{L} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sigma\!\big(r_\phi(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}) - r_\phi(x^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)})\big)$$ **Step 5 — Convert to a minimisation loss.** The negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) is minimised: $$\mathcal{L}_{RM}(\phi) \;=\; -\,\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l) \sim \mathcal{D}}\!\Big[\log \sigma\!\big(r_\phi(x,y_w) - r_\phi(x,y_l)\big)\Big]$$ Taking the gradient with respect to $\phi$ and performing stochastic gradient descent yields the trained reward model $r_\phi$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — KL-Regularised Reward for PPO **Goal:** Define the per-trajectory reward to be maximised, ensuring the fine-tuned model does not stray too far from the SFT model. **Step 1 — Per-token reward.** For each token $t$, add the KL divergence between the current policy and the reference policy: $$\tilde{r}_t \;=\; r_\phi(s_t, a_t) \;-\; \beta\,\text{KL}\!\big(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t) \,\big\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot \mid s_t)\big)$$ **Step 2 — Trajectory-level return.** Sum over the full sequence of length $T$: $$R(\tau) \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \tilde{r}_t \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{T} r_\phi(s_t, a_t)}_{\text{task reward}} \;-\; \underbrace{\beta \sum_{t=1}^{T} \text{KL}\big(\pi_\theta \Vert \pi_{\text{ref}}\big)}_{\text{drift penalty}}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** The first term pushes the model towards high-reward responses. The second term penalises divergence from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$, controlled by coefficient $\beta$. Increasing $\beta$ keeps the model close to the SFT distribution; decreasing $\beta$ lets the model explore more aggressively (at the risk of reward hacking). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PPO Clipped Surrogate Objective **Goal:** Derive the PPO update rule starting from standard policy gradient. **Step 1 — Vanilla policy gradient.** The objective to maximise is $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[R(\tau)]$. Using the **likelihood-ratio trick**: $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\!\Big[\sum_{t=1}^{T} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, R_t\Big]$$ **Step 2 — Importance-sampled surrogate.** Replace on-policy samples with off-policy samples from an older policy $\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}$ using the ratio $\rho_t$: $$\mathcal{L}^{\text{IS}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_t\!\Big[\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t\Big], \qquad \rho_t(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t \mid s_t)}$$ **Step 3 — Trust-region intuition.** When $\rho_t$ drifts far from $1$, the importance-sampling estimate becomes high-variance. Constrain $\rho_t \in [1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon]$. **Step 4 — Clipped objective (take the minimum).** The clipped surrogate objective is: $$\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_t\!\Big[\min\!\big(\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t,\; \text{clip}(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon)\,\hat{A}_t\big)\Big]$$ **Step 5 — Why the minimum?** - When $\hat{A}_t > 0$ (good action), the clip caps the upward push at $\rho_t = 1+\epsilon$. - When $\hat{A}_t < 0$ (bad action), the clip caps the downward push at $\rho_t = 1-\epsilon$. - Taking $\min$ ensures the objective is a *lower bound* on the unclipped surrogate, guaranteeing pessimism — the foundation of PPO's stability. **Step 6 — Total PPO loss.** Combine policy loss, value loss, and entropy bonus: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO}} \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}} \;-\; c_1\, \mathcal{L}^{\text{VF}} \;+\; c_2\, \mathcal{H}(\pi_\theta)$$ where $\mathcal{L}^{\text{VF}} = (V_\theta(s_t) - R_t)^2$ and $\mathcal{H}$ is the policy entropy. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Component Exists | Problem without RL mapping | Solution provided | |---|---| | Human values are not enumerable as labels | Pairwise preferences $\Rightarrow$ reward model | | Long sequences → sparse reward only at end | KL penalty applied per-token to provide dense signal | | Catastrophic forgetting / mode collapse | Frozen reference policy $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ + KL anchor | | High variance of REINFORCE updates | PPO clipped ratio $\rho_t \in [1\pm\epsilon]$ | | Reward model over-optimisation (Goodhart's law) | $\beta$-controlled KL term caps divergence | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mapping the RL Loop onto an LLM ```mermaid flowchart LR A[LLM Agent
policy pi_theta] --> B[Read Prompt x
state s_t] B --> C[Sample Token a_t
from pi_theta] C --> D[Append Token
state becomes s_t+1] D --> E{EOS token} E -- No --> C E -- Yes --> F[Reward Model r_phi
outputs scalar r_T] F --> G[Compute KL Penalty
vs pi_ref] G --> H[PPO Update
theta equals theta plus delta] H --> A ``` **Reading the diagram (left-to-right loop):** - **LLM Agent** holds the trainable policy $\pi_\theta$. - The **prompt** defines the initial state $s_1$. - At each step, a **token** is sampled (action $a_t$), appended to the context (state transition). - The loop continues until the **end-of-sequence** token is generated. - The **reward model** then produces a single terminal scalar $r_T$. - The **KL penalty** measures how far $\pi_\theta$ has drifted from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$. - **PPO** uses both signals to update $\theta$. ## 4.2 The Three-Stage RLHF Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Stage 1
Pretrain LM on Corpus] --> B[Stage 1
SFT on Demonstrations] B --> C[Stage 2
Collect Pairwise Preferences] C --> D[Stage 2
Train Reward Model r_phi] D --> E[Stage 3
PPO with KL Anchor] E --> F[Aligned Instruction Following LLM] F --> G[Deployment Chatbot] ``` ## 4.3 Reward Model Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Prompt x] --> E1[Transformer Encoder] YW[Winner y_w] --> E1 YL[Loser y_l] --> E1 E1 --> H1[Hidden representation] H1 --> M1[Linear Head r_phi] M1 --> S[Scalar r] S --> C[Compare Delta r equals r_w minus r_l] C --> L[Cross Entropy Loss] ``` ## 4.4 PPO Update Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB SP[Sampling pi_theta_old] --> RR[Rollout Buffer stores states actions rewards] RR --> AE[Compute Advantages via GAE] AE --> CL[Clipped Surrogate Objective] CL --> GD[Gradient Descent on theta] GD --> UP[Update pi_theta] UP --> SP ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram-reading tip for the exam:** When asked *"Draw the RLHF pipeline"*, label the **three stages explicitly** (SFT → Reward Model → PPO) and include the **KL penalty** as a side-branch in Stage 3. Examiners award full marks only when both the data flow and the loss function are visible. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **List any six elements of the classical RL framework and map each to its corresponding element in the LLM setting used during RLHF.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | RL Element | LLM Equivalent | |---|---|---| | 1 | Agent | The language model with parameters $\theta$ (the policy network) | | 2 | Environment | Prompt $x$ concatenated with tokens generated so far | | 3 | State $s_t$ | Token sequence $(x, y_1, \dots, y_{t-1})$ at decoding step $t$ | | 4 | Action $a_t$ | The next token drawn from vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ | | 5 | Reward $r_t$ | Scalar output of the reward model $r_\phi(x, y)$ | | 6 | Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Token-level categorical distribution $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ | | 7 | Episode | Generation of one full response terminating at `` | *(Any six ⇒ full 3 marks; four-to-five ⇒ 2 marks; less than four ⇒ ≤ 1 mark.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Sample Paper]` **Why is a learned reward model necessary in RLHF instead of using human-defined rewards directly?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Scalability:** Humans cannot annotate numerical rewards for every token of every response; pairwise preferences are far cheaper to collect. *(1 mark)* 2. **Ambiguity of human values:** Qualities like *helpfulness* and *harmlessness* cannot be expressed as a closed-form function. A learned model captures these implicit judgements from data. *(1 mark)* 3. **On-line deployment:** Once trained, $r_\phi$ can produce rewards in milliseconds per query, allowing RL to explore and update the policy millions of times. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the **Bradley-Terry preference model** used to train the reward model in RLHF. Clearly state the loss function and explain each symbol. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Explain the **PPO clipped surrogate objective** used in the RL fine-tuning stage. Why is the clipping necessary, and what role does the KL penalty play? **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution **(a) Bradley-Terry Derivation \[7 marks\]** We assume that the probability of a human preferring response $y_w$ over $y_l$ for prompt $x$ depends only on the difference of their latent scalar rewards $r(x,y)$. Choosing the logistic sigmoid link $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ gives: $$P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) \;=\; \sigma\!\big(r_\phi(x,y_w) - r_\phi(x,y_l)\big)$$ Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^N$, the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) loss to be minimised w.r.t. $\phi$ is: $$\mathcal{L}_{RM}(\phi) \;=\; -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sigma\!\big(r_\phi(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}) - r_\phi(x^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)})\big)$$ | Symbol | Meaning | Marks | |---|---|---| | $r_\phi(\cdot,\cdot)$ | Reward model with parameters $\phi$ | 1 | | $y_w, y_l$ | Winner and loser completions | 1 | | $\sigma$ | Logistic sigmoid link | 1 | | $\mathcal{D}$ | Preference dataset of pairwise comparisons | 1 | | $\mathcal{L}_{RM}$ | Cross-entropy loss on preference | 1 | | Gradient update step | $\phi \leftarrow \phi - \eta \nabla_\phi \mathcal{L}_{RM}$ | 2 | **(b) PPO Clipped Surrogate & KL Penalty \[7 marks\]** Vanilla policy gradient is unstable because $\pi_\theta$ can move arbitrarily far per update. PPO introduces a *clipped probability ratio*: $$\rho_t(\theta) \;=\; \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t \mid s_t)}$$ The clipped surrogate objective is: $$\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_t\!\Big[\min\!\big(\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t,\; \text{clip}(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon)\,\hat{A}_t\big)\Big]$$ - **Why clip?** Limits $\rho_t$ to $[1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon]$ (typically $\epsilon = 0.2$), preventing destructively large updates; the $\min$ enforces a pessimistic lower bound. *(3 marks)* - **Why KL penalty?** Without it, the model drifts far from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$, causing **mode collapse** and **reward hacking**. The composite reward is: $$R(x,y) = r_\phi(x,y) - \beta\,\text{KL}\!\big(\pi_\theta \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}}\big)$$ Higher $\beta$ ⇒ closer to SFT; lower $\beta$ ⇒ more aggressive optimisation. *(4 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **three-stage RLHF pipeline**. For each stage, state the objective and the loss function. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Discuss the problem of **reward hacking** in RLHF. How does the KL-regularisation term mitigate it, and what are its limitations? **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution **(a) Three-Stage RLHF Pipeline \[7 marks\]** **Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):** Pre-train on a large corpus, then fine-tune on curated $(x, y^\star)$ demonstrations so the model learns instruction format. $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}}(\theta) = -\sum_t \log \pi_\theta(y_t^\star \mid x, y_{ [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing $r_\phi$ with the LLM loss** — the reward model is *separate* from the policy. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are conflated. > 2. **Forgetting the KL penalty** — writing only the PPO clipped objective without mentioning the KL term loses 2 marks; the KL anchor is the single most distinctive feature of RLHF vs. vanilla RL. > 3. **Treating the reward as dense** — in LLMs the reward is sparse (terminal). Mention this explicitly. > 4. **Skipping the Bradley-Terry derivation** — the question often says "derive" or "obtain". A formula-only answer earns ≤ 3 of 7 marks. > 5. **No block diagram** — for the 14-mark pipeline question, the absence of a labelled diagram costs 1–2 marks. > 6. **Wrong symbols** — confusing $\theta$ (policy) with $\phi$ (reward model) is a frequent and costly slip. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "What to Write in the Last 60 Seconds Before Submitting"** > > - [ ] **MDP mapping** is the first thing to state: Agent = LLM, State = token sequence, Action = next token, Reward = $r_\phi$, Policy = $\pi_\theta$. > - [ ] **Three stages**: SFT → Reward Model → PPO with KL. > - [ ] **Bradley-Terry formula**: $P(y_w \succ y_l) = \sigma(r_w - r_l)$; loss is the negative log-likelihood. > - [ ] **Composite reward**: $R = r_\phi - \beta\,\text{KL}(\pi_\theta \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}})$. > - [ ] **PPO clipped ratio**: $\rho_t = \pi_\theta / \pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}$, clipped to $[1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon]$, $\epsilon \approx 0.2$. > - [ ] **Why PPO not REINFORCE**: clipping gives stability and prevents mode collapse. > - [ ] **Why KL penalty**: prevents reward hacking; anchors to SFT distribution $\pi_{\text{ref}}$. > - [ ] **Reward is sparse** (terminal only); KL is dense (per-token). > - [ ] **Symbols**: $\theta$ = policy params; $\phi$ = reward model params; $\beta$ = KL weight; $\gamma$ = discount; $\epsilon$ = clip range. > - [ ] **Real-world artefacts**: InstructGPT, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini all use this exact pipeline. > - [ ] **Draw the block diagram** for any 7-mark sub-question on the pipeline. > - [ ] **Mention Goodhart's law** when discussing reward hacking.

topics.title: Evolution of RLHF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evolution of RLHF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)** is a multi-stage alignment paradigm in which a pretrained language model is adapted to complex human values, intentions, and stylistic preferences by using a learned **reward model**, itself trained on pairwise human preference judgments, to provide a dense scalar signal that drives a reinforcement learning optimizer (most classically **Proximal Policy Optimization, PPO**) over the policy distribution of the language model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PECMT862 · Module 3):** RLHF is presented as the bridge between *capability* (what a pretrained LLM can do) and *alignment* (what a user actually wants it to do). The "Evolution" perspective traces how a fragile 2017 reinforcement-learning hack matured into the dominant alignment stack behind InstructGPT, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama-Chat.

The technical stack of classical RLHF consists of three tightly coupled stages:

| Stage | Name | What is learned | Optimizer |
|-------|------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) | Initial instruction-following policy $\pi^{\text{SFT}}$ | Standard cross-entropy |
| 2 | Reward Modeling (RM) | Scalar reward function $r_\phi(x,y)$ | Binary cross-entropy on pairs |
| 3 | RL Fine-Tuning | Aligned policy $\pi_\theta^{\text{RL}}$ | PPO with KL anchor to $\pi^{\text{SFT}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "from Human Feedback"?** The reward signal is *not* programmatic (e.g., game score). It is **elicited** from human annotators who rank or compare model outputs. This makes RLHF the first widely-successful method for injecting *qualitative, holistic* preferences (helpfulness, harmlessness, honesty) into a differentiable training loop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Apprentice Chef

Imagine a young apprentice chef who can read 10,000 cookbooks (this is **pretraining** — the model has seen huge text corpora). A master chef (the **human annotator**) asks the apprentice to cook a dish. The apprentice produces several plates, and the master chef tastes them and says: *"This one is better than that one, because it is more balanced."*

Now a senior sous-chef (the **reward model**) studies thousands of these master-chef comparisons and learns a rule of thumb: *"balanced flavor, low saltiness, golden color"* — this is exactly what a reward model does: it internalizes the comparison signal into a scalar score.

Finally, a head chef (the **RL policy optimizer / PPO**) tells the apprentice: *"Keep cooking. For every new dish, I'll score it with the sous-chef's rule. Adjust your recipes to maximize that score — but don't drift too far from the original cookbook style, or you'll forget the basics."* The "don't drift too far" rule is the **KL-divergence penalty** that anchors the policy to the SFT model.

**Key intuition:** RLHF is *not* a single algorithm — it is a **three-protocol pipeline** that converts human taste into a mathematical reward, then uses RL to bake that taste into the model weights.

## 1.3 Historical Anchors at a Glance

- **2017 — Christiano et al. (OpenAI / DeepMind / Berkeley):** Introduced learning from preference comparisons; originally applied to Atari and MuJoCo. This is the *founding paper*.
- **2019 — Böhm et al. (Google), Ziegler et al. (OpenAI):** First successful transfer of preference-learning to **text summarization** and **text continuation** — the prototype of modern LLM-RLHF.
- **2022 — Ouyang et al. (OpenAI) — InstructGPT:** Scaled RLHF to **instruct** a 175B GPT-3; introduced the three-stage pipeline that became the industry template.
- **2022 — Bai et al. (Anthropic) — Constitutional AI / RLAIF:** Replaced *some* human feedback with *AI-generated feedback guided by a written constitution* — first major evolution beyond pure human labelers.
- **2023 — Rafailov et al. (Stanford) — DPO:** Proved RLHF can be re-formulated as a **direct supervised loss**, bypassing explicit reward models and RL loops.
- **2024 onward — KTO, IPO, SimPO, ORPO, online DPO, iterative DPO:** A family of RLHF variants optimizing different divergences, sample efficiency, and preference noise robustness.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, the historical timeline is **frequently asked** as a 3-mark question. Memorize the chain: *Preference learning (2017) → Summarization (2019) → InstructGPT (2022) → Constitutional AI (2022) → DPO (2023) → Modern variants (2024+)*.

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Pipeline (Operational Walk-Through)

### Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)

The pretrained LLM (call its parameters $\theta_0$) is fine-tuned on a curated dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}$ of (prompt, demonstration) pairs written by human labelers. The objective is ordinary next-token cross-entropy:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}}(\theta) \;=\; -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}}}\left[\sum_{t=1}^{|y|} \log \pi_\theta\!\left(y_t \mid x, y_{<t}\right)\right]$$

- **Why this stage exists:** Pretrained models are *next-token predictors*, not *assistants*. SFT teaches the format and tone of being helpful.
- **Limitation that motivates RLHF:** SFT loss treats every token equally. It cannot express *"this whole answer is more helpful than that one."* It also suffers from **exposure bias** and tends to over-fit to the (small) demonstration set, producing a model that is *less* diverse than its pretrained parent.

### Stage 2 — Reward Modeling (RM)

The SFT model is used to sample multiple candidate responses $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_K\}$ for each prompt $x$. Human annotators rank or pair-compare these candidates. The reward model $r_\phi(x, y) : \mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is trained to predict these comparisons.

The standard assumption is the **Bradley–Terry (1952) model**: the probability that response $y_w$ is preferred over $y_l$ is

$$P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w)\right)}{\exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w)\right) + \exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_l)\right)} \;=\; \sigma\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)\right)$$

where $\sigma$ is the logistic sigmoid. The RM is trained by **binary cross-entropy** on a dataset of preference triples $(x, y_w, y_l)$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) \;=\; -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l)\sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{pref}}}\left[\log \sigma\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)\right)\right]$$

- **Architectural note:** $r_\phi$ is typically initialized from the SFT model with the final unembedding head replaced by a single scalar projection.
- **Reward hacking warning:** Because $r_\phi$ is only an *approximation* of human preference, later stages may exploit its errors. This is the famous *Goodhart's Law* failure mode.

### Stage 3 — RL Fine-Tuning with PPO

The policy $\pi_\theta$ is now optimized to maximize expected reward, **anchored** to the SFT policy to prevent runaway divergence:

$$\max_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{x\sim\mathcal{D},\, y\sim\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x)}\!\left[\,r_\phi(x, y)\,\right] \;-\; \beta\,\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x) \,\Big\Vert\, \pi^{\text{SFT}}(\cdot\mid x)\right)$$

In practice, this is solved with **PPO**, a first-order policy-gradient method that uses a *clipped surrogate objective* for stability. Per-token PPO loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO}}(\theta) \;=\; -\mathbb{E}_{t}\!\left[\min\!\Big(\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t,\;\mathrm{clip}(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon)\,\hat{A}_t\Big)\right]$$

where the importance-sampling ratio is

$$\rho_t(\theta) \;=\; \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t \mid s_t)}$$

and $\hat{A}_t$ is the **generalized advantage estimate** of how much better action $a_t$ was than expected.

> [!NOTE]
> **The four losses running inside PPO simultaneously:**
> 1. **Policy loss** — drives the model toward higher-reward completions.
> 2. **Value loss** — trains a learned baseline $V_\psi(s_t)$ that subtracts reward variance.
> 3. **KL penalty** — keeps $\pi_\theta$ close to $\pi^{\text{SFT}}$.
> 4. **Entropy bonus** — encourages exploration / diversity.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Equation | Meaning | KTU use case |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma\!\big(r_\phi(x,y_w) - r_\phi(x,y_l)\big)$ | Bradley–Terry preference probability | Reward model training |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}} = -\log \sigma\!\big(\Delta r\big)$ | Pairwise ranking loss (RM objective) | Derivation questions |
| 3 | $\max_\theta\; \mathbb{E}[r_\phi] - \beta\,\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}(\pi_\theta \Vert \pi_{\text{SFT}})$ | RLHF master objective | Conceptual "what is RLHF?" |
| 4 | $\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}(P\Vert Q) = \sum_i P(i)\log\frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | Per-token KL divergence penalty | Stability argument |
| 5 | $\rho_t(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t\mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t\mid s_t)}$ | Importance sampling ratio in PPO | PPO derivation |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{clip}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\min(\rho_t \hat A_t,\; \mathrm{clip}(\rho_t,1-\epsilon,1+\epsilon)\hat A_t)\right]$ | Clipped PPO surrogate | PPO step |
| 7 | $\hat{A}_t = \sum_{l=0}^{T-t}(\gamma\lambda)^l \delta_{t+l}$, $\delta_t = r_t + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | GAE advantage | KTU 14-mark derivations |
| 8 | $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{policy}} = -\mathbb{E}[\hat{A}_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)]$ | Policy gradient theorem (REINFORCE baseline) | Foundational derivation |
| 9 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\log \sigma\!\big(\beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\big)$ | Direct Preference Optimization (2023) | Evolution question |
| 10 | $r_\phi(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)$ | Implicit reward recovered from DPO | Evolution question |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions & units:** the KL coefficient $\beta$ is dimensionless and typically tuned in $[0.05, 0.5]$; PPO clip $\epsilon$ is usually $0.2$; GAE discount $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.99]$; GAE $\lambda \in [0.9, 0.97]$. Reward scores are **uncalibrated scalars** — only **differences** matter (Bradley–Terry is translation-invariant).

## 2.3 Why the KL Anchor Matters — Real-World Engineering Consequence

Without the KL penalty, the RL optimizer is *incentivized* to produce outputs that maximize $r_\phi$, even if those outputs are degenerate (e.g., the same high-reward token repeated forever, or generating a special token that $r_\phi$ accidentally scores high). Empirically (Bai et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2022), removing the KL constraint within $\sim 100$ PPO updates causes:

- Output collapse to a single high-reward token.
- Mode collapse: loss of linguistic diversity.
- **Reward hacking:** the policy discovers adversarial inputs to the reward model.

This is the core engineering reason RLHF is framed as a *constrained* optimization problem rather than a free RL problem.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bradley–Terry Reward-Model Loss from First Principles

We are given a dataset of preference triples $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $y_w$ is the *winning* (preferred) response and $y_l$ is the *losing* response.

**Step 1 — Define preference probability.** Under the assumption that preferences follow a logistic model, the probability of preferring $y_w$ over $y_l$ given prompt $x$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \frac{\exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w)\right)}{\exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w)\right) + \exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_l)\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

We factor out $\exp(r_\phi(x, y_w))$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_l) - r_\phi(x, y_w)\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition of the logistic sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, this becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \sigma\!\left(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the negative log-likelihood.** Treating each triple as an i.i.d. sample, the log-likelihood of the dataset is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(\phi) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P\!\left(y_w^{(i)} \succ y_l^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sigma\!\left(r_\phi\!\left(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}\right) - r_\phi\!\left(x^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)}\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a minimization loss.** Negate and average:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) &= -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sigma\!\left(\Delta r^{(i)}\right), \quad \text{where } \Delta r^{(i)} = r_\phi(x^{(i)}, y_w^{(i)}) - r_\phi(x^{(i)}, y_l^{(i)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient for backprop.** Using $\frac{d}{dz}\log\sigma(z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_\phi \mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}} &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\sigma(\Delta r) - 1\right)\left(\nabla_\phi r_\phi(x,y_w) - \nabla_\phi r_\phi(x,y_l)\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that the reward model is *pushed* to increase the score gap in the direction of human preference — the gradient is zero exactly when $\Delta r \to +\infty$ (i.e., the reward model is fully confident in the preferred response).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The PPO Clipped Surrogate Objective

We begin from the standard **surrogate objective** of TRPO/PPO:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{\text{CPI}}(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}_{t}\!\left[\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where CPI stands for *Conservative Policy Iteration*. The issue is that $L^{\text{CPI}}$ can be *unboundedly* large when $\rho_t$ drifts far from $1$ (off-policy failure). PPO clips the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}_{t}\!\left[\min\!\left(\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t,\; \mathrm{clip}\!\left(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon\right)\hat{A}_t\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why the minimum?** Because the clip creates a *pessimistic* lower bound. We can expand the logic per-token:

- If $\hat{A}_t > 0$ (good action): we want to increase its probability, i.e. increase $\rho_t$. The clip caps $\rho_t$ at $1+\epsilon$, so the surrogate stops improving once the new policy moves more than a fraction $\epsilon$ further. The minimum selects the *clipped* (smaller) value.
- If $\hat{A}_t < 0$ (bad action): we want to *decrease* its probability, so we want $\rho_t$ to drop. The clip caps it at $1-\epsilon$ from below. Again, the minimum selects the *clipped* (more negative) value.

**Final PPO loss (negated for gradient descent):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO}}(\theta) &= -\mathbb{E}_{t}\!\left[\min\!\left(\rho_t(\theta)\,\hat{A}_t,\; \mathrm{clip}\!\left(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon\right)\hat{A}_t\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

In RLHF-for-LLMs, the full per-token PPO objective additionally includes the per-token KL penalty and (optionally) a small entropy bonus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO+KL}}(\theta) &= -\mathbb{E}_{t}\!\left[\hat{A}_t \rho_t(\theta) - \beta\,\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t) \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}(\cdot \mid s_t)\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — From RLHF to DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023)

DPO is the most important *evolution* of RLHF. We now derive its loss from the RLHF objective.

**Step 1 — Write the KL-regularized RLHF objective.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_\theta\; \mathbb{E}_{x, y\sim\pi_\theta}\!\left[r_\phi(x, y)\right] - \beta\,\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x) \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Closed-form solution.** This objective has an analytic optimum, obtained by the Lagrangian condition (pointwise optimality):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^*(y \mid x) &= \frac{1}{Z(x)}\,\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)\,\exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\,r_\phi(x, y)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Z(x) = \sum_{y'}\pi_{\text{ref}}(y'\mid x)\exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\beta}r_\phi(x, y')\right)$ is the partition function.

**Step 3 — Invert to express the reward in terms of the policy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_\phi(x, y) &= \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug into the Bradley–Terry preference model.** The $Z(x)$ term cancels when we take the difference between winning and losing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the DPO loss.** Replacing $\pi^*$ by the trainable $\pi_\theta$ and applying binary cross-entropy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) &= -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l)}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key consequence of the derivation:** DPO reduces the three-stage RLHF pipeline to a *two-stage* pipeline (SFT + DPO) and removes the need for a separately-trained reward model and an RL loop. The Bradley–Terry derivation above is **the single most evolution-defining result** in modern LLM alignment and is a high-probability KTU 14-mark derivation question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major RLHF-Era Algorithms

| Method | Year | Reward source | Optimizer | Stages | KL anchor | Sample efficiency | Stability |
|--------|------|---------------|-----------|--------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| SFT only | ≤ 2017 | — | Cross-entropy | 1 | — | High | High |
| Original RLHF (PPO) | 2019–2022 | Human-labeled RM | PPO | 3 | Explicit | Low | Low (reward hacking) |
| InstructGPT | 2022 | Human-labeled RM | PPO | 3 | Explicit | Medium | Medium |
| Constitutional AI (RLAIF) | 2022 | AI-generated, rule-filtered | PPO | 3 | Explicit | High | High |
| DPO | 2023 | Implicit, in policy | Binary CE | 2 | Implicit via ref model | High | High |
| KTO | 2024 | Binary (good/bad), no pairs | Custom | 1 | Implicit | Very high | High |
| IPO | 2023 | Pairs + regularization | CE | 2 | Implicit | High | High (no BT overfitting) |
| SimPO | 2024 | Pairs, length-normalized | Custom | 1 | Implicit | High | High |
| Online/Iterative DPO | 2024 | Self-generated, re-ranked | CE | 2 | Implicit | Highest | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examination tip:** If a 14-mark question asks "explain the evolution of RLHF", use the *Derivation 3* path to motivate DPO from the original objective. Examiners award full marks when a student can show that DPO is *not* a separate invention but a mathematical re-derivation of PPO-style alignment.
