# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECMT862]** 
*Define Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). List its three classical stages.*

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a paradigm that aligns a pretrained language model to human values by training a reward model on human preference judgments over model outputs, and then using reinforcement learning to fine-tune the LM to maximize that reward.

The three classical stages are:

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** of the pretrained LM on human-written demonstrations.
2. **Reward Modeling (RM)**, where a separate model is trained on pairwise human preference data using the Bradley–Terry model to predict a scalar reward.
3. **RL fine-tuning**, typically using PPO, to optimize the SFT policy against the reward model subject to a KL-divergence constraint against the SFT reference.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECMT862]** 
*What is Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)? How does it differ from classical RLHF?*

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), proposed by Rafailov et al. in 2023, is a closed-form alternative to the PPO stage of RLHF. It re-derives the optimal RLHF policy analytically and substitutes the reward model with an *implicit* reward given by the log-ratio of the trained policy to a frozen reference policy, weighted by a temperature $\beta$.

**Key differences from classical RLHF:**

- DPO requires **no explicit reward model** and **no RL loop** — it is a supervised binary cross-entropy loss on preference pairs.
- It needs only **two stages** (SFT + DPO) instead of three.
- It is **more stable and sample-efficient** than PPO, but less flexible for online or reward-shaping scenarios.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module contains one 14-mark question with internal choice. The question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Below, *Question A* and *Question B* are both fully solved, providing complete exam readiness.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024, PECMT862]**

**(a)** Explain the three stages of classical RLHF in detail. For each stage, state the objective function being optimized and the role of human input. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Bradley–Terry reward-model loss from the assumption that preferences follow a logistic model, and explain why the reward model is translation-invariant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT).** A pretrained LM with parameters $\theta_0$ is fine-tuned on a curated set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}}$ of (prompt, demonstration) pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}}}\!\left[\sum_{t=1}^{\vert y\vert} \log \pi_\theta(y_t \mid x, y_{<t})\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Role of human input:* Human annotators write the high-quality demonstrations that teach the model the format of being a helpful assistant.

**Stage 2 — Reward Modeling (RM).** Multiple candidate responses are sampled from the SFT model and ranked by humans. A reward model $r_\phi(x,y)$ is trained to predict these rankings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l)\sim\mathcal{D}_{\text{pref}}}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(r_\phi(x,y_w) - r_\phi(x,y_l)\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Role of human input:* Humans provide the *preference signal* — the ground truth that the reward model learns to imitate.

**Stage 3 — RL Fine-Tuning (PPO).** The policy $\pi_\theta$ is updated to maximize expected reward under a KL anchor to the SFT model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_\theta\; \mathbb{E}_{x\sim\mathcal{D},\,y\sim\pi_\theta}\!\left[r_\phi(x,y)\right] - \beta\,\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x)\,\Vert\,\pi^{\text{SFT}}(\cdot\mid x)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Role of human input:* Indirect — humans guide the *reward model*; the policy never directly sees human labels during this stage.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1.** Begin with the logistic / Bradley–Terry model assumption. Let $r_\phi(x, y)$ denote the latent reward assigned by the reward model. The probability that a human prefers $y_w$ over $y_l$ is given by the softmax over their rewards:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \frac{\exp(r_\phi(x, y_w))}{\exp(r_\phi(x, y_w)) + \exp(r_\phi(x, y_l))}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by $\exp(-r_\phi(x, y_w))$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \frac{1}{1 + \exp(r_\phi(x, y_l) - r_\phi(x, y_w))} = \sigma(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Take negative log-likelihood over the dataset to obtain a minimization loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) &= -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l)}\!\left[\log \sigma(\Delta r)\right], \quad \Delta r = r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Translation invariance. Consider the transformed reward $\tilde{r}_\phi(x, y) = r_\phi(x, y) + c(x)$ for any prompt-dependent constant $c(x)$. The Bradley–Terry probability becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\tilde{y}_w \succ \tilde{y}_l \mid x) &= \sigma\!\left([r_\phi(x,y_w) + c(x)] - [r_\phi(x,y_l) + c(x)]\right) = \sigma(\Delta r)
\end{aligned}
$$

The constant $c(x)$ **cancels**. Therefore the preference probability — and hence the loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}$ — depends only on *reward differences*, not absolute values. This is why during PPO fine-tuning we often **normalize** rewards per-prompt (e.g., subtract the running mean) before computing advantages.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A):** Many students forget to mention that the **value function** $V_\psi(s_t)$ is trained jointly with the policy in PPO. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** if you write only the policy loss without acknowledging the value baseline. Also, do not confuse the *reward model* $r_\phi$ with the *value function* $V_\psi$ — the former is **fixed** during PPO; the latter is **trained**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024, PECMT862]**

**(a)** Trace the evolution of RLHF from 2017 to 2024, highlighting four key milestones and the limitation each one addressed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the KL-regularized RLHF objective, derive the DPO loss and explain why DPO removes the need for an explicit reward model and a separate RL loop. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

| # | Year | Milestone | Limitation it addressed |
|---|------|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **2017** | Christiano et al. — *Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences* | Reward engineering is infeasible for complex, qualitative tasks (e.g., "be helpful"). Replaces hand-crafted rewards with learned pairwise preferences. |
| 2 | **2019** | Ziegler et al. — *Fine-Tuning Language Models from Human Preferences* (text continuation); Böhm et al. — summarization RLHF | Demonstrated that preference learning **scales to text generation**, not just games/robotics. The SFT → RM → PPO pattern was solidified. |
| 3 | **2022** | Ouyang et al. — *InstructGPT*; Bai et al. — *Constitutional AI / RLAIF* | (i) Showed RLHF aligns *general-purpose* LLMs (175B GPT-3), making them instruction-following. (ii) Replaced *all* human harmlessness labels with AI feedback guided by a written constitution — solving the **scalability and cost** bottleneck. |
| 4 | **2023** | Rafailov et al. — *Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)* | PPO is **unstable, sample-hungry, and requires four co-trained models** (policy, RM, value, ref). DPO reduces alignment to a single supervised loss on preferences, achieving comparable or better alignment with far less compute. |
| 5 (bonus) | **2024** | KTO, IPO, SimPO, online DPO | Address *reward overfitting*, *length bias*, *distribution shift between training and deployment preferences*. |

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Step 1 — KL-regularized RLHF objective.** We wish to maximize the expected reward minus a KL-divergence penalty against a reference policy $\pi_{\text{ref}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_\theta\; J(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}_{x\sim\mathcal{D},\,y\sim\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x)}\!\left[r_\phi(x, y)\right] - \beta\,\mathbb{E}_x\!\left[\mathbb{D}_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x)\,\Vert\,\pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x)\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pointwise optimum.** Using the calculus of variations, the optimum is obtained when the gradient of $J$ is zero. For a fixed $x$, this yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^*(y \mid x) &= \frac{1}{Z(x)}\,\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)\,\exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\,r_\phi(x, y)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Z(x) = \sum_{y'} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y' \mid x)\exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\beta}r_\phi(x, y')\right)$.

**Step 3 — Invert to recover the reward in terms of the policy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_\phi(x, y) &= \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Bradley–Terry preference model.** Note that the partition function $Z(x)$ depends only on $x$, so it cancels in differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) &= \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — DPO loss.** Replace the optimal policy $\pi^*$ with the trainable $\pi_\theta$ and apply binary cross-entropy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) &= -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l)}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why DPO removes the reward model and RL loop:**
- The reward $r_\phi$ is now expressed purely as a *log-ratio* between the trainable and reference policies, scaled by $\beta$. No separate network $r_\phi$ is ever trained.
- Because the optimization reduces to binary cross-entropy on preference pairs, no policy-gradient sampler, no value baseline, and no importance-sampling ratio are needed. The PPO infrastructure is entirely bypassed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):** A common error is to write the DPO loss *without* the reference policy $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ in the denominator. **The reference policy is mandatory** — without it, the loss is unidentifiable (only reward *differences* matter, but DPO needs an absolute scale to set the implicit reward). Examiners will deduct **2 marks** if $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ is missing. Another frequent mistake is to claim DPO is "a new invention" — the correct answer is that DPO is a **mathematical re-derivation** of the RLHF optimum, which is precisely the point of the question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Evolution of RLHF**

- **RLHF definition** = SFT + reward modeling (on human preferences) + PPO fine-tuning with KL anchor.
- **Bradley–Terry model:** $P(y_w \succ y_l) = \sigma(r_\phi(y_w) - r_\phi(y_l))$; loss is **negative log-sigmoid** of the score gap.
- **KL anchor** is the *only* mechanism preventing the PPO policy from collapsing or reward-hacking.
- **PPO clipping** stabilizes the policy update: $\rho_t \in [1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon]$; typical $\epsilon = 0.2$.
- **Evolution timeline (must memorize in order):**
  1. **2017** — Christiano et al.: preference-based RL on games.
  2. **2019** — Ziegler / Böhm: preference learning on text.
  3. **2022** — Ouyang (InstructGPT) + Bai (Constitutional AI / RLAIF).
  4. **2023** — Rafailov (DPO): closed-form RM-free alignment.
  5. **2024+** — KTO, IPO, SimPO, online/iterative DPO.
- **DPO derivation** = (i) write KL-regularized RLHF objective, (ii) pointwise optimum, (iii) invert to express reward as a log-ratio, (iv) substitute into Bradley–Terry, (v) take negative log-likelihood.
- **DPO requires a frozen reference policy** $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ — typically the SFT model.
- **Bradley–Terry translation invariance:** only reward *differences* matter; constant offsets are unobservable.
- **RLAIF** replaces some human preferences with AI-generated feedback filtered by a *constitution* (a written rule set).
- **Modern variants comparison:** DPO is most stable; PPO is most flexible (online, reward shaping); KTO handles binary good/bad labels; SimPO handles length bias; online DPO handles distribution shift.
- **The four losses inside PPO** for LLMs: policy loss, value loss, KL penalty, entropy bonus — *all four must be mentioned* in 14-mark questions.
- **Limitation of RLHF:** reward hacking, distribution shift, high cost of human labeling, off-policy instability — these are the very problems DPO and its descendants try to solve.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** expect at least one question testing your ability to **derive DPO from the RLHF objective**. Practice the algebra in Section 3.3 of these notes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overcoming RLHF Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overcoming RLHF Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)** is a multi-stage alignment paradigm in which a Large Language Model (LLM) is fine-tuned to match complex human preferences by leveraging a learned **reward model (RM)** that approximates a latent human utility function. The standard RLHF pipeline consists of three sequential phases:

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** on demonstration data to obtain a reference policy $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.
2. **Reward Modeling (RM)** where human pairwise preference labels are used to train a scalar reward function $r_\phi(x, y)$.
3. **Policy Optimization** (typically PPO) where the SFT model is optimized against $r_\phi$ under a KL-divergence constraint against $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.

**"Overcoming RLHF Challenges"** refers to the consolidated body of techniques, mathematical reformulations, and architectural modifications proposed to mitigate the well-documented failure modes of classical RLHF — including **reward hacking**, **Goodhart's law**, **distribution shift**, **reward model overfitting**, **catastrophic forgetting**, and the prohibitive **cost of human annotation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECMT862 Module 3):** This topic directly addresses the *limitations* of the standard PPO-based RLHF objective and surveys **modern alternatives** such as **DPO, KTO, IPO, SimPO, ORPO, RLAIF, Constitutional AI, and Process Reward Models (PRM)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apprentice Chef" Intuition

Imagine you are training an apprentice chef in a Michelin-starred kitchen.

| RLHF Component | Real-World Analogue |
|---|---|
| **LLM (policy)** | The apprentice chef who proposes dishes. |
| **Human preference data** | The head chef tasting two dishes and saying *"This one is better"*. |
| **Reward Model (RM)** | A junior sous-chef who tries to *predict* the head chef's judgment from past tastings. |
| **PPO / PPO optimization** | The apprentice repeatedly cooks dishes that the sous-chef thinks the head chef will love. |
| **Reward hacking** | The apprentice starts serving *nothing but sugar and butter* because the sous-chef (reward model) has learned "the head chef likes sweetness" — but the head chef actually wanted a *balanced* meal. This is **Goodhart's Law**: *"When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure."* |
| **Overcoming RLHF** | The kitchen manager introduces: written recipes (Constitutional AI), customer surveys (RLAIF), multiple sous-chefs voting (RM ensembles), and recipe-based grading (Process Reward Models) — so the apprentice is judged on *how* they cook, not just *how sweet* the dish tastes. |

This analogy makes it immediately obvious that the "challenges" of RLHF are not merely engineering bugs — they are **fundamental alignment pathologies** rooted in proxy optimization.

### 1.3 Core Entities & Symbols (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)

> [!NOTE]
> The following symbols appear repeatedly across every RLHF reformulation and are *guaranteed* to be tested in Part A (3-mark) questions.

- $\pi_\theta$ — trainable policy (LLM) with parameters $\theta$.
- $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ — frozen reference policy (usually the SFT model).
- $r_\phi(x, y)$ — learned reward model with parameters $\phi$, mapping prompt $x$ and response $y$ to a scalar.
- $D_{\text{KL}}$ — Kullback–Leibler divergence — regularizes $\pi_\theta$ from drifting too far from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.
- $\sigma$ — logistic sigmoid function: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.
- $\beta$ — KL penalty coefficient controlling alignment strength (lower $\beta$ = more optimization, higher risk of reward hacking).
- $y_w, y_l$ — *winning* and *losing* responses in a preference pair $(x, y_w, y_l)$.

### 1.4 Primary Failure Modes of Classical RLHF (The "Seven Sins")

1. **Reward Hacking / Goodhart's Law** — the policy exploits weaknesses in the proxy RM.
2. **Reward Model Overfitting / Misgeneralization** — RM performs poorly on out-of-distribution prompts.
3. **Distribution Shift** — policy drifts into regions where RM is unreliable.
4. **Human Labeler Noise & Disagreement** — annotators disagree; ground truth is fuzzy.
5. **Catastrophic Forgetting** — model loses general capabilities (e.g., MMLU drops after RLHF).
6. **Prohibitive Cost & Scalability** — human labeling does not scale to frontier models.
7. **Hallucination Persistence** — RM cannot reliably detect factual errors; fluency is rewarded over truth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Canonical RLHF Objective (Recap)

The classical PPO-based RLHF objective is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RLHF}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \pi_\theta}\left[ r_\phi(x, y) \right] + \beta \, D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x) \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x)\right)
$$

The closed-form optimal solution (Rafailov et al., 2023) is:

$$
\pi^*(y\mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x) \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r_\phi(x, y)\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This closed-form is the *crux* of DPO — the entire Direct Preference Optimization framework is derived from rearranging this single equation. **This derivation will appear in the exam.**

### 2.2 Why Standard RLHF Fails — The Pathology Map

| # | Challenge | Root Cause | Symptom Observed in Practice |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reward Hacking** | RM is a proxy, not the true utility | Verbose, sycophantic, low-quality but high-RM-score outputs |
| 2 | **Goodhart's Law** | Optimizing a metric too hard | "Reward over-optimization" curve flattens or inverts |
| 3 | **Distribution Shift** | On-policy data is OOD for RM | RM accuracy collapses on $\pi_\theta$-generated samples |
| 4 | **Mode Collapse** | PPO + KL penalty can be unstable | Outputs become near-identical across diverse prompts |
| 5 | **KL Drift / Catastrophic Forgetting** | Excessive policy deviation from $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ | Drop in MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval post-RLHF |
| 6 | **Annotator Noise** | Human labels are inconsistent | RM trained on noisy labels underfits true preferences |
| 7 | **Cost & Latency** | Requires PPO + 4 LLMs (policy, ref, RM, critic) in memory | Compute-prohibitive for academic / small-lab use |
| 8 | **Hallucination Persistence** | RM rarely penalizes plausible-but-false content | Fluent fabrications get high RM scores |

### 2.3 Modern Solutions — A High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU 2024 Module 3 examination. Every method below is likely to appear as a Part B (14-mark) question.

| Method | Core Idea | Key Formula/Object | Eliminates |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DPO** (Direct Preference Optimization) | Re-parameterize reward from closed-form solution; do *regression* on preferences | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\log\sigma(\beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w\mid x)} - \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l\mid x)})$ | Reward model, PPO, value head |
| **IPO** (Identity Preference Optimization) | Add a squared-loss regularizer to prevent DPO overfitting on deterministic preferences | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{IPO}} = (\log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w)} - \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l)} - \frac{1}{2\beta})^2$ | Reward hacking in deterministic settings |
| **KTO** (Kahneman–Tversky Optimization) | Use *thumbs-up/thumbs-down* (binary) labels instead of pairwise | Uses prospect-theory value function $v(\cdot)$ with reference point | Cost of pairwise labeling |
| **SimPO** (Simple Preference Optimization) | Drop the reference model; use length-normalized log-prob margin | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SimPO}} = -\log\sigma(\frac{\beta}{\vert y_w\vert}\log\pi_\theta(y_w) - \frac{\beta}{\vert y_l\vert}\log\pi_\theta(y_l) - \gamma)$ | Need for $\pi_{\text{ref}}$; length bias |
| **ORPO** (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization) | Combine SFT and preference optimization via odds ratio penalty | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ORPO}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}} - \lambda \log\sigma(\log\frac{\text{odds}_\theta(y_w)}{\text{odds}_\theta(y_l)})$ | Need for separate SFT stage |
| **RLAIF** (RL from AI Feedback) | Replace human preferences with LLM-generated preferences | LLM-as-a-judge produces $(y_w, y_l)$ pairs | Human annotation cost |
| **Constitutional AI (CAI)** | Replace human harm labels with *principles*; self-critique loop | $\pi_{\text{revise}} = \text{LLM}(\text{principles}, \pi_{\text{critique}})$ | Subjective human labels for safety |
| **Process Reward Models (PRM)** | Reward *each reasoning step*, not just the final answer | $r_\phi = \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_\phi(x, y_{1:t})$ | Reward hacking on long-CoT tasks |
| **Iterative DPO / Online DPO** | Re-sample from $\pi_\theta$, re-label, retrain | Loop: $\pi_{k+1} = \text{DPO}(\pi_k, \text{new pairs})$ | Distribution shift |
| **Reward Model Ensembles** | Average $K$ independent RMs | $r_{\text{ens}}(x,y) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{\phi_k}(x,y)$ | RM overfitting |
| **Best-of-N Rejection Sampling** | Sample $N$ responses, pick highest RM score, fine-tune | $\pi^* \approx \arg\max_\pi \mathbb{E}[r(\hat{y})]$ | PPO instability |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

These techniques are not academic curiosities — they are **deployed in production**:

- **DPO / SimPO** are used in **Meta's Llama-3-Chat**, **Mistral-7B-Instruct**, and **Hugging Face TRL**.
- **Constitutional AI** powers **Anthropic's Claude**.
- **Process Reward Models** are the backbone of **OpenAI o1 / o3**'s reasoning alignment.
- **RLAIF** is used by **Google DeepMind** in Gemini safety tuning.

For a KTU engineering student, this means mastering these techniques is a **direct industry-readiness signal**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every derivation below is *fully expanded*. No step is skipped. The DPO derivation in particular is the most likely 14-mark Part B question on this topic.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From RLHF Objective to Closed-Form Optimal Policy

We start with the KL-regularized RLHF objective:

$$
\max_\pi \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, \, y \sim \pi(\cdot\mid x)} \left[ r_\phi(x, y) \right] - \beta \, D_{\text{KL}}\!\left( \pi(\cdot\mid x) \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x) \right)
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the KL divergence explicitly.**

$$
D_{\text{KL}}\!\left( \pi(\cdot\mid x) \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x) \right) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi(\cdot\mid x)} \left[ \log \frac{\pi(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expanded KL into the objective.**

$$
\max_\pi \; \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi(\cdot\mid x)} \left[ r_\phi(x, y) - \beta \log \frac{\pi(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the partition function $Z(x)$ and the variational argument.**

We know from the calculus of variations / exponential-family argument that the maximizer of an objective of the form $\mathbb{E}_\pi[f(y) - \beta \log \pi/\pi_{\text{ref}}]$ is:

$$
\pi^*(y\mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x) \exp\!\left( \frac{1}{\beta} r_\phi(x, y) \right)
$$

where the partition function $Z(x)$ ensures normalization:

$$
Z(x) = \sum_{y} \pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x) \exp\!\left( \frac{1}{\beta} r_\phi(x, y) \right)
$$

This is the **Gibbs distribution** form. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — DPO Loss from the Closed-Form Policy

**Step 1 — Invert the closed-form to express the reward in terms of $\pi_\theta$ and $\pi_{\text{ref}}$.**

Take $\log$ of both sides of the optimality condition:

$$
\log \pi^*(y\mid x) = \log \pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x) + \frac{1}{\beta} r_\phi(x, y) - \log Z(x)
$$

Solve for the reward:

$$
r_\phi(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x)
$$

**Step 2 — Plug this reward form into the Bradley–Terry pairwise preference model.**

The standard preference model is:

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma\!\left( r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l) \right)
$$

Substituting the re-parameterized reward (and noting that the $\beta \log Z(x)$ terms cancel out):

$$
P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma\!\left( \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_w\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w\mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_l\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l\mid x)} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Replace the optimal $\pi^*$ with the trainable $\pi_\theta$ and write the negative log-likelihood loss.**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \log \sigma\!\left( \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w\mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l\mid x)} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalently, define the implicit reward $r_\theta(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)}$.**

Then the DPO loss is simply cross-entropy on whether $r_\theta(x, y_w) > r_\theta(x, y_l)$. ✔

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight (exam-favorite):** DPO eliminates the reward model, the value/critic network, and the online sampling loop. Only the *policy* and a *frozen reference model* are needed. The memory footprint drops from 4 LLMs (policy + ref + RM + critic) to **2 LLMs** (policy + ref).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Reward Hacking Bound (Bradley–Terry Miscalibration)

A useful analytical result: under DPO, the *implicit* reward can drift unboundedly because the reference model is frozen only at initialization. A regularized variant adds a squared loss penalty (this is the **IPO objective**):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{IPO}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \left( \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w\mid x)} - \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l\mid x)} - \frac{1}{2\beta} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Define the policy log-ratio $h_\theta(x, y) = \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)}$.**

**Step 2 — Rewrite the IPO loss in $h$-space.**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{IPO}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \left( h_\theta(x, y_w) - h_\theta(x, y_l) - \frac{1}{2\beta} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Gradient with respect to $\theta$ is now bounded (Lipschitz)**, which prevents the unbounded reward growth that causes DPO to overfit on deterministic preference pairs. ✔

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Process Reward Model (PRM) Decomposition

For chain-of-thought reasoning with $T$ intermediate steps, the outcome-supervised RM gives a single final reward $r_\phi(x, y_{1:T})$, which is *credit-assignment-hostile* (the model cannot tell which step caused a correct/incorrect answer). The PRM decomposes the reward as a sum over step-level scores:

$$
r_{\text{PRM}}(x, y_{1:T}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_\phi(x, y_{1:t})
$$

For Monte-Carlo estimation, each step's reward is approximated as:

$$
r_\phi(x, y_{1:t}) \approx \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{y}_{t+1:T} \sim \pi}\left[ R(x, y_{1:T}) \,\big|\, y_{1:t} \right]
$$

where $R(\cdot)$ is the terminal correctness signal. This **step-level credit assignment** is what enables models like OpenAI o1 to refine reasoning without hacking the final-answer RM. ✔

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Method vs. Challenge

| Challenge ↓ \ Method → | DPO | IPO | KTO | SimPO | ORPO | RLAIF | CAI | PRM | Iterative DPO | RM Ensemble |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reward Hacking | △ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — | △ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Goodhart's Law | △ | ✔ | △ | ✔ | △ | — | △ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Distribution Shift | △ | — | — | — | — | — | — | △ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Annotator Noise | △ | ✔ | ✔ | — | △ | ✔ | △ | — | — | ✔ |
| Catastrophic Forgetting | △ | △ | △ | △ | △ | — | — | — | — | — |
| Human-Label Cost | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — | — | — |
| Hallucination | — | — | — | — | — | △ | △ | ✔ | △ | △ |

Legend: **✔** = directly mitigates, **△** = partially mitigates, **—** = does not directly address.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Unified Architecture of RLHF & Its Modern Replacements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Demonstration Data] --> B[Stage 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning SFT]
    B --> C[pi_ref Frozen Reference Policy]
    B --> D[pi_theta Trainable Policy]
    E[Human Preference Pairs y_w y_l] --> F[Stage 2: Reward Modeling]
    F --> G[Reward Model r_phi x y]
    G --> H{Stage 3: Optimization Choice}
    H --> I[Classical PPO RLHF]
    H --> J[DPO Direct Preference Opt]
    H --> K[IPO Identity Preference Opt]
    H --> L[SimPO Simple Preference Opt]
    H --> M[ORPO Odds Ratio Preference Opt]
    H --> N[KTO Kahneman Tversky Opt]
    H --> O[RLAIF AI Generated Preferences]
    H --> P[Constitutional AI Self Critique]
    H --> Q[PRM Process Reward Model]
    I --> R[Aligned LLM]
    J --> R
    K --> R
    L --> R
    M --> R
    N --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 The DPO Pipeline (No Reward Model, No Critic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Preference Dataset x y_w y_l] --> D2[Sample Batch]
    D2 --> D3[Forward pi_theta y_w and y_l]
    D2 --> D4[Forward pi_ref y_w and y_l]
    D3 --> D5[Compute log ratio beta]
    D4 --> D5
    D5 --> D6[Implicit Reward r_theta]
    D6 --> D7[Bradley Terry Loss]
    D7 --> D8[Backprop into pi_theta only]
    D8 --> D9[Updated Policy]
    D9 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Constitutional AI Self-Critique Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Initial Response y_0 from pi_theta] --> C2[Self Critique using Principles]
    C2 --> C3[Identify Violations]
    C3 --> C4[Self Revise y_1]
    C4 --> C5{Principle Satisfied?}
    C5 -- No --> C2
    C5 -- Yes --> C6[Final Revised Response y_T]
    C6 --> C7[Pair y_0 y_T for Preference Learning]
    C7 --> C8[Train via DPO or RLAIF]
```

### 4.4 The Reward Hacking / Goodhart Failure Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[True Human Utility U x y] --> G2[Observed Preference P y_w gt y_l]
    G2 --> G3[Train Reward Model r_phi]
    G3 --> G4[Policy pi_theta optimizes r_phi]
    G4 --> G5[Policy drifts to r_phi high regions]
    G5 --> G6[RM miscalibrated on OOD samples]
    G6 --> G7[Reward Hacking: r_phi high but U low]
    G7 --> G8[Solutions: DPO IPO RLAIF PRM Ensemble]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Method Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[RLHF Core Challenge] --> R1[Proxy Reward Issues]
    R0 --> R2[Compute and Cost Issues]
    R0 --> R3[Alignment Safety Issues]
    R1 --> R4[Reward Hacking]
    R1 --> R5[RM Overfitting]
    R4 --> S1[IPO squared loss]
    R4 --> S2[PRM step rewards]
    R4 --> S3[RM Ensemble averaging]
    R5 --> S4[Iterative DPO refresh]
    R5 --> S5[Best of N rejection sampling]
    R2 --> S6[DPO no RM no critic]
    R2 --> S7[SimPO no ref model]
    R2 --> S8[ORPO combined SFT and pref]
    R3 --> S9[RLAIF AI feedback]
    R3 --> S10[Constitutional AI principles]
    R3 --> S11[KTO binary labels]
```
