topics.title: Bias and Fairness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bias and Fairness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Bias in Large Language Models?

In the context of Large Language Models (LLMs), **bias** refers to systematic, repeatable errors in a model's outputs that produce prejudiced, unfair, or skewed results favoring or discriminating against particular groups, attributes, or perspectives. These biases typically reflect patterns present in the training data and become encoded into the model's learned parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Bias in LLMs is defined as a **statistical skew** in the conditional probability distribution $P_\theta(y \mid x)$ (the model's predicted output $y$ given input $x$ under learned parameters $\theta$) that systematically deviates from the *true* or *ideal* fair distribution due to spurious correlations learned from training corpora containing societal, demographic, or representation-based prejudices.

Mathematically, a model exhibits bias if for a protected attribute $A$ (e.g., gender, race, religion):

$$P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a_i) \neq P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a_j) \quad \text{for some } i \neq j$$

Where $\hat{Y}$ is the predicted outcome, $A$ is a protected attribute, and $a_i$, $a_j$ are distinct values of that attribute.

## 1.2 What is Fairness?

**Fairness** is the corresponding ethical and mathematical property that ensures a model's decisions or predictions do not unjustly favor or disadvantage individuals or groups based on protected characteristics. A fair LLM produces equitable outputs across demographic subgroups while maintaining predictive utility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Fairness in LLMs (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Group Fairness** – Equal treatment across demographic groups.
> 2. **Individual Fairness** – Similar individuals receive similar predictions.
> 3. **Counterfactual Fairness** – Predictions remain the same in a counterfactual world where the protected attribute is flipped.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Mirror Analogy:** Imagine an LLM as a giant mirror that reflects the internet's text. If the internet contains 100 books praising apples and only 1 book about oranges, the mirror (LLM) will overwhelmingly reflect "apples are best." This reflection distortion is **bias**. Asking the mirror to balance the reflection fairly — giving equal, accurate, and respectful representation to all fruits — is **fairness**.

A simpler real-world analogy: think of bias as a *tilted weighing scale*. No matter what you place on it, the heavier side always wins. **Fairness** is the act of re-leveling that scale so that legitimate differences (like actual mass) determine the outcome — not the tilt.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Protected Attribute ($A$)** | A sensitive variable (gender, race, age, religion) that should not unjustifiably influence predictions. |
| **Privileged Group** | The group that historically or systemically receives favorable outcomes. |
| **Unprivileged Group** | The group subject to systematic disadvantage in model outputs. |
| **Disparate Impact** | When a model's outcome rates differ significantly across groups. |
| **Representational Harm** | Bias in how groups are portrayed (e.g., stereotyping). |
| **Allocational Harm** | Bias in how resources/opportunities are distributed. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Bias in LLMs (The Bias Pipeline)

Bias enters an LLM at multiple stages. KTU examiners frequently test this pipeline.

### 2.1.1 Data-Level Bias
The training corpus is the largest contributor. Sources include:
- **Selection Bias** – The corpus is not a uniform sample of human knowledge (e.g., over-representation of English, Western perspectives).
- **Historical Bias** – Pre-existing societal prejudices frozen in older text.
- **Sampling Bias** – Under-representation of dialects, minority languages, and marginalized communities.
- **Annotation Bias** – Human annotators' own prejudices leak into labeled datasets (RLHF, instruction tuning).

### 2.1.2 Model-Level Bias
- **Architectural Bias** – Transformers have positional and attention biases (recency bias, attention sinks).
- **Optimization Bias** – Loss functions optimize for average likelihood, ignoring minority tails.
- **Inductive Bias** – Pre-training objectives (next-token prediction) inherently favor majority patterns.

### 2.1.3 Deployment-Level Bias
- **Prompt-Induced Bias** – User prompts can elicit biased completions via leading questions.
- **Feedback Loop Bias** – Model outputs influence real-world text, which is later scraped back as training data.
- **Interface Bias** – UI choices (suggested follow-ups, default personas) reinforce stereotypes.

## 2.2 Categories of Bias Manifestation

- **Gender Bias** – Associating "nurse" with "she" and "engineer" with "he".
- **Racial/Ethnic Bias** – Generating negative associations for specific ethnic groups.
- **Cultural Bias** – Favoring Western norms, holidays, foods, and history.
- **Religious Bias** – Stereotyping or demeaning particular faiths.
- **Age Bias** – Depicting older adults as technologically incompetent.
- **Socioeconomic Bias** – Associating low-income descriptors with negative traits.
- **Confirmation Bias** – Sycophancy (model agreeing with the user's stated belief).

## 2.3 Fairness Metrics — The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following fairness metrics are examinable. Memorize the *formulas* and the *thresholds*.

### 2.3.1 Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity)

A model satisfies **Demographic Parity** if the probability of a positive prediction is independent of the protected attribute $A$:

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = 1) \quad \forall \, a \in A$$

The **Disparate Impact (DI)** ratio quantifies violation:

$$DI = \frac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a_{\text{unpriv}})}{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a_{\text{priv}})}$$

A model is considered fair under the **80% Rule** if $DI \geq 0.8$.

### 2.3.2 Equalized Odds

A model satisfies **Equalized Odds** if the true positive rate (TPR) and false positive rate (FPR) are equal across groups:

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) \quad \forall \, a$$

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 0, A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 0) \quad \forall \, a$$

### 2.3.3 Equal Opportunity

A relaxation of Equalized Odds requiring only TPR equality:

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, A = a_i) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, A = a_j)$$

### 2.3.4 Predictive Parity (Calibration)

$$P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = 1, A = a) = P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = 1) \quad \forall \, a$$

### 2.3.5 Counterfactual Fairness

For individual $i$ with attributes $(X_i, A_i)$, a model is counterfactually fair if:

$$P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a} \mid X = x, A = a) = P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'} \mid X = x, A = a)$$

for all values $a'$ of the protected attribute.

## 2.4 Consolidated KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Reference Table** — Use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve markdown table syntax.

| Metric | Formula | Ideal Value | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|-------------|----------------|
| **Demographic Parity Difference** | $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \vert$ | $0$ | $0$ = perfectly equal |
| **Disparate Impact Ratio** | $\dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a_{\text{unpriv}})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a_{\text{priv}})}$ | $1$ | $\geq 0.8$ = legally fair |
| **Equal Opportunity Difference** | $\vert TPR_{a=0} - TPR_{a=1} \vert$ | $0$ | Equal true positive rates |
| **Average Odds Difference** | $\dfrac{1}{2}\left[ \vert FPR_{a=0} - FPR_{a=1} \vert + \vert TPR_{a=0} - TPR_{a=1} \vert \right]$ | $0$ | Combined FPR & TPR gap |
| **Theil Index** | $T = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{b_i}{\bar{b}} \ln\dfrac{b_i}{\bar{b}}$ | $0$ | Inequality measure |
| **KL-Divergence Bias Score** | $D_{KL}(P_{a=0} \Vert P_{a=1})$ | $0$ | Distribution shift cost |
| **StereoSet Score (SS)** | $SS = \dfrac{1}{2}\left( s_{\text{stereo}} + s_{\text{anti}} \right)$ | $0.5$ ideal | Stereotype association |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Relevance

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Hiring Tools** (e.g., resume screeners) must pass Equal Opportunity audits under EEOC guidelines.
> - **Healthcare LLMs** must satisfy Equalized Odds to ensure diagnostic equity.
> - **Credit Scoring Models** are legally required to maintain Disparate Impact $\geq 0.8$.
> - **Content Moderation LLMs** need Counterfactual Fairness to avoid political/religious skew.

Bias–Fairness analysis is therefore not merely academic — it is a **regulatory, ethical, and commercial necessity** for any deployed LLM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)

We derive the DPD from first principles for a binary classifier with protected attribute $A \in \{0, 1\}$ and prediction $\hat{Y} \in \{0, 1\}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Selection Rate

The selection (positive prediction) rate for group $a$ is defined as:

$$SR_a = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = \frac{\#\{\hat{y}_i = 1 \mid a_i = a\}}{N_a}$$

where $N_a$ is the number of samples in group $a$.

### Step 2 — Expand Using Bayes' Theorem

$$SR_a = \sum_{y \in \{0,1\}} P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = a) \, P(Y = y \mid A = a)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Difference

$$DPD = \vert SR_1 - SR_0 \vert = \left\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) \right\vert$$

### Step 4 — Fairness Condition

The model is *Demographically Parity Fair* if and only if:

$$DPD = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad SR_1 = SR_0$$

> A practical tolerance threshold used in industry: $DPD \leq 0.05$ (5% rule).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Disparate Impact from Confusion Matrix

Suppose we have a 2×2 confusion matrix breakdown across two demographic groups.

### Step 1 — Build Per-Group Confusion Matrices

For privileged group ($A = 0$):

$$CM_0 = \begin{bmatrix} TN_0 & FP_0 \\ FN_0 & TP_0 \end{bmatrix}$$

For unprivileged group ($A = 1$):

$$CM_1 = \begin{bmatrix} TN_1 & FP_1 \\ FN_1 & TP_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Selection Rates from Matrices

$$SR_0 = \frac{TP_0 + FP_0}{N_0}, \qquad SR_1 = \frac{TP_1 + FP_1}{N_1}$$

### Step 3 — Disparate Impact Ratio

$$DI = \frac{SR_1}{SR_0} = \frac{(TP_1 + FP_1)/N_1}{(TP_0 + FP_0)/N_0}$$

### Step 4 — 80% Rule Application

A model is legally compliant (US EEOC 4/5ths rule) if:

$$DI \geq 0.8 \quad \text{and} \quad DI \leq 1.25$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Odds Difference (AOD)

### Step 1 — Define TPR and FPR Per Group

$$TPR_a = \frac{TP_a}{TP_a + FN_a}, \qquad FPR_a = \frac{FP_a}{FP_a + TN_a}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Rate Differences

$$\Delta_{TPR} = TPR_0 - TPR_1, \qquad \Delta_{FPR} = FPR_0 - FPR_1$$

### Step 3 — Average Odds Difference

$$AOD = \frac{1}{2}\left( \vert \Delta_{TPR} \vert + \vert \Delta_{FPR} \vert \right) = \frac{1}{2}\left( \vert TPR_0 - TPR_1 \vert + \vert FPR_0 - FPR_1 \vert \right)$$

### Step 4 — Fairness Condition

$$AOD = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad TPR_0 = TPR_1 \;\; \text{and} \;\; FPR_0 = FPR_1$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** A hiring classifier screens 1000 resumes. For Group A (privileged) and Group B (unprivileged), the following confusion matrices are observed:

**Group A (Privileged):**
- $TP_A = 80$, $FP_A = 20$, $FN_A = 40$, $TN_A = 360$ → $N_A = 500$

**Group B (Unprivileged):**
- $TP_B = 50$, $FP_B = 30$, $FN_B = 70$, $TN_B = 350$ → $N_B = 500$

### Step 1 — Compute Selection Rates

$$SR_A = \frac{80 + 20}{500} = \frac{100}{500} = 0.20$$

$$SR_B = \frac{50 + 30}{500} = \frac{80}{500} = 0.16$$

### Step 2 — Disparate Impact

$$DI = \frac{SR_B}{SR_A} = \frac{0.16}{0.20} = 0.80$$

### Step 3 — Demographic Parity Difference

$$DPD = \vert SR_A - SR_B \vert = \vert 0.20 - 0.16 \vert = 0.04$$

### Step 4 — Average Odds Difference

$$TPR_A = \frac{80}{80+40} = 0.667, \quad TPR_B = \frac{50}{50+70} = 0.417$$

$$FPR_A = \frac{20}{20+360} = 0.0526, \quad FPR_B = \frac{30}{30+350} = 0.0789$$

$$AOD = \frac{1}{2}\left( \vert 0.667 - 0.417 \vert + \vert 0.0526 - 0.0789 \vert \right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.250 + 0.0263) = 0.138$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|--------|-------|---------|
| $DI = 0.80$ | Meets 80% rule threshold | ✅ Pass |
| $DPD = 0.04$ | $< 0.05$ | ✅ Pass |
| $AOD = 0.138$ | Substantial gap | ⚠️ Borderline |

The model passes group fairness marginally but shows **clear TPR disparity**, indicating unprivileged group candidates with true qualification are being missed at a higher rate.

---

## 3.5 Bias Mitigation Techniques — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Stage | Technique | Mathematical Idea | Trade-off |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Pre-processing** | Re-sampling, Re-weighting | Rebalance $P(A, X)$ | May distort true distribution |
| **Pre-processing** | Data Augmentation (Counterfactual) | Generate $(X, A=\bar{a})$ | Synthetic data may be unrealistic |
| **In-processing** | Adversarial Debiasing | Minimize $L_{\text{task}} - \lambda L_{\text{adv}}$ | Training instability |
| **In-processing** | Fair Representation Learning | Encode $Z = f(X)$ s.t. $Z \perp A$ | Information loss |
| **Post-processing** | Threshold Adjustment | Choose $t_a$ per group for Equalized Odds | Reduces calibration |
| **Post-processing** | Output Reranking | Re-rank by fairness score | Latency cost |
| **Prompt-level** | Debiasing Prompts | Add fairness instructions in context | Surface-level only |
| **Fine-tuning** | RLHF with Fairness Reward | Add fairness term to reward model | Reward hacking risk |

The composite objective in adversarial debiasing:

$$\min_\theta \max_\phi \; L_{\text{task}}(\theta) - \lambda \, L_{\text{adv}}(\phi, \theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the predictor, $\phi$ is the adversary trying to predict $A$ from $\hat{Y}$, and $\lambda$ balances fairness vs utility.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The LLM Bias Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corpus Collection] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Pre-training Phase]
    C --> D[Fine-tuning and RLHF]
    D --> E[Evaluation Phase]
    E --> F[Deployment Phase]
    F --> G[User Feedback Loop]
    G --> A

    A1[Selection Bias] -.injects.-> A
    B1[Sampling Bias] -.injects.-> B
    C1[Architectural Bias] -.injects.-> C
    D1[Annotation Bias] -.injects.-> D
    E1[Benchmark Bias] -.injects.-> E
    F1[Prompt Induced Bias] -.injects.-> F
    G1[Feedback Loop Bias] -.injects.-> G
```

## 4.2 Fairness Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Protected Attribute A] --> D[Dataset Split]
    X[Input Features X] --> D
    D --> M[Trained LLM]
    M --> Y[Predictions Yhat]
    Y --> FM[Fairness Metrics Module]
    P --> FM
    Y --> FM
    FM --> R1[Demographic Parity]
    FM --> R2[Equalized Odds]
    FM --> R3[Counterfactual Fairness]
    FM --> R4[Disparate Impact]
    R1 --> AUD[Audit Report]
    R2 --> AUD
    R3 --> AUD
    R4 --> AUD
    AUD --> DEC{Compliant?}
    DEC -- Yes --> OK[Deploy with Monitoring]
    DEC -- No --> MIT[Apply Mitigation]
    MIT --> M
```

## 4.3 Mitigation Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identify Bias Type] --> Q1{Group vs Individual?}
    Q1 -- Group --> Q2{Data Access?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P1[Reweight Resample]
    Q2 -- No --> P2[Adversarial Debiasing]
    Q1 -- Individual --> Q3{Needs Counterfactual?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Counterfactual Data Augmentation]
    Q3 -- No --> P4[Fair Representation Learning]
    P1 --> V[Re evaluate Metrics]
    P2 --> V
    P3 --> V
    P4 --> V
    V --> PASS{Metrics within Threshold?}
    PASS -- Yes --> END[Deploy]
    PASS -- No --> MIT[Post processing Threshold Tuning]
    MIT --> END
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fair LLM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        UQ[User Query]
        CTX[Context Window]
    end
    subgraph FB[Fairness Block]
        DP[Demographic Parity Filter]
        EO[Equalized Odds Filter]
        CF[Counterfactual Fairness Filter]
        ST[Stereotype Detector]
    end
    subgraph CR[Core Reasoning Engine]
        LLM[Transformer Backbone]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Block]
        RG[Response Generator]
        AU[Auditor Module]
        FB2[Feedback Logger]
    end
    UQ --> LLM
    CTX --> LLM
    LLM --> DP
    DP --> EO
    EO --> CF
    CF --> ST
    ST --> RG
    RG --> AU
    AU -- Fail --> FB2
    AU -- Pass --> USER[Final Output to User]
    FB2 --> FT[Continuous Fine tuning]
    FT --> LLM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU):** When asked to "draw a block diagram of a fair LLM pipeline," use the architecture above as a reference. The **Auditor Module** is a frequent board-exam focal point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define **bias** in the context of Large Language Models. List any four sources of bias.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Bias in LLMs is a systematic skew in the model's output distribution $P(\hat{Y}\mid X)$ caused by spurious correlations learned from training data, leading to prejudiced or unfair predictions.
>
> **Four Sources (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Selection Bias** – Non-uniform sampling of training corpora.
> 2. **Annotation Bias** – Human labeler prejudice in fine-tuning datasets.
> 3. **Architectural Bias** – Inductive biases of transformer attention.
> 4. **Feedback Loop Bias** – Model outputs polluting future training data.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is **Demographic Parity**? Write the formula for Disparate Impact.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Demographic Parity (1.5 Marks):** A fairness criterion requiring that the probability of a positive prediction is independent of the protected attribute:
> $$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = 1) \quad \forall \, a$$
>
> **Disparate Impact Formula (1.5 Marks):**
> $$DI = \frac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a_{\text{unpriv}})}{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a_{\text{priv}})}$$
> A model is considered fair under the **80% rule** if $0.8 \leq DI \leq 1.25$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of bias** that can manifest in Large Language Models, with at least five examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the **bias mitigation techniques** used at the pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing stages. Provide one example technique for each stage. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Bias in LLMs manifests in two broad forms: **representational harm** (how groups are portrayed) and **allocational harm** (how resources are distributed).

**Types of Bias (5 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Gender Bias** — LLMs associate professions with specific genders (e.g., "doctor → he", "nurse → she"). Measurable via the **WinoBias** and **StereoSet** benchmarks.

2. **Racial/Ethnic Bias** — Models produce negative sentiment scores or stereotypical descriptions for specific ethnic groups, detectable via **Bias-in-Bios** and **CrowS-Pairs** datasets.

3. **Cultural Bias** — Western-centric knowledge dominates. For example, holidays like Thanksgiving are detailed while Diwali is briefly described.

4. **Religious Bias** — Models may produce derogatory completions for minority religions. Tested using **ReligionBias** dataset.

5. **Age Bias** — Older individuals are stereotyped as technologically incompetent. Detected via age-conditioned completions.

6. **Socioeconomic Bias** — Lower-income descriptors correlate with negative traits.

7. **Confirmation Bias (Sycophancy)** — Models agree with the user's stated belief rather than providing objective answers.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Understanding bias types is essential for designing targeted mitigation strategies and ethical evaluation pipelines.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

| Stage | Technique | Description (Marks) |
|-------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Pre-processing** | Re-weighting / Re-sampling | Adjust sample weights so privileged group contributes proportionally less. (2 Marks) |
| **Pre-processing** | Counterfactual Data Augmentation | Generate $(X, A=\bar{a})$ samples to balance attribute occurrences. (1 Mark) |
| **In-processing** | Adversarial Debiasing | Train predictor against an adversary trying to recover $A$ from $\hat{Y}$. Objective: $\min_\theta \max_\phi L_{\text{task}} - \lambda L_{\text{adv}}$. (2 Marks) |
| **In-processing** | Fair Representation Learning | Encode input as $Z = f(X)$ such that $Z \perp A$ statistically. (1 Mark) |
| **Post-processing** | Threshold Tuning per Group | Choose decision threshold $t_a$ per group to satisfy Equalized Odds. (1 Mark) |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** No single technique eliminates all bias; a layered pipeline combining multiple stages is most effective.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Define **fairness** in LLMs. Explain **group fairness**, **individual fairness**, and **counterfactual fairness** with appropriate formulations. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A binary classifier produces the following per-group confusion matrices. Compute the **Disparate Impact**, **Demographic Parity Difference**, and **Average Odds Difference**. Comment on whether the model is fair. (7 Marks)

- **Group 0 (Privileged):** $TP = 90$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 370$ → $N_0 = 500$
- **Group 1 (Unprivileged):** $TP = 60$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 60$, $TN = 360$ → $N_1 = 500$

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Fairness Definition (1 Mark):**
Fairness is the property that an LLM's outputs do not unjustly favor or harm individuals/groups based on protected characteristics.

**Group Fairness (2 Marks):**
Requires that aggregate outcome statistics are equal across demographic groups. Captured by metrics like Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds, and Predictive Parity.

**Individual Fairness (2 Marks):**
Similar individuals must receive similar predictions. Formally, for a similarity metric $d(X_i, X_j)$:

$$d(X_i, X_j) \approx 0 \;\Rightarrow\; d(\hat{Y}_i, \hat{Y}_j) \approx 0$$

**Counterfactual Fairness (2 Marks):**
Predictions should remain the same in a counterfactual world where the protected attribute is changed. Formally:

$$P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a} \mid X = x, A = a) = P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'} \mid X = x, A = a)$$

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Selection Rates (1.5 Marks):**

$$SR_0 = \frac{TP_0 + FP_0}{N_0} = \frac{90 + 10}{500} = 0.20$$

$$SR_1 = \frac{TP_1 + FP_1}{N_1} = \frac{60 + 20}{500} = 0.16$$

**Step 2 — Disparate Impact (1.5 Marks):**

$$DI = \frac{SR_1}{SR_0} = \frac{0.16}{0.20} = 0.80$$

Meets the 80% rule threshold marginally.

**Step 3 — Demographic Parity Difference (1 Mark):**

$$DPD = \vert SR_0 - SR_1 \vert = \vert 0.20 - 0.16 \vert = 0.04$$

Within the 5% tolerance.

**Step 4 — Average Odds Difference (2 Marks):**

$$TPR_0 = \frac{90}{90+30} = 0.75, \quad TPR_1 = \frac{60}{60+60} = 0.50$$

$$FPR_0 = \frac{10}{10+370} = 0.0263, \quad FPR_1 = \frac{20}{20+360} = 0.0526$$

$$AOD = \frac{1}{2}\left( \vert 0.75 - 0.50 \vert + \vert 0.0263 - 0.0526 \vert \right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.25 + 0.0263) = 0.138$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**
While $DI$ and $DPD$ are within acceptable limits, the **TPR gap of 0.25** reveals a substantial **Equal Opportunity violation** — qualified unprivileged candidates are being missed at twice the rate of privileged ones. The model is **group-fair on aggregate but individually unfair on outcomes**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Demographic Parity with Equalized Odds** — they are *not* equivalent. Demographic Parity ignores ground truth; Equalized Odds conditions on it. (–2 marks typical deduction)
> 2. **Forgetting absolute value bars** in DPD and AOD formulas — sign matters for direction but the metric itself is non-negative. Use `\vert` in LaTeX.
> 3. **Omitting the 80% rule citation** when computing Disparate Impact. Always state the legal threshold.
> 4. **Mislabeling TPR vs FPR** in confusion matrix derivations. Memorize: TPR = TP / (TP + FN); FPR = FP / (FP + TN).
> 5. **Listing bias types without examples** — KTU examiners want at least one concrete instance per bias type for full marks.
> 6. **Skipping the "Comment on fairness"** step in numerical problems — even a one-line verdict is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Yield for KTU Module 3 Exam:**

- **Bias** is systematic skew in $P(\hat{Y}\mid X, A)$ due to training data or model architecture.
- **Fairness** is the absence of unjust discrimination based on protected attributes $A \in \{\text{gender, race, age, religion, ...}\}$.
- **Sources of bias** flow through: Corpus → Preprocessing → Pre-training → Fine-tuning → Deployment → Feedback → back to Corpus.
- **Demographic Parity** requires $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a)$ to be equal across $a$.
- **Disparate Impact** $DI = SR_{\text{unpriv}} / SR_{\text{priv}}$; threshold $\geq 0.8$ for compliance.
- **Equalized Odds** requires TPR *and* FPR equality across groups.
- **Equal Opportunity** relaxes Equalized Odds to TPR-only.
- **Counterfactual Fairness** requires invariance under protected attribute flips.
- **Bias mitigation stages**: Pre-processing (re-weight, augment), In-processing (adversarial, fair representations), Post-processing (threshold tuning, reranking).
- **AOD** is the average of absolute TPR and FPR gaps across groups.
- **Industry benchmarks**: WinoBias, StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, BBQ, Bias-in-Bios.
- **The 5% rule**: $DPD \leq 0.05$ is the practical industry tolerance.
- **The 80% rule (4/5ths rule)**: US EEOC standard for hiring/legal fairness.
- **Always compute at least TWO fairness metrics** — they are mathematically incompatible; reliance on one is insufficient.
- **Audit pipelines are mandatory** for any deployed LLM in healthcare, finance, hiring, and education.
- **No universal fairness** — fairness–accuracy trade-offs exist; choose metrics aligned with use case.
- **Rigorously draw at least one block diagram** in 14-mark answers (bias pipeline OR mitigation architecture).
- **RLHF can introduce sycophancy bias** — note this in any fine-tuning discussion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Toxicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toxicity in Large Language Models (LLMs)** refers to the generation of text that is **rude, disrespectful, harmful, offensive, threatening, or otherwise socially unacceptable**, which has the potential to cause a reader to disengage from a conversation or feel psychologically harmed. In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus, toxicity is treated as a *first-class alignment failure mode* arising from the unfiltered statistical nature of pre-training on web-scale corpora.

According to the widely cited Perspective API taxonomy (Jigsaw / Google), toxicity is formally classified into the following **sub-categories**:

| Sub-category | KTU 2024 Terminology | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Severe Toxicity** | Overt Hate / Harassment | Explicitly hateful, aggressive content |
| **Insult** | Personal Attack | Content demeaning an individual or group |
| **Profanity** | Lexical Violation | Use of swear words or vulgar language |
| **Threat** | Violent Intent | Implied or explicit threats of physical harm |
| **Identity Attack** | Targeted Bias | Discriminatory content based on race, gender, religion, etc. |
| **Sexually Explicit** | NSFW Content | Inappropriate or adult-themed language |
| **Flirtation** | Suggestive Tone | Unwanted romantic or sexual advances |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Perspective API):** Toxicity score $\tau \in [0, 1]$, where $\tau \geq 0.5$ is conventionally treated as the **toxicity threshold** $\tau_{th}$ for binary classification.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Unfiltered Newsstand":** Imagine a child raised in a library that contains *every newspaper ever printed* — including tabloids full of gossip, hate mail, conspiracy pamphlets, and rude letters to the editor. The child learns language by reading all of it. When asked a question, the child simply reproduces the statistical patterns it absorbed. Sometimes the answer is wise (the New York Times section); sometimes it is vile (the trash-mail section). **Toxicity in an LLM is exactly this problem** — the model is a brilliant mimic of *all* human text, including the dark corners. Mitigation strategies are like hiring an *editor* to guide the mimic away from the tabloid racks.

## 1.3 Why Toxicity is a First-Class LLM Problem

LLMs are pre-trained on corpora such as CommonCrawl, OpenWebText, and Reddit, which inherently contain toxic content. The autoregressive objective $\max_\theta P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ does not penalize toxicity — it simply rewards *fluent continuation*. Hence, **toxicity is a direct consequence of the training objective, not a bug**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Toxicity (Operational Pipeline)

The toxicity pipeline in an LLM can be decomposed into three causal layers:

1. **Data Layer (Pre-training)**
   - Unfiltered web crawls contain slurs, hate speech, NSFW text.
   - Statistical co-occurrence: toxic words appear near neutral words, so the model "absorbs" them as plausible completions.

2. **Fine-tuning Layer (Alignment)**
   - Even with RLHF, residual toxicity persists because reward models cannot capture every subtle form.
   - *Sycophancy* and *role-play jailbreaks* (e.g., "DAN") can surface toxic outputs.

3. **Decoding Layer (Inference)**
   - Greedy / beam search picks the highest-probability next token, which can be toxic.
   - Nucleus sampling and temperature scaling were *not* designed for safety, only diversity.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Mitigation Strategies

Mitigation strategies are classified along the **intervention axis**:

- **Pre-training interventions:** Data filtering, deduplication, quality classifiers (e.g., DSIR).
- **Training-time interventions:** RLHF, Constitutional AI, DPO, safety fine-tuning.
- **Decoding-time interventions:** GeDi, DExpert, Self-Debiasing, SafeDecoding.
- **Prompt-time interventions:** System prompts, in-context safety exemplars.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every equation a KTU 2024 student is expected to recognize for this topic.

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Toxicity Score (Perspective) | $\tau = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{h} + b)$ | $\sigma$ = sigmoid, $\mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ BERT embedding, $\mathbf{w}$ classifier weight, $b$ bias |
| 2 | Toxicity Threshold | $\tau_{th} = 0.5$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Expected Max Toxicity (EMT) | $EMT = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{P}_{prompt}}\left[\max_{i=1..N} \tau(t_i)\right]$ | $t_i$ = $i$-th continuation, $N$ = samples, $\mathcal{P}_{prompt}$ = prompt distribution |
| 4 | Toxicity Probability | $P_{tox} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\tau(t_i) \geq \tau_{th}]$ | $\mathbb{1}$ = indicator function |
| 5 | Detoxification Loss (GeDi) | $\mathcal{L}_{detox} = -\log P(\text{nontoxic} \mid x)$ | Cross-entropy with non-toxic class-conditional LM |
| 6 | Combined Training Loss (weighted) | $\mathcal{L} = \alpha \mathcal{L}_{LM} + \beta \mathcal{L}_{safety}$ | $\alpha, \beta$ = tunable weights, dimensionless |
| 7 | DExpert Logit Mixing | $\log p_{safe}(x_t) = \log p_{expert}(x_t) - \log p_{anti}(x_t) + C$ | $p_{expert}$ = anti-toxic model, $p_{anti}$ = pro-toxic model, $C$ = normalizer |
| 8 | PPLM Energy Function | $\Delta \log p(x_t \mid x_{<t}) + \lambda \cdot \text{EMB}(x_t)$ | $\lambda$ = classifier guidance strength |
| 9 | RLHF Reward | $R(x) = R_{helpfulness}(x) - \mu \cdot R_{toxicity}(x)$ | $\mu$ = safety-vs-helpfulness trade-off hyperparameter |
| 10 | Perplexity-Toxicity Trade-off | $\Delta_{trade} = \frac{\Delta PPL}{\Delta \tau_{drop}}$ | $\Delta PPL$ = perplexity increase, $\Delta \tau_{drop}$ = toxicity reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** All industry-grade LLMs (GPT-4, Claude, Llama-Guard, Gemini) report **Expected Maximum Toxicity (EMT)** as a primary safety benchmark. A 5% drop in EMT is considered a *significant* alignment improvement for a frontier model.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

Toxicity mitigation is not merely a sociological concern — it is a **legal, regulatory, and commercial requirement**. The EU AI Act (2024), India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, and NIST AI RMF all mandate toxic-output control. Therefore, KTU 2024 graduates designing production LLM systems *must* incorporate toxicity evaluation as a non-functional requirement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Maximum Toxicity (EMT) Metric

The **Expected Maximum Toxicity** is the canonical metric reported in the RealToxicityPrompts benchmark (Gehman et al., 2020). It captures *worst-case* behavior over multiple stochastic samples.

**Step 1 — Define the sampling procedure.**

For a given prompt $x$, the LLM generates $N$ continuations $\{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_N\}$ using stochastic decoding (e.g., nucleus sampling with $p=0.9$).

**Step 2 — Score each continuation.**

Each continuation $t_i$ is passed through a toxicity classifier to produce $\tau_i \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 3 — Take the per-prompt maximum.**

$$
M(x) = \max_{i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}} \tau(t_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Average over the prompt distribution.**

$$
EMT = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{P}_{prompt}}[M(x)] = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{D}|} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{D}} M(x)
$$

where $\mathcal{D}$ is the evaluation prompt set (e.g., RealToxicityPrompts with $\approx 100\text{k}$ prompts).

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

If $EMT = 0.85$, then on average the *most toxic* sample out of 25 generations scores 0.85 — a strong indicator the model is unsafe.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DExpert Logit-Mixing for Decoding-Time Detoxification

**DExpert (Liu et al., 2021)** uses a *logit-subtraction* trick to suppress toxicity without retraining the base LLM.

**Step 1 — Train two auxiliary models.**
- **Expert model** $p_{expert}$: fine-tuned on *non-toxic* data.
- **Anti-expert model** $p_{anti}$: fine-tuned on *toxic* data.

**Step 2 — Form the combined logit distribution.**

At decoding time, for each token $x_t$:

$$
\log p_{safe}(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \log p_{expert}(x_t \mid x_{<t}) - \log p_{anti}(x_t \mid x_{<t}) + C(x_{<t})
$$

where $C(x_{<t})$ is a context-dependent normalizer to keep the distribution valid (sums to 1).

**Step 3 — Why subtraction works.**

When the base LLM is about to produce a toxic word, both $p_{expert}$ and $p_{anti}$ assign it some probability. By *subtracting* the anti-expert's confidence, we **remove the toxic mode** from the output distribution, leaving only the safe modes. This is mathematically equivalent to:

$$
p_{safe} \propto \frac{p_{expert}}{p_{anti}}
$$

**Step 4 — Sampling.** Use the normalized $p_{safe}$ in any decoding algorithm (nucleus, top-k, greedy).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** DExpert is like a "subtractive filter" — imagine two musicians playing the same melody, one trained to play jazz and one trained to play heavy metal. The jazz musician knows when to stay quiet during heavy-metal sections. The anti-expert is the heavy-metal version; by subtracting it, we get clean jazz.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RLHF Reward Formulation with Toxicity Penalty

**Step 1 — Base reward (helpfulness).**

$$
R_{base}(x) = R_{helpfulness}(x) \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Toxicity penalty term.**

$$
R_{tox}(x) = \max(0, \tau(x) - \tau_{th})
$$

This is zero if the output is below threshold, and grows linearly with over-toxicity.

**Step 3 — Combined reward.**

$$
R_{total}(x) = R_{helpfulness}(x) - \mu \cdot R_{tox}(x)
$$

**Step 4 — PPO objective.**

The LLM is fine-tuned via PPO to maximize $\mathbb{E}_{x \sim \pi_\theta}[R_{total}(x)]$.

**Step 5 — Hyperparameter $\mu$.**

- If $\mu$ is too small → toxicity persists.
- If $\mu$ is too large → model becomes evasive / refuses benign queries ("I cannot answer that...").

**Step 6 — Practical values.** $\mu \in [0.5, 2.0]$ in most open-source RLHF pipelines (e.g., TRL library).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Detoxification Methods

| Method | Intervention Stage | Compute Cost | Toxicity Drop | Perplexity Impact | Requires Retraining? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Filtering (DSIR)** | Pre-training | Low | Moderate | Low | Yes (smaller clean corpus) |
| **RLHF** | Training | Very High | High | Moderate | Yes |
| **DPO** | Training | High | High | Low | Yes |
| **Constitutional AI** | Training | Very High | High | Low | Yes |
| **DExpert** | Decoding | Low (auxiliary forward pass) | High | Negligible | No |
| **GeDi** | Decoding | Moderate | Moderate-High | Moderate | No (uses classifiers only) |
| **PPLM** | Decoding | Very High (gradient per step) | Moderate | High | No |
| **Self-Debiasing** | Decoding | Low | Low-Moderate | Negligible | No |
| **System-Prompt Engineering** | Prompt | Negligible | Low | None | No |
| **Llama-Guard / Guardrails** | Post-hoc | Low | Very High (block-level) | None | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The frontier of the field is moving from *post-hoc filtering* (cheap, brittle) toward *training-time constitutional alignment* (expensive, robust) — and the *trade-off frontier* is the central engineering question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Toxicity Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[userInput] --> B[llmGenerator]
    B --> C[generatedText]
    C --> D[perspectiveAPI]
    C --> E[detoxifyClassifier]
    C --> F[customSafetyFilter]
    D --> G[toxicityScore]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{scoreGEQThreshold}
    H -- yes --> I[blockOutput]
    H -- no --> J[releaseToUser]
    I --> K[fallbackRefusalMessage]
    J --> L[userReceivesSafeText]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Decoding-Time Detoxification via DExpert

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[inputPrompt] --> B[baseLLMForwardPass]
    A --> C[expertLMForwardPass]
    A --> D[antiExpertLMForwardPass]
    B --> E[logitMixingModule]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[subtractLogits]
    F --> G[softmaxNormalizer]
    G --> H[safeTokenDistribution]
    H --> I[decodingAlgorithm]
    I --> J[nonToxicOutput]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: RLHF-Based Toxicity Mitigation Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[promptsDataset] --> B[samplerFromPolicy]
    B --> C[generatedResponse]
    C --> D[humanRater]
    D --> E[rewardLabelHelpful]
    D --> F[toxicityClassifier]
    F --> G[toxicityScore]
    E --> H[combinedReward]
    G --> H
    H --> I[PPOUpdate]
    I --> J[updatedPolicy]
    J --> B
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Lifecycle of Toxicity in an LLM (Causal Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[dataSourceWebCrawl] --> P2[preTrainingCorpus]
    P2 --> P3[baseLLM]
    P3 --> P4[alignmentStage]
    P4 --> P5[deployedModel]
    P5 --> P6[userPrompt]
    P6 --> P7[generatedOutput]
    P7 --> P8{toxicityCheck}
    P8 -- clean --> P9[delivery]
    P8 -- toxic --> P10[refusalOrRewrite]
    P2 -.-> P11[toxicitySeedInWeights]
    P4 -.-> P12[residualBias]
    P11 --> P3
    P12 --> P5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1. Define toxicity in the context of Large Language Models. List any four sub-categories of toxicity as defined by the Perspective API.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Toxicity in LLMs refers to the generation of text that is rude, disrespectful, or socially harmful, causing readers to disengage from the conversation. It arises from training on unfiltered web corpora. (1 mark)

Four sub-categories per Perspective API: (½ mark each, total 2 marks)
1. **Severe Toxicity** — explicit hate speech
2. **Insult** — personal demeaning remarks
3. **Threat** — violent intent
4. **Identity Attack** — bias against protected groups

(Alternatives accepted: Profanity, Sexually Explicit, Flirtation)

---

### **Q2. What is the Expected Maximum Toxicity (EMT) metric? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EMT captures the *worst-case* toxic behavior of a model by averaging, over a prompt distribution, the maximum toxicity score across $N$ stochastic samples per prompt. (2 marks)

$$
EMT = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{P}_{prompt}}\left[\max_{i=1..N} \tau(t_i)\right]
$$

(1 mark for the formula)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the three intervention stages for mitigating toxicity in LLMs. Provide one example technique for each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of the DExpert decoding-time detoxification method. Derive its logit-mixing equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three Intervention Stages (7 marks)**

The three stages are:

1. **Pre-training stage intervention** (2 marks)
   - Operates on raw training data *before* the LLM is trained.
   - Example: **Data Selection via DSIR (Data Selection via Importance Resampling)** — assigns importance weights to documents using a small proxy model trained on a high-quality reference corpus, then re-samples the training set.
   - Benefit: Removes toxic content at the source.
   - Cost: Reduces training data volume, may hurt downstream performance.

2. **Training-stage intervention** (2 marks)
   - Operates during fine-tuning / alignment.
   - Example: **RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)** — uses human preference labels + a toxicity penalty term to construct a reward function, then optimizes the LLM with PPO.
   - Benefit: Deep, robust alignment.
   - Cost: Expensive; requires human annotators; risk of reward hacking.

3. **Decoding-stage intervention** (3 marks)
   - Operates at inference time, no retraining required.
   - Example: **DExpert** — uses an expert (non-toxic) and anti-expert (toxic) model to subtract toxic logits from the base LLM's distribution.
   - Benefit: Plug-and-play, cheap.
   - Cost: Requires auxiliary model forward passes; small latency overhead.

**Part (b) — DExpert Block Diagram + Derivation (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** See Section 4.2 of these notes. The flow is:
`Input → Base LLM + Expert LM + Anti-Expert LM → Logit Subtraction → Softmax → Decoding → Safe Output`.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Define the three logit vectors at token $x_t$ given context $x_{<t}$:
- Base: $z_{base} = \log p_{base}(x_t \mid x_{<t})$
- Expert: $z_{expert} = \log p_{expert}(x_t \mid x_{<t})$
- Anti-expert: $z_{anti} = \log p_{anti}(x_t \mid x_{<t})$

Step 2: The safe logit is the expert logit minus the anti-expert logit:

$$
z_{safe} = z_{expert} - z_{anti}
$$

Step 3: Add a constant offset $C(x_{<t})$ to keep numerical stability:

$$
z_{safe} = z_{expert} - z_{anti} + C(x_{<t})
$$

Step 4: Convert to a probability distribution:

$$
p_{safe}(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \frac{\exp(z_{safe})}{\sum_{v \in V} \exp(z_{safe}(v))}
$$

Step 5: Use $p_{safe}$ in any decoding algorithm.

**Why it works:** When the base LLM is unsure, both expert and anti-expert logit values are similar → subtraction yields small correction. When the model is about to generate a toxic token, the anti-expert assigns *high* probability → subtracting it dramatically reduces the toxic token's score, redirecting probability mass to safe alternatives.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define toxicity score. Describe the RealToxicityPrompts benchmark and explain how Expected Maximum Toxicity (EMT) is computed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the RLHF-based toxicity mitigation pipeline. Derive the combined reward function that balances helpfulness and toxicity. Discuss the trade-off when adjusting the penalty weight $\mu$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Toxicity Score, RealToxicityPrompts, EMT (7 marks)**

**Toxicity Score (2 marks):** A scalar $\tau \in [0, 1]$ produced by a classifier (e.g., Perspective API, Detoxify), defined as:

$$
\tau = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{h} + b)
$$

where $\mathbf{h}$ is a contextual embedding of the input text and $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function. A score $\geq 0.5$ typically flags the text as toxic.

**RealToxicityPrompts Benchmark (2 marks):** Introduced by Gehman et al. (2020), it contains $\approx 100{,}000$ naturally occurring web prompts stratified by difficulty (Somewhat-Toxic, Very-Toxic, Non-Toxic). It is the *de facto* standard for evaluating LLM toxicity.

**EMT Computation (3 marks):**

Step 1: For each prompt $x_j \in \mathcal{D}$ (with $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \approx 100\text{k}$), sample $N=25$ continuations.

Step 2: Score each continuation: $\{\tau(t_{j,1}), \dots, \tau(t_{j,25})\}$.

Step 3: Per-prompt max: $M(x_j) = \max_i \tau(t_{j,i})$.

Step 4: Average:

$$
EMT = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert} \sum_{j=1}^{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert} M(x_j)
$$

**Part (b) — RLHF Pipeline + Reward Derivation (7 marks)**

**Pipeline (3 marks):** (See Section 4.3 block diagram.)
1. Sample a prompt.
2. Generate $K$ responses from the current policy $\pi_\theta$.
3. Human raters rank them by helpfulness; toxicity classifier scores them.
4. Train a reward model $R_\phi$ on these labels.
5. Update $\pi_\theta$ with PPO using the combined reward.

**Reward Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
R_{tox}(x) = \max(0, \tau(x) - \tau_{th})
$$

$$
R_{total}(x) = R_{helpfulness}(x) - \mu \cdot \max(0, \tau(x) - \tau_{th})
$$

**Trade-off Discussion (1 mark):**
- Small $\mu$ → toxicity persists; high helpfulness retained.
- Large $\mu$ → model over-refuses; helpfulness collapses (high false-refusal rate).
- Optimal $\mu \in [0.5, 2.0]$ empirically; tuned on a held-out validation set balancing toxicity-rate vs. refusal-rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing toxicity with bias.** Toxicity is *content-level harm*; bias is *statistical disparity in outputs*. They are related but distinct — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for conflating them.
> 2. **Forgetting the threshold.** Writing $\tau \geq 0.5$ is mandatory; stating just "high score" loses ½ mark.
> 3. **Omitting the normalizer $C(x_{<t})$** in the DExpert derivation → lose 1 mark on numerical validity.
> 4. **Writing $p_{safe} = p_{expert} - p_{anti}$** (probabilities instead of logits) — this is mathematically invalid; only the *logits* are subtracted.
> 5. **Forgetting the sampling step** in the EMT formula — EMT requires *multiple* samples per prompt, not a single greedy output.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Toxicity Module**

- **Definition:** Toxicity = rude, disrespectful, harmful text generated by an LLM. Score $\tau \in [0, 1]$; threshold $\tau_{th} = 0.5$.
- **Sub-categories:** Severe Toxicity, Insult, Threat, Identity Attack, Profanity, Sexually Explicit, Flirtation.
- **Root Cause:** Pre-training on unfiltered web data; objective rewards fluency, not safety.
- **EMT Metric:** Average of per-prompt max toxicity over $N$ samples. Standard $N = 25$.
- **Three Intervention Stages:** Pre-training (data filtering), Training (RLHF, DPO, Constitutional AI), Decoding (DExpert, GeDi, PPLM, Self-Debiasing).
- **DExpert Equation:** $\log p_{safe} = \log p_{expert} - \log p_{anti} + C$. Operates in *logit space*, not probability space.
- **RLHF Reward:** $R_{total} = R_{help} - \mu \cdot \max(0, \tau - \tau_{th})$. $\mu$ controls safety-helpfulness trade-off.
- **Best Decoding Method (no retrain):** DExpert — high toxicity drop, negligible perplexity impact.
- **Best Training Method:** RLHF + Constitutional AI — high robustness, high compute cost.
- **Industry Standard Benchmark:** RealToxicityPrompts (Gehman et al., 2020).
- **Key Trade-off Frontier:** Perplexity vs. Toxicity (lower toxicity often costs fluency).
- **Regulatory Drivers:** EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, India's DPDP Act.
- **Examiner Loves:** Numerical thresholds, derivation steps, comparison tables, block diagrams.
- **Examiner Penalizes:** Vague answers, missing formulas, conflating bias and toxicity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Privacy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Privacy in LLMs

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, **Privacy** refers to the set of mathematical, algorithmic, and system-level guarantees and mechanisms designed to prevent the unintended disclosure, memorization, inference, or leakage of sensitive information contained in the training corpus, user prompts, or model parameters. Privacy in LLMs is formally studied under the umbrella of **Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning (PPML)** and encompasses the protection of **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)**, **training-data confidentiality**, and **user-query confidentiality**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines privacy in LLMs across **three orthogonal threat dimensions**:

1. **Training-Data Privacy** — Preventing the model from regurgitating, memorizing, or allowing extraction of verbatim or near-verbatim training samples.
2. **Inference-Data Privacy** — Protecting user prompts, retrieved context (in RAG pipelines), and generated outputs from being exposed to unauthorized parties.
3. **Model-Parameter Privacy** — Ensuring that model weights and gradients do not encode recoverable sensitive information about any individual record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECMT862 Module 3):**
> Privacy is treated as a **first-class system property** of LLMs, on par with accuracy and fairness. The syllabus requires students to understand (a) attack surfaces, (b) formal privacy frameworks (Differential Privacy), (c) mitigation strategies (DP-SGD, Federated Learning, Machine Unlearning), and (d) the regulatory landscape (GDPR, DPDP Act 2023).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honest Librarian"

Imagine a **librarian** (the LLM) who has read **millions of private diaries** (the training corpus) to become knowledgeable. When a visitor asks a question, the librarian answers intelligently. However, three risks exist:

- **Loose Lips:** The librarian might accidentally quote a diary entry word-for-word, revealing someone's medical history.
- **Guessing:** A clever visitor might craft questions that cause the librarian to reveal *whether* a specific diary was in the library (membership inference).
- **Memory Tracing:** Even if the librarian doesn't quote, a forensic linguist might examine the librarian's brain (the model weights) to reconstruct private information.

**Privacy mechanisms** in LLMs are the equivalent of:
- **Redacting** sensitive passages before the librarian reads them (data anonymization).
- **Adding controlled noise** to the librarian's memory so exact recall is impossible (Differential Privacy).
- **Building the librarian's knowledge from many distributed branch libraries** so no single diary is fully seen (Federated Learning).
- **Designing an "amnesia" protocol** so that if a diary is later withdrawn, its influence can be scrubbed (Machine Unlearning).

---

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of LLM Privacy (Syllabus Anchor)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of LLM Privacy (Must memorize for KTU exams):**

| Pillar | Technical Name | Core Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | **Data Anonymization & Redaction** | "How do we sanitize the corpus before training?" |
| **P2** | **Differential Privacy (DP)** | "How do we mathematically bound leakage of any one record?" |
| **P3** | **Federated & Split Learning** | "How do we train without centralizing raw data?" |
| **P4** | **Machine Unlearning** | "How do we remove a record's influence after training?" |

---

## 1.4 Threat Model — Who is the Adversary?

A privacy threat model specifies the adversary's **knowledge**, **capabilities**, and **goals**. In LLM privacy, we typically consider:

- **Honest-but-Curious (HbC) Server:** The inference provider (e.g., an API) follows the protocol but tries to extract user data from prompts/logs.
- **Malicious User / Black-Box Attacker:** Crafts adversarial prompts to extract memorized training data (e.g., Carlini et al., 2021 — the "Extraction Attack").
- **Malicious Insider:** Has access to model checkpoints and performs **weight inspection** or **gradient inversion**.
- **Reconstruction Adversary:** Uses auxiliary information (public datasets, side channels) to de-anonymize outputs.

The **adversary's goal** is operationalized via three canonical attacks:

1. **Data Extraction Attack** — Recover verbatim training sequences.
2. **Membership Inference Attack (MIA)** — Decide if a record $x$ was in $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$.
3. **Reconstruction Attack** — Recover input from observed gradients or model outputs.

---

## 1.5 Why Privacy is a Unique Challenge for LLMs

Unlike traditional ML, LLMs introduce three **scaling-induced privacy risks**:

1. **Emergent Memorization:** Larger models memorize *more* (scaling laws of memorization show that sequence duplication probability grows with parameter count).
2. **Unstructured Targets:** PII is embedded in free-form text, making structured anonymization (k-anonymity, l-diversity) difficult.
3. **Long Context Windows:** Modern LLMs ingest 100K+ tokens per query, multiplying the surface area for prompt leakage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Privacy Budget $\varepsilon$ (epsilon):** A scalar quantifying the *worst-case* information leakage. Lower $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy. The de-facto standard is **$(\varepsilon, \delta)$-Differential Privacy**, where $\delta$ is a small failure probability (typically $\leq 10^{-5}$).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Privacy Attack — Membership Inference Attack (MIA)

The **Membership Inference Attack** is the foundational building block of LLM privacy analysis. It answers the question:

> *"Given a trained model $\mathcal{M}$ and a target data point $x$, can an adversary determine whether $x \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$?"*

### 2.1.1 Formal Setup

Let:
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \sim \mathcal{P}$ be the training set drawn from population distribution $\mathcal{P}$.
- $\mathcal{A}$ be a randomized training algorithm that produces a model $\mathcal{M} = \mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}})$.
- $\mathcal{I}$ be the **inference adversary** that, given $(\mathcal{M}, x)$, outputs a binary decision $\hat{m} \in \{0, 1\}$ where $1$ means "member".

The **MIA advantage** is defined as:

$$
\text{Adv}_{\mathcal{M}}^{\text{MIA}} = \Pr[\mathcal{I}(\mathcal{M}, x) = 1 \mid x \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}] - \Pr[\mathcal{I}(\mathcal{M}, x) = 1 \mid x \notin \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}]
$$

A model with **Adv ≈ 0** is privacy-preserving; a model with **Adv ≈ 1** memorizes everything.

### 2.1.2 The Loss-Threshold Attack (Yeom et al., 2018)

The simplest MIA exploits the empirical observation that models fit training data better than held-out data (lower loss). The adversary computes the per-sample loss $\ell(x, y; \mathcal{M})$ and applies a threshold $\tau$:

$$
\hat{m} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \ell(x, y; \mathcal{M}) < \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Training loss is minimized on members, so the loss distribution of members is **stochastically dominated** by that of non-members. The further apart these distributions, the higher the Adv.

### 2.1.3 Reference Attack for LLMs — perplexity-based MIA

For autoregressive LMs with vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$, define the **perplexity** of a sequence $x = (x_1, \dots, x_T)$ as:

$$
\text{PPL}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_{\mathcal{M}}(x_t \mid x_{<t})\right)
$$

The adversary uses the decision rule: $x$ is a **member** if $\text{PPL}(x) < \tau_{\text{PPL}}$. This is the de-facto MIA baseline for GPT-style LLMs.

---

## 2.2 Differential Privacy (DP) — The Gold Standard

### 2.2.1 Pure $\varepsilon$-Differential Privacy

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ satisfies **$\varepsilon$-differential privacy** if, for all **adjacent datasets** $D, D'$ differing in exactly one record, and for all measurable output sets $S \subseteq \mathcal{Y}$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

**Intuition:** The output distribution is *almost* the same whether or not any individual record is present. Therefore, observing the output reveals *almost nothing* about any individual.

### 2.2.2 $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-Approximate Differential Privacy

To allow a small failure probability (useful for high-dimensional mechanisms like DP-SGD), we relax to:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention (Dwork et al.):** Choose $\delta \ll 1/|\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}|$, typically $\delta = 10^{-5}$ for academic benchmarks.

### 2.2.3 The Gaussian Mechanism

For a real-valued query $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ with **$\ell_2$-sensitivity**:

$$
\Delta_2 f = \max_{D \sim D'} \| f(D) - f(D') \|_2
$$

The **Gaussian Mechanism** is:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I_k)
$$

This satisfies $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP when:

$$
\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta_2 f}{\varepsilon} \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}
$$

### 2.2.4 The Laplace Mechanism (Pure $\varepsilon$-DP)

For $\ell_1$-sensitivity $\Delta_1 f$, add Laplace noise:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta_1 f / \varepsilon)
$$

Satisfies **pure $\varepsilon$-DP** (with $\delta = 0$).

### 2.2.5 DP-SGD (Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent)

DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) is the workhorse for training private deep models, including LLMs. The per-step algorithm:

1. **Compute per-example gradients** $g_i = \nabla_{\theta} \ell(\theta; x_i)$ for each $x_i$ in the mini-batch of size $B$.
2. **Clip per-example gradient norms** to a threshold $C$:

$$
\tilde{g}_i = g_i \cdot \min\!\left(1, \frac{C}{\|g_i\|_2}\right)
$$

3. **Sum the clipped gradients** and add Gaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I)$:

$$
\tilde{G} = \frac{1}{B}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{B} \tilde{g}_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I)\right)
$$

4. **Update parameters:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \tilde{G}$.

The **per-step privacy cost** is computed via **Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP)** composition, and the cumulative cost across $T$ steps must remain within the target $(\varepsilon, \delta)$.

### 2.2.6 The Moments Accountant & RDP

The standard Gaussian composition gives loose bounds. The **Moments Accountant** tracks the privacy loss random variable's moments, giving a much tighter bound. For DP-SGD with sampling rate $q = B/|\mathcal{D}|$, noise multiplier $\sigma$, and $T$ steps, the $(\varepsilon, \delta)$ guarantee is computed via the RDP functional:

$$
D_{\alpha}(\mathcal{M}(D) \| \mathcal{M}(D')) \leq T \cdot \frac{q^2 \alpha}{\sigma^2} + \text{lower-order terms}
$$

for Rényi order $\alpha > 1$.

---

## 2.3 Composition Theorems

### 2.3.1 Sequential Composition (Basic)

If $\mathcal{M}_1$ is $(\varepsilon_1, \delta_1)$-DP and $\mathcal{M}_2$ is $(\varepsilon_2, \delta_2)$-DP, then their adaptive composition is $(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2, \delta_1 + \delta_2)$-DP.

### 2.3.2 Advanced Composition (Dwork, Rothblum, Vadhan)

For $k$-fold adaptive composition of $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP mechanisms:

$$
\varepsilon' = \sqrt{2k \ln(1/\delta')} \cdot \varepsilon + k \varepsilon (e^{\varepsilon} - 1)
$$

for a new failure probability $\delta'$.

---

## 2.4 Federated Learning for LLMs

In **Federated Learning (FL)**, data never leaves the client device. The server aggregates locally-trained model updates:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \Delta_k^{(t)}
$$

where $\Delta_k^{(t)}$ is the update from client $k$, $n_k$ is client $k$'s sample count, and $N = \sum_k n_k$.

**Privacy enhancement:** Combine FL with **Secure Aggregation** (Bonawitz et al., 2017) and **DP** on the client updates so the server only sees a noisy aggregate. This is the basis of **Federated DP-SGD**.

---

## 2.5 Machine Unlearning

Given a model trained on $\mathcal{D}$ and a forget set $\mathcal{D}_f \subset \mathcal{D}$, **machine unlearning** produces a model $\mathcal{M}'$ that is *indistinguishable* (in distribution) from a model retrained on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_f$:

$$
\mathcal{M}(\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D})) \overset{d}{\approx} \mathcal{M}(\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_f))
$$

### 2.5.1 Exact Unlearning via SISA (Bourtoule et al., 2021)

**SISA — Sharded, Isolated, Sliced, and Aggregated:**

- Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $S$ disjoint shards $\{D_1, \dots, D_S\}$.
- Train an independent sub-model on each shard.
- To unlearn $x \in D_s$: retrain only the sub-model for $D_s \setminus \{x\}$.

**Cost reduction:** From $O(|\mathcal{D}|)$ retraining to $O(|\mathcal{D}|/S)$.

### 2.5.2 Approximate Unlearning (Certified Removal)

**Certified Removal (Guo et al., 2020):** For strongly convex losses, Newton's method updates can be *inverted*. Adding noise scaled to the inverse Hessian gives $( \varepsilon, \delta )$-certified removal:

$$
\theta' = \theta - H^{-1} \nabla \ell(\theta; x_f) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 H^{-2})
$$

where $H$ is the Hessian and $x_f$ is the forgotten record.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table (Must memorize for ESE):**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon$-DP definition | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)\in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')\in S]$ | $\varepsilon > 0$ |
| 2 | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)\in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')\in S] + \delta$ | $\delta \ll 1/N$ |
| 3 | $\ell_2$-sensitivity | $\Delta_2 f = \max_{D\sim D'} \|f(D) - f(D')\|_2$ | scalar |
| 4 | Gaussian noise scale | $\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta_2 f}{\varepsilon}\sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}$ | scalar |
| 5 | Laplace noise scale | $b = \Delta_1 f / \varepsilon$ | scalar |
| 6 | Gradient clipping | $\tilde{g}_i = g_i \cdot \min(1, C/\|g_i\|_2)$ | $C$ is clip norm |
| 7 | Sequential composition | $\varepsilon_{\text{tot}} = \sum_i \varepsilon_i$, $\delta_{\text{tot}} = \sum_i \delta_i$ | additive |
| 8 | MIA advantage | $\text{Adv} = \Pr[\text{TP}] - \Pr[\text{FP}]$ | $\in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | Perplexity | $\text{PPL}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{T}\sum_t \log p(x_t\mid x_{<t})\right)$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | SISA retrain cost | $O(\vert\mathcal{D}\vert/S)$ vs. $O(\vert\mathcal{D}\vert)$ | $S$ shards |
| 11 | Certified removal noise | $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 H^{-2})$ | Hessian-scaled |
| 12 | Federated avg | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_k \frac{n_k}{N}\Delta_k^{(t)}$ | weighted avg |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Use Cases (Likely KTU 2-Mark Question):**
> - **Healthcare LLMs** (e.g., Med-PaLM 2): DP-SGD on Electronic Health Records (EHR).
> - **On-device Assistants** (Gboard, Apple Intelligence): Federated Learning with DP.
> - **Right-to-be-Forgotten compliance** (GDPR Art. 17, India DPDP Act 2023): Machine Unlearning.
> - **Enterprise RAG systems:** PII redaction in retrieved documents before they enter the context window.
> - **Open-weight model releases:** Releasing only DP-trained weights to bound downstream memorization.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Definition to Gaussian Mechanism

**Goal:** Show that adding Gaussian noise of scale $\sigma = c \Delta_2 f / \varepsilon$ achieves $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP.

### Step 1 — State the mechanism

We have a query $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ and define:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Z, \quad Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I_k)
$$

### Step 2 — Bound the privacy loss random variable

For adjacent $D, D'$, define $\Delta = f(D) - f(D')$ with $\|\Delta\|_2 \leq \Delta_2 f$. For any output $y$:

$$
\frac{p_{\mathcal{M}(D)}(y)}{p_{\mathcal{M}(D')}(y)} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\|y - f(D)\|^2 / 2\sigma^2\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\|y - f(D')\|^2 / 2\sigma^2\right)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\|y - f(D')\|^2 - \|y - f(D)\|^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic simplification of the numerator

Using the identity $\|a\|^2 - \|b\|^2 = (\|a\| - \|b\|)(\|a\| + \|b\|)$ and letting $u = y - f(D')$:

$$
\|y - f(D')\|^2 - \|y - f(D)\|^2 = \|u\|^2 - \|u - \Delta\|^2 = 2 u^\top \Delta - \|\Delta\|^2
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the ratio

$$
\frac{p_{\mathcal{M}(D)}(y)}{p_{\mathcal{M}(D')}(y)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{2 u^\top \Delta - \|\Delta\|^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Bound $u^\top \Delta$ via Cauchy–Schwarz

$$
u^\top \Delta \leq \|u\| \cdot \|\Delta\| \leq \|u\| \cdot \Delta_2 f
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the tail bound for the Gaussian

The event $\mathcal{E} = \{ \|u\| \geq t \}$ has probability $\Pr[\mathcal{E}] = \bar{\Phi}(t/(\sigma \sqrt{2}))$, where $\bar{\Phi}$ is the Gaussian tail. For $u \notin \mathcal{E}$:

$$
\frac{p_{\mathcal{M}(D)}(y)}{p_{\mathcal{M}(D')}(y)} \leq \exp\!\left(\frac{2 t \Delta_2 f}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{t \Delta_2 f}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

### Step 7 — Set $t = \sigma^2 \varepsilon / \Delta_2 f$ to make the exponent equal $\varepsilon$

This yields the bound:

$$
\frac{p_{\mathcal{M}(D)}(y)}{p_{\mathcal{M}(D')}(y)} \leq e^{\varepsilon} \quad \text{except on } \mathcal{E}
$$

### Step 8 — Choose $\sigma$ to control $\Pr[\mathcal{E}]$

We require $\Pr[\mathcal{E}] = \bar{\Phi}(t/(\sigma\sqrt{2})) \leq \delta$. Solving for $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta_2 f}{\varepsilon} \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25 / \delta)}
$$

**Conclusion:** With this choice of $\sigma$, the Gaussian mechanism satisfies $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Privacy Budget Accumulation in DP-SGD

**Goal:** Compute the cumulative $(\varepsilon, \delta)$ after $T$ DP-SGD steps with sampling rate $q$ and noise multiplier $\sigma$.

### Step 1 — RDP of subsampled Gaussian mechanism

For Rényi order $\alpha > 1$, the RDP of one DP-SGD step is bounded by:

$$
D_{\alpha}(\mathcal{M}_{\text{step}} \| \mathcal{M}_{\text{step}}') \leq \frac{q^2 \alpha}{\sigma^2} + O(q^3 / \sigma^3)
$$

where $q = B / |\mathcal{D}|$ is the sampling probability per step.

### Step 2 — RDP composition is additive

The total RDP after $T$ steps is:

$$
D_{\alpha}^{\text{total}} = T \cdot D_{\alpha}(\mathcal{M}_{\text{step}})
$$

### Step 3 — Convert RDP to $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP

For any $\alpha > 1$:

$$
\varepsilon(\delta) = D_{\alpha}^{\text{total}} + \frac{\log(1/\delta)}{\alpha - 1}
$$

### Step 4 — Optimize over $\alpha$

Choose $\alpha^*$ that minimizes the above expression. This is the **standard moments-accountant formula** used in Opacus (Meta) and TensorFlow Privacy.

### Step 5 — Numerical example (typical LLM scale)

For training a 1.3B-parameter LLM on $|\mathcal{D}| = 10^6$ sequences with $B = 1024$, $q = 1024/10^6 = 1.024 \times 10^{-3}$, $\sigma = 1.0$, and $T = 100{,}000$ steps:

$$
D_{\alpha}^{\text{total}} \approx 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{(10^{-3})^2 \cdot \alpha}{1.0} = 0.1 \alpha
$$

For $\alpha = 10$, $\delta = 10^{-6}$:

$$
\varepsilon = 1.0 + \frac{\log(10^6)}{9} \approx 1.0 + 1.535 = 2.535
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 1.3B-parameter model can be DP-trained with $\varepsilon \approx 2.5$, which is considered **moderate privacy**. Stronger privacy ($\varepsilon < 1$) requires either more data, larger batches, or more noise (utility trade-off).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MIA Advantage Upper Bound for DP Models

**Theorem (Yeom et al., 2018):** For a model trained with $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP, the MIA advantage is bounded by:

$$
\text{Adv}_{\mathcal{M}}^{\text{MIA}} \leq e^{\varepsilon} - 1 + \delta
$$

### Proof Sketch

1. The training algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ is $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP, so for any model $\mathcal{M}$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}) = \mathcal{M}] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}') = \mathcal{M}] + \delta
$$

2. For any inference adversary $\mathcal{I}$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{I}(\mathcal{M}, x) = 1 \mid x \in \mathcal{D}] = \sum_{\mathcal{M}} \Pr[\mathcal{I}(\mathcal{M}, x) = 1] \Pr[\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}) = \mathcal{M}]
$$

3. Bounding the ratio of these probabilities by $e^{\varepsilon} + \delta$ gives the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** DP provides a **provable, worst-case** upper bound on MIA success — this is the key reason DP is the gold standard for LLM privacy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Mitigation Strategies

| Method | Privacy Guarantee | Utility Cost | Compute Cost | Suitable for LLMs? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Anonymization (Regex/NER)** | Heuristic, not formal | Medium | Low | Yes, preprocessing |
| **k-Anonymity / l-Diversity** | Weak against linkage attacks | Medium | Low | Limited (text) |
| **DP-SGD** | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP | High (10–30% BLEU drop) | ~1.5× | Yes (small/medium LLMs) |
| **Federated Learning + DP** | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP | High | High (communication) | Yes (on-device) |
| **SISA Unlearning** | Exact (if shards retrained) | None | $O(1/S)$ per request | Yes, post-hoc |
| **Certified Removal** | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-removal | Moderate | Moderate | Limited to convex models |
| **PII Redaction in RAG** | None on model itself | None | Low | Yes, complementary |
| **Output Filtering (canary tokens)** | Empirical, not formal | Low | Low | Yes, defense-in-depth |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — DP-SGD Budget Calculation

**Problem:** A startup trains a 350M-parameter LLM with DP-SGD. They use batch size $B = 4096$, dataset size $N = 4{,}096{,}000$ sequences, target $\varepsilon = 1.0$, $\delta = 10^{-6}$, and noise multiplier $\sigma = 2.0$. How many training steps are allowed?

### Step 1 — Compute sampling rate

$$
q = \frac{B}{N} = \frac{4096}{4{,}096{,}000} = 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 2 — RDP per step at order $\alpha = 20$

$$
D_{20}^{\text{step}} \approx \frac{q^2 \cdot 20}{\sigma^2} = \frac{10^{-6} \cdot 20}{4} = 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $T$ given target $\varepsilon$

Using $\varepsilon = T \cdot D_{\alpha}^{\text{step}} + \log(1/\delta) / (\alpha - 1)$:

$$
1.0 = T \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{\log(10^6)}{19} = T \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{13.8155}{19}
$$

$$
1.0 = T \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} + 0.727
$$

$$
T = \frac{1.0 - 0.727}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.273}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 54{,}600 \text{ steps}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to epochs

$$
\text{Epochs} = \frac{T \cdot B}{N} = \frac{54{,}600 \times 4096}{4{,}096{,}000} \approx 54.6
$$

**Conclusion:** The model can be trained for **~54 epochs** while keeping the privacy budget at $\varepsilon = 1.0$, $\delta = 10^{-6}$. This is realistic for many fine-tuning scenarios. $\blacksquare$

```mermaid
    C["Clip g_i to norm C"]:::proc
    D["Sum clipped gradients"]:::proc
    E["Add Gaussian noise N(0, sigma^2 C^2 I)"]:::noise
    F["Noisy aggregate G_tilde"]:::proc
    G["Parameter update: theta = theta - eta * G_tilde"]:::model
    H["Privacy Accountant: track epsilon, delta"]:::proc

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G --> A
    G -.tracks.-> H
    H -.halts.-> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Flow:** The noise is added **after** gradient clipping. This ordering is essential — it ensures the noise scale is calibrated to a *known* sensitivity $\Delta = C$, independent of the actual gradient values.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Federated Learning with Differential Privacy

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef client fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef sec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef server fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    C1["Client 1: Local data D_1, local model m_1"]:::client
    C2["Client 2: Local data D_2, local model m_2"]:::client
    C3["Client 3: Local data D_3, local model m_3"]:::client
    Cn["Client K: Local data D_K, local model m_K"]:::client

    SA["Secure Aggregation Protocol"]:::sec
    Noise["Add DP noise N(0, sigma^2) to aggregate"]:::sec
    Srv["Central Server: global model theta"]:::server

    C1 -->|encrypted update delta_1| SA
    C2 -->|encrypted update delta_2| SA
    C3 -->|encrypted update delta_3| SA
    Cn -->|encrypted update delta_K| SA
    SA --> Noise --> Srv
    Srv -.broadcasts theta.-> C1
    Srv -.broadcasts theta.-> C2
    Srv -.broadcasts theta.-> C3
    Srv -.broadcasts theta.-> Cn
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The server **never sees** any individual client's update — only the noisy aggregate. Secure Aggregation uses cryptographic masking so the aggregate is computed without revealing individual contributions.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Machine Unlearning Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef req fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    Start["Deletion Request: forget record x_f"]:::req
    Q1{"Was x_f in training data?"}:::logic
    Q2{"Is exact unlearning feasible?"}:::logic
    SISA["SISA: Retrain only affected shard"]:::action
    Approx["Approximate unlearning: noise + Newton step"]:::action
    FineTune["Selective fine-tune with x_f excluded"]:::action
    Verify["Verify with MIA: Adv near 0?"}:::logic
    Done["Return unlearned model M'"]:::out

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -->|No| Done
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| SISA
    Q2 -->|No, but convex| Approx
    Q2 -->|No, non-convex LLM| FineTune
    SISA --> Verify
    Approx --> Verify
    FineTune --> Verify
    Verify -->|Pass| Done
    Verify -->|Fail| FineTune
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — The Privacy-Utility Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef axis fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef curve fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    X["Epsilon - Privacy Budget"]:::axis
    Y["Model Utility - Accuracy / BLEU"]:::axis
    P1["High Privacy - Low Epsilon - Low Utility"]:::curve
    P2["Low Privacy - High Epsilon - High Utility"]:::curve
    Curve["Privacy-Utility Frontier"]:::curve

    X --- Y
    X --> P1
    X --> P2
    P1 -.->|trade-off| P2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Moving left on the x-axis (stronger privacy) always reduces utility. The frontier curve is **concave** — diminishing returns on utility as $\varepsilon \to \infty$. The "sweet spot" for most LLM deployments is $\varepsilon \in [1, 8]$.
