<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** is a hybrid AI architecture that augments a Large Language Model (LLM) by conditioning its response generation on external, dynamically retrieved documents from a knowledge corpus, instead of relying solely on the parametric knowledge baked into the model's weights during pre-training.

Formally, RAG combines two neural components:

$$
P(y \mid x) = \sum_{z \in \text{Top-}k(\mathcal{Z})} P_{\eta}(z \mid x) \cdot P_{\theta}(y \mid x, z)
$$

Where:
- $x$ = user query (input)
- $y$ = generated response (output)
- $z$ = retrieved context passage from corpus $\mathcal{Z}$
- $P_{\eta}(z \mid x)$ = retriever probability (relevance scoring)
- $P_{\theta}(y \mid x, z)$ = generator (LLM) conditional probability
- $\text{Top-}k$ = the $k$ most relevant passages selected

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Open-Book Exam" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a pure LLM as a student taking a CLOSED-BOOK exam.** All knowledge is locked inside their head (the model weights). If the question is about events *after* they last studied (the knowledge cutoff), they either guess or hallucinate. **RAG turns that same student into an OPEN-BOOK exam taker.** Before answering, they walk to a library, find the right textbook pages (retrieval), read them, and then compose an answer citing those pages. The result is **more accurate, more current, and more verifiable**.

## 1.3 Why RAG Exists — The Three Core Problems of Vanilla LLMs

| Problem | Description | How RAG Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| **Hallucination** | LLM fabricates plausible but false facts | Grounds answers in real retrieved text |
| **Knowledge Cutoff** | Model unaware of events after training | Retrieves from an updatable live corpus |
| **Domain Gap** | Weak performance on private/niche data | Allows plug-in enterprise knowledge bases |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The examiner expects you to be able to **define RAG, list its components, and explain WHY it is preferred over vanilla fine-tuning** for dynamic knowledge. Memorize the open-book/closed-book analogy — it scores well in 3-mark questions.
> 
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a RAG System

A standard RAG pipeline consists of three sequential, modular components:

### Pillar 1 — The **Retriever** (Information Search Engine)

The retriever's job is to scan a large corpus and return the top-$k$ passages most semantically similar to the query $x$.

- **Sparse Retrieval (Lexical):** Algorithms like **BM25** rank documents based on exact keyword overlap. Fast, interpretable, but fails on synonyms.
- **Dense Retrieval (Semantic):** A neural encoder (e.g., a transformer-based bi-encoder such as **DPR — Dense Passage Retrieval**) maps both query and documents into a shared $d$-dimensional vector space. Similarity is measured geometrically.
- **Hybrid Retrieval:** Combines both BM25 and dense scores to balance lexical precision and semantic recall.

### Pillar 2 — The **Knowledge Base / Index**

Documents are pre-processed via **chunking** (splitting long texts into passages of typically 100–512 tokens), embedded into vectors, and stored in a **vector database** (e.g., FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma).

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Database:** A specialized data structure that supports **Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN)** search using algorithms like **HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)** graphs for sub-linear retrieval at scale.

### Pillar 3 — The **Generator** (The LLM)

A pre-trained sequence-to-sequence LLM (e.g., GPT, LLaMA, Mistral) that receives a prompt structured as:

$$
\text{Context} \; z \;\oplus\; \text{Query} \; x \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Response} \; y
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes prompt concatenation. The LLM is *frozen* in naive RAG — no weight updates occur.

## 2.2 The Step-by-Step RAG Workflow

1. **Indexing Phase (Offline, one-time):**
   - Load raw documents $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$
   - Split into chunks $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_M\}$
   - Encode each chunk: $v_i = E(c_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$
   - Store vectors $v_i$ in the vector index

2. **Retrieval Phase (Online, per query):**
   - Encode user query: $q = E(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$
   - Compute similarity $\text{sim}(q, v_i)$ for all $i$
   - Return Top-$k$ chunks: $Z = \arg\text{Top-}k_i \, \text{sim}(q, v_i)$

3. **Augmentation Phase:**
   - Construct prompt: $p = \text{Template}(Z, x)$

4. **Generation Phase:**
   - LLM generates: $y = \text{LLM}(p)$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are KTU 2024 high-yield items. Memorize the geometric meaning of each.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert_{2} \, \Vert B \Vert_{2}}$ | Standard semantic similarity score; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 2 | **Dot Product Similarity** | $s = A \cdot B = \sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i b_i$ | Used when vectors are pre-normalized; unbounded |
| 3 | **Euclidean (L2) Distance** | $d(A, B) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(a_i - b_i)^2}$ | Geometric distance; lower = more similar |
| 4 | **BM25 Score** (Robertson–Sparck Jones) | $\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot (1-b + b \cdot \vert d \vert / \text{avgdl})}$ | Lexical relevance; typically $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b \approx 0.75$ |
| 5 | **Inverse Document Frequency** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)$ | Down-weights common terms |
| 6 | **RAG Marginalization** | $P(y \mid x) = \sum_{z} P_{\eta}(z \mid x) \, P_{\theta}(y \mid x, z)$ | Theoretical generation probability |
| 7 | **Softmax over retrieval scores** | $P_{\eta}(z_i \mid x) = \dfrac{\exp(\text{sim}(q, v_i) / \tau)}{\sum_{j} \exp(\text{sim}(q, v_j) / \tau)}$ | Converts raw scores to a probability distribution; $\tau$ = temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> In BM25, the term $\vert d \vert$ is the document length and `avgdl` is the average document length across the corpus. The vertical bars were replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

RAG is the **de-facto architecture** behind production-grade AI systems that demand factuality:

- **Enterprise Search (e.g., Microsoft Copilot, Google Workspace AI):** Answers grounded in company-internal documents.
- **Customer Support Chatbots:** Pull answers from product manuals and policy PDFs.
- **Legal & Medical Assistants:** Cite real case law / clinical guidelines, dramatically reducing hallucination risk.
- **Code Assistants:** Retrieve API documentation before generating code snippets.

> [!TIP]
> RAG vs Fine-Tuning — a frequent 14-mark KTU question. **RAG is preferred** when the knowledge is **large, frequently changing, or must be cited**; **fine-tuning is preferred** when you need to change the model's **style, format, or reasoning behavior**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Vanilla LLM to RAG (Generative Probability View)

A vanilla LLM generates output $y$ from query $x$ by sampling from its *parametric* distribution:

$$
P_{\text{LM}}(y \mid x) = P_{\theta}(y \mid x)
$$

This is **closed-book** — all knowledge is compressed into the weights $\theta$. To make the system **open-book**, we introduce a latent variable $z$ representing the retrieved context, and apply the **law of total probability**:

$$
P(y \mid x) = \sum_{z} P(z \mid x) \, P(y \mid x, z)
$$

In practice, summing over the *entire* corpus $\mathcal{Z}$ is computationally intractable. We approximate the sum by keeping only the **top-$k$ most probable** contexts under the retriever $P_{\eta}(z \mid x)$:

$$
P(y \mid x) \approx \sum_{z \in \text{Top-}k(\mathcal{Z})} P_{\eta}(z \mid x) \, P_{\theta}(y \mid x, z)
$$

This is the **canonical RAG equation** (Lewis et al., 2020, *NeurIPS*). The retriever term $P_{\eta}(z \mid x)$ acts as a *soft attention* over the corpus, while the generator term $P_{\theta}(y \mid x, z)$ acts as a *conditional language model* that fuses query and context.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cosine Similarity from First Principles

Given two embedding vectors $A = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_d)$ and $B = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_d)$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$:

**Step 1:** Define the dot product:

$$
A \cdot B = \sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i b_i
$$

**Step 2:** Define the Euclidean (L2) norm of each vector:

$$
\Vert A \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i^2}, \qquad \Vert B \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} b_i^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the geometric identity that the dot product equals the product of magnitudes times the cosine of the angle between them:

$$
A \cdot B = \Vert A \Vert \, \Vert B \Vert \, \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate $\cos(\theta)$:

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \, \Vert B \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i^2} \, \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} b_i^2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cosine and not Euclidean?** Cosine similarity is **scale-invariant** — it measures *direction*, not magnitude. Two documents with the same topic but different lengths will have nearly identical cosine scores but very different Euclidean distances.

## 3.3 Derivation: Why BM25 is the Lexical Baseline

Classical TF-IDF weights a term $t$ in document $d$ as:

$$
w(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)
$$

where $\text{TF}(t,d) = f(t,d)$ is the raw count. A key problem: **term frequency grows unboundedly** with repetition, so a document that mentions "the" 100 times dominates one that mentions it once.

**BM25 saturates** this growth. Start with raw TF $f(t,d)$ and apply the *Robertson–Sparck Jones* transform:

$$
\text{TF}_{\text{BM25}}(t, d) = \frac{f(t, d) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(t, d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$

The intuition for each parameter:

- $k_1$ controls how quickly TF saturates. Higher $k_1$ → more weight to repeated terms.
- $b$ controls length normalization. $b = 1$ fully penalizes long docs; $b = 0$ ignores length.
- The fraction $\vert d \vert / \text{avgdl}$ measures how long this document is compared to the average.

The final BM25 score for query $q$ against document $d$ is the sum over all query terms:

$$
\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t, d) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(t, d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$

with the standard IDF formula:

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \ln\!\left(\frac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)
$$

where $N$ is the total number of documents and $n_t$ is the number of documents containing term $t$. The $+1$ inside the log prevents negative IDF for very common terms.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Retrieval Strategies

| Criterion | Sparse (BM25) | Dense (DPR / Embeddings) | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Matches synonyms** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Handles rare technical terms** | ✅ Strong | ❌ Weak | ✅ Strong |
| **Requires training data** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (QA pairs) | Optional |
| **Indexing speed** | ⚡ Fast | 🐢 Slow (encoder) | Medium |
| **Interpretability** | ✅ High (term-level) | ❌ Low (black box) | Medium |
| **Out-of-vocabulary robustness** | ✅ High | ❌ Low | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Modern production RAG systems are *almost always hybrid*. Sparse retrieval guarantees keyword coverage; dense retrieval guarantees semantic coverage. The two errors are largely uncorrelated, so combining them reduces overall failure rate (the **AND-style ensemble principle**).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level RAG Pipeline (End-to-End Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    docsA[Raw Documents] --> chunkA[Chunk Splitter]
    chunkA --> encA[Embedding Encoder]
    encA --> vecDB[(Vector Database Index)]
    
    userQ[User Query] --> encQ[Query Encoder]
    encQ --> simCalc[Similarity Search Top-k]
    vecDB --> simCalc
    simCalc --> topDocs[Top-k Relevant Chunks]
    
    topDocs --> promptBuilder[Prompt Template Constructor]
    userQ --> promptBuilder
    promptBuilder --> llmCtx[Frozen LLM Generator]
    llmCtx --> answer[Grounded Response with Citations]
    
    style vecDB fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style llmCtx fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style answer fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Comparison: Naive RAG vs Advanced RAG vs Modular RAG

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NaiveRAG[Naive RAG Basic Pipeline]
        N1[Index Documents] --> N2[Retrieve Top-k] --> N3[Concatenate with Query] --> N4[LLM Generates Answer]
    end
    
    subgraph AdvRAG[Advanced RAG Optimized Pipeline]
        A1[Query Rewriting and Expansion] --> A2[Hybrid Retrieval BM25 plus Dense]
        A2 --> A3[Re-ranking with Cross Encoder]
        A3 --> A4[Context Compression]
        A4 --> A5[LLM Generates Answer]
    end
    
    subgraph ModRAG[Modular RAG Flexible Pipeline]
        M1[Intelligent Router] --> M2{Query Type}
        M2 --> M3[Vector Search]
        M2 --> M4[Web Search Fallback]
        M2 --> M5[SQL Database Query]
        M2 --> M6[Calculator Tool]
        M3 --> M7[Response Synthesizer]
        M4 --> M7
        M5 --> M7
        M6 --> M7
        M7 --> M8[Final Answer with Source Attribution]
    end
    
    style NaiveRAG fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AdvRAG fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ModRAG fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Internal Mechanics of a Vector Database Lookup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Query Embedding q in R^d] --> N1[Normalize to Unit Length]
    N1 --> HNSW[Approximate Nearest Neighbor via HNSW Graph]
    HNSW --> C1[Candidate Set of size ef]
    C1 --> Refine[Re-rank by Exact Cosine Similarity]
    Refine --> TopK[Select Top-k Vectors]
    TopK --> Map[Lookup Original Text Chunks]
    Map --> Output[Return List of Passages to LLM]
    
    style HNSW fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TopK fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Output fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A well-labeled RAG pipeline diagram is worth **3 to 4 marks on its own** in a 14-mark question. Always label **(a) Offline Indexing, (b) Online Retrieval, (c) Augmented Generation** as three distinct phases.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Mention any two advantages it offers over a vanilla LLM.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a neural architecture that combines a *retriever* (which fetches relevant passages from an external knowledge corpus) with a *generator* (an LLM) so that the generated response is **conditioned on the retrieved context** in addition to the user query.
>
> **Advantages (2 marks — write any two):**
> 1. **Reduced Hallucination:** Responses are grounded in real retrieved documents, drastically lowering fabrication.
> 2. **Updatable Knowledge:** The corpus can be refreshed without retraining the LLM, overcoming the knowledge-cutoff problem.
> 3. **Domain Adaptability:** Plug in private enterprise data without expensive fine-tuning.
> 4. **Verifiability / Citations:** The system can point to source passages, enabling auditability.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between sparse and dense retrieval mechanisms used in RAG.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Sparse Retrieval (e.g., BM25) | Dense Retrieval (e.g., DPR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | High-dimensional word-count vectors | Low-dimensional learned embeddings ($d = 768$ typically) |
| **Similarity** | Term-overlap / TF-IDF | Cosine or dot product in semantic space |
| **Synonym Handling** | Poor (exact match) | Strong (semantic) |
| **Training Required** | No | Yes |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [OPTION 1]**

#### **Q.A (a) Explain the complete architecture of a RAG system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the retriever and the generator in detail. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

A Retrieval-Augmented Generation system operates in two phases — **offline indexing** and **online retrieval-augmented generation**.

**Step 1 — Offline Indexing (2 marks):**

Raw documents $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$ from sources such as PDFs, web pages, or internal wikis are first split into smaller, semantically coherent *chunks* $c_i$ (typically 100–300 tokens). Each chunk is then passed through an embedding encoder $E(\cdot)$ — usually a transformer bi-encoder such as `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` — to produce a fixed-dimensional vector $v_i = E(c_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. These vectors are stored in a vector database (FAISS, Pinecone, Chroma) along with a pointer back to the original chunk text.

**Step 2 — Query Encoding (1 mark):**

The user submits a query $x$. The same encoder $E(\cdot)$ produces a query vector $q = E(x)$. For cosine similarity, both query and chunk vectors are L2-normalized to unit length so that the dot product equals the cosine.

**Step 3 — Retrieval (2 marks):**

The retriever computes the similarity score between $q$ and every stored vector $v_i$:

$$
s_i = \cos(q, v_i) = \frac{q \cdot v_i}{\Vert q \Vert \, \Vert v_i \Vert}
$$

The Top-$k$ chunks with the highest scores are returned. In production, *approximate* nearest-neighbor (ANN) search via **HNSW** graphs is used to avoid the $O(N)$ cost of exact search.

**Step 4 — Augmented Generation (2 marks):**

The retrieved chunks $Z = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_k\}$ and the original query $x$ are inserted into a prompt template:

$$
p = \text{"Context: } z_1 \oplus z_2 \oplus \dots \oplus z_k \text{. Question: } x \text{ Answer concisely."}
$$

This prompt is fed to a frozen LLM (e.g., LLaMA-3, GPT-4) which generates the final answer $y$, often with inline citations to the source chunks.

**Diagram Reference:** See the pipeline in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### **Q.A (b) With suitable equations, explain the cosine similarity metric and BM25 scoring used in retrieval. State one advantage of each. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Cosine Similarity (3.5 marks):**

For two embedding vectors $A$ and $B$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, the cosine of the angle between them is:

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i^2} \, \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} b_i^2}}
$$

The closer $\cos(\theta)$ is to $+1$, the more semantically similar the two vectors are. **Advantage:** *Scale-invariant* — it captures directional alignment regardless of vector magnitude, making it ideal for comparing variable-length documents encoded into fixed-size vectors.

**BM25 (3.5 marks):**

The BM25 relevance score of a query $q$ against a document $d$ is:

$$
\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t, d) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(t, d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$

The IDF term is:

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \ln\!\left(\frac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)
$$

where $f(t,d)$ is term frequency of $t$ in $d$, $N$ is the total document count, $n_t$ is the number of documents containing $t$, $\vert d \vert$ is document length, and `avgdl` is the average document length. **Advantage:** *Interpretable* — every term's contribution is explicit, and it robustly handles rare technical terms that dense retrievers may mis-embed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [OPTION 2]**

#### **Q.B (a) Discuss the limitations of vanilla Large Language Models and explain how RAG addresses each. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

A vanilla LLM, no matter how large, suffers from three structural limitations:

**Limitation 1 — Knowledge Cutoff (2 marks):**

LLMs are trained on static snapshots of the internet. Once trained, the model is *frozen* in time. It cannot answer questions about events that occurred after its training data was collected.
> **How RAG fixes it:** RAG retrieves from a *live, updatable* corpus. Adding new documents to the vector database takes seconds, with no retraining required.

**Limitation 2 — Hallucination (2 marks):**

LLMs are next-token predictors, not knowledge bases. They can produce fluent, plausible, but factually false statements — especially in low-resource domains.
> **How RAG fixes it:** The LLM is *conditioned* on real retrieved context. Empirically, RAG reduces hallucination by 40–60% in fact-seeking QA tasks because the model is forced to anchor its tokens in real text.

**Limitation 3 — Opaque / Un-citable Reasoning (1.5 marks):**

Vanilla LLMs cannot tell you *which source* a fact came from, making them unsuitable for legal, medical, or enterprise use.
> **How RAG fixes it:** Every retrieved chunk is traceable. The system can return the source URL or document title alongside the answer.

**Limitation 4 — Domain Specificity (1.5 marks):**

A general LLM performs poorly on a company's internal jargon, internal product names, or private sales data.
> **How RAG fixes it:** The corpus can be replaced wholesale with internal documents, instantly specializing the system without expensive fine-tuning.

#### **Q.B (b) With a block diagram, describe the three paradigms of RAG — Naive, Advanced, and Modular. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Naive RAG (2 marks):**

The original "vanilla" RAG pattern introduced in 2020. Steps: **(1)** chunk documents, **(2)** embed, **(3)** store, **(4)** retrieve Top-$k$, **(5)** concatenate with query, **(6)** prompt LLM. **Limitation:** Suffers from poor recall, low precision at the chunk boundaries, and no re-ranking — irrelevant context can confuse the LLM.

**Advanced RAG (2.5 marks):**

Adds *pre-retrieval* and *post-retrieval* optimization:

- **Pre-retrieval:** Query rewriting, HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings), query expansion.
- **Retrieval:** Hybrid search (BM25 + dense), sliding-window chunking with overlap, metadata filtering.
- **Post-retrieval:** Cross-encoder *re-ranking* (e.g., Cohere Rerank, MonoT5), context compression via LLM-based summarization.

**Modular RAG (2.5 marks):**

The most flexible paradigm. The pipeline is decomposed into *independent, swappable modules* connected by an intelligent router. Modules include vector search, web search, SQL queries, calculators, and specialized sub-LLMs. The router selects which modules to invoke based on the query type. This is the foundation of modern agentic RAG systems.

**Diagram Reference:** See **Section 4.2** of these notes for a side-by-side comparison.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing RAG with Fine-Tuning:** Many students write "RAG retrains the model" — this is WRONG. RAG keeps the LLM **frozen**; it only conditions the prompt. Fine-tuning updates the weights. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the offline/online distinction:** Always clarify the **indexing phase is offline** (one-time) and the **retrieval phase is online** (per-query). Failing to do so costs clarity marks.
> 3. **Missing the marginalization equation:** A direct 3-mark sub-question often asks for the **RAG probability equation** $P(y \mid x) = \sum_z P_\eta(z \mid x) P_\theta(y \mid x, z)$. Memorize it verbatim.
> 4. **In diagrams, never use generic "End" nodes** — label them clearly as "Final Response," "Vector DB," etc. Unlabeled boxes are penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this page the night before the exam.**

- **RAG** = Retriever (finds context) + Generator (LLM writes answer) grounded in an external corpus.
- The **closed-book → open-book** analogy is the best one-liner definition.
- Core equation: $P(y \mid x) \approx \sum_{z \in \text{Top-}k} P_\eta(z \mid x) \, P_\theta(y \mid x, z)$
- **Cosine similarity** is the *default* dense retrieval metric; range $[-1, 1]$; scale-invariant.
- **BM25** is the *default* sparse retrieval metric; uses $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b \approx 0.75$.
- **RAG ≠ Fine-tuning.** RAG is for *knowledge*; fine-tuning is for *style/format/reasoning*.
- **Three paradigms:** Naive (basic), Advanced (with re-ranking + query rewriting), Modular (routed, agentic).
- **Chunking** typically uses 100–512 token windows with overlap to preserve context boundaries.
- **Vector DBs** use **HNSW** graphs for sub-linear ANN search.
- **Hybrid retrieval** (BM25 + dense) is the production standard for maximum recall.
- **Hallucination, Knowledge Cutoff, Domain Gap** are the *three problems* RAG solves.
- The LLM in a *naive* RAG is **frozen** — no weight updates occur.
- RAG is **preferred** when knowledge is dynamic, large, or requires **source citation**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimizing RAG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimizing RAG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Optimization** refers to the systematic enhancement of the three sequential stages of a RAG pipeline — **Indexing, Retrieval, and Generation** — to maximize the contextual precision, factual faithfulness, and computational efficiency of LLM responses grounded in external knowledge bases.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECMT862)** syllabus framing, *Optimizing RAG* encompasses the techniques used to push a baseline "Naive RAG" architecture toward an "Advanced / Modular RAG" architecture by tuning the retriever, the index, the query, and the post-retrieval context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Optimization in RAG is **NOT** about tuning the LLM weights. It is about engineering the *context window* — i.e., the documents, embeddings, query formulation, and reranking — that gets fed to a frozen LLM.

The optimization spectrum is typically classified along three axes:

1. **Pre-Retrieval Optimization** — improving *what* gets indexed and *how* queries are formulated.
2. **Retrieval Optimization** — improving the *embedding model, index structure (HNSW, IVF), and search algorithm (dense, sparse, hybrid)*.
3. **Post-Retrieval Optimization** — reranking, compressing, filtering, and reformatting the retrieved context before generation.

| RAG Maturity Level | Description | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Naive RAG** | Simple chunking + cosine similarity + top-k | Low (definition only) |
| **Advanced RAG** | Pre-retrieval (query rewrite) + reranking + hybrid search | **High (core)** |
| **Modular RAG** | Self-RAG, CRAG, agentic routing, iterative refinement | **High (14-mark scope)** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Overworked Librarian"**
>
> Imagine you ask a librarian (the LLM) a research question. A **Naive RAG** librarian is given the **wrong books** (poor retrieval) opened to **irrelevant pages** (bad chunking) and told to write a report in **30 seconds** (no reranking, no compression). The result is hallucinated nonsense.
>
> An **Optimized RAG** librarian, on the other hand:
> 1. **Clarifies your question** (query rewriting / expansion).
> 2. **Picks the right books** (better embeddings, hybrid search).
> 3. **Skims and highlights** the relevant passages (reranking).
> 4. **Trims the noise** (context compression).
> 5. **Cross-checks facts** (self-correction / CRAG).
>
> Same librarian, same books, but the **information pipeline** is now optimized. *This is RAG optimization.*

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these **bold** metrics — they appear in nearly every KTU Part A and Part B question on this module.

- **Recall@K** — fraction of relevant documents in the top-K retrieved.
- **MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)** — $1/rank$ of the first correct document.
- **NDCG@K (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain)** — measures ranking quality with graded relevance.
- **Context Relevance** — semantic similarity between query and retrieved chunks.
- **Faithfulness** — degree to which the generated answer is grounded in the retrieved context.
- **Answer Relevance** — semantic similarity between the query and the final generated answer.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Optimized RAG Pipeline — Structured Logic Steps

The pipeline is decomposed into **five engineering blocks**, each independently optimizable:

### Block A — Indexing Optimization (Offline)
- **Chunking Strategy Selection**: Fixed-size, recursive, semantic, or document-aware.
- **Embedding Model Fine-Tuning**: Domain-adapt contrastive learning (e.g., TSDAE, SimCSE).
- **Metadata Enrichment**: Injecting titles, timestamps, and source provenance.
- **Index Type**: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) for ANN; IVF for large-scale.

### Block B — Query Optimization (Pre-Retrieval)
- **Query Rewriting** — reformulate ambiguous queries using an LLM.
- **Query Expansion** — append synonyms, pseudo-relevance feedback terms.
- **Query Decomposition** — break a multi-hop query into sub-queries.
- **HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings)** — generate a fake answer, embed it, retrieve neighbors of the *answer* instead of the *question*.

### Block C — Retrieval Optimization
- **Dense Retrieval** — bi-encoder cosine similarity.
- **Sparse Retrieval** — BM25 / SPLADE.
- **Hybrid Retrieval** — combination of dense + sparse via RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion).
- **Multi-Vector Retrieval** — ColBERT (late interaction).

### Block D — Reranking & Compression (Post-Retrieval)
- **Cross-Encoder Reranking** — query and document jointly encoded for accurate scoring.
- **Cohere Rerank / BGE-Reranker** — production-grade rerankers.
- **Contextual Compression** — drop irrelevant sentences via extractive compression.

### Block E — Generation Optimization
- **Prompt Engineering** — system prompt with grounding instructions.
- **Citation Injection** — force the LLM to attribute claims to source IDs.
- **Self-RAG / CRAG** — model critiques its own output and triggers web fallback.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why we do it | How it improves RAG |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Chunking** | Fixed-size splits cut sentences mid-thought | Preserves topical coherence, raises Recall@K |
| **HyDE** | Short queries are semantically sparse | Bridges query–document vocabulary gap |
| **Hybrid Search** | Dense misses exact keywords; BM25 misses paraphrases | Fuses complementary signals |
| **Reranking** | Bi-encoders compress to one vector → information loss | Recomputes similarity with full cross-attention |
| **Context Compression** | LLMs lose focus in long contexts (>8K tokens) | Reduces noise, lowers cost, boosts faithfulness |
| **Self-RAG** | LLMs hallucinate unsupported claims | Adds "reflection tokens" to verify grounding |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Inside this table, the pipe character is replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. All formulas are mathematically verified.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \dfrac{q \cdot d}{\vert\vert q\vert\vert \, \vert\vert d\vert\vert}$ | $q, d$ = query, doc vectors | Dense retrieval scoring |
| 2 | **BM25 Score** | $\text{BM25}(q,d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f(t,d)\,(k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}$ | $f(t,d)$=term freq, $\vert d\vert$=doc len, $avgdl$=avg doc len, $k_1, b$ = hyperparams | Sparse retrieval |
| 3 | **IDF** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)$ | $N$ = total docs, $n_t$ = docs containing $t$ | BM25 sub-component |
| 4 | **Recall@K** | $\text{Recall@K} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved}_K \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | Sets of relevant / retrieved docs | Retrieval coverage |
| 5 | **MRR** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \dfrac{1}{\text{rank}_i}$ | $\text{rank}_i$ = rank of 1st correct doc | Ranking quality |
| 6 | **NDCG@K** | $\text{NDCG@K} = \dfrac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}},\ \text{DCG@K} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \dfrac{2^{rel_i} - 1}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | $rel_i$ = graded relevance | Graded ranking quality |
| 7 | **RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion)** | $\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r \in R} \dfrac{1}{k + r_r(d)}$ | $r_r(d)$ = rank of $d$ in retriever $r$, $k$ = 60 (typical) | Hybrid search fusion |
| 8 | **Faithfulness Score** | $F = \dfrac{\vert \text{Claims}_{\text{supported}} \vert}{\vert \text{Claims}_{\text{total}} \vert}$ | Atomic claim-level decomposition | Hallucination check |
| 9 | **Information Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Useful tokens}}{\text{Total context tokens}}$ | Token-level utilization | Compression efficiency |
| 10 | **Latency Budget** | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{embed}} + T_{\text{retrieve}} + T_{\text{rerank}} + T_{\text{LLM}}$ | Sum of pipeline stages | Production SLA planning |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These techniques are the **backbone of production AI systems** at companies like Meta (RAG over Wikipedia in Retrieval-Enhanced Transformers), Microsoft (Copilot over enterprise docs), and Perplexity (web-scale RAG with reranking). For KTU, the **practical implication** is: RAG optimization reduces hallucination, lowers token cost, and enables enterprise-grade chatbots over private knowledge bases.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cosine Similarity for Dense Retrieval

We start from the geometric definition of the angle between two vectors in an $n$-dimensional embedding space.

**Step 1 — Define the inner product.**
The dot product of two vectors $q$ and $d$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is:

$$
q \cdot d \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \, d_i
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Euclidean (L2) norm.**
The L2 norm of a vector $x$ is its geometric length:

$$
\vert\vert x \vert\vert \;=\; \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} \;=\; \sqrt{x \cdot x}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the cosine definition.**
For two vectors, the cosine of the angle between them is:

$$
\cos(\theta) \;=\; \dfrac{q \cdot d}{\vert\vert q \vert\vert \, \vert\vert d \vert\vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Map to retrieval semantics.**
When $\theta = 0$ (vectors aligned), $\cos(\theta) = 1$ — perfect semantic match. When $\theta = 90°$, $\cos(\theta) = 0$ — orthogonal (irrelevant). When $\theta = 180°$, $\cos(\theta) = -1$ — opposite meaning.

**Step 5 — Final retrieval formula.**

$$
\boxed{\ \text{sim}(q, d) \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i d_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2} \;\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}}\ }
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the metric used by every dense retriever (DPR, BGE, E5, OpenAI `text-embedding-3`). It is **scale-invariant**, which is why embeddings are typically L2-normalized to unit length before indexing — then cosine similarity reduces to a single dot product, enabling **Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)** in FAISS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BM25 Score from First Principles

BM25 (Best Matching 25) is the dominant **sparse retrieval** function. It rewards term frequency but penalizes long documents.

**Step 1 — Term frequency saturation.**
A raw term count is unbounded. We introduce a saturating function with hyperparameter $k_1$ (typically 1.2):

$$
\text{TF}_{\text{sat}}(t, d) \;=\; \dfrac{f(t, d)}{f(t, d) + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Document length normalization.**
The factor $\dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}$ is **1.0** for an average-length document. If $b = 0$, length is ignored. If $b = 1$, full normalization. Typical $b = 0.75$.

**Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency.**
A rare term across the corpus is more informative:

$$
\text{IDF}(t) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N - n_t + 0.5}{n_t + 0.5} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine via summation.**
Score is the sum of per-query-term contributions:

$$
\boxed{\ \text{BM25}(q, d) \;=\; \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f(t, d) \, (k_1 + 1)}{f(t, d) + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}\ }
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** BM25 is **interpretable**, **keyword-exact**, and **complementary to dense retrieval** — which is why hybrid RAG (BM25 + dense) almost always beats either alone on benchmarks like BEIR.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) for Hybrid Search

RRF combines multiple ranked lists (e.g., BM25 list + dense list) **without** requiring score calibration.

**Step 1 — Problem statement.**
Given $R$ retrievers, each producing a ranked list of documents. We want a single fused ranking.

**Step 2 — Reciprocal rank score.**
For document $d$ in retriever $r$, assign a score of $\dfrac{1}{k + r_r(d)}$, where $r_r(d)$ is the 1-indexed rank and $k$ (default **60**) is a smoothing constant that prevents top-ranked items from dominating.

**Step 3 — Aggregate across retrievers.**

$$
\boxed{\ \text{RRF}(d) \;=\; \sum_{r \in R} \dfrac{1}{k + r_r(d)}\ }
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose three retrievers return ranks of document $X$ as $\{1, 3, 2\}$ with $k = 60$:

$$
\text{RRF}(X) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{61} + \dfrac{1}{63} + \dfrac{1}{62} \;\approx\; 0.01639 + 0.01587 + 0.01613 \;=\; 0.04839
$$

A document ranked $\{1, 1, 1\}$ scores $\dfrac{3}{61} \approx 0.04918$ — slightly higher, as expected.

> **Conversion Logic:** RRF is the **de facto** fusion method in production RAG (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Vespa) because it requires no score normalization — it just needs the **rank** from each retriever.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Technique vs. Bottleneck vs. Gain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam artifact**. Memorize the *bottleneck → technique* mapping.

| Pipeline Stage | Bottleneck | Optimization Technique | Expected Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chunking** | Mid-sentence splits | Recursive / semantic chunking | +10–25% Recall@K |
| **Embedding** | Out-of-domain vectors | Domain fine-tuning (TSDAE) | +5–15% NDCG |
| **Query** | Short / ambiguous | HyDE / query rewrite | +8–20% Recall@K |
| **Retrieval** | Lexical ↔ semantic gap | Hybrid (BM25 + dense) | +5–12% MRR |
| **Reranking** | Bi-encoder loss | Cross-encoder reranker | +10–30% NDCG@10 |
| **Context** | Lost-in-the-middle | Compression + reordering | +15% Faithfulness |
| **Generation** | Hallucination | Self-RAG / CRAG | +20% Faithfulness |
| **Latency** | Slow ANN search | HNSW + GPU FAISS | 5–10× speedup |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Optimized RAG Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([User Query]) --> qoptA[Query Optimizer]
    qoptA --> qrA[Query Rewriter LLM]
    qoptA --> qeA[Query Expansion]
    qoptA --> hydeA[HyDE Generator]
    qrA --> embA[Embedding Model]
    qeA --> embA
    hydeA --> embA

    embA --> retA{Retriever Router}
    retA --> denseA[Dense Retriever FAISS]
    retA --> sparseA[BM25 Sparse Index]
    retA --> hybridA[ColBERT Multi-Vector]

    denseA --> fuseA[RRF Fusion Layer]
    sparseA --> fuseA
    hybridA --> fuseA

    fuseA --> rerankA[Cross-Encoder Reranker]
    rerankA --> compressA[Contextual Compressor]
    compressA --> filterA[Relevance Filter]

    filterA --> promptA[Prompt Constructor]
    promptA --> llmA[Frozen LLM]
    llmA --> verifyA{Self-RAG Verifier}
    verifyA -->|Grounded| outA([Final Answer with Citations])
    verifyA -->|Not Grounded| webA[Web Fallback / CRAG]
    webA --> llmA

    style startA fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style outA fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style webA fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style verifyA fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
```

> **Engineering Note:** The yellow *Self-RAG Verifier* is the **modular RAG** extension. It issues reflection tokens (`[Retrieve]`, `[IsRel]`, `[IsSup]`, `[IsUse]`) to decide whether to re-retrieve or accept the answer.

---

## 4.2 Pre-Retrieval Optimization: Query Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inq([Raw User Query]) --> clsA{Query Classifier}
    clsA -->|Ambiguous| rwA[LLM Rewriter]
    clsA -->|Multi-hop| deA[Query Decomposer]
    clsA -->|Short| expA[Expansion via Synonyms]
    clsA -->|Vague| hydeB[HyDE Hypothetical Doc]
    rwA --> outq([Optimized Query Vector])
    deA --> outq
    expA --> outq
    hydeB --> outq

    style inq fill:#E1F5FE
    style outq fill:#C8E6C9
    style clsA fill:#FFF59D
```

---

## 4.3 Bi-Encoder vs. Cross-Encoder Architecture (Reranking Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIE["BI-ENCODER STAGE (Fast, Pre-Rank)"]
        q1[Query] --> enc1[Encoder E1]
        d1[Document] --> enc1
        enc1 --> sim1[Cosine Similarity]
        sim1 --> topk[Top-K Candidates K = 100]
    end

    subgraph CROE["CROSS-ENCODER STAGE (Slow, Accurate Re-Rank)"]
        topk --> concat[Concatenate Q + D]
        concat --> enc2[Joint Transformer Encoder]
        enc2 --> score[Relevance Score 0 to 1]
    end

    score --> final[Final Top-N where N = 5]

    style BIE fill:#E3F2FD
    style CROE fill:#FFF3E0
    style final fill:#C8E6C9
```

> **Engineering Note:** The bi-encoder encodes query and document **independently** (fast, cacheable). The cross-encoder encodes them **jointly** with full self-attention (slow, accurate). This **two-stage cascade** is the industry standard (used by Cohere Rerank, BGE-Reranker, MonoT5).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`
> **Define RAG optimization. List the three stages where optimization is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
RAG optimization is the systematic tuning of the retrieval-augmented generation pipeline to improve factual grounding, contextual relevance, and efficiency of LLM outputs. The three optimization stages are:

1. **Pre-Retrieval Optimization** — chunking strategy, embedding fine-tuning, query rewriting.
2. **Retrieval Optimization** — dense / sparse / hybrid search, ANN indexing.
3. **Post-Retrieval Optimization** — reranking, contextual compression, reranking, and self-verification.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for correct sub-items.)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]`
> **What is HyDE? How does it improve retrieval?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings)** is a pre-retrieval optimization technique in which a *smaller LLM* generates a *hypothetical answer* to the user's query *without actually retrieving any documents*. This fake answer is then embedded, and the system retrieves real documents that are **semantically similar to the answer**, rather than to the short question. (2 marks)

It improves retrieval by **bridging the query–document vocabulary gap**: short queries are semantically sparse, but a generated hypothetical answer carries rich topical signal. This boosts Recall@K on zero-shot benchmarks by 5–20%. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of an **Advanced RAG pipeline** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **bi-encoder** and **cross-encoder** retrieval. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **BM25 scoring function** from first principles and explain each hyperparameter. Show how it is fused with dense retrieval using **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Advanced RAG Architecture (4 marks):**
An Advanced RAG pipeline consists of:

- **Indexing Stage**: Recursive chunking → embedding model (e.g., BGE-large) → vector store (FAISS / Pinecone) with HNSW index, alongside a BM25 sparse index.
- **Pre-Retrieval**: Query rewriting using a small LLM to disambiguate.
- **Retrieval Stage**: Parallel dense + sparse retrieval.
- **Post-Retrieval**: Cross-encoder reranker (e.g., Cohere Rerank) → top-5 contexts.
- **Generation**: Frozen LLM (e.g., Llama-3-8B) with a grounded system prompt and citation requirement.

**Bi-Encoder vs. Cross-Encoder (3 marks):**

| Property | Bi-Encoder | Cross-Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Encoding | Query & doc **separately** | Query & doc **jointly** |
| Speed | **Fast** (cacheable) | **Slow** (re-encode per pair) |
| Accuracy | Lower (independent vectors) | **Higher** (full self-attention) |
| Role in RAG | First-stage retrieval (top-100) | Second-stage reranking (top-5) |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**BM25 Derivation (4 marks)** — Refer to Section 3.2 above. State the final formula:

$$
\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{f(t, d)(k_1+1)}{f(t, d) + k_1\left(1 - b + \dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}
$$

**Hyperparameters (2 marks):**

- $k_1$ — term-frequency saturation. Typical $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$. Higher = more weight on repeated terms.
- $b$ — document-length normalization. Typical $b = 0.75$. $b = 0$ ignores length, $b = 1$ full normalization.

**RRF Fusion (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r=1}^{R} \dfrac{1}{k + r_r(d)},\quad k = 60
$$

This score-agnostic fusion combines the BM25 ranking and dense ranking into a single list.

---

> ### **Question B** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]`
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **chunking strategies** used in RAG indexing. Why is **semantic chunking** preferred over fixed-size chunking? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **Self-RAG** and **Corrective RAG (CRAG)**. How do they reduce hallucinations in LLM outputs? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Chunking Strategies (4 marks):**

1. **Fixed-Size Chunking** — splits text into $N$-token blocks with optional overlap. Simple, but cuts mid-sentence and breaks context.
2. **Recursive Chunking** — splits hierarchically by separators (paragraph → sentence → word). Preserves natural boundaries.
3. **Semantic Chunking** — uses embedding similarity between adjacent sentences; a new chunk starts when similarity drops below a threshold.
4. **Document-Aware Chunking** — uses document structure (headings, tables, code blocks) as natural boundaries.

**Why Semantic Chunking is Preferred (3 marks):**

- **Topical coherence**: each chunk is a self-contained semantic unit.
- **Higher Recall@K**: relevant passages are not split across chunk boundaries.
- **Better embedding quality**: chunks form well-defined semantic units, improving dense retrieval accuracy.
- Empirically, semantic chunking improves end-to-end RAG faithfulness by **10–20%** over fixed-size chunking on benchmarks like Natural Questions.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Self-RAG (3.5 marks):**
Self-RAG, introduced by Asai et al. (2023), trains the LLM to emit special **reflection tokens** at inference time:

- `[Retrieve]` — decide if retrieval is needed.
- `[IsRel]` — is the retrieved passage relevant?
- `[IsSup]` — does the passage support the generated claim?
- `[IsUse]` — is the final answer useful?

The model **critiques its own output** and triggers re-retrieval if grounding fails. This reduces hallucination by explicitly forcing the LLM to verify each atomic claim.

**CRAG — Corrective RAG (3.5 marks):**
CRAG (Yan et al., 2024) adds an **external evaluator** between retrieval and generation:

- A lightweight retriever-evaluator scores each retrieved document as `{Correct, Incorrect, Ambiguous}`.
- If `Correct` → use as-is.
- If `Incorrect` → discard and trigger a **web search fallback**.
- If `Ambiguous` → run a **knowledge refinement** step (extract only query-relevant sentences).

CRAG reduces hallucinations by **filtering out ungrounded context** before it reaches the LLM, typically improving faithfulness by 15–25% on hallucination benchmarks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "RAG optimization" with "LLM fine-tuning."** RAG optimization never changes LLM weights. It modifies the context pipeline. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this conceptual slip.
> 2. **Writing BM25 without showing the IDF component.** The full formula must include both TF saturation and IDF. Partial formula = 50% credit only.
> 3. **Mixing up bi-encoder and cross-encoder roles.** Bi-encoder = first stage (fast). Cross-encoder = second stage (accurate). Reversing them = 0 marks for the comparison.
> 4. **Forgetting the $k$ smoothing constant in RRF.** The default $k = 60$ is **essential** for the formula to be correct.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in the 14-mark question.** KTU mandates a *neat block diagram* for full marks in any architecture question. A textual-only answer loses at least 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **RAG Optimization ≠ LLM Fine-Tuning.** It improves the *context*, not the model weights.
- **Three stages of optimization:** Pre-Retrieval → Retrieval → Post-Retrieval.
- **Chunking matters:** Semantic > Recursive > Fixed-size. Chunk size typically 256–512 tokens with 10–20% overlap.
- **Embedding fine-tuning** uses contrastive losses (SimCSE, TSDAE) for domain adaptation.
- **Query optimization techniques:** Rewrite, Expand, Decompose, **HyDE** (bridge the vocabulary gap).
- **Hybrid Search = BM25 + Dense**, fused via **RRF** with $k = 60$.
- **Reranking cascade:** Bi-encoder (top-100) → Cross-encoder (top-5). Cross-encoders use joint self-attention → higher accuracy.
- **Context compression** removes irrelevant sentences, boosting faithfulness and reducing token cost.
- **Self-RAG** uses reflection tokens `[Retrieve]`, `[IsRel]`, `[IsSup]`, `[IsUse]` for self-critique.
- **CRAG** uses an evaluator to filter documents as Correct / Incorrect / Ambiguous, with web fallback.
- **Key Metrics:** Recall@K, MRR, NDCG@K, Faithfulness, Answer Relevance.
- **Production-grade stack:** Recursive chunking + BGE embeddings + FAISS-HNSW + BM25 + RRF + Cohere Rerank + Llama-3 + Self-RAG verifier.
- **Latency budget:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{embed}} + T_{\text{retrieve}} + T_{\text{rerank}} + T_{\text{LLM}}$.
- **Best gains (rule of thumb):** Reranker (+10–30% NDCG) > Semantic Chunking (+10–25% Recall) > HyDE (+8–20% Recall) > Hybrid (+5–12% MRR).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enhancing RAG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Enhancing RAG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** is a hybrid neural architecture that couples a parametric LLM (the generator) with a non-parametric external memory (the retriever) to ground the model's outputs in verifiable, up-to-date, domain-specific knowledge. **Enhancing RAG** refers to a family of architectural, algorithmic, and post-retrieval refinement techniques designed to improve the **relevance**, **faithfulness**, **recall**, and **robustness** of the retriever–generator pipeline beyond the naive "retrieve-then-read" baseline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Enhancement techniques are explicitly categorized into **pre-retrieval** (query-side), **retrieval** (index-side), **post-retrieval** (context-side), and **generation-side** (LLM-side) optimizations. A complete answer must classify the technique correctly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Open-Book Exam, But Smarter" Intuition

Imagine a student taking an **open-book exam**:
- **Naive RAG** = a student who grabs the *first book on the shelf* matching a keyword and copies a paragraph blindly.
- **Enhanced RAG** = the same student who (1) **rewrites the question** in their own words to search better, (2) **checks three different libraries** (dense + sparse search), (3) **skims and ranks** the most relevant pages, (4) **cross-references** with their own reasoning, and (5) **double-checks** whether the final answer actually matches the question.

The "enhancements" are simply the *smarter study habits* layered onto the basic open-book process.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of RAG Enhancement

| Pillar | Stage Touched | Primary Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| Query Enhancement | Pre-Retrieval | "How do I ask the *right* question?" |
| Index Enhancement | Retrieval | "Where is the *right* document?" |
| Retrieval Enhancement | Retrieval | "Which *method* retrieves best?" |
| Post-Retrieval Enhancement | Post-Retrieval | "How do I *clean* the context?" |
| Generation Enhancement | Generation | "How do I *reason* over the context?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize:** Recall@K, MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank), nDCG, Faithfulness, Answer Relevancy, Context Precision, Context Recall. **Bold constants:** **Top-K = 10** and **Chunk Size = 512 tokens** are the most commonly cited defaults in the KTU syllabus literature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Naive RAG Pipeline (Baseline)

The vanilla pipeline executes three sequential steps:

1. **Query Embedding:** $q \rightarrow E(q) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ using a sentence-transformer (e.g., `bge-small`, `e5-large`).
2. **Dense Retrieval:** Top-K nearest neighbors via cosine similarity $\text{sim}(q,d) = \frac{E(q) \cdot E(d)}{\vert E(q) \vert \cdot \vert E(d) \vert}$.
3. **Conditional Generation:** $y^* = \arg\max_{y} P_{\text{LLM}}(y \mid q, C)$ where $C = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_K\}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this fails:** Naive RAG suffers from (a) vocabulary mismatch between query and documents, (b) lost-in-the-middle effect when $K > 10$, (c) hallucination when retrieved context is irrelevant, and (d) lack of multi-hop reasoning.

## 2.2 Enhancement Taxonomy — The Why and How

### 2.2.1 Pre-Retrieval (Query-Side) Enhancements

**A. Query Rewriting / Transformation**
- *Why:* User queries are often short, ambiguous, or use different vocabulary than the corpus.
- *How:* A secondary LLM rewrites the query into a more searchable form.
- *Method:* **Rewrite-Retrieve-Read** (Ma et al., 2023) trains a small T5 model $R_\theta$ such that $q' = R_\theta(q)$, then retrieves with $q'$.

**B. Query Expansion (Multi-Query)**
- *Why:* Single query misses semantically related documents.
- *How:* LLM generates $N$ paraphrased queries $\{q'_1, \ldots, q'_N\}$; retrieval is union-ed.
- *Formula:* $C = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \text{TopK}(E(q'_i), \mathcal{D})$.

**C. Step-Back Prompting**
- *Why:* Original query is too specific; an abstracted "step-back" question retrieves broader context.
- *Example:* "What was GDP of India in 2023?" → "What are India's macroeconomic indicators over the last decade?"

**D. HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings)**
- *Why:* Dense retrievers work better on document-document similarity than query-document similarity.
- *How:* LLM generates a *hypothetical* answer $a_{\text{hyp}}$ *without* retrieval; embed $a_{\text{hyp}}$ and search.
- *Key insight:* $E(a_{\text{hyp}})$ lives in the document embedding space, closing the query–document gap.

### 2.2.2 Retrieval (Index-Side) Enhancements

**A. Hybrid Search (Dense + Sparse)**
- Combines semantic (vector) search with lexical (BM25) search.
- *Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF):*
$$
\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r \in \{dense, sparse\}} \frac{1}{k_0 + rank_r(d)}
$$
where $k_0 = 60$ is the standard damping constant.

**B. Hierarchical / Two-Stage Retrieval**
- Stage 1: Cheap BM25 retrieves Top-1000.
- Stage 2: Expensive cross-encoder reranks Top-1000 → Top-10.

**C. Chunking Strategy Optimization**
- **Fixed-size:** 256/512 tokens, 10–20% overlap.
- **Sentence-aware:** Splits on `nltk` sentence boundaries.
- **Semantic chunking:** Splits where cosine similarity between consecutive sentences drops below threshold $\tau$.
- **Parent-document retriever:** Embed small chunks, return large parent chunks.

### 2.2.3 Post-Retrieval (Context-Side) Enhancements

**A. Re-ranking**
- *Bi-encoder:* Encodes query and document independently (fast, used in retrieval).
- *Cross-encoder:* Concatenates $q \oplus d$ and scores jointly (slow, used in reranking).
- *Models:* `bge-reranker-large`, `Cohere Rerank-3`, `monoT5`.

**B. Context Compression / Filtering**
- Removes irrelevant sentences from retrieved chunks before passing to LLM.
- **LLMLingua** (Microsoft): Uses a small LM to compute perplexity of each token; deletes low-PPL tokens.
- **Selective Context:** Drops tokens with low self-information $-\log P(t)$.

**C. Lost-in-the-Middle Mitigation**
- Liu et al. (2023) showed LLM accuracy drops when relevant info is placed in the *middle* of long contexts.
- *Fix:* Re-rank so most relevant documents occupy the **start** and **end** of the context window.

### 2.2.4 Generation-Side (LLM-Side) Enhancements

**A. Chain-of-Thought RAG (CoT-RAG)**
- Forces LLM to reason step-by-step over retrieved context.
- *Prompt template:* "Based on the following context, think step by step, then answer."

**B. Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2023)**
- LLM emits special **reflection tokens**: `[Retrieve]`, `[IsRel]`, `[IsSup]`, `[IsUse]`.
- Self-evaluates whether retrieval is needed, whether context is relevant, whether the answer is supported.

**C. Corrective RAG (CRAG, Yan et al., 2024)**
- Adds a lightweight *retrieval evaluator* (T5-based) that scores each document as `{Correct, Incorrect, Ambiguous}`.
- For `Incorrect` documents, triggers a **web search fallback**.

**D. GraphRAG (Microsoft, 2024)**
- Builds a *knowledge graph* from the corpus using an LLM.
- Performs **community detection** (Leiden algorithm) on the graph.
- Generates *community summaries* that the LLM consumes for global, corpus-wide questions.

**E. Agentic RAG**
- Replaces the fixed retrieve→read pipeline with an **LLM agent** that iteratively decides *when*, *what*, and *how* to retrieve.
- Tools: vector search, web search, SQL, calculators.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Notation | Default Value / Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \frac{E(q) \cdot E(d)}{\vert E(q) \vert \cdot \vert E(d) \vert}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ | Dense retrieval |
| Reciprocal Rank Fusion | $\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r} \frac{1}{k_0 + rank_r(d)}$ | $k_0 = 60$ | Hybrid search |
| Recall@K | $\frac{\vert \text{relevant} \cap \text{retrieved}_K \vert}{\vert \text{relevant} \vert}$ | K = 10 typical | Evaluation |
| MRR | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{rank_i}$ | Higher is better | Evaluation |
| nDCG@K | $\frac{DCG@K}{IDCG@K}$ | Range $[0,1]$ | Evaluation |
| BM25 Score | $\sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot (1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl})}$ | $k_1=1.5$, $b=0.75$ | Sparse retrieval |
| Context Window | $W_{ctx}$ | 4K–128K tokens | LLM input |
| Chunk Overlap | $o$ | 10–20% of chunk size | Indexing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Enhanced RAG powers production systems in **enterprise search** (Microsoft Copilot), **legal tech** (Harvey AI), **medical QA** (MedRAG), **code assistants** (Cursor), and **customer support** (Zendesk AI). Mastery of these techniques is the #1 hiring skill for Applied AI roles in 2024–2026.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Hybrid Search Outperforms Pure Dense or Pure Sparse

Let $D = \{d_1, \ldots, d_N\}$ be the document corpus. For a query $q$, define:
- $R_{\text{dense}}(q) = $ top-K by cosine similarity $\text{sim}(q, d_i)$.
- $R_{\text{sparse}}(q) = $ top-K by BM25 score $\text{BM25}(q, d_i)$.

We want to show that $R_{\text{hybrid}}(q) = \text{RRF}(R_{\text{dense}} \cup R_{\text{sparse}})$ has higher expected recall.

**Step 1 — Failure mode of dense retrieval:**  
Dense retrievers fail on **out-of-vocabulary (OOV)** terms, **acronyms** ("RAG" → "Retrieval-Augmented Generation"), and **exact-match** queries ("What is the capital of Kazakhstan?").

**Step 2 — Failure mode of sparse retrieval:**  
BM25 fails on **paraphrases** ("car" vs "automobile"), **semantic synonyms**, and **cross-lingual** queries.

**Step 3 — Complementarity assumption:**  
Assume the two failure sets are approximately disjoint: $F_{\text{dense}} \cap F_{\text{sparse}} \approx \emptyset$.

**Step 4 — Recall bound:**  
$$
\text{Recall}_{\text{hybrid}} \geq 1 - \vert F_{\text{dense}} \cup F_{\text{sparse}} \vert / N \geq 1 - (\vert F_{\text{dense}} \vert + \vert F_{\text{sparse}} \vert)/N
$$

**Step 5 — RRF as the fuser:**  
For each document $d$, assign a fused score:

$$
\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r \in \{\text{dense}, \text{sparse}\}} \frac{1}{k_0 + \text{rank}_r(d)}
$$

where $\text{rank}_r(d)$ is the position of $d$ in the ranked list $R_r(q)$, and $k_0 = 60$.

**Step 6 — Intuition:**  
RRF rewards documents that appear *high* in *both* lists. A document at rank 1 in dense and rank 3 in sparse scores $\frac{1}{61} + \frac{1}{63} \approx 0.0326$, while a document at rank 1 in dense only scores $\frac{1}{61} \approx 0.0164$ — half the score. This naturally boosts "double-relevant" documents.

**Step 7 — Practical effect:**  
Empirically (Cormack et al., 2009), RRF improves nDCG@10 by **8–15%** over either retriever alone on TREC benchmarks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Enhancement

| Problem Symptom | Likely Cause | Recommended Enhancement |
|---|---|---|
| Low **Context Recall** (right docs not retrieved) | Poor query formulation | HyDE, Multi-Query, Step-Back |
| Low **Context Precision** (irrelevant docs retrieved) | Noisy similarity | Re-ranking, Hybrid Search |
| Low **Faithfulness** (LLM ignores context) | Lost-in-the-middle, long context | Re-ranking to edges, Compression |
| Low **Answer Relevancy** (off-topic answers) | Hallucination drift | Self-RAG, CRAG |
| **Multi-hop** questions fail | Single-step retrieval | Agentic RAG, Iterative retrieval |
| **Global / corpus-level** questions fail | Local chunk retrieval | GraphRAG |

## 3.3 Worked Example — End-to-End Enhanced RAG Flow

**Query:** "Explain how India aimed to achieve 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030 and list the key policy bottlenecks."

**Step 1 — Query Rewriting (Pre-Retrieval):**  
LLM transforms $q \rightarrow q' = $ "India 500 GW renewable energy target 2030 policies challenges" + "Ministry of Power India NEP 2022–2030".

**Step 2 — Multi-Query Expansion:**  
$q_1 = $ "India 500 GW renewable energy policy 2030"  
$q_2 = $ "MNRE India installed capacity 2030 targets"  
$q_3 = $ "India renewable energy policy bottlenecks land acquisition"

**Step 3 — Hybrid Retrieval (Retrieval):**  
- Dense: `bge-large-en` retrieves 50 candidates via cosine.
- Sparse: BM25 retrieves 50 candidates.
- RRF fuses → 30 unique candidates.

**Step 4 — Re-ranking (Post-Retrieval):**  
`bge-reranker-large` reranks 30 → 5 most relevant documents.

**Step 5 — Context Compression:**  
LLMLingua compresses 5 docs (avg 800 tokens) → 5 docs (avg 350 tokens), preserving key facts.

**Step 6 — Chain-of-Thought Generation (Generation):**  
LLM prompt: *"Use the context. Reason step by step. Cite each claim. Conclude with bottlenecks."*

**Step 7 — Self-Verification (Self-RAG):**  
LLM emits `[IsSup: Yes]`, `[IsUse: Yes]` reflection tokens → answer accepted.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Enhanced RAG Pipeline (Master Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userQuery[USER QUERY] --> qr[Query Rewriter LLM]
    qr --> mq[Multi Query Generator]
    mq --> q1[Query 1]
    mq --> q2[Query 2]
    mq --> q3[Query 3]
    q1 --> hybrid{Hybrid Retriever}
    q2 --> hybrid
    q3 --> hybrid
    hybrid --> dense[Dense Vector Search]
    hybrid --> sparse[BM25 Sparse Search]
    dense --> rrf[RRF Fusion Rank 60]
    sparse --> rrf
    rrf --> rerank[Cross Encoder Reranker]
    rerank --> topk[Top 5 Chunks]
    topk --> compress[Context Compression LLM Lingua]
    compress --> cot[Chain of Thought LLM Generator]
    cot --> selfrag{Self RAG Verifier}
    selfrag --> accept[Final Answer]
    selfrag --> reject[Trigger Web Search Fallback]
    reject --> hybrid
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — RAG Enhancement Taxonomy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Enhancing RAG]
    root --> pre[Pre Retrieval]
    root --> ret[Retrieval]
    root --> post[Post Retrieval]
    root --> gen[Generation]
    pre --> qr[Query Rewriting]
    pre --> mq[Multi Query]
    pre --> sb[Step Back Prompting]
    pre --> hyde[HyDE Hypothetical Docs]
    ret --> hs[Hybrid Search]
    ret --> ch[Chunking Strategy]
    ret --> mr[Metadata Filtering]
    post --> rr[Re Ranking Cross Encoder]
    post --> cc[Context Compression]
    post --> lim[Lost in Middle Fix]
    gen --> cot[Chain of Thought RAG]
    gen --> srag[Self RAG Reflection Tokens]
    gen --> crag[Corrective RAG T5 Evaluator]
    gen --> grag[Graph RAG Knowledge Graph]
    gen --> arag[Agentic RAG Iterative Tools]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Self-RAG Reflection Token Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q[User Query] --> tok1{Retrieve Token Needed}
    tok1 --> yes[Yes]
    tok1 --> no[No Internal Knowledge]
    yes --> ret[Retrieve Top K Documents]
    ret --> tok2{IsRel Relevant}
    tok2 --> rel[Yes Relevant]
    tok2 --> irrel[No Irrelevant]
    irrel --> no
    rel --> tok3{IsSup Supported}
    tok3 --> sup[Yes Supported]
    tok3 --> usup[No Not Supported]
    usup --> ret
    sup --> tok4{IsUse Useful}
    tok4 --> use[Yes Useful]
    tok4 --> unuse[No Not Useful]
    unuse --> ret
    use --> out[Final Grounded Answer]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — GraphRAG Architecture

```mermaid

    corpus[Document Corpus] --> chunk[Chunk Documents]
    chunk --> extract[LLM Entity Relation Extraction]
    extract --> graph[Build Knowledge Graph Nodes Edges]
    graph --> leiden[Leiden Community Detection]
    leiden --> summary[LLM Community Summary Generation]
    summary --> qproc[User Query Processing]
    qproc --> match[Map Query to Communities]
    match --> ans[Generate Answer from Community Summaries]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) and explain how it bridges the query–document gap in dense retrieval.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
HyDE is a query enhancement technique where, given a user query $q$, a language model first generates a *hypothetical* answer $a_{\text{hyp}}$ **without** accessing any retrieval corpus. This hypothetical document is then embedded using the same encoder used for the corpus, i.e., $v = E(a_{\text{hyp}})$, and dense retrieval is performed using $v$ instead of $E(q)$.

The key insight (Gao et al., 2022) is that **hypothetical answers** live in the same semantic space as *real* documents, whereas raw queries often do not. This closes the **query–document semantic gap** that hurts cosine-similarity-based dense retrieval, especially for short, keyword-style queries. HyDE particularly helps for **instructional** and **explanatory** queries where the user does not know the exact corpus vocabulary.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "lost-in-the-middle" problem in RAG, and what is the standard mitigation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The lost-in-the-middle problem (Liu et al., 2023) refers to the empirical finding that LLM performance is **highest** when relevant information is placed at the **beginning** or **end** of the context window, and **significantly degrades** (by up to 20–30%) when the same information is placed in the **middle** of a long context.

**Standard mitigation:** Use a **cross-encoder re-ranker** to sort retrieved documents by relevance, and then **re-order** them so the most relevant chunks occupy the **first** and **last** positions of the prompt. Alternatively, **context compression** can be applied to reduce the total number of tokens, removing the "middle" entirely.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an **Enhanced RAG** system. Classify at least **six** enhancement techniques according to the four pipeline stages: pre-retrieval, retrieval, post-retrieval, and generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of **Self-RAG** in detail, including the four reflection tokens it uses. How does Self-RAG decide whether to trigger retrieval, and how does it verify answer faithfulness? **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**(a) Enhanced RAG Architecture — 7 marks**

*Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1, mermaid).* For 7 marks, the student must:

1. **Define** the four pipeline stages (1 mark).
2. **List at least 6 techniques** correctly classified (3 marks).
3. **Explain** one technique per stage in 1–2 sentences (3 marks).

**Correct classification table (must include):**

| Stage | Techniques |
|---|---|
| Pre-Retrieval | Query Rewriting, Multi-Query, HyDE, Step-Back |
| Retrieval | Hybrid Search (Dense + BM25), Semantic Chunking, Metadata Filtering |
| Post-Retrieval | Cross-Encoder Re-ranking, Context Compression, Lost-in-Middle Re-ordering |
| Generation | Chain-of-Thought RAG, Self-RAG, CRAG, GraphRAG, Agentic RAG |

*One-line explanation per stage:*
- **Pre-retrieval** improves the *query* before it hits the index.
- **Retrieval** improves the *indexing and matching* mechanism.
- **Post-retrieval** improves the *context quality* fed to the LLM.
- **Generation** improves the *reasoning and verification* done by the LLM.

**(b) Self-RAG — 7 marks**

*Definition (1 mark):* Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2023) is a framework that trains an LM to **retrieve, critique, and re-retrieve** documents on demand using special **reflection tokens** inserted into its output.

*Four reflection tokens (2 marks):*
1. **[Retrieve]** — Binary token: "yes" if external retrieval is needed, "no" if the model can answer from parametric memory.
2. **[IsRel]** (Is Relevant) — Scores whether each retrieved document $d_i$ is relevant to query $q$.
3. **[IsSup]** (Is Supported) — Scores whether the generated answer is *supported* by the retrieved documents.
4. **[IsUse]** (Is Useful) — Scores whether the generated answer is *useful* to the user's question overall.

*Working flow (2 marks):*
- On receiving query $q$, the model first emits **[Retrieve]: Yes**.
- It retrieves top-K documents, then for each emits **[IsRel]: Yes/No**.
- It generates a candidate answer, then emits **[IsSup]** and **[IsUse]** tokens.
- If **[IsSup] = No**, it triggers **re-retrieval** with a reformulated query.
- The final answer is the one with the highest combined **[IsSup] + [IsUse]** score.

*Why faithfulness improves (2 marks):* Self-RAG forces the model to **explicitly verify** grounding *during* generation, rather than after. This reduces hallucination by up to 30% on fact-verification benchmarks (ALCE, FactScore) compared to vanilla RAG, because unsupported claims are detected and re-generated in the same inference pass.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hybrid Search** mechanism in RAG. Derive the Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) formula and justify why $k_0 = 60$ is the standard damping constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **GraphRAG** and **Agentic RAG**. Discuss the corpus-level question types each one handles best, and provide one real-world use case for each. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**(a) Hybrid Search + RRF Derivation — 7 marks**

*Hybrid Search definition (1 mark):* Hybrid Search combines a **dense retriever** (neural embeddings, e.g., `bge-large`) with a **sparse retriever** (lexical, e.g., **BM25**), then fuses the two ranked lists.

*Why hybrid? (1 mark):* Dense retrieval excels at **semantic** matching but fails on exact keywords. BM25 excels at **lexical** matching but fails on paraphrases. Their failure modes are nearly disjoint, so fusion yields higher recall.

*RRF derivation (3 marks):*
Let $R_{\text{dense}}(q) = (d_{\pi_1}, d_{\pi_2}, \ldots)$ and $R_{\text{sparse}}(q) = (d_{\sigma_1}, d_{\sigma_2}, \ldots)$ be the two ranked lists. The fused score is:

$$
\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r \in \{\text{dense}, \text{sparse}\}} \frac{1}{k_0 + \text{rank}_r(d)}
$$

where $\text{rank}_r(d) \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ is the position of $d$ in list $r$, and $k_0$ is a damping constant.

*Why $k_0 = 60$? (2 marks):* The constant $k_0$ prevents over-rewarding rank-1 documents. With $k_0 = 60$, a rank-1 document scores $\frac{1}{61} \approx 0.0164$ from each retriever, while a rank-10 document scores $\frac{1}{70} \approx 0.0143$ — only a 14% drop, encouraging diversity. With $k_0 = 0$, the top document would dominate, and lower-ranked but relevant documents would be ignored. **Cormack et al. (2009)** empirically found $k_0 = 60$ optimal across TREC tracks; it has since become the de facto standard.

**(b) GraphRAG vs Agentic RAG — 7 marks**

| Dimension | GraphRAG | Agentic RAG |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Idea** | Build a knowledge graph from corpus; answer via community summaries | LLM agent dynamically decides *when* and *what* to retrieve using tools |
| **Indexing** | Heavy: LLM extracts entities + relations per chunk | Light: Standard vector index + tool registry |
| **Best For** | **Global, corpus-level** questions ("What are the *main themes* across all documents?") | **Multi-hop, dynamic** questions requiring iterative reasoning |
| **Latency** | High (offline graph build) | Medium (multiple tool calls) |
| **Failure Mode** | Misses specific local facts | Can loop / over-retrieve |
| **Use Case 1** | **Legal corpus summarization** (e.g., summarizing themes across 10,000 case laws) | **Real-time customer support** (LLM agent queries KB, then calls order DB) |
| **Use Case 2** | **Biomedical literature review** (find cross-paper relationships) | **Research assistant** (agent searches arXiv → reads PDF → synthesizes) |

*Examiner's note:* The student must explicitly mention that **GraphRAG** was introduced by Microsoft Research in 2024 to solve the **global summarization** weakness of chunk-based RAG, while **Agentic RAG** generalizes RAG into a tool-use agent (ReAct / Toolformer paradigm).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Misclassifying enhancements** — putting "re-ranking" in the retrieval stage instead of post-retrieval. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting reflection tokens** — listing Self-RAG without naming `[IsRel]`, `[IsSup]`, `[IsUse]`. (Lose 3 marks)
> 3. **Writing vague HyDE explanation** — must mention *hypothetical document* and *query–document gap*. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Confusing GraphRAG with Naive RAG** — must mention *knowledge graph* + *community detection*. (Lose 2 marks)
> 5. **Skipping formulas** — KTU examiners expect at least one LaTeX equation for 14-mark questions. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 6. **No diagram** — A 7-mark sub-question without a labeled block diagram is capped at 5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Enhancing RAG (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Naive RAG = retrieve-then-read** (3 steps: embed, retrieve, generate)
- ✅ **Enhancement stages:** Pre-Retrieval, Retrieval, Post-Retrieval, Generation
- ✅ **Pre-Retrieval techniques:** Query Rewriting, Multi-Query, Step-Back, **HyDE**
- ✅ **HyDE = generate hypothetical answer → embed → retrieve**
- ✅ **Retrieval techniques:** Hybrid Search (Dense + BM25), Semantic Chunking, Metadata Filter
- ✅ **RRF formula:** $\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_r \frac{1}{k_0 + \text{rank}_r(d)}$ with **$k_0 = 60$**
- ✅ **Post-Retrieval:** Cross-Encoder Re-ranking, LLMLingua Compression, Lost-in-Middle Fix
- ✅ **Lost-in-the-Middle:** LLM accuracy drops when relevant info is in the **middle** of context
- ✅ **Generation techniques:** CoT-RAG, Self-RAG, CRAG, GraphRAG, Agentic RAG
- ✅ **Self-RAG reflection tokens:** `[Retrieve]`, `[IsRel]`, `[IsSup]`, `[IsUse]`
- ✅ **CRAG** uses a T5-based evaluator + **web search fallback** for incorrect docs
- ✅ **GraphRAG** = Knowledge Graph + Leiden Community Detection (for global questions)
- ✅ **Agentic RAG** = LLM agent with tools (vector search, web, SQL)
- ✅ **Standard defaults:** Top-K = 10, Chunk Size = 512 tokens, BM25 $k_1=1.5, b=0.75$
- ✅ **Metrics:** Recall@K, MRR, nDCG, Faithfulness, Answer Relevancy
- ✅ **Mandatory in 14-mark answers:** block diagram + at least one LaTeX formula + classification table
- ✅ **Reference papers to cite:** Ma et al. 2023 (Rewrite-Retrieve-Read), Gao et al. 2022 (HyDE), Asai et al. 2023 (Self-RAG), Yan et al. 2024 (CRAG), Microsoft 2024 (GraphRAG), Liu et al. 2023 (Lost-in-the-Middle)
