topics.title: Evaluating RAG Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluating RAG Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Evaluation** is the systematic, multi-dimensional process of quantitatively and qualitatively measuring the performance of a RAG pipeline. A RAG pipeline combines an **Information Retrieval (IR)** component (typically a dense vector search over a knowledge corpus) with a **Generative LLM** that conditions its output on the retrieved evidence. Evaluation, therefore, is not a single metric but a *composite assessment* covering the retriever, the augmenter, and the generator as a unified system.

According to the **RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment)** framework — the de-facto academic benchmark for RAG evaluation — a RAG system is judged along three orthogonal axes known as the **RAG Triad**:

1. **Context Relevance** — precision of the retriever
2. **Groundedness / Faithfulness** — adherence of the generator to retrieved evidence
3. **Answer Relevance** — semantic alignment of the final output with the user query

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT862 Module 4):**
> Evaluating a RAG application is *qualitatively different* from evaluating a vanilla LLM. We no longer ask "is the answer correct?" in isolation — we ask "is the answer correct **given the retrieved context**?" This shifts the evaluation paradigm from *open-generation* to *grounded-generation* assessment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Open-Book Exam" Paradigm

Imagine a student taking an **open-book examination**:

| Exam Component | Real-world Actor | RAG Analogue |
|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| The Textbook kept open | The Knowledge Base / Vector DB | Retrieved Context Chunks |
| The Student's Brain | The LLM (Generator) | Response Synthesis |
| The Question Paper | User Query | Input Prompt |
| The Examiner's Checklist | Evaluation Metrics | RAG Triad |

The examiner does **not** simply check if the final answer is right. The examiner checks three things:
1. **Did the student open the *right* page?** → *Context Relevance*
2. **Did the student copy/paraphrase *only* from the open book, and not invent facts?** → *Faithfulness*
3. **Does the final written answer actually *address* the question asked?** → *Answer Relevance*

If any one of the three fails, the student loses marks — even if the final answer *sounds* plausible. This is precisely why vanilla LLM benchmarks (like perplexity) are insufficient for RAG, and why a dedicated **triadic evaluation framework** is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constant / Reference Standard:**
> The industry-standard quantitative reference for RAG evaluation is the **RAGAS score** $S_{RAGAS} \in [0, 1]$, where $1$ indicates a perfect RAG response and $0$ indicates complete failure across all three triad dimensions.

## 1.3 Why RAG Evaluation is Harder than LLM Evaluation

- **Non-determinism:** LLMs generate stochastic outputs; same query + same context can yield different responses.
- **Context dependence:** Two answers may be semantically equivalent but lexically different.
- **Multi-component failure modes:** A bad answer can originate from a bad retriever, a bad generator, *or* a bad prompt template.
- **Ground-truth scarcity:** Real-world corpora rarely have human-annotated ground-truth contexts.
- **Hallucination detection:** The model may blend retrieved facts with parametric memory, making attribution difficult.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When KTU asks "Why can't we just use accuracy for RAG?", the expected answer is the three bullet points above. Memorize them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RAG Triad — Operational Decomposition

A RAG system can be formalized as a function $f_\theta: (q, \mathcal{C}) \rightarrow a$, where $q$ is the user query, $\mathcal{C}$ is the retrieved context set, and $a$ is the generated answer. Evaluation measures the quality of this mapping.

### 2.1.1 Dimension 1 — Context Relevance (Retriever Quality)

**Operational question:** *"Of the $k$ documents retrieved, how many are actually useful for answering $q$?"*

Context Relevance is computed as a **soft precision** over the retrieved set. Let $\mathcal{R} = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k\}$ be the top-$k$ retrieved chunks, and let $rel(r_i, q) \in \{0, 1\}$ be a binary relevance judgment (often produced by an LLM-judge).

**Logic Flow:**
1. Embed the user query $q$ and each retrieved chunk $r_i$ into a shared semantic space.
2. For each chunk, judge relevance via cosine similarity thresholding OR an LLM-as-judge.
3. Aggregate judgments into a precision score.

### 2.1.2 Dimension 2 — Faithfulness / Groundedness (Generator Honesty)

**Operational question:** *"Is every claim in the answer $a$ explicitly supported by $\mathcal{C}$?"*

This is the **anti-hallucination** dimension. The answer is decomposed into atomic claims, and each claim is verified against the context.

**Logic Flow:**
1. Split the generated answer $a$ into a set of $n$ atomic statements $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$.
2. For each $s_i$, check: is $s_i$ entailed by $\mathcal{C}$?
3. Compute the ratio of entailed statements to total statements.

### 2.1.3 Dimension 3 — Answer Relevance (Query Alignment)

**Operational question:** *"Does the final answer actually address the user's question, ignoring hallucinations?"*

**Logic Flow:**
1. Generate $m$ synthetic questions $Q' = \{q'_1, \ldots, q'_m\}$ that the answer $a$ could plausibly answer (via an LLM).
2. Compute the mean cosine similarity between the original $q$ and each $q'_j$.
3. High similarity = high answer relevance.

## 2.2 LLM-as-a-Judge Methodology

Since human annotation is expensive, modern RAG evaluation uses an **LLM-as-a-Judge** pattern. A separate, typically stronger LLM (e.g., GPT-4, Claude Opus) is prompted with a rubric and asked to score the RAG output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The judge LLM must be *different* from the generator LLM, otherwise it suffers from **self-enhancement bias** (a model scoring its own outputs favorably).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Range | Measures |
|--------|----------------|--------------------------|-------|----------|
| **Context Precision** | $P_{ctx}$ | $\frac{\vert \{r_i \in \mathcal{R} : rel(r_i, q) = 1\} \vert}{k}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Retriever precision |
| **Context Recall** | $R_{ctx}$ | $\frac{\vert \text{relevant} \cap \mathcal{R} \vert}{\vert \text{relevant} \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Retriever recall |
| **Context F1** | $F1_{ctx}$ | $\frac{2 \cdot P_{ctx} \cdot R_{ctx}}{P_{ctx} + R_{ctx}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Retriever balance |
| **Faithfulness** | $F$ | $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[s_i \models \mathcal{C}]}{n}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Anti-hallucination |
| **Answer Relevancy** | $AR$ | $\frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{sim}(q, q'_j)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Query alignment |
| **BLEU (n-gram)** | $BLEU_n$ | $BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \log p_i\right)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Lexical overlap |
| **ROUGE-L (F1)** | $R_{L}$ | $\frac{(1+\beta^2) R_{lcs} P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Longest common subsequence |
| **MRR** | $MRR$ | $\frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \frac{1}{rank_i}$ | $[0, 1]$ | First-hit rank |
| **NDCG@k** | $NDCG@k$ | $\frac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Ranked retrieval quality |
| **Hallucination Rate** | $HR$ | $1 - F$ | $[0, 1]$ | Frequency of fabrication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In production LLM systems (e.g., enterprise search, legal-tech chatbots, medical assistants), the Faithfulness metric is often the **most safety-critical** dimension — a hallucinated legal citation or fabricated drug dosage can have catastrophic real-world consequences.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Enterprise Search (e.g., Bloomberg GPT, Morgan Stanley AI Assistant):** Evaluates RAG on internal financial reports where hallucination = regulatory risk.
- **Medical QA (e.g., Med-PaLM 2, Hippocratic AI):** Uses Faithfulness as a hard gate before deployment.
- **Legal-Tech (e.g., Harvey, Casetext):** Relies on Context Recall to ensure case law retrieval is exhaustive.
- **Code Generation (e.g., Cursor, Copilot Workspace):** Evaluates whether generated code cites the correct repository files.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The RAGAS Faithfulness Score

The Faithfulness metric $F$ decomposes a generated answer $a$ into a set of atomic statements and checks each against the retrieved context $\mathcal{C}$.

### Step 1 — Statement Segmentation

Given an answer $a$ consisting of $T$ tokens, we apply a segmentation function $seg(\cdot)$ (typically an LLM call) to extract atomic statements.

$$
S = seg(a) = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}, \quad s_i \in \text{strings}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The string $a$ is mapped to a discrete set of declarative claims. Each $s_i$ is a self-contained factual assertion that can be independently verified.

### Step 2 — NLI-Based Verification

For each statement $s_i$, we compute a Natural Language Inference (NLI) verdict $v_i$ against the context $\mathcal{C}$.

$$
v_i = \text{NLI}(s_i, \mathcal{C}) \in \{0, 1\}
$$

where $v_i = 1$ if and only if $\mathcal{C}$ logically entails $s_i$, and $v_i = 0$ otherwise.

**Conversion Logic:** An NLI model (e.g., a fine-tuned DeBERTa) treats $s_i$ as the *premise* and $\mathcal{C}$ as the *hypothesis* (or vice versa), outputting an entailment label.

### Step 3 — Aggregation

The Faithfulness score is the fraction of entailed statements.

$$
F = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We sum the binary verdicts and normalize by the total number of statements. If $n = 0$ (empty answer), $F$ is undefined and conventionally set to $1$ (a vacuous truth).

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a RAG system produces the answer:

*"The Eiffel Tower is in Paris. It was built in 1889. It is made of solid gold."*

**Step 1 — Segment:**

$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\} = \{\text{"Eiffel Tower in Paris"}, \text{"built in 1889"}, \text{"made of solid gold"}\}
$$

So $n = 3$.

**Step 2 — Verify against context** (assume context is a Wikipedia excerpt on the Eiffel Tower):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= 1 \quad \text{(context confirms Paris)} \\
v_2 &= 1 \quad \text{(context confirms 1889)} \\
v_3 &= 0 \quad \text{(context says wrought iron, not gold — hallucination)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate:**

$$
F = \frac{1 + 1 + 0}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
$$

**Interpretation:** The answer is **66.7% faithful** — one out of three claims is hallucinated. This is below the typical production threshold of $F \geq 0.90$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Context Precision @ k (RAGAS Variant)

The RAGAS-style context precision weights retrieved chunks by their *position* in the ranked list, penalizing systems that put relevant chunks at the bottom.

### Step 1 — Define Position-Weighted Precision

For a ranked list $\mathcal{R} = (r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k)$, where $r_1$ is the top-ranked chunk, define an indicator $rel_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

$$
P_{ctx}@k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( rel_i \cdot v_i \right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} rel_i}
$$

where $v_i$ is a *precision-at-position* term defined as:

$$
v_i = \frac{\text{\# relevant chunks above position } i + 1}{\text{position } i}
$$

### Step 2 — Worked Example

Suppose $k = 5$ and the relevance labels (top to bottom) are: $(1, 0, 1, 1, 0)$.

**Compute $v_i$ for each relevant position:**

- $i = 1$: relevant count above $= 0$, so $v_1 = \frac{0+1}{1} = 1.000$
- $i = 3$: relevant count above $= 1$ (only position 1), so $v_3 = \frac{1+1}{3} \approx 0.667$
- $i = 4$: relevant count above $= 2$ (positions 1 and 3), so $v_4 = \frac{2+1}{4} = 0.750$

**Aggregate:**

$$
P_{ctx}@5 = \frac{(1 \cdot 1.000) + (0) + (1 \cdot 0.667) + (1 \cdot 0.750) + (0)}{1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0} = \frac{2.417}{3} \approx 0.806
$$

**Interpretation:** Context precision is **0.806**, meaning relevant chunks are mostly ranked highly, but the system is penalized slightly for placing a relevant chunk at position 3 instead of position 2.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RAG Failure Mode Diagnostic

The following matrix maps a low score on a triad dimension to its root cause — a high-yield KTU question pattern.

| Low Score On | Likely Root Cause | Remediation Strategy |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Context Precision | Retriever returns noisy/irrelevant chunks | Re-rank with cross-encoder, increase embedding quality |
| Context Recall | Knowledge base is incomplete; chunking too coarse | Improve chunking strategy, expand corpus |
| Faithfulness | LLM is over-using parametric memory | Tighten prompt: "Answer ONLY from context", reduce temperature |
| Answer Relevance | Query is ambiguous; prompt template is weak | Add query rewriting, chain-of-thought, clarify prompt |
| All three low | Fundamental pipeline mismatch (wrong embedding model, wrong LLM) | Full architectural review, A/B test new components |

## 3.4 The RAGAS Composite Score

The unified RAGAS score is the **harmonic mean** of the three primary metrics, ensuring that a weakness in any one dimension pulls the overall score down (harmonic mean penalizes imbalance).

$$
S_{RAGAS} = \frac{3}{\frac{1}{P_{ctx}} + \frac{1}{F} + \frac{1}{AR}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Unlike an arithmetic mean, the harmonic mean is dominated by the *smallest* component. A RAG system with $(P_{ctx} = 0.99, F = 0.50, AR = 0.99)$ will have a much lower RAGAS score than its arithmetic mean suggests, correctly reflecting the dangerous hallucination risk.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End RAG Evaluation Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the complete data flow of a RAG evaluation pipeline, including the LLM-as-a-Judge feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeQ[User Query Q]
    nodeEmbed[Embedding Model]
    nodeVDB[(Vector Database)]
    nodeRet[Retriever Top-K]
    nodeCtx[Context Chunks C]
    nodeGen[Generator LLM]
    nodeAns[Generated Answer A]
    nodeSeg[Statement Segmenter]
    nodeStmtSet[Atomic Statements S]
    nodeJudge1[LLM Judge Relevance]
    nodeJudge2[LLM Judge Faithfulness]
    nodeJudge3[LLM Judge Answer Rel]
    nodeScore[RAGAS Composite Score]
    nodeDash[Evaluation Dashboard]

    nodeQ --> nodeEmbed
    nodeEmbed --> nodeVDB
    nodeVDB --> nodeRet
    nodeRet --> nodeCtx
    nodeQ --> nodeGen
    nodeCtx --> nodeGen
    nodeGen --> nodeAns

    nodeAns --> nodeSeg
    nodeSeg --> nodeStmtSet
    nodeCtx --> nodeJudge1
    nodeQ --> nodeJudge1
    nodeCtx --> nodeJudge2
    nodeStmtSet --> nodeJudge2
    nodeAns --> nodeJudge3
    nodeQ --> nodeJudge3

    nodeJudge1 --> nodeScore
    nodeJudge2 --> nodeScore
    nodeJudge3 --> nodeScore
    nodeScore --> nodeDash
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU students):**
> - The **left vertical chain** (Q → Embed → VDB → Retriever → Context) is the *retrieval path*.
> - The **center path** (Q + Context → Generator → Answer) is the *generation path*.
> - The **right vertical chain** (Answer → Segmenter → Statements) and the **three judges** form the *evaluation path*.
> - The arrows flowing *into* the judges from the left indicate that **ground-truth references** (Context, original Query) are used as comparators — this is the *groundedness* of the evaluation.

## 4.2 The RAG Triad — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQuery[Input Query]
    nodeCTX[Retrieved Context]
    nodeOUT[Generated Output]

    nodeCR[Context Relevance]
    nodeFT[Faithfulness]
    nodeAR[Answer Relevance]

    nodeQuery --> nodeCTX
    nodeCTX --> nodeOUT

    nodeCTX --> nodeCR
    nodeOUT --> nodeFT
    nodeCTX --> nodeFT
    nodeOUT --> nodeAR
    nodeQuery --> nodeAR

    nodeCR --> nodeAgg[Aggregate RAGAS Score]
    nodeFT --> nodeAgg
    nodeAR --> nodeAgg
```

## 4.3 LLM-as-a-Judge Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeRAGOut[RAG System Output]
    nodeRubric[Evaluation Rubric Prompt]
    nodeJudgeLLM[Stronger Judge LLM]
    nodeVerdict{Verdict Generation}
    nodeScores[Structured Scores JSON]
    nodeCI[Continuous Integration Gate]

    nodeRAGOut --> nodeJudgeLLM
    nodeRubric --> nodeJudgeLLM
    nodeJudgeLLM --> nodeVerdict
    nodeVerdict --> nodeScores
    nodeScores --> nodeCI
    nodeCI -->|Pass or Fail| nodeDeploy[Deploy or Block]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice Note:** The `Continuous Integration Gate` node represents a *deployment-time check* — in production MLOps pipelines (e.g., for a KTU-style industrial AI project), the RAGAS score is used as a quality gate. If $S_{RAGAS} < 0.85$, the new model version is **automatically blocked** from promotion to production.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Explain the three dimensions of the RAG Triad used for evaluating RAG applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The RAG Triad, as defined in the RAGAS framework, consists of three orthogonal evaluation dimensions:

1. **Context Relevance (1 mark):** Measures whether the retrieved context chunks are pertinent to the user query. It evaluates the *retriever's* precision. Computed as the fraction of retrieved chunks deemed relevant to $q$.

2. **Faithfulness / Groundedness (1 mark):** Measures whether the generated answer is factually supported by the retrieved context. It evaluates *hallucination resistance*. Computed as the fraction of atomic claims in the answer that are entailed by $\mathcal{C}$.

3. **Answer Relevance (1 mark):** Measures whether the generated answer actually addresses the user's question, independent of factual correctness. Computed via mean cosine similarity between the original query and synthetic questions re-derived from the answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Note:** A student who lists the three dimensions but fails to mention the *evaluation target* of each (retriever vs. generator vs. output) loses **0.5 marks**. Always pair each metric with what it measures.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Why is standard perplexity an inadequate metric for evaluating RAG applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Perplexity measures the *fluency* of a language model's next-token predictions against a reference corpus, but it has three critical limitations for RAG systems:

1. **It ignores the retrieval component (1 mark):** Perplexity is computed on the LLM alone, completely bypassing the retriever. A RAG system with a perfect LLM but a broken retriever would still score well on perplexity.

2. **It cannot detect hallucination (1 mark):** A confidently-wrong, fluent hallucination would receive a lower perplexity than a cautious, hedged but correct answer, inverting the desirability ranking.

3. **It does not measure groundedness (1 mark):** Perplexity cannot tell whether the generated text is *derived from* the retrieved context $\mathcal{C}$ or from the model's parametric memory, which is the central concern of RAG evaluation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a RAG evaluation pipeline. Discuss the role of LLM-as-a-Judge in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical formulation of the RAGAS Faithfulness score. Given a generated answer with three atomic claims, two of which are supported by the retrieved context, compute the faithfulness score. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** diagram. The RAG evaluation pipeline consists of three coupled paths:

1. **Retrieval Path (2 marks):** User Query → Embedding Model → Vector Database → Retriever (Top-K) → Context Chunks $\mathcal{C}$. This path produces the evidence basis.

2. **Generation Path (2 marks):** Query + Context → Generator LLM → Generated Answer $A$. This is the production path of the RAG system.

3. **Evaluation Path (3 marks):** Three parallel LLM judges assess:
   - Context Relevance: judges $(\mathcal{C}, q)$
   - Faithfulness: judges $(A, \mathcal{C})$ via statement decomposition
   - Answer Relevance: judges $(A, q)$ via synthetic question generation
   Their outputs aggregate into the RAGAS composite score.

**Role of LLM-as-a-Judge (1 mark, integrated above):** The judge LLM replaces costly human annotation. It must be a *different, typically stronger* model than the generator to avoid self-enhancement bias. The judge receives a structured rubric prompt and outputs scores, which feed into CI/CD quality gates.

**Part (b) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Segment the answer into atomic statements. Let $a$ be the answer and $seg(\cdot)$ be a segmentation function (typically an LLM call).

$$
S = seg(a) = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Verify each statement against the context using an NLI (Natural Language Inference) model.

$$
v_i = \text{NLI}(s_i, \mathcal{C}) \in \{0, 1\}
$$

where $v_i = 1$ if $\mathcal{C} \models s_i$ (entailment) and $v_i = 0$ otherwise.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Aggregate verdicts into a single faithfulness score.

$$
F = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Numerical Computation:**

Given $n = 3$ atomic claims and 2 are supported by context:

$$
F = \frac{1 + 1 + 0}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
$$

**Interpretation:** The RAG system's answer is **66.7% faithful** — one of three claims is a hallucination. This falls below typical production thresholds.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast BLEU, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore as evaluation metrics for RAG-generated answers. Which is most suitable for open-ended RAG evaluation, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A RAG system retrieves 5 documents in the order $(R, I, R, R, I)$ where $R$ denotes relevant and $I$ denotes irrelevant. Compute the RAGAS Context Precision @ 5 using the position-weighted formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | BLEU | ROUGE-L | BERTScore |
|--------|------|---------|-----------|
| **Core Mechanism (1 mark)** | Modified n-gram precision with brevity penalty | Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) based F1 | Contextual embedding cosine similarity |
| **What it Rewards (1 mark)** | Lexical n-gram overlap with reference | Recall of in-order matches | Semantic similarity via embeddings |
| **Limitation (1 mark)** | Cannot recognize synonyms ("car" vs "automobile") | Insensitive to paraphrasing; LCS penalizes reordering | Requires a reference answer; computationally heavier |
| **Reference Dependency (1 mark)** | Requires ≥1 reference | Requires ≥1 reference | Requires ≥1 reference |
| **Best Use Case (1 mark)** | Translation, short factual answers | Summarization, long-form answers | Open-ended, paraphrased, semantic QA |
| **RAG Suitability (2 marks)** | Low — penalizes valid paraphrasing | Medium — good for extractive RAG | **High — best for RAG**, since RAG answers are often paraphrased and semantically equivalent but lexically divergent |

**Conclusion (1 mark, integrated):** **BERTScore is most suitable** for open-ended RAG evaluation because it captures *semantic equivalence* through contextual embeddings, tolerates valid paraphrasing, and is robust to the lexical diversity typical of LLM-generated answers.

**Part (b) — RAGAS Context Precision @ 5 (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify relevant positions:**

The retrieved sequence is $(R, I, R, R, I)$ at positions $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$. Relevant positions are $i \in \{1, 3, 4\}$. Total relevant count = $3$.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Compute $v_i$ for each relevant position:**

Recall $v_i = \frac{(\text{relevant above position } i) + 1}{i}$:

- $i = 1$: relevant above $= 0$
$$v_1 = \frac{0 + 1}{1} = 1.000$$
- $i = 3$: relevant above $= 1$ (position 1)
$$v_3 = \frac{1 + 1}{3} \approx 0.667$$
- $i = 4$: relevant above $= 2$ (positions 1 and 3)
$$v_4 = \frac{2 + 1}{4} = 0.750$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply the RAGAS Context Precision formula:**

$$
P_{ctx}@5 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} rel_i \cdot v_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} rel_i}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
(1 \cdot 1.000) + (0) + (1 \cdot 0.667) + (1 \cdot 0.750) + (0) = 2.417
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 3
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final Result:**

$$
P_{ctx}@5 = \frac{2.417}{3} \approx 0.806
$$

**Interpretation:** The retriever places most relevant chunks at the top, but is penalized for the irrelevant chunk at position 2 pushing a relevant chunk to position 3.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns (PECMT862 Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Confusing RAG evaluation with LLM evaluation (–2 marks typical):** Students often write about perplexity, BLEU, accuracy in isolation *without* mentioning the RAG Triad. The question is about RAG, not vanilla LLM benchmarking.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the formula's denominator in Context Precision (–1 mark):** Students compute $v_i$ correctly but forget to normalize by the total relevant count, yielding a number > 1. Always check: precision must lie in $[0, 1]$.
>
> 3. **Using arithmetic mean instead of harmonic mean for RAGAS composite (–1 mark):** The composite is a **harmonic mean** of the three triad metrics. Using arithmetic mean is a conceptual error.
>
> 4. **Omitting the self-enhancement bias caveat for LLM-as-a-Judge (–0.5 marks):** Always state that the judge LLM should be *different* from the generator LLM.
>
> 5. **Not defining what each metric measures (–0.5 marks per metric):** When writing about Faithfulness, specify it measures *hallucination resistance*. When writing about Context Relevance, specify it measures *retriever precision*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RAG Evaluation = RAG Triad:** Context Relevance + Faithfulness + Answer Relevance. Memorize all three.
- **Faithfulness formula:** $F = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ where $v_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the NLI verdict for each atomic claim.
- **Context Precision @ k (RAGAS):** Position-weighted; penalizes relevant chunks that appear late in the ranked list.
- **Context Recall:** Fraction of *all* relevant documents that were retrieved — measures retriever *coverage*.
- **RAGAS Composite:** Harmonic mean of the three primary metrics — penalizes imbalance.
- **LLM-as-a-Judge:** Judge LLM ≠ Generator LLM (avoid self-enhancement bias).
- **Why perplexity fails for RAG:** Ignores retriever, cannot detect hallucination, cannot measure groundedness.
- **BERTScore > BLEU/ROUGE for open-ended RAG:** Captures semantic equivalence, tolerates paraphrasing.
- **Hallucination Rate:** $HR = 1 - F$ — a critical safety metric in medical, legal, and financial RAG.
- **MRR & NDCG:** Standard IR metrics applicable to the retriever's *ranking quality* (not just binary relevance).
- **Failure Mode Matrix:** Low Context Precision → bad retriever; Low Faithfulness → bad prompt/hallucination; Low Answer Relevance → ambiguous query.
- **Engineering Threshold:** Production-grade RAG systems typically require $S_{RAGAS} \geq 0.85$ and $F \geq 0.90$ before deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks to "evaluate" a RAG system, the model answer **must** contain: (1) the RAG Triad, (2) at least one mathematical formula with worked numerical computation, (3) a diagram showing the evaluation loop, and (4) a discussion of the LLM-as-a-Judge pattern. Hitting all four elements typically secures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LLMs in Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Productionizing an LLM** refers to the end-to-end engineering discipline of taking a trained (or fine-tuned) Large Language Model from a research artifact and deploying it as a reliable, scalable, secure, cost-efficient, observable, and low-latency service that real users can query through APIs, UIs, or downstream pipelines, while maintaining continuous quality, safety, and governance.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 PECMT862 syllabus (Module 4)**, "LLMs in Production" spans six engineering pillars:

1. **Serving Infrastructure** — GPUs/TPUs, model servers (vLLM, TGI, Triton, TensorRT-LLM), KV-cache management.
2. **Latency & Throughput Engineering** — TTFT, TPOT, batch scheduling, speculative decoding.
3. **Cost & Resource Economics** — token economics, quantization, autoscaling.
4. **Reliability & Observability** — SLAs/SLOs, tracing, drift detection, continuous evaluation.
5. **Safety, Security & Governance** — guardrails, PII redaction, prompt-injection defense, audit logs.
6. **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) & Tooling** — vector DBs, agentic orchestration, function calling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU expects this clarity):**
> - *Inference* = a single forward pass of the model to generate tokens.
> - *Serving* = the system-level engineering that hosts, schedules, batches, monitors, and secures thousands of inference calls per second.
> - *Production* = the business + engineering reality: SLAs, cost caps, uptime, user trust, compliance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "AI Restaurant"

Imagine a fully-trained chef (the LLM). The chef knows 10,000 recipes (parameters) and can cook any dish (text generation) in his head.

- **Research Lab** = chef cooks for himself, no time pressure, ingredients are unlimited.
- **Production** = the chef is now running a 500-seat restaurant:

| Restaurant Element | LLM Production Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Kitchen layout (stoves, ovens) | **GPU cluster / model server (vLLM, TGI)** |
| Order tickets queueing up | **Request batcher / continuous batching** |
| Refrigerator of pre-chopped veggies | **KV-cache (paged attention)** |
| Waiter calling out "Order #7 ready!" | **Token streaming (SSE / WebSocket)** |
| Customer waiting too long | **TTFT (Time To First Token)** |
| Chef finishing one dish at a time | **TPOT (Time Per Output Token)** |
| Health inspector | **Guardrails & content moderation** |
| Cash register / billing | **Token economics & cost monitoring** |
| Yelp reviews | **Online evaluation (LLM-as-judge, RAGAS)** |
| Mystery shopper | **A/B testing of prompts / models** |
| Takeout menu | **Prompt templates / function-calling schema** |
| Supplier delivery (fresh produce) | **RAG retrieval from vector DB** |

> [!TIP]
> The single biggest mental shift: a research LLM is judged by *benchmark accuracy*, but a production LLM is judged by **$/1M tokens**, **p99 latency**, and **uptime percentage**.

---

## 1.3 Core Production Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **TTFT (Time To First Token):** latency before the first token appears. Target ≈ **< 300 ms** for chat UX.
- **TPOT (Time Per Output Token):** average time between successive tokens after the first. Target ≈ **< 50 ms**.
- **E2E Latency** = TTFT + (TPOT × number_of_output_tokens).
- **Throughput:** tokens/sec/GPU (for batched) or requests/sec.
- **Goodput:** throughput measured *only* over requests meeting the latency SLO.
- **Cost per 1K / 1M tokens** (input + output billed separately by most APIs).
- **GPU Utilization (%)** and **GPU Memory Utilization (%)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SLO Example (industry):** TTFT p50 < 200 ms, TTFT p99 < 800 ms, TPOT p99 < 80 ms, availability ≥ 99.9% ("three nines" = ~8.7 hrs downtime/year).

---

## 1.4 Why This Module Matters (KTU Industry Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LLM Inference Pipeline (Operational Breakdown)

Step 1 — **Request arrives** at the API gateway (FastAPI / gRPC / Envoy).
↓
Step 2 — **Authentication & Rate Limiting** (API key, JWT, token bucket).
↓
Step 3 — **Pre-processing** (system prompt injection, RAG retrieval, tokenization).
↓
Step 4 — **Scheduler** places the request into a **continuous batch** on a GPU slot.
↓
Step 5 — **Forward pass** of the LLM; the **KV-cache** is allocated per request.
↓
Step 6 — **Decoding loop** (autoregressive sampling, with stopping criteria).
↓
Step 7 — **Streaming** partial tokens back via SSE / WebSocket.
↓
Step 8 — **Post-processing** (guardrail check, PII redaction, content moderation).
↓
Step 9 — **Logging & Telemetry** (Langfuse, Phoenix, OpenTelemetry traces).
↓
Step 10 — **Billing & Quota Update**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Continuous Batching" is the #1 throughput trick:**
> Static batching wastes GPU cycles because shorter sequences in the batch must wait for the longest one. **Continuous batching (à la vLLM, TGI)** lets finished requests leave the batch and new ones join every decoding step, raising GPU utilization from ~30% to ~80%+.

---

## 2.2 Quantization: The Cheat-Sheet Every KTU Student Must Know

Quantization maps high-precision weights to lower-precision representations to **shrink memory** and **speed up matrix multiplies**, at the cost of slight accuracy loss.

| Precision | Bytes / Param | 7B Model Size | Relative Speed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FP32 | 4 | ~28 GB | 1× | Training (baseline) |
| FP16 / BF16 | 2 | ~14 GB | ~1.5-2× | Training + inference |
| INT8 | 1 | ~7 GB | ~2-3× | Server-side inference |
| INT4 (GPTQ/AWQ) | 0.5 | ~3.5 GB | ~3-4× | Edge / cost-optimized |
| INT2 (QuIP#) | 0.25 | ~1.75 GB | ~5× | Experimental |

> [!NOTE]
> **BF16 vs FP16:** BF16 has the *same exponent range* as FP32 (8 bits) but only 7 bits of mantissa. This makes BF16 robust to gradient overflow in training, while FP16 is faster on older hardware. For inference, BF16 is the default in 2024+ GPUs (H100, A100, RTX 4090).

---

## 2.3 KV-Cache & Paged Attention — The Hidden Memory Hog

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** During autoregressive decoding, the model caches the **Key (K)** and **Value (V)** tensors of every previous token so attention does not have to be recomputed. For a 7B model, 2048 context, FP16 KV-cache ≈ **2 × n_layers × n_heads × head_dim × seq_len × 2 bytes** ≈ **2 GB per request**!

Paged Attention (Kwon et al., SOSP 2023 — **do not fabricate authors/editions**, but the technique is real and foundational) stores the KV-cache in **non-contiguous fixed-size pages** (like OS virtual memory), eliminating fragmentation and enabling near-100% memory utilization.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Definition of Symbols | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **E2E Latency** | $L_{E2E} = TTFT + (TPOT \times N_{out})$ | $N_{out}$ = output token count | milliseconds (ms) |
| 2 | **Total Cost per Request** | $C = (P_{in} \cdot c_{in} + P_{out} \cdot c_{out}) / 1000$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}$ = input/output tokens; $c_{in}, c_{out}$ = $/1K tokens | USD |
| 3 | **KV-Cache Memory** | $M_{KV} = 2 \cdot L \cdot h \cdot d_{head} \cdot S \cdot b_{kv}$ | $L$=layers, $h$=heads, $d_{head}$=head dim, $S$=seq len, $b_{kv}$=bytes per element | bytes |
| 4 | **Model Memory (weights)** | $M_W = N_{params} \cdot b_{w}$ | $N_{params}$=parameter count, $b_w$=bytes/param | bytes |
| 5 | **FLOPs per Token (forward)** | $\approx 2 \cdot N_{params}$ | rule of thumb for transformer forward pass | FLOPs |
| 6 | **GPU Memory Total** | $M_{total} \geq M_W + M_{KV} + M_{activations} + M_{overhead}$ | sum of weights + KV + activations + framework overhead | bytes (must fit VRAM) |
| 7 | **Quantization Size** | $M_{quant} = M_W / (b_{w,orig} / b_{w,quant})$ | e.g., FP16 → INT4 halves memory 4× | bytes |
| 8 | **Throughput (batched)** | $\Theta = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{B} N_{out,i}}{T_{batch}}$ | $B$=batch size, $T_{batch}$=wall time | tokens/sec |
| 9 | **Speculative Decoding Speedup** | $S \leq 1 + (1 - p) \cdot (k-1)$ | $k$=draft length, $p$=rejection rate; upper bound | dimensionless |
| 10 | **p99 Latency SLO** | $L_{p99} \leq L_{SLO}$ | 99% of requests must complete within budget | ms |
| 11 | **Goodput** | $\Theta_{good} = \Theta \cdot \Pr(L \leq L_{SLO})$ | throughput weighted by SLO compliance | tokens/sec |
| 12 | **Billion-Parameter Energy (rule of thumb)** | $E \approx k \cdot N_{params} \cdot T_{tokens}$ | empirical constant $k \approx 10^{-9}$ J/(param·token) | Joules |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Formula 3 uses `\cdot` not `|`** — KTU table-markdown cannot contain the pipe symbol inside cells or it breaks rendering. Memorize: **2 × L × h × d_head × S × b_kv**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: KV-Cache Memory Footprint

**Problem.** A production LLM has $L = 32$ layers, $h = 32$ attention heads, head dimension $d_{head} = 128$, and serves a context of $S = 4096$ tokens in FP16. Compute the KV-cache memory per request.

**Step 1 — Identify stored tensors.**

During decoding, for every layer and every token, the model stores the **K** vector and the **V** vector.

**Step 2 — Per-token storage in one layer.**

For one layer, one token, one head:
- $K$ vector length = $d_{head}$ elements.
- $V$ vector length = $d_{head}$ elements.
- Total scalars per token per layer = $2 \cdot h \cdot d_{head}$.

**Step 3 — Multiply by sequence length.**

For $S$ tokens and $L$ layers:

$$
M_{KV,\text{scalars}} = 2 \cdot L \cdot h \cdot d_{head} \cdot S
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes using $b_{kv} = 2$ for FP16.**

$$
M_{KV} = 2 \cdot L \cdot h \cdot d_{head} \cdot S \cdot b_{kv}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numbers.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{KV} &= 2 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 128 \cdot 4096 \cdot 2 \\
&= 2 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 128 \cdot 4096 \cdot 2 \\
\text{Step-by-step: } & 2 \times 32 = 64 \\
& 64 \times 32 = 2048 \\
& 2048 \times 128 = 262\,144 \\
& 262\,144 \times 4096 = 1\,073\,741\,824 \\
& 1\,073\,741\,824 \times 2 = 2\,147\,483\,648 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to gigabytes.**

$$
M_{KV} = \dfrac{2\,147\,483\,648}{1024^3} \approx 2.0 \text{ GB per request}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production implication:** A single H100 GPU (80 GB VRAM) can hold the 7B model (~14 GB FP16) plus only ~30 such concurrent 2K-context requests before OOM. This is why **paged attention + continuous batching** are mandatory, not optional.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Budget

**Problem.** A chatbot has SLO: $L_{E2E} \le 2.5$ s for a 600-token answer. TPOT measured is 35 ms. What is the maximum allowable TTFT?

**Step 1 — Write the equation.**

$$
L_{E2E} = TTFT + TPOT \cdot N_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for TTFT.**

$$
TTFT = L_{E2E} - TPOT \cdot N_{out}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
TTFT = 2500 \text{ ms} - 35 \text{ ms} \times 600
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
35 \times 600 &= 21\,000 \text{ ms} \\
TTFT &= 2500 - 21\,000 = -18\,500 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

A **negative** TTFT is impossible, so the SLO is **infeasible** with current TPOT. The team must either:

(a) Reduce $N_{out}$ (shorter answers, max_tokens cap).

(b) Reduce TPOT (faster model, smaller model, speculative decoding, quantization).

(c) Relax the SLO (business decision).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite question style:** "Given SLO and TPOT, find max TTFT" or "given TTFT, find min acceptable output length". Practice plugging numbers into the latency equation — it is a guaranteed 3-7 mark question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Serving Strategy

| Workload Profile | Recommended Stack | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Low traffic, single GPU | vLLM (PagedAttention) or TGI | Easy to deploy, good defaults |
| High throughput, variable lengths | vLLM + continuous batching | Best $/token ratio |
| Latency-critical, predictable | TensorRT-LLM + static engine | Optimized kernels, deterministic |
| Multi-modal (vision + text) | vLLM with multimodal support or SGLang | Native multi-modal schedulers |
| OpenAI-compatible API needed | LiteLLM proxy in front | Drop-in replacement, easy migration |
| Edge / on-device | llama.cpp / Ollama / MLX (Apple Silicon) | CPU/Metal inference, INT4 quantized |
| Enterprise with strict governance | Azure OpenAI / AWS Bedrock / Vertex AI | VPC isolation, audit logs, regional residency |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cost Model for a Monthly Deployment

**Problem.** A startup serves 5 million requests/month. Average input = 800 tokens, output = 300 tokens. Input price = \$0.003 / 1K tokens, output = \$0.015 / 1K tokens (Llama-3 70B-class on a major API). Compute monthly bill.

**Step 1 — Total input tokens.**

$$
P_{in,\text{total}} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 800 = 4 \times 10^9 \text{ tokens}
$$

**Step 2 — Total output tokens.**

$$
P_{out,\text{total}} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 300 = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ tokens}
$$

**Step 3 — Input cost.**

$$
C_{in} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^9}{1000} \times 0.003 = 4 \times 10^6 \times 0.003 = \$12\,000
$$

**Step 4 — Output cost.**

$$
C_{out} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{1000} \times 0.015 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.015 = \$22\,500
$$

**Step 5 — Total.**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 12\,000 + 22\,500 = \$34\,500/\text{month}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output tokens are typically 5× more expensive than input tokens** because generation is sequential and cannot be parallelized like prefill. Always optimize prompts to keep outputs short!

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End LLM Production System (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client App or Web UI] --> B[API Gateway FastAPI or Envoy]
    B --> C[Auth and Rate Limiter JWT Token Bucket]
    C --> D[Prompt Builder and RAG Retriever]
    D --> E[Vector Database Pinecone or Weaviate or Qdrant]
    D --> F[Token Budget Controller]
    F --> G[Model Server vLLM or TGI or Triton]
    G --> H[KV Cache Paged Attention Pool]
    G --> I[Continuous Batch Scheduler]
    I --> J[GPU Cluster H100 or A100]
    J --> G
    G --> K[Streaming Response SSE or WebSocket]
    K --> L[Output Guardrails NeMo Guardrails or Guardrails AI]
    L --> M[PII Redaction and Moderation]
    M --> N[Response to Client]
    G --> O[Telemetry and Tracing OpenTelemetry and Langfuse]
    G --> P[Cost and Token Metering]
    O --> Q[Metrics Dashboard Grafana]
    P --> R[Billing Pipeline]
    L --> S[Quality Evaluator LLM as Judge RAGAS]
    S --> T[Feedback Loop Fine tune or Prompt Update]
    T --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single request flows **A → B → C → D → F → G → K → L → M → N**. The **telemetry loop (G → O → S → T → D)** closes the production system: every production response is continuously evaluated and the insights feed back into the prompt/RAG layer. This closed loop is the essence of **LLMOps**.

---

## 4.2 Inference Loop: Prefill vs Decode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Prompt Tokens] --> B[Prefill Phase Parallel]
    B --> C[Compute KV Cache for all prompt tokens]
    C --> D[First Token Generated]
    D --> E[Decode Phase Step by Step]
    E --> F[New KV Cache Entry]
    F --> G{Sampling Top p or Top k or Temperature}
    G --> H[Output Token]
    H --> I{Stop or EOS or Max Length}
    I -->|No| E
    I -->|Yes| J[Final Response]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Prefill** is **compute-bound** (heavy matrix multiplies, parallelizable).
> **Decode** is **memory-bandwidth-bound** (one token at a time, must read all weights).
> This asymmetry is why **chunked prefill**, **speculative decoding**, and **quantization** all target decode.

---

## 4.3 Continuous Batching vs Static Batching

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Static[Static Batching Waits for Slowest]
        S1[Request A long] --> S2[Request B short]
        S2 --> S3[Request C medium]
        S3 --> S4[GPU Idle until all done]
    end
    subgraph Continuous[Continuous Batching PagedAttention]
        C1[Slot 1] --> C2[Slot 2]
        C2 --> C3[Slot 3]
        C1 -. new request joins .- C2
        C3 -. finished leaves .- C4[Empty Slot reused]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 RAG Pipeline in Production

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Query] --> B[Query Rewriter HyDE or Multi Query]
    B --> C[Embed Query BGE or OpenAI or Cohere]
    C --> D[Vector Search Top k from Qdrant or FAISS]
    D --> E[Re ranker Cross Encoder]
    E --> F[Context Window Assembly]
    F --> G[Prompt with Citations]

    H --> K[Eval RAGAS Faithfulness and Relevance]
    K --> L[Drift Alerts to Dashboard]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q.** Define **TTFT** and **TPOT** in the context of LLM serving. Why is output-token latency typically 5× more expensive than input-token latency in production APIs?

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per key point):**

- **TTFT (Time To First Token):** the wall-clock time between the user's request hitting the API and the model emitting the first generated token. It dominates perceived "responsiveness" in streaming UIs. *(1 mark)*
- **TPOT (Time Per Output Token):** the average inter-token latency during the decode phase, i.e., time between successive generated tokens. Multiply by output length to get generation time. *(1 mark)*
- **Why output is costlier:** the decode phase is **memory-bandwidth-bound** and **sequential** (each new token depends on all previous ones, so it cannot be parallelized across the sequence). Prefill (input) is **compute-bound** and parallelizable, hence cheaper per token. This is why API pricing bills output tokens at 3-5× the input rate. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q.** List and briefly explain **three production techniques** to reduce LLM inference cost without changing the model architecture.

**Model Answer (1 mark per technique):**

1. **Quantization** (FP16 → INT8 / INT4) shrinks weight memory and speeds up matrix multiplies. A 7B model drops from ~14 GB to ~3.5 GB, allowing more requests per GPU. *(1 mark)*
2. **Continuous batching with paged attention** (vLLM) keeps GPU utilization high by inserting and removing requests every step instead of waiting for the longest in a static batch — often 2-4× throughput. *(1 mark)*
3. **Prompt caching / prefix caching** stores the KV-cache of common system prompts or few-shot examples, so repeat prefixes skip the prefill stage. Useful for chat apps with fixed system prompts. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model]`

#### (a) Explain the architecture of an end-to-end LLM production system. Discuss the roles of the API gateway, model server, KV-cache, and guardrails. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A production LLM system is a **layered architecture** designed to deliver reliable, low-latency, safe responses at scale.

**1. API Gateway (1.5 marks)**
- Entry point for all client requests (REST, gRPC, or WebSocket).
- Performs **TLS termination, authentication (API keys / OAuth / JWT)**, **rate limiting** (token-bucket or sliding-window), and **request logging**.
- Often implemented with FastAPI, Kong, or Envoy.

**2. Pre-processing & Prompt Builder (1 mark)**
- Injects the system prompt, retrieves RAG context from the vector DB, and concatenates chat history.
- Enforces a **token budget** to prevent abuse and keep costs predictable.

**3. Model Server (2 marks)**
- The actual inference engine — e.g., **vLLM, TGI, Triton, TensorRT-LLM**.
- Responsible for: (i) loading model weights into GPU VRAM, (ii) **continuous batching**, (iii) **KV-cache management** with paged attention, (iv) **streaming** generated tokens back to the client.

**4. KV-Cache Pool (1 mark)**
- Stores attention K/V tensors for every active request so the model does not recompute attention for past tokens.
- Managed in fixed-size pages (PagedAttention) to eliminate fragmentation and maximize concurrent requests per GPU.

**5. Output Guardrails (1 mark)**
- Post-generation filters that block: (i) **PII leaks** (using regex or NER), (ii) **toxic / harmful content** (classifier or LLM-as-judge), (iii) **prompt-injection echoes**, (iv) **hallucinations** (e.g., require citations).
- Tools: NeMo Guardrails, Guardrails AI, LLM-as-judge.

**6. Observability & Telemetry (0.5 mark)**
- Every request emits traces (OpenTelemetry), metrics (Prometheus), and logs (Loki) capturing TTFT, TPOT, token counts, cost, and user feedback.

---

#### (b) A startup must serve 2 million chat requests per month. Average input = 600 tokens, output = 250 tokens. API price: \$0.0025 / 1K input tokens and \$0.012 / 1K output tokens. Calculate the monthly bill. If the team reduces average output to 200 tokens by prompt-tuning, what is the new monthly bill and percentage savings? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Original total input tokens (1 mark):**

$$
P_{in} = 2 \times 10^6 \times 600 = 1.2 \times 10^9 \text{ tokens}
$$

**Step 2 — Original total output tokens (1 mark):**

$$
P_{out} = 2 \times 10^6 \times 250 = 5 \times 10^8 \text{ tokens}
$$

**Step 3 — Original input cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_{in} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9}{1000} \times 0.0025 = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.0025 = \$3\,000
$$

**Step 4 — Original output cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_{out} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^8}{1000} \times 0.012 = 5 \times 10^5 \times 0.012 = \$6\,000
$$

**Step 5 — Original total (1 mark):**

$$
C_{orig} = 3000 + 6000 = \$9\,000/\text{month}
$$

**Step 6 — New output tokens at 200 tokens/request (1 mark):**

$$
P_{out,new} = 2 \times 10^6 \times 200 = 4 \times 10^8
$$

$$
C_{out,new} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^8}{1000} \times 0.012 = 400\,000 \times 0.012 = \$4\,800
$$

**Step 7 — New total + savings (1 mark):**

$$
C_{new} = 3000 + 4800 = \$7\,800
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = \dfrac{9000 - 7800}{9000} \times 100\% \approx 13.33\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> Even a 20% reduction in output tokens yields > 13% monthly savings. **This is why KTU expects students to argue that prompt-engineering output brevity is a high-leverage production optimization.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model, Alternative Choice]`

#### (a) Discuss **continuous batching** and **paged attention**. How do they jointly improve GPU utilization during LLM serving? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Problem with Static Batching (2 marks)**

In a static (naïve) batch, $B$ requests are processed together. The GPU must wait for the **longest** request to finish decoding before releasing the batch. Shorter requests sit idle, wasting FLOPs and KV-cache memory. Empirically, GPU SM utilization drops to 30-50%.

**2. Continuous Batching (2.5 marks)**

In continuous batching (à la vLLM, TGI), the scheduler is invoked **every decoding step**:
- When a request finishes, its slot is **immediately freed** and a waiting request is admitted.
- When a new request arrives mid-batch, it can be admitted at the next step without waiting.
- This keeps every active SM busy, raising GPU utilization to **80-95%** and improving throughput 2-4×.

**3. Paged Attention (2.5 marks)**

Paged Attention treats the KV-cache like **OS virtual memory**:
- Each request's KV tensors are stored in **fixed-size pages** (typically 16 tokens each).
- A page table maps logical token positions to physical pages, allowing non-contiguous storage.
- Benefits: (i) **zero fragmentation** → higher effective VRAM use, (ii) **memory sharing** across requests with the same prefix (e.g., shared system prompt), (iii) **predictable memory accounting** → no OOM crashes, (iv) enables **>20× larger effective batch sizes**.

**Joint effect:** Continuous batching supplies the *workload elasticity*; paged attention supplies the *memory elasticity*. Together they make modern LLM serving economically viable at scale.

---

#### (b) Compare **RAG** with **fine-tuning** for production deployment. When should each be chosen? Mention two real-world scenarios where RAG is strictly preferred. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | RAG | Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge source** | External vector DB (live data) | Baked into model weights |
| **Update cost** | Re-index documents (cheap) | Retrain / LoRA (expensive) |
| **Latency overhead** | +50-200 ms retrieval | None |
| **Hallucination risk** | Lower (grounded) | Higher (parametric memory) |
| **Data privacy** | Documents stay in your DB | May leak training data |
| **Use case** | Evolving knowledge bases | Style, tone, format, tool use |

**When to choose RAG (1 mark each scenario, 2 marks for examples):**

1. **Live factual knowledge** — e.g., an Indian legal-tech chatbot pulling the latest Supreme Court judgments. Re-indexing a fresh judgment takes minutes; fine-tuning the model would take days and is outdated the moment new laws are passed.
2. **Source-cited answers** — e.g., a medical reference assistant that must cite the exact paragraph of a clinical guideline. RAG can return the source document ID; fine-tuning cannot expose its sources.

**When to choose fine-tuning (1 mark):**

- Style/format adaptation, e.g., training a model to always output valid JSON for function-calling, or to adopt a specific brand voice.

**Hybrid pattern (extra credit, 1 mark):**

> [!TIP]
> Many production systems use **RAG + fine-tuning together**: fine-tune for style/format, RAG for facts. This is the **state-of-the-art** pattern for enterprise copilots.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark question traps students fall into (KTU 2024 valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing inference with serving** — Inference = single forward pass. Serving = the entire system around it. If asked "explain LLM inference", do *not* spend 12 marks on transformer architecture; spend 5 on inference, 7 on serving.
> 2. **Ignoring cost economics** — Many students discuss latency but forget the **\$/1M tokens** dimension. KTU 2024 marking schemes explicitly award 2-3 marks for "cost awareness".
> 3. **Writing about fine-tuning in Module 4** — Module 3 covers fine-tuning. Module 4 is about **production deployment**. If the question says "in production", discuss serving, monitoring, scaling — not LoRA loss curves.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks for missing diagrams in any 14-mark "system" question. Always draw a labelled block diagram of the production pipeline.
> 5. **Mixing up KV-cache with model weights** — They are *different* memory components. KV-cache is **per-request** and **dynamic**; weights are **global** and **static**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting PII / safety** — In 2024 scheme, every production-system answer must mention at least one guardrail (PII, hallucination, jailbreak). Omission = 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU Module-4 Revision Pack — read this on exam morning.**

- [ ] **TTFT + TPOT × N_out = E2E Latency** — must be memorised.
- [ ] **Output tokens cost 3-5× more than input tokens** (sequential decode).
- [ ] **Continuous batching + Paged Attention** are the two killer serving innovations.
- [ ] **KV-cache memory** = $2 \cdot L \cdot h \cdot d_{head} \cdot S \cdot b_{kv}$ bytes per request.
- [ ] **Quantization**: FP32 → FP16/BF16 → INT8 → INT4 (4× → 2× → 1× → 0.5× bytes/param).
- [ ] **BF16 > FP16** for training stability; FP16 still common for inference on older GPUs.
- [ ] **vLLM** = open-source, PagedAttention, continuous batching. **TGI** = HuggingFace, production-ready. **TensorRT-LLM** = NVIDIA, peak performance.
- [ ] **RAG = cheap, live, citable; Fine-tuning = style, format, expensive**. Hybrid wins in 2024.
- [ ] **Production pillars**: serving, latency, cost, observability, safety, RAG/tooling.
- [ ] **Guardrails are non-negotiable** in 2024+: PII, jailbreak, hallucination, toxicity.
- [ ] **SLA = 99.9% (three nines) ≈ 8.7 h downtime/year**; 99.99% (four nines) ≈ 52 min/year.
- [ ] **Speculative decoding** uses a small draft model to propose $k$ tokens; the large model verifies them in one parallel pass → 2-3× speedup.
- [ ] **Online evaluation** (LLM-as-judge, user thumbs) + **offline evaluation** (RAGAS, BLEU) close the LLMOps feedback loop.
- [ ] **OpenTelemetry + Langfuse** is the de-facto observability stack for LLM apps.
- [ ] **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in any 14-mark "production system" answer.
- [ ] **Vector DBs**: Pinecone (managed), Weaviate (open-source), Qdrant (Rust, fast), Milvus (scalable), FAISS (research-grade).
- [ ] **Speculative Decoding upper bound**: $S \le 1 + (1 - p)(k - 1)$.
- [ ] **Cost reduction levers**, in order of impact: (1) shorten outputs, (2) cache prefixes, (3) quantize, (4) batch, (5) smaller model.
- [ ] **Token streaming (SSE / WebSocket)** is what gives the "typing" UX — every modern chatbot uses it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LLM Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LLM Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LLM Applications** refers to the broad spectrum of real-world tasks, systems, and production-grade pipelines built on top of pre-trained Large Language Models (such as GPT-4, LLaMA, PaLM, Mistral, and Claude) by leveraging their emergent abilities in natural language understanding, generation, reasoning, and instruction following. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the topic encompasses application architectures, prompting strategies, evaluation methodologies, domain-specific fine-tuning paradigms, and deployment considerations of LLMs in industry and research.

In technical terms, an *LLM application* is a system $A$ that takes a user input $x \in \mathcal{X}$ and produces an output $y \in \mathcal{Y}$ by conditioning on a base model $M_\theta$ and an optional context store $\mathcal{C}$:

$$A(x) = M_\theta(\text{format}(x, \mathcal{C}, p))$$

where $p$ is the prompt template, $\mathcal{C}$ is retrieved context (in RAG systems), and $M_\theta$ is the underlying LLM with parameters $\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the *engineering perspective* — how LLMs are composed into pipelines, retriever-reader systems, agentic workflows, evaluation harnesses, and fine-tuning paradigms like PEFT, LoRA, and RLHF/RLAIF.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an LLM as a **newly hired polymath graduate** — someone who has read almost every book in the library but has no specialized work experience. They can *theoretically* answer almost anything, but to be useful at your company, you must:

- **Give them a job description** (the **prompt template / system prompt**).
- **Hand them a file folder of relevant documents** before each question (this is **Retrieval-Augmented Generation, or RAG**).
- **Train them on a few examples from your domain** (this is **fine-tuning / PEFT**).
- **Set up a quality control process** (this is **evaluation and guardrails**).
- **Let them call tools and other employees** (this is **agentic workflows**).

The *application layer* is everything that turns a raw model into a productive employee.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of LLM Applications

| Pillar | One-Line Definition | Canonical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Prompting** | Crafting instructions to elicit the desired behavior | Zero-shot, few-shot, Chain-of-Thought |
| **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** | Grounding the model in external knowledge | Chat-with-your-PDF, enterprise search |
| **Fine-Tuning / Alignment** | Specializing the model for a domain or style | Medical-LLaMA, Code-Llama |
| **Agents & Tool Use** | Letting the model plan, call APIs, and act | AutoGPT, LangChain agents |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** The most commonly reported LLM evaluation metrics in application literature are **BLEU** ($\uparrow$ for translation), **ROUGE-L** ($\uparrow$ for summarization), **Exact Match (EM)** and **F1 Score** ($\uparrow$ for QA), **Perplexity (PPL)** ($\downarrow$ for fluency), and **LLM-as-a-Judge** win-rates (measured as a percentage, typically in the range **0–100%**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of LLM Applications

### 2.1.1 Natural Language Generation (NLG) Tasks
- **Text Generation / Completion:** Auto-regressive continuation $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$.
- **Summarization:** Map long document $D$ to a short abstract $S$; evaluated via **ROUGE**.
- **Machine Translation:** Learn $P(y \mid x)$ across languages.
- **Creative Writing:** Story, poem, script generation with controllable style.
- **Dialogue / Chat:** Multi-turn conversational generation with state tracking.

### 2.1.2 Natural Language Understanding (NLU) Tasks
- **Question Answering (QA):** Extractive, abstractive, multiple-choice.
- **Sentiment Analysis / Classification:** Single-label or multi-label.
- **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Sequence labeling reformulated as token classification.
- **Relation Extraction:** Identifying semantic relations between entities.
- **Natural Language Inference (NLI):** Entailment, contradiction, neutral.

### 2.1.3 Reasoning & Code Tasks
- **Arithmetic, Commonsense, and Logical Reasoning** — often unlocked by Chain-of-Thought prompting.
- **Code Generation** (HumanEval, MBPP benchmarks) — Python, Java, SQL synthesis.
- **Code Repair, Explanation, and Translation** between programming languages.

### 2.1.4 Retrieval & Knowledge-Intensive Tasks
- **Open-Domain QA** using retriever-reader architecture.
- **Knowledge-grounded dialogue.**
- **Fact verification and claim checking.**

### 2.1.5 Agentic & Tool-Using Applications
- **Web agents** (browse, click, fill forms).
- **API-calling agents** (booking, email, SQL).
- **Multi-agent orchestration** (AutoGen, CrewAI patterns).

## 2.2 Application Architecture Patterns

### Pattern A — Direct Prompting
The simplest pattern: the user query is wrapped in a template and sent to the model. Best for general chat, brainstorming, and tasks where world knowledge inside $\theta$ is sufficient.

### Pattern B — Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
A two-stage pipeline:
1. **Retriever** $R(q)$ returns top-$k$ relevant chunks from a vector store $\mathcal{V}$.
2. **Generator** $G$ produces the answer conditioned on retrieved context.

$$A(q) = M_\theta(\text{prompt}(q, R(q)))$$

**Why RAG?** It mitigates **hallucination**, **knowledge cutoffs**, and **private-data leakage** by externalizing knowledge.

### Pattern C — Fine-Tuned Application
Domain adaptation is done by updating $\theta$ (or a low-rank adapter $A$) on a curated dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\text{domain}}$.

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{domain}}} \left[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}(M_\theta(x), y) \right]$$

**LoRA** parameterizes updates as $\Delta W = B A$ with $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, $r \ll \min(d,k)$.

### Pattern D — Agentic Workflow
The model operates in a loop: **Think → Act → Observe → Reflect**.

$$\text{State}_{t+1} = f(\text{State}_t, M_\theta(\text{Plan}(\text{State}_t)))$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Range / Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Perplexity** | $\text{PPL}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | $\geq 1$, lower is better | Fluency, LM eval |
| 2 | **BLEU-n (modified precision)** | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $[0,1]$, higher is better | Translation |
| 3 | **ROUGE-L (F-measure)** | $F_{lcs} = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2) R_{lcs} P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}}$ | $[0,1]$ | Summarization |
| 4 | **Exact Match (EM)** | $\text{EM} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\hat{a}_i = a_i]$ | $[0,1]$ | Extractive QA |
| 5 | **F1 Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | QA, NER |
| 6 | **Token Accuracy** | $\text{Acc}_{\text{tok}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{1}[\hat{t}_i = t_i]$ | $[0,1]$ | Sequence labeling |
| 7 | **BERTScore (cosine)** | $\text{BS} = \frac{1}{\vert x \vert}\sum_{x_i \in x}\max_{y_j \in y} \langle \mathbf{e}_{x_i}, \mathbf{e}_{y_j}\rangle$ | $[0,1]$ | Semantic similarity |
| 8 | **Pass@k (Code)** | $\text{Pass@k} = 1 - \dfrac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$ | $[0,1]$ | Code generation |
| 9 | **LoRA parameter count** | $\vert\theta_{\text{LoRA}}\vert = r(d+k)$ | integer | Fine-tuning efficiency |
| 10 | **RAG context length** | $L_{\text{ctx}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\ell(c_i)$ | tokens | Retrieval budgeting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** In KTU examination scripts, always report metrics with their directionality arrow — for instance, "Perplexity **↓ (lower is better)**" and "F1 **↑ (higher is better)**". Examiners explicitly look for this convention.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Representative Application | Why LLMs Win |
|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Clinical note generation, radiology report structuring | Language-grounded, low-data interface |
| **Legal** | Contract review, case-law retrieval, clause extraction | Long-context comprehension (≥100K tokens) |
| **Education** | Personalized tutoring, automated grading, lesson planning | Adaptive explanation, multi-style output |
| **Software Engineering** | Copilot-style code completion, bug fixing, test generation | Strong code pre-training (e.g., Code-Llama) |
| **Customer Support** | Tier-1 chatbots, ticket triage, sentiment routing | Conversational fluency, intent detection |
| **Finance** | Earnings call summarization, ESG report analysis, KYC automation | Numeric + textual reasoning |
| **Search & E-commerce** | Semantic search, product description generation, recommendation | Dense retrieval + generative re-ranking |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — ROUGE-L F-Measure from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive the ROUGE-L score given a candidate summary $C$ and reference summary $R$, where $\text{LCS}(C, R)$ is the length of their Longest Common Subsequence.

### Step 1: Compute the LCS length
Let $\ell = \vert \text{LCS}(C, R) \vert$. This is the maximum number of tokens that appear in both $C$ and $R$ in the same relative order (not necessarily contiguously).

### Step 2: Compute ROUGE-L Recall
Recall measures how much of the reference is captured by the candidate:

$$R_{lcs} = \frac{\ell}{\vert R \vert}$$

The interpretation: if the reference has $\vert R \vert$ tokens and the candidate preserves $\ell$ of them in order, recall is the fraction preserved.

### Step 3: Compute ROUGE-L Precision
Precision measures how much of the candidate is supported by the reference:

$$P_{lcs} = \frac{\ell}{\vert C \vert}$$

### Step 4: Combine into F-measure
Apply the weighted harmonic mean with parameter $\beta$ controlling the relative importance (default $\beta = 1$, i.e., equal weighting):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{lcs} &= \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P_{lcs} \cdot R_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 \cdot P_{lcs}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{\ell}{\vert R \vert} \cdot \dfrac{\ell}{\vert C \vert}}{\dfrac{\ell}{\vert R \vert} + \beta^2 \cdot \dfrac{\ell}{\vert C \vert}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify by canceling $\ell$

$$
F_{lcs} = \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot \ell}{\vert C \vert + \beta^2 \cdot \vert R \vert}
$$

This is the **standard closed-form ROUGE-L formula** cited in Lin (2004) and used in nearly every KTU-graded summarization question.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Pass@k for Code Generation

**Setup:** A code generation system samples $n$ candidate programs; $c$ of them pass the hidden unit tests. We want the probability that at least one of $k$ randomly chosen samples passes.

### Step 1: Total combinations
The number of ways to choose $k$ candidates from $n$ is $\binom{n}{k}$.

### Step 2: Combinations where none pass
The number of ways to choose $k$ candidates from the $n-c$ failing ones is $\binom{n-c}{k}$.

### Step 3: Probability that all $k$ are failures
$$
P(\text{all fail}) = \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}
$$

### Step 4: Probability that at least one passes

$$
\text{Pass@k} = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}
$$

This is an **unbiased estimator** that allows evaluating code models with a finite $n$ sample budget. It is the standard metric on **HumanEval** and **MBPP**, both of which appear in KTU-graded code-LLM questions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ROUGE-L

Let reference $R = $ *"the cat sat on the mat"* ($\vert R \vert = 6$) and candidate $C = $ *"the cat is on the mat"* ($\vert C \vert = 6$).

**Step A — LCS:** The longest common subsequence is *"the cat on the mat"* (length $\ell = 5$).

**Step B — Recall:**

$$R_{lcs} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**Step C — Precision:**

$$P_{lcs} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**Step D — F-measure (β = 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{lcs} &= \frac{(1+1^2) \cdot 5}{6 + 1^2 \cdot 6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10}{12} \approx 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

So the candidate receives a **ROUGE-L = 0.833**, an excellent score since the only differing token (*"sat"* vs. *"is"*) does not affect the LCS.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Application Pattern

| Criterion | Prompting | RAG | Fine-Tuning | Agentic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Need for up-to-date facts** | Low | **High** | Low | Medium |
| **Private / proprietary data** | Poor | **Good** | Excellent | Good |
| **Latency budget (ms)** | **<500** | 500–2000 | <500 | 2000+ |
| **Compute cost ($/1k queries)** | **Lowest** | Medium | Lowest | Highest |
| **Reasoning depth required** | Medium | Medium | High | **Highest** |
| **Hallucination tolerance** | High | Low | Low | Medium |
| **Number of training examples needed** | 0 | 0 | 1k–100k | 0–100 |

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule for KTU 14-Mark Questions:** If a question mentions *"private company data"* or *"latest information"*, the canonical answer involves **RAG**. If it mentions *"specific tone/style"* or *"domain jargon"*, the canonical answer is **fine-tuning (often PEFT/LoRA)**. If it mentions *"calling external APIs"* or *"multi-step planning"*, the canonical answer is an **agentic workflow**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level LLM Application Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userQ[User Query] --> promptLayer[Prompt Template Layer]
    promptLayer --> contextBuild{Context Builder}
    vectorDB[(Vector Store)] --> retriever[Retriever top k]
    retriever --> contextBuild
    tools[External Tools / APIs] --> contextBuild
    contextBuild --> llmCore[LLM Core Mtheta]
    llmCore --> postProc[Post Processing]
    postProc --> guard[Guardrails / Safety Filter]
    guard --> userOut[Final Response to User]
    llmCore -. feedback .-> evalPipeline[Evaluation Pipeline]
    evalPipeline -. metrics .-> monitor[Monitoring Dashboard]
```

## 4.2 RAG Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    docs[Document Corpus] --> chunk[Chunking Module]
    chunk --> embed[Embedding Model]
    embed --> vectorDB[(Vector Database)]
    userQ[User Query] --> qEmbed[Query Embedder]
    qEmbed --> similarity[Similarity Search top k]
    vectorDB --> similarity
    similarity --> rerank[Optional Cross Encoder Rerank]
    rerank --> promptBuild[Prompt Builder with Context]
    promptBuild --> llmGen[LLM Generator]
    llmGen --> answer[Grounded Answer]
    answer --> cite[Citation Injector]
    cite --> finalResp[Final Cited Response]
```

## 4.3 Agentic Workflow Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[User Goal] --> plan[Planner LLM Decides Next Action]
    plan --> act[Execute Tool / API Call]
    act --> observe[Observe Tool Output]
    observe --> reflect[Reflect on Progress]
    reflect --> decision{Goal Achieved?}
    decision -->|No| plan
    decision -->|Yes| finalOut[Synthesize Final Output]
    reflect -. memory .-> memoryStore[(Episodic Memory Store)]
    memoryStore -. recalls context .-> plan
```

## 4.4 Fine-Tuning vs. RAG vs. Prompting Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New LLM Application Task] --> q1{Need proprietary knowledge?}
    q1 -->|No| q2{Need specialized style or format?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q3{Frequently changing knowledge?}
    q3 -->|Yes| ragAns[Use RAG with Vector Store]
    q3 -->|No| ftAns[Use Domain Fine Tuning with LoRA]
    q2 -->|Yes| fewShot[Use Few Shot Prompting]
    q2 -->|No| zeroShot[Use Zero Shot Prompting]
    ragAns --> eval[Evaluate with RAGAS or TruLens]
    ftAns --> eval
    fewShot --> eval
    zeroShot --> eval
    eval --> monitor[Deploy with Monitoring]
```

## 4.5 End-to-End LLM Application Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1. Use Case Definition] --> B[2. Data Collection and Curation]
    B --> C[3. Prompt Engineering and Baseline]
    C --> D{4. Sufficient Quality?}
    D -->|No| E[5a. RAG Implementation]
    D -->|No| F[5b. Fine Tuning PEFT]
    E --> G[6. Evaluation Harness]
    F --> G
    G --> D
    D -->|Yes| H[7. Safety and Red Teaming]
    H --> I[8. Deployment with Guardrails]
    I --> J[9. Continuous Monitoring]
    J --> K[10. Drift Detection and Retraining]
    K -. feedback loop .-> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
Differentiate between **Prompt Engineering**, **RAG**, and **Fine-Tuning** as strategies for building LLM applications. Mention one specific use case where each is most appropriate.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Strategy | Core Idea | Parameter Updates? | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prompt Engineering** | Carefully craft instructions and in-context examples to steer model output | **No** — only the input changes | General-purpose chatbots, quick prototyping, low-cost tasks |
| **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** | Augment the prompt with external documents retrieved at query time | **No** — but an external index is added | Chat-with-PDF, enterprise Q&A over proprietary documents, fact-grounded responses |
| **Fine-Tuning** | Update model weights on a domain-specific labeled dataset | **Yes** — full or partial (LoRA, PEFT) | Specialized style imitation (medical reports), domain jargon mastery, deterministic format adherence |

**1-Mark Allocation:** Correct identification of the three strategies (1 mark), valid differentiation along at least one axis (1 mark), appropriate use cases (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
List and briefly explain any **three standard evaluation metrics** used for LLM-based applications, and state whether each is "higher-is-better" or "lower-is-better".

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Perplexity (PPL)** — geometric mean of the inverse token probabilities; measures how *surprised* the model is. **Lower is better** (↓). Used for language modeling fluency.

2. **F1 Score** — harmonic mean of precision and recall; widely used in extractive QA and NER. **Higher is better** (↑). Robust to class imbalance.

3. **ROUGE-L** — longest-common-subsequence-based F-measure between candidate and reference summary. **Higher is better** (↑). Standard for summarization.

4. *(Optional, for extra credit)* **BLEU** — modified n-gram precision with brevity penalty for translation. **Higher is better** (↑).

**1-Mark Allocation:** Three correct metrics named (1 mark), correct formulas or intuition (1 mark), correct directionality arrows (1 mark).

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice provided. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** architecture in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of the retriever, vector store, embedding model, and generator. Discuss how RAG mitigates the problems of **hallucination** and **knowledge staleness** in LLMs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A developer wants to build a **legal-document Q&A system** for a law firm. The firm has **50,000 private PDF contracts** that change monthly. The system must produce answers with **citations** to specific clauses. **(7 Marks)**

- **(i)** Recommend an appropriate application architecture and justify why it is superior to vanilla prompting and full fine-tuning. **(3 Marks)**
- **(ii)** Suggest a suitable **chunking strategy**, **embedding model class**, and **vector database** for this use case. Justify each choice. **(2 Marks)**
- **(iii)** Define **RAGAS metrics** (Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, Context Precision, Context Recall) and explain what each measures in evaluating this system. **(2 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### (a) RAG Architecture (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a hybrid architecture that combines a **parametric** memory (the LLM's weights) with a **non-parametric** memory (an external document index) so that generation is conditioned on both.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    docs[Document Corpus] --> ch[Chunker]
    ch --> emb[Embedding Model E]
    emb --> vdb[(Vector Store V)]
    q[User Query q] --> qEmb[Query Embedder E]
    qEmb --> sim[Top k Similarity Search]
    vdb --> sim
    sim --> rerank[Optional Reranker]
    rerank --> pb[Prompt Builder]
    pb --> llm[LLM Generator M]
    llm --> ans[Answer with Citations]
```

**Operational Flow (2 marks):**
1. **Offline indexing:** Documents are chunked into passages $c_i$ of size 200–500 tokens with overlap. Each chunk is embedded as $\mathbf{e}_i = E(c_i) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and stored in a vector index $\mathcal{V}$.
2. **Online retrieval:** The query $q$ is embedded as $\mathbf{e}_q = E(q)$ and the top-$k$ chunks are retrieved via cosine similarity $\text{sim}(q, c_i) = \frac{\mathbf{e}_q \cdot \mathbf{e}_i}{\vert \mathbf{e}_q \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{e}_i \vert}$.
3. **Generation:** Retrieved chunks are concatenated into the prompt and passed to the LLM, which conditions on them to produce a grounded answer.

**Mitigating Hallucination & Staleness (2 marks):**
- **Hallucination ↓:** The model is forced to anchor its answer in retrieved text. If a question is unanswerable from the corpus, RAG systems can return *"I don't know based on the provided documents"*, dramatically reducing fabricated answers.
- **Staleness ↓:** New documents can be **indexed in real time** without retraining the model. This solves the knowledge-cutoff limitation of static LLMs.

#### (b) Legal Q&A System (7 Marks)

**(i) Recommended Architecture: RAG with Citation Injection (3 Marks)**
