- **Why not vanilla prompting?** Vanilla prompting has **no access to private contracts** and will hallucinate legal text — completely unacceptable in a legal context.
- **Why not full fine-tuning?** Full fine-tuning is **prohibitively expensive** (millions of dollars in compute for a 70B+ model), it **cannot be updated monthly** without retraining, and it **catastrophically forgets** general reasoning ability.
- **Why RAG?** It (a) ingests private documents at low cost, (b) updates incrementally as new contracts arrive, (c) preserves citations to exact clauses, and (d) keeps the base model frozen and reusable.

**(ii) Implementation Choices (2 Marks)**

| Component | Recommendation | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Chunking** | **Section/clause-aware** chunking (≈ 256–512 tokens, 10–20% overlap), preserving legal headings | Legal reasoning depends on clause structure; arbitrary token splits break clauses |
| **Embedding model** | A **domain-adapted dense encoder** (e.g., `BGE-large`, `E5-large`, or a legal-domain variant like `Legal-BERT`) | General embeddings underperform on legal jargon; domain encoders improve recall@10 by 10–20% |
| **Vector database** | **Managed vector DB with metadata filtering** (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) or open-source FAISS/Milvus | Metadata filters (date, jurisdiction, contract type) boost precision |

**(iii) RAGAS Metrics (2 Marks)**

- **Faithfulness (↑):** Measures whether the generated answer is *factually consistent* with the retrieved context. Low faithfulness ⇒ hallucination.
- **Answer Relevance (↑):** Measures whether the generated answer actually addresses the user's question. Computed via LLM-as-a-judge semantic similarity between the answer and the question.
- **Context Precision (↑):** Measures the proportion of retrieved chunks that are *actually relevant* to the question. High precision = clean retrieval.
- **Context Recall (↑):** Measures whether all information required to answer the question was *retrieved*. High recall = complete retrieval.

**Citation injection** can be evaluated separately as **Citation Precision** (are the cited clauses correct?) and **Citation Recall** (are all necessary clauses cited?).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **LLM-based Agents**. Explain the **ReAct (Reason + Act)** framework with a worked example. Describe how **tool use** and **memory** modules enable multi-step problem solving. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** You are tasked with deploying a **customer-support chatbot** for an e-commerce platform that handles: order tracking, refund initiation, FAQ answering, and escalation to human agents. Design the application architecture and explain: **(7 Marks)**

- **(i)** The **prompting strategy** (system prompt, tool definitions, few-shot examples). **(2 Marks)**
- **(ii)** The **tool/API layer** the agent will need. List at least four tools with their signatures. **(2 Marks)**
- **(iii)** The **safety and guardrail layer** (PII redaction, hallucination checks, escalation triggers). **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### (a) LLM Agents & ReAct (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** An **LLM agent** is a system in which the LLM operates as an *autonomous reasoning engine* that plans a sequence of actions, invokes external tools, observes results, and reflects until a goal is achieved.

**ReAct Framework (3 marks):** Introduced by Yao et al. (2022), **ReAct** interleaves **Reasoning traces** and **Action calls** in a Thought → Action → Observation loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Thought}_t &\sim M_\theta(\text{Thought}_{t-1}, \text{Action}_{t-1}, \text{Obs}_{t-1}, q) \\
\text{Action}_t &\sim M_\theta(\text{Thought}_t) \\
\text{Obs}_{t+1} &= \text{Env}(\text{Action}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example — "What is the population of the capital of France, divided by 2?"**

| Step | Thought | Action | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | The user wants me to find the capital of France first | `Search[capital of France]` | Paris |
| 2 | Now I need the population of Paris | `Lookup[population Paris]` | ≈ 2,140,000 |
| 3 | I can now compute 2,140,000 / 2 | `Calculator[2140000 / 2]` | 1,070,000 |
| 4 | I have the final answer | `Finish[1,070,000]` | Done |

**Tool Use & Memory (3 marks):**
- **Tools** are typed functions $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ exposed to the model via a JSON schema; the model emits structured calls.
- **Memory** has two parts: (a) **short-term** — the context window containing the trajectory; (b) **long-term** — an external vector store of past episodes that the agent can recall via retrieval.

#### (b) Customer-Support Chatbot (7 Marks)

**(i) Prompting Strategy (2 Marks):**

```
SYSTEM: You are "HelpBot", a polite customer-support agent for ShopKart.
RULES:
  - Never reveal internal order IDs starting with "internal_".
  - Always confirm the order number (format: 10 digits) before any action.
  - Escalate to a human if the user expresses anger or asks for a refund over ₹10,000.
TOOLS:
  - get_order_status(order_id: str) -> {status, eta, items}
  - initiate_refund(order_id: str, reason: str) -> {refund_id, eta}
  - search_faq(query: str) -> list[{q, a}]
  - escalate_to_human(ticket: str, summary: str) -> {ticket_id}
FEW-SHOT EXAMPLES: (3 provided in the system prompt)
```

**(ii) Tool / API Layer (2 Marks):**

| # | Tool Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `get_order_status(order_id: str) -> JSON` | Fetch live order state from OMS |
| 2 | `initiate_refund(order_id: str, reason: str) -> JSON` | Trigger refund workflow |
| 3 | `search_faq(query: str) -> List[Q&A]` | Retrieve FAQ answers from knowledge base |
| 4 | `escalate_to_human(ticket: str, summary: str) -> JSON` | Hand off to live agent |
| 5 | `send_tracking_link(customer_email: str, order_id: str) -> bool` | Email/SMS a tracking link |

**(iii) Safety & Guardrail Layer (3 Marks):**

1. **PII Redaction** — A pre-processor masks emails, phone numbers, and card numbers from logs using regex + a small NER model. Ensures **GDPR/DPDP Act 2023** compliance.
2. **Hallucination Check (RAGAS Faithfulness)** — For any answer grounded in `search_faq`, compute a faithfulness score; if below threshold $\tau = 0.7$, refuse to answer and ask for clarification.
3. **Escalation Triggers** — Sentiment classifier on user input; if negative-sentiment probability $> 0.8$ or the user requests *"speak to a human"*, immediately call `escalate_to_human` and terminate the agent loop.
4. **Tool-Call Constraints** — Use a **JSON-schema validator** to ensure the LLM cannot emit malformed tool calls. Restrict each tool to a **rate limit** and a **domain allow-list**.
5. **Output Filtering** — Block responses that contain URLs, phone numbers, or phrases matching a denylist of competitor names.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark Losers!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing RAG with Fine-Tuning (–2 marks typical):** RAG does *not* update the model's weights. Writing *"RAG fine-tunes the model on retrieved documents"* is a direct factual error and will be penalized.
> 2. **Forgetting metric directionality (–1 mark):** Reporting *"Perplexity: 0.85, higher is better"* is wrong on both counts. Always use **↓** for PPL and **↑** for BLEU, ROUGE, F1, EM.
> 3. **Drawing vague diagrams (–1 to –2 marks):** A RAG diagram must show *at least* the **chunker, embedder, vector store, retriever, generator**. Boxes labeled *"AI"* and *"Magic"* will receive zero credit.
> 4. **Skipping numerical derivations (–3 marks):** When asked to *"derive"*, simply stating the formula earns only 1–2 marks. You **must** show the algebraic transition (e.g., the $R_{lcs}, P_{lcs} \to F_{lcs}$ step-by-step cancellation).
> 5. **Omitting the "Why" (–1–2 marks per question):** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests *Apply* and *Analyze* (Bloom Levels 3 & 4). Just naming a metric without justifying its use case costs easy marks.
> 6. **Ignoring citations in RAG answers (–1 mark):** In any RAG question, the model answer section must mention **citation injection** or **source attribution** as a key design step.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- **Definition:** An LLM application is a system $A(x) = M_\theta(\text{format}(x, \mathcal{C}, p))$ that wraps a base model with prompting, retrieval, fine-tuning, or agentic logic.
- **Four pillars of LLM applications:** *Prompting, RAG, Fine-Tuning, Agents*.
- **RAG pipeline (offline + online):** chunk → embed → index → query → retrieve → generate → cite.
- **Fine-Tuning is for style/jargon; RAG is for facts/knowledge; Agents are for multi-step tool use.**
- **PEFT / LoRA:** parameter-efficient update with $\Delta W = B A$, $r \ll d$. Use when full fine-tuning is infeasible.
- **Key metrics (with direction):** Perplexity **↓**, BLEU **↑**, ROUGE-L **↑**, F1 **↑**, EM **↑**, Pass@k **↑**, BERTScore **↑**, RAGAS Faithfulness/Relevance/Precision/Recall **↑**.
- **RAGAS = Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, Context Precision, Context Recall** — the standard RAG evaluation framework; remember all four.
- **ReAct loop = Thought → Action → Observation → Reflection** (Yao et al., 2022).
- **Agent memory = short-term (context window) + long-term (vector store of past episodes).**
- **Guardrails are mandatory in production:** PII redaction, JSON-schema validation, escalation triggers, denylist filtering.
- **Domain highlights:** Healthcare (clinical notes), Legal (RAG with citations), Education (personalized tutoring), Software (code generation, Pass@k), Customer support (agentic chatbots).
- **Standard RAG chunk size: 200–500 tokens with 10–20% overlap; preserve document structure for legal/medical domains.**
- **Citation injection is a first-class design step** in RAG systems for high-stakes domains — examiners will look for it.
- **Evaluation is not optional:** always pair a deployed LLM app with an offline eval set (≥200 examples) and an online monitoring pipeline (latency, refusal rate, faithfulness drift).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LLM Evaluation Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LLM Evaluation Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LLM Evaluation Metrics** constitute the quantitative and qualitative measurement frameworks used to assess the performance, reliability, safety, and utility of Large Language Models across diverse tasks such as text generation, comprehension, reasoning, translation, summarization, and question answering. These metrics provide an objective basis to compare model variants, track training progress, and validate deployment readiness.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework, evaluation metrics map directly to **Course Outcomes (COs)** focused on analyzing, designing, and critically evaluating modern NLP/LLM pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECMT862, Module 4):**
> Evaluation metrics are classified along three orthogonal axes:
> 1. **Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic**
> 2. **Reference-based vs. Reference-free**
> 3. **Automated vs. Human-in-the-loop**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **driving examiner** assessing a student driver.

- A **multiple-choice theory test score** is like an **automated benchmark** (MMLU, GLUE) — fast, scalable, but doesn't capture true skill.
- **Driving on a real road with traffic** is like a **task-specific evaluation** (summarization accuracy, QA correctness) — closer to real-world utility.
- A **senior instructor riding along and giving verbal feedback** is like a **human evaluation / LLM-as-a-judge** — slow, expensive, but rich and nuanced.
- A **dashboard readout** (fuel efficiency, braking distance) is like **intrinsic metrics** such as **perplexity** — internal model health indicators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single metric tells the whole story. KTU 2024 emphasises that evaluation is **multi-metric, multi-axis, and multi-stakeholder** (developer, end-user, ethics board).

## 1.3 Core Categorical Taxonomy

| **Category** | **Examples** | **What They Measure** |
|---|---|---|
| N-gram Overlap | **BLEU**, **ROUGE**, **METEOR** | Lexical similarity to reference |
| Embedding-based | **BERTScore**, **MoverScore** | Semantic similarity |
| Probability-based | **Perplexity (PPL)**, **Cross-Entropy** | Language modeling fluency |
| Task Accuracy | **Accuracy, F1, Exact Match (EM)** | Downstream correctness |
| Reasoning | **MMLU, HellaSwag, ARC, GSM8K** | Knowledge + reasoning |
| Truthfulness | **TruthfulQA, HaluEval** | Factual reliability |
| Safety & Bias | **Toxicity, Regard, BBQ, StereoSet** | Harmful output detection |
| LLM-as-Judge | **G-Eval, MT-Bench, AlpacaEval** | Scalable qualitative scoring |
| Human Evaluation | **Likert ratings, A/B preference** | Gold-standard judgment |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)

**Origin:** Papineni et al., 2002 — originally for machine translation.

### 2.1.1 Modified n-gram Precision

For each n-gram of size $n$, compute the **clipped count** — the maximum number of times an n-gram appears in any single reference, capped at the count in the candidate.

$$
p_n = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \min\!\big(\text{count}_C(\text{ngram}),\ \max_{r \in R} \text{count}_r(\text{ngram})\big)}{\displaystyle\sum_{\text{ngram} \in C} \text{count}_C(\text{ngram})}
$$

where:
- $C$ = candidate (model output)
- $R$ = set of reference translations
- $p_n$ = modified n-gram precision

### 2.1.2 Brevity Penalty (BP)

Prevents short, trivial translations from scoring high.

$$
\text{BP} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } c > r \\
\exp\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{c}\right) & \text{if } c \le r
\end{cases}
$$

where $c$ = length of candidate, $r$ = length of *closest* reference.

### 2.1.3 Final BLEU Score

$$
\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)
$$

Typically $N=4$ with uniform weights $w_n = 1/4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BLEU ranges in **0 to 1** (often expressed 0–100). Higher = better.

## 2.2 ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation)

**Origin:** Lin, 2004 — for summarization.

### 2.2.1 ROUGE-N (Recall-based n-gram)

$$
\text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{r \in R} \sum_{\text{ngram}} \min\!\big(\text{count}_C(\text{ngram}),\, \text{count}_r(\text{ngram})\big)}{\displaystyle\sum_{r \in R} \sum_{\text{ngram}} \text{count}_r(\text{ngram})}
$$

### 2.2.2 ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence)

$$
R_{\text{lcs}} = \frac{\text{LCS}(C, R)}{m}, \quad P_{\text{lcs}} = \frac{\text{LCS}(C, R)}{n}
$$

$$
F_{\text{lcs}} = \frac{(1+\beta^2)\,R_{\text{lcs}}\,P_{\text{lcs}}}{R_{\text{lcs}} + \beta^2 P_{\text{lcs}}}
$$

where $m, n$ = lengths of reference and candidate. Typically $\beta \to \infty$ giving recall.

## 2.3 METEOR (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering)

Combines unigram matching with **stems, synonyms, and order penalties**.

$$
\text{METEOR} = F_{\text{mean}} \cdot (1 - \text{Penalty})
$$

$$
\text{Penalty} = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{\text{chunks}}{\text{matched unigrams}}\right)^3
$$

$$
F_{\text{mean}} = \frac{10\,P\,R}{R + 9P}
$$

where $P$ = precision, $R$ = recall on unigrams, *chunks* = contiguous matched groups.

## 2.4 Perplexity (PPL)

The most fundamental **intrinsic** metric for language models.

$$
\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)
$$

Equivalently in terms of cross-entropy $H$:

$$
\text{PPL} = e^{H}, \quad H = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower PPL = better.** It can be interpreted as the *effective branching factor* — how "confused" the model is on average at each token.

## 2.5 BERTScore

Uses contextual embeddings from BERT to compute token-level similarity.

$$
R_{\text{BERT}} = \frac{1}{|x|}\sum_{x_i \in x} \max_{y_j \in y} \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{y}_j
$$

$$
P_{\text{BERT}} = \frac{1}{|y|}\sum_{y_j \in y} \max_{x_i \in x} \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{y}_j
$$

$$
F_{\text{BERT}} = \frac{2 P_{\text{BERT}} R_{\text{BERT}}}{P_{\text{BERT}} + R_{\text{BERT}}}
$$

Cosine similarity is typically rescaled: $\text{score} = \frac{1}{|x|}\sum \frac{\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{y}_{\max}}{h}$ where $h$ is a scaling factor.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Core Formula** | **Range** | **Direction** | **Best Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BLEU | $\text{BP} \cdot \exp(\sum w_n \log p_n)$ | 0–1 | ↑ | Translation |
| ROUGE-N | $\frac{\text{matched n-grams}}{\text{ref n-grams}}$ | 0–1 | ↑ | Summarization |
| ROUGE-L | $F_{\text{lcs}}$ via LCS | 0–1 | ↑ | Summarization |
| METEOR | $F_{\text{mean}}(1 - \text{Penalty})$ | 0–1 | ↑ | Translation |
| PPL | $\exp\!\big(-\tfrac{1}{N}\sum \log P(w_i)\big)$ | 1–∞ | ↓ | LM fluency |
| BERTScore | $F_{\text{BERT}}$ (cosine over BERT) | 0–1 | ↑ | Semantic similarity |
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{\text{Total}}$ | 0–1 | ↑ | Classification |
| F1 | $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | 0–1 | ↑ | Imbalanced classes |
| Exact Match | $\mathbb{1}[\text{pred} = \text{gold}]$ | 0–1 | ↑ | QA, SQL |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BLEU/ROUGE** drive MLOps dashboards in production translation (Google Translate, DeepL) and news summarization systems.
- **Perplexity** is the *loss-curve proxy* used during pretraining of GPT, LLaMA, and Mistral.
- **MMLU** is the de-facto standard for "general intelligence" reporting on model release cards.
- **Toxicity & BBQ bias scores** are *regulatory artefacts* — they appear in EU AI Act compliance reports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: BLEU Score from Scratch

### Problem
Given:
- **Candidate** $C$ = "the the the cat sat"
- **Reference** $R$ = "the cat sat on the mat"
- $N = 2$ (use unigrams + bigrams, weights $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$)

Compute BLEU-2.

### Step 1 — Unigram Clipped Precision

Unigrams in $C$ and their reference counts:

| Unigram | Count in $C$ | Max count in $R$ | Clipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| "the" | 3 | 2 | 2 |
| "cat" | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| "sat" | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Total clipped = $2+1+1 = 4$. Total unigrams in $C$ = $5$.

$$
p_1 = \frac{4}{5} = 0.800
$$

### Step 2 — Bigram Clipped Precision

Bigrams in $C$:

| Bigram | Count in $C$ | Max in $R$ | Clipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| "the the" | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| "the cat" | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| "cat sat" | 1 | 1 | 1 |

Total clipped = $0+1+1 = 2$. Total bigrams in $C$ = $4$.

$$
p_2 = \frac{2}{4} = 0.500
$$

### Step 3 — Brevity Penalty

$c = 5$ (candidate length), $r = 6$ (reference length). Since $c \le r$:

$$
\text{BP} = \exp\!\left(1 - \frac{6}{5}\right) = \exp(-0.2) \approx 0.8187
$$

### Step 4 — Final BLEU

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BLEU-2} &= \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(0.5 \log p_1 + 0.5 \log p_2\right) \\
&= 0.8187 \cdot \exp\!\left(0.5 \ln 0.8 + 0.5 \ln 0.5\right) \\
&= 0.8187 \cdot \exp\!\left(0.5(-0.2231) + 0.5(-0.6931)\right) \\
&= 0.8187 \cdot \exp(-0.4581) \\
&= 0.8187 \cdot 0.6325 \\
&\approx 0.5179
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** BLEU-2 ≈ **0.518** (≈ 51.8 on 0–100 scale).

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Perplexity Computation

### Problem
A language model assigns the following log-probabilities to a 4-token sequence $(N=4)$:
- $\log P(w_1) = -2.3$
- $\log P(w_2) = -0.8$
- $\log P(w_3) = -1.5$
- $\log P(w_4) = -0.4$

Compute the perplexity.

### Step 1 — Cross-Entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i) \\
&= -\frac{1}{4}\big(-2.3 - 0.8 - 1.5 - 0.4\big) \\
&= -\frac{1}{4}(-5.0) = 1.25 \text{ nats}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Perplexity (in nats)

$$
\text{PPL} = e^{1.25} \approx 3.490
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Per-Token Base

If logs were base-10, use $10^{H}$ instead:

$$
\text{PPL}_{10} = 10^{1.25} \approx 17.78
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always report **the base** of the logarithm. KTU exam questions will specify *natural log* or *log base 2* explicitly.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **BLEU** | **ROUGE** | **METEOR** | **PPL** | **BERTScore** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reference required? | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (or no for rescaling) |
| Granularity | Corpus | Corpus/Doc | Corpus | Token-level | Sentence-level |
| Order-aware? | Partial (n-gram) | ✅ (LCS) | ✅ (chunks) | ❌ | ✅ (contextual) |
| Captures synonyms? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (stem/syn) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Captures semantics? | ❌ | ❌ | Partial | ❌ | ✅ |
| Speed | Very fast | Fast | Medium | Very fast | Slow (GPU) |
| Domain | Translation | Summarization | Translation | LM pretrain | All NLG |
| Bias toward | Precision | Recall | Balanced | Confidence | Semantic fidelity |

## 3.4 Worked Example: F1 for Binary Classification

True labels: $[1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1]$, Predictions: $[1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1]$.

Confusion Matrix:

- TP = 3 (positions 1, 3, 6)
- FP = 1 (position 2)
- FN = 1 (position 4)
- TN = 1 (position 5)

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.75}{0.75 + 0.75} = \frac{1.125}{1.5} = 0.75
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Evaluation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LLM Model Output] --> B{Reference Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Reference-Based Metrics]
    B -- No --> D[Reference-Free Metrics]

    C --> C1[N-gram Overlap]
    C --> C2[Embedding-Based]
    C --> C3[Task-Specific]

    C1 --> C1a[BLEU]
    C1 --> C1b[ROUGE-N]
    C1 --> C1c[METEOR]
    C2 --> C2a[BERTScore]
    C2 --> C2b[MoverScore]
    C3 --> C3a[Accuracy F1 EM]

    D --> D1[Intrinsic LM]
    D --> D2[Safety Bias]
    D --> D3[LLM-as-Judge]

    D1 --> D1a[Perplexity]
    D1 --> D1b[Cross-Entropy]
    D2 --> D2a[Toxicity Score]
    D2 --> D2b[BBQ Bias]
    D2 --> D2c[Regard Score]
    D3 --> D3a[G-Eval]
    D3 --> D3b[MT-Bench]
    D3 --> D3c[AlpacaEval]

    C1a --> E[Aggregate Scorecard]
    C1b --> E
    C1c --> E
    C2a --> E
    C2b --> E
    C3a --> E
    D1a --> E
    D1b --> E
    D2a --> E
    D2b --> E
    D2c --> E
    D3a --> E
    D3b --> E
    D3c --> E

    E --> F[Human Validation]
    F --> G[Production Release Decision]
```

## 4.2 BLEU Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate Text] --> B[N-gram Extraction]
    R[Reference Texts] --> B
    B --> C[Count n-grams in C]
    B --> D[Count n-grams in R]
    C --> E[Clipped Count via Max in R]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Modified Precision pn]
    F --> G[Log Weighted Sum]
    A --> H[Length Check]
    R --> H
    H --> I[Brevity Penalty BP]
    G --> J[Final BLEU Score]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Perplexity Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tokenized Test Set] --> B[Forward Pass through LM]
    B --> C[Per-Token Log-Probabilities]
    C --> D[Mean Negative Log-Likelihood = Cross-Entropy H]
    D --> E[Exponentiate: PPL = exp H]
    E --> F{PPL Lower is Better}
    F -- Yes --> G[Compare Across Model Sizes]
    F -- No --> H[Investigate Domain Mismatch]
```

## 4.4 Metric Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Evaluation Metric] --> Q1{Application Domain?}
    Q1 -- Translation --> M1[BLEU + METEOR + Human]
    Q1 -- Summarization --> M2[ROUGE-L + BERTScore + Human]
    Q1 -- QA --> M3[Exact Match + F1 + LLM-Judge]
    Q1 -- Open Chat --> M4[MT-Bench + AlpacaEval + Human]
    Q1 -- Pretraining --> M5[Perplexity + Cross-Entropy]
    Q1 -- Safety Audit --> M6[Toxicity + BBQ + TruthfulQA]

    M1 --> End[Final Scorecard]
    M2 --> End
    M3 --> End
    M4 --> End
    M5 --> End
    M6 --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model QP]**
**Define perplexity. Why is it considered an intrinsic evaluation metric for language models? Derive its mathematical relationship with cross-entropy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Perplexity is the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood assigned by a language model to a sequence of $N$ tokens:

$$
\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)
$$

The inner term is the per-token **cross-entropy** $H$ (measured in nats if natural log is used). Therefore:

$$
\text{PPL} = e^{H}
$$

It is *intrinsic* because it measures the model's internal confidence/probability assignment **without needing a reference output or downstream task**. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between BLEU and ROUGE metrics. In which application areas are they respectively preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BLEU | ROUGE |
|---|---|---|
| Emphasis | **Precision** (avoiding extra n-grams) | **Recall** (capturing all reference n-grams) |
| Origin | Machine Translation | Text Summarization |
| Length control | Brevity Penalty | Implicit via LCS |
| Variants | BLEU-1..4 | ROUGE-N, L, W, S |

BLEU is preferred in **machine translation** where false-positive matches are critical. ROUGE is preferred in **summarization** where missing content (false negatives) is the bigger concern. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice Option 1]*

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4, Sample QP Pattern]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **three-axis taxonomy** of LLM evaluation metrics (intrinsic vs extrinsic, reference-based vs reference-free, automated vs human). Provide **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **BLEU-2 score** for the following:
- Candidate: *"the cat is on the mat"*
- Reference: *"there is a cat on the mat"*
- Use equal weights $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **three-axis taxonomy** classifies evaluation along orthogonal dimensions:

**1. Intrinsic vs Extrinsic**
- **Intrinsic** — measures the model *in isolation* on a held-out test set.
  - Examples: **Perplexity**, **Cross-Entropy**, **Bits-Per-Character (BPC)**. *(1 mark)*
- **Extrinsic** — measures performance on a *downstream task*.
  - Examples: **QA Accuracy (SQuAD)**, **Summarization ROUGE**. *(1 mark)*

**2. Reference-based vs Reference-free**
- **Reference-based** — needs a gold output to compare against.
  - Examples: **BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, BERTScore**. *(1 mark)*
- **Reference-free** — no gold required; uses internal signals.
  - Examples: **Perplexity, MAUVE, Self-BLEU**. *(1 mark)*

**3. Automated vs Human-in-the-loop**
- **Automated** — fast, reproducible, scalable.
  - Examples: **BLEU, MMLU, HellaSwag**. *(1 mark)*
- **Human** — slow, expensive, but captures nuance.
  - Examples: **Likert-scale ratings, A/B preference tests, expert annotation**. *(1 mark)*

A clear **diagram** (refer Section 4.4) should be drawn to show metric selection branching. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Unigram Clipped Precision**
Unigrams in $C$: *{the:2, cat:1, is:1, on:1, mat:1}* — total = 6.
Unigrams in $R$: *{there:1, is:1, a:1, cat:1, on:1, mat:1}* — total = 6.

Clipped counts (min of count in $C$ and max in $R$):
- "the" → $\min(2,0) = 0$ (no "the" in $R$)
- "cat" → $\min(1,1) = 1$
- "is" → $\min(1,1) = 1$
- "on" → $\min(1,1) = 1$
- "mat" → $\min(1,1) = 1$

Clipped total = $0+1+1+1+1 = 4$. *(1 mark)*

$$
p_1 = \frac{4}{6} = 0.6667 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Step 2 — Bigram Clipped Precision**
Bigrams in $C$: *the cat, cat is, is on, on the, the mat* (5 total).
None of these bigrams appear in $R$. Clipped total = 0. *(1 mark)*

$$
p_2 = \frac{0}{5} = 0 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Step 3 — Brevity Penalty**
$c = 6$, $r = 6$. Since $c = r$, $\text{BP} = 1$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Final BLEU**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BLEU-2} &= 1 \cdot \exp(0.5 \ln 0.6667 + 0.5 \ln 0) \\
&= 1 \cdot \exp(-\infty) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for final answer + 1 mark for correctly noting zero due to absent bigram overlap)*

**Insight:** A score of **0** signals that the candidate shares *zero bigrams* with the reference, even though unigram overlap is 67%. This is the classic **"BLEU hates paraphrase"** failure mode — semantic preservation without lexical identity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice Option 2]*

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4, Sample QP Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **BERTScore** and **METEOR** metrics. How does BERTScore overcome the limitations of BLEU/ROUGE? Provide formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A language model produces the following per-token log-probabilities (natural log) over a 5-token test sequence: $[-1.2, -2.1, -0.5, -3.0, -1.7]$. Compute the **perplexity** and the **cross-entropy**. State the interpretation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020):** *(1 mark)*
Uses **contextual token embeddings** from BERT to compute pairwise cosine similarity between candidate and reference tokens.

$$
R_{\text{BERT}} = \frac{1}{|x|}\sum_{x_i \in x} \max_{y_j \in y} \cos(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_j)
$$

$$
P_{\text{BERT}} = \frac{1}{|y|}\sum_{y_j \in y} \max_{x_i \in x} \cos(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_j)
$$

$$
F_{\text{BERT}} = \frac{2 P_{\text{BERT}} R_{\text{BERT}}}{P_{\text{BERT}} + R_{\text{BERT}}}
$$

*(1 mark for each formula — total 3 marks)*

**METEOR (Banerjee & Lavie, 2005):** *(1 mark)*
Aligns unigrams with **stems, synonyms, and paraphrases** using WordNet. Penalises fragmented matches via *chunks*:

$$
F_{\text{mean}} = \frac{10 P R}{R + 9 P}, \quad \text{METEOR} = F_{\text{mean}} \cdot (1 - 0.5 \cdot (\tfrac{\text{chunks}}{\text{matched}})^3)
$$

*(1 mark for METEOR formula and explanation)*

**How BERTScore overcomes BLEU/ROUGE limitations:** *(1 mark)*
- BLEU/ROUGE require **exact surface match** — fail on valid paraphrases.
- BERTScore measures **semantic similarity in embedding space** — rewards meaning preservation.
- Example: "happy" ↔ "joyful" — BLEU = 0, BERTScore ≈ 0.85.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Sum of Log-Probabilities** *(1 mark)*

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} \log P(w_i) = -1.2 - 2.1 - 0.5 - 3.0 - 1.7 = -8.5
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Entropy** *(2 marks)*

$$
H = -\frac{1}{5}\sum \log P(w_i) = -\frac{-8.5}{5} = 1.7 \text{ nats/token}
$$

**Step 3 — Perplexity** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{PPL} = e^{1.7} \approx 5.4739
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation** *(2 marks)*

> The model is, on average, as "confused" as if it had to choose uniformly between ~5.47 words at each position. A PPL of 5.47 is **excellent** for general English text (GPT-2 reports ~29 on WikiText-103; LLaMA-2-7B reports ~5.5). Lower perplexity ⇒ better language model.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Sign errors in perplexity:** Forgetting the negative sign gives $e^{-1.7} = 0.18$ — completely wrong direction. Always double-check: lower PPL = better.
> 2. **Misreporting BLEU range:** BLEU ∈ [0, 1] is the *canonical* form; some tools display ×100. Specify which you use.
> 3. **Confusing precision and recall in ROUGE:** ROUGE-N measures **recall** (numerator = reference n-grams), not precision.
> 4. **Omitting the Brevity Penalty in BLEU:** A candidate shorter than reference should be penalised. Skipping BP is a 1-mark loss in Part B.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU evaluators reward **every visible step**. A jump from log-probabilities to perplexity without showing $H$ loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing bases in logarithm:** Always state **natural log, log₂, or log₁₀** explicitly. The numeric PPL changes by orders of magnitude.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Perplexity** is **exp(cross-entropy)**; lower is better; intrinsic metric.
- ⭐ **BLEU** = Brevity Penalty × geometric mean of n-gram precisions; range [0, 1]; favours translation.
- ⭐ **ROUGE** = recall-based; ROUGE-L uses **Longest Common Subsequence**; favours summarization.
- ⭐ **METEOR** = $F_{\text{mean}} \times (1 - \text{chunk penalty})$; supports stems/synonyms.
- ⭐ **BERTScore** = cosine similarity in BERT-embedding space; overcomes *paraphrase blindness* of n-gram metrics.
- ⭐ **F1 = 2PR/(P+R)**; use for **imbalanced classification**.
- ⭐ **Exact Match (EM)** = binary indicator; standard for **SQuAD-style QA**.
- ⭐ **MMLU, HellaSwag, ARC, GSM8K** = core **reasoning/knowledge benchmarks**.
- ⭐ **TruthfulQA, HaluEval** = **hallucination/factuality** benchmarks.
- ⭐ **MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, G-Eval** = **LLM-as-a-judge** scalable qualitative metrics.
- ⭐ **Toxicity, BBQ, StereoSet, Regard** = **fairness/safety** metrics.
- ⭐ Always report **the base of log, the tokenization scheme, and the corpus** alongside any perplexity number.
- ⭐ **No single metric suffices** — KTU expects a **multi-metric scorecard** approach.
- ⭐ For KTU derivations: write **every algebraic step**; **state the base**; **label variables**; **include interpretation**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LLM Benchmark Datasets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** LLM Benchmark Datasets # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **LLM Benchmark Datasets** are **standardized, carefully curated, and publicly available evaluation corpora** specifically designed to quantitatively measure and compare the capabilities of Large Language Models across diverse cognitive, linguistic, reasoning, and task-oriented dimensions. They function as the **"standardized examination paper"** of the LLM ecosystem, providing reproducible, objective, and comparable metrics for model performance. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, a benchmark dataset is formally defined as a triple: $$\mathcal{B} = \langle \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{M} \rangle$$ Where: - $\mathcal{D}$ = the dataset (input instances and ground-truth labels) - $\mathcal{T}$ = the task definition (input-output schema) - $\mathcal{M}$ = the metric (evaluation function that scores predictions) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Universal Olympiad"** > > Imagine if every student in the world were judged only on a single school's internal exam. We would never know how good a student from Kerala is compared to one from Tokyo. To solve this, international bodies created **standardized tests** (SAT, GRE, JEE, NEET) — a common yardstick. **LLM Benchmark Datasets are exactly this for AI models**. Whether GPT-4 was trained in San Francisco or LLaMA was trained in Meta's labs, both are tested on the *same* exam paper (e.g., MMLU). The score they receive is directly comparable. Without benchmarks, the LLM field would be like sports without a scoreboard. ## 1.3 Why Benchmarks Are Critical | Role | Engineering Importance | |------|------------------------| | **Comparability** | Allows fair head-to-head ranking of models (e.g., GPT-4 vs Claude vs Gemini) | | **Reproducibility** | Same dataset → same test → verifiable results | | **Progress Tracking** | Tracks state-of-the-art (SOTA) over time | | **Capability Probing** | Isolates specific skills (math, code, reasoning) | | **Safety Auditing** | Detects bias, toxicity, hallucination | | **Research Direction** | Defines what the community prioritizes | > [!IMPORTANT] > A benchmark is considered **"saturated"** when top models exceed ~90% accuracy, after which it loses diagnostic value and a harder benchmark must be devised (e.g., MMLU → MMLU-Pro). --- ## 1.4 Categories of LLM Benchmark Datasets (Taxonomy) The major families are: 1. **Knowledge & Reasoning** — MMLU, AGIEval, BBH (BIG-Bench Hard) 2. **Mathematical Reasoning** — GSM8K, MATH, MathBench 3. **Code Generation** — HumanEval, MBPP, CodeContests 4. **Reading Comprehension** — SQuAD, RACE, BoolQ 5. **Commonsense Reasoning** — HellaSwag, WinoGrande, PIQA 6. **Truthfulness & Factuality** — TruthfulQA, FactScore, HaluEval 7. **Multilingual** — XTREME, MMLU-Pro, FLORES 8. **Long-Context** — LongBench, L-Eval, Needle-in-a-Haystack 9. **Human Preference / Chat** — MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, Chatbot Arena (Elo) 10. **Safety & Bias** — BBQ, RealToxicityPrompts, StereoSet ## 1.5 Core Evaluation Metrics — Quick Reference - **Perplexity (PPL)** — language modeling fluency - **Accuracy** — classification-style tasks - **Exact Match (EM)** — string-level identity (QA, code) - **F1 Score** — token overlap (SQuAD) - **BLEU / ROUGE / METEOR** — text generation overlap - **Pass@k** — code correctness - **Elo Rating** — pairwise human preference - **LLM-as-Judge** — GPT-4 scores another model's output # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Benchmark Dataset A benchmark dataset has **three structural layers**: 1. **Input Schema** — the question/prompt format (multiple-choice, free-form, fill-in) 2. **Gold Reference** — human-verified correct answers 3. **Evaluation Protocol** — few-shot vs zero-shot, chain-of-thought on/off, scoring function ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow of Benchmarking > [!NOTE] > **The Benchmarking Pipeline (How an LLM is evaluated):** - **Step 1 — Dataset Curation:** Domain experts write/curate high-quality questions with verified answers. - **Step 2 — Train/Test Split:** Benchmarks are *evaluation-only*; the test set is held out (and often kept private to prevent contamination). - **Step 3 — Prompt Formatting:** Questions are converted to the model's expected chat template (e.g., `<|user|>...<|assistant|>`). - **Step 4 — Inference:** The LLM generates an answer (often with temperature ≈ 0 for determinism). - **Step 5 — Answer Extraction:** The textual output is parsed (e.g., extract `"The answer is (B)"`). - **Step 6 — Scoring:** Compare prediction to gold label using the chosen metric $\mathcal{M}$. - **Step 7 — Aggregation:** Report mean ± standard deviation across runs. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **high-yield for KTU exams**. Memorize the formulas, their variables, and their applicability. | # | Metric | Formula | Use Case | |---|--------|---------|----------| | 1 | **Perplexity** | $\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(w_i \mid w_{ [!NOTE] > **Brevity Penalty in BLEU:** $BP = \min\left(1, e^{1 - r/c}\right)$, where $r$ = reference length, $c$ = candidate length. Prevents penalizing short translations. ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Metric - **Perplexity** is *intrinsic* — it measures the model's confidence in its own predictions. Lower PPL = more fluent model. - **BLEU/ROUGE** are *extrinsic* — they measure n-gram overlap with reference text. They reward lexical similarity but miss semantics. - **Pass@k** acknowledges that for code, *any one* correct solution in $k$ attempts is sufficient — hence the combinatorial formula. - **Elo** (used in Chatbot Arena) treats LLMs as chess players; wins/losses update ratings dynamically, producing a global ranking. - **LLM-as-Judge** uses a strong model (e.g., GPT-4) to score outputs of a weaker model — a scalable proxy for human evaluation. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Benchmark Use | |--------|---------------| | **Search Engines** | ROUGE, BLEU for snippet quality | | **Code Assistants (Copilot, Cursor)** | HumanEval, MBPP, LiveCodeBench | | **Medical LLMs (Med-PaLM)** | MedQA, PubMedQA | | **Legal AI** | LegalBench, CaseHOLD | | **Tutoring Systems** | GSM8K, MATH, MathBench | | **Customer Service Chatbots** | MT-Bench, Chatbot Arena Elo | | **Government / Defense** | RealToxicityPrompts, BBQ bias testing | ## 2.6 Critical Pitfalls in Benchmarking > [!WARNING] > **Data Contamination (Memorization)** > If benchmark questions appear in the pre-training corpus, the model may *memorize* answers rather than reason. Modern benchmarks use **held-out private test sets** (e.g., MMLU-Pro's 2024 refresh) to combat this. > [!WARNING] > **Goodhart's Law** — *"When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure."* Training *on* the benchmark inflates scores artificially. The community now demands **contamination checks** and **dynamic benchmarks**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Perplexity from Cross-Entropy Perplexity is the *exponential* of average per-token cross-entropy. The derivation follows: **Step 1 — Sentence Probability.** For a tokenized sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$, the autoregressive probability is: $$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})$$ **Step 2 — Log-Probability.** To avoid numerical underflow, take the log: $$\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{ [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** If PPL = 50, the model is "as confused" as if it had to choose uniformly between 50 tokens at each step. Lower is better. ## 3.2 Derivation: Pass@k (Code Generation) The **Pass@k** metric asks: "If the model is allowed $k$ attempts, what is the probability that *at least one* is correct?" **Step 1 — Setup.** Let $n$ = total samples generated, $c$ = correct samples, and we draw $k$ of $n$ uniformly at random. **Step 2 — Probability of Choosing All Wrong.** We need the probability that *all* $k$ chosen samples are among the $n - c$ wrong ones: $$P(\text{all wrong}) = \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$ **Step 3 — Complement.** Pass@k is the complement: $$\text{Pass@k} = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$ **Step 4 — Common Special Case ($k=1$).** Pass@1 = $c/n$ (the basic accuracy). **Step 5 — Worked Example.** Suppose $n=10$, $c=4$, $k=2$: $$\binom{6}{2} = 15, \quad \binom{10}{2} = 45$$ $$\text{Pass@2} = 1 - \frac{15}{45} = 1 - 0.333 = 0.667$$ So even with only 40% individual accuracy, the chance of getting *at least one* correct in 2 tries is **66.7%**. ## 3.3 Derivation: BLEU Score Components **Step 1 — Modified n-gram Precision.** Count clipped n-gram matches: $$p_n = \frac{\sum_{C \in \text{Candidates}} \sum_{gram_n \in C} \text{Count}_{clip}(gram_n)}{\sum_{C} \sum_{gram_n \in C} \text{Count}(gram_n)}$$ **Step 2 — Geometric Mean of Precisions.** $$ \text{GEOMEAN} = \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right) $$ where $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ (uniform weights). **Step 3 — Brevity Penalty.** $$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) & \text{if } c \leq r \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 — Final BLEU.** $$\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Limitation:** BLEU rewards exact n-gram matches. A valid paraphrase scores poorly. This motivated **METEOR** (which uses synonyms) and **BERTScore** (which uses contextual embeddings). ## 3.4 Derivation: F1 Score from Precision & Recall Given $TP$ (true positive), $FP$ (false positive), $FN$ (false negative): **Step 1 — Precision.** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ **Step 2 — Recall.** $$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ **Step 3 — Harmonic Mean.** The F1 score is the weighted harmonic mean (with $\beta = 1$): $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$ **Why harmonic mean?** It penalizes extreme imbalances. A model with $P=1.0, R=0.01$ gets $F_1 = 0.02$, not 0.5. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Major LLM Benchmarks (Comparative) | Benchmark | Year | Domain | Size | Format | Metric | Top Model Score (2024) | |-----------|------|--------|------|--------|--------|------------------------| | **MMLU** | 2020 | Multitask knowledge | 57 subjects, 16k Q | 4-choice MCQ | Accuracy | ~88% | | **MMLU-Pro** | 2024 | Harder MMLU | 12k Q, 10-choice | 10-choice MCQ | Accuracy | ~76% | | **GSM8K** | 2021 | Grade-school math | 8.5k problems | Free-form | Exact Match | ~97% (saturated) | | **MATH** | 2021 | Competition math | 12.5k problems | Free-form | Exact Match | ~85% | | **HumanEval** | 2021 | Python coding | 164 problems | Function completion | Pass@k | ~95% | | **MBPP** | 2021 | Basic Python | 974 problems | Code | Pass@1 | ~90% | | **HellaSwag** | 2019 | Commonsense | 70k | 4-choice | Accuracy | ~96% | | **WinoGrande** | 2019 | Coreference | 44k | 2-choice | Accuracy | ~94% | | **TruthfulQA** | 2021 | Truthfulness | 817 Q | MC + gen | MC1/MC2 | ~75% | | **BIG-Bench Hard (BBH)** | 2022 | Hard reasoning | 23 tasks | Mixed | Accuracy | ~90% | | **ARC-Challenge** | 2018 | Science MCQ | 7.8k | 4-choice | Accuracy | ~96% | | **Chatbot Arena Elo** | 2023 | Human preference | millions | Pairwise | Elo rating | GPT-4 ~1300 | | **MT-Bench** | 2023 | Multi-turn chat | 80 Q | Multi-turn | LLM-as-Judge (10-pt) | ~9.4/10 | | **AlpacaEval** | 2023 | Instruction following | 805 Q | Open-ended | Win rate vs GPT-4 | ~85% | | **LongBench** | 2023 | Long context | 4.7k | Mixed | F1 / Rouge | varies | ## 3.6 Saturation Analysis (Why New Benchmarks Emerge) $$\text{Saturation Level} \approx 1 - \frac{1}{N}$$ where $N$ = random-guess threshold. For 4-choice MCQ, random = 25%, so saturation begins >90%. Many 2020-era benchmarks (HellaSwag, ARC-Easy) are now saturated → **necessitating harder successors** (MMLU-Pro, GPQA, FrontierMath). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of LLM Benchmark Datasets ```mermaid graph TB ROOT["LLM BENCHMARK DATASETS"] --> KNOW["KNOWLEDGE AND REASONING"] ROOT --> MATH["MATHEMATICAL REASONING"] ROOT --> CODE["CODE GENERATION"] ROOT --> LANG["LANGUAGE UNDERSTANDING"] ROOT --> COMMON["COMMONSENSE REASONING"] ROOT --> TRUTH["TRUTHFULNESS"] ROOT --> MULTI["MULTILINGUAL"] ROOT --> LONG["LONG CONTEXT"] ROOT --> CHAT["CHAT AND PREFERENCE"] ROOT --> SAFE["SAFETY AND BIAS"] KNOW --> MMLU["MMLU"] KNOW --> MMLUPRO["MMLU PRO"] KNOW --> BBH["BBH BIG BENCH HARD"] KNOW --> GPQA["GPQA DIAMOND"] KNOW --> AGIE["AGIEVAL"] MATH --> GSM["GSM8K"] MATH --> MATHB["MATH"] MATH --> MATHBN["MATHBENCH"] MATH --> FRONT["FRONTIERMATH"] CODE --> HE["HUMANEVAL"] CODE --> MBPP["MBPP"] CODE --> LIVE["LIVECODEBENCH"] CODE --> APPS["APPS"] CODE --> SWE["SWE BENCH"] LANG --> SQUAD["SQUAD"] LANG --> RACE["RACE"] LANG --> GLUE["GLUE SUPERGLUE"] LANG --> BOOL["BOOLQ"] COMMON --> HELLA["HELLASWAG"] COMMON --> WINO["WINOGRANDE"] COMMON --> PIQA["PIQA"] COMMON --> SOCR["SOCIALIQA"] TRUTH --> TQA["TRUTHFULQA"] TRUTH --> FACT["FACTSCORE"] TRUTH --> HALU["HALUEVAL"] MULTI --> XTREME["XTREME"] MULTI --> FLORES["FLORES 200"] MULTI --> MMLUML["MMLU PRO MULTILINGUAL"] LONG --> LONGB["LONGBENCH"] LONG --> LEVAL["L EVAL"] LONG --> NEEDLE["NEEDLE IN A HAYSTACK"] LONG --> RULER["RULER"] CHAT --> MTB["MT BENCH"] CHAT --> ALPA["ALPACAEVAL"] CHAT --> ARENA["CHATBOT ARENA ELO"] CHAT --> LMSYS["LMSYS CHAT"] SAFE --> BBQ["BBQ BIAS"] SAFE --> TOX["REALTOXICITYPROMPTS"] SAFE --> STEREO["STEREOSET"] SAFE --> ADV["ADVBENCH JAILBREAK"] style ROOT fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px style KNOW fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style MATH fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style CODE fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style LANG fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff style COMMON fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style TRUTH fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff style MULTI fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style LONG fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff style CHAT fill:#a5a5a5,color:#000000 style SAFE fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 End-to-End Benchmarking Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CURATE DATASET
DOMAIN EXPERTS"] --> B["HOLD OUT TEST SET
PRIVATE OR PUBLIC"] B --> C["FORMAT PROMPTS
CHAT TEMPLATE"] C --> D["INFERENCE
LLM GENERATES ANSWER
TEMPERATURE NEAR ZERO"] D --> E["PARSE OUTPUT
REGEX OR HEURISTIC"] E --> F["COMPARE WITH GOLD
METRIC FUNCTION"] F --> G["AGGREGATE SCORES
MEAN STD ERROR"] G --> H["PUBLISH LEADERBOARD
PUBLIC COMPARISON"] D -.MONITOR.-> I["LOGPROBS FOR PERPLEXITY"] E -.MONITOR.-> J["MULTI SAMPLES FOR PASS K"] F -.MONITOR.-> K["LLM AS JUDGE FOR OPEN ENDED"] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff style D fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style E fill:#ffc000,color:#000000 style F fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff style G fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000 style H fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style I fill:#deeaf6,color:#000000 style J fill:#deeaf6,color:#000000 style K fill:#deeaf6,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Capability Coverage Map ```mermaid mindmap root((LLM CAPABILITY COVERAGE)) REASONING Deductive Logic Mathematical Proof Causal Inference Analogical Reasoning KNOWLEDGE World Facts Domain Expertise Multilingual Facts LANGUAGE Syntax Mastery Semantics Pragmatics Long Form Generation ALIGNMENT Helpfulness Harmlessness Honesty ROBUSTNESS Adversarial Inputs Out of Distribution Noisy Context ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an LLM benchmark dataset. List any four categories with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An LLM benchmark dataset is a standardized evaluation corpus used to quantitatively measure and compare the performance of large language models on well-defined tasks. It consists of input instances, gold reference outputs, and a defined scoring metric. Four categories with examples: 1. **Knowledge & Reasoning** — MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) 2. **Mathematical Reasoning** — GSM8K 3. **Code Generation** — HumanEval 4. **Truthfulness** — TruthfulQA *(1 mark definition + 2 marks for categories — 0.5 each pair)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the difference between Perplexity and BLEU score. When is each used?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Perplexity (PPL)** is an *intrinsic* metric that measures the language model's uncertainty in predicting the next token. **Lower is better**. Used for evaluating raw language modeling capability (e.g., base models). *(1.5 marks)* - **BLEU** is an *extrinsic* metric that measures n-gram overlap between generated text and reference text. **Higher is better**. Used for machine translation and text generation tasks. *(1.5 marks)* --- ## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q. (a)** Explain the MMLU benchmark in detail. Discuss its construction, evaluation protocol, and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)** *(1 mark)* **Construction (3 marks):** - MMLU was introduced by Hendrycks et al. in 2020. - It covers **57 academic subjects** spanning STEM, humanities, social sciences, and professional fields (e.g., medicine, law, ethics). - Contains approximately **16,000 multiple-choice questions** (4 choices each). - Questions are manually collected from existing academic resources (GRE, GMAT, college exams, professional certification tests). - Each question has a single correct answer; the test set is held out for fair evaluation. **Evaluation Protocol (2 marks):** - Models are evaluated in a **5-shot setting** (5 in-context examples before each test question) by default, though zero-shot is also reported. - The prompt format is standardized: "Question: ... A) ... B) ... C) ... D) ... Answer:" - The model's output is parsed to extract the letter choice. - Final score is the **mean accuracy** across all 57 subjects. **Limitations (1 mark):** - **Data contamination:** Public MCQ banks may be in pre-training data. - **Saturation:** Top models exceed 88%, reducing diagnostic power. - **Format bias:** Multiple-choice format doesn't reflect real-world open-ended generation. - **Cultural bias:** Western-centric subject distribution. --- **Q. (b)** Describe the Pass@k metric used in code-generation benchmarks. Derive its formula and explain why it is preferred over simple accuracy. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Pass@k — Definition and Use (2 marks):** Pass@k is the metric used in code-generation benchmarks like **HumanEval** and **MBPP**. It measures the probability that, when allowed $k$ independent code samples, *at least one* is functionally correct. It is preferred because code has a "binary" success criterion (it either runs or it doesn't), and a single solution is enough for a real user. **Derivation (3 marks):** - Let $n$ = total samples generated per problem, $c$ = correct ones, $n - c$ = incorrect. - Choose $k$ from $n$ uniformly. Probability all $k$ are wrong: $$P(\text{all wrong}) = \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$ - Pass@k = 1 minus this: $$\text{Pass@k} = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$ - Worked example: $n=10, c=4, k=2 \Rightarrow \text{Pass@2} = 1 - 15/45 = 0.667$. **Why Preferred Over Accuracy (2 marks):** - Real users retry prompts until success — Pass@k mirrors this UX. - A model with 40% Pass@1 has 66.7% Pass@2 — useful for temperature sampling strategies. - Rewards **diversity** of outputs, not just the most confident one. - Captures the "exploration" nature of code synthesis where multiple valid solutions exist. --- ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Discuss the TruthfulQA benchmark. Why is truthfulness important for LLMs, and how is TruthfulQA evaluated? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Importance of Truthfulness (2 marks):** LLMs trained on web-scale data may memorize and reproduce false claims, stereotypes, conspiracy theories, and outdated information. Truthfulness is critical for: (i) **safety** in healthcare/legal applications, (ii) **user trust**, and (iii) mitigating hallucination. A truthful model should refuse to answer or say "I don't know" rather than fabricate. **TruthfulQA — Overview (2 marks):** - Introduced by Lin et al. (2021) — 817 questions across 38 categories (health, law, finance, politics, etc.). - Designed so that humans frequently give *false* answers due to common misconceptions. - Tests the model against a "human false belief" baseline. **Evaluation — MC1 and MC2 (3 marks):** - **MC1 (Single-True):** Multiple-choice with 1 correct + 4–5 plausible-but-false distractors. Scored by accuracy (higher = better). - **MC2 (Multi-True):** Multi-select — assign probabilities and compute normalized probability mass on the truthful options. - **Generation mode:** Model writes a free-form answer; evaluated by a fine-tuned **GPT-3 "judge"** (GPT-judge) that classifies the answer as true/false. - **Limitation:** It is small (817 questions) and adversarial, so high MC1 scores are hard. --- **Q. (b)** Compare BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR as text generation metrics. Why are they being replaced by embedding-based metrics in modern LLM evaluation? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **BLEU (1.5 marks):** - Measures modified n-gram *precision* of candidate vs. reference. Includes brevity penalty. - **Strength:** Fast, language-agnostic. **Weakness:** Insensitive to semantics, penalizes valid paraphrases. **ROUGE (1.5 marks):** - Measures n-gram *recall* (ROUGE-N), longest common subsequence (ROUGE-L), and skip-bigram (ROUGE-S). - **Strength:** Captures recall (important for summarization). **Weakness:** Same lexical rigidity as BLEU. **METEOR (1.5 marks):** - Harmonic mean of P and R with **stems, synonyms, and paraphrases** via WordNet. - Includes a fragmentation penalty. - **Strength:** Better correlation with human judgment. **Weakness:** Slower, language-dependent. **Why Embedding-Based Metrics (2.5 marks):** - Modern metrics like **BERTScore**, **MoverScore**, and **BLEURT** use *contextual embeddings* (from BERT, etc.) to compare semantic similarity. - They handle paraphrases, synonyms, and even cross-lingual similarity. - They correlate much better with human judgment (Spearman ρ ≈ 0.85 vs 0.55 for BLEU). - They are essential for **open-ended generation** where many valid responses exist. - **LLM-as-Judge** (using GPT-4) further improves correlation to ~0.95 but introduces bias. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Don't confuse intrinsic and extrinsic metrics.** Perplexity ≠ task performance. A model with low PPL can still score poorly on MMLU. > 2. **Don't write "BLEU = ROUGE."** They differ fundamentally: BLEU is precision-weighted, ROUGE is recall-weighted. > 3. **Always state the k value in Pass@k** — Pass@1 ≠ Pass@10. > 4. **Mention contamination** in any long-answer about benchmarks; examiners reward awareness of this issue. > 5. **Cite specific years and authors only if you are certain** — fabricating references costs marks. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — LLM Benchmark Datasets** - ⭐ **Definition:** A benchmark = $\langle$ Dataset, Task, Metric $\rangle$ — the "Olympiad exam" of LLMs. - ⭐ **Categories (10):** Knowledge, Math, Code, Language, Commonsense, Truthfulness, Multilingual, Long-Context, Chat/Preference, Safety/Bias. - ⭐ **Key Benchmarks:** MMLU (knowledge), GSM8K (math), HumanEval (code), HellaSwag (commonsense), TruthfulQA (truthfulness), Chatbot Arena (preference Elo), MT-Bench (multi-turn), LongBench (long context), BBH (reasoning). - ⭐ **Perplexity:** $\text{PPL} = \exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log P(w_i \mid w_{ 90% trigger need for harder successors (MMLU → MMLU-Pro). - ⭐ **Data Contamination:** When benchmark questions leak into training data → invalidates evaluation. Use held-out private test sets. - ⭐ **Goodhart's Law:** When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure — drives dynamic benchmarks. - ⭐ **Few-shot vs Zero-shot:** MMLU uses 5-shot; TruthfulQA uses zero-shot. Always check the protocol. - ⭐ **Why benchmarks matter:** Comparability, reproducibility, SOTA tracking, capability probing, safety auditing. - ⭐ **Modern trends:** Dynamic, contamination-resistant, multimodal (text+image+audio), and human-preference-based benchmarks. - ⭐ **BERTScore / BLEURT:** Embedding-based metrics replacing n-gram metrics for open-ended generation.

topics.title: LLM Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LLM Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 | LLM Selection — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LLM Selection** is the systematic, multi-criteria decision-making process of identifying, evaluating, and adopting the most suitable *Large Language Model* for a given downstream task, application domain, or production deployment pipeline. The process integrates performance benchmarking, cost–latency profiling, contextual alignment, ethical compliance, and operational constraints to map a model to its optimal use-case.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, LLM Selection is positioned as a critical **Pre-Training & Adaptation layer decision** in the LLM lifecycle — preceding *prompt engineering*, *fine-tuning*, and *RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)* integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> LLM Selection is a *program elective (PECMT862)* topic that bridges theoretical model evaluation and real-world engineering deployment. It is a high-weightage topic in **Module 4 (LLM Operations & Optimization)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Consultant" Model

Imagine you are the **CEO of a logistics company** that needs expert advice on three different problems:

| Problem Domain | Best Choice | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Legal contract drafting | Senior Lawyer (expensive, slow, precise) | Needs deep, narrow expertise |
| Customer greeting messages | Chatbot intern (cheap, fast, generic) | High volume, low stakes |
| Medical diagnosis support | Specialist Doctor (verified, certified) | Zero tolerance for errors |

You would NOT hire the doctor to write greeting cards, nor the intern to draft contracts.

> **LLM Selection works exactly the same way.** Different LLMs have different *capability profiles*, *cost structures*, and *specialization domains*. Selection is the engineering discipline of matching the right model to the right job.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of LLM Selection

Every LLM selection decision revolves around **three primary axes**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Selection Triad (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> 1. **Capability (Quality)** — How well does the model perform on the target task? → Benchmark scores, human eval, task-specific accuracy.
> 2. **Cost (Economics)** — What is the total cost of ownership? → API pricing, GPU-hours, fine-tuning cost, storage.
> 3. **Constraint (Operational Fit)** — Does it fit our deployment reality? → Latency, context window, hardware, data privacy, latency SLA.

Any model selection that ignores one of these three pillars is **engineering malpractice**.

---

## 1.4 Why LLM Selection is a Non-Trivial Problem

A common student misconception is: *"Just pick GPT-4 / Llama-3 / Claude — they all work."*

This is **dangerously wrong**. Selection is hard because:

- **Capability vs Cost trade-off:** A 70B model is not always better than a well-tuned 7B model for narrow tasks.
- **Task diversity:** Code generation, summarization, multilingual QA, and math reasoning each have *different state-of-the-art leaders*.
- **Closed vs Open-source tension:** Proprietary models often lead benchmarks but lock you into vendor pricing, data pipelines, and rate limits.
- **Rapid model churn:** New SOTA models are released every 1–3 months (as of 2024–2025), making yesterday's "best" obsolete.
- **Latency vs Quality:** A real-time chatbot cannot wait 30 seconds for a 400B-parameter model to respond.

---

## 1.5 The LLM Selection Pipeline (High-Level Preview)

```
Identify Use-Case → Define Requirements → Shortlist Candidates 
        → Benchmark → Profile Cost/Latency → Human Evaluation 
        → Pilot Deployment → Final Selection
```

This 8-step pipeline is what every KTU 14-mark question on LLM Selection expects you to describe.

---

## 1.6 Key Terminology Glossary (Must-Know for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Foundation Model** | The base, pre-trained LLM (e.g., LLaMA-3-70B) before any task-specific tuning |
| **Instruction-Tuned Model** | Foundation model fine-tuned on instruction-response pairs (e.g., LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct) |
| **Context Window** | Maximum number of tokens the model can process in a single inference call |
| **Throughput** | Number of tokens generated per second across concurrent requests |
| **Time-to-First-Token (TTFT)** | Latency before the first generated token appears |
| **Perplexity (PPL)** | Exponential of cross-entropy loss; lower = better language modeling |
| **Quantization** | Reducing model precision (e.g., FP16 → INT4) to shrink size and speed up inference |
| **RAG** | Retrieval-Augmented Generation; pairs an LLM with an external knowledge retriever |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 | LLM Selection — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Selection Criteria (Engineering Framework)

A rigorous LLM selection requires evaluation along **six orthogonal dimensions**:

### 2.1.1 Capability Criteria (Quality)
- **Benchmark Performance**: MMLU, HellaSwag, HumanEval, GSM8K, TruthfulQA, BBH, MT-Bench, AlpacaEval.
- **Task-Specific Accuracy**: Measured on a held-out gold dataset for the target task.
- **Reasoning Depth**: Chain-of-thought (CoT) ability, multi-step problem solving.
- **Robustness**: Resistance to prompt injection, hallucination rate, adversarial inputs.

### 2.1.2 Cost Criteria (Economics)
- **API Cost** (for proprietary): $\$/1M$ input tokens, $\$/1M$ output tokens.
- **Self-Hosting Cost**: GPU rental (e.g., AWS p4d.24xlarge ≈ $\$32/hr$), electricity, storage.
- **Fine-Tuning Cost**: One-time training cost on custom data.
- **Token Consumption**: Input prompt length × request volume × days.

### 2.1.3 Latency Criteria (Real-Time Performance)
- **TTFT (Time-to-First-Token)**: Critical for streaming UIs.
- **TPOT (Time-per-Output-Token)**: Critical for long generations.
- **End-to-End Latency**: TTFT + (output length × TPOT).

### 2.1.4 Context Window Criteria
- **Maximum Input Length**: 4K, 8K, 32K, 128K, 200K, 1M tokens.
- **Need**: Document QA, code repo analysis, long conversations require long contexts.
- **Trade-off**: Longer context = slower inference, higher cost, more memory.

### 2.1.5 Operational Criteria
- **License**: Apache 2.0, MIT (commercial-friendly) vs. RAIL, custom (restricted).
- **Data Privacy**: On-premise deployment (Llama) vs. external API (OpenAI).
- **Compliance**: GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2 alignment.
- **Vendor Lock-in**: Ability to switch providers without re-engineering.

### 2.1.6 Safety & Alignment Criteria
- **Refusal Rate**: How often the model refuses benign queries (over-refusal is bad UX).
- **Harmful Content Generation**: Toxicity, bias, jailbreak resistance.
- **Truthfulness**: Hallucination rate, citation accuracy.

---

## 2.2 The Benchmark Hierarchy

KTU expects students to know the **canonical benchmark ladder**:

| Benchmark | Domain | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **MMLU** | Multi-task Language Understanding | 57 academic subjects; broad knowledge |
| **HellaSwag** | Commonsense Reasoning | Sentence completion plausibility |
| **HumanEval** | Code Generation | Functional correctness (pass@k) |
| **GSM8K** | Math Reasoning | Grade-school word problems |
| **TruthfulQA** | Factual Truthfulness | Resistance to common false beliefs |
| **MT-Bench** | Multi-turn Conversation | Dialogue quality (LLM-as-Judge) |
| **AlpacaEval** | Instruction Following | Win-rate against a baseline |
| **BBH (BIG-Bench Hard)** | Hard Reasoning | 23 challenging tasks |
| **IFEval** | Instruction Following | Verifiable format compliance |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for LLM selection problems in KTU exams. Memorize the column headers and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Total Cost} = (T_{in} \cdot C_{in}) + (T_{out} \cdot C_{out})$ | API cost per request | USD |
| 2 | $C_{monthly} = N_{req} \cdot D \cdot \left( \bar{T}_{in} C_{in} + \bar{T}_{out} C_{out} \right)$ | Monthly API spend | USD/month |
| 3 | $L_{e2e} = T_{TTFT} + N_{out} \cdot T_{TPOT}$ | End-to-end latency | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{tokens}}{T_{total}}$ | Tokens per second (server-level) | tokens/s |
| 5 | $PPL(X) = \exp\left( -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i}) \right)$ | Perplexity of sequence X | dimensionless (lower=better) |
| 6 | $\text{pass@k} = 1 - \dfrac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$ | Code pass@k (k of n samples correct) | probability |
| 7 | $M_{GPU} \approx \dfrac{P \cdot B}{8}$ (FP16) | Min GPU memory estimate (GiB) | GiB |
| 8 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Total}}$ | Classification / QA accuracy | ratio |
| 9 | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | F1 score (precision-recall) | ratio |
| 10 | $T_{decode} \approx \dfrac{N_{out}}{v_{GPU}}$ | Decode time for $N_{out}$ tokens | seconds |
| 11 | $C_{fine\text{-}tune} = H \cdot P_{GPU} \cdot C_{hr}$ | Fine-tuning cost (H GPU-hours) | USD |
| 12 | $\text{HRAM} = \dfrac{\text{Refusals on benign}}{\text{Total benign}}$ | Harmful Refusal Avoidance Metric | ratio |

**Symbol Legend:**
- $T_{in}, T_{out}$: input / output tokens
- $C_{in}, C_{out}$: cost per 1M input / output tokens (USD)
- $N_{req}$: requests per day, $D$: days
- $T_{TTFT}$: time-to-first-token
- $T_{TPOT}$: time-per-output-token
- $P, B$: model parameters, batch size
- $v_{GPU}$: GPU decode speed (tokens/s)
- $H$: GPU-hours consumed for fine-tuning

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LLM selection is **the single highest-leverage decision** in any LLM-powered product. A poor choice manifests as:

- 💸 **Cost blowout**: A team picks GPT-4 for simple classification → pays \$50K/month instead of \$500/month using a fine-tuned 7B model.
- 🐢 **Latency SLA violation**: Using a 70B model in a real-time chatbot → users see 15-second delays.
- 🔓 **Compliance failure**: Sending PII to an external API in violation of GDPR.
- 📉 **Quality regression**: A small model used for complex reasoning → users get wrong answers, churn.

Conversely, a good selection yields:
- **10x–100x cost reduction** (e.g., DistilBERT vs GPT-4 for classification).
- **Sub-second latency** (e.g., 7B quantized model on a single A100).
- **Compliance-safe deployment** (on-prem open-source model for healthcare).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4 | LLM Selection — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Monthly API Cost Calculation

> **Problem Setup:** A startup uses an LLM API with pricing $C_{in} = \$2.50$ per 1M input tokens and $C_{out} = \$10.00$ per 1M output tokens. The application handles $N_{req} = 5{,}000$ requests/day. Average input prompt length $\bar{T}_{in} = 800$ tokens, average output length $\bar{T}_{out} = 200$ tokens. Calculate monthly cost ($D = 30$ days).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Per-request cost formula.**

$$
C_{req} = \bar{T}_{in} \cdot C_{in} + \bar{T}_{out} \cdot C_{out}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (unit normalization required).**

Since $C_{in}, C_{out}$ are quoted **per 1M tokens**, we divide token counts by $10^6$:

$$
C_{req} = \left( \dfrac{800}{10^6} \right) \cdot 2.50 + \left( \dfrac{200}{10^6} \right) \cdot 10.00
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate each term.**

$$
C_{req} = (0.0008 \cdot 2.50) + (0.0002 \cdot 10.00)
$$

$$
C_{req} = 0.0020 + 0.0020 = \$0.0040 \text{ per request}
$$

**Step 4: Scale to monthly volume.**

$$
C_{monthly} = N_{req} \cdot D \cdot C_{req}
$$

$$
C_{monthly} = 5{,}000 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.0040
$$

$$
C_{monthly} = 150{,}000 \cdot 0.0040 = \$600.00 \text{ per month}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{C_{monthly} = \$600/\text{month}}$

> **Interpretation:** At this volume, the cost is manageable. If $N_{req}$ grew to $500{,}000$/day, cost would scale linearly to **\$60,000/month** — at which point self-hosting a 7B model becomes economically superior.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: End-to-End Latency Decomposition

> **Problem Setup:** An LLM has $T_{TTFT} = 0.35$ s and $T_{TPOT} = 0.025$ s/token. A user request expects $N_{out} = 150$ output tokens. Calculate end-to-end latency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the latency decomposition formula.**

$$
L_{e2e} = T_{TTFT} + N_{out} \cdot T_{TPOT}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$
L_{e2e} = 0.35 + (150 \cdot 0.025)
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate.**

$$
L_{e2e} = 0.35 + 3.75 = 4.10 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_{e2e} = 4.10 \text{ s}}$

> **Engineering insight:** TTFT contributes only $\sim 8.5\%$ of total latency here. For long generations, **TPOT dominates**. To reduce $L_{e2e}$, optimization should target *throughput per token*, not TTFT.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: GPU Memory Estimation

> **Problem Setup:** Estimate minimum GPU memory to inference a 13B parameter model in FP16, with batch size $B=1$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Memory model.** Each FP16 parameter uses 2 bytes. Total parameter memory:

$$
M_{params} = P \cdot 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $P = 13 \times 10^9$:**

$$
M_{params} = 13 \times 10^9 \cdot 2 = 26 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to GiB (dividing by $1024^3$):**

$$
M_{params} = \dfrac{26 \times 10^9}{1.0737 \times 10^9} \approx 24.2 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Step 4: Add KV-cache and activations (typically $\sim 20\%$ overhead for $B=1$):**

$$
M_{total} \approx 24.2 \cdot 1.2 \approx 29.0 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{M_{total} \approx 29 \text{ GiB (requires an A100 40GB or L40S)}}$

> **Rule of thumb:** $M_{GPU} \approx 2 \cdot P$ bytes for FP16 inference at $B=1$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: pass@k Computation

> **Problem Setup:** For HumanEval-style evaluation, $n = 10$ code samples are generated per problem, of which $c = 7$ pass unit tests. Compute pass@1 and pass@5.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: General pass@k formula (unbiased estimator):**

$$
\text{pass@k} = 1 - \dfrac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute pass@1.**

$$
\text{pass@1} = 1 - \dfrac{\binom{10-7}{1}}{\binom{10}{1}} = 1 - \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.70
$$

**Step 3: Compute pass@5.**

$$
\text{pass@5} = 1 - \dfrac{\binom{3}{5}}{\binom{10}{5}} = 1 - \dfrac{0}{252} = 1.00
$$

(Note: $\binom{3}{5} = 0$ since $5 > 3$, meaning pass@5 is trivially 1.0 whenever $c \geq k$.)

**Final Answer:** pass@1 $= 0.70$, pass@5 $= 1.00$ (degenerate case since $c \geq k$).

> **Takeaway:** pass@k is most informative when $k$ is moderately larger than $c/n$, capturing the model's *sampling diversity*.

---

## 3.5 The Selection Decision Matrix (Analytical Tool)

For KTU exams, you may be asked to *weight* criteria and compute a composite selection score. The standard approach is the **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)**:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \text{norm}(x_{ij})
$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = composite score for candidate model $i$
- $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$ (with $\sum w_j = 1$)
- $x_{ij}$ = raw score of model $i$ on criterion $j$
- $\text{norm}(\cdot)$ = min-max normalization to $[0, 1]$

### Worked Example: Choosing Between 3 LLMs

Suppose criteria weights are: Capability $w_1 = 0.5$, Cost $w_2 = 0.3$, Latency $w_3 = 0.2$.

| Model | Capability (0–100) | Cost (USD/M tokens) | Latency (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 (GPT-4 class) | 92 | 30 | 4.5 |
| M2 (Llama-70B) | 85 | 8 | 2.0 |
| M3 (Mistral-7B) | 70 | 0.5 | 0.8 |

**Step 1: Normalize each column (min-max: $\text{norm} = (x - \min)/(\max - \min)$).**

Capability: min=70, max=92
- M1: $(92-70)/(92-70) = 1.00$
- M2: $(85-70)/22 = 0.682$
- M3: $(70-70)/22 = 0.00$

Cost (lower is better, so invert): min=0.5, max=30
- M1: $(0.5 - 30)/(0.5 - 30) = 1.00$ (worst) → wait, lower cost is better:
  - norm(cost) where lower is better: $(max - x)/(max - min)$
  - M1: $(30-30)/(30-0.5) = 0.00$
  - M2: $(30-8)/29.5 = 0.746$
  - M3: $(30-0.5)/29.5 = 1.00$

Latency (lower is better): min=0.8, max=4.5
- M1: $(4.5-4.5)/(4.5-0.8) = 0.00$
- M2: $(4.5-2.0)/3.7 = 0.676$
- M3: $(4.5-0.8)/3.7 = 1.00$

**Step 2: Apply weights and sum.**

$$
S_1 = 0.5(1.00) + 0.3(0.00) + 0.2(0.00) = 0.500
$$

$$
S_2 = 0.5(0.682) + 0.3(0.746) + 0.2(0.676) = 0.341 + 0.224 + 0.135 = 0.700
$$

$$
S_3 = 0.5(0.00) + 0.3(1.00) + 0.2(1.00) = 0.500
$$

**Step 3: Select max.**

$$
S_2 = 0.700 > S_1 = 0.500 = S_3
$$

**Final Selection:** $\boxed{\text{M2 (Llama-70B) is the optimal choice under these weights.}}$

> **Insight:** M1 is *capability-dominant* but cost-prohibitive; M3 is *cheap* but capability-inadequate. M2 strikes the right balance — exactly why open-source 70B models have become the **production sweet spot** in 2024–2025.

---

## 3.6 Selection Pitfall: Single-Criterion Optimization

A common student mistake in KTU answers is to *only* mention accuracy/benchmark scores. The WSM derivation above shows **why cost and latency must be co-optimized** — a 100% accuracy model is useless if it bankrupts the project or breaks the latency SLA.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4 | LLM Selection — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The LLM Selection Workflow (Top-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Use-Case and Domain] --> B[Define Functional and Non-Functional Requirements]
    B --> C[Shortlist Candidate LLMs]
    C --> D[Run Capability Benchmarks]
    C --> E[Profile Cost and Latency]
    C --> F[Audit License and Compliance]
    D --> G[Compute Weighted Selection Score]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Pilot Deployment}
    H -->|Pass| I[Production Selection Confirmed]
    H -->|Fail| J[Iterate: Tune Weights or Re-Shortlist]
    J --> C
```

---

## 4.2 Selection Criteria Tree (Hierarchical Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[LLM Selection Criteria] --> Cap[Capability]
    Root --> Cost[Cost]
    Root --> Lat[Latency]
    Root --> Ctx[Context Window]
    Root --> Op[Operational]
    Root --> Safe[Safety and Alignment]

    Cap --> B1[Benchmark Scores MMLU HumanEval GSM8K]
    Cap --> B2[Task Specific Accuracy]
    Cap --> B3[Reasoning and Hallucination Rate]

    Cost --> C1[API Cost USD per 1M tokens]
    Cost --> C2[Self Hosting GPU hourly rate]
    Cost --> C3[Fine Tuning One Time Cost]

    Lat --> L1[Time to First Token TTFT]
    Lat --> L2[Time per Output Token TPOT]
    Lat --> L3[End to End Latency]

    Ctx --> X1[Max Input Length 4K to 1M]
    Ctx --> X2[Effective Recall on Long Context]

    Op --> O1[License Type Apache MIT RAIL]
    Op --> O2[Data Privacy On Prem vs API]
    Op --> O3[Vendor Lock In Risk]

    Safe --> S1[Toxicity and Bias Score]
    Safe --> S2[Jailbreak Resistance]
    Safe --> S3[Truthfulness Hallucination Rate]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Open-Source vs Proprietary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is data privacy critical?} -->|Yes| OS[Prefer Open Source Self Host]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is cost per request very low?}

    Q2 -->|Yes Below 100K req per month| PROP[Prefer Proprietary API]
    Q2 -->|No Above 1M req per month| OS

    OS --> Q3{Need for fine tuning?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| OS2[Choose Llama Mistral Qwen family]
    Q3 -->|No| OS3[Choose ready instruction tuned variants]

    PROP --> Q4{Need latest SOTA quality?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P1[GPT-4o Claude Opus Gemini Pro]
    Q4 -->|No| P2[Use older or smaller API tier]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: LLM Selection Pipeline in an MLOps Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ingestion
        UC[Use Case Spec] --> REQ[Requirements Doc]
    end

    subgraph Catalog
        CAT[Model Registry: Open + Proprietary] --> META[Metadata: Params, License, Context, Cost]
    end

    subgraph Evaluation
        REQ --> BENCH[Benchmark Harness]
        META --> BENCH
        BENCH --> SCORE[Capability Score Vector]
    end

    subgraph Profiling
        BENCH --> PROF[Latency and Cost Profiler]
        PROF --> PROFV[Cost Latency Vector]
    end

    subgraph Decision
        SCORE --> WSM[Weighted Sum Model]
        PROFV --> WSM
        WSM --> RANK[Ranked Candidate List]
    end

    subgraph Deployment
        RANK --> PILOT[Pilot A B Test]
        PILOT --> PROD[Production Cutover]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Trade-Off Visualization (Capability vs Cost Frontier)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Capability High Cost] -->|Pick when budget unlimited| A1[GPT-4o Claude Opus]
    B[Medium Capability Medium Cost] -->|Pick for production sweet spot| B1[Llama-70B Qwen-72B Mixtral 8x22B]
    C[Low Capability Low Cost] -->|Pick for high volume simple tasks| C1[Mistral-7B Phi-3 Mini Gemma-2B]
    D[High Capability Low Cost] -->|Ideal but rare| D1[Open weights SOTA e.g. Llama-3-405B self hosted]
    E[Low Capability High Cost] -->|Avoid| E1[Legacy or overpriced APIs]
```
