<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 4 | LLM Selection — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**Define LLM Selection. List any four key criteria used to select an LLM for a production application.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
LLM Selection is the systematic engineering process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing the most appropriate Large Language Model for a given task, balancing capability, cost, latency, and operational constraints.

**Four Key Criteria:**

1. **Capability / Benchmark Performance** — Measured using MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, and task-specific accuracy on a held-out gold set.
2. **Cost** — Encompasses API pricing ($\$/1M$ tokens), self-hosting GPU cost, and fine-tuning overhead.
3. **Latency** — TTFT and TPOT must satisfy real-time user expectations; critical for chatbots and voice agents.
4. **Context Window** — Must be sufficient for the longest expected input (documents, code repos, conversation history).

> *Examiner's note: Naming only benchmarks is incomplete — cost, latency, and context are mandatory. Marks lost when students focus solely on accuracy.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**Compare open-source and proprietary LLMs in the context of model selection. Give two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Source (e.g., Llama-3, Mistral) | Proprietary (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Privacy** | On-premise deployment; no data leaves infrastructure | Data sent to vendor; subject to their ToS |
| **Cost at Scale** | Fixed GPU cost; cheaper at high volume | Per-token pricing; expensive at scale |
| **Customization** | Full fine-tuning and weight access allowed | Limited to prompting or vendor-provided fine-tuning |
| **State-of-the-Art** | Lagging by 3–6 months typically | Often the SOTA leader on public benchmarks |
| **Operational Burden** | Requires MLOps team, GPU management | Zero infra; vendor handles everything |

**Two advantages of open-source:** (1) data sovereignty and privacy, (2) long-term cost efficiency at scale.

**Two advantages of proprietary:** (1) best-in-class benchmark performance, (2) zero infrastructure overhead.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU pattern: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]:** Describe in detail the **six major criteria** for selecting an LLM for a real-world application. For each criterion, explain why it matters with one engineering example.

**Model Answer:**

The six major criteria are:

1. **Capability (Quality)** — Evaluated via standardized benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) and task-specific accuracy. *Why it matters:* A customer-facing legal assistant that hallucinates case law can cause professional liability. *Example:* Selecting GPT-4 over Mistral-7B for a contract-review tool because of higher reasoning accuracy on CUAD (Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset).

2. **Cost (Economics)** — Includes API cost ($\$/1M$ tokens), self-hosting GPU expense, and one-time fine-tuning. *Why it matters:* A miscalculation can cause 10–100x budget overrun. *Example:* A startup running 50M requests/month on GPT-4 spends $\sim$\$50K/month; switching to a fine-tuned Llama-3-8B on a single A100 reduces cost to $\sim$\$2K/month.

3. **Latency (Real-Time Performance)** — Decomposed into TTFT and TPOT. *Why it matters:* Streaming UIs require sub-2s response. *Example:* A voice assistant using a 400B model would have TTFT > 1s and TPOT > 100ms, violating the natural conversation rhythm. A 7B quantized model gives TTFT < 200ms.

4. **Context Window** — Maximum input length. *Why it matters:* Document QA, code analysis, and long dialogues need 32K–1M token context. *Example:* Legal document analysis needs $\geq 100K$ context; a 4K-context model would truncate the case file.

5. **Operational Fit** — License, deployment topology, vendor lock-in, regulatory compliance. *Why it matters:* A healthcare app in the EU cannot use a US-hosted API without GDPR-compliant data processing agreements. *Example:* A hospital self-hosts Llama-3 on a HIPAA-compliant on-prem cluster rather than calling OpenAI's API.

6. **Safety & Alignment** — Hallucination rate, toxicity, bias, jailbreak resistance. *Why it matters:* A misaligned model in a mental-health chatbot can produce harmful advice. *Example:* Using a model with high TruthfulQA score and low HRAM (Harmful Refusal Avoidance Metric) is essential for sensitive applications.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]:** An e-commerce company wants to deploy an LLM for product description generation. They evaluate three models with the following normalized scores:

| Criterion | Weight | Model A | Model B | Model C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capability | 0.4 | 0.90 | 0.70 | 0.60 |
| Cost | 0.3 | 0.50 | 0.85 | 0.95 |
| Latency | 0.2 | 0.60 | 0.80 | 0.95 |
| Safety | 0.1 | 0.85 | 0.75 | 0.65 |

Apply the **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)** to determine the best model. Show all calculations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply the WSM formula $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot x_{ij}$.**

For **Model A:**
$$
S_A = (0.4 \cdot 0.90) + (0.3 \cdot 0.50) + (0.2 \cdot 0.60) + (0.1 \cdot 0.85)
$$
$$
S_A = 0.360 + 0.150 + 0.120 + 0.085 = 0.715
$$

For **Model B:**
$$
S_B = (0.4 \cdot 0.70) + (0.3 \cdot 0.85) + (0.2 \cdot 0.80) + (0.1 \cdot 0.75)
$$
$$
S_B = 0.280 + 0.255 + 0.160 + 0.075 = 0.770
$$

For **Model C:**
$$
S_C = (0.4 \cdot 0.60) + (0.3 \cdot 0.95) + (0.2 \cdot 0.95) + (0.1 \cdot 0.65)
$$
$$
S_C = 0.240 + 0.285 + 0.190 + 0.065 = 0.780
$$

**Step 2: Rank by composite score.**

$$
S_C = 0.780 > S_B = 0.770 > S_A = 0.715
$$

**Step 3: Decision.**

$\boxed{\text{Model C is the optimal choice for product description generation.}}$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although Model A has the highest capability (0.90), its cost and latency penalties outweigh that advantage. Model C wins because product description generation is a relatively *easy task* where cost-efficiency dominates — a classic case where **bigger is not better**.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **eight-step LLM Selection Pipeline** in detail. For each step, state the output artifact that is produced.

**Model Answer:**

| Step | Name | Description | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Use-Case Identification** | Define the business problem (chatbot, summarizer, code-gen) and target users. | Use-Case Specification Document |
| 2 | **Requirements Definition** | Specify accuracy, latency, cost, and compliance targets. | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) sheet |
| 3 | **Shortlisting Candidates** | Filter 100+ models down to 5–10 viable candidates based on license, language, and modality. | Shortlist table (5–10 models) |
| 4 | **Benchmarking** | Run standardized benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) + task-specific eval on a held-out gold set. | Capability score report |
| 5 | **Cost & Latency Profiling** | Measure API cost, self-hosting cost, TTFT, TPOT under realistic load. | Profiling report (USD/month, ms) |
| 6 | **Human Evaluation** | Domain experts rate outputs on relevance, factuality, and tone. | Human eval rubric scores |
| 7 | **Pilot Deployment** | A/B test top 2–3 candidates with real users at 5–10% traffic. | Pilot metrics dashboard |
| 8 | **Final Selection** | Choose winner based on weighted composite score + pilot results. | Selection Decision Memo |

> **Key Insight:** Steps 4 and 5 are the *quantitative heart* of the pipeline; steps 6 and 7 are the *qualitative heart*. Skipping pilot deployment (step 7) is the #1 cause of post-deployment failures.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]:** A startup's chatbot receives **200,000 requests/day**. The selected LLM has:
- Input: 500 tokens average, priced at **\$3.00 per 1M tokens**
- Output: 150 tokens average, priced at **\$12.00 per 1M tokens**

Calculate the **monthly cost** and suggest **two strategies** to reduce it by at least 50%.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Per-request cost.**

$$
C_{req} = \left( \dfrac{500}{10^6} \right) \cdot 3.00 + \left( \dfrac{150}{10^6} \right) \cdot 12.00
$$

$$
C_{req} = 0.0015 + 0.0018 = \$0.0033 \text{ per request}
$$

**Step 2: Monthly cost (D = 30 days).**

$$
C_{monthly} = 200{,}000 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.0033
$$

$$
C_{monthly} = 6{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.0033 = \$19{,}800 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 3: Two reduction strategies targeting ≥ 50% saving (i.e., monthly ≤ \$9,900).**

**Strategy 1: Prompt compression + caching.**
Reduce input tokens from 500 → 250 (50% reduction) by summarizing system prompts and using semantic caching for repeated queries.

New cost per request:
$$
C'_{req} = \left( \dfrac{250}{10^6} \right) \cdot 3.00 + \left( \dfrac{150}{10^6} \right) \cdot 12.00
$$
$$
C'_{req} = 0.00075 + 0.0018 = \$0.00255
$$
New monthly cost:
$$
C'_{monthly} = 6{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.00255 = \$15{,}300
$$
**Savings:** $19{,}800 - 15{,}300 = \$4{,}500$ (~22.7%).

**Strategy 2: Route simple queries to a smaller, cheaper model (e.g., Mistral-7B self-hosted).**
Assume 60% of queries are simple (routed to 7B at \$0.50/M input + \$1.50/M output) and 40% are complex (still use the original model).

Cost for 60% (simple) traffic:
$$
C_{simple} = 0.6 \cdot 6{,}000{,}000 \cdot \left( \dfrac{500}{10^6} \cdot 0.50 + \dfrac{150}{10^6} \cdot 1.50 \right)
$$
$$
C_{simple} = 3{,}600{,}000 \cdot (0.00025 + 0.000225) = 3{,}600{,}000 \cdot 0.000475 = \$1{,}710
$$

Cost for 40% (complex) traffic (unchanged):
$$
C_{complex} = 0.4 \cdot 19{,}800 = \$7{,}920
$$

Total:
$$
C_{total} = 1{,}710 + 7{,}920 = \$9{,}630
$$

**Combined Savings:** $19{,}800 - 9{,}630 = \$10{,}170$ ($\approx 51.4\%$).

$\boxed{\text{Final monthly cost: } \$9{,}630 \text{ (a 51.4\% reduction, meeting the ≥50\% target)}.}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $10^6$ divisor** when applying per-million-token pricing. Always normalize token counts to millions before multiplying.
> 2. **Ignoring input vs output token asymmetry** — output tokens are typically 3–5x more expensive. A 50% input reduction is not a 50% cost reduction.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** — "Assume 60% of queries are simple" must be explicitly written, or the routing strategy is unverifiable.
> 4. **Confusing weighted sum normalization direction** — for "lower-is-better" criteria (cost, latency), you must invert the normalization: $(max - x)/(max - min)$.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Definition:** LLM Selection = systematic multi-criteria decision to choose the best LLM for a task.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Capability + Cost + Constraint (operational). All three must be addressed.
- ✅ **Six Criteria:** Capability, Cost, Latency, Context Window, Operational Fit, Safety & Alignment.
- ✅ **Eight-Step Pipeline:** Use-Case → Requirements → Shortlist → Benchmark → Profile → Human Eval → Pilot → Final Select.
- ✅ **Open vs Proprietary trade-off:** Privacy + cost vs SOTA quality + zero infra.
- ✅ **Top Benchmarks to Memorize:** MMLU (knowledge), HumanEval (code), GSM8K (math), MT-Bench (dialogue), TruthfulQA (truth).
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - Cost: $C_{req} = (T_{in}/10^6) C_{in} + (T_{out}/10^6) C_{out}$
  - Latency: $L_{e2e} = T_{TTFT} + N_{out} \cdot T_{TPOT}$
  - Memory: $M_{GPU} \approx 2P$ bytes (FP16, B=1)
  - WSM: $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot \text{norm}(x_{ij})$
- ✅ **Common 14-Mark Question Patterns:**
  - *Describe the LLM selection criteria* → enumerate the six, give one example each.
  - *Compare X vs Y* → tabular comparison + one-line verdict.
  - *Numerical cost/latency calculation* → show unit normalization, step-by-step arithmetic, final answer in a box.
  - *Apply WSM to given scores* → compute $S_i$ for each candidate, rank, conclude.
  - *Suggest cost-reduction strategy* → identify high-cost component + propose 2 engineering levers + recompute.
- ✅ **Watch Words for Answers:** "trade-off", "domain-specific", "production sweet spot", "pilot deployment", "compliance", "vendor lock-in".
- ✅ **Avoid:** "Bigger model = always better" — this is a guaranteed mark-deduction phrase in KTU 2024.
- ✅ **Production Sweet Spot (2024–2025):** Open-source 7B–70B models fine-tuned with LoRA/QLoRA are the *default* answer for most cost-sensitive production scenarios.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tooling for Application Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tooling for Application Development # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tooling for Application Development** in the context of Large Language Models (LLMs) refers to the curated ecosystem of **software frameworks, libraries, SDKs, orchestration engines, vector databases, and deployment platforms** that abstract low-level model mechanics (tokenization, inference, GPU memory management, embedding generation) and expose them as **composable, production-grade primitives** for building LLM-powered applications. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this sits at the intersection of: - **Model Layer** (Hugging Face Transformers, Ollama, vLLM) - **Orchestration Layer** (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack) - **Data Layer** (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, FAISS) - **Interface Layer** (Gradio, Streamlit, Chainlit) - **Evaluation/Observability Layer** (LangSmith, Weights & Biases, MLflow) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy — The "Smart Chef's Kitchen"** > > Imagine you want to cook a gourmet meal (an LLM application). The **LLM** is your *master chef* (very powerful, but only speaks one language — prompts). You cannot ask the chef to do grocery shopping, manage inventory, take reservations, or design the menu. That is why you need: > > - **Knives & Utensils** (Hugging Face, Tokenizers) → prepares raw ingredients (text → tokens) > - **The Recipe Book** (Prompt Templates, LangChain) → standardized cooking instructions > - **The Pantry** (Vector DB — Pinecone/Chroma) → remembers past ingredients/context > - **The Waiter** (API layer — FastAPI/Flask) → takes orders from customers > - **The Dining Room** (Gradio/Streamlit UI) → where customers interact > - **The Head Manager** (Orchestrator — LangChain/LlamaIndex) → coordinates everything > > **Tooling** = the entire kitchen infrastructure that turns a raw chef (LLM) into a functioning restaurant (production application). ## 1.3 Why Tooling is Non-Negotiable in 2024-Scheme LLMs | Problem Without Tooling | Solution Provided by Tooling | |---|---| | Manual tokenization & prompt crafting | `transformers.AutoTokenizer`, `langchain.PromptTemplate` | | Stateless LLM has no memory | Conversation buffer, vector retrieval, summarization memory | | Hallucination on private data | **RAG** (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) via vector DB | | Slow inference on large models | **vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM** for high-throughput serving | | No UI for non-technical users | **Gradio, Streamlit, Chainlit** | | Cannot call external APIs from LLM | **Function calling / Tools / Agents** | ## 1.4 Core Categorization of Tools > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies LLM tooling into **five functional pillars**: > > 1. **Model Serving & Inference** — vLLM, Ollama, HuggingFace TGI > 2. **Orchestration & Chaining** — LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack > 3. **Retrieval & Memory** — FAISS, Chroma, Pinecone, Weaviate > 4. **Interface & UX** — Gradio, Streamlit, Chainlit > 5. **Evaluation & Observability** — LangSmith, RAGAS, TruLens # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Layer LLM Application Stack ### Layer 1 — Model Serving & Inference Engine **Purpose:** Load weights, tokenize input, run forward passes on GPU, return generations with optimal memory and latency. | Tool | Creator | Key Feature | |---|---|---| | **Hugging Face Transformers** | Hugging Face | Standard `pipeline()` API for 200k+ models | | **vLLM** | UC Berkeley | PagedAttention → **24× higher throughput** | | **Ollama** | Ollama | Local CLI for running Llama, Mistral, Phi on laptop | | **llama.cpp** | Georgi Gerganov | Quantized (4-bit/8-bit) CPU inference | | **TGI** (Text Generation Inference) | Hugging Face | Production-grade serving with Rust | | **TensorRT-LLM** | NVIDIA | Hardware-accelerated NVIDIA GPU inference | > [!NOTE] > **PagedAttention (vLLM Innovation):** Borrowed from OS virtual memory paging. Allocates KV-cache in **non-contiguous fixed-size pages (typically 16 tokens each)** to eliminate fragmentation. This is the mathematical heart of modern serving — the formula for memory savings: > > $$\text{Memory}_{\text{saved}} = \frac{\text{KV}_{\text{blockSize}} \cdot n_{\text{layers}} \cdot n_{\text{heads}} \cdot d_{\text{head}} \cdot 2 \cdot \text{precisionBytes}}{\text{FragmentationFactor}}$$ ### Layer 2 — Orchestration & Chaining **Purpose:** Compose multi-step LLM workflows, manage state, enable agents. **LangChain Core Abstractions (must-memorize for KTU):** | Abstraction | Role | |---|---| | `LLM` / `ChatModel` | Wraps the underlying model (OpenAI, Anthropic, local) | | `PromptTemplate` | Parameterized prompt strings | | `Chain` | Sequence of calls: `prompt → llm → output_parser` | | `Agent` | LLM-driven decision loop using tools | | `Tool` | Callable function exposed to the agent | | `Memory` | Persists conversation across turns | | `Retriever` | Interface to vector store for RAG | | `DocumentLoader` | Ingests PDFs, web pages, databases | | `TextSplitter` | Chunks documents for embedding | **LlamaIndex vs LangChain — KTU High-Yield Distinction:** | Aspect | LangChain | LlamaIndex | |---|---|---| | Primary Focus | General-purpose LLM orchestration | **Data-centric / RAG-first** | | Strength | Agents, tool-use, complex chains | Indexing, querying structured data | | Data Connectors | ~200+ | ~300+ | | Query Engine | Manual via retrievers | Native `query_engine.query()` | ### Layer 3 — Retrieval & Vector Databases **Vector embedding mathematics (essential for KTU derivations):** Given a text chunk $c$, an embedding model $f$ produces a dense vector: $$\vec{c} = f(c) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$ Typical dimensions: $d \in \{384, 768, 1024, 1536, 3072\}$ **Similarity Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet:** $$\text{Cosine Similarity} = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert_{2} \, \Vert\vec{B}\Vert_{2}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} A_i B_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} A_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} B_i^2}}$$ $$\text{Euclidean Distance} = d(\vec{A}, \vec{B}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} (A_i - B_i)^2}$$ $$\text{Dot Product} = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} A_i B_i$$ $$\text{BM25 (Sparse Retrieval)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \frac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert D \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}$$ Where $k_1 \approx 1.2$ and $b \approx 0.75$ are tunable constants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Cosine similarity ranges in $[-1, 1]$, but when using **normalized embeddings** (unit vectors), it is equivalent to **dot product** and ranges in $[0, 1]$ for non-negative embeddings. Examiners often test this! **Vector DB Comparison Table:** | Database | Index Type | Hosting | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | **FAISS** | IVF, HNSW, PQ | In-memory | Research, single-node | | **Chroma** | HNSW | Local / cloud | Prototyping | | **Pinecone** | Proprietary | Managed cloud | Production scale | | **Weaviate** | HNSW | Self/managed | Hybrid sparse+dense | | **Milvus** | IVF, HNSW, DiskANN | Self/managed | Billion-scale | | **Qdrant** | HNSW | Self/managed | Rust-based, fast | ### Layer 4 — Interface & UX Layer | Tool | Use Case | Rendering | |---|---|---| | **Gradio** | Quick demos, model playgrounds | Auto-generated widgets | | **Streamlit** | Data dashboards, internal tools | Reactive scripts | | **Chainlit** | Chatbot-first LLM apps | Native chat UI | | **Next.js + Vercel AI SDK** | Production chat SaaS | React components | | **FastAPI + WebSockets** | Streaming + custom frontends | Low-level control | ### Layer 5 — Evaluation & Observability > [!NOTE] > **RAGAS Metrics** (high-yield for KTU): > > $$\text{Faithfulness} = \frac{\vert \text{claims supported by context} \vert}{\vert \text{total claims} \vert}$$ > > $$\text{Answer Relevancy} = 1 - \frac{\text{distances between Q and generated A}}{\text{max distance}}$$ > > $$\text{Context Precision} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (\text{Precision@k} \cdot v_k)}{\text{total relevant items}}$$ > > $$\text{Context Recall} = \frac{\vert \text{relevant items retrieved} \vert}{\vert \text{relevant items in ground truth} \vert}$$ ## 2.2 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Fields - **Healthcare:** RAG over PubMed embeddings → evidence-grounded diagnostic assistant - **Legal:** LangChain agents over case-law vector stores - **Finance:** Function-calling agents for real-time stock-API invocation - **Education:** Gradio tutors backed by RAG over textbook corpora - **Customer Support:** Multi-agent systems (router → specialist → summarizer) # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cosine Similarity from First Principles **Problem Setup:** Given two embedding vectors $\vec{A} = (1, 2, 3)$ and $\vec{B} = (4, 5, 6)$ produced by an embedding model of dimension $d=3$, compute their cosine similarity and explain its role in RAG retrieval. **Step 1 — Dot Product (Numerator)** $$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} A_i B_i = (1)(4) + (2)(5) + (3)(6) = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32$$ **Step 2 — Magnitude of $\vec{A}$ (First term in denominator)** $$\Vert\vec{A}\Vert_{2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{3} A_i^2} = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14}$$ **Step 3 — Magnitude of $\vec{B}$ (Second term in denominator)** $$\Vert\vec{B}\Vert_{2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{3} B_i^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 5^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{16 + 25 + 36} = \sqrt{77}$$ **Step 4 — Combine via Cosine Formula** $$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert_{2} \cdot \Vert\vec{B}\Vert_{2}} = \frac{32}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{77}} = \frac{32}{\sqrt{1078}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation** $$\sqrt{1078} \approx 32.833 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos(\theta) \approx \frac{32}{32.833} \approx 0.9747$$ **Interpretation:** Since $\cos(\theta) \approx 0.975$ is very close to **1.0**, the two vectors point in nearly identical directions → the two text chunks are **semantically highly similar**. In RAG, this chunk would be retrieved for the corresponding query. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Recap:** > - Step 1 multiplied components → dot product > - Step 2/3 used L2 norm → vector magnitudes > - Step 4 unified via the cosine ratio → bounded in $[-1, 1]$ > - Step 5 mapped similarity to semantic meaning --- ## 3.2 Derivation: RAG End-to-End Retrieval Cost **Problem Setup:** You are building a RAG system for a PDF corpus. Compute the total inference cost per query. **Given Parameters:** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Avg chunks retrieved ($k$) | $k$ | **5** | | Avg chunk tokens | $t_c$ | **200** | | Avg query tokens | $t_q$ | **30** | | Prompt overhead tokens | $t_p$ | **150** | | Output max tokens | $t_o$ | **300** | | Input cost per 1M tokens | $C_{\text{in}}$ | **\$2.50** | | Output cost per 1M tokens | $C_{\text{out}}$ | **\$10.00** | **Step 1 — Total Input Tokens** $$T_{\text{in}} = t_q + k \cdot t_c + t_p = 30 + (5)(200) + 150 = 30 + 1000 + 150 = 1180$$ **Step 2 — Total Output Tokens** $$T_{\text{out}} = t_o = 300$$ **Step 3 — Input Cost (USD)** $$\text{Cost}_{\text{in}} = \frac{T_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{in}}}{10^6} = \frac{1180 \cdot 2.50}{1{,}000{,}000} = \$0.00295$$ **Step 4 — Output Cost (USD)** $$\text{Cost}_{\text{out}} = \frac{T_{\text{out}} \cdot C_{\text{out}}}{10^6} = \frac{300 \cdot 10.00}{1{,}000{,}000} = \$0.00300$$ **Step 5 — Total Cost Per Query** $$\text{Cost}_{\text{total}} = \text{Cost}_{\text{in}} + \text{Cost}_{\text{out}} = 0.00295 + 0.00300 = \$0.00595$$ **Step 6 — Monthly Cost at 100k Queries/Day** $$\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = 0.00595 \cdot 100{,}000 \cdot 30 = \$17{,}850$$ **Interpretation:** Output tokens dominate when $t_o$ is large. **Optimization levers:** reduce $k$ (re-rank), use cheaper models for re-ranking, or compress retrieved chunks. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Vector Quantization Memory Savings **Problem Setup:** FAISS uses Product Quantization (PQ) to compress embeddings. Compute memory savings. **Given:** $d = 1536$ (embedding dim), $m = 48$ sub-quantizers, original = **float32 (4 bytes)**, PQ codes = **1 byte each**. **Step 1 — Original Memory Per Vector** $$\text{Mem}_{\text{orig}} = d \cdot 4 = 1536 \cdot 4 = 6144 \text{ bytes} = 6.0 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 2 — PQ Memory Per Vector** $$\text{Mem}_{\text{PQ}} = m \cdot 1 = 48 \cdot 1 = 48 \text{ bytes} = 0.0469 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 3 — Compression Ratio** $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{Mem}_{\text{orig}}}{\text{Mem}_{\text{PQ}}} = \frac{6144}{48} = 128\times$$ **Step 4 — For a Corpus of $N = 10$ million vectors** $$\text{Mem}_{\text{saved}} = N \cdot (\text{Mem}_{\text{orig}} - \text{Mem}_{\text{PQ}}) = 10^7 \cdot (6144 - 48) = 60.96 \text{ GB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off:** PQ introduces **approximation error** in distance computation — typically 5–10% recall loss. Use **OPQ (Optimized PQ)** or **IVF-PQ** for better quality. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Tooling Stack to Use? | Application Scenario | Recommended Stack | Justification | |---|---|---| | Personal chatbot on laptop | Ollama + LangChain + Chroma | Zero-setup, local, free | | Enterprise document Q&A | vLLM + LangChain + Pinecone | Production-grade serving, scalable retrieval | | Real-time web agent | LangChain Agents + Tavily + OpenAI | Tool-use, function calling | | Multimodal R&D demo | Gradio + HuggingFace + FAISS | Fastest prototype-to-UI path | | Billion-scale search | Milvus + Haystack + TGI | Distributed, GPU-accelerated | | Regulated / on-prem | llama.cpp + Weaviate + LangChain | Air-gapped, full control | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Five-Layer LLM Application Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L5["Layer 5: Observability and Evaluation"] L5a["LangSmith"] L5b["RAGAS"] L5c["Weights and Biases"] end subgraph L4["Layer 4: Interface and UX"] L4a["Gradio"] L4b["Streamlit"] L4c["Chainlit"] end subgraph L3["Layer 3: Retrieval and Memory"] L3a["FAISS"] L3b["Chroma"] L3c["Pinecone"] L3d["Weaviate"] end subgraph L2["Layer 2: Orchestration and Chaining"] L2a["LangChain"] L2b["LlamaIndex"] L2c["Haystack"] L2d["DSPy"] end subgraph L1["Layer 1: Model Serving and Inference"] L1a["HuggingFace Transformers"] L1b["vLLM with PagedAttention"] L1c["Ollama Local Runner"] L1d["TGI and TensorRTLLM"] end L4 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L2 --> L3 L5 -.monitors.-> L2 L5 -.monitors.-> L1 L5 -.monitors.-> L3 L4 -.user queries.-> L5 ``` --- ## 4.2 RAG Pipeline — End-to-End Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["User Query"] --> B["Query Embedder
SentenceTransformer"] B --> C["Vector Search
Cosine Similarity"] C --> D{"Top K Chunks
Retrieved"} D --> E["Re-Ranker
Optional Cross Encoder"] E --> F["Prompt Constructor
Context + Question"] F --> G["LLM Inference
vLLM or Ollama"] G --> H["Post Processor
Streaming Parser"] H --> I["Final Answer
Gradio UI"] subgraph OFFLINE["Offline Indexing Pipeline"] J["PDF or Web Source"] --> K["Document Loader"] K --> L["Text Splitter
Chunk Size 500"] L --> M["Embedding Model"] M --> N["Vector Database
Chroma or FAISS"] end N -.retrieval.-> C ``` --- ## 4.3 LangChain Agent Loop — Function Calling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["START: User Goal"] --> S1["LLM Receives Goal
Plus Tool Schemas"] S1 --> S2{"LLM Decides:
Reason or Act?"} S2 -->|"Reason"| S3["Thought:
I need to call search API"] S2 -->|"Final Answer"| S4["Return Answer to User"] S3 --> S5["Action:
Call Tool search_documents"] S5 --> S6["Tool Executes
Returns Observation"] S6 --> S7["Observation Appended
to Agent Scratchpad"] S7 --> S1 S4 --> S9["END"] S1 -.uses.-> S8["Available Tools:
search web search docs
calculator python repl
sql db query"] S5 -.invokes.-> S8 ``` --- ## 4.4 Prompt Chaining Pattern (Sequential Orchestration) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Step 1:
Outline Generator"] --> P2["Step 2:
Section Expander"] P2 --> P3["Step 3:
Critic Agent"] P3 --> P4{"Passes
Quality Bar?"} P4 -->|"Yes"| P5["Final Output"] P4 -->|"No"| P2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This loop is **exactly** the LangChain `LLMRouterChain` + `TransformChain` pattern. KTU frequently asks students to draw or describe this iterative refinement loop. --- ## 4.5 Token Budget Allocation Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB TB1["Total Context Window
128k tokens for GPT-4 Turbo"] --> TB2["System Prompt
500 tokens"] TB2 --> TB3["Retrieved Context
8k tokens"] TB3 --> TB4["Chat History
Compressed to 4k tokens"] TB4 --> TB5["User Query
200 tokens"] TB5 --> TB6["Reserved for Output
Remaining to 128k"] ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** **Differentiate between LangChain and LlamaIndex. Which is more suitable for a RAG-heavy enterprise document search application and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | LangChain | LlamaIndex | |---|---|---| | Design Focus | General-purpose LLM orchestration, agents, chains | Data ingestion, indexing, query engines | | RAG Support | Requires manual retriever + chain wiring | Native `VectorStoreIndex`, `query_engine` | | Agent Support | First-class via `create_react_agent` | Limited | | Best For | Multi-tool agents, complex chains | RAG over private documents | **For RAG-heavy enterprise document search → LlamaIndex is more suitable** because it provides out-of-the-box `VectorStoreIndex`, `SummaryIndex`, `KeywordTableIndex`, and advanced features like **recursive retrieval** and **document agents** — reducing boilerplate code significantly compared to manually assembling retriever → prompt → chain in LangChain. *(3 marks for clear differentiation + justified choice)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Adapted)]** **Explain the concept of PagedAttention as implemented in vLLM. Why is it critical for high-throughput LLM serving?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **PagedAttention** is a memory management technique in **vLLM** that treats the **KV (Key-Value) cache** like an operating system manages virtual memory using paging. - Instead of allocating one contiguous KV-cache block per sequence (which causes **fragmentation** and **memory waste of up to 60–80%**), vLLM partitions the cache into **fixed-size blocks** (typically 16 tokens). - A **block table** maps logical sequence positions to physical GPU memory pages. - During attention computation, the kernel gathers scattered blocks on-the-fly using the block table. **Critical importance:** 1. **Reduces KV-cache fragmentation** → near-zero waste. 2. **Enables continuous batching** → multiple sequences share GPU memory dynamically. 3. **Yields up to 24× higher throughput** vs. naive HuggingFace serving. 4. **Supports parallel sampling, beam search, prefix-sharing** efficiently. *(1 mark each for definition, mechanism, and importance — total 3 marks)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each. Below are two complete alternative choices. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — RAG Pipeline Engineering** **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 4 Weightage]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** system. Explain the role of each component: document loader, text splitter, embedding model, vector database, retriever, re-ranker, LLM, and output parser. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Definition:** RAG = a pattern where external knowledge is retrieved from a vector store and injected into the LLM prompt to ground its responses and reduce hallucinations. **Component Roles:** | # | Component | Role | Example Tool | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Document Loader** | Ingests heterogeneous sources (PDF, HTML, SQL, Notion) | `PyPDFLoader`, `UnstructuredLoader` | | 2 | **Text Splitter** | Chunks long docs into passages (typically 200–500 tokens with 10–20% overlap) | `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` | | 3 | **Embedding Model** | Converts text chunks into dense vectors | `text-embedding-3-small`, `bge-large-en` | | 4 | **Vector Database** | Stores embeddings + metadata, supports ANN search | Chroma, FAISS, Pinecone | | 5 | **Retriever** | Queries the vector DB for top-$k$ similar chunks | `vectorstore.as_retriever(k=5)` | | 6 | **Re-ranker** (optional) | Re-scores retrieved chunks via cross-encoder for higher precision | `CohereRerank`, `bge-reranker` | | 7 | **LLM** | Generates final answer conditioned on retrieved context | GPT-4o, Llama-3, Mistral | | 8 | **Output Parser** | Validates and structures the LLM output | `PydanticOutputParser`, `JsonOutputParser` | **Block Diagram (drawn in answer sheet — simplified ASCII):** ``` [Docs] → [Loader] → [Splitter] → [Embedder] → [VectorDB] ↓ [User Query] → [Query Embedder] → [Similarity Search] → [Top-k Chunks] ↓ [Prompt Constructor] ↓ [LLM Call] ↓ [Output Parser] ↓ [Final Answer] ``` **Justification of design:** Splitting, embedding, and storing offline decouples retrieval latency from generation. Re-ranking adds a precision boost at minimal cost. Output parsers enforce schema correctness. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for component descriptions with examples)* --- **(b)** Consider a corpus of **10 million text passages**, each with an embedding of dimension **$d = 768$** stored as float32 in a vector database. Compute: *(i)* total memory required in **GB**, *(ii)* memory after applying **Product Quantization with $m = 96$ sub-quantizers (1 byte per code)**, *(iii)* the compression ratio, and *(iv)* comment on the recall-quality trade-off. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Original Memory** $$\text{Mem}_{\text{per vector}} = 768 \cdot 4 \text{ bytes} = 3072 \text{ bytes} \approx 3.0 \text{ KB}$$ $$\text{Mem}_{\text{total}} = 10^7 \cdot 3072 \text{ bytes} = 3.072 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} = 30.72 \text{ GB}$$ **(ii) Memory After Product Quantization** $$\text{Mem}_{\text{PQ per vector}} = 96 \cdot 1 \text{ byte} = 96 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.0938 \text{ KB}$$ $$\text{Mem}_{\text{PQ total}} = 10^7 \cdot 96 = 9.6 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} = 0.96 \text{ GB}$$ **(iii) Compression Ratio** $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{30.72}{0.96} = 32\times$$ **(iv) Recall-Quality Trade-off:** - **Gain:** 32× memory reduction → enables billion-scale search on a single node; faster distance computation via lookup tables (Asymmetric Distance Computation). - **Loss:** PQ introduces **approximation error**. Typical **recall@10 drops by 5–10%** vs. exact search. Mitigations: use **OPQ (Optimized PQ)** for rotation-aware quantization, or **IVF-PQ hybrid** to limit search space before quantization. For mission-critical retrieval (legal, medical), consider **Residual Quantization (RQ)** or **Scalar Quantization (SQ)** with int8 for higher fidelity. *(7 marks: 1+2+1+3 distribution across the four parts)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — LangChain Orchestration and Tool Use** **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 4 Weightage]** **(a)** Describe the **LangChain Agent architecture** with a neat diagram. Explain how the **ReAct (Reason + Act)** loop works, the role of the **agent scratchpad**, and how function-calling tools are integrated. Use the example of a "Web Research Agent" that can search the web and write Python code. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Definition:** A LangChain Agent is an LLM-driven loop that decides *which action* to take, *observes* the result, and iterates until it can produce a final answer. **ReAct Loop Steps:** 1. **Input:** User goal + list of available tool schemas (name, description, JSON parameters). 2. **Reasoning Step:** LLM emits a `Thought:` ("I need to find the latest AI news"). 3. **Action Step:** LLM emits `Action: web_search` with `Action Input: {"query": "latest AI news"}`. 4. **Observation:** Tool executes and returns output appended to **agent scratchpad**. 5. **Loop:** LLM sees updated scratchpad → next thought-action pair. 6. **Termination:** LLM emits `Final Answer:` → loop exits. **Role of Agent Scratchpad:** A **running history buffer** of all `(Thought, Action, Observation)` tuples. Critical because: - Provides **context** to LLM across multiple iterations. - Prevents **repetition** by exposing prior actions. - Bounded by `max_iterations` to avoid infinite loops. **Function-Calling Tool Integration (modern OpenAI/Anthropic API):** - Tools registered via `langchain.tools.Tool` or `@tool` decorator. - LLM receives a JSON schema; emits structured `tool_calls`. - LangChain's `AgentExecutor` dispatches to the appropriate Python function. - Example: `search_web(query)`, `python_repl(code)`, `wikipedia_lookup(topic)`. **ASCII Diagram:** ``` [User Goal] → [LLM with Tool Schemas] ↓ Thought + Action? ↓ [Agent Executor] ↓ [Tool Dispatch: web_search or python_repl] ↓ Observation ↓ [Scratchpad Append] ↓ (loop until Final Answer) ↓ [Final Output] ``` *(7 marks: 2 diagram + 5 mechanism description)* --- **(b)** Compare the following deployment setups in terms of **latency, cost, privacy, and scalability**: *(i)* OpenAI GPT-4o via API, *(ii)* self-hosted Llama-3-70B on vLLM with 4× A100 GPUs, *(iii)* Ollama on a MacBook Pro M3. Recommend the best option for each of these scenarios: *(A)* a college project demo, *(B)* a hospital processing patient records, *(C)* a customer-facing chatbot with 100k daily users. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Comparison Matrix:** | Dimension | GPT-4o API | Self-Hosted Llama-3-70B + vLLM | Ollama on MacBook | |---|---|---|---| | **Latency (TTFT)** | ~300–600 ms (network) | ~50–200 ms (local GPU) | ~800–2000 ms (CPU/Metal) | | **Cost / 1M tokens** | \$2.50 in / \$10 out | One-time GPU + power | Free after hardware | | **Privacy** | Data leaves premises | Full control | Full control | | **Scalability** | Auto, unlimited | Manual GPU provisioning | Single-machine only | | **Setup Effort** | Low (API key) | High (Docker, CUDA, vLLM) | Very low (`ollama run`) | **Recommendations:** **(A) College project demo → Ollama on MacBook Pro M3** - **Reason:** Zero cost, zero setup (`ollama run llama3`), sufficient for demos. Privacy and SLA not critical. **(B) Hospital processing patient records → Self-Hosted Llama-3-70B on vLLM (on-prem)** - **Reason:** HIPAA/GDPR compliance requires data to never leave hospital infrastructure. GPU investment is justified for continuous high-quality inference. Open-source weights allow fine-tuning on medical data. **(C) Customer-facing chatbot with 100k daily users → OpenAI GPT-4o via API (hybrid with self-hosted fallback)** - **Reason:** 100k users/day demands elastic scaling that only managed APIs provide. Auto-scaling, redundancy, and SLA guarantees outweigh the per-token cost. For cost-sensitive parts (intent classification, routing), route to a smaller self-hosted model in a hybrid pattern. *(7 marks: 2 for matrix + 1 each for the three scenario recommendations + 2 for justifications)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to justify the choice** in (b) scenarios — students often just name the option without explaining *why*, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing FAISS with Chroma** — FAISS is a *library* by Meta (no server); Chroma is a *database* with persistence. Examiners penalize this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the block diagram** in (a) questions — diagrams carry **2 marks minimum**; a textual-only answer is graded at most 5/7. > 4. **Wrong units in memory calculations** — always convert bytes → KB → MB → GB explicitly. > 5. **Writing `|A|` instead of $\Vert A \Vert$` for vector magnitude** in formula derivations — use LaTeX `\|` or `\Vert`. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ✅ Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Tooling is 5 layers:** Model Serving → Orchestration → Retrieval → Interface → Observability. - [ ] **LangChain** = general orchestration (agents, chains, tools); **LlamaIndex** = data-centric (RAG-first). - [ ] **vLLM's PagedAttention** → KV-cache paging → up to **24× throughput** boost. - [ ] **Hugging Face Transformers** is the standard library for **inference + training** of 200k+ models. - [ ] **Ollama** runs quantized local LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Phi) on consumer hardware. - [ ] **Vector DBs:** FAISS (in-memory), Chroma (local dev), Pinecone (managed cloud), Weaviate (hybrid), Milvus (billion-scale). - [ ] **Cosine similarity** ∈ $[-1, 1]$; for unit-normalized embeddings, it equals **dot product**. - [ ] **Product Quantization (PQ)** achieves **~32× compression** with 5–10% recall loss. - [ ] **RAG pipeline:** Loader → Splitter → Embedder → VectorDB → Retriever → Re-ranker → LLM → Parser. - [ ] **ReAct agent loop:** Thought → Action → Observation → Scratchpad → loop until Final Answer. - [ ] **Function calling** = structured JSON schema + LLM emits tool name + args. - [ ] **Gradio** for quick demos; **Streamlit** for dashboards; **Chainlit** for chatbots. - [ ] **RAGAS metrics:** Faithfulness, Answer Relevancy, Context Precision, Context Recall. - [ ] **Cost formula:** $\text{Cost} = \frac{T_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{out}} \cdot C_{\text{out}}}{10^6}$ - [ ] **Deployment trade-off triangle:** Cost ↔ Latency ↔ Privacy (you can optimize two, not all three). ### 🎯 High-Yield Definitions for KTU Viva 1. **PagedAttention** — Memory-efficient KV-cache management via paging (vLLM). 2. **RAG** — Retrieval-Augmented Generation; grounds LLM outputs in external documents. 3. **ReAct** — Reasoning + Acting agent paradigm. 4. **Vector Embedding** — Dense numerical representation of text in $\mathbb{R}^d$. 5. **Tool Calling** — Mechanism for LLMs to invoke external functions via JSON schema. 6. **Prompt Template** — Parameterized, reusable prompt string with input variables. 7. **LangSmith** — Observability platform for tracing LangChain runs. 8. **Token** — Smallest unit of text processed by an LLM (~0.75 words in English). ### ⚠️ Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid - Treating **LangChain and LlamaIndex as competitors** — they are *complementary* (you can use LlamaIndex as a retriever inside a LangChain chain). - Forgetting that **vLLM requires NVIDIA GPUs** — does not run on CPU or AMD. - Confusing **Gradio's `Interface`** (single function) with **`Blocks`** (custom layout). - Writing **temperature = 0** for creative tasks — temperature 0 = greedy decoding, used for factual/repeatable outputs.

topics.title: Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inference in Large Language Models (LLMs)** is the operational phase in which a *pre-trained* neural network is used to *generate output* (text, tokens, embeddings) for a *new, unseen input prompt*. Unlike training, which involves updating model parameters via backpropagation, inference is a **forward-only, read-only computation** that maps an input token sequence to a probability distribution over the next token, repeatedly, until a stopping condition is met.

Mathematically, given a trained LLM with parameters $\theta$ and an input prompt $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, inference produces an output sequence $\hat{y} = (\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \dots, \hat{y}_m)$ by sampling or selecting tokens from the conditional distribution:

$$\hat{y}_t \sim P_\theta(\cdot \mid x, \hat{y}_{<t})$$

The complete generation process is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \prod_{t=1}^{m} P_\theta(y_t \mid x, y_{<t})$$

## 1.2 The Two Major Phases of an LLM Lifecycle

| Phase | Purpose | Operations | Cost Profile |
|-------|---------|------------|--------------|
| **Training** | Learn parameters $\theta$ from corpus | Forward + Backward pass, gradient updates | Very High (GPU-weeks) |
| **Inference** | Apply $\theta$ to new prompts | Forward pass only, no gradient updates | Lower per-query, but **scales with users** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam vs. Study" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an LLM as a student who has spent **years studying** (training) every book in a library. When you sit the student down for an **exam** (inference), they do not re-read the books. They simply use what they have memorized to **answer questions one word at a time**, carefully choosing each next word based on the question and the words they have already spoken. The "study" (training) is expensive and slow; the "exam" (inference) is fast and repetitive, but happens millions of times per day across the world.

## 1.4 Why Inference is Harder Than It Looks

While inference removes the cost of backpropagation, three new engineering challenges emerge:

1. **Latency** — Users expect sub-second response time; a 70B parameter model naively takes seconds per token.
2. **Memory** — Storing the Key-Value (KV) cache grows linearly with sequence length and can exceed GPU RAM.
3. **Throughput** — A production system (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini) serves millions of concurrent users, demanding extreme parallelism.

The field of LLM **Inference Engineering** is dedicated to solving these three problems without degrading output quality.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the syllabus, *Inference* spans the full pipeline from a raw prompt to a finished answer: tokenization → forward pass → decoding strategy → stopping criteria → post-processing. Examiners expect students to know **all four decoding strategies** (greedy, beam, top-k, top-p) and at least one **optimization technique** (KV-cache, speculative decoding, or quantization).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inference Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

The inference of an LLM follows a deterministic pipeline. Every step below is **separately examinable** in KTU papers.

**Step 1 — Tokenization.** The raw text prompt is split into subword tokens (e.g., using BPE or SentencePiece). A prompt of length $N$ characters becomes a sequence of $n$ token IDs: $x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbb{Z}^n$.

**Step 2 — Embedding Lookup.** Each token ID is mapped to a dense vector of dimension $d_{model}$ via an embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d_{model}}$, where $V$ is the vocabulary size.

$$h_0 = E[x] \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$$

**Step 3 — Transformer Forward Pass.** The embeddings pass through $L$ stacked transformer blocks, each performing multi-head self-attention and a feed-forward network. The output of the final layer at the *last position* is the **logit vector**:

$$z = W_{LM} \cdot h_L^{(n)} \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$$

where $W_{LM}$ is the language model head.

**Step 4 — Logit Processing & Decoding.** The logits $z$ are transformed (temperature scaling, top-k, top-p filtering) into a probability distribution $P$ over the vocabulary. A decoding algorithm then **selects** or **samples** a token $\hat{y}_1$ from $P$.

**Step 5 — Autoregressive Step.** The chosen token is appended to the input, and Steps 2–4 are repeated to produce $\hat{y}_2$, then $\hat{y}_3$, and so on, until a stop token is emitted or max length is reached.

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Decoding Strategies

### 2.2.1 Greedy Decoding

At every step, the token with the **highest probability** is chosen deterministically.

$$\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_{v \in V} P_\theta(v \mid x, \hat{y}_{<t})$$

- **Pros:** Fastest possible, deterministic, low latency.
- **Cons:** Prone to repetition, generic outputs, can get stuck in repetition loops.

### 2.2.2 Beam Search

Maintains $k$ parallel hypotheses ("beams") and at each step expands each beam by the top $k$ candidates, keeping the $k$ highest-scoring sequences overall. The score of a beam of length $L$ is:

$$\text{score}(\hat{y}_{1:L}) = \frac{1}{L^\alpha} \sum_{t=1}^{L} \log P_\theta(\hat{y}_t \mid x, \hat{y}_{<t})$$

where $\alpha$ is a *length normalization* exponent (commonly $\alpha = 0.6$ to $0.8$).

- **Pros:** Higher BLEU/ROUGE scores, more coherent long-form outputs.
- **Cons:** $k \times$ more memory; can produce "safe" but bland text; less diverse.

### 2.2.3 Sampling with Temperature

Logits are first divided by a **temperature** $T > 0$, then passed through softmax:

$$P_T(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{u \in V} \exp(z_u / T)}$$

- $T \to 0$ : approaches **greedy** (sharp, deterministic).
- $T = 1$ : standard **softmax** distribution.
- $T > 1$ : flatter, more **random/creative** distribution.

### 2.2.4 Top-k and Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling

**Top-k sampling** restricts sampling to the $k$ most probable tokens, redistributing probability among them.

**Top-p (nucleus) sampling** sorts tokens by probability, computes the cumulative distribution, and selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative mass first exceeds $p$. Sampling occurs only from this nucleus.

$$V^{(p)} = \min \left\{ S \subseteq V : \sum_{v \in S} P(v) \geq p \right\}$$

The probability is then renormalized over $V^{(p)}$:

$$P^{(p)}(v) = \frac{P(v)}{\sum_{u \in V^{(p)}} P(u)} \quad \text{for } v \in V^{(p)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern practice (GPT-4, Llama-3, Claude):** All four strategies are typically **combined** — temperature + top-p + a repetition penalty — and the exact recipe is a closely guarded engineering secret.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|--------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Next-token probability | $P_\theta(y_t \mid x, y_{<t})$ | Core autoregressive output |
| 2 | Softmax with temperature | $P_T(v) = \frac{e^{z_v/T}}{\sum_u e^{z_u/T}}$ | Controls randomness |
| 3 | Greedy decoding | $\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_v P(v)$ | Deterministic, fastest |
| 4 | Beam search score | $\frac{1}{L^\alpha}\sum_t \log P(\hat{y}_t)$ | Rank candidate sequences |
| 5 | Top-p cumulative | $\sum_{v \in S} P(v) \geq p$ | Dynamic truncation set |
| 6 | KV-Cache memory | $M_{KV} = 2 L n_{kv} d_h B$ | Memory planning (bytes) |
| 7 | FLOPs per token (forward) | $\approx 2 N$ where $N$ = params | Latency estimation |
| 8 | Perplexity | $PPL = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log p_\theta(x_i)\right)$ | Quality benchmark |
| 9 | Speculative acceptance | $\min\left(1, \frac{q(y \mid x') p(y \mid x)}{p(y \mid x) q(y \mid x')}\right)$ | Draft-then-verify |
| 10 | Throughput | $\tau = \frac{\text{tokens generated}}{\text{wall-clock time}}$ | Server-side metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Trap Rule:** In markdown tables, the vertical pipe `|` symbol in formulas (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) **breaks** table parsing. Always render absolute value as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as a bare `|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How LLM Inference is Used |
|--------|---------------------------|
| **Chatbots & Assistants** | Real-time streaming token generation under 200ms TTFT (Time To First Token) |
| **Code Completion** (Copilot) | Sub-100ms latency with speculative decoding; uses draft model to guess tokens |
| **Search Engines** (Bing Chat, Perplexity) | Batch inference with retrieval-augmented prompts, throughput-optimized |
| **Embeddings & RAG** | Final-layer hidden states reused for vector search, no decoding needed |
| **Edge / Mobile** (Gemini Nano, Phi-3-mini) | Quantized (4-bit/8-bit) models running on-device with <2GB RAM |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Logits to a Sampled Token (The Complete Path)

We trace, with full algebraic transparency, how a single output token is produced at inference time.

**Starting point — the raw logit vector.** Let the final transformer layer output, after the language model head, be:

$$z = (z_1, z_2, \dots, z_V) \in \mathbb{R}^V$$

where $V$ is vocabulary size (e.g., $V = 50{,}257$ for GPT-2, $V = 128{,}256$ for Llama-3).

**Step 1 — Apply temperature scaling.** The temperature $T$ divides each logit:

$$z'_v = \frac{z_v}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Compute softmax.** Convert scaled logits to probabilities:

$$P(v) = \frac{\exp(z'_v)}{\sum_{u=1}^{V} \exp(z'_u)}$$

**Step 3 — Sort probabilities in descending order.** Let $v_{(1)}, v_{(2)}, \dots, v_{(V)}$ be indices such that $P(v_{(1)}) \geq P(v_{(2)}) \geq \dots \geq P(v_{(V)})$.

**Step 4 — Apply top-p filter (nucleus).** Find the smallest $k$ such that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} P(v_{(i)}) \geq p$$

Define the nucleus set $V^{(p)} = \{v_{(1)}, v_{(2)}, \dots, v_{(k)}\}$.

**Step 5 — Renormalize.** Set the probability of tokens outside the nucleus to zero, and rescale:

$$P'(v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P(v)}{\sum_{u \in V^{(p)}} P(u)} & \text{if } v \in V^{(p)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Sample.** Draw a uniform random number $r \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$ and select the token $\hat{y}_t$ such that:

$$\sum_{v : P'(v) < P'(\hat{y}_t)} P'(v) < r \leq \sum_{v : P'(v) \leq P'(\hat{y}_t)} P'(v)$$

This is the **inverse-CDF sampling** step.

## 3.2 Derivation: KV-Cache Memory Footprint

The KV-cache stores the keys and values of all previous tokens so the model does not recompute them at every step.

For a transformer with $L$ layers, $n_{kv}$ KV-heads (in Grouped-Query Attention, GQA), head dimension $d_h$, batch size $B$, and current sequence length $\ell$:

$$\text{Memory for keys} = L \cdot n_{kv} \cdot d_h \cdot \ell \cdot B \cdot s$$

$$\text{Memory for values} = L \cdot n_{kv} \cdot d_h \cdot \ell \cdot B \cdot s$$

Combined (factor of 2 for both K and V):

$$M_{KV} = 2 \cdot L \cdot n_{kv} \cdot d_h \cdot \ell \cdot B \cdot s$$

where $s$ is the bytes per element (2 for FP16, 1 for INT8, 0.5 for INT4).

**Numerical example (Llama-3 70B, FP16):** $L = 80$, $n_{kv} = 8$ (GQA), $d_h = 128$, $\ell = 4096$, $B = 1$, $s = 2$.

$$M_{KV} = 2 \times 80 \times 8 \times 128 \times 4096 \times 1 \times 2 = 1.34 \text{ GB}$$

This is why long-context inference is a memory, not compute, problem.

## 3.3 Derivation: Speculative Decoding Acceptance Rule

Speculative decoding uses a small, fast **draft model** $q$ to propose $\gamma$ tokens; the large **target model** $p$ verifies them in parallel. For each proposed token $y$, the acceptance probability is:

$$\alpha(y) = \min\left(1,\ \frac{p(y \mid x') \cdot q(y \mid x)}{q(y \mid x') \cdot p(y \mid x)}\right)$$

where $x$ is the original context and $x'$ is the context with previously accepted speculative tokens prepended. If $u \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1) < \alpha(y)$, accept; else reject and resample from a corrected distribution:

$$y' \sim \frac{\max(0,\ p(\cdot \mid x') - q(\cdot \mid x'))}{\sum_v \max(0,\ p(v \mid x') - q(v \mid x'))}$$

The expected accepted length per step is $1 + \gamma \cdot E[\min(\alpha)]$ which, with well-matched draft models, gives a **2×–3× speedup** with mathematically identical output distribution to the target model alone.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decoding Strategy Trade-offs

| Strategy | Determinism | Diversity | Latency | Memory | Best For |
|----------|-------------|-----------|---------|--------|----------|
| **Greedy** | 100% | Very Low | Lowest | $1\times$ | Translation, factual QA |
| **Beam (k=5)** | High | Low | Higher ($k\times$) | $k\times$ | Summarization, math |
| **Top-k (k=50)** | Low | High | Low | $1\times$ | Creative writing |
| **Top-p (p=0.9)** | Low | High | Low | $1\times$ | Open-ended chat |
| **Temp + Top-p** | Tunable | Tunable | Low | $1\times$ | Modern assistants |
| **Speculative** | Tunable | Same as target | **2–3× lower** | Slightly higher | Production serving |
| **Contrastive** | Tunable | Anti-repetition | Medium | $1\times$ | Long-form generation |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Greedy vs. Top-p

Suppose a 4-token vocabulary $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with logits $z = (2.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.1)$ at $T = 1$.

**Step 1 — Softmax at T=1:**

$$P(A) = \frac{e^2}{e^2+e^1+e^{0.5}+e^{0.1}} = \frac{7.389}{7.389+2.718+1.649+1.105} = \frac{7.389}{12.861} \approx 0.574$$

$$P(B) = \frac{2.718}{12.861} \approx 0.211$$

$$P(C) = \frac{1.649}{12.861} \approx 0.128$$

$$P(D) = \frac{1.105}{12.861} \approx 0.086$$

**Step 2 — Greedy:** $\hat{y} = A$ (probability 0.574).

**Step 3 — Top-p with p = 0.8:** Sort descending: $A(0.574), B(0.211), C(0.128), D(0.086)$. Cumulative: $0.574, 0.785, 0.913, 0.999$. The smallest set with cumulative $\geq 0.8$ is $\{A, B, C\}$ (cumulative $0.913$).

Renormalize:

$$P'(A) = \frac{0.574}{0.913} \approx 0.629,\quad P'(B) \approx 0.231,\quad P'(C) \approx 0.140,\quad P'(D) = 0$$

Now $D$ is **never** sampled; sampling among $A, B, C$ with renormalized probabilities.

This shows top-p's *adaptive* truncation: low-probability tokens like $D$ are removed even though they would survive a fixed top-k=3 filter in a different context.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End LLM Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Prompt Text] --> B[Tokenizer BPE or SentencePiece]
    B --> C[Token IDs x1 to xn]
    C --> D[Embedding Layer E in RV x dmodel]
    D --> E[Transformer Block 1]
    E --> F[Transformer Block 2]
    F --> G[Dot Dot Dot]
    G --> H[Transformer Block L]
    H --> I[LM Head projection WLM]
    I --> J[Logit Vector z in RV]
    J --> K[Logit Processor T scale top-k top-p penalty]
    K --> L[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    L --> M{Decoding Strategy}
    M -->|Greedy| N[argmax select]
    M -->|Beam Search| O[Maintain top-k beams]
    M -->|Sampling| P[Inverse CDF sample]
    N --> Q[Chosen Token yt]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R{Stop Token or Max Length}
    R -->|No| S[Append yt to Context]
    S --> C
    R -->|Yes| T[Detokenize and Stream Output]
```

## 4.2 KV-Cache Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Step1[Step 1 Process Prompt]
        A1[All prompt tokens] --> A2[Compute K and V for each layer]
        A2 --> A3[Store in KV Cache memory]
    end
    subgraph Step2[Step 2 to T Generate New Tokens]
        B1[New token xt] --> B2[Compute Kt and Vt only for new token]
        B2 --> B3[Append to KV Cache]
        B3 --> B4[Attend over FULL cached K and V]
        B4 --> B5[Sample next token]
        B5 --> B1
    end
    A3 --> B4
```

**Reading guide:** The KV-cache eliminates redundant recomputation of attention keys and values for tokens that have already been processed. The memory cost grows linearly with sequence length, but the *compute* cost per new token stays roughly constant — this is the single most important inference optimization.

## 4.3 Decoding Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to choose decoding strategy] --> Q1{Is output factual and unique?}
    Q1 -->|Yes translate or math| Greedy[Greedy Decoding]
    Q1 -->|No creative or chat| Q2{Need diversity?}
    Q2 -->|Low diversity| Beam[Beam Search k=4 or 5]
    Q2 -->|High diversity| Q3{Determinism needed?}
    Q3 -->|No, want variation| Sample[Sampling]
    Q3 -->|Yes, want tunable| Temp[Temp + Top-p + Top-k]
    Sample --> Temp
    Beam --> Temp
    Greedy --> Done[Final Answer]
    Temp --> Done
```

## 4.4 Speculative Decoding Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Target[Large Target Model p]
        T1[Input x] --> T2[Parallel verify gamma tokens]
    end
    subgraph Draft[Small Draft Model q]
        D1[Input x] --> D2[Sequentially generate gamma tokens]
    end
    D2 -->|Proposed tokens| T2
    T2 -->|Acceptance alpha| Judge{Accept each token?}
    Judge -->|Yes| Append[Append to context]
    Judge -->|No| Resample[Resample from corrected p minus q]
    Append --> Output[Final output]
    Resample --> Output
    Output --> T1
```

**Reading guide:** The draft model is typically 10×–100× smaller (e.g., a 7B model drafting for a 70B target). The target model processes all draft tokens in a **single forward pass** using parallel attention masking, which is the source of the speedup.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the training phase and the inference phase of a Large Language Model. Which phase is more expensive in terms of energy and compute, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Training | Inference |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Purpose | Learn parameters $\theta$ from corpus | Apply $\theta$ to new prompts |
| Operations | Forward + Backward + Update | Forward only |
| Frequency | Once (or a few times) | Millions of times per day in production |
| Per-call cost | High (single model, hours/days) | Low (one query) |
| **Aggregate cost** | **Higher per call** | **Higher globally** (scales with users) |

Training is more **per-call** expensive because it computes gradients for hundreds of billions of parameters across trillions of tokens, requiring thousands of GPU-hours. Inference, while cheaper per query, is performed vastly more often, so its **aggregate carbon footprint** can rival training. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the role of the KV-cache in transformer inference. Why is it essential for autoregressive generation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

During autoregressive generation, the model produces one token at a time. Without a KV-cache, every new token would require recomputing attention keys and values for *all* previously generated tokens, leading to **quadratic compute cost** $O(L^2)$ per token.

The KV-cache stores the keys and values of past tokens in memory, so each new step only computes the KV for the **single new token** and reuses cached values. This reduces per-token cost to **linear** $O(L)$ and yields a 5×–10× wall-clock speedup for long sequences. The trade-off is increased GPU memory usage, addressed by techniques like Multi-Query Attention (MQA), Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), and KV-cache compression. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the four major decoding strategies used during LLM inference: **Greedy Decoding, Beam Search, Top-k Sampling, and Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling**. For each, provide the mathematical formulation and discuss one advantage and one disadvantage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deployed LLM has $L = 32$ layers, $n_{kv} = 32$ KV-heads, head dimension $d_h = 128$, and serves a batch of $B = 4$ requests, each with sequence length $\ell = 2048$. The model uses FP16 precision (2 bytes/element). **(7 marks)**

    **(i)** Compute the total KV-cache memory in gigabytes.
    **(ii)** If the same model is quantized to INT8 (1 byte/element), what is the new memory footprint?
    **(iii)** Briefly explain one additional technique to reduce KV-cache memory further.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks (1 mark per strategy, plus comparison)**

1. **Greedy Decoding:** $\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_v P(v \mid x, y_{<t})$. Advantage: deterministic and fastest. Disadvantage: repetitive, generic, no exploration.
2. **Beam Search:** Maintains top-$k$ beams; ranks by $\frac{1}{L^\alpha}\sum_t \log P(\hat{y}_t)$. Advantage: higher-quality long-form output. Disadvantage: $k \times$ memory and compute, can be bland.
3. **Top-k Sampling:** Restricts sampling to the $k$ highest-probability tokens. Advantage: balances quality and diversity. Disadvantage: fixed $k$ does not adapt to distribution shape.
4. **Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling:** Sorts tokens by probability, takes smallest set whose cumulative mass $\geq p$. Advantage: *adaptive* to distribution sharpness. Disadvantage: can produce very low-quality tail tokens if $p$ is too high.

Modern systems combine **temperature + top-p + repetition penalty** for best results. (7 marks)

**(b) — 7 marks**

**(i)** Using $M_{KV} = 2 \cdot L \cdot n_{kv} \cdot d_h \cdot \ell \cdot B \cdot s$:

$$M_{KV} = 2 \times 32 \times 32 \times 128 \times 2048 \times 4 \times 2$$

$$= 2 \times 32 \times 32 \times 128 \times 2048 \times 4 \times 2$$

$$= 2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \text{ bytes} = 2.0 \text{ GB}$$

(2 marks)

**(ii)** For INT8, $s = 1$:

$$M_{KV}^{INT8} = \frac{2.0}{2} = 1.0 \text{ GB}$$

(1 mark)

**(iii)** Additional techniques: **(1)** Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) — share KV across multiple query heads, reducing $n_{kv}$ by 4×–8×; **(2)** PagedAttention (vLLM) — non-contiguous memory paging to reduce fragmentation; **(3)** KV-cache compression via low-rank projection or token eviction; **(4)** Sliding-window attention with selective recomputation. (Any one well-explained technique: 4 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end inference pipeline** of an LLM from prompt input to generated output. Clearly mark the **autoregressive loop**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Speculative Decoding** as an inference optimization. Derive the acceptance probability formula and explain why the output distribution is **provably identical** to standard decoding from the target model alone. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

The pipeline is:

1. **Tokenization** (BPE/SentencePiece) → token IDs
2. **Embedding** → dense vectors
3. **Transformer forward pass** through $L$ layers
4. **LM head** projects hidden state to vocabulary logits
5. **Logit processing** (temperature, penalties)
6. **Softmax** to probabilities
7. **Decoding** (greedy/beam/sample) → one token
8. **Stop check** → if no, **append token and loop back to step 3**; if yes, detokenize and stream.

The **autoregressive loop** is the cycle from step 3 back to step 8 producing one new token per iteration. (7 marks)

**(b) — 7 marks**

In speculative decoding, a small draft model $q$ generates $\gamma$ candidate tokens sequentially, which the large target model $p$ then **verifies in a single parallel forward pass**.

For each candidate token $y$, the acceptance probability that preserves the target distribution $p$ is derived from the **rejection-sampling scheme**:

$$\alpha(y) = \min\left(1,\ \frac{p(y \mid x')}{q(y \mid x')} \cdot \frac{q(\text{prefix} \mid x)}{p(\text{prefix} \mid x)}\right)$$

For a single-token verification with prefix aligned, the simpler form is:

$$\alpha(y) = \min\left(1,\ \frac{p(y \mid x)}{q(y \mid x)}\right)$$

The output distribution is provably identical to $p$ because the algorithm is a **coupling of two MCMC-style chains** (Leviathan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023): every accepted token comes from $p$, and every rejected token is resampled from a corrected distribution whose marginal equals $p$. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing training and inference loops:** Students often write "the model is trained to predict the next token" inside an inference answer. Mark this wrong — training uses gradients; inference does not.
> 2. **Forgetting the autoregressive nature:** Beam search and sampling are *still* autoregressive. State this explicitly.
> 3. **Misapplying temperature:** Temperature $T$ is applied **before** softmax, not after. Writing $P(v) = \text{softmax}(z) / T$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in KV-cache:** The "2" comes from storing *both* keys and values. Many students write $M_{KV} = L \cdot n_{kv} \cdot d_h \cdot \ell \cdot B \cdot s$, which is off by 2× and loses 1 mark in the numerical.
> 5. **Top-p vs. Top-k confusion:** Top-k is a *fixed count*; top-p is an *adaptive mass cutoff*. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points:**

- **Inference** = forward-only pass; no gradients, no parameter updates.
- The pipeline is: **Tokenize → Embed → Transformer → LM Head → Logit Process → Softmax → Decode → Loop**.
- **Greedy decoding** is deterministic, fastest, but bland and repetitive.
- **Beam search** keeps $k$ hypotheses; uses length-normalized log-probability scoring.
- **Temperature $T$** divides logits before softmax; $T \to 0$ → greedy, $T \to \infty$ → uniform.
- **Top-k** filters to the $k$ highest-probability tokens (fixed size).
- **Top-p (nucleus)** filters to the smallest set whose cumulative probability $\geq p$ (adaptive).
- **Repetition penalty** multiplies logits of previously generated tokens by a factor $\rho < 1$ to discourage loops.
- **KV-cache** stores K and V of past tokens to avoid recomputation; memory is $2 L n_{kv} d_h \ell B s$.
- **Speculative decoding** uses a small draft model + parallel verification for 2×–3× speedup with **identical output distribution**.
- **Quantization** (FP16 → INT8 → INT4) halves/quarters memory and boosts throughput.
- **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)** and **Multi-Query Attention (MQA)** reduce KV-cache by sharing K/V across heads.
- **PagedAttention** (vLLM) and **FlashAttention** are the production-grade inference kernels used by ChatGPT-class systems.
- **Key metrics:** **TTFT** (Time To First Token), **TPOT** (Time Per Output Token), **throughput** (tokens/sec), **perplexity** (quality).
- **Perplexity formula:** $PPL = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log p_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ — lower is better.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** When asked to "explain inference," always cover — (1) the pipeline, (2) decoding strategies with formulas, (3) at least one optimization (KV-cache or speculative decoding), and (4) one trade-off (latency vs. throughput vs. quality). This structure scores full marks under KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT862 - LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** LLMOps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LLMOps (Large Language Model Operations)** is defined as a specialized discipline within the broader MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) framework that encompasses the end-to-end operational tooling, best practices, automation workflows, governance protocols, and engineering methodologies required to deploy, monitor, maintain, scale, secure, and continuously improve large language models in production environments.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus, LLMOps is positioned as the engineering backbone that bridges the gap between experimental LLM research and reliable, enterprise-grade production systems. It unifies principles from:

- **DevOps** (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment)
- **MLOps** (Model lifecycle and versioning)
- **DataOps** (Data pipeline governance)
- **ModelOps** (AI governance and compliance)

The primary objective of LLMOps is to ensure that LLMs are delivered to end users with **high availability**, **low latency**, **predictable cost**, **strong safety guardrails**, and **measurable performance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT862 Module 4):** LLMOps is treated as a horizontal capability layer that touches every other module of the course — from prompt engineering and fine-tuning (Modules 2–3) to evaluation and deployment (Module 4).

---

## 1.2 Core LLMOps Terminology Cheat-Sheet

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Foundation Model** | A large pre-trained model (e.g., LLaMA, GPT) that serves as a base for downstream tasks. |
| **Inference** | The act of running a trained LLM on new input to generate a response. |
| **Token** | The atomic unit of text processed by an LLM; ≈ 0.75 English words or ≈ 4 characters. |
| **Prompt Template** | A reusable, parameterized text structure sent to the LLM. |
| **RAG** | Retrieval-Augmented Generation — augmenting prompts with external context fetched at query time. |
| **Vector Database** | A specialized store for high-dimensional embeddings enabling semantic similarity search. |
| **Quantization** | Reducing model numerical precision (e.g., FP16 → INT8) to shrink memory and speed up inference. |
| **Latency** | Time elapsed between sending a prompt and receiving the first generated token. |
| **Throughput** | Number of tokens (or requests) processed per unit of time. |
| **Drift** | Statistical deviation of production data/model behavior from training baseline. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Power Grid"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Think of LLMOps as the **smart power grid for an entire nation of language-based services**.
> - An LLM is like a **nuclear reactor** — immensely powerful, expensive to build, dangerous if mishandled, and capable of powering millions of homes.
> - **MLOps** is the operational discipline for a single power plant.
> - **LLMOps** is the national grid operator's playbook: it manages *electricity generation* (training), *transmission* (deployment), *load balancing* (auto-scaling), *billing* (token costs), *blackout prevention* (rate limiting and failover), *metering* (observability), and *regulation* (safety and compliance).
>
> Just as a grid operator cares about **voltage stability, frequency regulation, peak demand handling, and fault isolation**, an LLMOps engineer cares about **token throughput, latency SLOs, hallucination rates, prompt injection defense, and cost-per-query budgets**.

---

## 1.4 Why LLMOps is Distinct from Traditional MLOps

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Treating LLMOps as merely "MLOps for big models." This is *incorrect*. LLMOps has **fundamentally different engineering concerns** because of the generative, prompt-driven, and API-mediated nature of LLMs.

| Dimension | Traditional MLOps | LLMOps |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Tabular features | Free-form natural language (prompts) |
| **Output** | Class label / regression value | Multi-token generated text |
| **Determinism** | Mostly deterministic | Stochastic by default (temperature, sampling) |
| **Model size** | MB to a few GB | **10 GB to 700+ GB** |
| **Compute footprint** | CPU / single GPU | **Multi-GPU clusters, TPUs, custom ASICs** |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, AUC | BLEU, ROUGE, LLM-as-Judge, Human Eval |
| **Iteration unit** | Model retrain | Prompt change, fine-tune, RAG re-index |
| **Primary cost** | Training compute | **Inference token cost** |
| **Serving** | Dedicated model per task | One foundation model serves many tasks |
| **Versioning** | Model weights + data | Weights + data + prompts + embeddings + RAG index |
| **Failure modes** | Numerical drift, data skew | Hallucination, prompt injection, jailbreaks, toxicity |

---

## 1.5 The Six Pillars of LLMOps

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme evaluates students on their ability to **map engineering problems to the correct LLMOps pillar**. Memorize these six pillars:

1. **Data & Embedding Management** — Curating corpora, generating embeddings, maintaining vector stores.
2. **Prompt Engineering Operations** — Versioning prompt templates, A/B testing, regression testing prompts.
3. **Fine-Tuning & Continual Learning** — PEFT (LoRA, QLoRA), RLHF, DPO pipelines.
4. **Model Deployment & Serving** — Quantization, batching, serverless vs. dedicated inference, vLLM, TGI, Triton.
5. **Monitoring, Observability & Evaluation** — Token usage, latency, hallucination, drift, LLM tracing.
6. **Governance, Safety & Cost Engineering** — Rate limits, RBAC, PII redaction, budget alerts, fairness audits.
