# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LLMOps Reference Architecture (Layered View)

An enterprise-grade LLMOps platform is best understood as a stack of cooperating layers. Each layer encapsulates a class of concerns and exposes clean interfaces to the layers above and below it.

### Layer 1 — Data & Foundation Layer
- **Raw corpora ingestion** from PDFs, databases, web crawls, SaaS APIs.
- **Cleaning & deduplication** (MinHash, exact-hash, fuzzy dedup).
- **Embedding generation** using a sentence-transformer (e.g., `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, `BGE-large`, `E5`).
- **Vector index construction** using ANN algorithms (HNSW, IVF-PQ, ScaNN).

### Layer 2 — Model & Adaptation Layer
- **Foundation model selection** (open-source vs. proprietary).
- **Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)**: LoRA, QLoRA, adapters.
- **Alignment**: SFT → RLHF / DPO / RLAIF.
- **Quantization**: FP32 → FP16 → BF16 → INT8 → INT4 (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF).

### Layer 3 — Prompt & Orchestration Layer
- **Prompt registry** with version control (GitOps for prompts).
- **Prompt templating engines** (Jinja2, Liquid, Mustache).
- **Agentic orchestration** (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Semantic Kernel).
- **Tool calling & function-calling routers**.

### Layer 4 — Serving & Infrastructure Layer
- **Inference servers**: vLLM, TGI (Text Generation Inference), TensorRT-LLM, Triton.
- **Schedulers**: continuous batching, paged attention (vLLM), speculative decoding.
- **GPU resource managers**: Kubernetes + GPU Operator, KubeRay, Modal, RunPod.
- **Edge & on-device**: llama.cpp, Ollama, MLX, CoreML.

### Layer 5 — Observability & Evaluation Layer
- **Tracing**: OpenTelemetry + OpenInference.
- **Logging**: Prompt logs, completion logs, token counts, cost.
- **Metrics**: TTFT, ITL, throughput, hallucination rate, refusal rate.
- **Evaluation harnesses**: RAGAS, TruLens, DeepEval, LangSmith.

### Layer 6 — Governance & Cost Layer
- **Identity & Access Management (IAM)**.
- **PII detection & redaction** (Microsoft Presidio, AWS Comprehend PII).
- **Rate limiting & quota management**.
- **Cost attribution** by team, project, or feature.
- **Compliance**: SOC2, ISO 27001, GDPR, India DPDP Act 2023.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-frequency in KTU 2024 Scheme exam questions** for PECMT862. Master the units and the boundary conditions.

### 2.2.1 Tokenization & Cost Economics

$$
C_{\text{query}} \;=\; \frac{(n_{\text{in}} \cdot p_{\text{in}} \;+\; n_{\text{out}} \cdot p_{\text{out}})}{10^{6}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{query}}$ = **monetary cost of a single query** (USD or INR)
- $n_{\text{in}}$ = **number of input tokens**
- $n_{\text{out}}$ = **number of output tokens**
- $p_{\text{in}}$ = **price per million input tokens** (USD/M)
- $p_{\text{out}}$ = **price per million output tokens** (USD/M)

### 2.2.2 Throughput & Latency Formulas

$$
\text{TTFT} \;=\; t_{\text{prefill}}(n_{\text{in}})
$$

$$
\text{ITL} \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{tokens/s per user}}
$$

$$
\text{TBT} \;\approx\; \text{TTFT} \;+\; n_{\text{out}} \cdot \text{ITL}
$$

Where:
- $\text{TTFT}$ = **Time To First Token** (cold-start dominated)
- $\text{ITL}$ = **Inter-Token Latency**
- $\text{TBT}$ = **Total Blocking Time** for the user

### 2.2.3 Effective Memory Requirement for an LLM

$$
M_{\text{eff}} \;\approx\; N_{\text{params}} \times B_{\text{quant}} \times (1 + O_{\text{kv}})
$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{eff}}$ = **effective VRAM in bytes**
- $N_{\text{params}}$ = **number of model parameters** (e.g., $7 \times 10^{9}$ for 7B)
- $B_{\text{quant}}$ = **bytes per parameter** (2 for FP16, 1 for INT8, 0.5 for INT4)
- $O_{\text{kv}}$ = **KV-cache overhead factor** (typically 0.1 – 0.3)

### 2.2.4 Cost of Fine-Tuning

$$
C_{\text{FT}} \;=\; (G_{\text{GPU}} \cdot h_{\text{GPU}}) \;+\; C_{\text{data}} \;+\; C_{\text{eval}} \;+\; C_{\text{storage}}
$$

Where:
- $G_{\text{GPU}}$ = **per-hour GPU cost**
- $h_{\text{GPU}}$ = **GPU-hours consumed**
- $C_{\text{data}}$, $C_{\text{eval}}$, $C_{\text{storage}}$ = ancillary costs

### 2.2.5 Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Recall Metric

$$
\text{Recall@k} \;=\; \frac{\mid \text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved@k} \mid}{\mid \text{Relevant} \mid}
$$

### 2.2.6 Hallucination Rate

$$
H \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\text{response}_i \text{ is unfaithful to context}_i\right]
$$

Where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the **indicator function** and $N$ is the size of the evaluation set.

### 2.2.7 Batching Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\text{batch}} \;=\; \frac{\text{achieved tokens/s}}{\text{theoretical max tokens/s}}
$$

A healthy LLM serving stack targets $\eta_{\text{batch}} \geq 0.65$.

---

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Industry

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Startups** use LLMOps to launch ChatGPT-wrappers in days, not months.
> - **Banks** use the governance layer to satisfy RBI/SEBI AI guidelines and the India DPDP Act 2023.
> - **Healthcare** uses the observability layer to detect hallucinated drug interactions.
> - **Edtech platforms** in Kerala (e.g., NITI Aayog-recognized startups) use cost-engineering to keep per-student monthly LLM bills below **₹30**.
> - **Research labs** use the fine-tuning layer to adapt Llama-3-8B to Malayalam, Sanskrit, or code-mixed Indian languages.

---

## 2.4 LLM Lifecycle Stages — The Continuous Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic: "D-F-M-S-M-O-G"** — *Discover, Formalize, Model, Serve, Monitor, Optimize, Govern*. This is the closed feedback loop of production LLMOps.

1. **Discover** — Identify use-case, success metrics, guardrails.
2. **Formalize** — Write prompt specs, define evaluation harness.
3. **Model** — Choose base model, fine-tune, distill, quantize.
4. **Serve** — Deploy via API, batch, autoscale.
5. **Monitor** — Log, trace, evaluate, alert.
6. **Optimize** — Prompt-tune, RAG-refresh, re-fine-tune.
7. **Govern** — Audit, retrain policy, retire unsafe models.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost Projection for a Production Chatbot

**Problem:** A B2B SaaS company in Kochi deploys a customer-support chatbot powered by an external LLM. The pricing is **\$3.00 per million input tokens** and **\$15.00 per million output tokens**. Average prompt length is 800 tokens; average response length is 250 tokens. Expected traffic is **20,000 conversations per day**. Derive the **monthly operating cost** (30 days).

**Given:**
$$
p_{\text{in}} = 3.00\;\text{USD/M}, \quad p_{\text{out}} = 15.00\;\text{USD/M}
$$
$$
n_{\text{in}} = 800\;\text{tokens}, \quad n_{\text{out}} = 250\;\text{tokens}
$$
$$
Q = 20{,}000\;\text{queries/day}, \quad D = 30\;\text{days}
$$

**Step 1 — Cost of a single query:**

$$
C_{\text{query}} \;=\; \frac{(n_{\text{in}} \cdot p_{\text{in}}) + (n_{\text{out}} \cdot p_{\text{out}})}{10^{6}}
$$

Substitute the numeric values:

$$
C_{\text{query}} \;=\; \frac{(800 \times 3.00) + (250 \times 15.00)}{10^{6}}
$$

Evaluate the numerator term by term:

$$
(800 \times 3.00) = 2400
$$

$$
(250 \times 15.00) = 3750
$$

$$
2400 + 3750 = 6150
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{\text{query}} \;=\; \frac{6150}{10^{6}} \;=\; 0.00615\;\text{USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily cost:**

$$
C_{\text{day}} \;=\; C_{\text{query}} \times Q \;=\; 0.00615 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
C_{\text{day}} \;=\; 123.00\;\text{USD/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Monthly cost (30 days):**

$$
C_{\text{month}} \;=\; C_{\text{day}} \times D \;=\; 123.00 \times 30
$$

$$
C_{\text{month}} \;=\; 3690\;\text{USD/month}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{month}} \;\approx\; \$3{,}690\;\text{per month}\;}
$$

At an exchange rate of **₹83/USD**, this equals approximately **₹3,06,270/month** — a budget item that demands an LLMOps cost-engineering intervention.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective VRAM for a 7B Model under INT4 Quantization

**Problem:** Estimate the VRAM needed to serve **Llama-3-8B** with INT4 quantization, assuming a KV-cache overhead factor of $O_{\text{kv}} = 0.20$.

**Given:**
$$
N_{\text{params}} = 8 \times 10^{9}, \quad B_{\text{quant}} = 0.5\;\text{bytes (INT4)}, \quad O_{\text{kv}} = 0.20
$$

**Step 1 — Compute weights-only footprint:**

$$
M_{\text{weights}} \;=\; N_{\text{params}} \times B_{\text{quant}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{weights}} \;=\; 8 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5 \;=\; 4 \times 10^{9}\;\text{bytes}
$$

Convert to GB using $1\;\text{GB} = 10^{9}\;\text{bytes}$:

$$
M_{\text{weights}} \;=\; 4\;\text{GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the KV-cache overhead factor:**

$$
M_{\text{eff}} \;=\; M_{\text{weights}} \times (1 + O_{\text{kv}})
$$

$$
M_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 4 \times (1 + 0.20) \;=\; 4 \times 1.20 \;=\; 4.8\;\text{GB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;M_{\text{eff}} \;\approx\; 4.8\;\text{GB}\;}
$$

This is why INT4 Llama-3-8B comfortably fits on a single consumer **RTX 3060 (12 GB)** or **Apple M2 Pro (16 GB unified memory)** — a key enabler of *on-device LLMOps*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RAG Recall@k for an Enterprise Document Search

**Problem:** For a set of 500 internal HR policy questions, a vector retriever returns the top 5 chunks. 380 queries have at least one relevant chunk in the top-5. Compute **Recall@5**.

**Given:**
$$
\mid \text{Relevant} \mid = 500, \quad \mid \text{Retrieved@5} \cap \text{Relevant} \mid = 380
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the recall formula:**

$$
\text{Recall@5} \;=\; \frac{380}{500}
$$

$$
\text{Recall@5} \;=\; 0.76 \;=\; 76\%
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** A Recall@5 of **76%** is borderline. KTU industry best-practice target for enterprise RAG is **≥ 85%**. Remediation options:

- Re-tune the embedding model (e.g., switch from `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` to `BGE-large-en-v1.5`).
- Apply **hybrid search** (BM25 + dense vectors).
- Apply **re-ranking** with a cross-encoder (e.g., `cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM`).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Serving Stack

| Workload profile | Recommended stack | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-traffic prototype** (< 100 req/day) | Serverless API (OpenAI, Claude, Gemini) | Zero infra, pay-per-token |
| **Medium-traffic SaaS** (1k–100k req/day) | **vLLM** on 1–4 A100s with continuous batching | High throughput, PagedAttention |
| **High-traffic consumer** (> 1M req/day) | **Triton + TensorRT-LLM** on multi-GPU cluster | Maximum GPU utilization |
| **Edge / on-device** | **llama.cpp / Ollama** with GGUF | CPU-friendly, no network |
| **Latency-critical (< 100 ms TTFT)** | Speculative decoding + small draft model | Trades compute for latency |
| **Privacy-critical (healthcare, defence)** | Self-hosted on **air-gapped K8s** | Data never leaves the perimeter |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Quantizing Model Size Across Precision Levels

Starting from a 70B parameter model in **FP32** (4 bytes/parameter):

**Step 1 — FP32 footprint:**

$$
M_{\text{FP32}} \;=\; 70 \times 10^{9} \times 4 \;=\; 280\;\text{GB}
$$

**Step 2 — FP16 footprint:**

$$
M_{\text{FP16}} \;=\; 70 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \;=\; 140\;\text{GB}
$$

**Step 3 — INT8 footprint:**

$$
M_{\text{INT8}} \;=\; 70 \times 10^{9} \times 1 \;=\; 70\;\text{GB}
$$

**Step 4 — INT4 footprint:**

$$
M_{\text{INT4}} \;=\; 70 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5 \;=\; 35\;\text{GB}
$$

| Precision | Bytes/param | 70B footprint | Relative to FP32 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FP32** | 4 | **280 GB** | 1.00× |
| **FP16 / BF16** | 2 | **140 GB** | 0.50× |
| **INT8** | 1 | **70 GB** | 0.25× |
| **INT4** | 0.5 | **35 GB** | 0.125× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality-vs-size tradeoff rule of thumb (board-exam friendly):**
> - FP16 → INT8: typically < 1% quality loss.
> - INT8 → INT4: typically 1–4% quality loss; requires GPTQ/AWQ calibration.
> - INT4 → INT2: research-grade, not production-ready.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram encodes the **closed feedback loop** central to LLMOps: production traces flow into the evaluation harness, evaluation signals drive cost/governance decisions, and those decisions loop back to refine prompts, embeddings, and even the base model via re-fine-tuning.

## 4.2 LLM Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Discover
    Discover --> Formalize: define metrics and guardrails
    Formalize --> Model: pick base and fine tune
    Model --> Serve: quantize and deploy
    Serve --> Monitor: enable tracing and logging
    Monitor --> Optimize: detect drift or hallucination
    Optimize --> Formalize: update prompts or retrain

    Govern --> [*]: model retirement
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a lifecycle diagram in the KTU exam, always include the **Monitor → Optimize → Formalize** feedback arc. Many students forget the loop and draw a one-way pipeline, which is technically *not* LLMOps — it is just *deployment*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define LLMOps. How does it differ from traditional MLOps? Mention any three distinct challenges specific to LLMOps. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

LLMOps (Large Language Model Operations) is the end-to-end engineering discipline that manages the lifecycle of large language models in production — including data curation, fine-tuning, deployment, monitoring, evaluation, cost optimization, and governance.

It differs from traditional MLOps in the following ways:

1. **Generative, non-deterministic outputs:** LLMs produce free-form text, not class labels, requiring new evaluation metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, LLM-as-Judge, human eval).
2. **Prompt as a first-class artifact:** Prompts must be versioned, tested, and rolled back like code.
3. **Massive compute footprint:** Models range from 7B to 700B+ parameters, demanding GPU clusters, quantization, and specialized serving engines (vLLM, Triton).
4. **Token-based economics:** Cost scales with input/output token count, not per-request compute.
5. **Novel failure modes:** Hallucination, prompt injection, jailbreaks, and toxicity require dedicated safety guardrails.

---

### Question 2: List and briefly explain the six pillars of LLMOps as per the KTU 2024 scheme. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Data and Embedding Management** — Ingesting, cleaning, chunking, embedding, and indexing enterprise corpora.
2. **Prompt Engineering Operations** — Versioning, A/B testing, and regression testing of prompt templates.
3. **Fine-Tuning and Continual Learning** — PEFT (LoRA/QLoRA), RLHF/DPO pipelines, and incremental re-training.
4. **Model Deployment and Serving** — Quantization, batching, scaling, and serving engines.
5. **Monitoring, Observability and Evaluation** — Latency, throughput, hallucination, drift, and tracing.
6. **Governance, Safety and Cost Engineering** — RBAC, PII redaction, rate limiting, budget alerts, and compliance.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** for Module-level ESE questions. Both options below carry identical mark weightage.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): LLMOps Reference Architecture and Cost Engineering `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Draw and explain the layered LLMOps reference architecture. Highlight the role of the observability and governance layers in ensuring production reliability."**

**Model Solution:**

The LLMOps reference architecture is a six-layer stack:

1. **Data and Foundation Layer** — Owns corpora ingestion, cleaning, embedding generation, and vector index construction.
2. **Model and Adaptation Layer** — Selects the base model, applies PEFT (LoRA/QLoRA), performs alignment (RLHF/DPO), and applies quantization.
3. **Prompt and Orchestration Layer** — Hosts the prompt registry, templating engine, and agentic orchestration framework.
4. **Serving and Infrastructure Layer** — Runs inference engines (vLLM, TGI, Triton) on K8s with auto-scaling.
5. **Observability and Evaluation Layer** — Implements OpenTelemetry tracing, logs every prompt-completion pair, computes latency/throughput/hallucination metrics, and runs regression evaluations.
6. **Governance and Cost Layer** — Enforces RBAC, PII redaction, rate limits, and per-team cost attribution.

**Role of Observability:**
- Detects latency regressions and triggers autoscaling.
- Flags hallucination spikes via LLM-as-Judge evaluators.
- Surfaces prompt-injection attempts via content classifiers.
- Provides audit trails for compliance (SOC2, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023).

**Role of Governance:**
- Enforces per-tenant rate limits to prevent abuse.
- Implements content safety filters (toxicity, PII leakage).
- Caps monthly token budgets per team to prevent cost overruns.
- Logs all access events for forensic analysis.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A company uses an external LLM priced at \$2.00 per million input tokens and \$10.00 per million output tokens. Average input = 600 tokens, average output = 200 tokens, daily traffic = 15,000 queries. Compute the monthly cost in USD and INR (assume ₹83/USD, 30 days). Suggest two LLMOps interventions to reduce the cost by 40%."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cost per query:**

$$
C_{\text{query}} \;=\; \frac{(600 \times 2.00) + (200 \times 10.00)}{10^{6}} \;=\; \frac{1200 + 2000}{10^{6}} \;=\; 0.0032\;\text{USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily cost:**

$$
C_{\text{day}} \;=\; 0.0032 \times 15{,}000 \;=\; 48.00\;\text{USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Monthly cost:**

$$
C_{\text{month}} \;=\; 48.00 \times 30 \;=\; 1440\;\text{USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to INR:**

$$
C_{\text{INR}} \;=\; 1440 \times 83 \;=\; \text{₹}1{,}19{,}520/\text{month}
$$

**Two LLMOps interventions to cut cost by 40%:**

1. **Prompt compression + RAG:** Reduce average input tokens from 600 → 250 by removing redundant system instructions and using retrieved context selectively. This alone reduces input cost by **~58%**.
2. **Output capping + caching:** Cap maximum output tokens at 200 (already at limit) and cache responses to frequently asked questions using a semantic cache (e.g., GPTCache). A 30% cache hit rate reduces QPS to the LLM by 30%.

Combined effect ≈ **40–50% cost reduction**, bringing monthly cost to ≈ **₹60,000–70,000**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): LLM Serving, Quantization, and Observability `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain the concept of LLM quantization. Derive the VRAM footprint of a 13B parameter model under FP16, INT8, and INT4 precision. Assume KV-cache overhead factor of 0.15."**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Quantization:**
Quantization reduces the numerical precision of model weights (and optionally activations) from higher-bit formats (FP32/FP16) to lower-bit formats (INT8/INT4). This shrinks the model's memory footprint, accelerates inference, and reduces energy consumption — at the cost of a small accuracy drop. Common algorithms include GPTQ, AWQ, and GGUF.

**Given:**
$$
N_{\text{params}} = 13 \times 10^{9}, \quad O_{\text{kv}} = 0.15
$$

**Case 1 — FP16 ($B = 2$ bytes):**

$$
M_{\text{FP16}} = 13 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \times 1.15 = 29.9\;\text{GB}
$$

**Case 2 — INT8 ($B = 1$ byte):**

$$
M_{\text{INT8}} = 13 \times 10^{9} \times 1 \times 1.15 = 14.95\;\text{GB}
$$

**Case 3 — INT4 ($B = 0.5$ byte):**

$$
M_{\text{INT4}} = 13 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5 \times 1.15 = 7.475\;\text{GB}
$$

**Summary table:**

| Precision | Bytes/param | Footprint with KV |
|---|---|---|
| FP16 | 2 | **29.9 GB** |
| INT8 | 1 | **14.95 GB** |
| INT4 | 0.5 | **7.48 GB** |

This is why INT4 13B models fit comfortably on a single 16 GB GPU.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Discuss the key metrics monitored in the LLMOps observability layer. Distinguish between latency metrics (TTFT, ITL, TBT) and quality metrics (hallucination rate, refusal rate, faithfulness)."**

**Model Solution:**

The LLMOps observability layer captures two broad categories of metrics:

**A. Latency / Performance Metrics:**

1. **TTFT (Time To First Token):** Time from request submission to arrival of the first generated token. Dominated by prefill phase. Critical for chat UX (target ≤ 500 ms).
2. **ITL (Inter-Token Latency):** Average delay between successive generated tokens. Dominated by decode phase. Target ≤ 50 ms/token for smooth streaming.
3. **TBT (Total Blocking Time):** End-to-end perceived latency ≈ TTFT + $n_{\text{out}} \times$ ITL.
4. **Throughput:** Aggregate tokens/second across all concurrent users.
5. **Queue depth & GPU utilization:** Operational metrics for autoscaling.

**B. Quality / Correctness Metrics:**

1. **Hallucination rate:** Fraction of responses that contain factually unfaithful content. Computed via LLM-as-Judge or human eval.
2. **Refusal rate:** Fraction of legitimate queries that the model wrongly refuses (over-alignment failure).
3. **Faithfulness:** For RAG systems, the degree to which the answer is grounded in retrieved context. Measured by RAGAS.
4. **Answer Relevance:** Semantic similarity between answer and the ground-truth reference.
5. **Context Precision/Recall:** Quality of the retriever in the RAG pipeline.
6. **Toxicity / Bias scores:** Safety metrics from classifiers like Detoxify.

**Distinction:** Latency metrics measure **operational health** (is the system fast and available?). Quality metrics measure **functional correctness** (are the answers right and safe?). A production-grade LLMOps platform must monitor **both**, and an alert must fire when either dimension degrades beyond SLO.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LLMOps Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing LLMOps with MLOps.** If the question says "LLMOps," do not write a generic MLOps answer. Mention prompt versioning, RAG, token costs, and hallucination explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the units.** When computing cost, always state the currency (USD/INR) and the time period (per query, per day, per month). Marks are deducted for ambiguous units.
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps.** In a 7-mark numerical, every substitution must be shown. Writing only the final answer caps marks at 2/7.
> 4. **Drawing a one-way lifecycle diagram.** The closed feedback loop (Monitor → Optimize → Model/Formalize) is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring governance and safety.** A purely technical LLMOps answer that omits PII, prompt injection, and compliance loses 1–2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):**

- ✅ LLMOps = end-to-end operational discipline for LLMs in production.
- ✅ Six pillars: **Data, Prompts, Fine-Tuning, Serving, Observability, Governance**.
- ✅ Three layers of difference from MLOps: **generative output, prompt-as-code, token economics**.
- ✅ Cost formula: $C_{\text{query}} = (n_{\text{in}} \cdot p_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}} \cdot p_{\text{out}}) / 10^{6}$.
- ✅ Latency formula: $\text{TBT} \approx \text{TTFT} + n_{\text{out}} \cdot \text{ITL}$.
- ✅ Memory formula: $M_{\text{eff}} = N_{\text{params}} \times B_{\text{quant}} \times (1 + O_{\text{kv}})$.
- ✅ Quantization ladder: FP32 (4 B) → FP16 (2 B) → INT8 (1 B) → INT4 (0.5 B).
- ✅ RAG quality metric: Recall@k = $|\text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved@k}| / |\text{Relevant}|$.
- ✅ Hallucination rate: $H = \frac{1}{N} \sum \mathbb{1}[\text{unfaithful}]$.
- ✅ Lifecycle loop: **D-F-M-S-M-O-G** (Discover → Formalize → Model → Serve → Monitor → Optimize → Govern).
- ✅ Serving engines: vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM, Triton, llama.cpp, Ollama.
- ✅ Eval harnesses: RAGAS, TruLens, DeepEval, LangSmith.
- ✅ Safety: PII redaction, prompt-injection defense, rate limiting, RBAC.
- ✅ Compliance: SOC2, ISO 27001, GDPR, India DPDP Act 2023.
- ✅ Always draw the **closed feedback loop** in lifecycle diagrams.
- ✅ Always specify **units** (USD/INR, ms/s, GB) in numerical answers.
